01-04-0527 Justinianus became the emperor of Byzantium.
01-04-1572 The Sea Beggars under Guillaume de la Marck landed in Holland and captured the small town of Briel.
01-04-1578 William Harvey of England discovered blood circulation.
01-04-1621 The Plymouth, MA, colonists created the first treaty with Native Americans.
01-04-1724 Jonathan Swift published Drapier's letters.
01-04-1748 The ruins of Pompeii were found.
01-04-1778 Oliver Pollock, a New Orleans businessman, created the "$" symbol.
01-04-1789 The U.S. House of Representatives held its first full meeting in New York City. Frederick Muhlenberg of Pennsylvania was elected the first House Speaker.
01-04-1793 In Japan, the volcano Unsen erupted killing about 53,000.
01-04-1826 Samuel Mory patented the internal combustion engine.
01-04-1853 Cincinnati became the first U.S. city to pay fire fighters a regular salary.
01-04-1863 The first wartime conscription law goes into effect in the U.S.
01-04-1864 The first travel accident policy was issued to James Batterson by the Travelers Insurance Company.
01-04-1865 At the Battle of Five Forks in Petersburg, VA, Gen. Robert E. Lee began his final offensive.
01-04-1867 Blacks voted in the municipal election in Tuscumbia, AL.
01-04-1867 The International Exhibition opened in Paris.
01-04-1867 Singapore, Penang & Malakka became British crown colonies.
01-04-1868 The Hampton Institute opened.
01-04-1872 The first edition of "The Standard" was published.
01-04-1873 The British White Star steamship Atlantic sank off Nova Scotia killing 547.
01-04-1873 Mehmed Kemals play "Vatan" premiered in Constantinople.
01-04-1876 The first official National League (NL) baseball game took place. Boston beat Philadelphia 6-5.
01-04-1881 Anti-Jewish riots took place in Jerusalem.
01-04-1881 Kingdom post office in Netherlands opened.
01-04-1889 The first dishwashing machine was marketed (in Chicago).
01-04-1891 The London-Paris telephone connection opened.
01-04-1905 The British East African Protectorate became the colony of Kenya.
01-04-1905 Paris and Berlin were linked by telephone.
01-04-1916 The first U.S. national women's swimming championships were held.
01-04-1918 England's Royal Flying Corps was replaced by the Royal Air Force.
01-04-1924 Adolf Hitler was sentenced to five years in prison for high treason in relation to the "Beer Hall Putsch."
01-04-1924 Imperial Airways was formed in Britain.
01-04-1927 The first automatic record changer was introduced by His Master's Voice.
01-04-1928 China's Chiang Kai-shek began attacking communists.
01-04-1929 Louie Marx introduced the Yo-Yo.
01-04-1930 Leo Hartnett of the Chicago Cubs broke the altitude record for a catch by catching a baseball dropped from the Goodyear blimp 800 feet over Los Angeles, CA.
01-04-1931 An Earthquake devastated Managua Nicaragua killing 2,000.
01-04-1931 Jackie Mitchell became the first female in professional baseball when she signed with the Chattanooga Baseball Club.
01-04-1933 Nazi Germany began the persecution of Jews by boycotting Jewish businesses.
01-04-1934 Bonnie & Clyde killed 2 police officers.
01-04-1935 The first radio tube to be made of metal was announced.
01-04-1937 Aden became a British colony.
01-04-1938 The first commercially successful fluorescent lamps were introduced.
01-04-1938 The Baseball Hall of Fame opened in Cooperstown, NY.
01-04-1939 The U.S. recognized the Franco government in Spain at end of Spanish civil war.
01-04-1941 The first contract for advertising on a commercial FM radio station began on W71NY in New York City.
01-04-1945 U.S. forces invaded Okinawa during World War II. It was the last campaign of World War II.
01-04-1946 Weight Watchers was formed.
01-04-1946 A tidal wave (tsunami) struck the Hawaiian Islands killing more than 170 people.
01-04-1948 The Berlin Airlift began.
01-04-1949 "Happy Pappy" premiered. It was the first all-black-cast variety show.
01-04-1950 Italian Somalia became a United Nations trust territory under Italian administration.
01-04-1952 The Big Bang theory was proposed in "Physical Review" by Alpher, Bethe & Gamow.
01-04-1953 The U.S. Congress created the Department of Health Education and Welfare.
01-04-1954 The U.S. Air Force Academy was formed in Colorado.
01-04-1955 "One Man's Family" was seen on TV for the final time after a six-year run on NBC-TV.
01-04-1960 France exploded 2 atom bombs in the Sahara Desert.
01-04-1960 The U.S. launched TIROS-1. It was the first weather satellite.
01-04-1963 Workers of the International Typographical Union ended their strike that had closed nine New York City newspapers. The strike ended 114 days after it began on December 8, 1962.
01-04-1963 The Soap operas "General Hospital" and "Doctors" premiered on television.
01-04-1970 The U.S. Army charged Captain Ernest Medina in the My Lai massacre.
01-04-1970 U.S. President Nixon signed the bill, the Public Health Cigarette Smoking Act, that banned cigarette advertisements to be effective on January 1, 1971.
01-04-1971 The United Kingdom lifted all restrictions on gold ownership.
01-04-1972 North Vietnamese and Viet Cong troops renewed their offensive in South Vietnam.
01-04-1973 Japan allowed its citizens to own gold.
01-04-1976 Apple Computer began operations.
01-04-1979 Iran was proclaimed to be an Islamic Republic by Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini after the fall of the Shah.
01-04-1980 A failed assassination attempt against Iraqi vice-premier Tariq Aziz occurred.
01-04-1982 The U.S. transferred the Canal Zone to Panama.
01-04-1983 New York Islander Mike Bossy became the first National Hockey League (NHL) player to score 60 goals in 3 consecutive seasons.
01-04-1986 The U.S. submarine Nathaniel Green ran aground in the Irish Sea.
01-04-1987 Steve Newman became the first man to walk around the world. The walk was 22,000 miles and took 4 years.
01-04-1987 U.S. President Reagan told doctors in Philadelphia, "We've declared AIDS public health enemy No. 1."
01-04-1991 Iran released British hostage Roger Cooper after 5 years.
01-04-1991 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that jurors could not be barred from serving due to their race.
01-04-1991 The Warsaw Pact was officially dissolved.
01-04-1992 Players began the first strike in the 75-year history of the National Hockey League (NHL).
01-04-1996 U.S. President Bill Clinton threw out the first ball preceding a game between the Kansas City Royals and the Baltimore Orioles.
01-04-1997 David Carradine received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
01-04-1998 A federal judge dismissed the Paula Jones' sexual harassment lawsuit against U.S. President Clinton saying that the claims fell "far short" of being worthy of a trial.
01-04-1999 In Zhytomyr, Ukraine, Anatoliy Onoprienko was sentenced to death for the deaths of 52 men, women and children. 43 of the killings occurred in a 6-month period.
01-04-1999 The Canadian territory of Nunavut was created. It was carved from the eastern part of the Northwest Territories and covered about 772,000 square miles.
01-04-2001 China began holding 24 crewmembers of a U.S. surveillance plane. The EP-3E U.S. Navy crew had made an emergency landing after an in-flight collision with a Chinese fighter jet. The Chinese pilot was missing and presumed dead. The U.S. crew was released on April 11, 2001.
01-04-2001 Former Yugoslav President Slobodan Milosevic was arrested on corruption charges after a 26-hour standoff with the police at his Belgrade villa.
01-04-2003 North Korea test-fired an anti-ship missile off its west coast.
01-04-2003 Jason Mewes was ordered to complete drug rehabilitation or face five years in jail stemming from a drug conviction in 1999.
01-04-2004 U.S. President George W. Bush signed the Unborn Victims of Violence Act. The bill made it a crime to harm a fetus during an assault on a pregnant woman.
01-04-2004 Gateway Inc. announced that it would be closing all of its 188 stores on April 9.
02-04-1513 Spanish explorer Juan Ponce de Leon landed in Florida.
02-04-1792 The U.S. Congress passed the Coinage Act to regulate the coins of the United States. The act authorized $10 Eagle, $5 half-Eagle & 2.50 quarter-Eagle gold coins & silver dollar, dollar, quarter, dime & half-dime to be minted.
02-04-1801 During the Napoleonic Wars, the Danish fleet was destroyed by the British at the Battle of Copenhagen.
02-04-1860 The first Italian Parliament met in Turin.
02-04-1865 Confederate President Davis and most of his Cabinet fled the Confederate capital of Richmond, VA.
02-04-1872 G.B. Brayton received a patent for the gas-powered streetcar.
02-04-1877 The first Egg Roll was held on the grounds of the White House in Washington, DC.
02-04-1889 Charles Hall patented aluminum.
02-04-1902 The first motion picture theatre opened in Los Angeles with the name Electric Theatre.
02-04-1905 The Simplon rail tunnel officially opened. The tunnel went under the Alps and linked Switzerland and Italy.
02-04-1910 Karl Harris perfected the process for the artificial synthesis of rubber.
02-04-1914 The U.S. Federal Reserve Board announced plans to divide the country into 12 districts.
02-04-1917 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson presented a declaration of war against Germany to the U.S. Congress.
02-04-1932 A $50,000 ransom was paid for the infant son of Charles and Anna Lindbergh. He child was not returned and was found dead the next month.
02-04-1935 Sir Watson-Watt was granted a patent for RADAR.
02-04-1944 The Soviet Union announced that its troops had crossed the Prut River and entered Romania.
02-04-1947 "The Big Story" debuted on NBC radio. It was on the air for eight years.
02-04-1947 The U.N. Security Council voted to appoint the U.S. as trustee for former Japanese-held Pacific Islands.
02-04-1951 U.S. General Dwight Eisenhower assumed command of all allied forces in the Western Mediterranean area and Europe.
02-04-1956 "The Edge of Night" and "As the World Turns" debuted on CBS-TV.
02-04-1958 The National Advisory Council on Aeronautics was renamed NASA.
02-04-1960 France signed an agreement with Madagascar that proclaimed the country an independent state within the French community.
02-04-1963 Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King began the first non-violent campaign in Birmingham, AL.
02-04-1966 South Vietnamese troops joined in demonstrations at Hue and Da Nang for an end to military rule.
02-04-1967 In Peking, hundreds of thousands demonstrated against Mao foe Liu Shao-chi.
02-04-1972 Burt Reynolds appeared nude in "Cosmopolitan" magazine.
02-04-1978 The first episode of "Dallas" aired on CBS.
02-04-1982 Argentina invaded the British-owned Falkland Islands. The following June Britain took the islands back.
02-04-1984 John Thompson became the first black coach to lead his team to the NCAA college basketball championship.
02-04-1984 In Jerusalem, three Arab gunmen wounded 48 people when they opened fire into a crowd of shoppers.
02-04-1985 The NCAA Rules Committee adopted the 45-second shot clock for men’s basketball to begin in the 1986 season.
02-04-1986 On a TWA airliner flying from Rome to Athens a bomb exploded under a seat killing four Americans.
02-04-1987 The speed limit on U.S. interstate highways was increased to 65 miles per hour in limited areas.
02-04-1988 U.S. Special Prosecutor James McKay declined to indict Attorney General Edwin Meese for criminal wrongdoing.
02-04-1989 An editorial in the "New York Times" declared that the Cold War was over.
02-04-1989 General Prosper Avril, Haiti's military leader, survived a coup attempt. The attempt was apparently provoked by Avril's U.S.-backed efforts to fight drug trafficking.
02-04-1990 Iraqi President Saddam Hussein threatened to incinerate half of Israel with chemical weapons if Israel joined a conspiracy against Iraq.
02-04-1992 Mob boss John Gotti was convicted in New York of murder and racketeering. He was later sentenced to life in prison.
02-04-1995 The costliest strike in professional sports history ended when baseball owners agreed to let players play without a contract.
02-04-1996 Russia and Belarus signed a treaty that created a political and economic alliance in an effort to reunite the two former Soviet republics.
02-04-1996 Lech Walesa resumed his old job as an electrician at the Gdansk shipyard. He was the former Solidarity union leader who became Poland's first post-war democratic president.
02-04-2002 Israeli troops surrounded the Church of the Nativity. More than 200 Palestinians had taken refuge at the church when Israel invaded Bethlehem.
03-04-1776 Harvard College conferred the first honorary Doctor of Laws degree to George Washington.
03-04-1829 James Carrington patented the coffee mill.
03-04-1860 The Pony Express connected St. Joseph, MO and Sacramento, CA. The Pony Express only lasted about a year and a half.
03-04-1862 Slavery was abolished in Washington, DC.
03-04-1865 Union forces occupy Confederate capital of Richmond, Virginia.
03-04-1866 Rudolph Eickemeyer and G. Osterheld patented a blocking and shaping machine for hats.
03-04-1882 The American outlaw Jesse James was shot in the back and killed by Robert Ford for a $5,000 reward. There was later controversy over whether it was actually Jesse James that had been killed.
03-04-1910 Alaska's Mt. McKinley, the highest mountain in North America was climbed.
03-04-1933 First Lady Eleanor Roosevelt informed newspaper reporters that beer would be served at the White House. This followed the March 22 legislation that legalized "3.2" beer.
03-04-1936 Richard Bruno Hauptmann was executed for the kidnapping and death of the son of Charles and Anne Lindbergh.
03-04-1942 The Japanese began their all-out assault on the U.S. and Filipino troops at Bataan.
03-04-1946 Lt. General Masaharu Homma, the Japanese commander responsible for the Bataan Death March, was executed in the Philippines.
03-04-1948 Harry Truman signed the Marshall Plan to revive war-torn Europe. It was $5 billion in aid for 16 countries.
03-04-1949 Dean Martin and Jerry Lewis debuted on radio on the "Martin and Lewis Show". The NBC program ran until 1952.
03-04-1953 "TV Guide" was published for the first time.
03-04-1967 The U.S. State Department said that Hanoi might be brainwashing American prisoners.
03-04-1968 Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his "mountaintop" speech just 24 hours before he was assassinated.
03-04-1968 North Vietnam agreed to meet with U.S. representatives to set up preliminary peace talks.
03-04-1972 Charlie Chaplin returned to the U.S. after a twenty-year absence.
03-04-1979 Jane Byrne became the first female mayor in Chicago.
03-04-1982 John Chancellor stepped down as anchor of the "The NBC Nightly News." Roger Mudd and Tom Brokaw became the co-anchors of the show.
03-04-1984 Sikh terrorists killed a member of the Indian Parliament in his home.
03-04-1984 Col. Lansana Konte became the new president of Guinea when the armed forces seized power after the death of Sekou Toure.
03-04-1985 The U.S. charged that Israel violated the Geneva Convention by deporting Shiite prisoners.
03-04-1987 Riots disrupted mass during the Pope's visit to Santiago, Chili.
03-04-1993 The Norman Rockwell Museum opened in Stockbridge, MA.
03-04-1996 An Air Force jetliner carrying Commerce Secretary Ron Brown crashed in Croatia, killing all 35 people aboard.
03-04-1996 Unabomber suspect Theodore Kaczynski was arrested. He pled guilty in January 1998 to five Unabomber attacks in exchange for a life sentence without chance for parole.
03-04-1998 The Dow Jones industrial average climbed above 9,000 for the first time.
03-04-2000 A U.S. federal judge ruled that Microsoft had violated U.S. antitrust laws by keeping "an oppressive thumb" on its competitors. Microsoft said that they would appeal the ruling.
03-04-2000 The Nasdaq set a one-day record when it lost 349.15 points to close at 4,233.68.
04-04-0896 Formosus ended his reign as pope.
04-04-1541 Ignatius of Loyola became the first superior-general of the Jesuits.
04-04-1581 Francis Drake completed the circumnavigation of the world.
04-04-1687 King James II ordered that his declaration of indulgence be read in church.
04-04-1812 The territory of Orleans became the 18th U.S. state and will become known as Louisiana.
04-04-1818 The U.S. flag was declared to have 13 red and white stripes and 20 stars and that a new star would be added for the each new state.
04-04-1841 U.S. President William Henry Harrison, at the age of 68, became the first president to die in office. He had been sworn in only a month before he died of pneumonia.
04-04-1848 Thomas Douglas became the first San Francisco public teacher.
04-04-1850 The city of Los Angeles was incorporated.
04-04-1862 In the U.S., the Battle of Yorktown began as Union General George B. McClellan closed in on Richmond, VA.
04-04-1887 Susanna M. Salter became mayor of Argonia, KS, making her the first woman mayor in the U.S.
04-04-1902 British Financier Cecil Rhodes left $10 million in his will that would provide scholarships for Americans to Oxford University in England.
04-04-1905 In Kangra, India, an earthquake killed 370,000 people.
04-04-1914 The first known serialized moving picture opened in New York City, NY. It was "The Perils of Pauline".
04-04-1917 The U.S. Senate voted 90-6 to enter World War I on the Allied side.
04-04-1918 The Battle of Somme, an offensive by the British against the German Army ended.
04-04-1932 After five years of research, professor C.G. King, of the University of Pittsburgh, isolated vitamin C.
04-04-1945 Hungary was liberated from Nazi occupation.
04-04-1945 During World War II, U.S. forces liberated the Nazi death camp Ohrdruf in Germany.
04-04-1949 Twelve nations signed a treaty to create The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO).
04-04-1953 Fifteen doctors were released by Soviet leaders. The doctors had been arrested before Stalin had died and were accused of plotting against him.
04-04-1967 The U.S. lost its 500th plane over Vietnam.
04-04-1967 Johnny Carson quit "The Tonight Show." He returned three weeks later after getting a raise of $30,000 a week.
04-04-1968 Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated at the age of 39.
04-04-1969 Dr. Denton Cooley implanted the first temporary artificial heart.
04-04-1971 Veterans stadium in Philadelphia, PA, was dedicated this day.
04-04-1974 Hank Aaron tied Babe Ruth's major league baseball home-run record with 714.
04-04-1975 More than 130 people, most of them children, were killed when a U.S. Air Force transport plane evacuating Vietnamese orphans crashed just after takeoff from Saigon.
04-04-1979 Zulfikar Ali Bhutto, the president of Pakistan, was executed. He had been convicted of conspiring to murder a political opponent.
04-04-1981 Henry Cisneros became the first Mexican-American elected mayor of a major U.S. city, which was San Antonio, TX.
04-04-1984 U.S. President Reagan proposed an international ban on chemical weapons.
04-04-1986 Wayne Gretzky set an NHL record with his 213th point of the season.
04-04-1987 The U.S. charged the Soviet Union with wiretapping a U.S. Embassy.
04-04-1988 Arizona Governor Evan Mecham was voted out of office by the Arizona Senate. Mecham was found guilty of diverting state funds to his auto business and of trying to impede an investigation into a death threat to a grand jury witness.
04-04-1990 In the U.S., securities law violator Ivan Boesky was released from federal custody.
04-04-1991 Pennsylvanian Senator John Heinz and six others were killed when a helicopter collided with Heinz's plane over a schoolyard in Merion, PA.
04-04-1992 Sali Berisha became the first non-Marxist president of Albania since World War II.
04-04-1994 Netscape Communications (Mosaic Communications) was founded.
04-04-1995 U.S. Senator Alfonse D'Amato ridiculed judge Lance Ito using a mock Japanese accent on a nationally syndicated radio program. D'Amato apologized two days later for the act.
04-04-1999 The Colorado Rockies and the San Diego Padres played the first major league season opener to be held in Mexico. The Rockies beat the Padres 8-2.
05-04-1242 Russian troops repelled an invasion attempt by the Teutonic Knights.
05-04-1614 American Indian Pocahontas married English colonist John Rolfe in Virginia.
05-04-1621 The Mayflower sailed from Plymouth, MA, on a return trip to England.
05-04-1792 U.S. President George Washington cast the first presidential veto. The measure was for apportioning representatives among the states.
05-04-1806 Isaac Quintard patented the cider mill.
05-04-1827 James H. Hackett became the first American actor to appear abroad as he performed at Covent Garden in London, England.
05-04-1843 Queen Victoria proclaimed Hong Kong to be a British crown colony.
05-04-1869 Daniel Bakeman, the last surviving soldier of the U.S. Revolutionary War, died at the age of 109.
05-04-1887 Anne Sullivan taught Helen Keller the meaning of the word "water" as spelled out in the manual alphabet.
05-04-1892 Walter H. Coe patented gold leaf in rolls.
05-04-1895 Playwright Oscar Wilde lost his criminal libel case against the Marquess of Queensberry. Wilde had been accused of homosexual practices.
05-04-1908 The Japanese Army reached the Yalu River as the Russians retreated.
05-04-1919 Eamon de Valera became president of Ireland.
05-04-1923 Firestone Tire and Rubber Company began the first regular production of balloon tires.
05-04-1930 Mahatma Ghandi defied British law by making salt in India.
05-04-1933 The first operation to remove a lung was performed at Barnes Hospital in St. Louis, MO.
05-04-1941 German commandos secured docks along the Danube River in preparation for Germany’s invasion of the Balkans.
05-04-1951 Americans Julius and Ethel Rosenberg were sentenced to death for committing espionage for the Soviet Union.
05-04-1953 Jomo Kenyatta was convicted and sentenced to 7 years in prison for orchestrating the Mau-Mau rebellion in Kenya.
05-04-1955 Winston Churchill resigned as British prime minister.
05-04-1984 Kareem Abdul-Jabbar (Los Angeles Lakers) became the all-time NBA regular season scoring leader when he broke Wilt Chamberlain's record of 31,419 career points.
05-04-1985 John McEnroe said "any man can beat any woman at any sport, especially tennis."
05-04-1986 A discotheque in Berlin was bombed by Libyans. The U.S. attacked Libya with warplanes on April 15, 1986.
05-04-1987 FOX Broadcasting Company launched "Married....With Children" and "The Tracey Ullman Show". The two shows were the beginning of the FOX lineup.
05-04-1989 In Poland, accords were signed between Solidarity and the government that set free elections for June 1989. The eight-year ban on Solidarity was also set to be lifted.
05-04-1998 The Akashi Kaikyo Bridge in Japan opened becoming the largest suspension bridge in the world. It links Shikoku and Honshu. The bridge cost about $3.8 billion.
05-04-1999 Two Libyans suspected of bombing a Pan Am jet in 1988 were handed over so they could be flown to the Netherlands for trial. 270 people were killed in the bombing.
05-04-1999 In Laramie, WY, Russell Henderson plead guilty to kidnapping and felony murder in the death of Matthew Shepard.
05-04-2004 Near Mexico City's international airport, lightning struck the jet Mexican President Vicente Fox was on.
06-04-1199 English King Richard I was killed by an arrow at the siege of the castle of Chaluz in France.
06-04-1789 The first U.S. Congress began regular sessions at the Federal Hall in New York City.
06-04-1814 Granted sovereignty in the island of Elba and a pension from the French government, Napoleon Bonaparte abdicates at Fountainebleau. He was allowed to keep the title of emperor.
06-04-1830 Joseph Smith and five others organized the Mormon Church in Seneca, NY.
06-04-1830 Relations between the Texans and Mexico reached a new low when Mexico would not allow further emigration into Texas by settlers from the U.S.
06-04-1862 The American Civil War Battle of Shiloh began in Tennessee.
06-04-1865 At the Battle of Sayler's Creek, a third of Lee's army was cut off by Union troops pursuing him to Appomattox.
06-04-1875 Alexander Graham Bell was granted a patent for the multiple telegraph, which sent two signals at the same time.
06-04-1896 The first modern Olympic Games began in Athens, Greece.
06-04-1903 French Army Nationalists were revealed for forging documents to guarantee a conviction for Alfred Dryfus.
06-04-1909 Americans Robert Peary and Matthew Henson claimed to be the first men to reach the North Pole.
06-04-1916 Charlie Chaplin became the highest-paid film star in the world when he signed a contract with Mutual Film Corporation for $675,000 a year. He was 26 years old.
06-04-1917 The U.S. Congress approved a declaration of war on Germany and entered World War I on the Allied side.
06-04-1924 Four planes leave Seattle on the first successful flight around the world.
06-04-1927 William P. MacCracken, Jr. earned license number ‘1’ when the Department of Commerce issued the first aviator’s license.
06-04-1931 "Little Orphan Annie" debuted on the NBC Blue network.
06-04-1938 The United States recognized the German conquest of Austria.
06-04-1941 German forces invaded Greece and Yugoslavia.
06-04-1945 "This is Your FBI" debuted on ABC radio.
06-04-1953 Iranian Premier Mossadegh demanded that the shah's power be reduced.
06-04-1957 Trolley cars in New York City completed their final runs.
06-04-1959 Hal Holbrook opened in the off-Broadway presentation of "Mark Twain Tonight."
06-04-1965 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson authorized the use of ground troops in combat operations in Vietnam.
06-04-1967 In South Vietnam, 1,500 Viet Cong attacked Quangtri and freed 200 prisoners.
06-04-1985 William J. Schroeder became the first artificial heart recipient to be discharged from the hospital.
06-04-1987 Dennis Levine began a two-year jail term for insider trading.
06-04-1987 Sugar Ray Leonard took the middleweight title from Marvin Hagler.
06-04-1988 Mathew Henson was awarded honors in Arlington National Cemetery. Henson had discovered the North Pole with Robert Peary.
06-04-1997 Mario Lemieux (Pittsburgh Penguins) announced that he would retire from the National Hockey League (NHL) following the playoffs of the current season.
06-04-1998 Citicorp and Travelers Group announced that they would be merging. The new creation was the largest financial-services conglomerate in the world. The name would become Citigroup.
06-04-1998 The Dow Jones industrial average closed above 9,000 points for the first time.
06-04-1998 Federal researchers in the U.S. announced that daily tamoxifen pills could cut breast cancer risk among high-risk women.
06-04-1998 Pakistan successfully tested medium-range missiles capable of attacking neighboring India.
06-04-1999 Carmen Electra filed for a divorce from Dennis Rodman. They had only been married six months.
07-04-1652 The Dutch established a settlement at Cape Town, South Africa.
07-04-1712 A slave revolt broke out in New York City.
07-04-1798 The territory of Mississippi was organized.
07-04-1862 Union General Ulysses S. Grant defeated Confederates at the Battle of Shiloh, TN.
07-04-1864 The first camel race in America was held in Sacramento, California.
07-04-1888 P.F. Collier published a weekly periodical for the first time under the name "Collier’s."
07-04-1922 U.S. Secretary of Interior leased Teapot Dome naval oil reserves in Wyoming.
07-04-1927 The first long-distance TV transmission was sent from Washington, DC, to New York City. The audience saw an image of Commerce Secretary Herbert Hoover.
07-04-1930 The first steel columns were set for the Empire State Building.
07-04-1933 Prohibition ended in the United States.
07-04-1940 Booker T. Washington became the first black to be pictured on a U.S. postage stamp.
07-04-1943 British and American armies linked up between Wadi Akarit and El Guettar in North Africa to form a solid line against the German army.
07-04-1945 The Japanese battleship Yamato, the world’s largest battleship, was sunk during the battle for Okinawa. The fleet was headed for a suicide mission.
07-04-1948 The musical "South Pacific" by Rogers and Hammerstein debuted on Broadway.
07-04-1948 The United Nations' World Health Organization began operations.
07-04-1953 The Big Four met for the first time in 2 years to seek an end to their air conflicts.
07-04-1953 IBM unveiled the IBM 701 Electronic Data Processing Machine. It was IBM's first commercially available scientific computer.
07-04-1957 The last of New York City's electric trolleys completed its final run from Queens to Manhattan.
07-04-1963 At the age of 23, Jack Nicklaus became the youngest golfer to win the Green Jacket at the Masters Tournament.
07-04-1963 Yugoslavia proclaimed itself a Socialist republic.
07-04-1963 Josip Broz Tito was proclaimed to be the leader of Yugoslavia for life.
07-04-1966 The U.S. recovered a hydrogen bomb it had lost off the coast of Spain.
07-04-1967 Israel reported that they had shot down six Syrian MIGs.
07-04-1969 The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously struck down laws prohibiting private possession of obscene material.
07-04-1970 John Wayne won his first and only Oscar for his role in "True Grit." He had been in over 200 films.
07-04-1971 U.S. President Nixon pledged to withdraw 100,000 more men from Vietnam by December.
07-04-1980 The U.S. broke diplomatic relations with Iran and imposed economic sanctions in response to the taking of hostages on November 4, 1979.
07-04-1983 Specialist Story Musgrave and Don Peterson made the first Space Shuttle spacewalk.
07-04-1985 In Goteborg, Sweden, China swept all of the world table tennis titles except for men's doubles.
07-04-1985 In Sudan, Gen. Swar el-Dahab took over the Presidency while President Gaafar el-Nimeiry was visiting the U.S. and Egypt.
07-04-1985 The Soviet Union announced a unilateral freeze on medium-range nuclear missiles.
07-04-1987 In Oklahoma a 16-month-old baby was killed by a pit bull. On the same day a 67-year-old man was killed by another pit bull in Dayton, OH.
07-04-1988 Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev agreed to final terms of a Soviet withdrawal from Afghanistan. Soviet troops began leaving on May 16, 1988.
07-04-1988 In Fort Smith, AR, 13 white supremacists were acquitted on charges for plotting to overthrow the U.S. federal government.
07-04-1989 A Soviet submarine carrying nuclear weapons sank in the Norwegian Sea.
07-04-1990 In the U.S., John Poindexter was found guilty of five counts at his Iran-Contra trial. The convictions were later reversed on appeal.
07-04-1990 At Cincinnati's Contemporary Arts Center a display of Robert Mapplethorpe's photographs went on display. On the same day the center and its director were indicted on obscenity charges. The charges resulted in acquittal.
07-04-1994 Civil war erupted in Rwanda between the Patriotic Front rebel group and government soldiers. Hundreds of thousands were slaughtered in the months that followed.
07-04-1998 Mary Bono, the widow of Sonny Bono, won a special election to serve out the remainder of her husband's congressional term.
07-04-1999 Yugoslav authorities sealed off Kosovo's main border crossings to prevent ethnic Albanians from leaving.
07-04-2000 U.S. President Clinton signed the Senior Citizens Freedom to Work Act of 2000. The bill reversed a Depression-era law and allows senior citizens to earn money without losing Social Security retirement benefits.
07-04-2002 The Roman Catholic archdiocese announced that six priests from the Archdiocese of New York were suspended over allegations of sexual misconduct.
08-04-1513 Explorer Juan Ponce de Leon claimed Florida for Spain.
08-04-1525 Albert von Brandenburg, the leader of the Teutonic Order, assumes the title "Duke of Prussia" and passed the first laws of the Protestant church, making Prussia a Protestant state.
08-04-1789 The U.S. House of Representatives held its first meeting.
08-04-1832 About 300 American troops of the 6th Infantry left Jefferson Barracks, St. Louis, to confront the Sauk Indians in the Black Hawk War.
08-04-1834 In New York City, Cornelius Lawrence became the first mayor to be elected by popular vote in a city election.
08-04-1839 The first Intercollegiate Rodeo was held at the Godshall Ranch, Apple Valley, CA.
08-04-1873 Alfred Paraf patented the first successful oleomargarine.
08-04-1911 The first squash tournament was played at the Harvard Club in New York City.
08-04-1913 The Seventeenth amendment was ratified, requiring direct election of senators.
08-04-1935 The Works Progress Administration was approved by the U.S. Congress.
08-04-1939 Italy invaded Albania.
08-04-1942 The Soviets opened a rail link to the besieged city of Leningrad.
08-04-1943 Wendell Wilkie’s "One World" was published for the first time.
08-04-1946 The League of Nations assembled in Geneva for the last time.
08-04-1947 The first illustrated insurance policy was issued by the Allstate Insurance Company.
08-04-1952 U.S. President Truman seized steel mills to prevent a nationwide strike.
08-04-1953 The bones of Sitting Bull were moved from North Dakota to South Dakota.
08-04-1962 Bay of Pigs invaders got thirty years imprisonment in Cuba.
08-04-1974 Hank Aaron hits 715th home run breaking Babe Ruth's record.
08-04-1975 Frank Robinson of the Cleveland Indians became first black manager of a major league baseball team.
08-04-1985 India filed suit against Union Carbide for the Bhopal disaster.
08-04-1985 Phyllis Diller underwent a surgical procedure for permanent eyeliner to eliminate the need for eyelid makeup.
08-04-1986 Clint Eastwood was elected mayor of Carmel, CA.
08-04-1987 Los Angeles Dodgers executive Al Campanis resigned over remarks he had made. While on ABC's "Nightline" Campanis said that blacks "may not have some of the necessities" to hold managerial jobs in major-league baseball.
08-04-1988 Former U.S. President Reagan aid Lyn Nofzinger was sentenced to prison for illegal lobbying for Wedtech Corp.
08-04-1998 The widow of Martin Luther King Jr. presented new evidence in an appeal for new federal investigation of the assassination of her husband.
08-04-2000 19 U.S. troops were killed when a Marine V22 Osprey crashed during a training mission in Arizona.
08-04-2002 Ed McMahon filed a $20 million lawsuit against his insurance company, two insurance adjusters, and several environmental cleanup contractors. The suit alleged breach of contract, negligence and intentional infliction of emotional distress concerning a toxic mold that had spread through McMahon's Beverly Hills home.
09-04-0193 In the Balkans, the distinguished soldier Septimius Seversus was proclaimed emperor by the army in Illyricum.
09-04-0715 Constantine ended his reign as Catholic Pope.
09-04-1241 In the Battle of Liegnitz, Mongol armies defeated the Poles and the Germans.
09-04-1454 The city states of Venice, Milan and Florence signed a peace agreement at Lodi, Italy.
09-04-1667 In Paris, The first public art exhibition was held at the Palais-Royale.
09-04-1682 Robert La Salle claimed the lower Mississippi River and all lands that touch it for France.
09-04-1770 Captain James Cook discovered Botany Bay on the Australian continent.
09-04-1831 Robert Jenkins lost an ear. The event started a war between Britain and Spain.
09-04-1833 Peterborough, NH, opened the first municipally supported public library in the United States.
09-04-1838 The National Galley opened in London.
09-04-1865 At Appomattox Court House, Virginia, General Robert E. Lee surrendered his Confederate Army to Union General Ulysses S. Grant in the parlor of William McClean's home. Grant allowed Rebel officers to keep their sidearms and permitted soldiers to keep their horses and mules. Though there were still Confederate armies in the field, the war was officially over. The four years of fighting had killed 360,000 Union troops and 260,000 Confederate troops.
09-04-1866 The Civil Rights Bill passed over U.S. President Andrew Johnson's veto.
09-04-1867 The U.S. Senate ratified the treaty with Russia that purchased the territory of Alaska by one vote.
09-04-1869 The Hudson Bay Company ceded its territory to Canada.
09-04-1870 The American Anti-Slavery Society was dissolved.
09-04-1872 S.R. Percy received a patent for dried milk.
09-04-1900 British forces routed the Boers at Kroonstadt, South Africa.
09-04-1905 The first aerial ferry bridge went into operation in Duluth, MN.
09-04-1912 The first exhibition baseball game was held at Fenway Park in Boston. The game was between Red Sox and Havard.
09-04-1913 The Brooklyn Dodgers' Ebbets Field opened.
09-04-1914 In London, the first full-color film, "The World, The Flesh & the Devil," was shown.
09-04-1916 The German army launched it’s third offensive during the Battle of Verdun.
09-04-1917 The Battle of Arras began as Canadian troops began a massive assault on Vimy Ridge.
09-04-1918 Latvia proclaimed its independence.
09-04-1921 The Russo-Polish conflict ended with signing of Riga Treaty.
09-04-1928 Mae West made her debut on Broadway in the production of "Diamond Lil."
09-04-1940 Germany invaded Norway and Denmark.
09-04-1942 In the Battle of Bataan, American and Filipino forces were overwhelmed by the Japanese Army.
09-04-1945 National Football League officials decreed that it was mandatory for football players to wear socks in all league games.
09-04-1945 At Bari, Italy, the Liberty exploded and killed 360 people. The ship was carrying aerial bombs.
09-04-1947 169 people were killed and 1,300 were injured by a series of tornadoes in Texas, Oklahoma and Kansas.
09-04-1950 Bob Hope made his first television appearance on "Star-Spangled Review" on NBC-TV.
09-04-1953 TV Guide was published for the first time.
09-04-1957 The Suez Canal was cleared for all shipping.
09-04-1959 NASA announced the selection of America's first seven astronauts.
09-04-1963 Winston Churchill became the first honorary U.S. citizen.
09-04-1965 "TIME" magazine featured a cover with the entire "Peanuts" comic gang.
09-04-1965 The Houston Astrodome held its first baseball game.
09-04-1967 The first Boeing 737 was rolled out for use.
09-04-1968 Murdered civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr., was buried.
09-04-1976 The U.S. and Russia agreed on the size of nuclear tests for peaceful use.
09-04-1984 Nicaragua asked the World Court to declare U.S. support for guerilla raids illegal.
09-04-1985 Japanese Premier Nakasone urged Japanese people to buy foreign products.
09-04-1986 It was announced that Patrick Duffy's character on the TV show Dallas would be returning after being killed off.
09-04-1987 Dikye Baggett became the first person to undergo corrective surgery for Parkinson’s disease.
09-04-1988 The U.S. imposed economic sanctions on Panama.
09-04-1989 16 civilians were killed during rioting in Soviet Georgia.
09-04-1989 Hundreds of thousands marched past the White House in support of the right to abortion.
09-04-1991 Georgia voted to secede from the U.S.S.R.
09-04-1992 Former Panamanian ruler Manuel Noriega was convicted in Miami, FL, of eight drug and racketeering charges.
09-04-1998 The National Prisoner of War Museum opened in Andersonville, GA, at the site of an infamous Civil War camp.
09-04-1998 More than 150 Muslims died in stampede in Mecca, Saudi Arabia, on last day of the haj pilgrimage.
09-04-1999 In Djibouti, Ismail Omar Guelleh of the ruling Popular Rally for Progress and the Front for the Restoration of Unity and Democracy was elected president.
09-04-1999 In Niger, President Ibrahim Baré Maďnassara was assassinated. Daouda Malam Wanke was designated president two days later.
09-04-2000 CBS-TV aired "Failsafe." It was the first live full-length show to by aired by CBS in 39 years.
10-04-1741 Frederick II of Prussia defeated Maria Theresa's forces at Mollwitz and conquered Silesia.
10-04-1790 The U.S. patent system was established.
10-04-1809 Austria declared war on France and its forces entered Bavaria.
10-04-1814 Napoleon was defeated at the Battle of Toulouse by the British and the Spanish. The defeat led to his abdication and exile to Elba.
10-04-1825 The first hotel opened in Hawaii.
10-04-1849 Walter Hunt patented the safety pin. He sold the rights for $100.
10-04-1854 The constitution of the Orange Free State in south Africa was proclaimed.
10-04-1862 Union forces began the bombardment of Fort Pulaski in Georgia along the Tybee River.
10-04-1865 During the American Civil War, at Appomattox, General Robert E. Lee issued his last order.
10-04-1866 The American Society for Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (ASPCA) was incorporated.
10-04-1902 South African Boers accepted British terms of surrender.
10-04-1912 The Titanic set sail from Southampton, England.
10-04-1916 The Professional Golfers Association (PGA) held its first championship tournament.
10-04-1919 In Mexico, revolutionary leader Emiliano Zapata was killed by government troops.
10-04-1922 The Genoa Conference opened. The meeting was used to discuss the reconstruction of Europe after World War I.
10-04-1925 F. Scott Fitzgerald published "The Great Gatsby" for the first time.
10-04-1930 The first synthetic rubber was produced.
10-04-1932 Paul von Hindenburg was elected president of Germany with 19 million votes. Adolf Hitler came in second with 13 million votes.
10-04-1938 Germany annexed Austria. 99.75 percent of Austrians had voted in a referundum to merge with Germany.
10-04-1941 In World War II, U.S. troops occupied Greenland to prevent Nazi infiltration.
10-04-1941 Ford Motor Co. became the last major automaker to recognize the United Auto Workers as the representative for its workers.
10-04-1944 Russian troops recaptured Odessa from the Germans.
10-04-1945 German Me 262 jet fighters shot down ten U.S. bombers near Berlin.
10-04-1953 Warner Bros. released "House of Wax." It was the first 3-D movie to be released by a major Hollywood studio.
10-04-1953 Actress Hedy Lamarr became a U.S. citizen.
10-04-1959 Japan's Crown Prince Akihito married commoner Michiko Shoda.
10-04-1960 The U.S. Senate passed the Civil Rights Bill.
10-04-1961 Gary Player of South Africa became the first foreign golfer to win the Masters Golf Tournament in Augusta, Georgia.
10-04-1963 129 people died when the nuclear-powered submarine USS Thresher failed to surface off Cape Cod, MA.
10-04-1967 The 13-day strike by the American Federation of Radio-TV Artists (AFTRA) came to an end less than two hours before the 39th Academy Awards presentation went on the air.
10-04-1968 U.S. President Johnson replaced General Westmoreland with General Creighton Abrams in Vietnam.
10-04-1971 The American table tennis team arrived in China. They were the first group of Americans officially allowed into China since the founding of the People Republic in 1949. The team had recieved the surprise invitation while in Japan for the 31st World Table Tennis Championship.
10-04-1972 An earthquake in southern Iran killed more than 5,000 people.
10-04-1972 The U.S. and the Soviet Union joined with 70 other nations in signing an agreement banning biological warfare.
10-04-1973 In Switzerland, 108 people died when a plane crashed while attempting to land at Basel.
10-04-1974 Yitzhak Rabin replaced resigning Israeli Prime Minister, Golda Meir. Meir resigned over differences within her Labor Party.
10-04-1980 Spain and Britain agreed to reopen the border between Gibraltar and Spain. It had been closed since 1969.
10-04-1981 Imprisoned IRA hunger striker Bobby Sands was elected to the British Parliament.
10-04-1988 On Wall Street, 48 million shares of Navistar International stock changed hands in a single-block trade. It was the largest transaction ever executed on the New York Stock Exchange.
10-04-1990 Three European hostages kidnapped at sea in 1987 by Palestinian extremists were released in Beirut.
10-04-1992 A bomb exploded in London's financial district. The bomb, set off by the Irish Republican Army, killed three people and injured 91.
10-04-1992 Outside Needles, CA, comedian Sam Kinison was killed when a pickup truck slammed into his car on a desert road between Los Angeles and Las Vegas.
10-04-1992 In Los Angeles, financier Charles Keating Jr. was sentenced to nine years in prison for swindling investors when his Lincoln Savings and Loan collapsed. The convictions were later overturned.
10-04-1993 South African Communist Party leader Chris Hani was assassinated.
10-04-1994 NATO warplanes launched air strikes for the first time on Serb forces that were advancing on the Bosnian Muslim town of Gordazde. The area had been declared a U.N. safe area.
10-04-1996 U.S. President Clinton vetoed a bill that would have outlawed a technique used to end pregnancies in their late stages.
10-04-1997 Rod Steiger received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
10-04-1998 Negotiators reached a peace accord on governing British ruled Northern Ireland. Britain's direct rule was ended.
10-04-1999 The www.June4.org web site was launched by Chinese dissidents and human rights activists to promote their campaign for democracy in China.
10-04-2000 Monitors from the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) reported irregularities in the voting in Georgia's presidential election on April 9. President Eduard Shevardnadze was reelected to a new five-year term.
10-04-2000 Ken Griffey Jr. became the youngest player in baseball history to reach 400 home runs. He was 30 years, 141 days old.
10-04-2001 Jane Swift took office as the first female governor of Massachusetts. She succeeded Paul Cellucci, who had resigned to become the U.S. ambassador to Canada.
10-04-2001 The Netherlands legalized mercy killings and assisted suicide for patients with unbearable, terminal illness.
10-04-2002 Former Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu spoke before the U.S. Senate as a representative of the Israeli government. He warned that suicide bombers would spread to the U.S. if Israel was not allowed to finish its military offensive in the West Bank. Netanyaho also cited the goals of dismantling the terror regime and expelling Arafat from the region, ridding the Palestinian territories of terrorist weapons and establishing "physical barriers" to protect Israelis from future Palestinian attacks.
11-04-1512 The forces of the Holy League were heavily defeated by the French at the Battle of Ravenna.
11-04-1689 William III and Mary II were crowned as joint sovereigns of Britain.
11-04-1713 The Treaty of Utrecht was signed, ending the War of Spanish Succession.
11-04-1783 After receiving a copy of the provisional treaty on March 13, the U.S. Congress proclaimed a formal end to hostilities with Great Britain.
11-04-1803 A twin-screw propeller steamboat was patented by John Stevens.
11-04-1814 Napoleon was forced to abdicate his throne. The allied European nations had marched into Paris on March 30, 1814. He was banished to the island of Elba.
11-04-1876 The stenotype was patented by John C. Zachos.
11-04-1876 The Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks was organized.
11-04-1895 Anaheim, CA, completed its new electric light system.
11-04-1898 U.S. President William McKinley asked Congress for a declaration of war with Spain.
11-04-1899 The treaty ending the Spanish-American War was declared in effect.
11-04-1921 Iowa became the first state to impose a cigarette tax.
11-04-1921 The first live sports event on radio took place this day on KDKA Radio. The event was a boxing match between Johnny Ray and Johnny Dundee.
11-04-1940 Andrew Ponzi set a world's record in a New York pocket billiards tournament when he ran 127 balls straight.
11-04-1941 Germany bombers blitzed Conventry, England.
11-04-1945 U.S. troops reached the Elbe River in Germany.
11-04-1945 During World War II, American soldiers liberated the Nazi concentration camp of Buchenwald in Germany.
11-04-1947 Jackie Robinson became the first black player in major-league history. He played in an exhibition game for the Brooklyn Dodgers.
11-04-1951 U.S. President Truman fired General Douglas MacArthur as head of United Nations forces in Korea.
11-04-1961 Israel began the trial of Adolf Eichman, accused of World War II war crimes.
11-04-1968 U.S. President Johnson signed the 1968 Civil Rights Act.
11-04-1970 Apollo 13 blasted off on a mission to the moon that was disrupted when an explosion crippled the spacecraft. The astronauts did return safely.
11-04-1974 The Judiciary committee subpoenas U.S. President Richard Nixon to produce tapes for impeachment inquiry.
11-04-1979 Idi Amin was deposed as president of Uganda as rebels and exiles backed by Tanzanian forces seized control.
11-04-1980 The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission issued regulations specifically prohibiting sexual harassment of workers by supervisors.
11-04-1981 U.S. President Ronald Reagan returned to the White House from the hospital after recovering from an assassination attempt.
11-04-1985 Scientists in Hawaii measured the distance between the earth and moon within one inch.
11-04-1985 The White House announced that President Reagan would visit the Nazi cemetery at Bitburg.
11-04-1986 Dodge Morgan sailed solo nonstop around the world in 150 days.
11-04-1986 In Groton, CT, the submarine Nautilus exhibit opened to the public.
11-04-1986 Kellogg's stopped giving tours of its breakfast-food plant. The reason for the end of the 80-year tradition was said to be that company secrets were at risk due to spies from other cereal companies.
11-04-1991 U.N. Security Council issued a formal cease-fire with Iraq.
11-04-1996 Forty-three African nations signed the African Nuclear Weapons Free Zone Treaty.
11-04-1996 Seven-year-old Jessica Dubroff was killed with her father and flight instructor when her plane crashed after takeoff from Cheyenne, Wyoming. Jessica had hoped to become the youngest person to fly cross-country.
11-04-1998 Northern Ireland's biggest political party, the Ulster Unionists, announced its backing of the historic peace deal.
11-04-1999 Daouda Malam Wanke was designated president of Niger. President Ibrahim Baré Maďnassara had been assassinated on April 9.
11-04-2001 China agreed to release 24 crewmembers of a U.S. surveillance plane. The EP-3E Navy crew had been held since April 1 on Hainon, where the plane had made an emergency landing after an in-flight collision with a Chinese fighter jet. The Chinese pilot was missing and presumed dead.
12-04-1096 Peter the Hermit gathered his army in Cologne.
12-04-1204 The Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople.
12-04-1606 England adopted the original Union Jack as its flag.
12-04-1770 The British Parliament repealed the Townsend Acts.
12-04-1782 The British navy won its only naval engagement against the colonists in the American Revolution at the Battle of Saints, off Dominica.
12-04-1799 Phineas Pratt patented the comb cutting machine.
12-04-1811 The first colonists arrived at Cape Disappointment, Washington.
12-04-1833 Charles Gaylor patented the fireproof safe.
12-04-1861 Fort Sumter was shelled by Confederacy, starting America's Civil War.
12-04-1864 Confederate Gen. Nathan Bedford Forrest captured Fort Pillow, in Tennessee and slaughters the black Union troops there.
12-04-1877 A catcher's mask was used in a baseball game for the first time by James Alexander Tyng.
12-04-1892 Voters in Lockport, New York, became the first in the U.S. to use voting machines.
12-04-1905 The Hippodrome opened in New York City.
12-04-1911 Pierre Prier completed the first non-stop London-Paris flight in three hours and 56 minutes.
12-04-1916 American cavalrymen and Mexican bandit troops clashed at Parrel, Mexico.
12-04-1927 The British Cabinet came out in favor of women voting rights.
12-04-1934 F. Scott Fitzgerald novel "Tender Is the Night" was first published.
12-04-1938 The first U.S. law requiring a medical test for a marriage license was enacted in New York.
12-04-1944 The U.S. Twentieth Air Force was activated to begin the strategic bombing of Japan.
12-04-1945 In New York, the organization of the first eye bank, the Eye Bank for Sight Restoration, was announced.
12-04-1945 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt died in Warm Spring, GA. He died of a cerebral hemorrhage at the age of 63. Harry S. Truman became president.
12-04-1955 The University of Michigan Polio Vaccine Evaluation Center announced that the polio vaccine of Dr. Jonas Salk was "safe, effective and potent."
12-04-1961 Soviet Yuri Alexeyevich Gagarin became first man to orbit the Earth.
12-04-1963 Police used dogs and cattle prods on peaceful civil rights demonstrators in Birmingham, AL.
12-04-1966 Emmett Ashford became the first African-American major league umpire.
12-04-1967 Jim Brown made his TV acting debut on the NBC show "I Spy."
12-04-1969 Lucy and Snoopy of the comic strip "Peanuts" made the cover of "Saturday Review."
12-04-1981 The space shuttle Columbia blasted off from Cape Canaveral, FL, on its first test flight.
12-04-1983 Harold Washington was elected the first black mayor of Chicago.
12-04-1984 Astronauts aboard the space shuttle Challenger made the first satellite repair in orbit by returning the Solar Max satellite to space.
12-04-1984 Israeli troops stormed a bus that had been hijacked the previous evening by four Arab terrorists. All the passengers were rescued and 2 of the hijackers were killed.
12-04-1985 U.S. Senator Jake Garn of Utah became the first senator to fly in space as the shuttle Discovery lifted off from Cape Canaveral, FL.
12-04-1985 In Spain, an explosion in a restaurant near a U.S. base killed 17 people.
12-04-1985 Federal inspectors declared that four animals of the Ringling Brothers and Barnum & Bailey Circus were not unicorns. They were goats with horns that had been surgically implanted.
12-04-1987 Texaco filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy after it failed to settle a legal dispute with Pennzoil Co.
12-04-1988 Harvard University won a patent for a genetically altered mouse. It was the first patent for a life form.
12-04-1988 The Chinese government named a new array of younger leaders to ensure economic reform.
12-04-1989 In the U.S.S.R, ration cards were issued for the first time since World War II. The ration was prompted by a sugar shortage.
12-04-1992 Disneyland Paris opened in Marne-La-Vallee, France.
12-04-1993 NATO began enforcing a no-fly zone over Bosnia and Herzegovina.
12-04-2000 More than 1,500 anti-drug agents raided four cities in Colombia and arrested 46 members of the "most powerful" heroin ring.
12-04-2000 Robert Cleaves, 71, was convicted of second degree murder and was sentenced to 16 years in prison. Cleaves had repeatedly run over Arnold Guerreiro on September 30, 1998 with his car after the two had an argument.
12-04-2000 Israel's High Court ordered the release of eight Lebanese detainees that had been held for years without a trial.
12-04-2002 A first edition version of Beatrix Potter's "Peter Rabbit" sold for $64,780 at Sotheby's. A signed first edition of J.R.R. Tolkien's "The Hobbit" sold for $66,630. A copy of "Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone," signed by J.K. Rowling sold for $16,660. A 250-piece collection of rare works by Charles Dickens sold for $512,650.
12-04-2002 It was announced that the South African version of "Sesame Street" would be introducing a character that was HIV-positive.
12-04-2002 JCPenney Chairman Allen Questrom rang the opening bell to start the business day at the New York Stock Exchange as part of the company's centennial celebrations. James Cash (J.C.) Penney opened his first retail store on April 14, 1902.
13-04-1598 King Henry IV of France signed the Edict of Nantes which granted political rights to French Protestant Huguenots.
13-04-1759 The French defeated the European allies in Battle of Bergen.
13-04-1775 Lord North extended the New England Restraining Act to South, Carolina, Virginia, Pennsylvania, New Jersey and Maryland. The act prohibited trade with any country other than Britain and Ireland.
13-04-1782 Washington, NC, was incorporated as the first town to be named for George Washington.
13-04-1796 The first known elephant to arrive in the United States from Bengal, India.
13-04-1808 William "Juda" Henry Lane perfected the tap dance.
13-04-1829 The English Parliament granted freedom of religion to Catholics.
13-04-1849 The Hungarian Republic was proclaimed.
13-04-1861 After 34 hours of bombardment, the Union-held Fort Sumter surrenders to Confederates.
13-04-1870 The Metropolitan Museum of Art was founded in New York City.
13-04-1916 The first hybrid, seed corn was purchased for 15-cents a bushel by Samuel Ramsay.
13-04-1919 British forces killed hundreds of Indian nationalists in the Amritsar Massacre.
13-04-1933 The first flight over Mount Everest was completed by Lord Clydesdale.
13-04-1941 German troops captured Belgrade, Yugoslavia.
13-04-1943 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt dedicated the Jefferson Memorial.
13-04-1945 Vienna fell to Soviet troops.
13-04-1949 Philip S. Hench and associates announced that cortizone was an effective treatment for rheumatoid arthritis.
13-04-1954 Hank Aaron debuted with the Milwaukee Braves.
13-04-1959 A Vatican edict prohibited Roman Catholics from voting for Communists.
13-04-1960 The first navigational satellite was launched into Earth's orbit.
13-04-1961 The U.N. General Assembly condemned South Africa due to apartheid.
13-04-1962 In the U.S., major steel companies rescinded announced price increases. The John F. Kennedy administration had been applying pressure against the price increases.
13-04-1963 Pete Rose of the Cincinnati Reds got his first hit in the major leagues.
13-04-1964 Sidney Poitier became the first black to win an Oscar for best actor. It was for his role in the movie "Lilies of the Field."
13-04-1970 An oxygen tank exploded on Apollo 13, preventing a planned moon landing.
13-04-1972 The first strike in the history of major league baseball ended. Players had walked off the field 13 days earlier.
13-04-1976 The U.S. Federal Reserve introduced $2 bicentennial notes.
13-04-1979 The world's longest doubles ping-pong match ended after 101 hours.
13-04-1981 Washington Post reporter Janet Cooke received a Pulitzer Prize for her feature about an 8-year-old heroin addict named "Jimmy." Cooke relinquished the prize two days later after admitting she had fabricated the story.
13-04-1984 U.S. President Reagan sent emergency military aid to El Salvador without congressional approval.
13-04-1984 Christopher Walker was killed in a fight with police in New Hampshire. Walker was wanted as a suspect in the kidnappings of 11 young women in several states.
13-04-1990 The Soviet Union accepted responsibility for the World War II murders of thousands of imprisoned Polish officers in the Katyn Forest. The Soviets had previously blamed the massacre on the Nazis.
13-04-1997 Tiger Woods became the youngest person to win the Masters Tournament at the age of 21. He also set a record when he finished at 18 under par.
13-04-1998 NationsBank and BankAmerica announced a $62.5 billion merger, creating the country's first coast-to-coast bank.
13-04-1998 Dolly, the world's first cloned sheep, gave natural birth to a healthy baby lamb.
13-04-1999 Jack Kervorkian was sentenced in Pontiac, MI, to 10 to 25 years in prison for the second-degree murder of Thomas Youk. Youk's assisted suicide was videotaped and shown on "60 Minutes" in 1998.
13-04-2000 Richard Gordon was charged with trying to extort $250,000 from Louie Anderson in exchange for not telling the tabloid media about Anderson once asking him for sex. Gordon was held without bail pending a court hearing.
13-04-2000 It was announced that 69 people had died when the Arlahada, a Philippine ferry, capsized. 70 people were rescued.
13-04-2002 Twenty-five Hindus were killed and about 30 were wounded when grenades were thrown by suspected Islamic guerrillas near Jammu-Kashir.
13-04-2002 Venezuela's interim president, Pedro Carmona, resigned a day after taking office. Thousands of protesters had supported over the ousting of president Hugo Chavez.
14-04-1543 Bartoleme Ferrelo returned to Spain after discovering San Francisco Bay in the New World.
14-04-1775 The first abolitionist society in U.S. was organized in Philadelphia with Ben Franklin as president.
14-04-1793 A royalist rebellion in Santo Domingo was crushed by French republican troops.
14-04-1828 The first edition of Noah Webster's dictionary was published under the name "American Dictionary of the English Language."
14-04-1860 The first Pony Express rider arrived in San Francisco with mail originating in St. Joseph, MO.
14-04-1865 U.S. President Abraham Lincoln was assassinated in Ford's Theater by John Wilkes Booth. He actually died early the next morning.
14-04-1894 First public showing of Thomas Edison's kinetoscope took place.
14-04-1902 James Cash (J.C.) Penney opened his first retail store in Kemmerer, WY. It was called the Golden Rule Store.
14-04-1910 U.S. President William Howard Taft threw out the first ball for the Washington Senators and the Philadelphia Athletics.
14-04-1912 The Atlantic passenger liner Titanic, on its maiden voyage hit an iceberg and began to sink. 1,517 people lost their lives and more than 700 survived.
14-04-1918 The U.S. First Aero Squadron engaged in America's first aerial dogfight with enemy aircraft over Toul, France.
14-04-1925 WGN became the first radio station to broadcast a regular season major league baseball game. The Cubs beat the Pirates 8-2.
14-04-1931 King Alfonso XIII of Spain went into exile and the Spanish Republic was proclaimed.
14-04-1939 The John Steinbeck novel "The Grapes of Wrath" was first published.
14-04-1946 The civil war between Communists and nationalist resumed in China.
14-04-1953 Viet Minh invaded Laos with 40,00 troops.
14-04-1956 Ampex Corporation of Redwood City, CA, demonstrated the first commercial magnetic tape recorder for sound and picture.
14-04-1959 The Taft Memorial Bell Tower was dedicated in Washington, DC.
14-04-1969 For the first time, a major league baseball game was played in Montreal, Canada.
14-04-1981 America's first space shuttle, Columbia, returned to Earth after a three-day test flight. The shuttle orbited the Earth 36 times during the mission.
14-04-1984 The Texas Board of Education began requiring that the state's public school textbooks describe the evolution of human beings as "theory rather than fact".
14-04-1985 The Russian paper "Pravda" called U.S. President Reagan's planned visit to Bitburg to visit the Nazi cemetery an "act of blasphemy".
14-04-1986 U.S. President Reagan announced the U.S. air raid on military and terrorist related targets in Libya.
14-04-1987 Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev proposed banning all missiles from Europe.
14-04-1988 Representatives from the U.S.S.R., Pakistan, Afghanistan and the U.S. signed an agreement that called for the withdrawal of Soviet forces from Afghanistan. The last Soviet troop left Afghanistan on February 15, 1989.
14-04-1988 In New York, real estate tycoons Harry and Leona Helmsley were indicted for income tax evasion.
14-04-1990 Cal Ripken of the Baltimore Orioles began a streak of 95 errorless games and 431 total chances by a shortstop.
14-04-1994 Two American F-15 warplanes inadvertently shot down two U.S. helicopters over northern Iraq. 26 people were killed including 15 Americans.
14-04-1998 The state of Virginia ignored the requests from the World Court and executed a Paraguayan for the murder of a U.S. woman.
14-04-1999 Pakistan test-fired a ballistic missile that was capable of carrying a nuclear warhead and reaching its rival neighbor India.
14-04-2000 After five years of deadlock, Russia approved the START II treaty that calls for the scrapping of U.S. and Russian nuclear warheads. The Russian government warned it would abandon all arms-control pacts if Washington continued with an anti-missile system.
14-04-2002 U.S. President George W. Bush sent a letter of congratulations to JCPenny's associates for being in business for 100 years. James Cash (J.C.) Penney had opened his first retail store on April 14, 1902.
15-04-1784 The first balloon was flown in Ireland.
15-04-1794 "Courrier Francais" became the first French daily newspaper to be published in the U.S.
15-04-1813 U.S. troops under James Wilkinson attacked the Spanish-held city of Mobile that would be in the future state of Alabama.
15-04-1817 The first American school for the deaf was opened in Hartford, CT.
15-04-1850 The city of San Francisco was incorporated.
15-04-1858 At the Battle of Azimghur, the Mexicans defeated Spanish loyalists.
15-04-1861 U.S. President Lincoln mobilized the Federal army.
15-04-1865 U.S. President Abraham Lincoln died from injuries inflicted by John Wilkes Booth.
15-04-1871 "Wild Bill" Hickok became the marshal of Abilene, Kansas.
15-04-1880 William Gladstone became Prime Minister of England.
15-04-1892 The General Electric Company was organized.
15-04-1899 Thomas Edison organized the Edison Portland Cement Company.
15-04-1912 The ocean liner Titanic sank at 2:27 a.m. in the North Atlantic after hitting an iceberg the evening before. 1,517 people died and more than 700 people survived.
15-04-1917 The British defeated the Germans at the battle of Arras.
15-04-1919 British troops killed 400 Indians at Amritsar, India.
15-04-1923 Insulin became generally available for people suffering with diabetes.
15-04-1934 In the comic strip "Blondie," Dagwood and Blondie Bumstead welcomed a baby boy, Alexander. The child would be nicknamed, Baby Dumpling.
15-04-1940 French and British troops landed at Narvik, Norway.
15-04-1945 During World War II, British and Canadian troops liberated the Nazi concentration camp Bergen-Belsen.
15-04-1947 Jackie Robinson played his first major league baseball game for the Brooklyn Dodgers. Previously he had only appeared in exhibition games.
15-04-1948 The Arabs were defeated in the first Jewish-Arab battle.
15-04-1952 U.S. President Harry Truman signed the official Japanese peace treaty.
15-04-1952 The first B-52 prototype was tested in the air.
15-04-1953 In Buenos Aires, six people were killed by a bomb at a rally addressed by President Peron.
15-04-1953 Pope Pius XII gave his approval of psychoanalysis but warned of possible abuses.
15-04-1953 Charlie Chaplin surrendered his U.S. re-entry permit rather than face proceedings by the U.S. Justice Department. Chaplin was accused of sympathizing with Communist groups.
15-04-1955 Ray Kroc started the McDonald's restaurant chain.
15-04-1956 The worlds’ first, all-color TV station was dedicated. It was WNBQ-TV in Chicago and is now WMAQ-TV.
15-04-1956 General Motors announced that the first free piston automobile had been developed.
15-04-1959 Cuban leader Fidel Castro began a U.S. goodwill tour.
15-04-1960 The Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) was organized at Shaw University.
15-04-1967 Richard Speck was found guilty of murdering eight student nurses.
15-04-1983 Tokyo Disneyland opened.
15-04-1984 Ten members of a family were found murdered in their home in New York City. An infant was found crawling among the corpses.
15-04-1986 U.S. F-111 warplanes attacked Libya in response to the bombing of a discotheque in Berlin on April 5, 1986.
15-04-1987 In Northhampton, MA, Amy Carter, Abbie Hoffman and 13 others were acquitted on civil disobedience charges related with a CIA protest.
15-04-1987 In New York City, Mbongeni Ngema's "Asinamali!" opened as the first South African play on Broadway.
15-04-1989 Students in Beijing launched a series of pro democracy protests upon the death of former Communist Party leader Hu Yaobang. The protests led to the Tienanmen Square massacre.
15-04-1989 In Sheffield, England, 93 people were killed and 180 were injured at a soccer game at Hillsborough Stadium when a crowd surged into an overcrowded standing area.
15-04-1994 The World Trade Organization was established.
15-04-1997 Christopher Reeve received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
15-04-1998 Pol Pot died at the age of 73. The leader of the Khmer Rouge regime thereby evaded prosecution for the deaths of 2 million Cambodians.
15-04-1999 In Algeria, former Foreign Minister Abdelaziz Bouteflika was elected president. All of the opposition candidates claimed that the vote was fraudulent and withdrew from the election.
15-04-1999 In Rawalpindi, Pakistan, a panel of two Lahore High Court judges convicted former Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto and her husband, Asif Ali Zardari, of corruption.
15-04-2000 600 anti-IMF (International Monetary Fund) protesters were arrested in Washington, DC, for demonstrating without a permit.
16-04-1969 Otho committed suicide after being defeated by Vitellius' troops at Bedriacum.
16-04-0556 Pelagius I began his reign as Catholic Pope.
16-04-1065 The Norman Robert Guiscard took Bari. Five centuries of Byzantine rule in southern Italy ended.
16-04-1175 Frederick I, Holy Roman Emperor, signed the Treaty of Montebello with the Lombard League.
16-04-1705 Queen Anne of England knighted Isaac Newton.
16-04-1746 Bonnie Prince Charles was defeated at the battle of Culloden, the last pitched battle fought in Britain.
16-04-1818 The U.S. Senate ratified Rush-Bagot amendment to form an unarmed U.S.-Canada border.
16-04-1851 A lighthouse was swept away in a gale at Minot’s Ledge, MA.
16-04-1854 San Salvador was destroyed by an earthquake.
16-04-1862 Confederate President Jefferson Davis approved conscription act for white males between 18 and 35.
16-04-1862 In the U.S., slavery was abolished by law in the District of Columbia.
16-04-1883 Paul Kruger became president of the South African Republic.
16-04-1900 The first book of postage stamps was issued. The two-cent stamps were available in books of 12, 24 and 48 stamps.
16-04-1905 Andrew Carnegie donated $10,000,000 of personal money to set up the Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching.
16-04-1912 Harriet Quimby became the first woman to fly across the English Channel.
16-04-1917 Vladimir Ilyich Lenin returned to Russia to start Bolshevik Revolution after years of exile.
16-04-1922 Annie Oakley shot 100 clay targets in a row, to set a women's record.
16-04-1922 The Soviet Union and Germany signed the Treaty of Rapallo under which Germany recognized the Soviet Union and diplomatic and trade relations were restored.
16-04-1935 "Fibber McGee and Molly" premiered.
16-04-1940 The first no-hit, no-run game to be thrown on an opening day of the major league baseball season was earned by Bob Feller. The Cleveland Indians beat the Chicago White Sox 1-0.
16-04-1942 The Island of Malta was awarded the George Cross in recognition for heroism under constant German air attack.
16-04-1944 The destroyer USS Laffey survived immense damage from attacks by 22 Japanese aircraft off Okinawa.
16-04-1945 American troops entered Nuremberg, Germany.
16-04-1947 The Zoomar lens, invented by Dr. Frank Back, was demonstrated in New York City. It was the first lens to exhibit zooming effects.
16-04-1947 In Texas City, TX, the French ship Grandcamp, carrying ammonium nitrate fertilizer, caught fire and blew up. The explosions and resulting fires killed 576 people.
16-04-1948 In Paris, the Organization for European Economic Co-operation was set up.
16-04-1951 75 people were killed when the British submarine Affray sank in the English Channel.
16-04-1953 The British royal yacht Britannia was launched.
16-04-1962 Walter Cronkite began anchoring "The CBS Evening News".
16-04-1968 The Pentagon announced that troops would begin coming home from Vietnam.
16-04-1968 Major league baseball’s longest night game was played. The 24 innings took six hours, six minutes to play.
16-04-1972 Apollo 16 blasted off on a voyage to the moon. It was the fifth manned moon landing.
16-04-1972 Two giants pandas arrived in the U.S. from China.
16-04-1975 The Khmer Rouge Rebels won control of Cambodia after a five years of civil war. They renamed the country Kampuchea and began a reign of terror.
16-04-1977 The ban on women attending West Point was lifted.
16-04-1978 In Orissa, India, 180 people died when a tornado hit.
16-04-1982 Queen Elizabeth proclaimed Canada's new constitution in effect. The act severed the last colonial links with Britain.
16-04-1985 Mickey Mantle was reinstated after being banned from baseball for several years.
16-04-1987 The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) sternly warned U.S. radio stations to watch the use of indecent language on the airwaves.
16-04-1987 The U.S. Patent Office began allowing the patenting of new animals created by genetic engineering.
16-04-1992 Italian financier Carlo de Benedetti and 32 others were convicted of fraud in connection with the 1982 collapse of Banco Ambrosiano.
16-04-1992 The House ethics committee listed 303 current and former lawmakers who had overdrawn their House bank accounts.
16-04-1995 The European Union and Canada agreed to protect threatened fish stocks in the north Atlantic.
16-04-1996 Britain's Prince Andrew and his wife, Sarah, the Duchess of York, announced that they were in the process of getting a divorce.
16-04-1996 An Italian court found former Prime Minister Bettino Craxi guilty on charges of corruption. He was sentenced to eight years and three months in prison.
16-04-1999 Wayne Gretzky announced his retirement from the National Hockey League (NHL).
16-04-2002 The U.S. Supreme Court overturned major parts of a 1996 child pornography law based on rights to free speech.
17-04-1492 Christopher Columbus signed a contract with Spain to find a passage to Asia and the Indies.
17-04-1521 Martin Luther was excommunicated from the Roman Catholic Church.
17-04-1524 New York Harbor was discovered by Giovanni Verrazano.
17-04-1535 Antonio Mendoza was appointed first viceroy of New Spain.
17-04-1629 Horses were first imported into the colonies by the American Massachusetts Bay Colony.
17-04-1704 John Campbell published what would eventually become the first successful American newspaper. It was known as the Boston "News-Letter."
17-04-1758 Frances Williams published a collection of Latin poems. He was the first African-American to graduate from a college in the western hemisphere.
17-04-1808 Bayonne Decree by Napoleon I of France ordered the seizure of U.S. ships.
17-04-1810 Pineapple cheese was patented by Lewis M. Norton.
17-04-1824 Russia abandoned all North American claims south of 54' 40'.
17-04-1860 New Yorkers learned of a new law that required fire escapes to be provided for tenement houses.
17-04-1861 Virginia became the eighth state to secede from the Union.
17-04-1864 U.S. Civil War General Grant banned the trading of prisoners.
17-04-1865 Mary Surratt was arrested as a conspirator in the Lincoln assassination.
17-04-1875 The game "snooker" was invented by Sir Neville Chamberlain.
17-04-1895 China and Japan signed the Treaty of Shimonoseki. It was the end of the first Sino-Japanese War. In the treaty China ceded Taiwan to Japan.
17-04-1916 The American Academy of Arts and Letters obtained a charter from the U.S. Congress.
17-04-1917 A bill in Congress to establish Daylight Saving Time was defeated. It was passed a couple of months later.
17-04-1935 "Lights Out" debuted on NBC Radio. It ran until 1952.
17-04-1941 Igor Sikorsky accomplished the first successful helicopter lift-off from water near Stratford, CT.
17-04-1941 The office of Price Administration was established in the U.S. to handle rationing.
17-04-1946 The last French troops left Syria.
17-04-1947 Jackie Robinson (Brooklyn Dodgers) performed a bunt for his first major league hit.
17-04-1961 About 1,400 U.S.-supported Cuban exiles invaded Cuba at the Bay of Pigs in an attempt to overthrow Fidel Castro. It was an unsuccessful attack.
17-04-1964 Jerrie Mock became first woman to fly an airplane solo around the world.
17-04-1964 The Ford Motor Company unveiled its new Mustang model.
17-04-1967 "The Joey Bishop Show" debuted on ABC-TV.
17-04-1967 The U.S. Supreme Court barred Muhammad Ali's request to be blocked from induction into the U.S. Army.
17-04-1969 In Los Angeles, Sirhan Sirhan was convicted of assassinating U.S. Senator Robert F. Kennedy.
17-04-1969 Czechoslovak Communist Party chairman Alexander Dubcek was deposed.
17-04-1970 Apollo 13 returned to Earth safely after an on-board accident with an oxygen tank.
17-04-1975 Khmer Rouge forces capture the capital of Cambodia, Phnom Penh. It was the end of the five-year war.
17-04-1983 In Warsaw, police routed 1,000 Solidarity supporters.
17-04-1984 In London, gunmen from the Libyan Embassy fired into an anti-Libya protest. One policewoman was killed and 10 others were wounded.
17-04-1985 The U.S. Postal Service unveiled its new 22-cent, "LOVE" stamp.
17-04-1985 In Lebanon, the cabinet resigned as Shiites took W. Beirut.
17-04-1987 In Sri Lanka, Tamil guerrillas killed 122 people in a road ambush.
17-04-1989 In Poland, courts gave Solidarity legal status.
17-04-1993 A federal jury in Los Angeles convicted two former police officers of violating the civil rights of beaten motorist Rodney King. Two other officers were acquitted.
17-04-1996 Erik and Lyle Menendez were sentenced to life in prison without parole for killing their parents.
17-04-1999 In India, the government of Prime Minister Atal Bihari Vajpayee collapsed after losing a vote of confidence.
17-04-2002 At the National Maritime Museum in London, the exhibit "Skin Deep - A History of Tattooing" opened.
18-04-1521 Martin Luther confronted the emperor Charles V in the Diet of Worms and refused to retract his views that led to his excommunication.
18-04-1676 Sudbury, Massachusetts, was attacked by Indians.
18-04-1775 American revolutionaries Paul Revere, William Dawes and Samuel Prescott rode though the towns of Massachusetts giving the warning that "the British are coming."
18-04-1791 National Guardsmen prevented Louis XVI and his family from leaving Paris.
18-04-1818 A regiment of Indians and blacks were defeated at the Battle of Suwann, in Florida, ending the first Seminole War.
18-04-1834 William Lamb became prime minister of England.
18-04-1838 The Wilkes' expedition to the South Pole set sail.
18-04-1846 The telegraph ticker was patented by R.E. House
18-04-1847 U.S. forces defeated the Mexicans at Cerro Gordo.
18-04-1853 The first train in Asia began running from Bombay to Tanna.
18-04-1861 Colonel Robert E. Lee turned down an offer to command the Union armies during the U.S. Civil War.
18-04-1877 Charles Cros wrote a paper that described the process of recording and reproducing sound. In France, Cros is regarded as the inventor of the phonograph. In the U.S., Thomas Edison gets the credit.
18-04-1895 New York State passed an act that established free public baths.
18-04-1906 San Francisco, CA, was hit with an earthquake. The orginal death toll was cited at about 700. Later information indicated that the death toll may have been 3 to 4 times the original estimate.
18-04-1910 Walter R. Brookins made the first airplane flight at night.
18-04-1923 Yankee Stadium opened in the Bronx, NY. The Yankees beat the Boston Red Sox 4-1. John Phillip Sousa's band played the National Anthem.
18-04-1924 Simon and Schuster, Inc. published the first "Crossword Puzzle Book."
18-04-1934 The first Laundromat opened in Fort Worth, TX.
18-04-1937 Leon Trotsky called for the overthrow of Soviet leader Josef Stalin.
18-04-1938 U.S. President Roosevelt threw out the first ball preceding the season opener between the Washington Senators and the Philadelphia Athletics.
18-04-1942 James H. Doolittle and his squadron, from the USS Hornet, raided Tokyo and other Japanese cities.
18-04-1942 The Vichy government capitulated to Adolf Hitler and invited Pierre Laval to form a new government in France.
18-04-1943 Traveling in a bomber, Japanese Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto, was shot down by American P-38 fighters.
