18-04-1945 American war correspondent Ernie Pyle was killed by Japanese gunfire on the Pacific island of Ie Shima, off Okinawa. He was 44 years old.
18-04-1946 The League of Nations was dissolved.
18-04-1949 The Republic of Ireland was established.
18-04-1950 The first transatlantic jet passenger trip was completed.
18-04-1954 Colonel Gamal Abdel Nasser seized power in Egypt.
18-04-1955 Albert Einstein died.
18-04-1956 Actress Grace Kelly and Prince Rainier of Monaco were married. The religious ceremony took place April 19.
18-04-1960 The Mutual Broadcasting System was sold to the 3M Company of Minnesota for $1.25 million.
18-04-1978 The U.S. Senate approved the transfer of the Panama Canal to Panama on December 31, 1999.
18-04-1979 The TV show "Real People" premiered.
18-04-1980 Rhodesia became in independent nation of Zimbabwe.
18-04-1983 The U.S. Embassy in Beirut was blown up by a suicide car-bomber. 63 people were killed including 17 Americans.
18-04-1984 Daredevils Mike MacCarthy and Amanda Tucker made a sky dive from the Eiffel Tower. The jump ended safely.
18-04-1985 Ted Turner filed for a hostile takeover of CBS.
18-04-1985 Tulane University abolished its 72-year-old basketball program. The reason was charges of fixed games, drug abuse, and payments to players.
18-04-1989 Thousands of Chinese students demanding democracy tried to storm Communist Party headquarters in Beijing.
18-04-1999 Wayne Gretzky (New York Rangers) played his final game in the NHL. He retired as the NHL's all-time leading scorer and holder of 61 individual records.
18-04-2000 The Nasdaq had the biggest one-day point gain in its history.
18-04-2000 Joan Lunden and Jeff Konigsberg were married.
18-04-2002 Actor Robert Blake and his bodyguard were arrested in connection with the shooting death of Blake's wife about a year before.
18-04-2002 The Amtrack Auto Train derailed in a remote area of north Florida. Four people were killed and 133 were injured.
18-04-2002 The city legislature of Berlin decided to make Marlene Dietrich an honorary citizen. Dietrich had gone to the United States in 1930. She refused to return to Germany after Adolf Hitler came to power.
19-04-1012 Aelfheah was murdered by Danes who had been ravaging the south of England. Aelfhear became the 29th Archbishop of Canterbury in 1005.
19-04-1539 Emperor Charles V reached a truce with German Protestants at Frankfurt, Germany.
19-04-1587 English admiral Sir Francis Drake entered Cadiz harbor and sank the Spanish fleet.
19-04-1689 Residents of Boston ousted their governor, Edmond Andros.
19-04-1713 Holy Roman Emperor Charles VI issued the Pragmatic Sanction, which gave women the rights of succession to Hapsburg possessions.
19-04-1764 The English Parliament banned the American colonies from printing paper money.
19-04-1770 Captain James Cook discovered New South Wales, Australia. Cook originally named the land Point Hicks.
19-04-1775 The American Revolution began as fighting broke out at Lexington, MA.
19-04-1782 The Netherlands recognized the new United States.
19-04-1794 Tadeusz Kosciuszko forced the Russians out of Warsaw.
19-04-1802 The Spanish reopened the New Orleans port to American merchants.
19-04-1839 The Kingdom of Belgium was recognized by all the states of Europe when the Treaty of London was signed.
19-04-1852 The California Historical Society was founded.
19-04-1861 Thaddeus S. C. Lowe sailed 900 miles in nine hours in a hot air balloon.
19-04-1861 The Baltimore riots resulted in four Union soldiers and nine civilians killed.
19-04-1861 U.S. President Lincoln ordered a blockade of Confederate ports.
19-04-1892 The Duryea gasoline buggy was introduced in the U.S. by Charles and Frank Duryea.
19-04-1897 The first annual Boston Marathon was held. It was the first of its type in the U.S.
19-04-1927 In China, Hankow communists declared war on Chaing Kai-shek.
19-04-1933 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt issued a proclamation that removed the U.S. went off of the gold standard.
19-04-1938 General Francisco Franco declared victory in the Spanish Civil War.
19-04-1939 Connecticut approved the Bill of Rights for the U.S. Constitution after 148 years.
19-04-1943 The Warsaw Ghetto uprising against Nazi rule began. The Jews were able to fight off the Germans for 28 days.
19-04-1951 General Douglas MacArthur gave his "Old Soldiers" speech before the U.S. Congress. In the address General MacArthur said that "Old soldiers never die, they just fade away."
19-04-1951 Shigeki Tanaka won the Boston Marathon. Tanaka had survived the atomic blast at Hiroshima, Japan during World War II.
19-04-1956 Actress Grace Kelly became Princess Grace of Monaco when she married Prince Rainier III of Monaco. The civil ceremony took place on April 18.
19-04-1958 The San Francisco Giants and the Los Angeles Dodgers played the first major league baseball game on the West Coast.
19-04-1960 Baseball uniforms began displaying player's names on their backs.
19-04-1967 Surveyor 3 landed on the moon and began sending photos back to the U.S.
19-04-1971 Russia launched the Salyut into orbit around Earth. It was the first space station.
19-04-1975 India launched its first satellite with aid from the USSR.
19-04-1977 Alex Haley received a special Pulitzer Prize for his book "Roots."
19-04-1982 NASA named Sally Ride to be first woman astronaut.
19-04-1987 In Phoenix, AZ, skydiver Gregory Robertson went into a 200-mph free-fall to save an unconscious colleague 3,500 feet from the ground.
19-04-1987 The last California condor known to be in the wild was captured and placed in a breeding program at the San Diego Wild Animal Park.
19-04-1989 A gun turret exploded aboard the USS Iowa. 47 sailors were killed.
19-04-1989 A giant asteroid passed within 500,000 miles of Earth.
19-04-1989 In El Salvador, Attorney General Alvadora was killed by a car bomb.
19-04-1993 The Branch-Davidian’s compound in Waco, TX, burned to the ground. It was the end of a 51-day standoff between the cult and U.S. federal agents. 86 people were killed including 17 children. Nine of the Branch Davidians escaped the fire.
19-04-1994 A Los Angeles jury awarded $3.8 million to Rodney King for violation of his civil rights.
19-04-1995 The Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City, OK, was destroyed by a bomb. It was the worst bombing on U.S. territory. 168 people were killed including 19 children, and 500 were injured. Timothy McVeigh was found guilty of the bombing on June 2, 1997.
19-04-1998 Wang Dan, a leader of 1989 Tienanmen Square pro democracy protests, was freed by the Chinese government.
19-04-2000 The Oklahoma City National Memorial was dedicated on the fifth anniversary of the bombing in Oklahoma that killed 168 people.
19-04-2000 Letters written by Greta Garbo were put on exhibit. The letters were made public ten years after Garbo's death.
19-04-2000 In the Philippines, Air Philippines GAP 541 crashed while preparing to land. 131 people were killed.
19-04-2002 The USS Cole was relaunched. In Yemen, 17 sailors were killed when the ship was attacked by terrorists on October 12, 2000. The attack was blamed on Osama bin Laden's al-Qaida network.
20-04-1139 The Second Lateran Council opened in Rome.
20-04-1534 Jacques Cartier, a French explorer, set sail from St. Malo to explore the North American coastline.
20-04-1653 In England, Oliver Cromwell expelled the Long Parliament for trying to pass the Perpetuation Bill that would have kept Parliament in the hands of only a few members.
20-04-1657 English Admiral Robert Blake fought his last battle when he destroyed the Spanish fleet in Santa Cruz Bay.
20-04-1689 The siege of Londonderry began. Supporters of James II attacked the city.
20-04-1769 Ottawa Chief Pontiac was murdered by an Illinois Indian in Cahokia.
20-04-1775 The British began the siege of Boston.
20-04-1792 France declared war on Austria, Prussia, and Sardinia. It was the start of the French Revolutionary wars.
20-04-1809 Napoleon defeated Austria at Battle of Abensberg, Bavaria.
20-04-1832 Hot Springs National Park was established by an act of the U.S. Congress. It was the first national park in the U.S.
20-04-1836 The U.S. territory of Wisconsin was created by the U.S. Congress.
20-04-1841 In Philadelphia, PA, Edgar Allen Poe's first detective story, "The Murders in the Rue Morgue," was published in Graham's Magazine.
20-04-1861 Robert E. Lee resigned from U.S. Army.
20-04-1865 Safety matches were first advertised.
20-04-1879 First mobile home (horse drawn) was used in a journey from London to Cyprus.
20-04-1902 Scientists Marie and Pierre Curie isolated the radioactive element radium.
20-04-1912 Fenway Park opened as the home of the Boston Red Sox.
20-04-1916 Sir Roger Casement landed in Ireland to incite rebellion against the British. Casement, a British diplomat, was captured within hours and was hanged for high treason on August 3.
20-04-1916 Wrigley Field opened in Chicago, IL.
20-04-1919 The Polish Army captured Vilno, Lithuania from the Soviets.
20-04-1934 The movie "Stand Up And Cheer" opened. It was Shirley Temple's debut.
20-04-1940 The First electron microscope was demonstrated by RCA.
20-04-1942 Pierre Laval, the premier of Vichy France, in a radio broadcast, establishes a policy of "true reconciliation with Germany."
20-04-1945 Soviet troops began their attack on Berlin.
20-04-1945 During World War II, Allied forces took control of the German cities of Nuremberg and Stuttgart.
20-04-1951 General MacArthur addressed the joint session of Congress after being relieved by U.S. President Truman.
20-04-1953 Operation Little Switch began in Korea. It was the exchange of sick and wounded prisoners of war. Thirty Americans were freed.
20-04-1953 The Boston marathon was won by Keizo Yamada with a record time of 2:18:51.
20-04-1959 "Desilu Playhouse" on CBS-TV presented a two-part show titled "The Untouchables."
20-04-1961 FM stereo broadcasting was approved by the FCC.
20-04-1962 The New Orleans Citizens' Council offered a free one-way ride for blacks to move to northern states.
20-04-1967 U.S. planes bombed Haiphong for first time during the Vietnam War.
20-04-1971 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld the use of busing to achieve racial desegregation in schools.
20-04-1972 The manned lunar module from Apollo 16 landed on the moon.
20-04-1977 Woody Allen's film "Annie Hall" premiered.
20-04-1978 The Korean Airliner 007 was shot down while in Russian airspace.
20-04-1984 In Washington, terrorists bombed an officers club at a Navy yard.
20-04-1984 Britain announced that its administration of Hong Kong would cease in 1997.
20-04-1985 In Madrid, Santiago Carillo was purged from the Communist Party. Carillo was a founder of Eurocommunism.
20-04-1987 In Argentina, President Raul Alfonsin quelled a military revolt.
20-04-1988 The U.S. Air Forces' Stealth (B-2 bomber) was officially unveiled.
20-04-1989 Scientist announced the successful testing of high-definition TV.
20-04-1991 Mikhail Gorbachev became the first Soviet head of state to visit South Korea.
20-04-1992 The worlds largest fair, Expo '92, opened in Seville, Spain.
20-04-1998 Kenyan runner Moses Tanui, 32, won the Boston Marathon for the second time. He also registered the third fast time with 2 hours 7 minutes and 34 seconds.
20-04-1999 13 people were killed at Columbine High School in Littleton, CO, when two teenagers opened fire on them with shotguns and pipebombs. The two gunmen then killed themselves.
20-04-1999 Jane Seymour received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
21-04-1526 Mongol Emperor Babur annihilated the Indian Army of Ibrahim Lodi.
21-04-1649 The Maryland Toleration Act was passed, allowing all freedom of worship.
21-04-1689 William III and Mary II were crowned joint king and queen of England, Scotland and Ireland.
21-04-1789 John Adams was sworn in as the first U.S. Vice President.
21-04-1836 General Sam Houston defeated Santa Anna at the Battle of San Jacinto. This battle decided the independence of Texas.
21-04-1856 The Mississippi River was crossed by a rail train for the first time (between Davenport, IA, and Rock Island, IL).
21-04-1862 The U.S. Congress established the U.S. Mint in Denver, CO.
21-04-1865 U.S. President Abraham Lincoln's funeral train left Washington.
21-04-1892 The first Buffalo was born in Golden Gate Park.
21-04-1898 The Spanish-American War began.
21-04-1914 U.S. Marines occupied Vera Cruz, Mexico.
21-04-1916 Bill Carlisle, the infamous ‘last train robber,’ robbed a train in Hanna, WY.
21-04-1918 German fighter ace Baron von Richthofen, "The Red Baron," was shot down and killed during World War I.
21-04-1940 "Take It or Leave It" premiered on CBS Radio.
21-04-1943 U.S. President Roosevelt announced that several Doolittle pilots had been executed by the Japanese.
21-04-1953 In New York, the Sidney Janis Gallery held the Dada exhibition.
21-04-1956 Leonard Ross, age 10, became the youngest prizewinner on the "The Big Surprise". He won $100,000.
21-04-1959 The largest fish ever hooked by a rod and reel was caught by Alf Dean. It was a 16-foot, 10-inch white shark that weighed 2,664 pounds.
21-04-1960 Brasilia became the capital of Brazil.
21-04-1961 The French army revolted in Algeria.
21-04-1967 Svetlana Alliluyeva (Svetlana Stalina) defected in New York City. She was the daughter of Joseph Stalin.
21-04-1967 In Athens, Army colonels took over the government and installed Constantine Kollias as premier.
21-04-1972 Apollo 16 astronauts John Young and Charles Duke explored the surface of the moon.
21-04-1975 South Vietnam president, Nguyen Van Thieu, resigned, condemning the United States.
21-04-1977 "Annie" opened on Broadway.
21-04-1984 In France, it was announced that doctors had found virus believed to cause AIDS.
21-04-1985 Manuel Ortega proposed a cease-fire for Nicaragua.
21-04-1986 Geraldo Rivera opened a vault that belonged to Al Capone at the Lexington Hotel in Chicago. Nothing of interest was found inside.
21-04-1987 Special occasion stamps were offered for the first time by the U.S. Postal Service. "Happy Birthday" and "Get Well" were among the first to be offered.
21-04-1992 Robert Alton Harris became the first person executed by the state of California in 25 years. He was put to death for the 1978 murder of two teen-age boys.
21-04-1994 Jackie Parker became the first woman to qualify to fly an F-16 combat plane.
21-04-1998 Astronomers announced in Washington that they had discovered possible signs of a new family of planets orbiting a star 220 light-years away.
21-04-2000 In Sinking Spring, PA, a man chased his estranged girlfriend through town and then forced her car into the path of an oncoming train. The woman and her 3 passengers were killed.
21-04-2000 North Carolina researchers announced that the heart of a 66 million-year-old dinosaur was more like a mammal or bird than that of a reptile.
21-04-2000 The 1998 Children's Online Privacy Protection Act went into effect.
21-04-2002 In the city of General Santos, 14 people were killed and 69 were injured in a bomb attack on a department store. The attack was blamed on Muslim extremists.
21-04-2003 North and South Korea agreed to hold Cabinet-level talks the following week.
22-04-1500 Portuguese navigator Pedro Alvares Cabral discovered Brazil.
22-04-1509 Henry VIII ascended to the throne of England upon the death of his father Henry VII.
22-04-1529 Spain and Portugal divided the eastern hemisphere in Treaty of Saragosa.
22-04-1745 The Peace of Fussen was signed, restoring the status quo of Germany.
22-04-1792 U.S. President George Washington proclaimed American neutrality in the war in Europe.
22-04-1861 Robert E. Lee was named commander of Virginia forces.
22-04-1864 The U.S. Congress mandated that all coins minted as U.S. currency bear the inscription "In God We Trust".
22-04-1876 Eight baseball teams began the inaugural season of the National League.
22-04-1889 At noon, the Oklahoma land rush officially started as thousands of Americans race for new, unclaimed land.
22-04-1898 The first shot of the Spanish-American war occurred when the USS Nashville captured a Spanish merchant ship.
22-04-1914 Babe Ruth made his pitching debut with the Baltimore Orioles.
22-04-1915 At the Second Battle Ypres the Germans became the first country to use poison gas.
22-04-1915 The New York Yankees wore pinstripes and the hat-in-the-ring logo for the first time.
22-04-1918 British naval forces attempted to sink block-ships in the German U-boat bases at the Battle of Zeeburgge.
22-04-1930 The U.S., Britain and Japan signed the London Naval Treaty, which regulated submarine warfare and limited shipbuilding.
22-04-1931 Egypt signed the treaty of friendship with Iraq.
22-04-1931 James G. Ray landed an autogyro on the lawn of the White House.
22-04-1944 During World War II, the Allies launched a major attack against the Japanese in Hollandia, New Guinea.
22-04-1952 An atomic test conducted in Nevada was the first nuclear explosion shown on live network television.
22-04-1954 The U.S. Senate Army-McCarthy televised hearings began.
22-04-1967 Randy Matson set a new world record with a shot put toss of 71 feet, and 5 1/2 inches in College Station, TX.
22-04-1970 The first "Earth Day" was observed by millions of Americans.
22-04-1976 Barbara Walters became first female nightly network news anchor.
22-04-1987 The American Physical Society said that the "Star Wars" missile system was "highly questionable" and would take ten years to research.
22-04-1993 The U.S. Holocaust Memorial Museum was dedicated in Washington, DC.
22-04-1997 In Lima, Peru government commandos storm and capture the residence of the Japanese ambassador ending a 126-day hostage crisis. In the rescue 71 hostages were saved. Those killed: one hostage (of a heart attack), two soldiers, and all 14 rebels.
22-04-1997 93 people are killed in the insurgency of extremist Muslims that continued in Algeria in a town south of Algiers.
22-04-1999 The Watson Family received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
22-04-2000 Elian Gonzalez was reunited with his father. He had to be taken from his Miami relatives by U.S. agents in a predawn raid.
22-04-2000 ABC-TV aired a small portion of the Clinton-DiCaprio interview.
22-04-2002 Philippino President Gloria Macapagal Arroyo ordered a state of emergency in the city of General Santos in response to a series of bombing attacks the day before. The attacks were blamed on Muslim extremists.
22-04-2005 Zacarias Moussaoui plead guilty to conspiring with hijackers in the September 11, 2001, plot to attack American buildings and citizens.
23-04-0303 Perseus was arrested, tortured, and put to death.
23-04-1348 The first English order of knighthood was founded. It was the Order of the Garter.
23-04-1500 Pedro Cabal claimed Brazil for Portugal.
23-04-1521 The Comuneros were crushed by royalist troops in Spain.
23-04-1759 The British seized Basse-Terre and Guadeloupe in the Antilies from France.
23-04-1789 U.S. President George Washington moved into Franklin House, New York. It was the first executive mansion.
23-04-1789 "Courier De Boston" was published for the first time. It was the first Roman Catholic magazine in the U.S.
23-04-1826 Missolonghi fell to Egyptian forces.
23-04-1861 Arkansas troops seized Fort Smith.
23-04-1872 Charlotte E. Ray became the first black woman lawyer.
23-04-1895 Russia, France, and Germany forced Japan to return the Liaodong peninsula to China.
23-04-1896 The Vitascope system for projecting movies onto a screen was demonstrated in New York City.
23-04-1900 The word "hillbilly" was first used in print in an article in the "New York Journal." It was spelled "Hill-Billie".
23-04-1908 U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt signed an act creating the U.S. Army Reserve.
23-04-1915 The A.C.A. became the National Advisory Council on Aeronautics (NACA).
23-04-1920 The Turkish Grand National Assembly had its first meeting in Ankara.
23-04-1921 Charles Paddock set a record time in the 300-meter track event when he posted a time of 33.2 seconds.
23-04-1924 The U.S. Senate passed the Soldiers Bonus Bill.
23-04-1940 About 200 people died in a dance-hall fire in Natchez, MS.
23-04-1945 The Soviet Army fought its way into Berlin.
23-04-1948 Johnny Longden became the first race jockey to ride 3,000 career winners.
23-04-1950 Chaing evacuated Hainan, leaving mainland China to Mao and the communists.
23-04-1951 The Associated Press began use of the new service of teletype setting.
23-04-1954 Hank Aaron of the Milwaukee Braves hit his first major-league home run on this day.
23-04-1964 Ken Johnson of the Houston Astros threw the first no-hitter for a loss. The game was lost 1-0 to the Cincinnati Reds due to two errors.
23-04-1967 The Soyuz 1 was launched by Russia.
23-04-1968 The Methodist Church and the Evangelical United Brethren Church merged to form the United Methodist Church.
23-04-1969 Sirhan Sirhan was sentenced to death for killing U.S. Senator Robert Kennedy. The sentence was later reduced to life in prison.
23-04-1971 The Soyuz 10 was launched.
23-04-1985 The Coca-Cola Company announced that it was changing its 99-year-old secret formula. New Coke was not successful, which resulted in the resumption of selling the original version.
23-04-1985 The U.S. House rejected $14 million in aid to Nicaragua.
23-04-1987 An apartment complex being built in Bridgeport, Connecticut collapsed. 28 construction workers were killed.
23-04-1988 A U.S. federal law took effect that banned smoking on flights that were under two hours.
23-04-1988 Kanellos Kanelopoulos set three world records for human-powered flight when he stayed in the air for 74 miles and four hours in his pedal-powered "Daedalus".
23-04-1989 It was reported that 277 had been killed in the most recent rebel attack in Afghanistan.
23-04-1989 Kareem Abdul-Jabbar played his last regular season game in the NBA.
23-04-1996 A New York civil-court jury ordered Bernhard Goetz to pay $43 million to Darrell Cabey. Cabey was paralyzed when he was shot in subway car in 1984.
23-04-1996 An auction of the late Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis' possessions began at Sotheby's in New York City.
23-04-1997 An infertility doctor in California announced that a 63-year-old woman had given birth in late 1996. The child was from a donor egg. The woman is the oldest known woman to give birth.
23-04-1998 James Earl Ray died, at age 70, while serving a life sentence for the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. Ray had confessed to the crime and then later insisted he had been framed.
23-04-1999 In Washington, DC, the heads of state and government of the 19 NATO nations celebrated the organization's 50th anniversary.
23-04-2003 U.S. President Bush signed legislation that authorized the design change of the 5-cent coin (nickel) for release in 2004. It was the first change to the coin in 65 years. The change, to commemorate the 200th anniversary of the Louisiana Purchase, was planned to run for only two years before returning to the previous design.
24-04-1519 Envoys of Montezuma II attended the first Easter mass in Central America.
24-04-1547 Charles V's troops defeated the Protestant League of Schmalkalden at the battle of Muhlburg.
24-04-1558 Mary, Queen of Scotland, married the French dauphin, Francis.
24-04-1800 The Library of Congress was established with a $5,000 allocation.
24-04-1805 The U.S. Marines attacked and captured the town of Derna in Tripoli.
24-04-1833 A patent was granted for first soda fountain.
24-04-1877 Russia declared war on the Ottoman Empire.
24-04-1877 In the U.S., federal troops were ordered out of New Orleans. This was the end to the North's post-Civil War rule in the South.
24-04-1884 Otto von Bismarck cables Cape Town that South Africa was now a German colony.
24-04-1889 The Edison General Electric Company was organized.
24-04-1897 William Price became the first to be named White House news reporter.
24-04-1898 Spain declared war on the U.S., rejecting America's ultimatum for Spain to withdraw from Cuba.
24-04-1915 During World War I, the Ottoman Turkish Empire began the mass deportation of Armenians.
24-04-1916 Irish nationalist launched the Easter Rebellion against British occupation forces. They were overtaken several days later.
24-04-1944 The first B-29 arrived in China, over the Hump of the Himalayas.
24-04-1948 The Berlin airlift began to relieve the surrounded city.
24-04-1952 Raymond Burr made his TV acting debut on the "Gruen Guild Playhouse" in an episode titled, "The Tiger."
24-04-1953 Winston Churchill was knighted by Queen Elizabeth II.
24-04-1955 "X-Minus One," a science fiction show, was first heard for the first time on NBC radio.
24-04-1961 Sandy Koufax of the Los Angeles Dodgers struck out 18 batters becoming the first major-league pitcher to do so on two different occasions.
24-04-1961 U.S. President Kennedy accepted "sole responsibility" following Bay of Pigs invasion of Cuba.
24-04-1962 MIT sent a TV signal by satellite for the first time.
24-04-1967 Soviet astronaut Vladimir Komarov died when his craft crashed with a tangled parachute.
24-04-1967 The newest Greek regime banned miniskirts.
24-04-1968 Leftist students took over several campus buildings at Columbia University.
24-04-1970 The People's Republic of China launched its first satellite.
24-04-1973 Albert Sabin reported that herpesviruses were factors in nine kinds of cancer.
24-04-1974 David Bowie released "Diamond Dogs."
24-04-1981 The IBM Personal Computer was introduced.
24-04-1987 In Palm Bay, FL, a gunman opened fire in a mall. He killed six and wounded 10.
24-04-1989 Thousands of students began striking in Beijing.
24-04-1990 The space shuttle Discovery blasted off from Cape Canaveral, FL. It was carrying the $1.5 billion Hubble Space Telescope.
24-04-1990 Michael Milken plead guilty to six felonies and agreed to pay a $600 million penalty. He was later sentenced to ten years in prison. Milken had sold junk-bond in the 1980s.
24-04-1994 Madonna visited the San Antonio Spurs locker room to congratulate David Robinson on his 71-point performance.
24-04-1997 The U.S. Senate ratified the Chemical Weapons Convention. The global treaty banned the development, production, storage and use of chemical weapons.
24-04-1998 ABC confirmed that it was canceling the TV series "Ellen." The show was the first series to feature an openly gay lead character.
24-04-2000 ABC-TV aired the TV movie "The Three Stooges."
24-04-2003 A U.S. official reported the North Korea had claimed to have nuclear weapons.
25-04-1590 The Sultan of Morocco launched his successful attack to capture Timbuktu.
25-04-1644 The Ming Chongzhen emperor committed suicide by hanging himself.
25-04-1684 A patent was granted for the thimble.
25-04-1707 At the Battle of Almansa, Franco-Spanish forces defeated the Anglo-Portugese.
25-04-1792 The guillotine was first used to execute highwayman Nicolas J. Pelletier.
25-04-1831 The New York and Harlem Railway was incorporated in New York City.
25-04-1846 The Mexican-American War ignited as a result of disputes over claims to Texas boundaries. The outcome of the war fixed Texas' southern boundary at the Rio Grande River.
25-04-1859 Work began on the Suez Canal in Egypt.
25-04-1860 The first Japanese diplomats to visit a foreign power reached Washington, DC. They remained in the U.S. capital for several weeks while discussing expansion of trade with the United States.
25-04-1862 Union Admiral Farragut occupied New Orleans, LA.
25-04-1864 After facing defeat in the Red River Campaign, Union General Nathaniel Bank returned to Alexandria, LA.
25-04-1867 Tokyo was opened for foreign trade.
25-04-1882 French commander Henri Riviere seized the citadel of Hanoi in Indochina.
25-04-1898 The U.S. declared war on Spain. Spain had declared war on the U.S. the day before.
25-04-1901 New York became the first state to require license plates for cars. The fee was $1.
25-04-1915 During World War I, Australian and New Zealand troops landed at Gallipoli in Turkey in hopes of attacking the Central Powers from below. The attack was unsuccessful.
25-04-1925 General Paul von Hindenburg took office as president of Germany.
25-04-1926 In Iran, Reza Kahn was crowned Shah and choose the name "Pehlevi."
25-04-1928 A seeing eye dog was used for the first time.
25-04-1938 "Your Family and Mine," a radio serial, was first broadcast.
25-04-1940 W2XBS (now WCBS-TV) in New York City presented the first circus on TV.
25-04-1945 U.S. and Soviet forces met at Torgau, Germany on Elbe River.
25-04-1945 Delegates from about 50 countries met in San Francisco to organize the United Nations.
25-04-1952 After a three-day fight against Chinese Communist Forces, the Gloucestershire Regiment was annihilated on "Gloucester Hill," in Korea.
25-04-1953 U.S. Senator Wayne Morse ended the longest speech in U.S. Senate history. The speech on the Offshore Oil Bill lasted 22 hours and 26 minutes.
25-04-1953 Dr. James D. Watson and Dr. Francis H.C. Crick suggested the double helix structure of DNA.
25-04-1954 The prototype manufacture of the first solar battery was announced by the Bell Laboratories in New York City.
25-04-1957 Operations began at the first experimental sodium nuclear reactor.
25-04-1959 St. Lawrence Seaway opened to shipping. The water way connects the Great Lakes and the Atlantic Ocean.
25-04-1961 Robert Noyce was granted a patent for the integrated circuit.
25-04-1962 The U.S. spacecraft, Ranger, crashed on the Moon.
25-04-1967 Colorado Governor John Love signed the first law legalizing abortion in the U.S. The law was limited to therapeutic abortions when agreed to, unanimously, by a panel of three physicians.
25-04-1971 The country of Bangladesh was established.
25-04-1974 Portuguese dictator Antonio Salazar was overthrown in a military coup.
25-04-1980 In Iran, a commando mission to rescue hostages was aborted after mechanical problems disabled three of the eight helicopters involved. During the evacuation, a helicopter and a transport plan collided and exploded. Eight U.S. servicemen were killed. The mission was aimed at freeing American hostages that had been taken at the U.S. embassy in Tehran on November 4, 1979. The event took place April 24th Washington, DC, time.
25-04-1982 In accordance with Camp David agreements, Israel completed its Sinai withdrawal.
25-04-1983 Soviet leader Yuri V. Andropov invited Samantha Smith to visit his country after receiving a letter in which the U.S. schoolgirl expressed fears about nuclear war.
25-04-1983 The Pioneer 10 spacecraft crossed Pluto's orbit, speeding on its endless voyage through the Milky Way.
25-04-1984 In France, over one million people demonstrated to show they favored the decentralization of education.
25-04-1984 David Anthony Kennedy, the son of Robert F. Kennedy, was found dead of a drug overdose in a hotel room.
25-04-1985 "Big River (The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn)" opened at the Eugene O’Neill Theatre on Broadway in New York City.
25-04-1987 In Washington, DC, 100,000 people protested the U.S. policy in Central America.
25-04-1987 Peter O'Toole opened in "Pygmalion" on Broadway.
25-04-1988 In Israel, John "Ivan the Terrible" Demjanuk was sentenced to death as a Nazi war criminal.
25-04-1990 Sandinista rule ended in Nicaragua.
25-04-1990 The U.S. Hubble Space Telescope was placed into Earth's orbit. It was released by the space shuttle Discovery.
25-04-1992 Islamic forces in Afghanistan took control of most of the capital of Kabul following the collapse of the Communist government.
25-04-1996 The main assembly of the Palestine Liberation Organization voted to revoke clauses in its charter that called for an armed struggle to destroy Israel.
25-04-1998 U.S. first lady Hillary Rodham Clinton on was questioned by Whitewater prosecutors on videotape about her work as a private lawyer for the failed savings and loan at the center of the investigation.
25-04-2003 Winnie Madikizela-Mandela, the anti-apartheid leader and ex-wife of former President Nelson Mandela, was sentenced to four years in prison for her conviction on fraud and theft charges. She was convicted of 43 counts of fraud and 25 of theft of money from a women's political league.
26-04-1478 Pazzi conspirators attacked Lorenzo and kill Giuliano de'Medici.
26-04-1514 Copernicus made his first observations of Saturn.
26-04-1607 The British established an American colony at Cape Henry, Virginia. It was the first permanent English establishment in the Western Hemisphere.
26-04-1819 The first Odd Fellows lodge in the U.S. was established in Baltimore, MD.
26-04-1865 Joseph E. Johnston surrendered the Army of Tennessee to Sherman during the American Civil War.
26-04-1865 John Wilkes Booth was killed by the U.S. Federal Cavalry.
26-04-1906 In Hawaii, motion pictures were shown for the first time.
26-04-1921 Weather broadcasts were heard for the first time on radio in St. Louis, MO.
26-04-1929 First non-stop flight from England to India was completed.
26-04-1931 New York Yankee Lou Gehrig hit a home run but was called out for passing a runner.
26-04-1931 NBC premiered "Lum and Abner." It was on the air for 24 years.
26-04-1937 German planes attacked Guernica, Spain, during the Spanish Civil War.
26-04-1937 "LIFE" magazine was printed without the word "LIFE" on the cover.
26-04-1937 "Lorenzo Jones" premiered on NBC radio.
26-04-1941 An organ was played at a baseball stadium for the first time in Chicago, IL.
26-04-1945 Marshal Henri Philippe Petain, the head of France's Vichy government during World War II, was arrested.
26-04-1952 Patty Berg set a new record for major women’s golf competition when she shot a 64 over 18 holes in a tournament in Richmond, CA.
26-04-1954 Grace Kelly was on the cover of "LIFE" magazine.
26-04-1964 The African nations of Tanganyika and Zanzibar merged to form Tanzania.
26-04-1964 The Boston Celtics won their sixth consecutive NBA title. They won two more before the streak came to an end.
26-04-1968 Students seized the administration building at Ohio State University.
26-04-1982 Argentina surrendered to Britain over Falkland Island crisis.
26-04-1983 Dow Jones Industrial Average broke 1,200 for first time.
26-04-1985 In Argentina, a fire at a mental hospital killed 79 people and injured 247.
26-04-1986 The world’s worst nuclear disaster to date occurred at Chernobyl, in the Ukraine. 31 died in the incident and thousands more were exposed to radioactive material.
26-04-1998 Auxiliary Bishop Juan Gerardi Conedera was bludgeoned to death two days after a report he'd compiled on atrocities during Guatemala's 36-year civil war was made public.
26-04-2000 Charles Wang and Sanjay Kumar purchased the NHL's New York Islanders.
26-04-2002 In Erfurt, Germany, an expelled student killed 17 people at his former school. The student then killed himself.
27-04-1296 The Scots were defeated by Edward I at the Battle of Dunbar.
27-04-1509 Pope Julius II excommunicated the Italian state of Venice.
27-04-1521 Portuguese explorer Ferdinand Magellan was killed by natives in the Philippines.
27-04-1565 The first Spanish settlement in Philippines was established in Cebu City.
27-04-1746 King George II won the battle of Culloden.
27-04-1805 A force led by U.S. Marines captured the city of Derna, on the shores of Tripoli.
27-04-1813 Americans under Gen. Pike capture York (present day Toronto) the seat of government in Ontario.
27-04-1861 U.S. President Lincoln suspended the writ of habeas corpus.
27-04-1861 West Virginia seceded from Virginia after Virginia seceded from the Union during the American Civil War.
27-04-1863 The Army of the Potomac began marching on Chancellorsville.
27-04-1865 In the U.S. the Sultana exploded while carrying 2,300 Union POWs. Between 1,400 - 2,000 were killed.
27-04-1880 Francis Clarke and M.G. Foster patented the electrical hearing aid.
27-04-1897 Grant's Tomb was dedicated.
27-04-1899 The Western Golf Association was founded in Chicago, IL.
27-04-1903 Jamaica Race Track opened in Long Island, NY.
27-04-1909 The sultan of Turkey, Abdul Hamid II, was overthrown.
27-04-1937 German bombers devastated Guernica, Spain.
27-04-1938 Geraldine Apponyi married King Zog of Albania. She was the first American woman to become a queen.
27-04-1938 A colored baseball was used for the first time in any baseball game. The ball was yellow and was used between Columbia and Fordham Universities in New York City.
27-04-1945 The Second Republic was founded in Austria.
27-04-1946 The SS African Star was placed in service. It was the first commercial ship to be equipped with radar.
27-04-1947 "Babe Ruth Day" was celebrated at Yankee Stadium.
27-04-1950 South Africa passed the Group Areas Act, which formally segregated races.
27-04-1953 The U.S. offered $50,000 and political asylum to any Communist pilot that delivered a MIG jet.
27-04-1953 Five people were killed and 60 injured when Mt. Aso erupted on the island of Kyushu.
27-04-1960 The submarine Tullibee was launched from Groton, CT. It was the first sub to be equipped with closed-circuit television.
27-04-1961 The United Kingdom granted Sierra Leone independence.
27-04-1965 "Pampers" were patented by R.C. Duncan.
27-04-1967 In Montreal, Prime Minister Lester Pearson lighted a flame to open Expo 67.
27-04-1975 Saigon was encircled by North Vietnamese troops.
27-04-1978 Pro-Soviet Marxists seized control of Afghanistan.
27-04-1982 The trial of John W. Hinckley Jr. began in Washington. Hinckley was later acquitted by reason of insanity for the shooting of U.S. President Reagan and three others.
27-04-1983 Nolan Ryan (Houston Astros) broke a 55-year-old major league baseball record when he struck out his 3,509th batter of his career.
27-04-1984 In London, Libyan gunmen left the Libyan Embassy 11 days after killing a policewoman and wounding 10 others.
27-04-1986 Captain Midnight (John R. MacDougall) interrupted HBO.
27-04-1989 Student protestors took over Tiananmen Square in Beijing.
27-04-1987 The U.S. Justice Department barred Austrian President Kurt Waldheim from entering the U.S. He claimed that he had aided in the deportation and execution of thousands of Jews and others as a German Army officer during World War II.
27-04-1992 The Federal Republic of Yugoslavia was proclaimed in Belgrade by the Republic of Serbia and its ally Montenegro.
27-04-1992 Russia and 12 other former Soviet republics won entry into the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank.
27-04-2005 The A380, the world's largest jetliner, completed its maiden flight. The passenger capability was 840.
27-04-2005 Russian President Vladimir Putin became the first Kremlin leader to visit Israel.
27-04-2006 In New York, NY, construction began on the 1,776-foot Freedom Tower on the site of former World Trade Center.
28-04-0357 Constantius II visited Rome for the first time.
28-04-1282 Villagers in Palermo led a revolt against French rule in Sicily.
28-04-1635 Virginia Governor John Harvey was accused of treason and removed from office.
28-04-1686 The first volume of Isaac Newton's "Principia Mathamatic" was published.
28-04-1788 Maryland became the seventh state to ratify the U.S. constitution.
28-04-1789 A mutiny on the British ship Bounty took place when a rebel crew took the ship and set sail to Pitcairn Island. The mutineers left Captain W. Bligh and 18 sailors adrift.
28-04-1818 U.S. President James Monroe proclaimed naval disarmament on the Great Lakes and Lake Champlain.
28-04-1896 The Addressograph was patented by J.S. Duncan.
28-04-1902 A revolution broke out in the Dominican Republic.
28-04-1910 First night air flight was performed by Claude Grahame-White in England.
28-04-1914 W.H. Carrier patented the design of his air conditioner.
28-04-1916 The British declared martial law throughout Ireland.
28-04-1919 The League of Nations was founded.
28-04-1920 Azerbaijan joined the USSR.
28-04-1930 The first organized night baseball game was played in Independence, Kansas.
28-04-1932 The yellow fever vaccine for humans was announced.
28-04-1937 The first animated-cartoon electric sign was displayed on a building on Broadway in New York City. It was created by Douglas Leight.
28-04-1945 Benito Mussolini and his mistress Clara Petacci were executed by Italian partisans as they attempted to flee the country.
28-04-1946 The Allies indicted Tojo with 55 counts of war crimes.
28-04-1947 Norwegian anthropologist Thor Heyerdahl and five others set out in a balsa wood craft known as Kon Tiki to prove that Peruvian Indians could have settled in Polynesia. The trip began in Peru and took 101 days to complete the crossing of the Pacific Ocean.
28-04-1952 The U.S. occupation of Japan officially ended when a treaty with the U.S. and 47 other countries went into effect.
28-04-1953 French troops evacuated northern Laos.
28-04-1957 Mike Wallace was seen on TV for the first time. He was the host of "Mike Wallace Interviews."
28-04-1959 Arthur Godfrey was seen for the last time in the final broadcast of "Arthur Godfrey and His Friends" on CBS-TV.
28-04-1965 The U.S. Army and Marines invaded the Dominican Republic to evacuate Americans.
28-04-1967 Muhammad Ali refused induction into the U.S. Army and was stripped of boxing title. He sited religious grounds for his refusal.
28-04-1969 Charles de Gaulle resigned as president of France.
28-04-1969 In Santa Rosa, CA, Charles M. Schulz's Redwood Empire Ice Arena opened.
28-04-1974 The last Americans were evacuated from Saigon.
28-04-1977 Christopher Boyce was convicted of selling U.S. secrets.
28-04-1985 The largest sand castle in the world was completed near St. Petersburg, FL. It was four stories tall.
28-04-1988 In Maui, HI, one flight attendant was killed when the fuselage of a Boeing 737 ripped open in mid-flight.
28-04-1989 Mobil announced that they were divesting from South Africa because congressional restrictions were too costly.
28-04-1992 The U.S. Agriculture Department unveiled a pyramid-shaped recommended-diet chart.
28-04-1994 Former CIA official Aldrich Ames, who had given U.S. secrets to the Soviet Union and then Russia, plead guilty to espionage and tax evasion. He was sentenced to life in prison without parole.
28-04-1996 U.S. President Clinton gave a 4 1/2 hour videotaped testimony as a defense witness in the criminal trial of his former Whitewater business partners.
28-04-1997 A worldwide treaty to ban chemical weapons took effect. Russia and other countries such as Iraq and North Korea did not sign.
28-04-1999 The U.S. House of Representatives rejected (on a tie vote of 213-213) a measure expressing supprot for NATO's five-week-old air campaign in Yugoslavia. The House also voted to limit the president's authority to use ground forces in Yugoslavia.
28-04-2000 Jay Leno received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
28-04-2001 A Russian rocket launched from Central Asia with the first space tourist aboard. The crew consisted of California businessman Dennis Tito and two cosmonauts. The destination was the international space station.
29-04-1289 Qala'un, the Sultan of Egypt, captured Tripoli.
29-04-1429 Joan of Arc lead Orleans, France, to victory over Britain.
29-04-1661 The Chinese Ming dynasty occupied Taiwan.
29-04-1672 King Louis XIV of France invaded the Netherlands.
29-04-1813 Rubber was patented by J.F. Hummel.
29-04-1852 The first edition of Peter Roget's Thesaurus was published.
29-04-1856 A peace treaty was signed between England and Russia.
29-04-1858 Austrian troops invaded Piedmont.
29-04-1861 The Maryland House of Delegates voted against seceding from Union.
29-04-1862 New Orleans fell to Union forces during the Civil War.
29-04-1864 Theta Xi was founded in Troy, New York.
29-04-1879 In Cleveland, OH, electric arc lights were used for the first time.
29-04-1913 Gideon Sundback patented an all-purpose zipper.
29-04-1916 Irish nationalists surrendered to British authorities in Dublin.
29-04-1918 Germany's Western Front offensive ended in World War I.
29-04-1924 An open revolt broke out in Santa Clara, Cuba.
29-04-1927 Construction of the Spirit of St. Louis was completed for Lindbergh.
29-04-1941 The Boston Bees agreed to change their name to the Braves.
29-04-1945 The German Army in Italy surrendered unconditionally to the Allies.
29-04-1945 In a bunker in Berlin, Adolf Hitler and Eva Braun were married. Hitler designated Admiral Karl Doenitz his successor.
29-04-1945 The Nazi death camp, Dachau, was liberated.
29-04-1946 Twenty-eight former Japanese leaders were indicted in Tokyo as war criminals.
29-04-1952 IBM President Thomas J. Watson, Jr., informed his company's stockholders that IBM was building "the most advanced, most flexible high-speed computer in the world." The computer was unveiled April 7, 1953, as the IBM 701 Electronic Data Processing Machine.
29-04-1954 Ernest Borgnine made his network television debut in "Night Visitor" on NBC-TV.
29-04-1961 ABC’s "Wide World of Sports" premiered.
29-04-1974 Phil Donahue’s TV show, "Donahue" moved to Chicago, IL.
29-04-1974 U.S. President Nixon announced he was releasing edited transcripts of secretly made White House tape recordings related to the Watergate scandal.
29-04-1975 The U.S. embassy in Vietnam was evacuated as North Vietnamese forces fought their way into Saigon.
29-04-1981 Steve Carlton, of the Philadelphia Phillies, became the first left-handed pitcher in the major leagues to get 3,000 career strikeouts.
29-04-1984 In California, the Diablo Canyon nuclear reactor went online after a long delay due to protests.
29-04-1985 Billy Martin was brought back, for the fourth time, to the position of manager for the New York Yankees.
29-04-1986 Roger Clemens of the Boston Red Sox set a major-league baseball record by striking out 20 Seattle Mariner batters.
29-04-1988 The Baltimore Orioles set a new major league baseball record by losing their first 21 games of the season.
29-04-1988 Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev promised more religious freedom.
29-04-1990 The destruction of the Berlin Wall began.
29-04-1992 Exxon executive Sidney Reso was kidnapped outside his Morris Township, NJ, home by Arthur Seale. Seale was a former Exxon security official. Reso died while in captivity.
29-04-1992 Rioting began after a jury decision to acquit four Los Angeles policemen in the Rodney King beating trial. 54 people were killed in 3 days.
29-04-1994 Israel and the PLO signed an agreement in Paris which granted Palestinians broad authority to set taxes, control trade and regulate banks under self-rule in the Gaza Strip and Jericho.
29-04-1996 Former CIA Director William Colby was missing and presumed drowned after an apparent boating accident in Maryland. Colby's body was later recovered.
29-04-1997 Staff Sgt. Delmar Simpson, a drill instructor at Aberdeen Proving Ground in Maryland, was convicted of raping six female trainees. He was sentenced to 25 years in prison and was dishonorably discharged.
29-04-1997 Astronaut Jerry Linenger and cosmonaut Vasily Tsibliyev went on the first U.S.-Russian space walk.
29-04-1998 The U.S., Canada and Mexico end tariffs on $1 billion in NAFTA trade.
29-04-1998 Brazil announced a plan to protect a large are of Amazon forest. The area was about the size of Colorado.
29-04-2002 Kelsey Grammer and his production company, Grammnet Inc., were ordered to pay more than $2 million in unpaid commissions to his former talent agency.
29-04-2003 Mr. T (Laurence Tureaud) filed a lawsuit against Best Buy Co. Inc., that claimed the store did not have permission to use his likeness in a print ad.
30-04-1930 Jesus of Nazareth was crucified.
30-04-0313 Licinius unified the whole of the eastern empire under his own rule.
30-04-1250 King Louis IX of France was ransomed for one million dollars.
30-04-1527 Henry VIII and King Francis of France signed the treaty of Westminster.
30-04-1563 All Jews were expelled from France by order of Charles VI.
30-04-1725 Spain withdrew from Quadruple Alliance.
30-04-1789 George Washington took office as first elected U.S. president.
30-04-1803 The U.S. purchased the Louisiana Territory from France for $15 million.
30-04-1812 Louisiana admitted as the 18th U.S. state.
30-04-1849 The republican patriot and guerrilla leader Giuseppe Garabaldi repulsed a French attack on Rome.
30-04-1864 Work began on the Dams along the Red River. The work would allow Union General Nathaniel Banks' troops to sail over the rapids above Alexandria, Louisiana.
30-04-1889 George Washington's inauguration became the first U.S. national holiday.
30-04-1900 Hawaii was organized as an official U.S. territory.
30-04-1900 Casey Jones was killed while trying to save the runaway train "Cannonball Express."
30-04-1930 The Soviet Union proposed a military alliance with France and Great Britain.
30-04-1931 The George Washington Bridge, linking New York City and New Jersey, opened.
30-04-1939 The first railroad car equipped with fluorescent lights was put into service. The train car was known as the "General Pershing Zephyr."
30-04-1939 Lou Gehrig played his last game with the New York Yankees.
30-04-1940 Belle Martell was licensed in California by state boxing officials. She was the first American woman, prizefight referee.
30-04-1943 The British submarine HMS Seraph dropped 'the man who never was,' a dead man the British planted with false invasion plans, into the Mediterranean off the coast of Spain.
30-04-1945 Adolf Hitler and Eva Braun committed suicide. They had been married for one day. One week later Germany surrendered unconditionally.
30-04-1945 Arthur Godfrey began his CBS radio morning show "Arthur Godfrey Time." It ran until this day in 1972.
30-04-1947 The name of Boulder Dam, in Nevada, was changed back to Hoover Dam.
30-04-1948 The Organization of American States held its first meeting in Bogota, Colombia.
30-04-1953 The British West Indian colonies agreed on the formation of the British Caribbean Federation that would eventually become a self-governing unit in the British Commonwealth.
30-04-1964 The FCC ruled that all TV receivers should be equipped to receive both VHF and UHF channels.
30-04-1968 U.S. Marines attacked a division of North Vietnamese in the village of Dai Do.
30-04-1970 U.S. troops invaded Cambodia to disrupt North Vietnamese Army base areas. The announcement by U.S. President Nixon led to widespread protests.
30-04-1972 The North Vietnamese launched an invasion of the South.
30-04-1973 U.S. President Nixon announced resignation of Haldeman, Ehrlichman, and other top aides.
30-04-1975 Communists North Vietnamese troops entered the Independence Palace of South Vietnam in Saigon. 11 Marines lifted off of the U.S. Embassy were the last soldiers to evacuate.
30-04-1980 Terrorists seized the Iranian Embassy in London.
30-04-1984 U.S. President Reagan signed cultural and scientific agreements with China. He also signed a tax accord that would make it easier for American companies to operate in China.
30-04-1991 An estimated 125,000 people were killed in a cyclone that hit Bangladesh.
30-04-1993 Monica Seles was stabbed in the back during a tennis match in Hamburg, Germany. The man called himself a fan of second- ranked Steffi Graf. He was convicted of causing grievous bodily harm and received a suspended sentence.
30-04-1997 ABC aired the "coming out" episode of the sitcom "Ellen." The title character, played by Ellen DeGeneres, admitted she was a lesbian.
30-04-1998 NATO was expanded to include Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic. The three nations were formally admitted the following April at NATO's 50th anniversary summit.
30-04-1998 United and Delta airlines announced their alliance that would give them control of 1/3 of all U.S. passenger seats.
30-04-1998 In the U.S., Federal regulators fined a contractor $2.25 million for improper handling of oxygen canisters on ValuJet that crashed in the Florida Everglades in 1996.
30-04-2001 Chandra Levy was last seen in Washington, DC. Her remains were found in Rock Creek Park on May 22, 2002. California Congressman Gary Condit was questioned in the case due to his relationship with Levy.
30-04-2002 Pakistani President Pervez Musharraf was overwhelmingly approved for another five years as president.
01-08-1498 Christopher Columbus landed on "Isla Santa" (Venezuela).
01-08-1619 The first black Americans (20) land at Jamestown, VA.
01-08-1774 Oxygen was isolated from air successfully by chemist Carl Wilhelm and scientist Joseph Priestly.
01-08-1779 Francis Scott Key was born. He was an American composer, attorney, poet, and social worker. He was the composer of the poem "Defence of Fort McHenry" which later became known as the "Star-Spangled Banner."
01-08-1790 The first U.S. census was completed. The population of the 17 states was 3,929,214.
01-08-1818 Maria Mitchell was born. She was the first female professional astronomer and the first women to be elected into the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.
01-08-1819 Herman Melville was born, the author of "Moby Dick."
01-08-1834 Slavery was outlawed in the British empire with an emancipation bill.
01-08-1873 Andrew S. Hallidie successfully tested a cable car. The design was done for San Francisco, CA.
01-08-1876 Colorado became the 38th state to join the United States.
01-08-1893 Shredded wheat was patented by Henry Perky and William Ford.
01-08-1894 The first Sino-Japanese War erupted. The dispute was over control of Korea.
01-08-1907 The U.S. Army established an aeronautical division that later became the U.S. Air Force.
01-08-1914 Germany declared war on Russia at the beginning of World War I.
01-08-1936 Adolf Hitler presided over the Olympic games as they opened in Berlin.
01-08-1943 Several deaths occurred in a race-related riot in Harlem, New York City.
01-08-1944 In Warsaw, Poland, an uprising against Nazi occupation began. The revolt only lasted two months.
01-08-1946 In the U.S., the Atomic Energy Commission was established.
01-08-1953 The first aluminum-faced building was completed. It was the first of this type in America.
01-08-1956 The Social Security Act was amended to provide benefits to disabled workers aged 50-64 and disabled adult children.
01-08-1957 The North American Air Defense Command (NORAD) was created by the United States and Canada.
01-08-1966 Fifteen people were shot and killed and 31 others were injured by Charles Joseph Whitman from a tower at the University of Texas at Austin. Whitman was killed in the tower.
01-08-1973 The movie "American Graffiti" opened.
01-08-1975 The Helsinki accords pledged the signatory nations to respect human rights.
01-08-1976 The Seattle Seahawks played their first (preseason) game. The Seahawks lost 27-20 to San Francisco.
01-08-1978 Pete Rose of the Cincinnati Reds ended his streak of hitting in 44 consecutive games.
01-08-1986 John McEnroe and Tatum O'Neal were married.
01-08-1986 Bert Blyleven became only the 10th pitcher to strike out 3,000 batters in his career.
01-08-1988 Martin Scorsese's "The Last Temptation of Christ" opened.
01-08-1991 Actress Hedy Lamar, 77, was arrested for shoplifting in Florida.
01-08-1993 Reggie Jackson was admitted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, NY.
01-08-1995 Westinghouse Electric Corporation announced a deal to buy CBS for $5.4 billion.
01-08-1998 The U.S. books and music chain Borders opens its first European outlet with a 40,000-square-foot store on London's Oxford Street.
01-08-2006 Cuban leader Fidel Castro turned over absolute power when he gave his brother Raul authority while he underwent an intestinal surgery.
02-08-1776 Members of the Continental Congress began adding their signatures to the Declaration of Independence.
02-08-1782 George Washington invented the Honorary Badge of Distinction.
02-08-1791 Samuel Briggs and his son Samuel Briggs, Jr. received a joint patent for their nail-making machine. They were the first father-son pair to receive a patent.
02-08-1824 In New York City, Fifth Avenue was opened.
02-08-1858 In Boston and New York City the first mailboxes were installed along streets.
02-08-1861 The United States Congress passed the first income tax. The revenues were intended for the war effort against the South. The tax was never enacted.
02-08-1876 "Wild Bill" Hickok was killed (shot from behind) while playing poker in Deadwood, SD. Jack McCall was later hanged for the shooting.
02-08-1887 Rowell Hodge patented barbed wire.
02-08-1892 Charles A. Wheeler patented the first escalator.
02-08-1921 Eight White Sox players were acquitted of throwing the 1919 World Series.
02-08-1922 Alexander Graham Bell died.
02-08-1926 John Barrymore and Mary Astor starred in the first showing of the Vitaphone System. The system was the combining of picture and sound for movies.
02-08-1934 German President Paul von Hindenburg died. His successor was Adolf Hitler.
02-08-1938 Bright yellow baseballs were used in a major league baseball game between the Dodgers and the Cardinals. It was hoped that the balls would be easier to see.
02-08-1939 Albert Einstein signed a letter to President Roosevelt urging the U.S. to have an atomic weapons research program.
02-08-1939 U.S. President Roosevelt signed the Hatch Act. The act prohibited civil service employees from taking an active part in political campaigns.
02-08-1943 The U.S. Navy patrol torpedo boat, PT-109, sank after being attacked by a Japanese destroyer. The boat was under the command of Lt. John F. Kennedy.
02-08-1945 The Allied conference at Potsdam was concluded.
02-08-1964 The Pentagon reported the first of two North Vietnamese attacks on U.S. destroyers in the Gulf of Tonkin.
02-08-1974 John Dean was sentenced to 1-4 years in prison for his involvement in the Watergate cover-up.
02-08-1980 A bomb exploded in a train station in Bologna, Italy. 85 people were killed.
02-08-1983 U.S. House of Representatives approved a law that designated the third Monday of January would be a federal holiday in honor of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. The law was signed by President Reagon on November 2.
02-08-1985 137 people were killed when a jumbo jet crashed at Dallas-Fort Worth International Airport. The Delta jet was attempting to land at the time of the crash.
02-08-1987 "Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs" was re-released. The film was 50 years old at the time of its re-release.
02-08-1988 U.S. military investigators concluded that "crew errors" were the cause of the shooting down of an Iranian passenger jet on July 3, 1988.
02-08-1990 Iraq invaded the oil-rich country of Kuwait. Iraq claimed that Kuwait had driven down oil prices by exceeding production quotas set by OPEC.
02-08-1995 China ordered the expulsion of two U.S. Air Force officers. The two were said to have been caught spying on military sights.
02-08-1999 In eastern India, at least 278 people were killed when two trains collided at a station.
03-08-1492 Christopher Columbus left Palos, Spain with three ships. The voyage would lead him to what is now known as the Americas. He reached the Bahamas on October 12.
03-08-1750 Christopher Dock completed the first book of teaching methods. It was titled "A Simple and Thoroughly Prepared School Management."
03-08-1880 The American Canoe Association was formed at Lake George, NY.
03-08-1900 Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. was founded.
03-08-1914 Germany declared war on France. The next day World War I began when Britain declared war on Germany.
03-08-1922 WGY radio in Schenectady, NY, presented the first full-length melodrama on radio. The work was "The Wolf", written by Eugene Walter.
03-08-1923 Calvin Coolidge was sworn in as the 30th president of the U.S. after the sudden death of President Harding.
03-08-1933 The Mickey Mouse Watch was introduced for the price of $2.75.
03-08-1936 The U.S. State Department advised Americans to leave Spain due to the Spanish Civil War.
03-08-1936 Jesse Owens won the first of his four Olympic gold medals.
03-08-1943 Gen. George S. Patton verbally abused and slapped a private. Later, Gen. Dwight D. Eisenhower ordered him to apologize for the incident.
03-08-1949 The National Basketball Association (NBA) was formed. The league was formed by the merger between the Basketball Association of America and the National Basketball League.
03-08-1956 Bedloe's Island had its name changed to Liberty Island.
03-08-1958 The Nautilus became the first vessel to cross the North Pole underwater. The mission was known as "Operation Sunshine."
03-08-1966 Lenny Bruce overdosed on morphine at the age of 40.
03-08-1979 "More American Graffiti" was released.
03-08-1979 Johnny Carson, the "Tonight Show" host, was on the cover of the Burbank, CA, telephone directory.
03-08-1981 U.S. traffic controllers with PATCO, the Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization, went on strike. They were fired just as U.S. President Reagan had warned.
03-08-1984 Mary Lou Retton won a gold medal at the Los Angeles Summer Olympics.
03-08-1985 Mail service returned to a nudist colony in Paradise Lake, FL. Residents promised that they’d wear clothes or stay out of sight when the mailperson came to deliver.
03-08-1988 The Iran-Contra hearings ended. No ties were made between U.S. President Reagan and the Nicaraguan Rebels.
03-08-1988 The Soviet Union released Mathias Rust. He had been taken into custody on May 28, 1987 for landing a plane in Moscow's Red Square.
03-08-1989 Shiite Muslim kidnappers suspended their threat to execute another hostage. It had been reported that the terrorist in Lebanon had hung Lt. Col. William R. Higgins three days before.
03-08-1989 Hashemi Rafsanjani was sworn in as the president of Iran.
03-08-1990 Thousands of Iraqi troops pushed within a few miles of the border of Saudi Arabia. This heightened world concerns that the invasion of Kuwait could spread.
03-08-1992 The U.S. Senate voted to restrict and eventually end the testing of nuclear weapons.
03-08-1992 Russia and Ukraine agreed to put the Black Sea Fleet under joint command. The agreement was to last for three years.
03-08-1994 Arkansas executed three prisoners. It was the first time in 32 years.
03-08-1995 Eyad Ismoil was flown from Jordan to the U.S. to face charges that he had driven the van that blew up in New York's World Trade Center.
03-08-2001 A grand jury indicted Robert Iler on charges that he and two teen-agers robbed two other teen-age boys for $40.
03-08-2004 In New York, the Statue of Liberty re-opened to the public. The site had been closed since the terrorist attacks on the U.S. on September 11, 2001.
03-08-2004 NASA launched the spacecraft Messenger. The 6 1/2 year journey was planned to arrive at the planet Mercury in March 2011.
04-08-1735 Freedom of the press was established with an acquittal of John Peter Zenger. The writer of the New York Weekly Journal had been charged with seditious libel by the royal governor of New York. The jury said that "the truth is not libelous."
04-08-1753 George Washington became a Master Mason.
04-08-1790 The Revenue Cutter Service was formed. This U.S. naval task force was the beginning of the U.S. Coast Guard.
04-08-1821 "The Saturday Evening Post" was published for the first time as a weekly.
04-08-1892 Andrew and Abby Borden were axed to death in their home in Fall River, MA. Lizzie, Andrew's daughter, was accused of the killings but was later acquitted.
04-08-1914 Britain declared war on Germany in World War I. The U.S. proclaimed its neutrality.
04-08-1921 The first radio broadcast of a tennis match occurred. It was in Pittsburgh, PA.
04-08-1922 The death of Alexander Graham Bell, two days earlier, was recognized by AT&T and the Bell Systems by shutting down all of its switchboards and switching stations. The shutdown affected 13 million phones.
04-08-1934 Mel Ott became the first major league baseball player to score six runs in a single game.
04-08-1944 Nazi police raided a house in Amsterdam and arrested eight people. Anne Frank, a teenager at the time, was one of the people arrested. Her diary would be published after her death.
04-08-1949 An earthquake in Ecuador destroyed 50 towns and killed more than 6000 people.
04-08-1954 The uranium rush began in Saskatchewan, Canada.
04-08-1956 William Herz became the first person to race a motorcycle over 200 miles per hour. He was clocked at 210 mph.
04-08-1957 Florence Chadwick set a world record by swimming the English Channel in 6 hours and 7 minutes.
04-08-1957 Juan Fangio won his final auto race and captured the world auto driving championship. It was his the fifth consecutive year to win.
04-08-1958 The first potato flake plant was completed in Grand Forks, ND.
04-08-1958 Billboard Magazine introduced its "Hot 100" chart, which was part popularity and a barometer of the movement of potential hits. The first number one song was Ricky Nelson's "Poor Little Fool."
04-08-1964 The bodies of Michael H. Schwerner, James E. Chaney, and Andrew Goodman were found in an earthen dam in Mississippi. The three were civil rights workers. They had disappeared on June 21, 1964.
04-08-1972 Arthur Bremer was found guilty of shooting George Wallace, the governor of Alabama. Bremer was sentenced to 63 years in prison.
04-08-1977 U.S. President Carter signed the measure that established the Department of Energy.
04-08-1983 New York Yankee outfielder Dave Winfield threw a baseball during warm-ups and accidentally killed a seagull. After the game, Toronto police arrested him for "causing unnecessary suffering to an animal."
04-08-1984 Carl Lewis won a gold medal in the Los Angeles Olympics.
04-08-1984 Upper Volta, and African republic, changed its name to Burkina Faso.
04-08-1985 Tom Seaver of the Chicago White Sox achieved his 300th victory.
04-08-1985 Rod Carew of the California angels got his 3,000th major league hit.
04-08-1986 The United States Football League called off its 1986 season. This was after winning only token damges in its antitrust lawsuit against the National Football League.
04-08-1987 The Fairness Doctrine was rescinded by the Federal Communications Commission. The doctrine had required that radio and TV stations present controversial issues in a balanced fashion.
04-08-1987 A new 22-cent U.S. stamp honoring noted author William Faulkner, went on sale in Oxford, MS. Faulkner had been fired as postmaster of that same post office in 1924.
04-08-1988 U.S. Rep. Mario Biaggi of New York was sentenced to prison. The conviction included charges of extortion, tax evasion, and acceptance of bribes in relation to the Wedtech scandal. Biaggi was paroled in 1990.
04-08-1989 Iranian President Hashemi Rafsanjani offered to assist end the hostage crisis in Lebanon.
04-08-1990 The European Community imposed an embargo on oil from Iraq and Kuwait. This was done to protest the Iraqi invasion of the oil-rich Kuwait.
04-08-1991 The Oceanos, a Greek luxury liner, sank off of South Africa's southeast coast. All of the 402 passengers and 179 crewmembers survived.
04-08-1992 Wang Hongwen died of a liver ailment. Hongwen was a member of the radical "Gang of Four". The gang had terrorized China during the Cultural Revolution.
04-08-1993 Stacey Koon and Laurence Powell, Los Angeles police officers were sentenced to 2 1/2 years in prison for violating Rodney King's civil rights.
04-08-1994 Yugoslavia withdrew its support for Bosnian Serbs. The border between Yugoslavia and Serb-held Bosnia was sealed.
04-08-1996 Josia Thugwane won a gold medal after finishing first in the marathon. He became the first black South African to win a gold medal.
04-08-1997 Teamsters began a 15-day strike against UPS (United Parcel Service). The strikers eventually won an increase in full-time positions and defeated a proposed reorganization of the companies pension plan.
05-08-1833 The village of Chicago was incorporated. The population was approximately 250.
05-08-1861 The U.S. federal government levied its first income tax. The tax was 3% of all incomes over $800. The wartime measure was rescinded in 1872.
05-08-1864 During the U.S. Civil War, Union forces led by Adm. David G. Farragut were led into Mobile Bay, Alabama.
05-08-1884 On Bedloe's Island in New York Harbor, the cornerstone for the Statue of Liberty was laid.
05-08-1914 The electric traffic lights were installed in Cleveland, Ohio.
05-08-1921 The first play-by-play broadcast of a baseball game was done by Harold Arlin. KDKA Radio in Pittsburgh, PA described the action between the Pirates and Philadelphia.
05-08-1921 The cartoon "On the Road to Moscow", by Rollin Kirby, was published in the "New York World". It was the first cartoon to win a Pulitzer Prize.
05-08-1923 Henry Sullivan became the first American to swim across the English Channel.
05-08-1924 In the New York "Daily News" debuted the comic strip "Little Orphan Annie," by Harold Gray.
05-08-1944 Polish insurgents liberated a German labor camp in Warsaw. 348 Jewish prisoners were freed.
05-08-1953 During the Korean conflict prisoners were exchanged at Panmunjom. The exchange was labeled Operation Big Switch.
05-08-1960 For the first time two major league baseball clubs traded managers. Detroit traded Jimmy Dykes for Cleveland's Joe Gordon.
05-08-1962 Marilyn Monroe was found dead in her home. The "probable suicide" was caused by an overdose of sleeping pills. Monroe was 36 at the time of her death.
05-08-1963 The Limited Test Ban Treaty was signed by the United States, Britain, and the Soviet Union. The treaty banned nuclear tests in space, underwater, and in the atmosphere.
05-08-1964 U.S. aircraft bombed North Vietnam after North Vietnamese boats attacked U.S. destroyers in the Gulf of Tonkin.
05-08-1969 The Mariner 7, a U.S. space probe, passed by Mars. Photographs and scientific data were sent back to Earth.
05-08-1974 U.S. President Nixon said that he expected to be impeached. Nixon had ordered the investigation into the Watergate break-in to halt.
05-08-1974 "Tank McNamara", the comic strip, premiered in 75 newspapers.
05-08-1981 The U.S. federal government started firing striking air traffic controllers.
05-08-1983 David Crosby was sentenced to eight years in prison. He had been charged with drug and firearm possession. He was paroled in 1986.
05-08-1984 Toronto’s Cliff Johnson set a major league baseball record by hitting the 19th pinch-hit home run in his career.
05-08-1986 It was revealed that artist Andrew Wyeth had secretly created 240 drawings and paintings of his neighbor. The works of Helga Testorf had been created over a 15-year period.
05-08-1989 In Honduras, five Central American presidents began meeting to discuss the timetable for the dismantling of the Nicaraguan Contra bases.
05-08-1990 U.S. President Bush angrily denounced the Iraqi invasion of Kuwait.
05-08-1991 An investigation was formally launched by Democratic congressional leaders to find out if the release of American hostages was delayed until after the Reagan-Bush presidential election.
05-08-1991 Iraq admitted to misleading U.N. inspectors about secret biological weapons.
05-08-1992 Federal civil rights charges were filed against four Los Angeles police officers. The officers had been acquitted on California State charges. Two of the officers were convicted and jailed on violation of civil rights charges.
05-08-1998 Iraqi President Saddam Hussein began not cooperating with U.N. weapons inspectors.
05-08-1998 Marie Noe of Philadelphia, PA was arrested and charged with first-degree murder, accused of smothering eight of her children to death between 1949 and 1968. Noe later received 20 years' probation.
05-08-1999 In Malibu, CA, Robert Downey Jr. was sentenced to three years in prison for missing scheduled drug tests.
05-08-1999 Mark McGwire (St. Louis Cardinals) hit his 500th career homerun. He also set a record for the fewest at-bats to hit the 500 homerun mark.
05-08-2002 The U.S. closed its consulate in Karachi, Pakistan. The consulate was closed after local authorities removed large concrete blocks and reopened the road in front of the building to normal traffic.
06-08-1787 The Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia began. The articles of the U.S. Constitution draft were to be debated.
06-08-1806 The Holy Roman Empire went out of existence as Emperor Francis II abdicated.
06-08-1825 Bolivia declared independence from Peru.
06-08-1879 The first Australian rules football game to be played at night took place at the Melbourne Cricket Ground. The game was to promote the introduction of electricity to the city of Melbourne.
06-08-1890 The electric chair was used for the first time when Auburn State Prison in New York executed convicted murderer William Kemmler.
06-08-1890 Cy Young achieved his first major league victory. He would accumulate 511 in his career.
06-08-1914 Austria-Hungary declared war against Russia. Serbia declared war against Germany.
06-08-1926 Gertrude Ederle became the first American woman to swim the English Channel. She was 19 years old at the time. The swim took her 14 1/2 hours.
06-08-1926 Warner Brothers premiered its Vitaphone system in New York. The movie was "Don Juan," starring John Barrymore.
06-08-1930 Joseph Force Crater, a New York Supreme Court Justice, mysteriously disappeared. He was declared legally dead in 1939.
06-08-1939 Dinah Shore started her own show on the NBC Blue radio network.
06-08-1945 The American B-29 bomber, known as the Enola Gay, dropped the first atomic bomb on an inhabited area. The bomb named "Little Boy" was dropped over the center of Hiroshima, Japan. An estimated 140,000 people were killed.
06-08-1949 Chicago White Sox player Luke Appling played in the 2,154th game of his 19-year, major league career.
06-08-1952 Satchel Paige, at age 46, became the oldest pitcher to complete a major league baseball game.
06-08-1960 Nationalization of U.S. and foreign-owned property in Cuba began.
06-08-1962 Jamaica became an independent dominion within the British Commonwealth.
06-08-1965 The Voting Rights Act was signed by U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson.
06-08-1969 The first fair ball to be hit completely out of Dodger Stadium occurred. Willie "Pops" Stargell, of the Pittsburgh Pirates, hit the ball 506 feet from home plate.
06-08-1981 Fire fighters in Indianapolis, IN, answered a false alarm. When they returned to their station it was ablaze due to a grease fire.
06-08-1981 Lee Trevino was disqualified from the PGA Championship in Duluth, GA when he had his scorecard signed by Tom Weiskopf instead of himself.
06-08-1985 The 40th anniversary of the Hiroshima atomic bombing brought tens of thousands of Japanese and foreigners to Hiroshima.
06-08-1986 William J. Schroeder died. He lived 620 days with the Jarvik-7 manmade heart. He was the world's longest surviving recipient of a permanent artificial heart.
06-08-1986 Timothy Dalton became the fourth actor to be named "James Bond."
06-08-1989 Jaime Paz Zamora was inaugurated as the president of Bolivia.
06-08-1990 The U.N. Security Council ordered a worldwide trade embargo with Iraq. The embargo was to punish Iraq for invading Kuwait.
06-08-1991 Harry Reasoner died at the age of 68. He was a newsman for CBS-TV.
06-08-1993 The U.S. Senate confirmed Louis Freeh to be the director of the FBI.
06-08-1993 Morihiro Hosokawa was elected prime minister of Japan.
06-08-1994 Randolph County High School, in Wedowee, AL, was destroyed by fire. The principle's stand against interracial dating had caused much tension in the school.
06-08-1995 Thousands of glowing lanterns were set afloat in rivers in Hiroshima, Japan, on the 50th anniversary of the first atomic bombing.
06-08-1996 NASA announced the discovery of evidence of primitive life on Mars. The evidence came in the form of a meteorite that was found in Antarctica. The meteorite was believed to have come from Mars and contained a fossil.
06-08-1997 Apple Computer and Microsoft agreed to share technology in a deal giving Microsoft a stake in Apple's survival.
06-08-1998 Former White House intern Monica Lewinsky spent 8 1/2 hours testifying before a grand jury about her relationship with U.S. President Clinton.
06-08-1998 The last new episode of Magic Johnson's talk show, "The Magic Hour," aired.
06-08-1999 Mark McGwire (St. Louis Cardinals) got the 500th homerun of his major league career. He also set a record for the fewest at-bats to hit the 500 homerun mark.
07-08-1789 The U.S. War Department was established by the U.S. Congress.
07-08-1782 George Washington created the Order of the Purple Heart.
07-08-1888 Theophilus Van Kannel received a patent for the revolving door.
07-08-1914 Germany invaded France.
07-08-1928 The U.S. Treasure Department issued a new bill that was one third smaller than the previous U.S. bills.
07-08-1934 The U.S. Court of Appeals upheld a lower court ruling striking down the government's attempt to ban the controversial James Joyce novel "Ulysses."
07-08-1942 U.S. forces landed at Guadalcanal, marking the start of the first major allied offensive in the Pacific during World War II.
07-08-1947 The balsa wood raft Kon-Tiki, which had carried a six-man crew 4,300 miles across the Pacific Ocean, crashed into a reef in a Polynesian archipelago.
07-08-1959 The U.S. launched Explorer 6, which sent back a picture of the Earth.
07-08-1960 The Cuban Catholic Church condemned the rise of communism in Cuba. Fidel Castro then banned all religious TV and radio broadcasts.
07-08-1964 The U.S. Congress passed the Gulf of Tonkin resolution, which gave President Johnson broad powers in dealing with reported North Vietnamese attacks on U.S. forces.
07-08-1974 French stuntman Philippe Petit walked a tightrope strung between the twin towers of New York's World Trade Center.
07-08-1976 Scientists in Pasadena, CA, announced that the Viking 1 spacecraft had found strong indications of possible life on Mars.
07-08-1981 After 128 years of publication, "The Washington Star" ceased all operations.
07-08-1987 The presidents of five Central American nations, met in Guatemala City, and signed an 11-point agreement designed to bring peace to their region.
07-08-1989 A small plane carrying U.S. Congressman Mickey Leland, D-TX, and 15 others disappeared during a flight in Ethiopia. The wreckage of the plane was found six days later. There were no survivors.
07-08-1990 U.S. President Bush ordered U.S. troops and warplanes to Saudi Arabia to guard against a possible invasion by Iraq.
07-08-1998 The U.S. embassies in Nairobi, Kenya and Dar es Salaam, Tanzania were bombed killing 224 people and injuring over 5,500. Osama bin Laden was later indicted by a U.S. federal grand jury in connection with the attacks.
07-08-1999 Tony Gwynn of the San Diego Padres got his 3,000th hit of his major league career.
07-08-2003 In California, Arnold Schwarzenegger announced that he would run for the office of governor.
07-08-2003 Stephen Geppi bought a 1963 G.I. Joe prototype for $200,000.
08-08-1588 The Spanish Armada was defeated by the English fleet ending an invasion attempt.
08-08-1815 Napoleon Bonaparte set sail for St. Helena, in the South Atlantic. The remainder of his life was spent there in exile.
08-08-1844 After the killing of Joseph Smith, Bringham Young was chosen to lead the Mormons.
08-08-1866 African-American explorer Matthew A. Henson was born. Henson, along with Robert Peary and their Eskimo guide, were the first people to reach the North Pole.
08-08-1876 Thomas Edison received a patent for the mimeograph. The mimeograph was a "method of preparing autographic stencils for printing."
08-08-1899 The refrigerator was patented by A.T. Marshall.
08-08-1900 In Boston, the first Davis Cup series began. The U.S. team defeated Great Britain three matches to zero.
08-08-1911 The number of representatives in the U.S. House of Representatives was established at 435. There was one member of Congress for every 211,877 residents.
08-08-1940 The German Luftwaffe began a series of daylight air raids on Great Britain.
