08-08-1942 Six Nazi saboteurs were executed in Washington after conviction. Two others were cooperative and received life in prison.
08-08-1945 The United Nations Charter was signed by U.S. President Truman.
08-08-1945 During World War II, the Soviet Union declared war on Japan.
08-08-1953 The U.S. and South Korea initiated a mutual security pact.
08-08-1956 Japan launched an oil tanker that was 780 feet long and weighed 84,730 tons. It was the largest oil tanker in the world.
08-08-1963 The "Great Train Robbery" took place in Britain. A gang of 15 thieves stole 2.6 million pounds in bank notes.
08-08-1966 Michael DeBakey became the first surgeon to install an artificial heart pump in a patient.
08-08-1974 U.S. President Nixon announced that he would resign the following day.
08-08-1978 The U.S. launched Pioneer Venus II, which carried scientific probes to study the atmosphere of Venus.
08-08-1985 Near Frankfurt, outside the Rhein-Mein U.S. air base, a bomb exploded killing two Americans. The bomb was blamed on the Red Army Faction.
08-08-1986 A car bomb exploded in Beirut, the third in 12 days, killing 17 people.
08-08-1988 It was announced that a cease-fire between Iraq and Iran had begun.
08-08-1989 The space shuttle Columbia took off from Cape Canaveral, FL. The trip was said to be a secret five-day military mission.
08-08-1990 American forces began positioning in Saudia Arabia.
08-08-1991 John McCarthy, a British TV producer was released by his Lebanese kidnappers. He had been held captive for more than five years. A rival group abducted Jerome Leyraud in retaliation and threatened to kill him if any more hostages were released.
08-08-1991 The slain bodies of former Iranian Prime Minister Shahpour Bakhriar and his chief of staff were found.
08-08-1991 The U.N. Security Council approved North and South Korea for membership.
08-08-1992 The "Dream Team" clinched the gold medal at the Barcelona Summer Olympics. The U.S. basketball team beat Croatia 117-85.
08-08-1993 Four U.S. soldiers were killed in Somalia when a land mine detonated underneath their vehicle.
08-08-1994 The first road link between Israel and Jordan opened.
08-08-1994 Representatives from China and Taiwan signed a cooperation agreement.
08-08-1995 Saddam Hussein's two eldest daughters, their husbands, and several senior army officers defected.
08-08-1999 Wade Boggs got his 3,000th hit of his major league baseball career.
08-08-2000 The submarine H.L. Hunley was raised from ocean bottom after 136 years. The sub had been lost during an attack on the U.S.S. Housatonic in 1864. The Hunley was the first submarine in history to sink a warship.
09-08-1678 American Indians sold the Bronx to Jonas Bronck for 400 beads.
09-08-1790 The Columbia returned to Boston Harbor after a three-year voyage. It was the first ship to carry the American flag around the world.
09-08-1831 The first steam locomotive began its first trip between Schenectady and Albany, NY.
09-08-1842 The U.S. and Canada signed the Webster-Ashburton Treaty, which solved a border dispute.
09-08-1848 Martin Van Buren was nominated for president by the Free-Soil Party in Buffalo, NY.
09-08-1854 "Walden" was published by Henry David Thoreau.
09-08-1859 The escalator was patented by Nathan Ames.
09-08-1893 "Gut Holz" was published. It was America's first bowling magazine.
09-08-1902 After the death of Queen Victoria, Edward VII was crowned king of England.
09-08-1910 A.J. Fisher received a patent for the electric washing machine.
09-08-1930 Betty Boop had her beginning in "Dizzy Dishes" created by Max Fleischer.
09-08-1936 Jesse Owens won his fourth gold medal at the Berlin Olympics. He was the first American to win four medals in one Olympics.
09-08-1942 Mohandas K. Gandhi was arrested Britain. He was not released until 1944.
09-08-1942 CBS radio debuted "Our Secret Weapon."
09-08-1944 The Forest Service and Wartime Advertising Council created "Smokey the Bear."
09-08-1945 The U.S. dropped an atomic bomb on Nagasaki. The bombing came three days after the bombing of Hiroshima. About 74,000 people were killed. Japan surrendered August 14.
09-08-1945 The first network television broadcast occurred in Washington, DC. The program announced the bombing of Nagasaki, Japan.
09-08-1956 The first statewide, state-supported educational television network went on the air in Alabama.
09-08-1965 Singapore proclaimed its independence from the Malaysian Federation.
09-08-1969 Sharon Tate and four other people were found murdered at Tate's residence in Los Angeles, CA. Charles Manson and several members of his cult were later convicted of the crime.
09-08-1973 The U.S. Senate committee investigating the Watergate affair filed suit against President Richard Nixon.
09-08-1974 U.S. President Richard Nixon formally resigned. Gerald R. Ford took his place, and became the 38th president of the U.S.
09-08-1975 The New Orleans Superdome as officially opened when the Saints played the Houston Oilers in exhibition football. The new Superdome cost $163 million to build.
09-08-1981 Major league baseball teams resumed play at the conclusion of the first mid-season players’ strike.
09-08-1984 Daley Thompson, of Britain, won is second successive Olympic decathlon.
09-08-1985 Arthur J. Walker, a retired Navy officer, was found guilty of seven counts of spying for the Soviet Union.
09-08-1988 Wayne Gretzky of the Edmonton Oilers was traded. The trade was at Gretzky's request. He was sent to the Los Angeles Kings.
09-08-1989 112 people were killed when a train fell into the San Rafael River in Mexico. The incident was caused by a bridge that collapsed.
09-08-1996 Boris Yeltsin was sworn in as president of Russia for the second time.
09-08-1999 Russian President Boris Yeltsin fired Prime Minister Sergei Stepashin and his entire cabinet for the fourth time in 17 months.
09-08-2000 Former Dallas Cowboy Michael Irvin was arrested on a Class B misdemeanor of possession of less than 2 ounces of marijuana.
09-08-2001 U.S. President Bush announced he would support federal funding for limited medical research on embryonic stem cells.
09-08-2001 In Jerusalem, a Palestinian suicide bomber detonated an explosive inside a pizzeria. The lunchtime bombing killed 15 and wounded about 90 others.
09-08-2004 Donald Duck received the 2,257th star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
09-08-2004 Trump Hotel and Casion Resorts announced plans to file for Chapter 11 bankruptcy.
10-08-1792 King Louis XVI was taken into custody by mobs during the French Revolution. He was executed the following January after being put on trial for treason.
10-08-1809 Ecuador began its fight for independence from Spain.
10-08-1821 Missouri became the 24th state to join the Union.
10-08-1846 The Smithsonian Institution was chartered by the U.S. Congress. The "Nation's Attic" was made possible by $500,000 given by scientist Joseph Smithson.
10-08-1856 In Louisiana, a hurricane came ashore and killed about 400 people.
10-08-1859 In Boston, MA, the first milk inspectors were appointed.
10-08-1869 The motion picture projector was patented by O.B. Brown.
10-08-1881 Thomas Edison's exhibit opened the Paris Electrical Exhibition.
10-08-1885 The first electric streetcar, to be used commercially, was operated in Baltimore, MD, by Leo Daft.
10-08-1914 Austria-Hungary invaded Russia.
10-08-1921 Franklin D. Roosevelt was stricken with polio.
10-08-1927 Mount Rushmore was formally dedicated. The individual faces of the presidents were dedicated later.
10-08-1944 U.S. forces defeated the remaining Japanese resistance on Guam.
10-08-1945 The day after the atomic bombing of Nagasaki, Japan announced they would surrender. The only condition was that the status of Emperor Hirohito would remain unchanged.
10-08-1947 William Odom completed an around-the-world flight. He set the solo record by completing the flight in 73 hours and 5 minutes.
10-08-1948 On ABC, "Candid Camera" made its TV debut. The original title was "Candid Microphone."
10-08-1949 In the U.S., the National Military Establishment had its name changed to the Department of Defense.
10-08-1954 Construction began on the St. Lawrence Seaway.
10-08-1965 In Austin, TX, a fire burned part of the 20th floor of the 27-story University of Texas main building. A collection that contained items once owned by escape artist Harry Houdini and circus magnate P. T. Barnum were damaged by smoke and water.
10-08-1969 Leno and Rosemary LaBianca were murdered. Members of the Charles Manson cult committed the crimes one day after the killing of Sharon Tate and four other people.
10-08-1973 Arnold Palmer did not make the cut for the final two rounds of the PGA Golf Championship. It was the first time in his career.
10-08-1977 The "Son of Sam," David Berkowitz, was arrested in Yonkers, NY. Berkowitz, a postal employee, had shot and killed six people and wounded seven others.
10-08-1981 Pete Rose hit a single and broke the National League all-time hit record with his 3,630 hit.
10-08-1988 U.S. President Reagan signed a measure that provided $20,000 payments to Japanese-Americans who were interned by the U.S. government during World War II.
10-08-1991 In Phoenix, AZ, nine Buddhists were found slain in their temple. Two teenagers were arrested for the crime.
10-08-1993 A massive deficit-reduction bill was signed into law by U.S. President Bill Clinton.
10-08-1994 In Germany, three men were arrested after being caught smuggling plutonium into the country.
10-08-1994 U.S. President Clinton claimed presidential immunity when he asked a federal judge to dismiss, at least for the time being, a sexual harassment lawsuit filed by Paula Corbin Jones.
10-08-1995 Timothy McVeigh and Terry Nichols were charged with 11 counts in the Oklahoma City bombing.
10-08-1995 Michael Fortier plead guilty in a plea-bargain agreement. The agreement required that he testify for the prosecution in the Oklahoma City Federal building bombing trial.
10-08-1995 Norma McCorvey, "Jane Roe" of the 1973 U.S. Supreme Court decision legalizing abortion, announced that she had joined the anti-abortion group Operation Rescue.
10-08-1999 Near an India-Pakistan border area an Indian fighter jet shot down a Pakistani naval aircraft. Sixteen people were killed.
10-08-2003 Ekaterina Dmitriev and Russian cosmonaut Yuri Malenchenko were married. Malenchenko was about 240 miles above the earth in the international space station. It was the first-ever marriage from space.
10-08-2006 In Great Britain, 24 people were arrested for their roles in a plot to blow up airliners traveling between Britain and the United States. In Pakistan, 7 people were arrested for their roles in the same plot.
11-08-1860 The first silver mill in America to be successful began. The mill was in Virginia City, NV.
11-08-1874 A patent for the sprinkler head was given to Harry S. Parmelee.
11-08-1877 The two moons of Mars were discovered by Asaph Hall, an American astronomer. He named them Phobos and Deimos.
11-08-1896 Harvey Hubbell received a patent for the electric light bulb socket with a pull-chain.
11-08-1909 The American ship Arapahoe became the first to ever use the SOS distress signal off the coast of Cape Hatteras, NC.
11-08-1924 Newsreel pictures were taken of U.S. presidential candidates for the first time.
11-08-1934 Alcatraz, in San Francisco Bay, received federal prisoners for the first time.
11-08-1941 The Atlantic Charter was signed by U.S. President Roosevelt and British Prime Minister Winston Churchill.
11-08-1942 During World War II, Pierre Laval publicly announced "the hour of liberation for France is the hour when Germany wins the war."
11-08-1945 The Allies informed Japan that they would determine Emperor Hirohito's future status after Japan's surrender.
11-08-1951 The first major league baseball game to be televised in color was broadcast. The Brooklyn Dodgers defeated the Boston Braves 8-1.
11-08-1954 Seven years of fighting came to an end in Indochina. A formal peace was in place for the French and the Communist Vietminh.
11-08-1956 Abstract artist Jackson Pollack died in an automobile accident in East Hampton, NY.
11-08-1962 Andrian Nikolayev, of the Soviet Union, was launched on a 94-hour flight. He was the third Russian to go into space.
11-08-1965 Riots and looting took place in the Watts section of Los Angeles, CA. During the week that followed 34 people were killed. In addition, over 1,000 were injured, 3,000 were arrested and over $40 million in damage was done.
11-08-1965 The U.S. conducted a second launch of "Surveyor-SD 2" for a landing on the Moon surface test.
11-08-1971 Harmon Killebrew of the Minnesota Twins got his 500th and 501st home runs of his major league baseball career.
11-08-1975 The U.S. vetoed the proposed admission of North and South Vietnam to the United Nations. The Security Counsel had already refused to consider South Korea's application.
11-08-1984 Carl Lewis won his fourth gold medal in the 1984 Summer Olympics.
11-08-1984 U.S. President Ronald Reagan was preparing for his weekly radio broadcast when, during testing of the microphone, the President said of the Soviet Union, "My fellow Americans, I am pleased to tell you that I just signed legislation that would outlaw Russia forever. We begin bombing in five minutes."
11-08-1984 The Cincinnati Reds honored major league All-Star and Hall of Fame catcher Johnny Bench by retiring his uniform (#5).
11-08-1988 Dick Thornburgh was unanimously confirmed by the U.S. Senate to be the next attorney general. He succeeded Edwin Meese III.
11-08-1990 Egyptian and Moroccan troops joined U.S. forces in Saudia Arabia to help protect from a possible Iraqi attack.
11-08-1991 Edward Tracey, an American, was released by Shiite Muslim kidnappers. He had been held for nearly five years. Jerome Leyraud was also released. Leyraud, a Frenchman, had been kidnapped three days earlier.
11-08-1991 The space shuttle Atlantis ended its nine-day journey by landing safely.
11-08-1992 In Bloomington, MN, the Mall of America opened. It was the largest shopping mall in the United States.
11-08-1994 The Tenth International Conference on AIDS ended in Japan.
11-08-1994 A U.S. federal jury awarded $286.8 million to about 10,000 commercial fishermen for losses as a result of the 1989 Exxon Valdez oil spill.
11-08-1995 All U.S. nuclear tests were banned by President Clinton.
11-08-1995 A federal investigation was opened concerning the deadly siege at Ruby Ridge, ID, in 1992. The investigation was to find out whether FBI officials approved a "shoot on sight" order.
11-08-1997 U.S. President Clinton made the first use of the line-item veto approved by Congress, rejecting three items in spending and tax bills.
11-08-1998 British Petroleum became No. 3 among oil companies with the $49 billion purchase of Amoco. It was the largest foreign takeover of a U.S. company.
11-08-2002 US Airways announced that it had filed for bankruptcy.
11-08-2002 Jason Priestly crashed his car during practice for a race in the Infiniti Pro Series. He suffered a spinal fracture, a moderate concussion, a broken nose, facial lacerations and broken bones in both feet.
11-08-2003 Charles Taylor, President of Liberia, flew into exile after ceding power to his vice president, Moses Blah.
11-08-2003 In Kabul, NATO took command of the 5,000-strong peacekeeping force in Afghanistan.
12-08-1656 "King Phillip's War" came to an end with the killing of Indian chief King Phillip. The war between the Indians and the Europeans lasted for two years.
12-08-1851 Isaac Singer was issued a patent on the double-headed sewing machine.
12-08-1865 Disinfectant was used for the first time during surgery by Joseph Lister.
12-08-1867 U.S. President Andrew Johnson sparked a move to impeach him when he defied Congress by suspending Secretary of War Edwin M. Stanton.
12-08-1877 Thomas Edison invented the phonograph and made the first sound recording.
12-08-1879 The first National Archery Association tournament took place in Chicago, IL.
12-08-1898 Hawaii was annexed by the U.S. Hawaii was later given territorial status and was given Statehood in 1959.
12-08-1898 The Spanish-American War was ended with the signing of the peace protocol. The U.S. acquired Guam, Puerto Rico and the Philippines. Hawaii was also annexed.
12-08-1915 "Of Human Bondage", by William Somerset Maugham, was first published.
12-08-1918 Regular airmail service began between Washington, DC, and New York City.
12-08-1937 Red Skelton appeared on network radio for the first time on the "Rudy Vallee Show" on NBC.
12-08-1944 In France, Pierre Laval released Edouard Herriot.
12-08-1944 Joseph P. Kennedy Jr. was killed with his co-pilot when their Navy plane exploded over England. Joseph Kennedy was the oldest son of Joseph and Rose Fitzgerald Kennedy.
12-08-1953 The Soviet Union secretly tested its first hydrogen bomb.
12-08-1960 The balloon satellite Echo One was launched by the U.S. from Cape Canaveral, FL. It was the first communications satellite.
12-08-1962 The Soviet Union launched Pavel Popovich into orbit. Popovich and Andrian Nikolayev, who was launch a day before, both landed on August 15.
12-08-1964 Mickey Mantle set a major league baseball record when he hit home runs from both the left and ride sides of the plate in the same game.
12-08-1969 The Boston Celtics were sold for $6 million. At the time it was the highest price paid for a pro basketball team.
12-08-1973 Jack Nicklaus won his 14th major golf title. The win broke the record that had been held by Bobby Jones for 50 years.
12-08-1977 The space shuttle Enterprise passed its first solo flight test.
12-08-1981 IBM unveiled its first PC.
12-08-1985 A Japan Air Lines Boeing 747 crashed into a mountain killing 520 people.
12-08-1986 It was announced by NASA that they had selected a new rocket design for the space shuttle. The move was made in an effort at correcting the flaws that were believed to have been responsible for the Challenger disaster.
12-08-1986 Rod Carew became the first player in the history of the California Angels franchise to have his uniform (#29) retired.
12-08-1988 "The Last Temptation of Christ" opened.
12-08-1990 The first U.S. casualty occurred during the Persian Gulf crisis when Air Force Staff Sergeant John Campisi died after being hit by a military truck.
12-08-1992 The U.S., Canada, and Mexico announced that the North American Free Trade Agreement had been created after 14 months of negotiations.
12-08-1993 U.S. President Clinton signed a relief package for the flooded areas of the Midwest United States.
12-08-1993 U.S. President Clinton lifted the ban on rehiring air traffic controllers that had been fired for going on strike in 1981.
12-08-1994 Major league baseball players went on strike rather than allow team owners to limit their salaries. The strike lasted for 232 days. As a result, the World Series was wiped out for the first time in 90 years.
12-08-1998 Swiss banks agreed to pay $1.25 billion as restitution to World War II Holocaust victims.
12-08-1999 Hang Thu Thi Ngyuen shot an arrow from a bow with her feet on "Guinness World Records: Primetime" and hit a target that was 16 feet and 5 inches away.
13-08-1521 Present day Mexico City was captured by Spanish conqueror Hernando Cortez from the Aztec Indians.
13-08-1704 The Battle of Blenheim was fought during the War of the Spanish Succession, resulting in a victory for English and Austrian forces.
13-08-1792 French revolutionaries took the entire French royal family and imprisoned them.
13-08-1784 The United States Legislature met for the final time in Annapolis, MD.
13-08-1846 The American Flag was raised for the first time in Los Angeles, CA.
13-08-1867 "Under the Gaslight", by Augustin Daly, opened in New York City, NY.
13-08-1876 The Reciprocity Treaty between the U.S. and Hawaii was ratified.
13-08-1889 A patent for a coin-operated telephone was issued to William Gray.
13-08-1907 The first taxicab started on the streets of New York City.
13-08-1912 The first experimental radio license was issued to St. Joseph's College in Philadelphia, PA.
13-08-1931 The first community hospital in the U.S. was dedicated in Elk City, OK.
13-08-1932 Adolf Hitler refused to take the post of vice-chancellor of Germany. He said he was going to hold out "for all or nothing."
13-08-1934 Al Capp's comic strip "L'il Abner" made its debut in newspapers.
13-08-1935 The first roller derby match was held at the Coliseum in Chicago, IL.
13-08-1942 Walt Disney's "Bambi" opened at Radio City Music Hall in New York City, NY.
13-08-1959 In New York, ground was broken on the $320 million Verrazano Narrows Bridge.
13-08-1960 "Echo I," a balloon satellite, allowed the first two-way telephone conversation by satellite to take place.
13-08-1961 Berlin was divided by a barbed wire fence to halt the flight of refugees. Two days later work on the Berlin Wall began.
13-08-1979 Lou Brock of the St. Louis Cardinals got his 3,000th career hit.
13-08-1985 The engagement of Maria Shriver and Arnold Schwarzenagger was announced.
13-08-1986 United States Football League standout Herschel Walker signed to play with the Dallas Cowboys of the National Football League.
13-08-1989 The wreckage of Texas Congressman Mickey Leland's plane was found a week after disappearing in Ethiopia. There were no survivors of the 16 passengers.
13-08-1990 Iraq transferred $3-4 billion in bullion, currency, and other goods seized from Kuwait to Baghdad.
13-08-1990 Magic Johnson announced the indefinite postponement of his wedding to fiancé Earletha Kelly.
13-08-1992 Woody Allen began legal action to win custody of his three children. A judge ruled against Allen in 1993.
13-08-1992 A gunmen dressed in military fatigues shot and killed three people and wounded four others before killing himself. The shootings took place in a plant nursery in Watsonville, CA.
13-08-1994 It was reported that aspirin not only helps reduce the risk of heart disease, but also helps prevent colon cancer.
14-08-1248 The rebuilding of the Cologne Cathedral in Cologne, Germany, began after being destroyed by fire.
14-08-1756 Daniel Boone married 16-year-old Rebecca Bryan.
14-08-1805 A peace treaty between the U.S. and Tunis was signed on board the USS Constitution.
14-08-1848 The Oregon Territory was established.
14-08-1873 "Field and Stream" magazine published its first issue.
14-08-1880 The Cologne Cathedral in Cologne, Germany was completed after 632 years of rebuilding.
14-08-1888 A patent for the electric meter was granted to Oliver B. Shallenberger.
14-08-1896 Gold was discovered in Canada's Yukon Territory. Within the next year more than 30,000 people rushed to the area to look for gold.
14-08-1900 An international force, consisting of eight nations, lifted the siege of Peking. It was an end to the Boxer Rebellion, which was aimed at purging China of foreigners.
14-08-1917 China declared war on Germany and Austria during World War I.
14-08-1919 About 1 million tons of ice and rock broke off of a glacier near Mont Blanc, France. Nine people were killed in the incident.
14-08-1935 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed the Social Security Act into law. The act created unemployment insurance and pension plans for the elderly.
14-08-1936 The first basketball competition was held at the Olympic Games in Berlin, Germany. The U.S. defeated Canada, 19-8.
14-08-1941 The U.S. Congress appropriated the funds to construct the Pentagon (approximately $83 million). The building was the new home of the U.S. War Department.
14-08-1941 U.S. President Roosevelt and British Prime Minister Winston Churchill issued the Atlantic Charter. The charter was a statement of principles that renounced aggression.
14-08-1944 The federal government allowed the manufacture of certain domestic appliances to resume on a limited basis.
14-08-1945 It was announced by U.S. President Truman that Japan had surrendered unconditionally. The surrender ended World War II.
14-08-1947 Pakistan became independent from British rule.
14-08-1953 The whiffle ball was invented.
14-08-1959 The first meeting was held to organize the American Football League.
14-08-1962 A U.S. mail truck was held up in Plymouth, MA. The robbers got away with more that $1.5 million dollars.
14-08-1969 British troops arrived in Northern Ireland to intervene in sectarian violence between Protestants and Roman Catholics.
14-08-1973 The U.S. bombing of Cambodia ended. The halt marked the official end to 12 years of combat in Indochina by the U.S.
14-08-1976 A charity softball game began for the Community General Hospital in Monticello, NY. The game was eventually called off due to weather after 30 hours. The final score was Gager's Diner's 491 to Bend 'n Elbow Tavern's 467.
14-08-1980 People for Ethical Treatment of Animals (PETA) was incorporated.
14-08-1981 Pope John Paul II left a Rome hospital. He had been there for three months following an assassination attempt.
14-08-1984 Patricia Ann Reagan and Paul Grilley were married on a movie set in California.
14-08-1986 U.S. officials announced that a U.S. Drug Enforcement agent had been abducted, interrogated and tortured by Mexican police.
14-08-1987 Mark McGwire set the record for major league home runs by a rookie when he connected for his 39th home run of the season.
14-08-1992 The U.S. announced that emergency airlifts of food to Somalia would begin. The action was being taken to stop mass deaths due to starvation.
14-08-1994 International terrorist "Carlos the Jackal" was captured in Sudan. The next day he was extradited to France.
14-08-1995 Shannon Faulkner became the first female cadet in the history of The Citadel, South Carolina's state military college. She quit the school less than a week later.
14-08-1996 In Peru, 35 people were electrocuted when a high extension line was knocked down by a rocket during a fireworks show.
14-08-1997 William Friedkin received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
14-08-1997 Timothy McVeigh was formally sentenced to death for the Oklahoma City bombing.
14-08-1998 A U.S. federal appeals court in Richmond, VA, ruled that the Food and Drug Administration had no authority to regulate tobacco. The FDA had established rules to make it harder for minors to buy cigarettes.
14-08-2000 A Russian submarine Kursk sank to the bottom of the Barrent Sea. There were 118 sailors on the nuclear-powered vessel. All of the crew were pronounced dead on August 22.
14-08-2000 Valujet was ordered to pay $11 million in fines and restitution for hazardous waste violations in the crash that killed 110 people in 1996.
14-08-2000 It was announced that Charles Grodin would be joining CBS' "60 Minutes II" as a commentator.
14-08-2002 In Texas, "Stone Cold" Steve Austin surrendered to San Antonio police. A warrant had been issued for his arrest stemming from a domestic violence complaint on June 15, 2002.
15-08-1057 Macbeth, the King of Scotland, was killed by the son of King Duncan.
15-08-1848 The dental chair was patented by M. Waldo Hanchett.
15-08-1877 Thomas Edison wrote to the president of the Telegraph Company in Pittsburgh, PA. The letter stated that the word, "hello" would be a more appropriate greeting than "ahoy" when answering the telephone.
15-08-1911 The product Crisco was introduced by Procter & Gamble Company.
15-08-1914 The Panama Canal was officially opened to commercial traffic as an American ship sailed from the Atlantic to the Pacific Ocean.
15-08-1918 Diplomatic ties between the U.S. and Russia were severed.
15-08-1935 Will Rogers and Wiley Post were killed in an airplane crash in near Point Barrow, AK.
15-08-1939 "The Wizard of Oz" premiered in Hollywood, CA. Judy Garland became famous for the movie's song "Somewhere Over the Rainbow."
15-08-1943 Because of his special talent to use food scraps in both unusual and appetizing recipes, the U.S. War Department awarded Sgt. Edward Dzuba the Legion of Merit.
15-08-1944 The Allied forces of World War II landed in southern France.
15-08-1945 The Allies proclaimed V-J Day a day after Japan agreed to surrender unconditionally.
15-08-1947 India became independent from Britain and was divided into the countries of India and Pakistan. India had been under British about 200 years.
15-08-1948 The Republic of Korea was proclaimed.
15-08-1948 CBS-TV inaugurated the first nightly news broadcast with anchorman Douglas Edwards.
15-08-1949 In San Francisco, a stunt leap off the Golden Gate Bridge was performed for the first time.
15-08-1961 East German workers began construction of the Berlin Wall.
15-08-1970 Mrs. Pat Palinkas became the first woman to ‘play’ in a pro football game when she held the ball for the Orlando, FL, Panthers.
15-08-1971 U.S. President Nixon announced a 90-day freeze on wages, rents and prices.
15-08-1974 President Park Chung-hee, of South Korea, escaped an assassination attempt. His wife was killed in the attempt.
15-08-1983 Six-month-old Lisa Harap of Queens Village, NY became the youngest identifiable living person to appear on a cover of "TIME" magazine.
15-08-1986 The U.S. Senate approved a package of economic sanctions against South Africa. The ban included the importing of steel, uranium, textiles, coal, and produce from South Africa.
15-08-1987 $100 million in damage was done in the Chicago area when 13 1/2 inches of rain fell.
15-08-1992 Four people were killed and 20 were injured in a shooting spree outside a club in Miami, FL.
15-08-1992 Vietnam blamed Hollywood for creating the "myth" concerning the issue of U.S. servicemen still being held prisoner in Indochina.
15-08-1994 Ilich Ramirez Sanchez was jailed in France. He was the international terrorist known as "Carlos the Jackal."
15-08-1994 The U.S. Social Security Administration became an independent government agency. It had been a part of the Department of Health and Human Services agency.
15-08-1996 Frederick Martin Davidson shot and killed three engineering professors. He was later convicted and sentenced to three life terms in prison.
15-08-1997 The U.S. Justice Department decided not to prosecute FBI officials in connection with the deadly 1992 Ruby Ridge siege in Idaho. The investigation dealt with an alleged cover-up.
15-08-1998 A car bomb in Omagh, Northern Ireland, killed 29 people and injured 370.
15-08-2000 A group of 100 people from North Korea arrived in South Korea for temporary reunions with relatives they had not seen for half a century. Also, a group of 100 South Koreans visited the North.
15-08-2001 Chandra Levy's parents appeared on CNN's "Larry King Live." They discussed Levy's disappearance on April 30, 2001.
15-08-2001 Astronomers announced the discovery of the first solar system outside our own. They had discovered two planets orbiting a star in the Big Dipper.
16-08-1777 During the American Revolutionary War, the Battle of Bennington took place. New England's minutemen routed the British regulars.
16-08-1812 Detroit fell to Indian and British troops in the War of 1812.
16-08-1829 The "Siamese twins," Chang and Eng Bunker, arrived in Boston, MA. They had come to the Western world to be exhibited. They were 18 years old and joined at the waist.
16-08-1858 A telegraphed message from Britain's Queen Victoria to U.S. President Buchanan was transmitted over the recently laid trans-Atlantic cable.
16-08-1861 U.S. President Lincoln prohibited the Union states from trading with the states of the Confederacy.
16-08-1906 1,500 people died in an earthquake in Valparaiso, Chile.
16-08-1920 The only fatality to occur in a major league baseball history happened. Ray Chapman (Cleveland Indians) was hit in the head with a fastball from Carl Mays of the New York Yankees.
16-08-1923 Carnegie Steel Corporation put into place the eight-hour workday for its employees.
16-08-1930 The first British Empire Games were held at Hamilton, Ontario, Canada. The event is now called the British Commonwealth Games.
16-08-1937 Harvard University became the first school to have graduate courses in traffic engineering and administration.
16-08-1948 Babe Ruth died at the age of 53.
16-08-1954 Sports Illustrated was published for the first time. It was claimed that 250,000 subscriptions had been sold before the first issue came off of the presses.
16-08-1954 Jack Paar replaced Walter Cronkite as host of "The Morning Show" on CBS-TV.
16-08-1960 Cyprus was granted independence by Britain.
16-08-1960 The free-fall world record was set by Joseph Kittinger. He fell more than 16 miles (about 84,000 feet) before opening his parachute over New Mexico.
16-08-1978 Xerox was fined for excluding Smith-Corona Mfg. from the copier market. The fine was $25.6 million.
16-08-1984 John DeLorean was acquitted on eight counts of a $24 million dollar cocaine conspiracy indictment.
16-08-1984 The U.S. Jaycees voted to admit women to full membership in the organization.
16-08-1987 156 people were killed when Northwest Airlines Flight 255 crashed. The only survivor was four-year old Cecelia Cichan.
16-08-1993 Harvey Weinstein was rescued from a 14-foot-deep pit by New York Police. He had been there for nearly two weeks while being held for ransom.
16-08-1995 Voters in Bermuda rejected independence from Great Britain.
16-08-1999 In Russia, Vladimir V. Putin was confirmed as prime minister by the lower house of parliament.
17-08-1790 The capital city of the U.S. moved to Philadelphia from New York City.
17-08-1807 Robert Fulton's "North River Steam Boat" (known as the "Clermont") began heading up New York's Hudson River on its successful round-trip to Albany.
17-08-1815 Napoleon began serving his exile when he arrived at the island of St. Helena.
17-08-1835 Solyman Merrick patented the wrench.
17-08-1859 A hot air balloon was used to carry mail for the first time. John Wise left Lafayette, IN for New York City with 100 letters. He had to land after only 27 miles.
17-08-1863 Federal batteries and ships bombarded Fort Sumter in Charleston, SC, harbor during the Civil War.
17-08-1877 F.P. Cahill became the first person to be killed by "Billy the Kid."
17-08-1894 John Wadsworth of Louisville set a major league record when he gave up 28 base hits in a single game.
17-08-1896 The Klondike gold rush was set off by George Carmack discovering gold on Rabbit Creek in Alaska.
17-08-1903 Joseph Pulitzer donated a million dollars to Columbia University. This started the Pulitzer Prizes in his name.
17-08-1915 Charles F. Kettering patented the electric, automobile self-starter.
17-08-1939 The movie "Wizard of Oz" opened.
17-08-1943 The Allied conquest of Sicily was completed as U.S. and British forces entered Messina.
17-08-1945 The nationalists of Indonesia declared their independence from the Netherlands.
17-08-1961 The Communist East German government completed the construction of the Berlin Wall.
17-08-1962 18-year-old Peter Fechter was killed by East German border guards when he attempted to cross the Berlin Wall into the western sector.
17-08-1969 Hurricane Camille hit the Mississippi Gulf Coast killing 248 people.
17-08-1973 Lee Trevino got the first hole in one of his career at the U.S.I. Golf Classic, in Sutton, MA.
17-08-1977 Florists Transworld Delivery (FTD) reported that in one day the number of orders for flowers to be delivered to Graceland had surpassed the number for any other event in the company's history.
17-08-1978 Maxie Anderson, Ben Abruzzo and Larry Newman became the first to land after a successful trans-Atlantic balloon flight. The voyage began in Presque Isle, ME and ended in Miserey, France.
17-08-1985 A year-long strike began when 1,400 Geo. A. Hormel and Co. meat packers walked off the job.
17-08-1987 Rudolph Hess died after apparently committing suicide. Hess was the last member of Adolf Hitler's inner circle.
17-08-1987 Charles Glass, American journalist, escaped his kidnappers and was rescued after being held for 62 days in Lebanon.
17-08-1988 Pakistani President Mohammad Zia ul-Haq and U.S. Ambassador Arnold Raphel were killed in a plane crash.
17-08-1991 At a shopping mall in Strathfield, Australia, a man killed seven people before killing himself. He had been armed with a rifle and a machete.
17-08-1992 Woody Allen admitted to being romantically involved with Soon-Yi Previn. The girl was the adopted daughter of Mia Farrow, Allen's longtime companion.
17-08-1993 Jack Kevorkian was charged in Wayne County, MI with assisting in the suicide of Thomas Hyde. Kevorkian was later acquitted.
17-08-1996 A military cargo plane crashed in Wyoming killing eight crewmembers and a Secret Service employee. The plane was carrying gear for U.S. President Clinton.
17-08-1996 Ross Perot was announced to be the Reform Party's presidential candidate. It was the party's first-ever candidate.
17-08-1998 The FBI announced that it was questioning a suspect concerning the bombings of the U.S. embassies in Tanzania and Kenya on August 7th, 1998.
17-08-1998 U.S. President Clinton admitted to having an improper relationship with Monica Lewinsky, a White House intern.
17-08-1998 NationsBank and BankAmerica merge to create the largest U.S. bank.
17-08-1998 Russia devalued the ruble.
17-08-1999 More than 15,000 people were killed in an earthquake in Turkey.
17-08-2002 In Santa Rosa, CA, the Charles M. Schulz Museum opened to the public.
18-08-1227 The Mongol conqueror Ghengis Khan died.
18-08-1587 Virginia Dare became the first child to be born on American soil of English parents. The colony that is now Roanoke Island, NC, mysteriously vanished.
18-08-1735 The "Evening Post" of Boston, MA, was published for the first time.
18-08-1774 Merriwether Lewis, U.S. explorer was born. He was the leader of the Lewis & Clark Expedition.
18-08-1840 The American Society of Dental Surgeons was founded in New York City, NY.
18-08-1846 Gen. Stephen W. Kearney and his U.S. forces captured Sante Fe, NM.
18-08-1894 The Bureau of Immigration was established by the U.S. Congress.
18-08-1914 The "Proclamation of Neutrality" was issued by U.S. President Woodrow Wilson. It was aimed at keeping the U.S. out of World War I.
18-08-1916 Abraham Lincoln's, the 16th president of the U.S., birthplace was made into a national shrine.
18-08-1919 The "Anti-Cigarette League of America" was formed in Chicago IL.
18-08-1920 Tennessee ratified the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. The Amendment guaranteed the right of all American women to vote.
18-08-1937 The first FM radio construction permit was issued in Boston, MA. The station went on the air two years later.
18-08-1938 The Thousand Islands Bridge was dedicated by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt. The bridge connects the U.S. and Canada.
18-08-1940 Canada and the U.S. established a joint defense plan against the possible enemy attacks during World War II.
18-08-1958 Vladimir Nabokov's novel "Lolita" was published.
18-08-1963 James Meredith graduated from the University of Mississippi. He was the first black man to accomplish this feat.
18-08-1966 The first pictures of earth taken from moon orbit were sent back to the U.S.
18-08-1980 George Brett of the Kansas City Royal's had his batting average reach the .400 mark.
18-08-1981 Herschel Walker of the University of Georgia took out an insurance policy with Lloyd’s of London. The all-American was insured for one million dollars.
18-08-1982 The volume on the New York Stock Exchange topped the 100-million level for the first time at 132.69 million shares traded.
18-08-1982 The longest baseball game played at Wrigley Field in Chicago, IL, went 21 innings before the Los Angeles Dodgers defeated the Cubs 2-1.
18-08-1983 22 people were killed and over $1 billion in damage was caused when hurricane Alicia hit the Texas coast.
18-08-1987 Earl Campbell announced his retirement from the National Football League (NFL).
18-08-1989 Luis Carlos Galan was assassinated outside Bogota, Columbia. Galann was the leading presidential hopeful.
18-08-1990 The first shots were fired by the U.S. in the Persian Gulf Crisis when a U.S. frigate fired rounds across the bow of an Iraqi oil tanker.
18-08-1991 An unsuccessful coup was attempted in against President Mikhail S. Gorbachev. The Soviet hard-liners were responsible. Gorbechev and his family were effectively imprisoned for three days while vacationing in Crimea.
18-08-1992 Larry Bird, after 13 years with the Boston Celtics, announced his retirement.
18-08-1997 Beth Ann Hogan became the first coed in the Virginia Military Institute's 158-year history.
18-08-1997 Patrick Swayze received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
18-08-1998 Sam Bowers', ex-Klansman, fifth trial began. Bowers was being tried again for the 1966 firebombing death of Vernon Dahmer, a civil right activist.
18-08-1998 Mrs. Field's Original Cookies announced that they would acquire the Great American Cookie Co.
18-08-2004 Donald Trump unveiled his board game (TRUMP the Game) where players bid on real estate, buy big ticket items and make billion-dollar business deals.
19-08-1692 Five women and a clergyman were executed after being convicted of witchcraft in Salem, MA.
19-08-1812 "Old Ironsides" (the USS Constitution) won a battle against the British frigate Guerriere east of Nova Scotia.
19-08-1848 The discovery of gold in California was reported by the New York Herald.
19-08-1856 The process of processing condensed milk was patented by Gail Borden.
19-08-1871 Orville Wright was born. Orville and his brother Wilbur were the first people to have a successful sustained and controlled flight of an aircraft with a motor.
19-08-1883 The French dress designer Gabrille "Coco" Chanel was born.
19-08-1909 The first car race to be run on brick occurred at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway.
19-08-1917 Team managers John McGraw and Christy Matthewson were arrested for breaking New York City's blue laws. The crime was their teams were playing baseball on Sunday.
19-08-1919 Afghanistan gained independence from Britain.
19-08-1921 Gene Roddenberry was born in El Paso, Texas. Roddenberry's first career was as an airline pilot. Later, he created the TV series Star Trek.
19-08-1929 "Amos and Andy," the radio comedy program, made its debut on NBC starring Freeman Gosden and Charles Correll.
19-08-1934 Adolf Hitler was approved for sole executive power in Germany as Fuehrer.
19-08-1940 The new Civil Aeronautics Administration awarded honorary license #1 to Orville Wright.
19-08-1942 About 6,000 Canadian and British soldiers launched a raid against the Germans at Dieppe, France. The suffered about 50 percent casualties.
19-08-1955 Severe flooding in the aftermath of Hurricane Diane, in the Northeast United States, claimed 200 lives.
19-08-1960 Francis Gary Powers, an American U-2 pilot, was convicted of espionage in Moscow.
19-08-1960 Two dogs were launched in a satellite into Earth's orbit by the Soviet Union.
19-08-1962 Homero Blancas shot a 55 at the Premier Invitational Golf Tournament held in Longview, TX. It was the lowest score in U.S. competitive golf history.
19-08-1974 During an anti-American protest in Nicosia, Cyprus, U.S. Ambassador Rodger P. Davies was fatally wounded by a bullet while in the American embassy.
19-08-1978 400 people were killed in a theater in Abadan, Iran. Moslem extremist set the fire.
19-08-1980 301 people died in a fire aboard a Saudi Arabian airliner.
19-08-1981 Two Libyan SU-22s were shot down by two U.S. Navy F-14 fighters in the Gulf of Sidra.
19-08-1981 The final episode of "Charlie's Angels was aired on ABC-TV.
19-08-1986 20 people were killed in a car bomb explosion in Tehran. Iran initially accused "American agents", however they later executed an "Iraqi agent."
19-08-1987 David Horowitz, consumer reporter in Burbank, CA, was held at gunpoint while on camera and forced to read the assailants note. The program went off the air while police removed the gunman.
19-08-1987 16 people were killed by gunman Michael Ryan in Britain. It was the countries worst mass killing.
19-08-1991 Soviet hard-liners announced that President Mikhail Gorbachev had been removed from power. Gorbachev returned to power two days later.
19-08-1993 "Cheers" ended an 11-year run on NBC-TV. The show debuted on September 30, 1982.
19-08-1995 Three U.S. diplomats were killed in an accident in their armored vehicle in Sarajevo, Bosnia-Herzegovina.
19-08-1996 A judge sentenced former Arkansas Gov. Jim Guy Tucker to four years probation for his Whitewater crimes.
19-08-1998 Daniel Arizmenid Lopez, known as the "ear lopper", was arrested in Mexico. Lopez was accused of heading a gang of kidnappers that are responsible for 21 kidnappings.
19-08-1998 The first piece of the 351 foot bronze statue of Christopher Columbus arrived in San Juan, Puerto Rico.
19-08-1999 Lorne Michaels received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
19-08-1999 In Belgrade, thousands of Serbs attended a rally to demand the resignation of Yugoslavia's President Slobodan Milosevic.
19-08-2002 A Russian military helicopter crashed after being shot by rebels in Chechnya. 119 people were killed.
19-08-2004 Google Inc. stock began selling on the Nasdaq Stock Market. The initial price was set at $85 and ended the day at $100.34 with more than 22 million shares traded.
20-08-1641 Scotland and Britain signed the Treaty of Pacification.
20-08-1741 Danish navigator Vitus Jonas Bering discovered Alaska.
20-08-1862 Horace Greeley's "The Prayer of Twenty Millions" was published.
20-08-1866 The National Labor Union in the U.S. advocated an eight-hour workday.
20-08-1866 It was formally declared by U.S. President Andrew Johnson that the American Civil War was over. The fighting had stopped months earlier.
20-08-1882 Tchaikovsky's "1812 Overture" debuted in Moscow.
20-08-1885 "The Mikado", by Gilbert and Sullivan, opened at the Fifth Avenue Theatre in New York City.
20-08-1914 German forces occupied Brussels, Belgium, during World War I.
20-08-1918 The British opened its Western Front offensive during World War I.
20-08-1923 The first American dirigible, the "Shenandoah," was launched in Lakehurst, NJ.
20-08-1939 Johnny Weissmuller married Beryl Scott.
20-08-1939 The National Bowling Association was founded in Detroit, MI. It was the first bowling association in the U.S. for African-Americans.
20-08-1940 France fell to the Germans during World War II.
20-08-1945 Tommy Brown of the Brooklyn Dodgers became the youngest player to hit a home run in a major league ball game. Brown was 17 years, 8 months and 14 days old.
20-08-1949 Cleveland’s Indians and Chicago’s White Sox played at Municipal Stadium in Cleveland before the largest crowd, 78,382 people, to see a nighttime major-league baseball game.
20-08-1953 It was announced by the Soviet Union that they had detonated a hydrogen bomb.
20-08-1955 In Morocco and Algeria hundreds of people were killed in anti-French rioting.
20-08-1955 Col. Horace A. Hanes, a U.S. Air Force pilot, flew to an altitude of 40,000 feet. Hanes reached a speed of 822.135 miles per hour in a Super Sabrejet.
20-08-1955 Bo Diddley made his first appearance at the Apollo Theater in New York City.
20-08-1964 A $1 billion anti-poverty measure was signed by U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson.
20-08-1967 The New York Times reported about a noise reduction system for album and tape recording developed by technicians R. and D.W. Dolby. Elektra Record's subsidiary, Checkmate Records became the first label to use the new Dolby process in its recordings.
20-08-1968 The Soviet Union and other Warsaw Pact nations began invading Czechoslovakia to crush the "Prague Spring" liberalization.
20-08-1977 Voyager 2 was launched by the United States. The spacecraft was carrying a 12 inch copper phonograph record containing greetings in dozens of languages, samples of music and sounds of nature.
20-08-1985 The original Xerox 914 copier was presented to the Smithsonian Institute's Museum of American History. Chester Carlson was the man who invented the machine.
20-08-1986 Patrick Henry Sherril, postal employee, killed 14 co-workers in a shooting spree at the post office in Edmon, OK.
20-08-1988 Eight British soldiers were killed by a landmine while in a military bus in Northern Ireland. The mine belonged to the Irish Republican Army.
20-08-1989 Jose and Kitty Menendez were shot to death by their sons Lyle and Erik. The first trials ended in hung juries.
20-08-1989 British conservationist George Adamson was killed by bandits in Kenya. Adamson was 83.
20-08-1989 In London, a pleasure boat sank in the Thames River killing 51 people.
20-08-1991 A rally of more that 100,000 people occurred outside the Russian parliament building to protest the coup that removed Gorbachev from power.
20-08-1995 348 people were killed in a train incident in northern India.
20-08-1997 NATO troops seized six police stations in Banja Luka that had been held by troops controlled by former Bosnian Serb President Radovan Karadzic.
20-08-1997 Britain began voluntary evacuation of its Caribbean island of Montserrat due to the volcanic activity of the Soufriere Hills.
20-08-1998 Canada's Supreme Court announced that Quebec could not secede without the federal government's consent.
20-08-1998 U.S. military forces attacked a terrorist camp in Afghanistan and a chemical plant in Sudan. Both targets were chosen for cruise missile strikes due to their connection with Osama bin Laden.
20-08-1998 The U.N. Security Council extended trade sanctions against Iraq for blocking arms inspections.
21-08-1680 The Pueblo Indians drove the Spanish out and took possession of Santa Fe, NM.
21-08-1831 Nat Turner, a former slave, led a violent insurrection in Virginia. He was later executed.
21-08-1841 A patent for venetian blinds was issued to John Hampton.
21-08-1878 The American Bar Association was formed by a group of lawyers, judges and law professors in Saratoga, NY.
21-08-1888 The adding machine was patented by William Burroughs.
21-08-1912 Arthur R. Eldred became the first American boy to become an Eagle Scout. It is the highest rank in the Boy Scouts of America.
21-08-1923 In Kalamazoo, Michigan, an ordinance was passed forbidding dancers from gazing into the eyes of their partner.
21-08-1929 The Chicago Cardinals traveled out of town for training camp. They were the first professional football team to do this.
21-08-1940 Exiled Communist revolutionary Leon Trotsky died in Mexico City from wounds that had inflicted by an assassin.
21-08-1943 Japan evacuated the Aleutian island of Kiaska. Kiaska had been the last North American foothold held by the Japanese.
21-08-1945 U.S. President Truman ended the Lend-Lease program that had shipped about $50 billion in aid to America's Allies during World War II.
21-08-1959 Hawaii became the 50th state. U.S. President Eisenhower also issued the order for the 50 star flag.
21-08-1963 In South Vietnam, martial law was declared. Army troops and police began to crackdown on the Buddhist anti-government protesters.
21-08-1971 Laura Baugh, at the age of 16, won the United States Women's Amateur Golf tournament. She was the youngest winner in the history of the tournament.
21-08-1983 Philippine politician Benigno Simeon Aquino was assassinated as he deplaned in Manila.
21-08-1984 Victoria Roche, a reserve outfielder, became the first girl to ever compete in a Little League World Series game.
21-08-1984 Clint Eastwood was given a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
21-08-1986 In Cameroon, a nation in West Africa, toxic gas erupted from a volcanic lake. The gas killed more than 1,700 people.
21-08-1987 A U.S. Marine was convicted for spying for the first time. Sergeant Clayton Lonetree was giving secrets to the KGB while working as a guard at the U.S. Embassy in Moscow. He served eight years in a military prison.
21-08-1988 An earthquake on the Nepal-India border killed over 1,000 people.
21-08-1989 Voyager 2, a U.S. space probe, got close to the Neptune moon called Tritan.
21-08-1989 In Columbia, The estates of drug lords were raided in a crackdown that occurred after the killing of a presidential candidate.
21-08-1991 The hard-line coup against Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev ended. The uprising that led to the collapse was led by Russian federation President Boris Yeltsin.
21-08-1992 Randall Weaver, a neo-Nazi leader, opened fire on U.S. marshals from his home in Idaho. Weaver surrendered 11 days later ending the standoff. During the standoff a deputy marshal, Weaver's wife and his son were killed.
21-08-1992 NBC News fired Authur Kent two weeks after he refused an assignment to war-torn Croatia.
21-08-1993 NASA lost contact with the Mars Observer spacecraft. The fate of the spacecraft was unknown. The mission cost $980 million.
21-08-1994 Ernesto Zedillo won the Mexican presidential election.
21-08-1995 In Jerusalem, Israel, a bus bombing by the Islamic Resistance Movement (Hamas) killed four and wounded more than 100.
21-08-1995 Nine people died in a plane crash in Georgia.
21-08-1996 The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 was signed by U.S. President Clinton. The act made it easier to obtain and keep health insurance.
21-08-1997 Hudson Foods Inc. closed a plant in Nebraska after it had recalled 25 million pounds of ground beef that was potentially contaminated with E. coli 01557:H7. It was the largest food recall in U.S. history.
21-08-1997 Afghanistan suspended its embassy operations in the United States.
21-08-1997 Cicely Tyson received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
21-08-1998 Samuel Bowers, a 73-year-old former Ku Klux Klan leader, was convicted in Hattiesburg, MS, of ordering a firebombing that killed civil rights activist Vernon Dahmer in 1966.
21-08-1998 Wesley Snipes received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
21-08-2002 In Pakistan, President Gen. Pervez Musharraf unilaterally amended the Pakistani constitution. He extended his term in office and granted himself powers that included the right to dissolve parliament.
21-08-2003 In Ghana, businessman Gyude Bryant was selected to oversee the two-year power-sharing accord between Liberia's rebels and the government. The accord was planned to guide the country out of 14 years of civil war.
22-08-1485 The War of the Roses ended with the death of England's King Richard III. He was killed in the Battle of Bosworth Field. His successor was Henry V II.
22-08-1567 The "Council of Blood" was established by the Duke of Alba. This was the beginning of his reign of terror in the Netherlands.
22-08-1572 Earl of Northumberland was executed for treason in York, England.
22-08-1582 King James VI was captured in the Ruthven raid while he was hunting. He was held captive until June of 1583.
22-08-1642 The English Civil War began when Charles I called Parliament and its soldiers traitors.
22-08-1762 Ann Franklin became the editor of the Mercury of Newport in Rhode Island. She was the first female editor of an American newspaper.
22-08-1770 Australia was claimed under the British crown when Captain James Cook landed there.
22-08-1775 The American colonies were proclaimed to be in a state of open rebellion by England's King George III.
22-08-1846 The U.S. annexed New Mexico.
22-08-1851 The schooner America outraced the Aurora off the English coast to win a trophy that became known as the America's Cup.
22-08-1865 A patent for liquid soap was received by William Sheppard.
22-08-1902 In Hartford, CT, President Theodore Roosevelt became the first president of the United States to ride in an automobile.
22-08-1906 The Victor Talking Machine Company of Camden, NJ began to manufacture the Victrola. The hand-cranked unit, with horn cabinet, sold for $200.
22-08-1910 Japan formally annexed Korea.
22-08-1911 It was announced that Leonardo da Vinci's "Mona Lisa" had been stolen from the Louvre Museum in Paris. The painting reappeared two years later in Italy.
22-08-1932 The BBC (British Broadcasting Corporation) began its first TV broadcast in England.
22-08-1938 Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers appeared on the cover of "LIFE" magazine.
22-08-1941 Nazi troops reached the outskirts of Leningrad during World War II.
22-08-1950 Althea Gibson became the first black tennis player to be accepted into a national competition.
22-08-1951 75,052 people watched the Harlem Globetrotters perform. It was the largest crowd to see a basketball game.
22-08-1959 Stephen Rockefeller married Anne Marie Rasmussen. Anne had once been a maid for the powerful and wealthy Rockefeller family.
22-08-1968 Pope Paul VI arrived in Bogota, Colombia, for the start of the first papal visit to Latin America.
22-08-1972 Due to its racial policies, Rhodesia was asked to withdraw from the 20th Olympic Summer Games.
22-08-1973 Henry Kissinger was named Secretary of State by U.S. President Nixon. Kissinger won the Nobel Peace Prize in the same year.
22-08-1984 The last Volkswagen Rabbit rolled off the assembly line in New Stanton, PA.
22-08-1985 55 people were killed in a fire aboard a British Airtours charter jet on a runway in England.
22-08-1986 Kerr-McGee Corp. agreed to pay the estate of the late Karen Silkwood $1.38 million to settle a 10-year-old nuclear contamination lawsuit.
22-08-1989 Huey P. Newton, co-founder of the Black Panthers, was shot to death in Oakland, CA. Tyrone Robinson was later convicted and sentenced to 32 years to life in prison for the killing.
22-08-1989 Nolan Ryan became the first major league pitcher to strike out 5000 batters. (MLB)
22-08-1990 U.S. President Bush signed an order for calling reservists to aid in the build up of troops in the Persian Gulf.
22-08-1990 The U.S. State Department announced that the U.S. Embassy in Kuwait would not be closed under President Saddam Hussein's demand.
22-08-1990 Angry smokers blocked a street in Moscow to protest the summer-long cigarette shortage.
22-08-1991 It was announced by Yugoslavia that a truce ordered on August 7th with Croatia had collapsed.
22-08-1991 Mikhail S. Gorbachev returned to Moscow after the collapse of the hard-liners' coup. On the same day he purged the men that had tried to oust him.
22-08-1992 In Rostock, Germany, neo-Nazi violence broke out against foreigners.
22-08-1995 Congressman Mel Reynolds of Illinois was convicted in Chicago of criminal sexual assault, sexual abuse, child pornography and obstruction of justice for having sex with a former campaign worker who had been underage at the time.
22-08-1996 U.S. President Clinton signed legislation that ended guaranteed cash payments to the poor and demanded work from recipients.
22-08-1998 "The Howard Stern Radio Show" premiered on CBS to about 70% of the U.S.
22-08-1998 Mark David Chapman said that he did not want any of the money that would be made from the sale of the signed "Double Fantasy" album that John Lennon signed for him the same day he was killed. Chapman was currently serving sentence for the December 8, 1980 murder.
22-08-2000 It was announced that all 118 crewmembers aboard the Kursk submarine were dead. The Russian vessel had sunk on August 4.
22-08-2004 In Oslo, Norway, a version of Edvard Munch's "The Scream" and his work "Madonna" were stolen from the Munch Museum. This version of "The Scream," one of four different versions, was a tempera painting on board.
23-08-1838 The first class was graduated from Mount Holyoke Female Seminary in South Hadley, MA. It was one of the first colleges for women.
23-08-1839 Hong Kong was taken by the British in a war with China.
23-08-1858 "Ten Nights in a Barroom" opened in New York City at the National Theater. It was a melodrama about the evils of drinking.
23-08-1877 The Texas outlaw Wes Hardin was captured in near Pensacola, FL.
23-08-1892 The printed streetcar transfer was patented by John H. Stedman.
23-08-1902 Fannie Merrit Farmer opened her cooking school, Miss Farmer’s School of Cookery, in Boston, MA.
23-08-1904 Hard D. Weed patented the grip-tread tire chain for cars.
23-08-1914 Tsingtao, China, was bombarded as Japan declared war on Germany in World War I.
23-08-1926 Rudolph Valentino died. He was 31 and had been a silent film star.
23-08-1927 Nicola Sacco and Bartolemeo Vanzetti were executed in Boston, MA, for the murder of two men during a 1920 robbery.
23-08-1939 Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union signed a non-aggression treaty.
23-08-1944 During World War II, Romanian prime minister Ion Antonescue was dismissed. Soon after the country would abandon the Axis and join the Allies.
23-08-1944 Marseilles was captured by Allied troops during World War II.
23-08-1947 Margaret Truman, U.S. President Truman's daughter, gave her first public performance as a singer. The event was at the Hollywood Bowl and had an audience of 15,000.
23-08-1952 The security pact of the Arab League went into effect.
23-08-1959 In the Peanuts comic strip, Sally debuted as an infant.
23-08-1962 The first live TV program was relayed between the U.S. and Europe through the U.S. Telstar satellite.
23-08-1970 U.S. swimmer Gary Hall broke three world records at the AAU (Amateur Athletic Union) outdoor swimming meet, held in Los Angeles, CA.
23-08-1979 Soviet dancer Alexander Godunov defected while the Bolshoi Ballet was on tour in New York City.
23-08-1982 The parliament of Lebanon elected Bashir Bemayel president. He was assassinated three weeks later.
23-08-1982 Gaylord Perry (Seattle Mariners) was tossed out of a game for throwing an illegal spitball.
23-08-1984 South Fork Ranch, the home of the fictitious Ewing clan of the CBS-TV show, "Dallas," was sold. The ranch was to be transformed from a tourist site into a hotel.
23-08-1986 Gennady Zakharov was arrested by the FBI and charged with espionage. Zakharov was a physicist that had been assigned by the United Nations.
23-08-1987 Robert Jarvik and Marilyn Mach vos Savant were married. The event was called the "Union of Great Minds" since Savant had an IQ of 228 and Jarvik was the inventor of the artificial heart.
23-08-1990 President Saddam Hussein appeared on Iraqi state television with a group of Western detainees that he referred to as "guests." He told the group that they were being held "to prevent the scourge of war."
23-08-1992 Hurricane Andrew hit the Bahamas with 120 mile per hour winds.
23-08-1992 An Amtrak passenger train in Wallingford, CT, hit a truck at a crossing. Three people were killed in the accident.
23-08-1993 Larry Nevers and Walter Budzyn were convicted in the beating death of Malice Green. Both former Detroit police officers received prison terms.
23-08-1993 It was confirmed by Los Angeles police that Michael Jackson was the subject of a criminal investigation.
23-08-1996 U.S. President Clinton imposed limits on peddling cigarettes to children.
23-08-1998 Protestors in Sudan carried a sign that bore the resemblance of Monica Lewinsky and the words "No War for Monika." The anti-U.S. demonstration was in Khartoum, Sudan.
23-08-1998 Michael Jones, a 16-year old boy, was shot when he refused to drop a water gun that appeared real to police officers. In New York City it was illegal to carry to possess a toy gun that looks real or is painted black.
23-08-1998 Boris Yeltsin dismissed the Russian government again.
23-08-1998 Kathryn Schoonover was arrested when she was caught stuffing envelopes with cyanide and preparing to send them to people around the U.S.
23-08-1999 Rescuers in Turkey found a young boy that had been buried in rubble from an earthquake for about a week.
23-08-1999 Robert Bogucki was rescued after getting lost in the Great Sandy Desert of Australia on July 11. During the 43 day ordeal Bogucki lost 44 pounds.
23-08-2000 Richard Hatch was revealed as the winning castaway on CBS' "Survivor." Hatch won $1,000,000 for his stay on the island of Pulau Tida in the South China Sea.
23-08-2001 California Congressman Gary Condit gave an interview to ABC's Connie Chung. Condit denied involvement in Chandra Levy's disappearance and avoided directly answering questions about whether they had an affair.
24-08-1979 Mount Vesuvius erupted killing approximately 20,000 people. The cities of Pompeii, Stabiae and Herculaneum were buried in volcanic ash.
24-08-0410 The Visigoths overran Rome. This event symbolized the fall of the Western Roman Empire.
24-08-1456 The printing of the Gutenberg Bible was completed.
24-08-1572 The Catholics began their slaughter of the French Protestants in Paris. The killings claimed about 70,000 people.
24-08-1680 Colonel Thomas Blood died. He was the Irish adventurer that had stolen the Crown Jewels from the Tower of London in 1671.
24-08-1814 Washington, DC, was invaded by British forces that set fire to the White House and Capitol.
24-08-1853 The first convention of the American Pharmaceutical Association was held.
24-08-1867 Johns Hopkins died. The railroad millionaire left $7.5 million in his will for the founding of a new medical school in his name.
24-08-1869 A patent for the waffle iron was received by Cornelius Swarthout.
24-08-1880 Joshua Lionel Cowen was born. He was the inventor of the toy electric train.
24-08-1891 Thomas Edison applied patents for the kinetoscope and kinetograph (U.S. Pats. 493,426 and 589,168).
24-08-1912 A four-pound limit was set for parcels sent through the U.S. Post Office mail system.
24-08-1932 Amelia Earhart became the first woman to fly across the U.S. non-stop. The trip from Los Angeles, CA to Newark, NJ, took about 19 hours.
24-08-1939 The leader of "Murder, Incorporated", Louis "Lepke" Buchalter, gave himself up to columnist Walter Winchell. Winchell then turned him over to FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover.
24-08-1949 The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) went into effect. The agreement was that an attack against on one of the parties would be considered "an attack against them all."
24-08-1954 The Communist Party was virtually outlawed in the U.S. when the Communist Control Act went into effect.
24-08-1959 Three days after Hawaiian statehood, Hiram L. Fong was sworn in as the first Chinese-American U.S. senator while Daniel K. Inouye was sworn in as the first Japanese-American U.S. representative.
24-08-1963 John Pennel pole-vaulted 17 feet and 3/4 inches becoming the first to break the 17-foot barrier.
24-08-1968 France became the 5th thermonuclear power when they exploded a hydrogen bomb in the South Pacific.
24-08-1970 A bomb went off at the University of Wisconsin's Army Math Research Center in Madison, WI. The bomb that killed Robert Fassnacht was set by anti-war extremists.
24-08-1975 Davey Lopes of the Los Angeles Dodgers set a major league baseball record when he stole his 38th consecutive base.
24-08-1981 Mark David Chapman was sentenced to 20 years to life in prison for the murder of John Lennon.
24-08-1985 27 anti-apartheid leaders were arrested in South Africa as racial violence rocked the country.
24-08-1986 Frontier Airlines shut down. Thousands of people were left stranded.
24-08-1987 Sergeant Clayton Lonetree was sentenced to 30 years in prison by a military jury for giving U.S. secrets to the Soviet Union.
24-08-1989 Pete Rose, the manager of the Cincinnati Reds, was banned from baseball for life after being accused of gambling on baseball.
24-08-1989 "Total war" was declared by Columbian drug lords on their government.
24-08-1989 The U.S. space probe, Voyager 2, sent back photographs of Neptune.
24-08-1990 Iraqi troops surrounded foreign missions in Kuwait.
24-08-1990 Irish hostage Brian Keenan was released. He had been held in Lebanon for 1,597 days.
24-08-1991 Russian President Mikhail Gorbachev resigned as the head of the Communist Party.
24-08-1992 Hurricane Andrew hit southern Florida causing 55 deaths in the Bahamas, Florida, and Louisiana.
24-08-1992 China and South Korea established diplomatic relations.
24-08-1995 Harry Wu, human rights activist, was expelled by China after he was convicted of spying.
24-08-1995 Microsoft's "Windows 95" went on sale.
24-08-1998 U.S. officials cited a soil sample as part of the evidence that a Sudan plant was producing precursors to the VX nerve gas. And, therefore made it a target for U.S. missiles on August 20, 1998.
24-08-1998 A donation of 24 beads was made, from three parties, to the Indian Museum of North America at the Crazy Horse Memorial. The beads are said to be those that were used in 1626 to buy Manhattan from the Indians.
24-08-1998 The U.S. and Britain agreed on the Netherlands as site for the trial of two Libyan suspects for the 1988 bombing of Pan Am Flight 103.
24-08-2001 In McAllen, TX, Bridgestone/Firestone agreed to settle out of court and pay a reported $7.5 million to a family in a rollover accident in their Ford Explorer.
24-08-2001 The remains of nine American servicemen killed in the Korean War were returned to the U.S. The bodies were found about 60 miles north of Pyongyang. It was estimated that it would be a year before the identies of the soldiers would be known.
24-08-2001 U.S. District Judge Colleen Kollar-Kotelly was randomly picked to take over the Microsoft monopoly case. The judge was to decide how Microsoft should be punished for illegally trying to squelch its competitors.
24-08-2001 NASA announced that operation of the Upper Atmosphere Research Satellite would end by September 30th due to budget restrictions. Though the satellite is best known for monitoring a hole in the ozone layer over Antarctica, it was designed to provide information about the upper atmosphere by measuring its winds, temperatures, chemistry and energy received from the sun.
24-08-2004 Salim Ahmed Hamdan was formally charged in the first U.S. military tribunal since World War II. Hamdan, Osama bin Laden's former chauffer, was charged with conspiracy as an al-Qaida member to commit war crimes, including murder.
24-08-2005 The planet Pluto was reclassified as a "dwarf planet" by the International Astronomical Union (IAU). Pluto's status was changed due to the IAU's new rules for an object qualifying as a planet. Pluto met two of the three rules because it orbits the sun and is large enough to assume a nearly round shape. However, since Pluto has an oblong orbit and overlaps the orbit of Neptune it disqualified Pluto as a planet.
25-08-1718 Hundreds of colonists from France arrived in Louisiana. Some settled in present-day New Orleans.
25-08-1814 The U.S. Library of Congress was destroyed by British forces.
25-08-1825 Uruguay declared independence from Brazil.
25-08-1840 Joseph Gibbons received a patent for the seeding machine.
25-08-1875 Captain Matthew Webb swam from Dover, England, to Calais, France making him the first person to swim the English Channel. The feat took about 22 hours.
25-08-1902 "Al-Hoda" began publication in New York City making it the first Arabic daily newspaper in the U.S.
25-08-1916 The National Park Service was established as part of the U.S. Department of the Interior.
25-08-1920 Ethelda Bleibtrey won the 100-meter freestyle swimming competition in Antwerp, Belgium. She was the first woman to win an Olympic competition for the U.S.
25-08-1920 The first airplane to fly from New York to Alaska arrived in Nome.
25-08-1921 The U.S. signed a peace treaty with Germany.
25-08-1940 Arno Rudolphi and Ann Hayward were married while suspended in parachutes at the World’s Fair in New York City.
25-08-1941 Soviet and British troops invaded Iran. This was in reaction to the Shah's refusal to reduce the number of German residents.
25-08-1941 Allied forces invaded Iran. Within four days the Soviet Union and England controlled Iran.
25-08-1941 U.S. President Roosevelt signed the bill appropriating funds for construction of the Pentagon.
25-08-1944 Paris, France, was liberated by Allied forces ending four years of German occupation.
25-08-1944 Romania declared war on Germany.
25-08-1946 Ben Hogan won the PGA in Portland, OR. It was his first major golf title.
25-08-1949 NBC Radio debuted "Father Knows Best." The show went to TV in 1954.
25-08-1950 U.S. President Truman ordered the seizure of U.S. railroads to avert a strike.
25-08-1967 American Nazi leader George Lincoln Rockwell was assassinated by a sniper.
25-08-1972 In Great Britain, computerized axial tomography (CAT scan) was introduced.
25-08-1978 The Turin shroud believed to be the burial cloth of Jesus Christ went on display for the first time in 45 years.
25-08-1981 The U.S. Voyager 2 sent back pictures and data about Saturn. The craft came within 63,000 miles of the planet.
25-08-1985 Samantha Smith was killed with her father in an airplane crash in Maine. Smith was the schoolgirl whose letter to Yuri V. Andropov resulted in her famous peace tour of the Soviet Union.
25-08-1987 Saudi Arabia denounced the "group of terrorists" that ran the Iranian government.
25-08-1988 Iran and Iraq began talks in Geneva after ending their eight years of war.
25-08-1990 Military action was authorized by the United Nations to enforce the trade embargo that had been placed on Iraq after their invasion of Kuwait.
25-08-1991 Byelorussia declared independence from the Soviet Union.
25-08-1992 It was reported by researchers that cigarette smoking significantly increased the risk of developing cataracts.
25-08-1993 Amy Biehl was killed in South Africa by a mob.
25-08-1993 Sheik Omar Abdel Rahman was indicted by a federal grand jury for terrorist activities, one of which was the World Trade Center bombing.
25-08-1993 The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at 3,652.09, an all-time high.
25-08-1995 Harry Wu, human rights activist, returned to the United States. He said the spying case against him in China was "all lies."
25-08-1997 The tobacco industry agreed to an $11.3 billion settlement with the state of Florida.
25-08-1998 A survey released said that 1/3 of Americans use the Internet.
25-08-1998 Gary Coleman plead innocent to the charge that he hit a woman in a mall after she had sought his autograph. Coleman was working at the mall as a security guard.
25-08-1998 A High Court judge sentenced 16 civilians to be hanged for their role in a coup in Sierra Leone in May of 1998. The restored government had treason cases against 40 more civilians and 38 soldiers.
25-08-1998 Seven Cuban-Americans were indicted by federal grand jury in Puerto Rico on charges of conspiracy to murder Cuban President Fidel Castro.
26-08-1498 Michelangelo was commissioned to make the "Pieta."
26-08-1743 Antoine Lavoisier was born. He was the chemist that proved that the union of oxygen and other chemicals is used in burning, rusting of metals and breathing.
26-08-1842 The first fiscal year was established by the U.S. Congress to start on July 1st.
26-08-1847 Liberia was proclaimed as an independent republic.
26-08-1873 Dr. Lee DeForest was born. He was the inventor of the Audion tube. The tube makes the broadcasting of sound possible.
26-08-1873 The school board of St. Louis, MO, authorized the first U.S. public kindergarten.
26-08-1883 A two-day eruption of the volcanic island Krakatoa began. The tidal waves that were associated with the eruption killed 36,000 people when they destroyed the island.
26-08-1896 In the Philippines, and insurrection began against the Spanish government.
26-08-1920 The 19th amendment to the U.S. Constitution went into effect. The amendment prohibited discrimination on the basis of sex in the voting booth.
26-08-1934 Adolf Hitler demanded that France turn over their Saar region to Germany.
26-08-1937 All Chinese shipping was blockaded by Japan.
26-08-1939 The first televised major league baseball games were shown. The event was a double-header between the Cincinnati Reds and the Brooklyn Dodgers.
26-08-1939 The radio program, "Arch Oboler’s Plays", presented the NBC Symphony for the first time.
26-08-1945 The Japanese were given surrender instructions on the U.S. battleship Missouri at the end of World War II.
26-08-1947 Don Bankhead became the first black pitcher in major league baseball.
26-08-1957 It was announced that an intercontinental ballistic missile was successfully tested by the Soviet Union.
26-08-1957 The first Edsel made by the Ford Motor Company rolled out.
26-08-1961 The International Hockey Hall of Fame in Toronto opened.
26-08-1974 Charles Lindberg died at the age of 72.
26-08-1973 A U.S. Presidential Proclamation was declared that made August 26th Women's Equality Day.
26-08-1978 Sigmund Jahn blasted off aboard the Russian Soyuz 31 and became the first German in space.
26-08-1986 Jennifer Levin was found strangled in New York City's Central Park. In the case called the "preppie murder" case Robert Chambers eventually plead guilty.
26-08-1987 The Fuller Brush Company announced plans to open two retail stores in Dallas, TX. The company that had sold its products door to door for 81 years.
26-08-1990 In Gainesville, FL, two slain college students were found in their apartment. Three more bodies would be found in the few days that followed.
26-08-1990 The 55 Americans at the U.S. Embassy in Kuwait left Baghdad by car and headed for the Turkish border.
26-08-1991 Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev promised that national elections would be held.
26-08-1992 A mistrial was declared in the Iran-Contra cover-up trial of CIA spy Clair George.
26-08-1992 A "no-fly zone" was imposed on the southern 1/3 of Iraq. The move by the U.S., France and Britain was aimed at protecting Iraqi Shiite Muslims.
26-08-1993 Dorothea Puente was convicted of murdering three people that had been tenants in her boarding house. She was sentenced to life without parole.
26-08-1996 Barbara Jewell asked U.S. President Clinton to clear her son's name in connection with the Centennial Olympic Park bombing. Richard Jewell was later cleared by the Justice Department.
26-08-1996 Rober Vesco, a U.S. financier, was convicted in a Cuban court of economic crimes.
26-08-1996 Chun Doo-hwan, the former military leader of South Korea, was sentenced to death. His crimes were mutiny, treason and embezzlement.
26-08-1998 The U.S. government announced that they were investigating Microsoft in an attempt to discover if they "bullied" Intel into delaying new technology.
26-08-1998 U.S. Attorney General Janet Reno ordered a review of the assassination of Martin Luther King, Jr.
26-08-1998 Sudan filed a criminal lawsuit against U.S. President Clinton and the United States for the bombing of the El-Shifa Pharmaceutical Industries Company. The Sudanese claimed that the plant was strictly civilian.
27-08-1660 The books of John Milton were burned in London due to his attacks on King Charles II.
27-08-1789 The Declaration of the Rights of Man was adopted by the French National Assembly.
27-08-1828 Uruguay was formally proclaimed to be independent during preliminary talks between Brazil and Argentina.
27-08-1858 The first cabled news dispatch was sent and was published by "The New York Sun" newspaper. The story was about the peace demands of England and France being met by China.
27-08-1859 The first oil well was successfully drilled in the U.S. by Colonel Edwin L. Drake near Titusville, PA.
27-08-1889 Charles G. Conn received a patent for the metal clarinet.
27-08-1889 Boxer Jack Dempsey was defeated for the first time of his career by George LaBlanche.
27-08-1892 The original Metropolitan Opera House in New York was seriously damaged by fire.
27-08-1894 The Wilson-Gorman Tariff Act was passed by the U.S. Congress. The provision within for a graduated income tax was later struck down by the U.S. Supreme Court.
27-08-1912 Edgar Rice Burroughs' "Tarzan of the Apes" was published for the first time.
27-08-1921 The owner of Acme Packing Company bought a pro football team for Green Bay, WI. J.E. Clair paid tribute to those who worked in his plant by naming the team the Green Bay Packers. (NFL)
27-08-1928 The Kellogg-Briand Pact was signed by 15 countries in Paris. Later, 47 other nations would sign the pact.
