27-08-1938 Robert Frost, in a fit of jealousy, set fire to some papers to disrupt a poetry recital by another poet, Archibald MacLeish.
27-08-1939 Nazi Germany demanded the Polish corridor and Danzig.
27-08-1945 American troops landed in Japan after the surrender of the Japanese government at the end of World War II.
27-08-1962 Mariner 2 was launched by the United States. In December of the same year the spacecraft flew past Venus. It was the first space probe to reach the vicinity of another planet.
27-08-1972 North Vietnam's major port at Haiphong saw the first bombings from U.S. warplanes.
27-08-1979 Lord Louis Mountbatten was killed in a boat explosion off the coast of Ireland. The Irish Republican Army claimed responsibility.
27-08-1981 Work began on recovering a safe from the Andrea Doria. The Andrea Doria was a luxury liner that had sank in 1956 in the waters off of Massachusetts.
27-08-1984 U.S. President Ronald Reagan announced that the first citizen to go into space would be a teacher. The teacher that was eventually chosen was Christa McAuliffe. She died in the Challenger disaster on January 28, 1986.
27-08-1984 Diane Sawyer became the fifth reporter on CBS-TV's "60 Minutes."
27-08-1984 The Menetta Lane Theatre in Greenwich Village opened. It was the first new off-Broadway theater to be built in 50 years in New York City.
27-08-1985 The Space Shuttle Discovery left for a seven-day mission in which three satellites were launched and another was repaired and redeployed.
27-08-1986 Nolan Ryan, while with the Houston Astros, earned his 250th career win against the Chicago Cubs.
27-08-1989 The first U.S. commercial satellite rocket was launched. A British communications satellite was onboard.
27-08-1990 52 Americans reached Turkey after leaving Iraq. Three young American men were detained by the Iraqis.
27-08-1990 The U.S. State Department ordered the expulsion of 36 Iraqi diplomats.
27-08-1991 The Soviet republic of Moldavia declared its independence.
27-08-1992 Federal troops were ordered to Florida for emergency relief due to Hurricane Andrew.
27-08-1996 California Governor Pete Wilson signed an order that would halt state benefits to illegal immigrants.
27-08-1998 In New York city, Mohamed Rashed Daoud Al-'Owhali appeared in a U.S. Federal Court to face charges of bombing attacks at the U.S. embassies in Kenya and Tanzania. He was one of two suspects released to the U.S. by Kenya.
27-08-1998 In a Florida boot camp for teens, two boys killed a counselor and used his car to escape. The boys, 16 and 17 years old, would be tried as adults for the pickax murder.
27-08-1998 James Brolin received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
27-08-1999 The final crew of the Russian space station Mir departed the station to return to Earth. Russia was forced to abandon Mir for financial reasons.
27-08-2001 The U.S. military announced that an Air Force RQ-1B "Predator" aircraft was lost over Iraq. It was reported that the unmanned aircraft "may have crashed or been shot down."
27-08-2001 A complaint was filed against California Congressman Gary Condit and two others for their efforts to obstruct justice in the disappearance of intern Chandra Levy. Condit was accused of conspiring to secure Anne Marie Smith's silence about an affair in their past.
27-08-2001 Work began on the future site of a World War II memorial on the U.S. capital's historic national Mall. The site is between the Washington Monument and the Lincoln Memorial.
28-08-1609 Delaware Bay was discovered by Henry Hudson.
28-08-1619 Ferdinand II was elected Holy Roman Emperor. His policy of "One church, one king" was his way of trying to outlaw Protestantism.
28-08-1774 The first American-born saint was born in New York City. Mother Elizabeth Ann Seton was canonized in 1975.
28-08-1811 Percy Bysshe Shelley and Harriet Westbrook eloped.
28-08-1830 "The Tom Thumb" was demonstrated in Baltimore, MD. It was the first passenger-carrying train of its kind to be built in America.
28-08-1833 Slavery was banned by the British Parliament throughout the British Empire.
28-08-1907 "American Messenger Company" was started by two teenagers, Jim Casey and Claude Ryan. The companies name was later changed to "United Parcel Service."
28-08-1916 Italy's declaration of war against Germany took effect during World War I.
28-08-1917 Ten suffragists were arrested as they picketed the White House.
28-08-1922 The first radio commercial aired on WEAF in New York City. The Queensboro Realty Company bought 10 minutes of time for $100.
28-08-1922 The Walker Cup was held for the first time at Southampton, NY. It is the oldest international team golf match in America.
28-08-1939 The first successful flight of a jet-propelled airplane took place. The plane was a German Heinkel He 178.
28-08-1941 The Football Writers Association of America was organized.
28-08-1947 Manolete was mortally wounded by a bull during a fight in Linares, Spain. He died the following day at age 30.
28-08-1955 Emmett Till was abducted from his uncle's home in Mississippi. Two white men had brutally murdered the black teen-ager after he supposedly whistled at a white woman.
28-08-1963 Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., gave his "I Have a Dream" speech at a civil rights rally in Washington, DC. More than 200,000 people attended.
28-08-1972 Mark Spitz captured the first of his seven gold medals at the Summer Olympics in Munich, Germany. He set a world record when he completed the 200-meter butterfly in 2 minutes and 7/10ths of a second.
28-08-1973 An earthquake hit an area southwest of Mexico City killing 520 people and injuring 1,000 more.
28-08-1981 John Hinckley, Jr. pled innocent to the charge of attempting to kill U.S. President Ronald Reagan. Hinckley was later acquitted by reason of insanity.
28-08-1981 "The New York Daily News" published its final afternoon edition.
28-08-1986 Jerry Whitworth, a retired Navy warrant officer, was convicted for his role in a Soviet spy ring. He was sentenced to 365 years in prison and fined $410,000.
28-08-1988 At an air show in Ramstein, West Germany, an Italian Air Force jet collided with 2 other jets and then plunged into a crowd. 70 people were killed.
28-08-1988 An unsuccessful coup attempt in the Philippines resulted in the death of 50 people. The coup was against President Corazon Aquino.
28-08-1989 Jim Bakker's fraud and conspiracy trial opened.
28-08-1990 Iraq declared Kuwait to be its 19th province and renamed Kuwait City al-Kadhima.
28-08-1990 27 people were killed and 350 injured when a tornado struck in Will County in Chicago.
28-08-1990 Two college students were found and believed to be the fourth and fifth victims in an apparent serial killing near the University of Florida at Gainesville.
28-08-1991 A subway operator in New York was charged with manslaughter after his train derailed, killing 5 people and injuring 133.
28-08-1994 A DEA plane crashed in Peru killing 5 U.S. agents.
28-08-1995 The biggest bank in the U.S. was created when Chase Manhattan and Chemical Bank announced their $10 billion deal.
28-08-1995 A mortar shell killed 38 people in Sarajevo, Bosnia-Herzegovina. The act triggered NATO airstrikes against the Bosnian Serbs.
28-08-1996 A divorce decree was issued for Britain's Charles and Princess Diana. This was the official end to the 15-year marriage.
28-08-1997 In Algeria, nearly 300 people were killed in a single late-night incident between the government and Islamic militants.
28-08-1998 The Pakistani prime minister created new Islamic order and legal system based on the Koran.
28-08-2004 George Brunstad, at age 70, became the oldest person to swim the English Channel. The swim from Dover, England, to Sangatte, France, took 15 hours and 59 minutes.
29-08-1533 Atahualpa, the last Incan King of Peru, was murdered on orders from Spanish conqueror Francisco Pizarro. The Inca Empire died with him.
29-08-1828 A patent was issued to Robert Turner for the self-regulating wagon brake.
29-08-1833 The "Factory Act" was passed in England to settle child labor laws.
29-08-1842 The Treaty of Nanking was signed by the British and the Chinese. The treaty ended the first Opium War and gave the island of Honk Kong to Britain.
29-08-1885 The first prizefight under the Marquis of Queensberry Rules was held in Cincinnati, OH. John L. Sullivan defeated Dominick McCaffery in six rounds.
29-08-1886 In New York City, Chinese Ambassador Li Hung-chang's chef invented chop suey.
29-08-1892 Pop (Billy) Shriver (Chicago Cubs) caught a ball that was dropped from the top of the Washington Monument in Washington, DC.
29-08-1907 The Quebec Bridge collapsed killing 75 workers. The bridge was being built across the St. Lawrence River above Quebec City.
29-08-1944 During the continuing celebration of the liberation of France from the Nazis, 15,000 American troops marched down the Champs Elysees in Paris.
29-08-1945 U.S. General Douglas MacArthur left for Japan to officially accept the surrender of the Japanese.
29-08-1949 At the University of Illinois, a nuclear device was used for the first time to treat cancer patients.
29-08-1957 Senator Strom Thurmond of South Carolina set a filibuster record in the U.S. when he spoke for 24 hours and 18 minutes.
29-08-1965 Gemini 5, carrying astronauts Gordon Cooper and Charles ("Pete") Conrad, splashed down in the Atlantic Ocean after eight days in space.
29-08-1966 Mia Farrow withdrew from the cast of the ABC-TV's "Peyton Place."
29-08-1967 The final episode of "The Fugitive" aired.
29-08-1971 Hank Aaron became the first baseball player in the National League to hit 100 or more runs in each of 11 seasons.
29-08-1973 U.S. President Nixon was ordered by Judge John Sirica to turn over the Watergate tapes. Nixon refused and appealed the order.
29-08-1977 Lou Brock brought his total of stolen bases to 893. The record he beat was held by Ty Cobb for 49 years.
29-08-1983 Two U.S. marines were killed in Lebanon by the militia group Amal when they fired mortar shells at the Beirut airport.
29-08-1983 The anchor of the USS Monitor, from the U.S. Civil War, was retrieved by divers.
29-08-1984 A B-1 bomber prototype crashed in the Mojave Desert killing one crew member and injuring two others.
29-08-1989 Seven bombs exploded in Medillin and Bogota, Columbia. Police blamed drug traffickers.
29-08-1990 Iraqi President Saddam Hussein, in a television interview, declared that America could not defeat Iraq.
29-08-1991 The Communist Party in the Soviet Union had its bank accounts frozen and activities were suspended because of the Party's role in the failed coup attempt against Mikhail Gorbachev.
29-08-1991 The republics of Russia and Ukraine signed an agreement to stay in the Soviet Union.
29-08-1992 The U.N. Security Council agreed to send troops to Somalia to guard the shipments of food.
29-08-1994 Mario Lemieux announced that he would be taking a medical leave of absence due to fatigue, an aftereffect of his 1993 radiation treatments. He would sit out the National Hockey Leagues (NHL) 1994-95 season.
29-08-1995 The Eduard Shevardnadze, the Georgian leader, survived an attempt on his life. The attempt was made in the form of a car bomb that exploded near his motorcade.
29-08-1995 At the O.J. Simpson trial, tapes of Mark Fuhrman were played. The recordings were of Fuhrman making racial comments.
29-08-1997 Hooded men killed more than 300 people in an Algerian farm village in the worst carnage since an Islamic insurgency began.
29-08-1998 Northwest Airlines pilots went on strike after their union rejected a last-minute company offer.
29-08-2001 In Dallas, TX, George Rivas was sentenced to death for the murder of a police office during a robbery. Rivas was the leader of a group of prison escapees referred to as the Texas 7.
29-08-2004 India test-launched a nuclear-capable missle able to carry a one-ton warhead. The weapon had a range of 1,560 miles.
30-08-1146 European leaders outlawed the crossbow.
30-08-1645 American Indians and the Dutch made a peace treaty at New Amsterdam. New Amsterdam later became known as New York.
30-08-1682 William Penn sailed from England and later established the colony of Pennsylvania in America.
30-08-1780 General Benedict Arnold secretly promised to surrender the West Point fort to the British army.
30-08-1806 New York City's second daily newspaper, the "Daily Advertiser," was published for the last time.
30-08-1862 The Confederates defeated Union forces at the second Battle of Bull Run in Manassas, VA.
30-08-1905 Ty Cobb made his major league batting debut with the Detroit Tigers.
30-08-1918 Fanny Dora Kaplan fired three shots at Vladimir Ilyich Lenin in an assassination attempt.
30-08-1928 The Independence of India League was established in India.
30-08-1941 During World War II, the Nazis severed the last railroad link between Leningrad and the rest of the Soviet Union.
30-08-1945 General Douglas MacArthur set up Allied occupation headquarters in Japan.
30-08-1951 The Philippines and the United States signed a defense pact.
30-08-1960 A partial blockade was imposed on West Berlin by East Germany.
30-08-1963 The "Hotline" between Moscow and Washington, DC, went into operation.
30-08-1965 Thurgood Marshall was confirmed by the U.S. Senate as a Supreme Court justice. Marshall was the first black justice to sit on the Supreme Court.
30-08-1979 Hurricane David hit the Caribbean island of Dominica. The hurricane took 1,100 lives in its journey through the Caribbean and the eastern U.S. seaboard.
30-08-1982 P.L.O. leader Yasir Arafat left Beirut for Greece.
30-08-1983 The space shuttle Challenger blasted off with Guion S. Bluford Jr. aboard. He was the first black American to travel in space.
30-08-1984 The space shuttle Discovery lifted off for the first time. On the voyage three communications satellites were deployed.
30-08-1984 U.S. President Ronald Reagan, and several others, were inducted into the Sportscasters Hall of Fame.
30-08-1989 Leona Helmsley was found guilty of income tax evasion by a New York federal jury.
30-08-1991 The Soviet republic of Azerbaijan declared its independence.
30-08-1992 15 people were killed and 31 injured in a Sarajevo market when an artillery shell exploded.
30-08-1993 On CBS-TV "The Late Show with David Letterman" premiered.
30-08-1994 Rosa Parks was robbed and beaten by Joseph Skipper. Parks was known for her refusal to give up her seat on a bus in 1955, which sparked the civil rights movement.
30-08-1994 The largest U.S. defense contractor was created when the Lockheed and Martin Marietta corporations agreed to a merger.
30-08-1996 An expedition to raise part of the Titanic failed when the nylon lines being used to raise part of the hull snapped.
30-08-1999 The residents of East Timor overwhelmingly voted for independence from Indonesia. The U.N. announced the result on September 4.
31-08-1823 Ferdinand VII was restored to the throne of Spain when invited French forces entered Cadiz. The event is known as the Battle of Trocadero.
31-08-1852 The first pre-stamped envelopes were created with legislation of the U.S. Congress.
31-08-1881 The first tennis championships in the U.S. were played.
31-08-1886 110 people were killed when an earthquake struck Charleston, SC.
31-08-1887 The kinetoscope was patented by Thomas Edison. The device was used to produce moving pictures.
31-08-1888 Mary Ann "Polly" Nicholls was found murdered in London. The murder is generally accepted as the first "Jack the Ripper" crime.
31-08-1920 The first news program to be broadcast on radio was aired. The station was 8MK in Detroit, MI.
31-08-1935 The act of exporting U.S. arms to belligerents was prohibited by an act signed by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt.
31-08-1940 Lawrence Olivier and Vivian Leigh were married.
31-08-1941 The radio program "The Great Gildersleeve" made its debut on NBC.
31-08-1946 Superman returned to radio on the Mutual Broadcasting System after being dropped earlier in the year.
31-08-1950 Gil Hodges of the Brooklyn Dodgers hit four home runs in a single game off of four different pitchers.
31-08-1954 70 people were killed when Hurricane Carol hit the northeastern coast of the U.S.
31-08-1959 Sandy Koufax set a National League record by striking out 18 batters.
31-08-1962 The Caribbean nations Tobago and Trinidad became independent within the British Commonwealth.
31-08-1964 California officially became the most populated state in America.
31-08-1965 The Department of Housing and Urban Development was created by the U.S. House of Representatives and the Senate.
31-08-1969 The boxer Rocky Marciano died in an airplane crash in Iowa.
31-08-1980 Poland's Solidarity labor movement was born with an agreement signed in Gdansk that ended a 17-day strike.
31-08-1981 The 30-year contract between Milton Berle and NBC-TV expired.
31-08-1985 The "Night Stalker" killer, Richard Ramirez, was captured by residents in Los Angeles, CA.
31-08-1986 82 people were killed when a small private plane collided with a Aeromexico DC-9 over Cerritos, CA.
31-08-1986 The Admiral Nakhimov, a Soviet passenger ship, collided with a merchant vessel in the Black Sea. 448 people were killed when both ships sank.
31-08-1988 A Delta Boeing 727 crashed during takeoff at Dallas-Fort Worth International Airport in Texas. Fourteen people were killed in the accident that was later blamed on the crew's failure to set the wing flaps in their proper position.
31-08-1989 Jim Bakker had an apparent breakdown in his attorney's office. This interrupted the fraud and conspiracy trial the PTL founder was undergoing.
31-08-1989 Great Britain's Princess Anne and Mark Phillips announced that they were separating. The marriage was 16 years old.
31-08-1990 U.N. Secretary-General Javier Perez de Cuellar met with the Iraqi Foreign Minister Tariq Aziz to try and negotiate a solution to the crisis in the Persian Gulf.
31-08-1990 East and West Germany signed a treaty that meant the harmonizing of political and legal systems.
31-08-1991 Uzbekistan and Kirghiziz declared their independence from the Soviet Union. They were the 9th and 10th republics to announce their plans to secede.
31-08-1991 In a "Solidarity Day" protest hundreds of thousands of union members marched in Washington, DC.
31-08-1992 Randy Weaver, a white separatist, surrendered to authorities after an 11 day siege at his cabin in Naples, ID.
31-08-1993 Russia withdrew its last soldiers from Lithuania.
31-08-1994 A cease-fire was declared by the Irish Republican Army after 25 years of bloodshed in Northern Ireland.
31-08-1994 Russia officially ended its military presence in the former East Germany and the Baltics after a half-century.
31-08-1995 Judge Lance Ito ruled that only two tapes of racist comments by Mark Fuhrman could be played in the trial of O.J. Simpson.
31-08-1996 Nadine Lockwoods body was found in her family's apartment by New York City police. The four-year-old girl had been starved to death.
31-08-1997 Princess Diana of Wales died at age 36 in a car crash in Paris. Her companion, Dodi Fayed, and their chauffeur were also killed.
31-08-1998 A ballistic missile was fired over Japan by North Korea. The missile landed in stages in the waters around Japan. There was no known target.
31-08-1998 U.S. embassies in Ghana and Togo were closed indefinitely because of security threats.
31-08-1998 An explosion in a market in Algiers, Algeria killed at least 17 and wounded approximately 60.
31-08-1998 "Titanic" became the first movie in North America to earn more than $600 million.
31-08-1999 At least 69 people were killed when a Boeing 737 crashed just after take off in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
01-12-1835 Hans Christian Andersen published his first book of fairy tales.
01-12-1909 The Pennsylvania Trust Company, of Carlisle, PA, became the first bank in the in the U.S. to offer a Christmas Club account.
01-12-1913 Ford Motor Co. began using a new movable assembly line that ushered in the era of mass production.
01-12-1913 The first drive-in automobile service station opened, in Pittsburgh, PA.
01-12-1919 Lady Astor was sworn in as the first female member of the British Parliament.
01-12-1925 The Locarno Pact finalized the treaties between World War I protagonists.
01-12-1934 Sergei M. Kirov, a collaborator of Joseph Stalin, was assassinated at the Leningrad party headquarters.
01-12-1941 In the U.S., the Civil Air Patrol was created. In April 1943 the Civil Air Patrol was placed under the jurisdiction of the Army Air Forces.
01-12-1942 In the U.S., nationwide gasoline rationing went into effect.
01-12-1943 In Teheran, leaders of the United States, the USSR and the United Kingdom met to reaffirm the goal set on October 30, 1943. The previous meeting called for an early establishment of an international organization to maintain peace and security.
01-12-1952 In Denmark, it was announced that the first successful sex-change operation had been performed.
01-12-1955 Rosa Parks, a black seamstress in Montgomery, AL, refused to give up her seat to a white man. Mrs. Parks was arrested marking a milestone in the civil rights movement in the U.S.
01-12-1959 12 countries, including the U.S. and USSR, signed a treaty that set aside Antarctica as a scientific preserve, which would be free from military activity.
01-12-1965 An airlift of refugees from Cuba to the United States began.
01-12-1969 The U.S. government held its first draft lottery since World War II.
01-12-1973 David Ben-Gurion, the founding father of Israel and its first prime minister, died at the age of 87.
01-12-1981 180 people were killed when a chartered Yugoslav DC9 jetliner crashed into a mountain while approaching Ajaccio Airport in Corsica.
01-12-1983 Rita M. Lavelle, a former Environmental Protection Agency official, was convicted in Washington of perjury and trying to obstruct a congressional inquiry.
01-12-1984 A remote-controlled Boeing 720 jetliner was deliberately crashed into California's Mojave Desert to test an anti-flame fuel additive. The test proved to be disappointing.
01-12-1986 U.S. President Ronald Reagan said he would welcome an investigation of the Iran-Contra affair if it were recommended by the Justice Department.
01-12-1987 NASA announced four companies had been given contracts to help build a space station. The companies were Boeing Aerospace, G. E.'s Astro-Space Division, McDonnell Douglas Aeronautics, and Rocketdyne Division of Rockwell International.
01-12-1989 Dissidents in the Philippine military launched an unsuccessful coup against Corazon Aquino's government.
01-12-1989 East Germany's Parliament abolished the Communist Party's constitutional guarantee of supremacy.
01-12-1990 Iraq accepted a U.S. offer to talk about resolving the Persian Gulf crisis.
01-12-1990 British and French workers digging the Channel Tunnel finally met under the English Channel.
01-12-1991 Ukrainians voted overwhelmingly for independence from the Soviet Union.
01-12-1991 Shiite Muslim kidnappers pledged to release American hostage Joseph Cicippio within 48 hours.
01-12-1992 Russian President Boris Yeltsin survived an impeachment attempt by hard-liners at the opening of the Russian Congress.
01-12-1992 Amy Fisher was sentenced to five to 15 years in prison for shooting Mary Jo Buttafuoco.
01-12-1993 In Minnesota, 18 people were killed when a Northwest Airline commuter plane crashed.
01-12-1994 The U.S. Senate gave final congressional approval to the 124-nation General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade.
01-12-1997 Michael Carneal, 14 years old, fired upon a morning prayer group at Heath High School in West Paducah, KY. Three students were killed and five were wounded. Carneal plead guilty but insane and was sentenced to life in prison with no chance for parole for 25 years.
01-12-1998 Exxon announced that it was buying Mobil for $73.7 billion creating the largest company in the world to date.
02-12-1804 Napoleon was crowned emperor of France at the Cathedral of Notre Dame in Paris.
02-12-1816 The first savings bank in the U.S., the Philadelphia Savings Fund Society, opened for business.
02-12-1823 U.S. President James Monroe outlined his doctrine opposing European expansion in the Western Hemisphere.
02-12-1859 John Brown, a militant abolitionist, was hanged for his raid on Harper's Ferry the previous October.
02-12-1862 Circus entrepreneur Charles Ringling was born.
02-12-1901 Gillette patented the first disposable razor.
02-12-1917 During World War I, hostilities were suspended on the eastern front.
02-12-1927 The Ford Motor Company unveiled the Model A automobile. It was the successor to the Model T.
02-12-1939 New York's La Guardia Airport began operations as an airliner from Chicago landed at 12:01 a.m.
02-12-1942 A self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction was demonstrated by Dr. Enrico Fermi and his staff at the University of Chicago.
02-12-1943 "Carmen Jones" opened on Broadway.
02-12-1954 The U.S. Senate voted to condemn Sen. Joseph R. McCarthy for what it called "conduct that tends to bring the Senate into dishonor and disrepute." The censure was related to McCarthy's controversial investigation of suspected communists in the U.S. government, military and civilian society.
02-12-1961 Cuban leader Fidel Castro declared in a nationally broadcast speech that he was a Marxist-Leninist and that he was going to lead Cuba to communism.
02-12-1969 The Boeing 747 jumbo jet got its first public preview as 191 people flew from Seattle, WA, to New York City, NY. Most of the passengers were reporters and photographers.
02-12-1970 The Environmental Protection Agency began operating under its first director, William Ruckelshaus.
02-12-1980 The Central Committee of Poland’s Communist Party announced major Politburo changes. The changes were apparently aimed at coping with labor unrest.
02-12-1982 Doctors at the University of Utah implanted a permanent artificial heart in the chest of retired dentist Barney Clark. He lived 112 days with the device. The operation was the first of its kind.
02-12-1985 A Philippine civilian court acquitted armed forces chief Gen. Fabian C. Ver of charges related to the 1983 shooting death of opposition leader Benigno S. Aquino. 25 other defendants were also acquitted.
02-12-1988 Benazir Bhutto was sworn in as prime minister of Pakistan.
02-12-1989 V.P. Singh was sworn in as prime minister of India.
02-12-1990 Chancellor Hekmut Kohl's coalition won the first free all-German elections since 1932.
02-12-1990 The Midwest section of the U.S. prepared for a massive earthquake predicted by Iben Browning. Nothing happened.
02-12-1991 American hostage Joseph Cicippio was released by his kidnappers. He had been held captive in Lebanon for over five years.
02-12-1992 Germany's lower house of parliament voted in favor of the Maastricht Treaty on European unity.
02-12-1993 Colombian drug lord Pablo Escobar was shot to death by security forces in Medellin.
02-12-1993 An unemployed man opened fire at an unemployment agency in Oxnard, CA. He killed three workers at the location and a police officer during a chase that ended in Ventura, where the man himself was gunned down.
02-12-1993 The space shuttle Endeavor blasted off on a mission to fix the Hubble Space Telescope.
02-12-1994 The U.S. government agreed not to seek a recall of allegedly fire-prone General Motors pickup trucks. Instead a deal was made with GM under which the company would spend more than $51 million on safety and research.
02-12-1994 "Hollywood Madam" Heidi Fleiss was convicted in Los Angeles of three counts of pandering.
02-12-1994 In Pensacola, FL, Paul Hill was given two life sentences for murdering a doctor and security guard outside an abortion clinic in July 1994.
02-12-1995 NASA launched a U.S.-European observatory on a $1 billion dollar mission intended to study the sun.
02-12-1997 U.S. Attorney General Janet Reno declined to seek an independent counsel investigation of telephone fund-raising by President Clinton and Vice President Gore. It was concluded that they had not violated election laws.
02-12-1997 Actress Anat Elimelech was killed by her boyfriend David Afuta. Afuta then killed himself.
02-12-1998 Microsoft Corp. chairman Bill Gates donated $100 million to help immunize children in developing countries.
02-12-1999 The British government transferred political power over the province of Northern Ireland to a the Northern Ireland Executive.
02-12-2001 Enron Corp. filed for Chapter 11 reorganization. The filing came five days after Dynegy walked away from a $8.4 billion buyout. It was the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history.
03-12-1818 Illinois was admitted as the 21st state of the union.
03-12-1828 Andrew Jackson was elected president of the United States.
03-12-1833 Oberlin College in Ohio opened as the first truly coeducational school of higher education in the United States.
03-12-1835 In Rhode Island, the Manufacturer Mutual Fire Insurance Company issued the first fire insurance policy.
03-12-1910 The neon lamp was displayed for the first time at the Paris Motor Show. The lamp was developed by French physicist Georges Claude.
03-12-1917 The Quebec Bridge opened for traffic after almost 20 years of planning and construction. The bridge suffered partial collapses in 1907 (August 29) and 1916 (September 11).
03-12-1931 Alka Seltzer was sold for the first time.
03-12-1947 The Tennessee Williams play "A Streetcar Named Desire" opened at Broadway's Ethel Barrymore Theater.
03-12-1948 The "Pumpkin Papers" came to public light. The House Un-American Activities Committee announced that former Communist spy Whittaker Chambers had produced microfilm of secret documents hidden inside a pumpkin on his Maryland farm.
03-12-1950 Paul Harvey began his national radio broadcast.
03-12-1950 Tom Fears (Los Angeles Rams) caught an NFL-record 18 passes against the Green Bay Packers. Terrell Owens (San Francisco 49ers) broke the record with 20 catches for 283 yards and a touchdown against the Chicago Bears on December 17, 2000.
03-12-1964 Police arrested about 800 students at the University of California at Berkeley. The arrest took place one day after the students staged a massive sit-in inside an administration building.
03-12-1967 In Cape Town, South Africa, a team of surgeons headed by Dr. Christian Barnard, performed the first human heart transplant on Louis Washkansky. Washkansky only lived 18 days.
03-12-1967 The famed luxury train, "20th Century Limited," completed its final run from New York to Chicago.
03-12-1968 The rules committee of Major League Baseball (MLB) announced that in 1969 the pitcher's mound would be lowered from 15 to 10 inches. This was done in order to "get more batting action."
03-12-1973 Pioneer 10 sent back the first close-up images of Jupiter. The first outer-planetary probe had been launched from Cape Canaveral, FL, on March 2, 1972.
03-12-1980 U.S. Representatives Frank Thompson, Jr. (D-NJ) and John Murphy (D-NY) were convicted on Abscam charges.
03-12-1982 Doctors at the University of Utah Medical Center removed the respirator of Barney Clark. The retired dentist had become the world's first recipient of a permanent artificial heart only one day before.
03-12-1983 3-foot-high concrete barriers were installed at two White House entrances.
03-12-1984 In Bhopal, India, more than 2,000 people were killed after a cloud of poisonous gas escaped from a pesticide plant. The plant was operated by a Union Carbide subsidiary.
03-12-1987 U.S. President Reagan said there was a good chance of progress toward a treaty on long-range weapons with Mikhail S. Gorbachev.
03-12-1988 In South Africa, 11 black funeral mourners were slain in Natal Province in an attack blamed on security forces.
03-12-1988 Barry Sanders of Oklahoma State University won the Heisman Trophy.
03-12-1990 A collision, on the ground, of a Northwest Airlines DC-9 and a Northwest Boeing 727 at Detroit Metropolitan Airport, resulted in a fire that claimed eight lives.
03-12-1991 After nearly five years, Shiite Muslim radicals in Lebanon released American hostage Allen Sutten.
03-12-1992 The UN Security Council unanimously approved a U.S.-led military mission to help starving Somalians.
03-12-1992 The Greek tanker "Aegean Sea" ran aground at La Coruna, Spain and spilled 21.5 million gallons of crude oil.
03-12-1993 Britain's Princess Diana announced she would be limiting her public appearances because she was tired of the media's intrusions into her life.
03-12-1993 Angola's government and its rebel enemies agreed to a cease-fire in their 18-year war.
03-12-1994 Rebel Serbs in Bosnia failed to keep a pledge to release hundreds of UN peacekeepers.
03-12-1994 AIDS activist Elizabeth Glaser died at the age 47. She and her two children were infected with HIV because of a blood transfusion.
03-12-1995 Former South Korean president Chun Doo-hwan was arrested for his role in a 1979 coup.
03-12-1997 Pierce Brosnan received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
03-12-1997 In Ottawa, Canada, more than 120 countries were represented to sign a treaty prohibiting the use and production of anti-personnel land mines. The United States, China and Russia did not sign the treaty.
03-12-1997 South Korea received $55 billion from the International Monetary Fund to bailout its economy.
03-12-1998 In Manilla, 28 people were killed in an orphanage that caught fire. Most of the victims were children.
03-12-1999 Tori Murden became the first woman to row across the Atlantic Ocean alone. It took her 81 days to reach the French Caribbean island of Guadeloupe from the Canary Islands.
03-12-1999 The World Trade Organization (WTO) concluded a four-day meeting in Seattle, WA, without setting an agenda for a new round of trade talks. The meeting was met with fierce protests by various groups.
03-12-1999 The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) lost radio contact with the Mars Polar Lander as it entered Mars' atmosphere. The spacecraft was unmanned.
04-12-1783 Gen. George Washington said farewell to his officers at Fraunces Tavern in New York.
04-12-1791 Britain's Observer newspaper was first published.
04-12-1812 Peter Gaillard patented the power mower.
04-12-1867 The National Grange of Husbandry was founded.
04-12-1875 William Marcy Tweed, the "Boss" of New York City's Tammany Hall political organization, escaped from jail and fled from the U.S.
04-12-1918 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson set sail for France to attend the Versailles Peace Conference. Wilson became the first chief executive to travel to Europe while in office.
04-12-1942 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt ordered the dismantling of the Works Progress Administration. The program had been created in order to provide jobs during the Great Depression.
04-12-1942 U.S. bombers attacked the Italian mainland for the first time during World War II.
04-12-1943 Baseball Commissioner Kenesaw Mountain Landis announced that any club was free to employ black players.
04-12-1945 The U.S. Senate approved American participation in the United Nations.
04-12-1965 The U.S. launched Gemini 7 with Air Force Lt. Col. Frank Borman and Navy Comdr. James A. Lovell on board.
04-12-1973 Pioneer 10 reached Jupiter.
04-12-1977 Jean-Bedel Bokassa, ruler of the Central African Empire, crowned himself emperor in a ceremony believed to have cost more than $100 million. He was deposed 2 years later.
04-12-1978 Dianne Feinstein became San Francisco's first woman mayor when she was named to replace George Moscone, who had been murdered.
04-12-1979 For the second time, the United Nations Security Council voted unanimously to urge Iran to free American hostages that had been taken on November 4.
04-12-1980 The bodies of four American nuns slain in El Salvador two days earlier were unearthed. Five national guardsmen were later convicted of the murders.
04-12-1983 U.S. jet fighters struck Syrian anti-aircraft positions in Lebanon in retaliation for attacks directed at American reconnaissance planes. Navy Lt. Robert O. Goodman Jr. was shot down and captured by Syria.
04-12-1984 A five-day hijack drama began as four men seized a Kuwaiti airliner en route to Pakistan and forced it to land in Tehran. Two American passengers were killed by the hijackers.
04-12-1986 Both U.S. houses of Congress moved to establish special committees to conduct their own investigations of the Iran-Contra affair.
04-12-1987 Cuban inmates at a federal prison in Atlanta freed their 89 hostages, peacefully ending an 11-day uprising.
04-12-1988 The government of Argentina announced that hundreds of heavily armed soldiers had ended a four-day military revolt.
04-12-1990 Iraq promised to release 3,300 Soviet citizens it was holding.
04-12-1991 Associated Press correspondent Terry Anderson was released after nearly seven years in captivity in Lebanon.
04-12-1991 Pan American World Airways ceased operations.
04-12-1992 U.S. President Bush ordered American troops to lead a mercy mission to Somalia.
04-12-1993 The Angolan government and its UNITA guerrilla foes formally adopted terms for a truce. The conflict was killing an estimated 1,000 people per day.
04-12-1994 Bosnian Serbs released 53 out of about 400 UN peacekeepers they were holding as insurance against further NATO airstrikes.
04-12-1997 The play revival "The Diary of Anne Frank" opened.
04-12-1997 The National Basketball Association (NBA) suspended Latrell Sprewell of the Golden State Warriors for one year for choking and threatening to kill his coach, P.J. Carlesimo.
04-12-2000 O.J. Simpson was involved in an incident with another motorist in Miami, FL. Simpson was accused of scratching the other motorists face while pulling off the man's glasses.
04-12-2001 O.J. Simpson's home in Florida was raided by the FBI in an ongoing two year international investigation into drug trafficking, satellite service pilfering and money laundering. Some satellite equipment was taken from Simpson's home and no drugs were found.
05-12-1492 Christopher Columbus discovered Hispaniola (now Haiti).
05-12-1560 Charles IX succeeded as King of France on the death of Francis II.
05-12-1766 James Christie, founder of the famous auctioneers, held his first sale in London.
05-12-1776 In Williamsburg, VA, at the College of William and Mary the first scholastic fraternity in America, Phi Beta Kappa, was organized.
05-12-1782 The first native U.S. president, Martin Van Buren, was born in Kinderhook, NY.
05-12-1792 The trial of France's King Louis XVI began.
05-12-1797 Napoleon Bonaparte arrived in Paris to command forces for the invasion of England.
05-12-1812 Napoleon Bonaparte left his army as they were retreating from Russia.
05-12-1839 General George Armstrong Custer was born in New Rumley, OH.
05-12-1848 U.S. President Polk triggered the Gold Rush of '49 by confirming the fact that gold had been discovered in California.
05-12-1876 The Stillson wrench was patented by D.C. Stillson. The device was the first practical pipe wrench.
05-12-1901 Movie producer Walt Disney was born in Chicago. He created his first Mickey Mouse cartoon at the age of 27.
05-12-1904 The Russian fleet was destroyed by the Japanese at Port Arthur, during the Russo-Japanese War.
05-12-1908 At the University of Pittsburgh, numerals were first used on football uniforms worn by college football players.
05-12-1913 Britain outlawed the sending of arms to Ireland.
05-12-1932 German physicist Albert Einstein was granted a visa making it possible for him to travel to the U.S.
05-12-1933 Prohibition came to an end when Utah became the 36th state to ratify the 21st Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.
05-12-1934 Fighting broke out between Italian and Ethiopian troops on the Somalian border.
05-12-1934 The Soviet Union executed 66 people charged with plotting against Joseph Stalin's government.
05-12-1935 In Montebello, CA, the first commercial hydrophonics operation was established.
05-12-1936 The Soviet Union adopted a new Constitution under a Supreme Council.
05-12-1944 During World War II, Allied troops took Ravenna, Italy.
05-12-1945 The so-called "Lost Squadron" disappeared. The five U.S. Navy Avenger bombers carrying 14 Navy flyers began a training mission at the Ft. Lauderdale Naval Air Station. They were never heard from again.
05-12-1951 The first push button-controlled garage opened in Washington, DC.
05-12-1955 The American Federation of Labor and the Congress of Industrial Organizations merged to form the AFL-CIO.
05-12-1956 British and French forces began a withdrawal from Egypt during the Suez War.
05-12-1958 Britain's first motorway, the Preston by-pass, was opened by Prime Minister Macmillan.
05-12-1961 United Nations forces launched an attack in Katanga, the Congo, near Elizabethville.
05-12-1962 The U.S. and the Soviet Union agreed to cooperate in the peaceful uses of outer space.
05-12-1971 The Soviet Union, at United Nations Security Council, vetoed a resolution calling for a cease-fire in hostilities between India and Pakistan over Kashmir.
05-12-1976 Jacques Chirac re-founded the Gaullist party as the RPR (Rassemblement pour la République).
05-12-1977 Egypt broke diplomatic relations with Syria, Libya, Algeria, Iraq and South Yemen due to peaceful relations with Israel.
05-12-1978 The American space probe Pioneer Venus I, orbiting Venus, and began beaming back its first information and picture of the planet.
05-12-1979 Sonia Johnson was formally excommunicated by the Mormon Church due to her outspoken support for the proposed Equal Rights Amendment to the Constitution.
05-12-1983 In west Beirut, Lebanon, more than a dozen people were killed when a car bomb shattered a nine-story apartment building.
05-12-1983 The video arcade game "NFL Football" was unveiled in Chicago. It was the first video arcade game to be licensed by the National Football League.
05-12-1984 Iran's official news agency quoted the hijackers of a Kuwaiti jetliner parked at Tehran airport as saying they would blow up the plane unless Kuwait released 14 imprisoned extremists.
05-12-1985 The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose above 1,500 for the first time.
05-12-1986 The Soviet Union said it would continue to abide by the SALT II treaty limits on nuclear weapons. This was despite the decision by the U.S. to exceed them.
05-12-1988 Jim Bakker and former aide Richard Dortch were indicted by a federal grand jury in North Carolina on fraud and conspiracy charges.
05-12-1989 Israeli soldiers killed five heavily armed Arab guerrillas who crossed the border from Egypt. The guerrillas were allegedly going to launch a terrorist attack commemorating the anniversary of the Palestinian uprising.
05-12-1989 East Germany's former leaders were placed under house arrest.
05-12-1992 Russian President Boris Yeltsin kept the power to appoint Cabinet ministers, defeating a constitutional amendment that would have put his team of reformers under the control of Russia's Congress.
05-12-1998 James P. Hoffa became the head of the Teamsters union, 23 years after his father was the head. His father disappeared and was presumed dead.
05-12-2001 In Germany, Afghan leaders signed a pact to create a temporary administration for post-Taliban Afghanistan. Two women were included in the cabinet structure. Hamid Karzai and his Cabinet were planned to take over power in Afghanistan on December 22.
06-12-1492 Columbus discovered Hispaniola (now Haiti) and the Dominican Republic.
06-12-1774 Austria became the first nation to introduce a state education system.
06-12-1790 The U.S. Congress moved from New York to Philadelphia.
06-12-1865 The 13th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. The amendment abolished slavery in the U.S.
06-12-1876 The city of Anaheim was incorporated for a second time.
06-12-1877 Thomas Edison demonstrated the first gramophone, with a recording of himself reciting Mary Had a Little Lamb.
06-12-1883 "Ladies' Home Journal" was published for the first time.
06-12-1884 The construction of the Washington Monument was completed by Army engineers. The project took 34 years.
06-12-1889 Jefferson Davis died in New Orleans. He was the first and only president of the Confederate States of America.
06-12-1907 In Monongah, WV, 361 people were killed in America's worst mine disaster.
06-12-1917 More than 1,600 people died when two munitions ships collided in the harbor at Halifax, Nova Scotia.
06-12-1917 Finland proclaimed independence from Russia.
06-12-1921 The Catholic Irish Free State was created as a self-governing dominion of Britain when an Anglo-Irish treaty was signed.
06-12-1923 U.S. President Calvin Coolidge became the first president to give a presidential address that was broadcast on radio.
06-12-1926 In Italy, Benito Mussolini introduced a tax on bachelors.
06-12-1947 Everglades National Park in Florida was dedicated by U.S. President Truman.
06-12-1957 AFL-CIO members voted to expel the International Brotherhood of Teamsters. The Teamsters were readmitted in 1987.
06-12-1957 America's first attempt at putting a satellite into orbit failed when the satellite blew up on the launch pad at Cape Canaveral, FL.
06-12-1960 Gene Autry and Bob Reynolds were granted the Los Angeles Angels baseball franchise by the American League.
06-12-1973 Gerald R. Ford was sworn in as the vice-president of the United States after vice-president Spiro Agnew resigned.
06-12-1982 11 soldiers and 6 civilians were killed when a bomb exploded in a pub in Ballykelly, Northern Ireland. The Irish National Liberation Army was responsible for planting the bomb.
06-12-1983 In Jerusalem, a bomb planted on a bus exploded killing six Israelis and wounding 44.
06-12-1985 Congressional negotiators reached an agreement on a deficit-cutting proposal that later became the Gramm-Rudman-Hollings law.
06-12-1989 The worst mass shooting in Canadian history occurred when a man gunned down 14 women at the University of Montreal's school of engineering. The man then killed himself.
06-12-1989 Egon Krenz resigned as leader of East Germany.
06-12-1990 Iraq announced that it would release all its 2,000 foreign hostages.
06-12-1990 U.S. Vice President Dan Quayle was enshrined in the Little League Museum's Hall of Excellence.
06-12-1992 Germany's primary political parties agreed to tighten postwar asylum laws.
06-12-1992 In India, thousands of Hindu extremists destroyed a mosque. The following two months of Hindu-Muslim rioting resulted in at least 2,000 people being killed.
06-12-1993 Former priest James R. Porter was sentenced to 18 to 20 years in prison. Porter had admitted molesting 28 children in the 1960s.
06-12-1994 Orange County, CA, filed for bankruptcy protection due to investment losses of about $2 billion. The county is one of the richest in the U.S. and became to largest municipality to file for bankruptcy.
06-12-1997 A Russian Antonov 124 military transport crashed into a residential area in Irkutsk, Russia, shortly after takeoff. 70 people were killed.
06-12-1998 In Venezuela, former Lieutenant Colonel Hugo Chavez was elected president. He had staged a bloody coup attempt against the government six years earlier.
06-12-1998 Astronauts aboard the space shuttle Endeavour connected the first two building blocks of the international space station in the shuttle cargo bay.
06-12-2002 Winona Ryder was sentenced to 36 months of probation and 480 hours of community service stemming from her conviction for shoplifting from Saks Fifth Avenue. She was also ordered to pay $10,000 in fines and restitution.
06-12-2002 Officials released the detailed plans for a $4.7 million memorial commemorating Princess Diana. The large oval fountain was planned to be constructed in London's Hyde Park.
07-12-1431 In Paris, Henry VI of England was crowned King of France.
07-12-1732 The original Covent Garden Theatre Royal (now the Royal Opera House) was opened.
07-12-1787 Delaware became the first state to ratify the U.S. constitution becoming the first of the United States.
07-12-1796 John Adams was elected to be the second president of the United States.
07-12-1836 Martin Van Buren was elected the eighth president of the United States.
07-12-1889 The first of 554 performances of "The Gondoliers" took place.
07-12-1907 At London's National Sporting Club, Eugene Corri became the first referee to officiate from inside a boxing ring.
07-12-1925 Swimmer Johnny Weissmuller set a world record in the 150-yard freestyle with a time of 1 minute, 25 and 2/5 seconds. He went on to play "Tarzan" in several movies.
07-12-1926 The gas operated refrigerator was patented by The Electrolux Servel Corporation.
07-12-1941 Pearl Harbor, located on the Hawaiian island of Oahu was attacked by nearly 200 Japanese warplanes. The attack resulted in the U.S. entering into World War II.
07-12-1946 A fire at the Winecoff Hotel in Atlanta killed 119 people. It was America's worst hotel fire disaster. The hotel founder, W. Frank Winecoff, was also killed in the fire.
07-12-1971 Libya announced the nationalization of British Petroleum's assets.
07-12-1972 Apollo 17 was launched at Cape Canaveral. It was the last U.S. moon mission.
07-12-1972 Imelda Marcos, wife of Philippine President Ferdinand E. Marcos, was stabbed and seriously wounded by an assailant. The man was then shot and killed by her bodyguards.
07-12-1974 President Makarios returned to Cyprus after five months in exile.
07-12-1980 General Antonio Ramlho Eanes was reelected president of Portugal. His right-wing opposition was thrown into disarray by the death of Premier Francisco Sa Carneiro in a plane crash.
07-12-1982 Charlie Brooks Junior, a convicted murderer, became the first prisoner in the U.S. to be executed by injection, at a prison in Huntsville, TX.
07-12-1983 Madrid, Spain, an Aviaco DC-9 collided on a runway with an Iberia Air Lines Boeing 727 that was accelerating for takeoff. The collision resulted in the death of all 42 people aboard the DC-9 and 51 on the Iberia jet.
07-12-1987 Soviet leader Mikhail S. Gorbachev set foot on American soil for the first time. He had come to the U.S. for a Washington summit with U.S. President Reagan.
07-12-1987 43 people were killed when a gunman opened fire on a fellow passenger and the two pilots aboard a Pacific Southwest Airlines jetliner.
07-12-1988 An estimated 25,000 people were killed when a major earthquake hit northern Armenia in the Soviet Union. The quake measured 6.9 on the Richter Scale.
07-12-1988 Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev announced the reduction of the number of Soviet military troops by half a million.
07-12-1989 East Germany's Communist Party agreed to cooperate with the plan for free elections and a revised constitution.
07-12-1992 The U.S. Supreme Court rejected a Mississippi abortion law which, required women to get counseling and then wait 24 hours before terminating their pregnancies.
07-12-1993 Six people were killed and 17 were injured when a gunman opened fire on a Long Island Rail Road commuter train.
07-12-1993 Energy Secretary Hazel O'Leary revealed that the U.S. government had conducted more than 200 nuclear weapons tests in secret at its Nevada test site.
07-12-1993 Surgeon General Joycelyn Elders suggested that the U.S. government study the impact of drug legalization.
07-12-1995 A probe sent from the Galileo spacecraft entered into Jupiter's atmosphere. The probe sent back data to the mothership before it was presumably destroyed.
07-12-1996 The space shuttle Columbia returned from the longest-ever shuttle flight of 17 days, 15 hours and 54 minutes.
07-12-1998 The U.N. evacuated 14 peacekeepers that were trapped by fighting between army and rebel forces in central Angola.
07-12-1998 U.S. Attorney General Janet Reno declined to seek an independent counsel investigation of President Clinton over 1996 campaign financing.
07-12-1999 A U.S. federal grand jury indicted a former convict in the 1995 disappearance of atheist leader Madalyn Murray O'Hair.
07-12-2002 In Amsterdam, Netherlands, two Van Gogh paintings were stolen from the Van Gogh Museum. The two works were "View of the Sea st Scheveningen" and "Congregation Leaving the Reformed Church in Nuenen." On July 26, 2004, two men were convicted for the crime and were sentenced to at least four years in prison each.
07-12-2002 In Mymensingh, Bangladesh, four movies theaters were bombed within 30 minutes of each other. At least 15 people were killed and over 200 were injured.
07-12-2003 A 12-inch by 26-inch painting of a river landscape and sailing vessel by Martin Johnson Heade was sold at auction for $1 million. The painting was found in the attic of a suburban Boston home where it had been stored for more than 60 years.
08-12-1765 Eli Whitney was born in Westboro, MA. Whitney invented the cotton gin and developed the concept of mass-production of interchangeable parts.
08-12-1776 George Washington's retreating army in the American Revolution crossed the Delaware River from New Jersey to Pennsylvania.
08-12-1854 Pope Pius IX proclaimed the dogma of the Immaculate Conception. The theory holds that Mary, mother of Jesus, was free of original sin from the moment she was conceived.
08-12-1863 U.S. President Abraham Lincoln announced his plan for the Reconstruction of the South.
08-12-1863 Tom King of England defeated American John Heenan and became the first world heavyweight champion.
08-12-1886 At a convention of union leaders in Columbus, OH, the American Federation of Labor was founded.
08-12-1941 The United States entered World War II when it declared war against Japan. The act came one day after the Japanese attacked Pearl Harbor. Britain and Canada also declared war on Japan.
08-12-1949 The Chinese Nationalist government moved from the Chinese mainland to Formosa due to Communists pressure.
08-12-1952 On the show "I Love Lucy," a pregnancy was acknowledged in a TV show for the first time.
08-12-1953 Los Angeles became the third largest city in the United States.
08-12-1962 Workers of the International Typographical Union began striking and closed nine New York City newspapers. The strike lasted 114 days and ended April 1, 1963.
08-12-1980 Zimbabwe’s manpower minister, Edgar Tekere, was found guilty in the killing of a white farmer. He was freed under a law that protected ministers acting to suppress terrorism.
08-12-1982 Norman D. Mayer demanding an end to nuclear weapons held the Washington Monument hostage. He threatened to blow it up with explosives he claimed were inside a van. 10 hours later he was shot to death by police.
08-12-1984 In Roanoke, Virginia, a jury found Hustler magazine publisher Larry Flynt innocent of libeling Reverend Jerry Falwell with a parody advertisement. However Falwell was awarded $200,000 for emotional distress.
08-12-1987 U.S. President Reagan and Soviet leader Mikhail S. Gorbachev signed a treaty agreeing to destroy their nations' arsenals of intermediate-range nuclear missiles.
08-12-1987 The "intefadeh" (Arabic for uprising) by Palestinians in the Israeli-occupied territories began.
08-12-1989 Communist leaders in Czechoslovakia offered to surrender their control over the government and accept a minority role in a coalition Cabinet.
08-12-1991 Russia, Byelorussia and Ukraine declared the Soviet national government to be dead. They forged a new alliance to be known as the Commonwealth of Independent States. The act was denounced by Russian President Gorbachev as unconstitutional.
08-12-1992 Americans got to see live television coverage of U.S. troops landing on the beaches of Somalia during Operation Restore Hope. (Due to the time diffence, it was December 9 in Somalia.)
08-12-1993 U.S. President Clinton signed into law the North American Free Trade Agreement.
08-12-1994 Bosnian Serbs released dozens of hostage peacekeepers, but continued to detain about 300 others.
08-12-1994 In Los Angeles, 12 alternate jurors were chosen for the O.J. Simpson murder trial.
08-12-1997 The second largest bank was created with the announcement that Union Bank Switzerland and the Swiss Bank Corporation would merge. The combined assets were more than $590 billion.
08-12-1997 Jenny Shipley was sworn in as the first female prime minister of New Zealand.
08-12-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that police could not search a person or their cars after ticketing for a routine traffic violation.
08-12-1998 The FBI opened its files on Frank Sinatra to the public. The file contained over 1,300 pages.
08-12-1998 Nkem Chukwu and Iyke Louis Udobi's first of eight babies was born. The other seven were delivered 12 days later.
08-12-1998 AT&T Corp. announced that it was buying IBM's data networking business for $5 billion cash.
08-12-1998 The first female ice hockey game in Olympic history was played. Finland beat Sweden 6-0.
08-12-1999 In Memphis, TN, a jury found that Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. had been the victim of a vast murder conspiracy, not a lone assassin.
08-12-1999 Russia and Belarus agreed in principle to form an economic and political confederation.
08-12-2000 Mario Lemieux announced to the Pittsburgh Penguins that he planned to return to the National Hockey League (NHL) as a player at age 35. He would be the first modern owner-player in U.S. pro sports.
09-12-1594 Gustavus II of Sweden was born.
09-12-1608 English poet John Milton was born in London.
09-12-1625 The Treaty of the Hague was signed by England and the Netherlands. The agreement was to subsidize Christian IV of Denmark in his campaign in Germany.
09-12-1783 The first executions at Newgate Prison took place.
09-12-1793 "The American Minerva" was published for the first time. It was the first daily newspaper in New York City and was founded by Noah Webster.
09-12-1803 The 12th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was passed by the U.S. Congress. With the amendment Electors were directed to vote for a President and for a Vice-President rather than for two choices for President.
09-12-1848 American author and creator of "Uncle Remus and Brer Rabbit," Joel Chandler Harris was born.
09-12-1854 Alfred Lord Tennyson's poem, "The Charge of the Light Brigade," was published in England.
09-12-1879 Thomas Edison organized the Edison Ore Milling Company.
09-12-1884 Levant M. Richardson received a patent for the ball-bearing roller skate.
09-12-1892 In London, "Widowers' Houses," George Bernard Shaw's first play, opened at the Royalty Theater.
09-12-1907 Christmas Seals went on sale for the first time, in the Wilmington, DE, post office.
09-12-1926 The United States Golf Association legalized the use of steel-shafted golf clubs.
09-12-1914 The Edison Phonograph Works was destroyed by fire.
09-12-1917 Turkish troops surrendered Jerusalem to British troops led by Viscount Allenby.
09-12-1940 During World War II, British troops opened their first major offensive in North Africa.
09-12-1940 The Longines Watch Company signed for the first FM radio advertising contract with experimental station W2XOR in New York City.
09-12-1941 China declared war on Japan, Germany and Italy.
09-12-1942 The Aram Khachaturian ballet "Gayane" was first performed by the Kirov Ballet.
09-12-1955 Sugar Ray Robinson knocked out Carl Olson and regained his world middleweight boxing title.
09-12-1958 In Indianapolis, IN, Robert H.W. Welch Jr. and 11 other men met to form the anti-Communist John Birch Society.
09-12-1960 Sperry Rand Corporation unveiled a new computer, known as "Univac 1107."
09-12-1960 The first episode of "Coronation Street" was screened on ITV.
09-12-1962 "Lawrence of Arabia," by David Lean had its world premiere in London.
09-12-1965 Nikolai V. Podgorny replaced Anastas I. Mikoyan as president of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet.
09-12-1975 U.S. President Gerald R. Ford signed a $2.3 billion seasonal loan authorization to prevent New York City from having to default.
09-12-1978 The first game of the Women's Pro Basketball League (WBL) was played between the Chicago Hustle and the Milwaukee Does.
09-12-1983 NATO foreign ministers called on the Soviet Union to join in a "comprehensive political dialogue" to ease tensions in the world.
09-12-1984 Iranian security men seized control of the plane ending a five-day hijacking of a Kuwaiti jetliner, which was parked at the Tehran airport.
09-12-1985 In Argentina, five former military junta members received sentences in prison for their roles in the "dirty war" in which nearly 9,000 people had "disappeared."
09-12-1987 West Bank Palestinians launched an intifada (uprising) against Israeli occupation.
09-12-1987 In the Gaza Strip, an Israeli patrol attacked the Jabliya refugee camp.
09-12-1990 Lech Walesa won Poland's first direct presidential election in the country's history.
09-12-1990 Slobodan Milosovic was elected president in Serbia's first free elections in 50 years.
09-12-1990 The first American hostages to be released by Iraq began arriving in the U.S.
09-12-1991 European Community leaders agreed to begin using a single currency in 1999.
09-12-1992 Britain's Prince Charles and Princess Diana announced their separation.
09-12-1992 Clair George, former CIA spy chief, was convicted of lying to the U.S. Congress about the Iran-Contra affair. U.S. President George Bush later pardoned George.
09-12-1992 U.S. troops arrived in Mogadishu, Somalia, to oversee delivery of international food aid, in operation 'Restore Hope'.
09-12-1993 The U.S. Air Force destroyed the first of 500 Minuteman II missile silos that were marked for elimination under an arms control treaty.
09-12-1993 Astronauts aboard the space shuttle Endeavor completed repairs to the Hubble Space Telescope.
09-12-1993 At Princeton University in New Jersey, scientists produced a controlled fusion reaction equivalent to 3 million watts.
09-12-1994 Representatives of the Irish Republican Army and the British government opened peace talks in Northern Ireland.
09-12-1994 U.S. President Clinton fired Surgeon General Joycelyn Elders after learning that she had told a conference that masturbation should be discussed in school as a part of human sexuality.
09-12-1996 UN Secretary General Boutros-Ghali approved a deal allowing Iraq to resume its exports of oil and easing the UN trade embargo imposed on Iraq in 1990.
09-12-1999 The U.S. announced that it was expelling a Russian diplomat that had been caught gathering information with an eavesdropping device at the U.S. State Department.
09-12-2002 United Airlines filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after losing $4 billion in the previos two years. It was the sixth largest bankruptcy filing.
09-12-2003 In Australia, thieves broke into a home and stole two 300-year-old etchings by Rembrandt. The 4-by-4-inch etchings, a self-portait and a depiction of the artist's mother, were valued around $518,000.
10-12-1520 Martin Luther publicly burned the papal edict. The papacy demanded that he recant or face excommunication. Luther refused and was formally expelled from the church in January 1521.
10-12-1768 The Royal Academy of Arts was founded in London by George III. Joshua Reynolds was its first president.
10-12-1787 Thomas H. Gallaudet, a pioneer of educating the deaf, was born in Philadelphia.
10-12-1817 Mississippi was admitted to the Union as the 20th American state.
10-12-1830 Emily Dickinson was born in Amherst, MA. Only seven of her works were published while she was alive.
10-12-1845 British civil engineer Robert Thompson patented the first pneumatic tires.
10-12-1851 American librarian Melvil Dewey was born. He created the "Dewey Decimal Classification" system.
10-12-1869 Women were granted the right to vote in the Wyoming Territory.
10-12-1896 Alfred Bernhard Nobel died in San Remo, Italy. He was a Swedish chemist who invented dynamite. In his in his will he stipulated that income from his $9 million estate be used for annual prizes for people judged to have made valuable humanitarian deeds.
10-12-1898 A treaty was signed in Paris that officially ended the Spanish-American War. Also, Cuba became independent of Spain.
10-12-1901 The first Nobel prizes were awarded.
10-12-1906 U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt became the first American to be awarded the Nobel Peace Prize, for helping mediate an end to the Russo-Japanese War.
10-12-1931 Jane Addams became a co-recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize, she was the first American woman to do so.
10-12-1939 The National Football League's attendance exeeded 1 million in a season for the first time.
10-12-1941 Japan invaded the Philippines.
10-12-1941 The Royal Naval battleships Prince of Wales and Repulse were sunk by Japanese aircraft in the Battle of Malaya.
10-12-1948 The United Nations General Assembly adopted its Universal Declaration on Human Rights.
10-12-1950 Dr. Ralph J. Bunche was presented the Nobel Peace Prize. He was the first African-American to receive the award. Bunche was awarded the prize for his efforts in mediation between Israel and neighboring Arab states.
10-12-1953 Hugh Hefner published the first "Playboy" magazine with an investment of $7,600.
10-12-1958 The first domestic passenger jet flight took place in the U.S. when 111 passengers flew from New York to Miami on a National Airlines Boeing 707.
10-12-1962 Frank Gifford (New York Giants) was on the cover of "Sports Illustrated."
10-12-1964 In Oslo, Norway, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. received the Nobel Peace Prize. He was the youngest person to receive the award.
10-12-1980 South Carolina Representative John W. Jenretter resigned to avoid being expelled from the U.S. House of Representatives following his conviction on charges to the FBI's Abscam investigation.
10-12-1982 The Law of the Sea Convention was signed by 118 countries in Montego Bay, Jamaica. 23 nations and the U.S. were excluded.
10-12-1983 Raul Alfonsin was inaugurated as Argentina's first civilian president after nearly eight years of military rule.
10-12-1984 South African Bishop Desmond Tutu received the Nobel Peace Prize.
10-12-1990 Industrialist Armand Hammer died at age 92.
10-12-1990 The U.S. Food & Drug Administration approved Norplant, a long-acting contraceptive implant.
10-12-1991 The play Revival "The Crucible" opened.
10-12-1992 Oregon Senator Bob Packwood apologized for what he called "unwelcome and offensive" actions toward women. However, he refused to resign.
10-12-1993 The crew of the space shuttle Endeavor deployed the repaired Hubble Space Telescope into Earth's orbit.
10-12-1994 Advertising executive Thomas Mosser of North Caldwell, NJ, was killed by a mail bomb that was blamed on the Unabomber.
10-12-1994 Yasser Arafat, Shimon Peres and Yitzhak Rabin received the Nobel Peace Prize. They pledged to pursue their mission of healing the Middle East.
10-12-1995 The first U.S. Marines arrived in the Bosnian capital of Sarajevo to join NATO soldiers sent to enforce peace in the former Yugoslavia.
10-12-1996 South Africa's President Mandela signed into law a new democratic constitution, completing the country's transition from white-minority rule to a non-racial democracy.
10-12-1998 Six astronauts opened the doors to the new international space station 250 miles above the Earth's surface.
10-12-1998 The Palestinian leadership scrapped constitutional clauses that rejected Israel's existence.
10-12-1999 After three years under suspicion of being a spy for China, computer scientist Wen Ho Lee was arrested. He was charged with removing secrets from the Los Alamos weapons lab. Lee later plead guilty to one count of downloading restricted data to tape and was freed. The other 58 counts were dropped.
10-12-2003 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld new restrictions on political advertising in the weeks before an election. The court did strike down two provisions of the new law that involved a ban on political contibutions from those too young to vote and a limitation on some party spending. (McConnell v. FEC, 02-1674)
10-12-2003 The U.S. barred firms based in certain countries, opponents of the Iraq war, from bidding on Iraqi reconstruction projects. The ban did not prevent companies from winning subcontracts.
11-12-1282 Llywelyn (Llewelyn ap Gruffydd) was killed in Cilmeri, central Wales.
11-12-1719 The first recorded sighting of the Aurora Borealis was in New England.
11-12-1769 Edward Beran of London patented venetian blinds.
11-12-1792 France's King Louis XVI went before the Convention, which had replaced the National Assembly, to face charges of treason. He was convicted and condemned and was sent to the guillotine the following January.
11-12-1816 Indiana was admitted to the Union as the 19th American state.
11-12-1844 Dr. Horace Wells became the first person to have a tooth extracted after receiving an anesthetic for the dental procedure. Nitrous Oxide, or laughing gas, was the anesthetic.
11-12-1872 Pinckney Benton Stewart Pinchback became America's first black governor when he took office as acting governor of Louisiana.
11-12-1882 Boston's Bijou Theater had its first performance. It was the first American playhouse lit exclusively by electricity.
11-12-1894 The world's first motor show opened in Paris with nine exhibitors.
11-12-1928 In Buenos Aires, police thwarted an attempt on the life of President-elect Herbert Hoover.
11-12-1930 The Bank of the United States in New York failed.
11-12-1936 Britain's King Edward VIII abdicated in order to marry American Wallis Warfield Simpson. He became the Duke of Windsor.
11-12-1937 The Fascist Council in Rome, withdrew Italy from the League of Nations.
11-12-1941 Germany and Italy declared war on the United States. The U.S in turn declared war on the two countries.
11-12-1943 The City Center of Music and Drama was dedicated in New York by Mayor Fiorello La Guardia.
11-12-1946 The United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund (UNICEF) was established by the U.N. General Assembly. The fund provides relief to children in countries devastated by war.
11-12-1951 Joe DiMaggio (New York Yankees) announced his retirement from major league baseball. DiMaggio only played for the Yankees during his 13-year career.
11-12-1961 The first direct American military support for South Vietnam occurred when a U.S. aircraft carrier carrying Army helicopters arrived in Saigon.
11-12-1967 The prototype of the Concorde was shown for the first time in Toulouse, France.
11-12-1973 West German Chancellor Willy Brandt and Czech Prime Minister Lubomir Strougal formally nullified the 1938 Munich pact when they signed a treaty sanctioning Hitler's seizure of Czechoslovakia's Sudetenland.
11-12-1980 U.S. President Jimmy Carter signed into law legislation creating $1.6 billion environmental "superfund" that would be used to pay for cleaning up chemical spills and toxic waste dumps.
11-12-1981 Muhammad Ali fought his last fight. He lost his 61st fight to Trevor Berbick.
11-12-1985 The U.S. House of Representatives joined the U.S. Senate by giving final congressional approval to the Gramm-Rudman deficit-reduction law.
11-12-1985 General Electric Company agreed to buy RCA Corporation for $6.3 billion. Also included in the deal was NBC Radio and Television.
11-12-1986 The government of South Africa expanded its media restrictions by imposing prior censorship and banning coverage of a wide range of peaceful anti-apartheid protests.
11-12-1987 Charlie Chaplin's trademark cane and bowler hat were sold at Christie's for Ł82,500.
11-12-1988 62 people were killed in a Mexico City marketplace when tons of illegal fireworks exploded.
11-12-1990 Ivana Trump was divorced from Donald Trump after 12 years of marriage.
11-12-1991 Salman Rushdie, under an Islamic death sentence for blasphemy, made his first public appearance since 1989 in New York, at a dinner marking the 200th anniversary of the First Amendment (which guarantees freedom of speech in the U.S.).
11-12-1994 Thousands of Russian troops, armored columns and jets entered Chechnya. The move by Moscow was an effort to restore control the breakaway republic.
11-12-1994 The world's largest free trade zone was created when leaders of 34 Western Hemisphere nations signed a free-trade declaration known as "The Miami Process."
11-12-1996 In Crystal City, VA, "The Art of the Toy" opened. The exhibit was at the Patent and Trademark Office Museum.
11-12-1997 Sinn Fein leader Gerry Adams became the first political ally of the IRA to meet a British leader in 76 years. He conferred with Prime Minister Tony Blair in London.
11-12-1997 More than 270 Tutsi refugees from the Democratic Republic of Congo were killed by Juto guerillas in Mudende, Rwanda.
11-12-1997 More than 150 countries agreed at a global warming conference in Kyoto, Japan, to control the Earth's "greenhouse gases."
11-12-1998 Scientists announced that they had deciphered the entire genetic blueprint of a tiny worm.
11-12-1998 The Mars Climate Orbiter blasted off on a nine-month journey to the Red Planet. However, the probe disappeared in September of 1999, apparently destroyed because scientists had failed to convert English measures to metric values.
11-12-1998 Majority Republicans on the House Judiciary Committee pushed through three articles of impeachment against U.S. President Clinton.
11-12-2000 Mario Lemeiux, owner of Pittsburgh Penquins, announced that he would end his three-plus year retirement and become an active National Hockey League (NHL) player again. When Lemieux returned officially he became the first owner/player in NHL history.
11-12-2001 U.S. Attorney General Ashcroft announced the first federal indictment directly related to the terrorist attacks on the United States on September 11, 2001. Zacarias Moussaoui was charged with six conspiracy charges. Moussaoui was in custody at the time of the attacks.
11-12-2001 Ted Turner purchased 12,000 acres in Nebraska for Bison ranches.
11-12-2001 It was announced that U.S. President George W. Bush would withdraw the U.S. from the 1972 Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty with Russia.
11-12-2001 Federal agents seized computers in 27 U.S. cities as part of "Operation Buccaneer." The raids were used to gain evidence against an international software piracy ring.
12-12-1787 Pennsylvania became the second state to ratify the U.S. Constitution.
12-12-1792 In Vienna, 22-year-old Ludwig van Beethoven received one of his first lessons in music composition from Franz Joseph Haydn.
12-12-1800 Washington, DC, was established as the capital of the United States.
12-12-1805 Henry Wells was born in Thetford, VT. He was one of the founders of the American Express Company and he teamed up with William Fargo to form the Wells Fargo Company.
12-12-1863 Norwegian painter Edvard Munch was born. His most known work is "The Scream."
12-12-1870 Joseph H. Rainey of South Carolina became the first black lawmaker to be sworn into the U.S. House of Representatives.
12-12-1896 Guglielmo Marconi gave the first public demonstration of radio at Toynbee Hall, London.
12-12-1897 The comic strip"The Katzenjammer Kids" (Hans and Fritz), by Rudolph Dirks, appeared in the New York Journal for the first time.
12-12-1899 George Grant patented the wooden golf tee.
12-12-1900 Charles M. Schwab formed the United States Steel Corporation.
12-12-1901 The first radio signal to cross the Atlantic was picked up near St. John's Newfoundland, by inventor Guglielmo Marconi.
12-12-1912 The Mother's Day International Association was incorporated with the purpose of furthering meaningful observations of Mother's Day.
12-12-1915 The first all-metal aircraft, the German Junkers J1, made its first flight.
12-12-1917 Father Edward Flanagan opened Boys Town in Nebraska. The farm village was for wayward boys. In 1979 it was opened to girls.
12-12-1925 The "Motel Inn," the first motel in the world, opened in San Luis Obispo, CA.
12-12-1937 Japanese aircraft sank the U.S. gunboat "Panay" on China's Yangtze River. Japan apologized for the attack, and paid $2.2 million in reparations.
12-12-1946 A United Nations committee voted to accept a six-block tract of Manhattan real estate to be the site of the UN's headquarters. The land was offered as a gift by John D. Rockefeller Jr.
12-12-1947 The United Mine Workers union withdrew from the American Federation of Labor.
12-12-1951 The U.S. Navy Department announced that the world's first nuclear powered submarine would become the sixth ship to bear the name Nautilus.
12-12-1955 It was announced that the Ford Foundation gave $500,000,000 to private hospitals, colleges and medical schools.
12-12-1955 British engineer Christopher Cockerell patented the first hovercraft.
12-12-1963 Kenya gained its independence from Britain.
12-12-1975 Sara Jane Moore pled guilty to a charge of trying to kill U.S. President Ford in San Francisco the previous September.
12-12-1982 20,000 women encircled Greenham Common air base in Britain in protest against proposed cite of U.S. Cruise missiles there.
12-12-1983 Car bombs were set off in front of the French and U.S. embassies in Kuwait City. Shiite extremists were responsible for the five deaths and 86 wounded. Total of five bombs went off in different locations.
12-12-1984 In a telephone conversation with U.S. President Reagan, William J. Schroeder complained of a delay in his Social Security benefits. Schroeder received a check the following day.
12-12-1985 248 American soldiers and eight crewmembers were killed when an Arrow Air charter crashed in Gander, Newfoundland after takeoff.
12-12-1989 Britain forcibly removed 51 Vietnamese from Hong Kong and returned them to their homeland.
12-12-1989 Leona Helmsley was fined $7 million and sentenced to four years in prison for tax evasion.
12-12-1994 The Brazilian Supreme Court acquitted former President Fernando Collor de Mello of corruption charges that had forced him to resign in 1992.
12-12-1994 IBM stopped shipments of personal computers with Intel's flawed Pentium chip.
12-12-1995 The U.S. Senate stopped a constitutional amendment giving Congress authority to outlaw flag burning and other forms of desecration against the American flag.
12-12-1995 Two French airmen shot down over Bosnia arrived home after almost four months of being held captive by the Bosnian Serbs.
12-12-1997 Ilich Ramirez Sanchez, the international terrorist known as "Carlos the Jackal," went on trial in Paris on charges of killing two French investigators and a Lebanese national. He was convicted and sentenced to life in prison.
12-12-1997 The U.S. Justice Department ordered Microsoft to sell its Internet browser separately from its Windows operating system to prevent it from building a monopoly of Web access programs.
12-12-1997 Denver Pyle received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
12-12-1998 The House Judiciary Committee rejected censure, and approved the final article of impeachment against U.S. President Clinton. The case was submitted to the full House for a verdict.
12-12-2000 The U.S. Supreme Court found that the recount ordered by the Florida Supreme Court in the 2000 U.S. Presidential election was unconstitutional. U.S. Vice President Al Gore conceded the election to Texas Gov. George W. Bush the next day.
12-12-2000 Timothy McVeigh, over the objections of his lawyers, abandoned his final round of appeals and asked that his execution be set within 120 days. McVeigh was convicted of the April 1995 truck bombing of the Alfred P. Murrah Fedal Building in Oklahoma City, OK, that killed 168 and injured 500.
12-12-2000 The Texas Rangers signed Alex Rodriguez to a record breaking 10-year, $252 million contract. The contract amount broke all major league baseball records and all professional sports records.
12-12-2001 The U.S. House of Representatives passed legislation that would implement minimum federal election standards and provide funding to help states modernize their voting systems.
