31-12-1999 Five hijackers left the airport where they had been holding 150 hostages on an Indian Airlines plane. They left with two Islamic clerics that they had demanded be freed from an Indian prison. The plane had been hijacked during a flight from Katmandu, Nepal to New Dehli on December 24.
31-12-1999 Sarah Knauss died at the age of 119 years. She was the world's oldest person. She was born September 24, 1880.
01-02-1788 Isaac Briggs and William Longstreet patented the steamboat.
01-02-1790 The U.S. Supreme Court convened for the first time in New York City.
01-02-1793 France declared war on Britain and Holland.
01-02-1793 Ralph Hodgson patented oiled silk.
01-02-1861 Texas voted to secede from the Union.
01-02-1862 "The Battle Hymn of the Republic," by Julia Ward Howe was first published in the "Atlantic Monthly."
01-02-1867 In the U.S., bricklayers start working 8-hour days.
01-02-1884 The first edition of the Oxford English Dictionary was published.
01-02-1893 Thomas A. Edison completed work on the world's first motion picture studio in West Orange, NJ.
01-02-1896 Puccini's opera "La Boheme" premiered in Turin.
01-02-1898 The Travelers Insurance Company of Hartford, CT, issued the first automobile insurance policy. Dr. Truman Martin of Buffalo, NY, paid $11.25 for the policy, which gave him $5,000 in liability coverage.
01-02-1900 Eastman Kodak Co. introduced the $1 Brownie box camera.
01-02-1913 Grand Central Station opened in New York City, NY. It was the largest train station in the world.
01-02-1919 The first Miss America was crowned in New York City.
01-02-1920 The first armored car was introduced.
01-02-1920 Canada's Royal North West Mounted Police changed their name to the Royal Canadian Mounted Police. The organization was commissioned in 1873.
01-02-1921 Carmen Fasanella registered as a taxicab owner and driver in Princeton, New Jersey. Fasanella retired November 2, 1989 after 68 years and 243 days of service.
01-02-1929 Weightlifter, Charles Rigoulet of France, achieved the first 400 pound ‘clean and jerk’ as he lifted 402-1/2 pounds.
01-02-1930 The Times published its first crossword puzzle.
01-02-1946 Norwegian statesman Trygve Lie was chosen to be the first secretary-general of the United Nations.
01-02-1951 The first telecast of an atomic explosion took place.
01-02-1951 The first X-ray moving picture process was demonstrated.
01-02-1953 CBS-TV debuted "Private Secretary."
01-02-1954 CBS-TV showed "The Secret Storm" for the first time.
01-02-1957 P.H. Young became the first black pilot on a scheduled passenger airline.
01-02-1958 The United Arab Republic was formed by a union of Egypt and Syria. It was broken 1961.
01-02-1960 Four black college students began a sit-in protest at a lunch counter in Greensboro, NC. They had been refused service.
01-02-1968 During the Vietnam War, South Vietnamese National Police Chief Brig. Gen. Nguyen Ngoc Loan executed a Viet Cong officer with a pistol shot to the head. The scene was captured in a news photograph.
01-02-1976 "Sonny and Cher" resumed on TV despite a real life divorce.
01-02-1979 Patty Hearst was released from prison after serving 22 months of a seven-year sentence for bank robbery. Her sentence had been commuted by U.S. President Carter.
01-02-1979 Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini was welcomed in Tehran as he ended nearly 15 years of exile.
01-02-1987 Terry Williams won the largest slot machine payoff, at the time, when won $4.9 million after getting four lucky 7s on a machine in Reno, NV.
01-02-1991 A USAir jetliner crashed atop a commuter plane at Los Angeles International Airport. 35 people were killed.
01-02-1994 Jeff Gillooly plead guilty in Portland, OR, for his role in the attack on figure skater Nancy Kerrigan. Gillooly, Tonya Harding's ex-husband, struck a plea bargain under which he confessed to racketeering charges in exchange for testimony implicating Harding.
01-02-1996 Visa and Mastercard announced security measures that would make it safe to shop on the Internet.
01-02-1998 Stuart Whitman received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
01-02-1999 Former White House intern Monica Lewinsky gave a deposition that was videotaped for senators weighing impeachment charges against U.S. President Clinton.
01-02-2001 Three Scottish judges found Abdel Basset al-Mergrahi guilty of the 1988 bombing of Pan Am Flight 103, which killed 270 people. The court said that Megrahi was a member of the Libyan intelligence service. Al-Amin Khalifa, who had been co-accused, was acquitted and freed.
01-02-2003 NASA's space shuttle Columbia exploded while re-entering the Earth's atmosphere. All seven astronauts on board were killed.
02-02-1536 The Argentine city of Buenos Aires was founded by Pedro de Mendoza of Spain.
02-02-1653 New Amsterdam, now known as New York City, was incorporated.
02-02-1802 The first leopard to be exhibited in the United States was shown by Othello Pollard in Boston, MA.
02-02-1848 The Mexican War was ended with the signing of the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo. The treaty turned over portions of land to the U.S., including Texas, New Mexico, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, California and parts of Colorado and Wyoming. The U.S. gave Mexico $15,000,000 and assumed responsibility of all claims against Mexico by American citizens. Texas had already entered the U.S. on December 29, 1845.
02-02-1848 The first shipload of Chinese emigrants arrived in San Francisco, CA.
02-02-1863 Samuel Langhorne Clemens used a pseudonym for the first time. He is better remembered by the pseudonym which is Mark Twain.
02-02-1870 The "Cardiff Giant" was revealed to be nothing more than carved gypsum. The discovery in Cardiff, NY, was alleged to be the petrified remains of a human.
02-02-1876 The National League of Professional Base Ball Clubs was formed in New York.
02-02-1878 Greece declared war on Turkey.
02-02-1880 The S.S. Strathleven arrived in London with the first successful shipment of frozen mutton from Australia.
02-02-1887 The beginning of groundhog day in Punxsutawney, PA.
02-02-1892 William Painter patented the crown-cork bottle cap.
02-02-1893 The Edison Studio in West Orange, NJ, made history when they filmed the first motion picture close-up. The studio was owned and operated by Thomas Edison.
02-02-1897 The Pennsylvania state capitol in Harrisburg was destroyed by fire. The new statehouse was dedicated nine years later on the same site.
02-02-1900 Six U.S. cities, Boston, Detroit, Milwaukee, Baltimore, Chicago, and St. Louis, agreed to form baseball's American League. (MLB)
02-02-1913 Grand Central Terminal officially opened at 12:01 a.m. Even though construction was not entirely complete more than 150,000 people visited the new terminal on its opening day.
02-02-1935 Leonard Keeler conducted the first test of the polygraph machine, in Portage, WI.
02-02-1943 During World War II, the remainder of Nazi forces from the Battle of Stalingrad surrendered to the Soviets. Stalingrad has since been renamed Volgograd.
02-02-1945 U.S. President Roosevelt and British Prime Minister Winston Churchill left for a summit in Yalta with Soviet leader Josef Stalin.
02-02-1946 The first Buck Rogers automatic pistol was made.
02-02-1946 The Mutual Broadcasting System aired "Twenty Questions" for the first time on radio. The show moved to television 3 years later.
02-02-1949 Golfer Ben Hogan was seriously injured in an auto accident in Van Horn, TX.
02-02-1950 "What's My Line" debuted on CBS television.
02-02-1962 The 8th and 9th planets aligned for the first time in 400 years.
02-02-1967 The American Basketball Association was formed by representatives of the NBA.
02-02-1971 Idi Amin assumed power in Uganda after a coup that ousted President Milton Obote.
02-02-1980 The situation known as "Abscam" began when reports surfaced that the FBI had conducted a sting operation that targeted members of the U.S. Congress. A phony Arab businessmen were used in the operation.
02-02-1989 The final Russian armored column left Kabul, Afghanistan, after nine years of military occupation.
02-02-1990 South African President F.W. de Klerk lifted a ban on the African National Congress and promised to free Nelson Mandela.
02-02-1998 U.S. President Clinton introduced the first balanced budget in 30 years.
02-02-1999 19 people were killed at Luanda international airport when a cargo plane crashed just after takeoff.
02-02-1999 Hugo Chávez Frías took office. He had been elected president of Venezuela in December 1998.
02-02-2004 It was reported that a white powder had been found in an office of Senate Majority Leader Bill Frist. The CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention) later confirmed that the powder was the poison ricin.
03-02-1488 The Portuguese navigator Bartholomeu Diaz landed at Mossal Bay in the Cape, the first European known to have landed on the southern extremity of Africa.
03-02-1690 The first paper money in America was issued by the Massachusetts colony. The currency was used to pay soldiers that were fighting in the war against Quebec.
03-02-1783 Spain recognized the independence of the United States.
03-02-1809 The territory of Illinois was created.
03-02-1815 The world's first commercial cheese factory was established in Switzerland.
03-02-1862 Thomas Edison printed the "Weekly Herald" and distributed it to train passengers traveling between Port Huron and Detroit, MI. It was the first time a newspaper had been printed on a train.
03-02-1869 Edwin Booth opened his new theatre in New York City. The first production was "Romeo and Juliet".
03-02-1900 In Frankfort, KY, gubernatorial candidate William Goebels died from an assasin's bullet wounds. On August 18, 1900, Ex-Sec. of State Caleb Powers was found guilt of conspiracy to murder Gov. Goebels.
03-02-1913 The 16th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. It authorized the power to impose and collect income tax.
03-02-1916 In Ottawa, Canada's original parliament buildings burned down.
03-02-1917 The U.S. broke off diplomatic relations with Germany, which had announced a policy of unrestricted submarine warfare.
03-02-1918 The Twin Peaks Tunnel began service. It is the longest streetcar tunnel in the world at 11,920 feet.
03-02-1919 The League of Nations held its first meeting in Paris.
03-02-1927 The Federal Radio Commission was created when U.S. President Calvin Coolidge signed a bill.
03-02-1941 In Vichy, France, the Nazis used force to restore Pierre Laval to office.
03-02-1945 Russia agreed to enter World War II against Japan.
03-02-1946 The first issue of "Holiday" magazine appeared.
03-02-1947 Percival Prattisbecame the first black news correspondent admitted to the House and Senate press gallery in Washington, DC. He worked for "Our World" in New York City.
03-02-1951 Dick Button won the U.S. figure skating title for the sixth time.
03-02-1951 The Tennessee Williams play, "The Rose Tattoo", opened on Broadway in New York.
03-02-1966 The first rocket-assisted controlled landing on the Moon was made by the Soviet space vehicle Luna IX.
03-02-1969 At the Palestinian National Congress in Cairo, Yasser Arafat was appointed leader of the PLO.
03-02-1972 The first Winter Olympics in Asia were held at Sapporo, Japan.
03-02-1984 Challenger 4 was launched as the tenth space shuttle mission.
03-02-1988 The U.S. House of Representatives handed rejected U.S. President Reagan's request for at least $36.25 million in aid to the Nicaraguan Contras.
03-02-1989 South African politician P.W. Botha unwillingly resigned both party leadership and the presidency after suffering a stroke.
03-02-1998 Texas executed Karla Faye Tucker. She was the first woman executed in the U.S. since 1984.
03-02-1998 In Italy, a U.S. Military plane hit a cable causing the death of 20 skiers on a lift.
04-02-1783 Britain declared a formal cessation of hostilities with its former colonies, the United States of America.
04-02-1789 Electors unanimously chose George Washington to be the first president of the United States.
04-02-1824 J.W. Goodrich introduced rubber galoshes to the public.
04-02-1847 In Maryland, the first U.S. Telegraph Company was established.
04-02-1861 Delegates from six southern states met in Montgomery, AL, to form the Confederate States of America.
04-02-1865 The Hawaiian Board of Education was formed.
04-02-1895 The Van Buren Street Bridge opened in Chicago, IL.
04-02-1901 "Captain Jinks of the Horse Marines" opened in New York City.
04-02-1904 The Russo-Japanese War began after Japan laid seige to Port Arthur.
04-02-1913 Louis Perlman received a patent for his demountable tire-carrying rims.
04-02-1932 The first Winter Olympics were held in the United States at Lake Placid, NY.
04-02-1935 CBS radio presented "Mrs. Wiggs of the Cabbage Patch" for the first time.
04-02-1936 Radium E. became the first radioactive substance to be produced synthetically.
04-02-1938 The play "Our Town", by Thornton Wilder, opened in New York City.
04-02-1941 The United Service Organizations (USO) was created.
04-02-1945 During World War II, U.S. President Roosevelt, British Prime Minister Winston Churchill and Soviet leader Josef Stalin began a conference at Yalta to outline plans for Germany's defeat.
04-02-1948 Ceylon gained independence within the British Commonwealth. The country later became known as Sri Lanka.
04-02-1952 Jackie Robinson was named Director of Communication for NBC. He was the first black executive of a major radio-TV network.
04-02-1953 "The Stooge" premiered at the Paramount Theatre in New York City.
04-02-1957 Smith-Corona Manufacturing Inc., of New York, began selling portable electric typewriters. The first machine weighed 19 pounds.
04-02-1964 The Admistrator of General Services announced that the 24th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution had been ratified. The amendment banned the poll tax.
04-02-1968 The world's largest hovercraft was launched at Cowes, Isle of Wight.
04-02-1973 The Reshef was unveiled as Israel's missile boat.
04-02-1974 Patricia (Patty) Hearst was kidnapped in Berkeley, CA, by the Symbionese Liberation Army.
04-02-1976 An earthquake in Guatemala and Honduras killed more than 22,000 people.
04-02-1985 U.S. President Ronald Reagan's defense budget called for a tripling of the expenditure on the "Star Wars" research program.
04-02-1993 Russian scientists unfurled a giant mirror in orbit and flashed a beam of sunlight across Europe during the night. Observers saw it only as an momentary flash.
04-02-1997 A civil jury in California found O.J. Simpson liable in the death of his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and Ron Goldman. Goldman's parents were awarded $8.5 million in compensatory damages.
04-02-1997 Two Israeli troop-carrying helicopters collided on their way to Lebanon, all 73 soldiers and airmen aboard were killed.
04-02-1997 President Milosevic of Serbia apparently surrendered to the will of his people, ordering his government to recognize opposition victories in local elections held in November 1996.
04-02-1997 Mario Lemieux (Pittsburgh Penguins) scored his 600th National Hockey League (NHL) goal during his 719th game. Lemieux reached the milestone second fastest in history. Gretzky had reached the plateau during his 718th game.
04-02-1998 In northeast Afghanistan, at least 5,000 people were killed in an earthquake that measured 6.1 on the Richter Scale.
04-02-1999 Warplanes from Israel attacked south Lebanon just after rockets were fired toward Israel. No casualies were claimed on either side.
04-02-1999 Gary Coleman was sentenced to a $400 fine, a suspended 90-day jail sentence, and ordered to attend 52 anger-management classes. The sentence stemmed from Coleman assaulting an autograph seeker on July 30, 1998.
04-02-1999 Amadou Diallo, an unarmed West African immigrant, was shot and killed in front of his Bronx home by four plainclothes New York City police officers. The officers had been conducting a nighttime search for a rape suspect.
04-02-2000 Austrian President Thomas Klestil swore in a coalition government that included Joerg Haider's far-right Freedom Party. European Union sanctions were a result of the action.
04-02-2003 Yugoslavia was formally dissolved by lawmakers. The country was replaced with a loose union of its remaining two republics, Serbia and Montenegro.
05-02-1782 The Spanish captured Minorca from the British.
05-02-1783 Sweden recognized the independence of the United States.
05-02-1846 "The Oregon Spectator", based in Oregon City, became the first newspaper published on the Pacific coast.
05-02-1861 Samuel Goodale patented the moving picture peep show machine.
05-02-1885 Congo State was established under Leopold II of Belgium, as a personal possession.
05-02-1881 Phoenix, AZ, was incorporated.
05-02-1900 The U.S. and Britain signed the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty, which gave the U.S. the right to build a canal in Nicaragua but not the right fortify it.
05-02-1917 Mexico's constitution was adopted.
05-02-1924 The BBC time signals, or "pips", from Greenwich Observatory were heard for the first time. They are broadcast every hour.
05-02-1931 Maxine Dunlap became the first woman licensed as a glider pilot.
05-02-1937 U.S. President Roosevelt proposed enlarging the U.S. Supreme Court. The plan failed.
05-02-1940 "Amanda of Honeymoon Hill" debuted on radio.
05-02-1953 The Walt Disney’s film "Peter Pan" opened at the Roxy Theatre in New York City.
05-02-1958 Gamel Abdel Nasser was formally nominated to become the first president of the United Arab Republic.
05-02-1961 The first issue of the "Sunday Telegraph" was published.
05-02-1962 French President Charles De Gaulle called for Algeria's independence.
05-02-1972 Bob Douglas became the first black man elected to the Basketball Hall of Fame in Springfield, MA.
05-02-1987 The Dow Jones industrial average closed above the 2,200-point for the first time. The market closed at 2201.49.
05-02-1988 A pair of indictments were unsealed in Florida, accusing Panama's military leader, Gen. Manuel Antonio Noriega, of bribery and drug trafficking.
05-02-1994 White separatist Byron De La Beckwith was convicted in Jackson, MS, of the 1963 murder of civil rights leader Medgar Evers.
05-02-1997 Switzerland's "Big Three" banks announced they would create a $71 million fund for Holocaust victims and their families.
05-02-1997 Investment bank Morgan Stanley announced a $10 billion merger with Dean Witter.
05-02-1999 Mike Tyson was sentenced to a year in jail for assaulting two people after a car accident on August 31, 1998. Tyson was also fined $5,000, had to serve 2 years of probation, and had to perform 200 hours of community service upon release.
05-02-2001 It was announced the Kelly Ripa would be Regis Philbin's cohost. The show was renamed to "Live! With Regis and Kelly."
05-02-2001 Tom Cruise and Nicole Kidman announced their separation.
05-02-2003 U.S. Secretary of State Colin Powell presented evidence to the U.N. concerning Iraq's material breach of U.N. Resolution 1441.
06-02-1778 The United States gained official recognition from France as the two nations signed the Treaty of Amity and Commerce and the Treaty of Alliance in Paris.
06-02-1788 Massachusetts became the sixth state to ratify the U.S. Constitution.
06-02-1815 The state of New Jersey issued the first American railroad charter to John Stevens.
06-02-1843 "The Virginia Minstrels" opened at the Bowery Amphitheatre in New York City. It was the first minstrel show in America.
06-02-1899 The U.S. Senate ratified a peace treaty between the U.S. and Spain.
06-02-1900 The Holland Senate ratified the 1899 peace conference decree that created in international arbitration court at The Hague.
06-02-1900 U.S. President McKinley appointed W.H. Taft as commissioner to report on the Philippines.
06-02-1911 The first old-age home for pioneers opened in Prescott, AZ.
06-02-1926 The National Football League adopted a rule that made players ineligible for competition until their college class graduated.
06-02-1932 Dog sled racing happened for the first time in Olympic competition.
06-02-1933 The 20th Amendment to the Constitution was declared in effect. The amendment moved the start of presidential, vice-presidential and congressional terms from March to January.
06-02-1937 K. Elizabeth Ohi became the first Japanese woman lawyer when she received her degree from John Marshall Law School in Chicago, IL.
06-02-1950 NBC radio debuted "Dangerous Assignment".
06-02-1952 Britain's King George VI died. His daughter, Elizabeth II, succeeded him.
06-02-1956 St. Patrick Center opened in Kankakee, IL. It was the first circular school building in the United States.
06-02-1959 The U.S., for the first time, successfully test-fired a Titan intercontinental ballistic missile from Cape Canaveral.
06-02-1971 NASA Astronaut Alan B. Shepard used a six-iron that he had brought inside his spacecraft and swung at three golf balls on the surface of the moon.
06-02-1972 Over 500,000 pieces of irate mail arrived at the mail room of CBS-TV, when word leaked out that an edited-for-TV version of the X-rated movie, "The Demand," would be shown.
06-02-1985 The French mineral water company, Perrier, debuted its first new product in 123 years. The new items were water with a twist of lemon, lime or orange.
06-02-1987 President Ronald Reagan turned 76 years old this day and became the oldest U.S. President in history.
06-02-1998 Washington National Airport was renamed for U.S. Ronald Reagan with the signing of a bill by U.S. President Clinton.
06-02-1999 King Hussein of Jordan transferred full political power to his oldest son the Crown Prince Abdullah.
06-02-1999 Excerpts of former White House intern Monica Lewinsky's videotaped testimony were shown at President Clinton's impeachment trial.
06-02-1999 Heavy fighting resumed along the common border between Ethiopia and Eritrea.
06-02-2000 Russia's acting President Vladimir Putin announced that Russian forces had captured Grozny, Chechnya. The capital city had been under the control of Chechen rebels.
06-02-2000 In Finland, Foreign Minister Tarja Halonen became the first woman to be elected president.
06-02-2000 U.S. First Lady Hillary Rodham Clinton formally declared that she was a candidate for a U.S. Senate seat from the state of New York.
06-02-2001 Ariel Sharon was elected Israeli prime minister.
06-02-2002 A federal judge ordered John Walker Lindh to be held without bail pending trial. Lindh was known as the "American Taliban."
07-02-1795 The 11 Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified.
07-02-1818 "Academician" began publication in New York City.
07-02-1877 The first Guernsey Cattle Club was organized in New York City.
07-02-1882 The last bareknuckle fight for the heavyweight boxing championship took place in Mississippi City.
07-02-1893 Elisha Gray patented a machine called the telautograph. It automatically signed autographs to documents.
07-02-1904 In Baltimore, a fire raged for about 30 hours and destroyed over 1,500 buildings.
07-02-1922 DeWitt and Lila Acheson Wallace offered 5,000 copies of "Reader's Digest" magazine for the first time.
07-02-1931 The American opera, "Peter Ibbetson", by Deems Taylor premiered in New York City.
07-02-1936 The U.S. Vice President’s flag was established by executive order.
07-02-1940 "Pinocchio" world premiered at the Center Theatre in Manhattan.
07-02-1941 The Tommy Dorsey Orchestra and Frank Sinatra recorded "Everything Happens to Me".
07-02-1943 The U.S. government announced that shoe rationing would go into effect in two days.
07-02-1944 During World War II, the Germans launched a counteroffensive at Anzio, Italy.
07-02-1959 The play, "The Rivalry," opened in New York City.
07-02-1962 The U.S. government banned all Cuban imports and re-export of U.S. products to Cuba from other countries.
07-02-1966 "Crawdaddy" magazine was published by Paul Williams for the first time.
07-02-1974 The nation of Grenada gained independence from Britain.
07-02-1976 Darryl Sittler (Toronto Maple Leafs) set a National Hockey League (NHL) record when he scored 10 points in a game against the Boston Bruins. He scored six goals and four assists.
07-02-1977 Russia launched Soyuz 24.
07-02-1984 Space shuttle astronauts Bruce McCandless II and Robert L. Stewart made the first untethered space walk.
07-02-1985 "Sports Illustrated" released its annual swimsuit edition. It was the largest regular edition in the magazine’s history at 218 pages.
07-02-1985 "New York, New York" became the official anthem of the Big Apple.
07-02-1986 Haitian President-for-Life Jean-Claude Duvalier fled his country. 28 years of family rule ended.
07-02-1991 The Rev. Jean-Bertrand Aristide was sworn in as Haiti's first democratically elected president.
07-02-1999 King Hussein of Jordan died. His son was sworn in as king four hours after the announcement that his father had died.
07-02-2000 California's legislature declared that February 13 would be "Charels M. Schulz Day."
07-02-2001 Robert Pickett, 47, fired several shots at the White House near the South Lawn. He was subdued after being shot in the knee. No one else was hurt in the incident.
07-02-2003 Nootka Sound, Sandra Bohn was cited for petting a killer whale under the federal Fisheries Act. She was later fined $74.
08-02-1587 Mary, the Queen of Scots, was executed.
08-02-1693 A charter was granted for the College of William and Mary in Williamsburg, VA.
08-02-1802 Simon Willard patented the banjo clock.
08-02-1861 The Confederate States of America was formed.
08-02-1861 A Cheyenne delegation and some Arapohoe leadeers accepted a new settlement (Treaty of Fort Wise) with the U.S. Federal government. The deal ceded most of their land but secured a 600-square mile reservation and annuity payments.
08-02-1896 The Western Conference was formed by representatives of Midwestern universities. The group changed its name to the Big 10 Conference.
08-02-1900 In South Africa, British troops under Gen. Buller were beaten at Ladysmith. The British fled over the Tugela River.
08-02-1904 The Russo-Japanese War began with Japan attacking Russian forces in Manchuria.
08-02-1910 William D. Boyce incorporated the Boy Scouts of America.
08-02-1918 "The Stars and Stripes" newspaper was published for the first time.
08-02-1922 The White House began using radio after U.S. President Harding had it installed.
08-02-1924 The first U.S. execution to make use of gas took place in Nevada State Prison.
08-02-1927 The original version of "Getting Gertie’s Garter" opened at the Hippodrome Theatre in New York City.
08-02-1936 The first National Football League draft was held. Jay Berwanger was the first to be selected. He went to the Philadelphia Eagles. (NFL)
08-02-1952 Queen Elizabeth II ascended to the British throne. Her father, George VI, had died on February 6.
08-02-1963 The Kennedy administration prohibited travel to Cuba and made financial and commercial transactions with Cuba illegal for U.S. citizens.
08-02-1963 Lamar Hunt, owner of the American Football League franchise in Dallas, TX, moved the operation to Kansas City. The new team was named the Chiefs. (NFL)
08-02-1968 In Orangeburg, SC, three college students died during a civil rights protest against a whites-only bowling alley after a confrontation with highway patrolmen.
08-02-1969 The last issue of the "Saturday Evening Post" was published.
08-02-1971 THe Nasdaq stock-market index debuted.
08-02-1973 U.S. Senate leaders named seven members of a select committee to investigate the Watergate scandal.
08-02-1974 The three-man crew of the Skylab space station returned to Earth after 84 days.
08-02-1978 The U.S. Senate deliberations were broadcast on radio for the first time. The subject was the Panama Canal treaties.
08-02-1980 U.S. President Jimmy Carter announced a plan to re-introduce draft registration.
08-02-1985 "The Dukes of Hazzard" ended its 6-1/2 year run on CBS television.
08-02-1993 General Motors sued NBC, alleging that "Dateline NBC" had rigged two car-truck crashes to show that some GM pickups were prone to fires after certain types of crashes. The suit was settled the following day by NBC.
08-02-1999 In Sri Lanka, 23 rebels were killed in fighting with Sri Lankan forces.
08-02-2002 The exhibit "Places of Their Own" opened at the National Museum of Women in the Arts. The works displayed were by Geogia O'Keeffe, Frida Kahlo and Emily Carr.
09-02-1825 The U.S. House of Representatives elected John Quincy Adams president. No candidate had received a majority of electoral votes.
09-02-1861 The Provisional Congress of the Confederate States of America elected Jefferson Davis as its president.
09-02-1870 The United States Weather Bureau was authorized by Congress. The bureau is officially known as the National Weather Service (NWS).
09-02-1884 Thomas Edison and Patrick Kenny executed a patent application for a chemical recording stock quotation telegraph (U.S. Pat. 314,115).
09-02-1885 The first Japanese arrived in Hawaii.
09-02-1895 Volley Ball was invented by W.G. Morgan.
09-02-1895 The first college basketball game was played as Minnesota State School of Agriculture defeated the Porkers of Hamline College, 9-3.
09-02-1900 Dwight F. Davis put up a new tennis trophy to go to the winner in matches against England. The trophy was a silver cup that weighed 36 pounds.
09-02-1909 The first forestry school was incorporated in Kent, Ohio.
09-02-1932 America entered the 2-man bobsled competition for the first time at the Olympic Winter Games held at Lake Placid, NY.
09-02-1942 The U.S. Joint Chiefs of Staff held its first formal meeting to coordinate military strategy during World War II.
09-02-1942 Daylight-saving "War Time" went into effect in the U.S.
09-02-1943 During World War II, the battle of Guadalcanal ended with an American victory over Japanese forces.
09-02-1950 U.S. Senator Joseph McCarthy charged that the State Department was riddled with Communists. This was the beginning of "McCarthyism."
09-02-1953 The movie "Superman" premiered.
09-02-1958 CBS radio debuted "Frontier Gentleman".
09-02-1960 A verbal agreement was reached between representatives of the American and National Football Leagues. Both agreed not to tamper with player contracts.
09-02-1960 The first star was placed on the Hollywood Walk of Fame. The star was for Joanne Woodward.
09-02-1969 The Boeing 747 flew its inaugural flight.
09-02-1971 The San Fernando Valley experienced the Sylmar earthquake that registered 6.4 on the Richter Scale.
09-02-1971 The Apollo 14 spacecraft returned to Earth after mankind's third landing on the moon.
09-02-1975 The Russian Soyuz 17 returned to Earth.
09-02-1984 NBC Entertainment president, Brandon Tartikoff, gave an interviewer the "10 Commandments for TV Programmers."
09-02-1989 Kohlberg Kravis Roberts and Co. completed the $25 billion purchase of RJR Nabisco, Inc.
09-02-1997 "The Simpsons" became the longest-running prime-time animated series. "The Flintstones" held the record previously.
09-02-2001 "Hannibal," the sequel to "Silence of the Lambs", opened in theaters.
10-02-1763 The Treaty of Paris ended the French and Indian War. In the treaty France ceded Canada to England.
10-02-1840 Britain's Queen Victoria married Prince Albert of Saxe Coburg-Gotha.
10-02-1846 Members of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints began their exodus to the west from Illinois.
10-02-1863 In New York City, two of the world’s most famous midgets, General Tom Thumb and Lavinia Warren were married.
10-02-1863 The fire extinguisher was patented by Alanson Crane.
10-02-1870 The city of Anaheim was incorporated for the first time.
10-02-1870 The YWCA was founded in New York City.
10-02-1879 The electric arc light was used for the first time.
10-02-1897 "The New York Times" began printing "All the news that's fit to print" on their front page.
10-02-1920 Major league baseball representatives outlawed pitches that involve tampering with the ball.
10-02-1923 Ink paste was manufactured for the first time by the Standard Ink Company.
10-02-1925 The first waterless gas storage tank was placed in service in Michigan City, IN.
10-02-1933 The singing telegram was introduced by the Postal Telegraph Company of New York City.
10-02-1933 Primo Carnera knocked out Ernie Schaaf in round 13 at Madison Square Garden in New York City. Schaaf died as a result of the knockout punch.
10-02-1934 The first imperforated, ungummed sheets of postage stamps were issued by the U.S. Postal Service in New York City.
10-02-1935 The Pennsylvania Railroad began passenger service with its electric locomotive. The engine was 79-1/2 feet long and weighed 230 tons.
10-02-1942 The Normandie, the former French liner, capsized in New York Harbor. The day before the ship had caught fire while it was being fitted for the U.S. Navy.
10-02-1949 "Death of a Salesman" opened at the Morocco Theatre in New York City.
10-02-1962 The Soviet Union exchanged capture American U2 pilot Francis Gary Powers for the Soviet spy Rudolph Ivanovich Abel being held by the U.S.
10-02-1967 The 25th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. The amendment required the appointment of a vice-president when that office became vacant and instituted new measures in the event of presidential disability.
10-02-1981 The Las Vegas Hilton hotel-casino caught fire. Eight people were killed and 198 were injured.
10-02-1989 Ron Brown became the first African American to head a major U.S. political party when he was elected chairman of the Democratic National Committee.
10-02-1990 South African President F.W. de Klerk announced that black activist Nelson Mandela would be released the next day after 27 years in captivity.
10-02-1992 Mike Tyson was convicted in Indianapolis of raping Desiree Washington, Miss Black American contestant.
10-02-1997 The U.S. Army suspended its top-ranking enlisted soldier, Army Sgt. Major Gene McKinney following allegations of sexual misconduct. McKinney was convicted of obstruction of justice and acquitted of 18 counts alleging sexual harassment of six military women.
10-02-1998 A man became the first to be convicted of committing a hate crime in cyberspace. The college dropout had e-mailed threats to Asian students.
10-02-1998 Voters in Maine repealed a 1997 gay rights law. Maine was the first state to abandone such legislation.
10-02-1999 Avalanches killed at least 10 people when they roared down the French Alps 30 miles from Geneva.
10-02-2005 North Korea publicly announced for the first time that it had nuclear arms. The country also rejected attempts to restart disarmament talks in the near future saying that it needed the weapons as protection against an increasingly hostile United States.
11-02-1752 The Pennsylvania Hospital opened as the very first hospital in America.
11-02-1808 Judge Jesse Fell experimented by burning anthracite coal to keep his house warm. He successfully showed how clean the coal burned and how cheaply it could be used as a heating fuel.
11-02-1812 The term "gerrymandering" had its beginning when the governor of Massachusetts, Elbridge Gerry, signed a redistricting law that favored his party.
11-02-1858 A French girl, Bernadette Soubirous, claimed to have seen a vision of the Virgin Mary near Lourdes.
11-02-1878 The first U.S. bicycle club, Boston Bicycle Club, was formed.
11-02-1929 The Lateran Treaty was signed. Italy now recognized the independence and sovereignty of Vatican City.
11-02-1936 Pumping began the process to build San Francisco's Treasure Island.
11-02-1937 General Motors agreed to recognize the United Automobile Workers Union, thereby ending the current sit-down strike against them.
11-02-1940 NBC radio presented "The Chamber Music Society of Lower Basin Street" for the first time.
11-02-1943 General Dwight David Eisenhower was selected to command the allied armies in Europe.
11-02-1945 During World War II, the Yalta Agreement was signed by U.S. President Roosevelt, British Prime Minister Winston Churchill and Soviet leader Josef Stalin.
11-02-1957 The NHL Players Association was formed in New York City.
11-02-1958 Ruth Carol Taylor was the first black woman to become a stewardess by making her initial flight.
11-02-1960 Jack Paar walked off while live on the air on the "Tonight Show" with four minutes left. He did this in response to censors cutting out a joke from the show the night before.
11-02-1968 The new 20,000 seat Madison Square Garden officially opened in New York. This was the fourth Garden.
11-02-1972 McGraw-Hill Publishing Co. and Life magazine canceled plans to publish an autobiography of Howard Hughes. The work turned out to be fake.
11-02-1979 Nine days after the Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini returned to Iran (after 15 years in exile) power was seized by his followers.
11-02-1982 ABC-TV’s presentation of "The Winds of War" concluded. The 18-hour miniseries cost $40 million to produce and was the most-watched television program in history at the time.
11-02-1984 The tenth Space Shuttle mission returned to Earth safely.
11-02-1989 Rev. Barbara C. Harris became the first woman to be consecrated as a bishop in the Episcopal Church.
11-02-1990 Nelson Mandela was freed after 27 years in captivity.
11-02-1990 In Tokyo, Japan, James "Buster" Douglas knocked out Mike Tyson in the tenth round to win the heavyweight championship.
11-02-1993 Janet Reno was appointed to the position of attorney general by U.S. President Clinton. She was the first female to hold the position.
11-02-2000 The space shuttle Endeavor took off. The mission was to gather information for the most detailed map of the earth ever made.
11-02-2000 Great Britain suspended self-rule in Northern Ireland after the Irish Republican Army (IRA) failed to begin decommissioning (disarming) by a February deadline.
11-02-2002 The six stars on NBC's "Friends" signed a deal for $24 million each for the ninth and final season of the series.
12-02-1541 The city of Santiago, Chile was founded.
12-02-1554 Lady Jane Grey was beheaded after being charged with treason. She had claimed the throne of England for only nine days.
12-02-1733 Savannah, GA, was founded by English colonist James Oglethorpe.
12-02-1870 In the Utah Territory, women gained the right to vote.
12-02-1878 Frederick W. Thayer patented the baseball catcher’s mask.
12-02-1879 The first artificial ice rink opened in North America. It was at Madison Square Garden in New York City, NY.
12-02-1880 The National Croquet League was organized in Philadelphia, PA.
12-02-1892 In the U.S., President Lincoln's birthday was declared to be a national holiday.
12-02-1907 A collision of the steamer Larchmont and a schooler resulted in the death of more than 300 people. The incident occurred off New England's Block Island.
12-02-1909 The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) was founded.
12-02-1912 China's boy emperor Hsuan T'ung announced that he was abdicating, ending the Manchu Ch'ing dynasty. Subsequently, the Republic of China was established.
12-02-1915 The cornerstone of the Lincoln Memorial was laid in Washington, DC.
12-02-1918 All theatres in New York City were shut down in an effort to conserve coal.
12-02-1924 U.S. President Calvin Coolidge made the first presidential political speech on radio.
12-02-1924 "The Eveready Hour" became radio’s first sponsored network program. The National Carbon Company was the first sponsor of a network show.
12-02-1934 The Export-Import Bank was incoporated.
12-02-1940 Mutual Radio presented the first broadcast of the radio play "The Adventures of Superman."
12-02-1968 "Soul on Ice" by Eldridge Cleaver was published for the first time.
12-02-1971 James Cash (J.C.) Penney died at the age of 95. The company closed for business for one-half day as a memorial to the company's founder.
12-02-1973 The State of Ohio went metric, becoming the first in the U.S. to post metric distance signs.
12-02-1973 American prisoners of war were released for the first time during the Vietnam conflict.
12-02-1985 Johnny Carson surprised his audience by shaving the beard he had been wearing on "The Tonight Show."
12-02-1993 In Liverpool, England, a 2-year-old boy, James Bulger, was lured away from his mother at a shopping mall and beaten to death. Two ten-year-old boys were responsible.
12-02-1998 A U.S. federal judge declared that the presidential line-item veto was unconstitutional.
12-02-1999 U.S. President Clinton was acquitted by the U.S. Senate on two impeachment articles. The charges were perjury and obstruction of justice.
12-02-2001 The space probe NEAR landed on the asteroid Eros. It was the first time that any craft had landed on a small space rock.
12-02-2002 Kenneth Lay, former Enron CEO, exercised his constitutional rights and refused to testify to the U.S. Congress about the collapse of Enron.
12-02-2002 The trial of former Yugoslav President Slobodan Milosevic began at the U.N. tribunal in The Hague. Milosevic was accused of war crimes during the Balkan wars of the 1990s.
12-02-2002 Pakistan charged three men in connection with the kidnapping of Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pearl in Karachi.
12-02-2002 Princess Stephanie of Monaco and Franco Knie won a defamation-of-character lawsuit against the Swiss magazine "Facts." The case involved a photomontage created by the magazine.
12-02-2003 The U.N. nuclear agency declared North Korea in violation of international treaties. The complaint was sent to the Security Council.
12-02-2004 Mattel announced that "Barbie" and "Ken" were breaking up. The dolls had met on the set of their first television commercial together in 1961.
13-02-1542 Catherine Howard was executed for adultery. She was the fifth wife of England's King Henry VIII.
13-02-1633 Galileo Galilei arrived in Rome for trial before the Inquisition.
13-02-1635 The Boston Public Latin School was established. It was the first public school building in the United States.
13-02-1741 "The American Magazine," the first magazine in the U.S., was published in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
13-02-1875 Mrs. Edna Kanouse gave birth to America’s first quintuplets. All five of the baby boys died within two weeks.
13-02-1880 Thomas Edison observed what became known as the Edison Effect for the first time.
13-02-1889 Norman Coleman became the first U.S. Secretary of Agriculture.
13-02-1900 The Anglo-German accord of 1899 was ratified by Reichstag, in which Britain renounced rights in Samoa in favor of Germany and the U.S.
13-02-1914 The American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers (known as ASCAP) was formed in New York City. The society was founded to protect the copyrighted musical compositions of its members.
13-02-1920 The League of Nations recognized the continued neutrality of Switzerland.
13-02-1920 The National Negro Baseball League was organized.
13-02-1935 In Flemington, New Jersey, a jury found Bruno Richard Hauptmann guilty of the kidnapping and death of the infant son of Charles and Anne Lindbergh. Hauptmann was later executed for the crimes.
13-02-1937 The comic strip "Prince Valiant" appeared for the first time.
13-02-1939 Virginia Payne became a new character in NBC’s soap opera, "The Carter’s of Elm Street". She played the part of Mrs. Carter.
13-02-1945 During World War II, the Soviets captured Budapest, Hungary, from the German army.
13-02-1945 During World War II, Allied aircraft began bombing the German city of Dresden.
13-02-1947 "Family Theatre" was heard for the first time on Mutual radio.
13-02-1955 Israel acquired 4 of the 7 Dead Sea scrolls.
13-02-1960 France detonated its first atomic bomb.
13-02-1965 Sixteen-year-old Peggy Fleming won the ladies senior figure skating title at Lake Placid, NY.
13-02-1971 South Vietnamese troops invaded Laos. They were backed by U.S. air and artillery support.
13-02-1984 Konstantin Chernenko was chosen to be general secretary of the Soviet Communist Party's Central Committee, succeeding the late Yuri Andropov.
13-02-1985 The Dow Jones industrial average closed at a record high of 1297.92 after it topped the 1300 mark earlier in the trading session.
13-02-1990 In Ottawa, the United States and its European allies forged an agreement with the Soviet Union and East Germany on a two-stage formula to reunite Germany.
13-02-1991 Hundreds of Iraqis were killed by two laser-guided bombs that destroyed an underground facility in Baghdad. U.S. officials identified the facility as a military installation, but Iraqi officials said it was a bomb shelter.
13-02-1997 Astronauts on the space shuttle Discovery brought the Hubble Space Telescope aboard for a tune up. The tune up allowed the telescope to see further into the universe.
13-02-1997 The Dow Jones industrial average passed the 7,000 mark for the first time. The day ended at 7,022.44.
13-02-1999 A bomb exploded just outside a government-owned bank in southern Kosovo. Nine people were killed.
13-02-2000 Charles M. Schulz's last original Sunday "Peanuts" comic strip appeared in newspapers. Schulz had died the day before.
13-02-2001 El Savador was hit with an earthquake that measured 6.6 on the Richter Scale. At least 400 people were killed.
13-02-2002 In Alexandria, VA, John Walker Lindh plead innocent to a 10-count federal indictment. He was charged with conspiring to kill Americans and aiding Osama bin Laden's terrorist network.
13-02-2002 Former New York mayor Rudolph Giuliani received an honorary knighthood from Queen Elizabeth II.
14-02-1778 The Stars and Stripes was carried to a foreign port, in France, for the first time. It was aboard the American ship Ranger.
14-02-1803 Moses Coats received a patent on the apple parer.
14-02-1849 The first photograph of a U.S. President, while in office, was taken by Matthew Brady in New York City. President James Polk was the subject of the picture.
14-02-1859 Oregon became the 33rd member of the Union.
14-02-1876 Alexander Graham Bell filed an application for a patent for the telephone. It was officially issued on March 7, 1876.
14-02-1889 In Los Angeles, CA, oranges began their first trip to the east.
14-02-1895 Oscar Wilde's final play, "The Importance of Being Earnest," opened at the St. James' Theatre in London.
14-02-1899 The U.S. Congress approved voting machines for use in federal elections.
14-02-1900 Russia imposed tighter imperial control over Finland in response to an international petition for Finland's freedom.
14-02-1900 In South Africa, British Gen. Roberts invaded Orange Free State with 20,000 troops.
14-02-1903 The U.S. Department of Commerce and Labor was established.
14-02-1912 The first diesel engine submarine was commissioned in Groton, CT.
14-02-1912 Arizona was admitted as the 48th U.S. state.
14-02-1918 The motion picture "Tarzan of the Apes" was released.
14-02-1920 The League of Women Voters was founded in Chicago. The first president of the organization was Maude Wood Park.
14-02-1929 The "St. Valentine's Day Massacre" took place in Chicago, IL. Seven gangsters who were rivals of Al Capone were killed.
14-02-1932 The U.S. won the first bobsled competition at the Winter Olympic Games at Lake Placid, NY.
14-02-1940 The first porpoise born in captivity arrived at Marineland in Florida.
14-02-1945 Peru, Paraguay, Chile and Ecuador joined the United Nations.
14-02-1946 ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) was unveiled. The device, built at the University of Pennsylvania, was the world's first general purpose electronic computer.
14-02-1954 The TV show "Letter to Loretta" changed its name to "The Loretta Young Show." The show premiered on September 20, 1953.
14-02-1957 Lionel Hampton’s only major musical work, "King David," made its debut at New York’s Town Hall.
14-02-1961 Lawrencium, element 103, was first produced in Berkely, CA.
14-02-1962 U.S. first lady Jacqueline Kennedy gave a tour of the White House on television.
14-02-1966 Rick Mount of Lebanon, IN, became the first high school, male athlete to be pictured on the cover of "Sports Illustrated".
14-02-1966 Wilt Chamberlain of the Philadelphia 76ers set a National Basketball Association (NBA) record as he reached a career high of 20,884 points after seven seasons.
14-02-1968 The fourth Madison Square Gardens opened.
14-02-1979 Twenty-year-old rookie, Don Maloney, of the New York Rangers, scored his first goal in the National Hockey League. It came on his first NHL shot.
14-02-1979 Adolph Dubs, the U.S. ambassador to Afghanistan, was kidnapped in Kabul by Muslim extremists. He was killed in a shootout between his abductors and police.
14-02-1980 Walter Cronkite announced his retirement from the "CBS Evening News."
14-02-1983 A 6-year-old boy became the first person to receive a heart and liver transplants in the same operation.
14-02-1985 Cable News Network reporter Jeremy Levin was freed. He had been being held in Lebanon by extremists.
14-02-1989 Iran's Ayatollah Khomeini called on Muslims to kill Salman Rushdie because of his novel "The Satanic Verses."
14-02-1989 The first satellite of the Global Positioning System was placed into orbit around Earth.
14-02-1989 Union Carbide agreed to pay $470 million to the government of India. The court-ordered settlement was a result of the 1984 Bhopal gas leak disaster.
14-02-1997 Astronauts on the space shuttle Discovery began a series of spacewalks that were required to overhaul the Hubble Space Telescope.
14-02-1998 U.S. authorities officially announced that Eric Rudolph was a suspect in a bombing of an abortion clinic in Alabama.
14-02-2002 The U.S. House of Representatives passed the Shays-Meehan bill. The bill, if passed by the U.S. Senate, would ban millions of unregulated money that goes to the national political parties.
14-02-2002 Sylvester Stallone filed a lawsuit against Kenneth Starr. The suit alleged that Starr had given bad advice about selling Planet Hollywood stock.
14-02-2003 In Madrid, Spain, a ceramic plate with a bullfighting motif painted by Pablo Picasso in 1949 was stolen from an art show. The plate was on sale for $12,400.
15-02-1758 Mustard was advertised for the first time in America.
15-02-1764 The city of St. Louis was established.
15-02-1799 Printed ballots were authorized for use in elections in the state of Pennsylvania.
15-02-1842 Adhesive postage stamps were used for the first time by the City Dispatch Post (Office) in New York City.
15-02-1879 U.S. President Hayes signed a bill that allowed female attorneys to argue cases before the U.S. Supreme Court.
15-02-1898 The USS Maine sank when it exploded in Havana Harbor for unknown reasons. More than 260 crew members were killed.
15-02-1900 The British threaten to use natives in their war with the Boers.
15-02-1903 Morris and Rose Michtom, Russian immigrants, introduced the first teddy bear in America.
15-02-1932 George Burns and Gracie Allen debuted as regulars on "The Guy Lombardo Show" on CBS radio.
15-02-1933 U.S. President-elect Roosevelt escaped an assination attempt in Miami. Chicago Mayor Anton J. Cermak was killed in the attack.
15-02-1942 During World War II, Singapore surrendered to the Japanese.
15-02-1943 "My True Story" was heard for the first time on ABC radio.
15-02-1946 Edith Houghton, at age 33, was signed as a baseball scout by the Philadelphia Phillies becoming the first female scout in the major leagues.
15-02-1953 The first American to win the women’s world figure skating championship was 17-year-old Tenley Albright.
15-02-1961 A Boeing 707 crashed in Belgium killing 73 people.
15-02-1962 CBS-TV bought the exclusive rights to college football games from the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) for a figure of $10,200,000.
15-02-1965 Canada displayed its new red and white maple leaf flag. The flag was to replace the old Red Ensign standard.
15-02-1982 During a storm, the Ocean Ranger, a drilling rig, sank off the coast of Newfoundland. 84 men were killed.
15-02-1985 The Center for Disease Control reported that more than half of all nine-year-olds in the U.S. showed no sign of tooth decay.
15-02-1989 After nine years of intervention, the Soviet Union announced that the remainder of its troops had left Afghanistan.
15-02-1991 The leaders of Czechoslovakia, Hungary and Poland signed the Visegard agreement, in which they pledged to cooperate in transforming thier countties to free-market economies.
15-02-1995 The FBI arrested Kevin Mitnick and charged him with cracking security in some of the nation's most protected computers. He served five years in jail.
16-02-1741 Benjamin Franklin published America’s second magazine, "The General Magazine and Historical Chronicle".
16-02-1804 A raid was led by Lt. Stephen Decatur to burn the U.S. Navy frigate Philadelphia. The ship had been taken by pirates.
16-02-1857 The National Deaf Mute College was incorporated in Washington, DC. It was the first school in the world for advanced education of the deaf. The school was later renamed Gallaudet College.
16-02-1862 During the U.S. Civil War, about 14,000 Confederate soldiers surrendered to Gen. Ulysses S. Grant at Fort Donelson, TN.
16-02-1868 The Jolly Corks organization, in New York City, changed it name to the Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks (BPOE).
16-02-1883 "Ladies Home Journal" began publication.
16-02-1914 The first airplane flight between Los Angeles and San Francisco took place.
16-02-1918 Lithuania proclaimed its independence.
16-02-1923 Howard Carter unsealed the burial chamber of Egyptian Pharaoh Tutankhamen. The next day he entered the chamber with several invited guests. He had originally found the tomb on November 4, 1922.
16-02-1932 The first fruit tree patent was issued to James E. Markham for a peach tree which ripens later than other varieties.
16-02-1937 Wallace H. Carothers received a patent for nylon. Carothers was a research chemist for Du Pont.
16-02-1938 The U.S. Federal Crop Insurance program was authorized.
16-02-1945 During World War II, U.S. troops landed on the island of Corregidor in the Philippines.
16-02-1946 The first commercially designed helicopter was tested in Connecticut.
16-02-1948 NBC-TV began airing its first nightly newscast, "The Camel Newsreel Theatre", which consisted of Fox Movietone newsreels.
16-02-1959 Fidel Castro seized power in Cuba after the overthrow of President Fulgencio Batista.
16-02-1960 The U.S.S. Triton began the first circumnavigation of the globe under water. The trip ended on May 10.
16-02-1962 Jimmy Bostwick defeated his brother, Pete, to win the U.S. Open Court-Tennis championships for the third time.
16-02-1963 Paul Anka married Marie-Ann DeZogheb in Paris.
16-02-1968 In the U.S., the first 911 emergency telephone system was inaugurated in Haleyville, AL.
16-02-1970 Joe Frazier began his reign as the undefeated heavyweight world champion when he knocked out Jimmy Ellis in five rounds. He lost the title on January 22, 1973, when he lost for the first time in his professional career to George Foreman.
16-02-1972 Wilt Chamberlain (Los Angeles Lakers) reached the 30,000-point mark in his NBA career during a game against the Phoenix Suns.
16-02-1977 The Anglican archbishop of Uganda, Janani Luwum, was killed in automobile accident. Two other men were also killed.
16-02-1985 "Kojak" returned to network television after an absence of seven years with the CBS-TV special, "Kojak: The Belarus File."
16-02-1987 John Demjanjuk went on trial in Jerusalem. He was accused of being "Ivan the Terrible", a guard at the Treblinka concentration camp. He was convicted, but the Israeli Supreme Court overturned the ruling.
16-02-1989 Investigators in Lockerbie, Scotland, announced that a bomb hidden inside a radio-cassette player was the reason that Pan Am Flight 103 was brought down the previous December. All 259 people aboard and 11 on the ground were killed.
16-02-1999 A bomb exploded at the government headquarters in Uzbekistan. Gunfire followed the incident. The event apparently was an attempt on the life of President Islam Karimov.
16-02-1999 Kurds seized embassies and held hostages across Europe following Turkey's arrest of Kurdish rebel leader Abdullah Ocalan.
16-02-1999 Testimony began in the Jasper, TX, trial of John William King. He was charged with murder in the gruesome dragging death of James Byrd Jr. King was later convicted and sentenced to death.
16-02-2002 The operator of a crematory in Noble, GA, was arrested after dozens of corpses were found stacked in storage sheds and scattered around in the surrounding woods.
16-02-2005 The Kyoto global warming pact went into effect in 140 nations.
16-02-2005 The NHL announced the cancellation of the 2004-2005 season due to a labor dispute. It was the first time a major sports league in North America lost an entire season to a labor dispute.
17-02-1801 The U.S. House of Representatives broke an electoral tie between Thomas Jefferson and Aaron Burr. Jefferson was elected president and Burr became vice president.
17-02-1817 The first gaslit streetlights appeared on the streets of Baltimore, MD.
17-02-1865 Columbia, SC, burned. The Confederates were evacuating and the Union Forces were moving in.
17-02-1876 Julius Wolff was credited with being the first to can sardines.
17-02-1878 In San Francisco, CA, the first large city telephone exchange opened. It had only 18 phones.
17-02-1897 The National Congress of Mothers was organized in Washington, DC, by Alice McLellan Birney and Phoebe Apperson Hearst. It was the forerunner of the National PTA.
17-02-1913 The Armory Show opened at the 69th Regiment Armory in New York City. The full-scale exhibition was of contemporary paintings and was organized by the Association of Painters and Sculptors.
17-02-1924 Swimmer Johnny Weissmuller set a world record in the 100-yard freestyle. He did it with a time of 52-2/5 seconds in Miami, FL.
17-02-1933 "Newsweek" was first published.
17-02-1933 Blondie Boopadoop married Dagwood Bumstead three years after Chic Young’s popular strip first debuted.
17-02-1934 The first high school automobile driver’s education course was introduced in State College, PA.
17-02-1944 During World War II, the Battle of Eniwetok Atoll began. U.S. forces won the battle on February 22, 1944.
17-02-1947 The Voice of America began broadcasting to the Soviet Union.
17-02-1964 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that congressional districts within each state had to be approximately equal in population. (Westberry v. Sanders)
17-02-1965 Comedienne Joan Rivers made her first guest appearances on "The Tonight Show starring Johnny Carson" on NBC-TV.
17-02-1968 The Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame opened in Springfield, MA.
17-02-1985 U.S. Postage stamp prices were raised from 20 cents to 22 cents for first class mail.
17-02-1992 In Milwaukee, serial killer Jeffrey Dahmer was sentenced to life in prison. In November of 1994, he was beaten to death in prison.
17-02-1995 Colin Ferguson was convicted of six counts of murder in the December 1993 Long Island Rail Road shootings. He was later sentenced to a minimum of 200 years in prison.
17-02-1996 World chess champion Garry Kasparov beat the IBM supercomputer "Deep Blue" in Philadelphia, PA.
17-02-1997 Pepperdine University announced that Kenneth Starr was leaving the Whitewater probe to take a full-time job at the school. Starr reversed the announcement four days later.
17-02-2005 U.S. President George W. Bush named John Negroponte as the first national intelligence director.
18-02-1564 The artist Michelanglelo died in Rome.
18-02-1685 Robert Cavelier, Sieur de LaSalle established Fort St. Louis at Matagorda Bay, and thus formed the basis for France's claim to Texas.
18-02-1735 The first opera performed in America. The work was "Flora" (or "Hob in the Well") was presented in Charleston, SC.
18-02-1841 The first continuous filibuster in the U.S. Senate began. It lasted until March 11th.
18-02-1861 Jefferson Davis was inaugurated as the President of the Confederate States.
18-02-1885 Mark Twain's "Adventures of Huckleberry Finn" was published in the U.S. for the first time.
18-02-1900 A San Francisco man claimed that X-rays had cured his cancer.
18-02-1913 The famous French painting "Nude Descending a Staircase", by the French artist, Marcel Duchamp, was displayed at an "Armory Show" in New York City.
18-02-1930 Elm Farm Ollie became the first cow to fly in an airplane.
18-02-1930 The planet Pluto was discovered by Clyde Tombaugh. The discovery was made as a result of photographs taken in January 1930.
18-02-1932 Sonja Henie won her 6th world women’s figure skating title in Montreal, Canada.
18-02-1938 "The Big Broadcast of 1938" was released.
18-02-1949 "Yours Truly Johnny Dollar" debuted on CBS radio.
18-02-1952 Greece and Turkey became members of NATO.
18-02-1953 "Bwana Devil" opened. It was the first three-dimensional feature.
18-02-1953 Lucille Ball and Desi Arnaz signed a contract worth $8,000,000 to continue the "I Love Lucy" TV show through 1955.
18-02-1964 "Any Wednesday" opened at the Music Box Theatre in New York City. The play established Gene Hackman as an actor.
18-02-1970 The Chicago Seven defendants were found innocent of conspiring to incite riots at the 1968 Democratic national convention.
18-02-1972 The California Supreme Court struck down the state's death penalty.
18-02-1977 The space shuttle Enterprise went on its maiden "flight" sitting on top of a Boeing 747.
18-02-1984 Reed Larson (Detroit Red Wings) got two assists to become the highest scoring, American-born player in the history of the National Hockey League. Larson broke the record by scoring his 432nd point.
18-02-1987 The executives of the Girl Scout movement decided to change the color of the scout uniform from the traditional Girl Scout green to the newer Girl Scout blue.
18-02-1998 In Russia, money shortages resulted in the shutting down of three plants that produced nuclear weapons.
18-02-1998 In Nevada, two white separatists were arrested and accused of plotting a bacterial attack on subways in New York City.
18-02-2000 The U.S. Commerce Department reported a deficit in trade goods and services of $271.3 billion for 1999. It was the largest calender-year trade gap in U.S. history.
18-02-2001 NASCAR driver Dale Earnhardt, Sr., was killed in a crash during the Daytona 500 race.
18-02-2003 In South Korea, at least 120 people were killed when a man lit a fire on a subway train.
19-02-1807 Former U.S. Vice President Aaron Burr was arrested in Alabama. He was later tried and acquitted on charges of treason.
19-02-1846 The Texas state government was formally installed in Austin.
19-02-1856 The tintype camera was patented by Professor Hamilton L. Smith.
19-02-1864 The Knights of Pythias was founded in Washington, DC. A dozen members formed what became Lodge No. 1.
19-02-1878 Thomas Alva Edison patented a music player (the phonograph).
19-02-1881 Kansas became the first state to prohibit all alcoholic beverages.
19-02-1922 Ed Wynn became the first big-name, vaudeville talent to sign on as a radio talent.
19-02-1942 U.S. President Roosevelt signed an executive order giving the military the authority to relocate and intern Japanese-Americans.
19-02-1942 The New York Yankees announced that they would admit 5,000 uniformed servicemen free to each of their home ball games during the coming season.
19-02-1942 Approximately 150 Japanese warplanes attacked the Australian city of Darwin.
19-02-1945 During World War II, about 30,000 U.S. Marines landed on Iwo Jima.
19-02-1949 Bollingen Foundation and Yale University awarded the first Bollingen Prize in poetry ($5,000) to Ezra Pound.
19-02-1953 The State of Georgia approved the first literature censorship board in the U.S. Newspapers were excluded from the new legislation.
19-02-1959 Cyprus was granted its independence with the signing of an agreement with Britain, Turkey and Greece.
19-02-1963 The Soviet Union informed U.S. President Kennedy it would withdraw "several thousand" of its troops in Cuba.
19-02-1985 Mickey Mouse was welcomed to China as part of the 30th anniversary of Disneyland. The touring mouse played 30 cities in 30 days.
19-02-1985 William Schroeder became the first artificial-heart patient to leave the confines of the hospital.
19-02-1985 Cherry Coke was introduced by the Coca-Cola Company.
19-02-1986 The U.S. Senate approved a treaty outlawing genocide. The pact had been submitted 37 years earlier for ratification.
19-02-1986 The Soviet Union launched the Mir space station.
19-02-1987 A controversial, anti-smoking ad aired for the first time on television. It featured Yul Brynner who died shortly after of lung cancer.
19-02-1997 Deng Xiaoping of China died at the age of 92. He was the last of China's major revolutionaries.
19-02-1999 Dennis Franz received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
19-02-2001 The museum at the Oklahoma City National Memorial Center was dedicated.
19-02-2002 NASA's Mars Odyssey spacecraft began using its thermal emission imaging system to map Mars.
20-02-1673 The first recorded wine auction took place in London.
20-02-1725 The first known Indian scalping by white men was reported in the New Hampshire colony.
20-02-1792 U.S. President George Washington signed the Postal Service Act thereby creating the U.S. Post Office.
20-02-1809 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled the power of the federal government was greater than that of any individual state.
20-02-1815 The USS Constitution, under Captain Charles Stewart fought the British ships Cyane and Levant. The Constitution captures both, but lost the Levant after encountering a British squadron. The Constitution and the Cyane returned to New York safely on May 15, 1815. The Cyane was purchased and became the USS Cyane.
20-02-1839 The U.S. Congress prohibited dueling in the District of Columbia.
20-02-1872 Luther Crowell received a patent for a machine that manufactured paper bags.
20-02-1872 The Metropolitan Museum of Art opened in New York City.
20-02-1872 Silas Noble and J.P. Cooley patented the toothpick manufacturing machine.
20-02-1873 The University of California got its first Medical School.
20-02-1880 The American Bell Company was incorporated.
20-02-1901 The first territorial legislature of Hawaii convened.
20-02-1921 The motion picture "The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse" was released starring Rudolph Valentino.
20-02-1931 The U.S. Congress allowed California to build the Oakland Bay Bridge.
20-02-1933 The U.S. House of Representatives completed congressional action on the amendment to repeal Prohibition.
20-02-1944 "Big Week" began as U.S. bombers began raiding German aircraft manufacturing centers during World War II.
20-02-1952 Emmett L. Ashford became the first black umpire in organized baseball. He was authorized to be a substitute in the Southwestern International League.
20-02-1952 "The African Queen" opened at the Capitol Theatre in New York City.
20-02-1958 Racing jockey Eddie Arcaro got win number 4,000, as he rode the winner at Santa Anita race track in Southern California.
20-02-1962 John Glenn made space history when he orbited the world three times in 4 hours, 55 minutes. He was the first American to orbit the Earth. He was aboard the Friendship 7 Mercury capsule.
20-02-1965 Ranger 8 crashed on the moon after sending back thousands of pictures of its surface.
20-02-1987 After 11 years, David Hartman left ABC’s "Good Morning America."
20-02-1987 A bomb exploded in a computer store in Salt Lake City, UT. The blast was blamed on the Unabomber.
20-02-1993 Two ten-year-old boys were charged by police in Liverpool, England, in the abduction and death of a toddler. The two boys were later convicted.
20-02-1998 American Tara Lipinski, at age 15, became the youngest gold medalist in winter Olympics history when she won the ladies' figure skating title at Nagano, Japan.
20-02-2001 FBI Agent Robert Phillip Hanssen was arrested and charged with spying for the Russians for 15 years.
20-02-2002 In Reqa Al-Gharbiya, Egypt, a fire raced through a train killing at least 370 people and injuring at least 65.
20-02-2003 In West Warwick, RI, 99 people were killed when fire destroyed the nightclub The Station. The fire started with sparks from a pyrotechnic display being used by Great White. Ty Longley, guitarist for Great White, was one of the victims in the fire.
21-02-1804 The first self-propelled locomotive on rails was demonstrated in Wales.
21-02-1842 John J. Greenough patented the sewing machine.
21-02-1858 The first electric burglar alame was installed in Boston, MA.
21-02-1866 Lucy B. Hobbs became the first woman to graduate from a dental school. The school was the Ohio College of Dental Surgery in Cincinnati.
21-02-1874 The Oakland Daily Tribune began publication.
21-02-1878 The first telephone directories issued in the U.S. were distributed to residents in New Haven, CT. It was a single page of only fifty names.
21-02-1904 The National Ski Association was formed in Ishpeming, MI.
21-02-1916 During World War I, the Battle of Verdun began in France. The battle ended on December 18, 1916 with a French victory over Germany.
21-02-1925 The first issue of "The New Yorker" was published.
21-02-1932 William N. Goodwin patented the camera exposure meter.
21-02-1943 "Free World Theatre" debuted on the Blue network (now ABC radio).
21-02-1945 "The Lion and the Mouse" was first broadcast on "Brownstone Theatre."
21-02-1947 Edwin Land demonstrated the Polaroid Land Camera to the Optical Society of America in New York City. It was the first camera to take, develop and print a picture on photo paper all in about 60 seconds. The photos were black and white. The camera went on sale the following year.
21-02-1950 The first International Pancake Race was held in Liberal, Kansas.
21-02-1965 Malcolm X was assassinated in New York City at the age of 39 by assassins identified as Black Muslims.
21-02-1968 An agreement between baseball players and club owners increased the minimum salary for major league players to $10,000 a year.
21-02-1973 Israeli fighter planes shot down a Libyan Airlines jet over the Sinai Desert. More than 100 people were killed.
21-02-1975 Former U.S. Attorney General John N. Mitchell and former White House aides H.R. Haldeman and John D. Ehrlichman were sentenced to 2 1/2 to 8 years in prison for their roles in the Watergate cover-up.
21-02-1988 In Baton Rouge, LA, TV evangelist Jimmy Swaggart confessed to his congregation that he was guilty of an unspecified sin. He announced that he was leaving the pulpit temporarily. Swaggart had been linked to an admitted prostitute.
21-02-1989 U.S. President Bush called Ayatollah Khomeini's death warrant against "Satanic Verses" author Salman Rushdie "deeply offensive to the norms of civilized behavior."
21-02-1995 Chicago stockbroker Steve Fossett became the first person to fly solo across the Pacific Ocean in a balloon. He landed in Leader, Saskatchewan, Canada.
21-02-1999 India's Prime Minister Atal Bihair Vajpayee concluded two days of meeting with Pakistan's Prime Minister Mohammad Nowaz Sharif.
21-02-2000 David Letterman returned to his Late Night show about five weeks after having an emergency quintuple heart bypass operation.
21-02-2003 David Hasselhoff and his wife Pamela were injured in a motorcycle accident. The accident was caused by a strong gust of wind. Hasselhoff fractured his lower back and broke several ribs. His wife fractured her left ankle and right wrist.
22-02-1630 Quadequine introduced popcorn to English colonists at their first Thanksgiving dinner.
22-02-1784 "Empress of China", a U.S. merchant ship, left New York City for the Far East.
22-02-1819 Spain ceded Florida to the United States.
22-02-1855 The U.S. Congress voted to appropriate $200,000 for continuance of the work on the Washington Monument. The next morning the resolution was tabled and it would be 21 years before the Congress would vote on funds again. Work was continued by the Know-Nothing Party in charge of the project.
22-02-1859 U.S. President Buchanan approved the Act of February 22, 1859, which incorporated the Washington National Monument Society "for the purpose of completing the erection now in progress of a great National Monument to the memory of Washington at the seat of the Federal Government."
22-02-1860 Organized baseball’s first game was played in San Francisco, CA.
22-02-1865 In the U.S., Tennessee adopted a new constitution that abolished slavery.
22-02-1879 In Utica, NY, Frank W. Woolworth opened his first 5 and 10-cent store.
22-02-1885 The Washington Monument was officially dedicated in Washington, DC. It opened to the public in 1889.
22-02-1892 "Lady Windermere's Fan", by Oscar Wilde, was first performed.
22-02-1920 The first dog race track to use an imitation rabbit opened in Emeryville, CA.
22-02-1923 The first successful chinchilla farm opened in Los Angeles, CA. It was the first farm of its kind in the U.S.
22-02-1924 U.S. President Calvin Coolidge delivered the first presidential radio broadcast from the White House.
22-02-1954 ABC radio’s popular "Breakfast Club" program was simulcast on TV for the first time.
22-02-1969 Barbara Jo Rubin became the first woman to win a U.S. thoroughbred horse race.
22-02-1973 The U.S. and Communist China agreed to establish liaison offices.
22-02-1984 The U.S. Census Bureau statistics showed that the state of Alaska was the fastest growing state of the decade with an increase in population of 19.2 percent.
22-02-1994 The U.S. Justice Department charged Aldrich Ames and his wife with selling national secrets to the Soviet Union. Ames was later convicted to life in prison. Ames' wife received a 5-year prison term.
22-02-1997 Scottish scientist Ian Wilmut and colleagues announced that an adult sheep had been successfully cloned. Dolly, the first cloned sheep to be born was born in July 1996.
22-02-2002 In the Philippines, An MH-47E Chinook helicopter crashed into the ocean. All 10 men aboard were killed.
23-02-1574 France began the 5th holy war against the Huguenots.
23-02-1660 Charles XI became the king of Sweden.
23-02-1792 The Humane Society of Massachusetts was incorporated.
23-02-1813 The first U.S. raw cotton-to-cloth mill was founded in Waltham, MA.
23-02-1820 The Cato Street conspiracy was uncovered.
23-02-1821 The Philadelphia College of Apothecaries established the first pharmacy college.
23-02-1822 Boston was incorporated as a city.
23-02-1836 In San Antonio, TX, the siege of the Alamo began.
23-02-1839 In Boston, MA, William F. Harnden organized the first express service between Boston and New York City. It was the first express service in the U.S.
23-02-1847 Santa Anna was defeated at the Battle of Buena Vista in Mexico by U.S. troops under Gen. Zachary.
23-02-1861 U.S. President-elect Lincoln arrived secretly in Washington to take his office after an assassination attempt in Baltimore.
23-02-1861 Texas became the 7th state to secede from the Union.
23-02-1870 The state of Mississippi was readmitted to the Union.
23-02-1874 Walter Winfield patented a game called "sphairistike." More widely known as lawn tennis.
23-02-1875 J. Palisa discovered asteroid #143 (aka Adria).
23-02-1883 Alabama became the first U.S. state to enact an antitrust law.
23-02-1886 Charles M. Hall completed his invention of aluminum.
23-02-1887 The French/Italian Riviera was hit by an earthquake that killed about 2,000.
23-02-1896 The Tootsie Roll was introduced by Leo Hirshfield.
23-02-1898 In France, Emile Zola was imprisoned for his letter, "J'accuse," which accused the government of anti-Semitism and wrongly jailing Alfred Dreyfus.
23-02-1900 The Battle of Hart's Hill took place in South Africa between the Boers and the British army.
23-02-1904 The U.S. acquired control of the Panama Canal Zone for $10 million.
23-02-1905 The Rotary Club was founded in Chicago, IL, by Attorney Paul Harris and three others.
23-02-1910 In Philadelphia, PA, the first radio contest was held.
23-02-1915 Nevada began enforcing convenient divorce law.
23-02-1916 The U.S. Congress authorizes the McKinley Memorial $1 gold coin.
23-02-1919 The Fascist Party was formed in Italy by Benito Mussolini.
23-02-1927 The Federal Radio Commission began assigning frequencies, hours of operation and power allocations for radio broadcasters. On July 1, 1934 the name was changed to the Federal Communications Commission (FCC).
23-02-1940 Russian troops conquered Lasi Island.
23-02-1940 Walt Disney's animated movie "Pinocchio" was released.
23-02-1945 The 28th Regiment of the Fifth Marine Division of the U.S. Marines reached the top of Mount Surabachi. A photograph of these Marines raising the American flag was taken.
23-02-1954 The first mass vaccination of children against polio began in Pittsburgh, PA.
23-02-1955 The French government was formed by Edgar Faure.
23-02-1957 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the NFL operations did fall within coverage of antitrust laws.
23-02-1958 Juan Fangio, 5-time world diving champion, was kidnapped by Cuban rebels.
23-02-1963 The 24th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. It prohibited poll taxes in federal elections.
23-02-1966 The Bitar government in Syria was ended with a military coup.
23-02-1967 Jim Ryun set a record in the half-mile run when ran it in 1:48.3.
23-02-1968 Wilt Chamberlain (Philadelphia 76ers) became the first player to score 25,000 career points in the NBA.
23-02-1970 Guyana became a republic.
23-02-1974 The Symbionese Liberation Army demanded $4 million more for the release of Patty Hearst. Hearst had been kidnapped on February 4th.
23-02-1980 Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini declared that Iran's new parliament would have to decide the fate of the hostages taken on November 4, 1979, at the U.S. embassy in Tehran.
23-02-1985 The TV show "Gimme a Break" was broadcast live before a studio audience. It was the first TV sitcom to be seen live since the 1950s.
23-02-1991 During the Persian Gulf War, ground forces crossed the border of Saudi Arabia into the country of Iraq. Less than four days later the war was over due to the surrender or withdraw of Iraqi forces.
23-02-1993 Gary Coleman won a $1,280,000 lawsuit against his parents.
23-02-1995 The Dow Jones Industrial closed about 4,000 for the first time at 4,003.33.
23-02-1997 It was announced by scientists in Scotland that they had succeeded in cloning an adult mammal. The animal was a lamb named "Dolly."
23-02-1997 NBC-TV aired "Schindler's List." It was completely uncensored.
23-02-1997 Ali Hassan Abu Kamal, a Palestinian teacher, opened fire on the 86th-floor observation deck of New York City's Empire State Building. He killed one person and wounded six more before killing himself.
23-02-1998 In central Florida, tornadoes killed 42 people and damaged and/or destroyed about 2,600 homes and businesses.
23-02-1999 In Ankara, Turkey, Abdullah Ocalan was charged with treason. The prosecutors were seeking the death penalty for the Kurdish rebel leader.
23-02-1999 White supremacict John William King was found guilty of kidnapping and murdering James Byrd Jr. Byrd was dragged behind a truck for two miles on a country road in Texas.
23-02-2000 Robby Knieval made a successful motorcycle jump of 200 feet over an oncoming train.
23-02-2005 The New York, NY, city medical examiner's office annouced that it had exhausted all efforts to identify the remains of the people killed at the World Trade Center on September 11, 2001, due to the limits of DNA technology. About 1,600 people had been identified leaving more than 1,100 unidentified.
24-02-1803 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled itself to be the final interpreter of all constitutional issues.
24-02-1821 Mexico declared independence from Spain.
24-02-1835 "Siwinowe Kesibwi" (The Shawnee Sun) was issued as the first Indian language monthly publication in the U.S.
24-02-1839 Mr. William S. Otis received a patent for the steam shovel.
24-02-1848 The Communist Manifesto was published.
