24-02-1857 The Los Angeles Vinyard Society was organized.
24-02-1857 The first shipment of perforated postage stamps was received by the U.S. Government.
24-02-1863 Arizona was organized as a territory.
24-02-1866 In Washington, DC, an American flag made entirely of American bunting was displayed for the first time.
24-02-1868 The first parade to use floats occurred in New Orleans at Mardi Gras.
24-02-1868 The U.S. House of Representatives impeached President Andrew Johnson due to his attempt to dismiss Secretary of War Edwin M. Stanton. The U.S. Senate later acquitted Johnson.
24-02-1886 Thomas Edison and Mina Miller were married.
24-02-1900 New York City Mayor Van Wyck signed the contract to begin work on New York's first rapid transit tunnel. The tunnel would link Manhattan and Brooklyn. The ground breaking ceremony was on March 24, 1900.
24-02-1903 In Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, an area was leased to the U.S. for a naval base.
24-02-1924 Johnny ‘Tarzan’ Weissmuller broke the world’s record in the 100-meter swimming event. He did it in 57 2/5 seconds.
24-02-1925 A thermit was used for the first time. It was used to break up a 250,000-ton ice jam that had clogged the St. Lawrence River near Waddington, NY.
24-02-1938 The first nylon bristle toothbrush was made. It was the first time that nylon yarn had been used commercially.
24-02-1942 The U.S. Government stopped shipments of all 12-gauge shotguns for sporting use for the wartime effort.
24-02-1942 The Voice of America (VOA) aired for the first time.
24-02-1945 During World War II, the Philippine capital of Manilla, was liberated by U.S. soldiers.
24-02-1946 Juan Peron was elected president of Argentina.
24-02-1956 The city of Cleveland invoked a 1931 law that barred people under the age of 18 from dancing in public without an adult guardian.
24-02-1980 NBC premiered the TV movie "Harper Valley P.T.A."
24-02-1981 Buckingham Palace announced the engagement of Britain's Prince Charles to Lady Diana Spencer.
24-02-1983 The Dow Jones industrial average closed above the 1100 mark for the first time.
24-02-1983 A U.S.congressional commission released a report that condemned the internment of Japanese-Americans during World War II.
24-02-1987 Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, of the Los Angeles Lakers, got his first three-point shot in the NBA.
24-02-1987 An exploding supernova was discovered in the Large Magellanic Cloud galaxy.
24-02-1988 The U.S. Supreme Court overturned a $200,000 award to Rev. Jerry Falwell that had been won against "Hustler" magazine. The ruling expanded legal protections for parody and satire.
24-02-1989 Iran’s Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini sentenced Salman Rushdie to death for his novel "The Satanic Verses". A bounty of one to three-million-dollars was also put on Rushidie's head.
24-02-1989 A United Airlines 747 jet rips open in flight killing 9 people. The flight was from Honolulu to New Zealand.
24-02-1992 "Wayne's World" opened in U.S. theaters.
24-02-1992 Tracy Gold began working on the set of "Growing Pains" again. She had left the show due to anorexia.
24-02-1994 In Los Angeles, Garrett Morris was shot during a robbery attempt. He eventually recovered from his injury.
24-02-1997 The U.S. The Food and Drug Administration named six brands of birth control as safe and effective "morning-after" pills for preventing pregnancy.
24-02-1997 Dick Enberg received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
24-02-1999 In southeast China, a domestic airliner crashed killing all 64 passengers.
25-02-1570 England's Queen Elizabeth I was excommunicated by Pope Pius V.
25-02-1751 Edward Willet displayed the first trained monkey act in the U.S.
25-02-1793 The department heads of the U.S. government met with U.S. President Washington for the first Cabinet meeting on U.S. record.
25-02-1836 Samuel Colt received a patent for the Colt 45.
25-02-1901 The United States Steel Corp. was incorporated by J.P. Morgan.
25-02-1913 The 16th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. It authorized a graduated income tax.
25-02-1919 The state of Oregon became the first state to place a tax on gasoline. The tax was 1 cent per gallon.
25-02-1928 The Federal Radio Commission issued the first U.S. television license to Charles Jenkins Laboratories in Washington, DC.
25-02-1930 The bank check photographing device was patented.
25-02-1933 The first aircraft carrier, Ranger, was launched.
25-02-1940 The New York Rangers and the Montreal Canadiens played in the first hockey game to be televised in the U.S. The game was aired on W2WBS in New York with one camera in a fixed position. The Rangers beat the Canadiens 6-2.
25-02-1948 Communists seized power in Czechoslovakia.
25-02-1950 "Your Show of Shows" debuted on NBC.
25-02-1956 Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev criticized the late Josef Stalin in a speech before a Communist Party congress in Moscow.
25-02-1972 Germany gave a $5 million ransom to Arab terrorist who had hijacked a jumbo jet.
25-02-1986 Phillippino President Ferdinand E. Marcos fled the Philippines after 20 years of rule after a tainted election.
25-02-1999 William King was sentenced to death for the racial murder of James Byrd Jr in Jasper, TX. Two other men charged were later convicted for thier involvement.
25-02-1999 In Moscow, China's Prime Minister Zhu Rongji and Russia's President Boris Yeltsin discussed trade and other issues.
25-02-2000 In Albany, NY, a jury acquitted four New York City police officers of second-degree murder and lesser charges in the February 1999 shooting death of Amadou Diallo.
26-02-1815 Napoleon Bonaparte escaped from the Island of Elba. He then began his second conquest of France.
26-02-1848 The second Frech Republic was proclaimed.
26-02-1863 U.S. President Lincoln signed the National Currency Act.
26-02-1870 In New York City, the first pneumatic-powered subway line was opened to the public.
26-02-1881 S.S. Ceylon began his world-wide cruise, beginning in Liverpool, England.
26-02-1907 The U.S. Congress raised their own pay to $7500.
26-02-1916 Mutual signed Charlie Chaplin to a film contract.
26-02-1919 In Arizona, the Grand Canyon was established as a National Park with an act of the U.S. Congress.
26-02-1929 U.S. President Coolidge signed a bill creating the Grand Teton National Park.
26-02-1930 New York City installed traffic lights.
26-02-1933 A ground-breaking ceremony was held at Crissy Field for the Golden Gate Bridge.
26-02-1945 In the U.S., a nationwide midnight curfew went into effect.
26-02-1952 British Prime Minister Winston Churchill announced that Britain had developed an atomic bomb.
26-02-1957 The Jean Hersholt Humanitarian Award was established by the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences.
26-02-1979 "Flatbush" debuted on CBS-TV.
26-02-1986 Corazon Aquino was inaugurated president of the Phillipines. Long time President Ferdinand Marcos went into exile.
26-02-1987 The Tower Commission rebuked U.S. President Reagan for failing to control his national security staff in the wake of the Iran-Contra affair.
26-02-1987 The U.S.S.R. conducted its first nuclear weapons test after a 19-month moratorium period.
26-02-1991 Iraqi President Saddam Hussein announced on Baghdad Radio that Iraqi troops were being withdrawn from Kuwait.
26-02-1993 Six people were killed and more than a thousand injured when a van exploded in the parking garage beneath the World Trade Center in New York City. The bomb had been built by Islamic extremists.
26-02-1995 Barings PLC collapsed after a securities dealer lost more than $1.4 billion by gambling on Tokyo stock prices. The company was Britain's oldest investment banking firm.
26-02-1998 A Texas jury rejected an $11 million lawsuit by Texas cattlemen who blamed Oprah Winfrey for price drop after on-air comment about mad-cow disease.
26-02-1998 In Oregon, a health panel rules that taxpayers must help to pay for doctor-assisted suicides.
26-02-2002 In Rome, Italy, a bomb exploded near the Interior Ministry. No injuries were reported.
27-02-1700 The Pacific Island of New Britain was discovered.
27-02-1801 The city of Washington, DC. was placed under congressioal jurisdiction.
27-02-1827 New Orleans held its first Mardi Gras celebration.
27-02-1861 In Warsaw, Russian troops fired on a crowd protesting Russian rule over Poland. Five protesting marchers were killed in the incident.
27-02-1867 Dr. William G. Bonwill invented the dental mallet.
27-02-1883 Oscar Hammerstein patented the first cigar-rolling machine.
27-02-1896 The "Charlotte Observer" published a picture of an X-ray photograph made by Dr. H.L. Smith. The photograph showed a perfect picture of all the bones of a hand and a bullet that Smith had placed between the third and fourth fingers in the palm.
27-02-1900 In South Africa, the British recieved an unconditional surrender from Boer Gen. Piet Cronje at Paardeberg.
27-02-1922 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld the 19th Amendment that guaranteed women the right to vote.
27-02-1933 The Reichstag, Germany's parliament building in Berlin, was set afire. The Nazis accused Communist for the fire.
27-02-1939 The U.S. Supreme Court outlawed sit-down strikes.
27-02-1949 Chaim Weizmann became the first Israeli president.
27-02-1951 The 22nd Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified, limiting U.S. Presidents to two terms.
27-02-1972 The Shanghai Communique was issued by U.S. President Nixon and Chinese Premier Chou En-lai.
27-02-1973 The American Indian Movement occupied Wouned Knee in South Dakota.
27-02-1974 "People" magazine was first issued by Time-Life (later known as Time-Warner).
27-02-1982 Wayne B. Williams was found guilty of murdering two young black people. 28 bodies had been found in the Atlanta area over a period of 22 months.
27-02-1986 The U.S. Senate approved the telecast of its debates on a trial basis.
27-02-1990 The Exxon Corporation and Exxon Shipping were indicted on five criminal counts in reference to the 1989 Exxon Valdez oil spill.
27-02-1991 U.S. President George Bush announced live on television that "Kuwait is liberated."
27-02-1997 In Ireland, divorce became legal.
27-02-1997 Don Cornelius received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
27-02-1998 Britain's House of Lords agreed to give a monarch's first-born daughter the same claim to the throne as any first-born son. This was the end to 1,000 years of male preference.
27-02-1999 Colin Prescot and Andy Elson set a new hot air balloon endurance record when they had been aloft for 233 hours and 55 minutes. The two were in the process of trying to circumnavigate the Earth.
27-02-1999 Nigeria returned to civilian rule when Gen. Olusegun Obasanjo became the country's first elected president since August of 1983.
27-02-2002 In Boston, twenty people working at Logan International Airport were charged with lying to get their jobs or security badges.
28-02-1827 The Baltimore & Ohio Railroad became the first railroad incorporated for commercial transportation of people and freight.
28-02-1844 Several people were killed aboard the USS Princeton when a 12-inch gun exploded.
28-02-1849 Regular steamboat service to California via Cape Horn arrived in San Francisco for the first time. The SS California had left New York Harbor on October 6, 1848. The trip took 4 months and 21 days.
28-02-1854 The Republican Party was organized in Ripon, WI. About 50 slavery opponents began the new political group.
28-02-1861 The U.S. territory of Colorado was organized.
28-02-1881 Thomas Edison hired Samuel Insull as his private secretary.
28-02-1883 The first vaudeville theater opened.
28-02-1885 AT&T (American Telephone and Telegraph) was incorporated. The company was capitalized on only $100,000 and provided long distance service for American Bell.
28-02-1893 Edward G. Acheson showed his patent for Carborundum.
28-02-1900 In South Africa, British troops relieved Ladysmith, which had been under siege since November 2, 1899.
28-02-1911 Thomas A. Edison, Inc. was organized.
28-02-1940 The first televised basketball game was shown. The game featured Fordham University and the University of Pittsburgh from Madison Square Gardens in New York.
28-02-1948 Bud Gartiser set a world record when he cleared the 50-yard low hurdles in 6.8 seconds.
28-02-1951 A Senate committee issued a report that stated that there were at least two major crime syndicates in the U.S.
28-02-1953 In a Cambridge University laboratory, scientists James D. Watson and Francis H.C. Crick discovered the double-helix structure of DNA.
28-02-1954 In San Francisco "Birth of a Planet" was aired. It was the first American phase-contrast cinemicrography film to be presented on television.
28-02-1956 A patent was issued to Forrester for a computer memory core.
28-02-1962 The John Glenn for President club was formed by a group of Las Vegas republicans.
28-02-1974 The U.S. and Egypt re-established diplomatic relations after a break of seven years.
28-02-1979 Mr. Ed, the talking horse from the TV show "Mr. Ed", died.
28-02-1983 "M*A*S*H" became the most watched television program in history when the final episode aired.
28-02-1986 Swedish Prime Minister Olof Palme was assassinated in Stockholm.
28-02-1993 U.S. Federal agents raided the compound of an armed religious cult in Waco, TX. The ATF had planned to arrest the leader of the Branch Davidians, David Koresh, on federal firearms charges. Four agents and six Davidians were killed and a 51-day standoff followed.
28-02-1994 NATO made its first military strike when U.S. F-16 fighters shot down four Bosnian Serb warplanes in violation of a no-fly zone over central Bosnia.
28-02-1995 The Denver International Airport opened after a 16-month delay.
28-02-1998 Serbian police began a campaign to wipe out "terrorist gangs" in the Yugoslav province of Kosovo.
28-02-2001 The Northwest region of the U.S., including the state of Washington, was hit by an earthquake that measured 6.9 on the Richter Scale. There were no deaths reported.
28-02-2002 In Ahmadabad, India, Hindus set fire to homes in a Muslim neighborhood. At least 55 people were killed in the attack.
28-02-2002 Sotheby's auction house announced that it had identified Peter Paul Reubens as the creator of the painting "The Massacre of the Innocents." The painting was previously thought to be by Jan van den Hoecke.
28-02-2002 It was announced that John Madden would be replacing Dennis Miller on "Monday Night Football." Madden signed a four-year $20 million deal with ABC Sports.
29-02-1288 Scotland established this day as one when a woman could propose marriage to a man. In the event that he refused the proposal he was required to pay a fine.
29-02-1860 The first electric tabulating machine was invented by Herman Hollerith.
29-02-1904 In Washington, DC, a seven-man commission was created to hasten the construction of the Panama Canal.
29-02-1940 Hattie McDaniel became the first black person to win an Oscar. She won Best Supporting Actress award for her role as Mammy in "Gone with the Wind."
29-02-1944 The invasion of the Admiralty Islands began with "Operation Brewer." U.S. General Douglas MacArthur led his forces onto Los Negros.
29-02-1944 Dorothy McElroy Vredenburgh of Alabama became the first woman to be appointed secretary of a national political party. She was appointed to the Democratic National Committee.
29-02-1944 The Office of Defense Transportation, for the second year in a row, restricted attendance at the Kentucky Derby to residents of the Louisville area. This was an effort to prevent a railroad traffic burden during wartime.
29-02-1952 In New York City, four signs were installed at 44th Street and Broadway in Times Square that told pedestrians when to walk.
29-02-1964 Dawn Fraser got her 36th world record. The Australian swimmer was timed at 58.9 seconds in the 100-meter freestyle in Sydney, Australia.
29-02-1972 Jack Anderson revealed a memo written by ITT's Washington lobbyist, Dita Beard, that connected ITT's funding of part of the Republican National Convention.
29-02-1988 "Day by Day" premiered on NBC-TV.
02-01-1492 The leader of the last Arab stronghold in Spain surrendered to Spanish forces loyal to King Ferdinand II and Queen Isabella I.
02-01-1788 Georgia became the 4th state to ratify the U.S. Constitution.
02-01-1842 In Fairmount, PA, the first wire suspension bridge was opened to traffic.
02-01-1859 Erastus Beadle published "The Dime Book of Practical Etiquette."
02-01-1872 Brigham Young, the 71-year-old leader of the Mormon Church, was arrested on a charge of bigamy. He had 25 wives.
02-01-1879 Thomas Edison began construction on his first generator.
02-01-1890 Alice Sanger became the first female White House staffer.
02-01-1893 The first commemorative postage stamps were issued.
02-01-1900 U.S. Secretary of State John Hay announced the Open Door Policy to prompt trade with China.
02-01-1900 The Chicago Canal opened.
02-01-1910 The first junior high school in the United States opened. McKinley School in Berkeley, CA, housed seventh and eighth grade students. In a separate building students were housed who attended grades 9-12.
02-01-1917 Royal Bank of Canada took over the Quebec Bank.
02-01-1921 The first religious broadcast on radio was heard on KDKA Radio in Pittsburgh, PA, as Dr. E.J. Van Etten of Calvary Episcopal Church preached.
02-01-1921 DeYoung Museum in Golden Gate Park opened.
02-01-1929 The United States and Canada reached an agreement on joint action to preserve Niagara Falls.
02-01-1935 Bruno Richard Hauptmann went on trial for the kidnap-murder of Charles Lindberghs baby. Hauptmann was found guilt and executed.
02-01-1942 The Philippine capital of Manila was captured by Japanese forces during World War II.
02-01-1953 "The Life of Riley" debuted on NBC-TV.
02-01-1955 Panamanian President Jose Antonio Remon was assassinated.
02-01-1957 The San Francisco and Los Angeles stock exchanges merged.
02-01-1959 CBS Radio ended four soap operas. "Our Gal Sunday", "This is Nora Drake", "Backstage Wife" and "Road of Life" all aired for the last time.
02-01-1960 U.S. Sen. John F. Kennedy of Massachusetts announced his candidacy for the Democratic presidential nomination.
02-01-1965 "Broadway" Joe Namath signed the richest rookie contract ($400,000) in the history of pro football.
02-01-1968 Dr. Christian Barnard performed the first successful heart transplant.
02-01-1968 Fidel Castro announced petroleum and sugar rationing in Cuba.
02-01-1971 In the U.S., a federally imposed ban on television cigarette advertisements went into effect.
02-01-1974 U.S. President Richard M. Nixon signed a bill requiring all states to lower the maximum speed limit to 55 MPH. The law was intended to conserve gasoline supplies during an embargo imposed by Arab oil-producing countries. Federal speed limits were abolished in 1995.
02-01-1983 The final edition of Garry Trudeau’s comic strip, "Doonesbury", appeared in 726 newspapers. "Doonesbury" began running again in September 1984.
02-01-1983 The musical "Annie" closed on Broadway at the Uris Theatre after 2,377 performances.
02-01-1985 The Rebels of UNLV beat Utah State in three overtime periods. The final score of 142-140 set a new NCAA record for total points in a basketball game (282). The game took over three hours to play.
02-01-1991 Sharon Pratt Dixon was sworn in as mayor of Washington, DC. She was the first black woman to head a city of that size and prominence.
02-01-1996 AT&T announced that it would eliminate 40,000 jobs over three years.
02-01-1998 Russia began circulating new rubles in effort to keep inflation in check and promote confidence.
03-01-1496 References in Leonardo da Vinci notebooks suggested that he tested his flying machine. The test didn't succeed and he didn't try to fly again for several years.
03-01-1521 Pope Leo X excommunicated Martin Luther.
03-01-1777 The Battle of Princeton took place in the War of Independence, in which George Washington defeated the British forces, led by Cornwallis.
03-01-1815 By secret treaty, Austria, Britain, and France formed a defensive alliance against Prusso-Russian plans to solve the Saxon and Polish problems.
03-01-1823 Stephen F. Austin received a grant from the Mexican government and began colonization in the region of the Brazos River in Texas.
03-01-1825 The first engineering college in the U.S. , Rensselaer School, opened in Troy, NY. It is now known as Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute.
03-01-1833 Britain seized control of the Falkland Islands in the South Atlantic. About 150 years later, Argentina seized the islands from the British, but Britain took them back after a 74-day war.
03-01-1868 The Shogunate was abolished in Japan and Meiji dynasty was restored.
03-01-1871 Henry W. Bradley patented oleomargarine.
03-01-1888 The drinking straw was patented by Marvin C. Stone.
03-01-1924 English explorer Howard Carter discovered the sarcophagus of Tutankhamen in the Valley of the Kings, near Luxor, Egypt.
03-01-1925 In Italy, Mussolini announced that he would take dictatorial powers.
03-01-1938 The first broadcast of "Woman in White" was presented on the NBC Red network. The program remained on radio for 10 years.
03-01-1938 The March of Dimes was established by U.S. President Franklin Delano Roosevelt. The organization fights poliomyelitis. The original name of the organization was the National Foundation for Infantile Paralysis.
03-01-1947 U.S. Congressional proceedings were televised for the first time. Viewers in Washington, Philadelphia and New York City saw some of the opening ceremonies of the 80th Congress.
03-01-1947 In Trenton, NJ, Al Herrin, passed away at age 92. He had claimed that he had not slept at all during his life.
03-01-1951 NBC-TV debuted "Dragnet."
03-01-1953 Frances Bolton and her son, Oliver from Ohio, became the first mother-son combination to serve at the same time in the U.S. Congress.
03-01-1957 The Hamilton Watch Company introduced the first electric watch.
03-01-1959 In the U.S., Alaska became the 49th state.
03-01-1961 The U.S. severed diplomatic relations with Cuba.
03-01-1962 Pope John XXIII excommunicated Cuban prime minister Fidel Castro.
03-01-1967 Jack Ruby died in a Dallas, TX, hospital.
03-01-1973 The Columbia Broadcasting System (CBS) sold the New York Yankees to a 12-man syndicate headed by George Steinbrenner for $10 million.
03-01-1980 Conservationist Joy Adamson, author of "Born Free," was killed in northern Kenya by a servant.
03-01-1983 Tony Dorsett (Dallas Cowboys) made the longest run from scrimmage in NFL history. Dorsett ran 99 yards in a game against the Minnesota Vikings.
03-01-1984 A woman died at Disneyland after falling from a ride. She had apparently unfastened her seatbelt while on the Matterhorn bobsled.
03-01-1988 Margaret Thatcher became the longest-serving British Prime Minister in the 20th century.
03-01-1990 Ousted Panamanian leader Manuel Noriega surrendered to U.S. forces, 10 days after taking refuge in the Vatican's diplomatic mission.
03-01-1991 The British government announced that seven Iraqi diplomats, another embassy staff member and 67 other Iraqis were being expelled from Britain.
03-01-1993 U.S. President George Bush and Russian President Boris Yeltsin signed the second Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty (START) in Moscow.
03-01-1995 WHO reported that the cumulative total of officially reported cases of AIDS had risen to 1,025,073 in 192 countries as at the end of 1994.
03-01-1995 The U.S. Postal Service raised the price of the first-class stamp to 32 cents.
03-01-1997 Bryant Gumbel signed off for the last time as host of NBC's "Today" show.
03-01-1998 China announced that it would spend $27.7 billion to fight erosion and pollution in the Yangtze and Yellow river valleys.
03-01-1999 Israeli authorities detained, and later expelled, 14 members of Concerned Christians. Israili officials claimed that the Denver, CO-based cult was plotting violence in Jerusalem to bring about the Second Coming of Christ.
03-01-2000 Charles M. Schulz's final original daily comic strip appeared in newspapers.
03-01-2001 The ATF (Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms) charged the "Texas 7" with weapons violations. An autopsy showed that Office Aubrey Hawkins, killed by the convicts, had been shot 11 times and run over with a vehicle.
03-01-2004 NASA's Spirit rover landed on Mars. The craft was able to send back black and white images three hours after landing.
04-01-1821 The first native-born American saint, Elizabeth Ann Seton, died in Emmitsburg, MD.
04-01-1850 The first American ice-skating club was organized in Philadelphia, PA.
04-01-1884 The socialist Fabian Society was founded in London.
04-01-1885 Dr. William Grant performed the first successful appendectomy. The patient was Mary Gartside.
04-01-1896 Utah became the 45th U.S. state.
04-01-1928 NBC Radio debuted "The Dodge Victory Hour" which starred Will Rogers, Paul Whiteman and his Orchestra and singer Al Jolson.
04-01-1935 Bob Hope was heard for the first time on network radio as part of "The Intimate Revue."
04-01-1936 The first pop music chart based on national sales was published by "Billboard" magazine.
04-01-1944 The attack on Monte Cassino was launched by the British Fifth Army in Italy.
04-01-1948 Britain granted independence to Burma.
04-01-1951 During the Korean conflict, North Korean and Communist Chinese forces captured the city of Seoul.
04-01-1953 Tufted plastic carpeting was introduced by Barwick Mills.
04-01-1957 "Collier’s" magazine was published for the last time. The periodical was published for 69 years.
04-01-1958 The Soviet satellite Sputknik I fell to the earth from its orbit. The craft had been launched on October 4, 1957.
04-01-1960 French author Albert Camus died in an automobile accident at age 46.
04-01-1962 New York City introduced a train that operated without conductors and motormen.
04-01-1965 The Fender Guitar Company was sold to CBS for $13 million.
04-01-1965 Poet T.S. Eliot died at age 76.
04-01-1965 In his State of the Union address, U.S. President Johnson proclaimed the building of the "Great Society."
04-01-1972 Rose Heilbron became the first woman judge in Britain at the Old Bailey, London.
04-01-1974 U.S. President Nixon refused to hand over tape recordings and documents subpoenaed by the Senate Watergate Committee.
04-01-1974 NBC-TV presented hockey in prime time. The Boston Bruins and the New York Rangers were the teams in the National Hockey League (NHL) game.
04-01-1981 The Broadway show "Frankenstein" lost an estimated $2 million, when it opened and closed on the same night.
04-01-1982 Bryant Gumbel moved from NBC Sports to the anchor desk where he joined Jane Pauley as co-host of the "Today" show on NBC.
04-01-1984 Wayne ‘The Great One’ Gretzky scored eight points (four goals and four assists) for the second time in his National Hockey League (NHL) career. Edmonton’s Oilers defeated the Minnesota North Stars, 12-8. The game was the highest-scoring NHL game to date.
04-01-1987 An Amtrak train bound from Washington to Boston collided with Conrail engines approaching from a side track, 16 people were killed.
04-01-1990 Charles Stuart jumped to his death from a Boston Harbor bridge. He had become a suspect in the murder of his wife. He had claimed that a gunman had shot him and his wife.
04-01-1990 Deposed Panamanian leader Manuel Noriega was arraigned in U.S. federal district court in Miami on drug-trafficking charges.
04-01-1991 The U.N. Security Council voted unanimously to condemn Israel's treatment of the Palestinians in the occupied territories.
04-01-1997 The Greek Cypriot government signed an agreement to buy S-300 surface-to-air missiles from Russia.
04-01-1999 A drifting Nicaraguan fishing boat was found by the Norwegian oil tanker Joelm. The fisherman had been lost at sea for 35 days after the engine of their vessel quit working.
04-01-1999 16 people were killed and 25 injured when gunmen opened fire on Shiite Muslim worshippers at a mosque in Islamabad, Pakistan.
04-01-1999 Former professional wrestler Jesse Ventura was sworn in as Minnesota's 37th governor.
04-01-2001 FBI agents in the Dallas area charged the "Texas 7" of unlawful flight to avoid federal prosecution for capital murder, broadening the manhunt nationwide.
04-01-2006 Nancy Pelosi became Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives. She was the first woman to hold the position.
05-01-1781 Richmond, VA, was burned by a British naval expedition led by Benedict Arnold.
05-01-1885 The Long Island Railroad Company became the first to offer piggy-back rail service which was the transportation of farm wagons on trains.
05-01-1896 It was reported by The Austrian newspaper that Wilhelm Roentgen had discovered the type of radiation that became known as X-rays.
05-01-1900 In Ireland, Nationalist leader John Edward Redmond called for a revolt against British rule.
05-01-1903 The general public could use the Pacific cable for the very first time.
05-01-1914 Ford Motor Company announced that there would be a new daily minimum wage of $5 and an eight-hour workday.
05-01-1925 Mrs. Nellie Taylor Ross was sworn in as the governor of Wyoming She was the first female governor in the U.S.
05-01-1933 Construction of the Golden Gate Bridge began.
05-01-1934 Both the National and American baseball leagues decided to use a uniform-size baseball. It was the first time in 33 years that both leagues used the same size ball. (MLB)
05-01-1935 Phil Spitalny’s All-Girl Orchestra was featured on CBS radio on the program, "The Hour of Charm."
05-01-1940 The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) got its very first demonstration of FM radio.
05-01-1944 The London "Daily Mail" was the first transoceanic newspaper to be published.
05-01-1948 Warner Brothers-Pathe showed the very first color newsreel. The footage was of the Tournament of Roses Parade and the Rose Bowl football classic.
05-01-1956 In the Peanuts comic strip, Snoop walked on two legs for the first time.
05-01-1961 "Mr. Ed" debuted. The show would run for six years.
05-01-1970 "All My Children" premiered on ABC.
05-01-1972 U.S. President Richard M. Nixon ordered the development of the space shuttle.
05-01-1987 U.S. President Ronald Reagan underwend prostate surgery.
05-01-1993 The state of Washington executed Westley Allan Dodd. It was America's first legal hanging since 1965. Dodd was an admitted child sex killer.
05-01-1996 Yahya Ayyash, a member of the Hamas in Israel, is killed by a booby-trapped cellular phone.
05-01-1998 U.S. Representative Sonny Bono died in skiing accident.
05-01-2002 A 15 year-old student pilot, Charles Bishop, crashed a small plane into a building in Tampa, FL. Bishop was about to begin a flying lesson when he took off without permission and without an instructor.
06-01-0871 England's King Alfred defeated the Danes at the Battle of Ashdown.
06-01-1205 Philip of Swabia was crowned as King of the Romans.
06-01-1453 Frederick III erected Austria into an Archduchy.
06-01-1540 King Henry VIII of England was married to Anne of Cleves, his fourth wife.
06-01-1720 The Committee of Inquiry on the South Sea Bubble published its findings.
06-01-1759 George Washington and Martha Dandridge Custis were married.
06-01-1838 Samuel Morse publicly demonstrated the telegraph for the first time.
06-01-1896 The first American women’s six-day bicycle race was held at Madison Square Garden in New York City.
06-01-1900 In India, it was reported that millions of people were dying from starvation.
06-01-1900 Off of South Africa, the British seized the German steamer Herzog. The boat was released on January 22, 1900.
06-01-1912 New Mexico became the 47th U.S. state.
06-01-1930 The first diesel-engine automobile trip was completed after a run of 792 miles from Indianapolis, IN, to New York City, NY.
06-01-1931 Thomas Edison executed his last patent application.
06-01-1941 Richard Widmark made his debut on radio in "The Home of the Brave."
06-01-1941 Alice Marble made her professional tennis debut when she defeated Ruth Hardwick of Great Britain at Madison Square Garden in New York City.
06-01-1942 The first commercial around-the-world airline flight took place. Pan American Airlines was the company that made history with the feat.
06-01-1942 The National Collegiate Football Rules Committee abolished the Y formation.
06-01-1945 The Battle of the Bulge ended with 130,000 German and 77,000 Allied casualties.
06-01-1950 Britain recognized the Communist government of China.
06-01-1952 "Peanuts" debuted in Sunday papers across the United States.
06-01-1963 "Wild Kingdom" premiered on NBC.
06-01-1967 U.S. and South Vietnamese forces launched a major offensive, known as Operation "Deckhouse V", in the Mekong River delta.
06-01-1974 CBS radio debuted "Radio Mystery Theatre."
06-01-1975 The Broadway show "The Wiz" opened.
06-01-1975 ABC-TV debuted "A.M. America."
06-01-1982 William G. Bonin was convicted in Los Angeles, CA, of being the "freeway killer" who had murdered 14 young men and boys.
06-01-1987 After a 29-year lapse, the Ford Thunderbird was presented with the Motor Trend Car of the Year Award. It was the first occurrence of a repeat winner of the award.
06-01-1994 Figure skater Nancy Kerrigan was clubbed on the right leg by an assailant at Cobo Arena in Detroit, MI. Four men were later sentenced to prison for the attack, including Tonya Harding's ex-husband.
06-01-1998 The spacecraft Lunar Prospect was launched into orbit around the moon. The craft was crashed into the moon, in an effort to find water under the lunar surface, on July 31, 1999.
06-01-1999 The 106th U.S. Congress opened. The first item on the agenda was the impeachment proceedings of U.S. President Bill Clinton. The trial was set to begin January 7, 1999.
06-01-1999 Bob Newhart received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
07-01-1558 Calais, the last English possession on mainland France, was recaptured by the French.
07-01-1610 Galileo Galilei sighted four of Jupiter's moons. He named them Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto.
07-01-1782 The Bank of North America opened in Philadelphia. It was the first commercial bank in the United States.
07-01-1785 French aeronaut/balloonist Jean-Pierre Blanchard successfully made the first air-crossing of the English Channel from the English coast to France.
07-01-1789 Americans voted for the electors that would choose George Washington to be the first U.S. president.
07-01-1887 Thomas Stevens completed the first worldwide bicycle trip. He started his trip in April 1884. Stevens and his bike traveled 13,500 miles in almost three years time.
07-01-1894 W.K. Dickson received a patent for motion picture film.
07-01-1896 "Fannie Farmer Cookbook" cookbook was published.
07-01-1904 The distress signal "CQD" was established. Two years later "SOS" became the radio distress signal because it was quicker to send by wireless radio.
07-01-1926 George Burns and Gracie Allen were married.
07-01-1927 Transatlantic telephone service Service began between New York and London. 31 calls were made on this first day.
07-01-1927 In Hinckley IL, the Harlem Globetrotters played their first game.
07-01-1929 The debut of "Buck Rogers 2429 A.D." occurred in newspapers around the U.S. The title of the comic strip was later changed to "Buck Rogers in the 25th Century."
07-01-1932 Chancellor Heinrich Brüning declared that Germany cannot, and will not, resume reparations payments.
07-01-1935 French Foreign Minister Pierre Laval and Italian Prime Minister Benito Mussolini signed the Italo-French agreements.
07-01-1940 "Gene Autry’s Melody Ranch" debuted on CBS Radio. The show aired for 16 years.
07-01-1941 The NBC Blue radio network presented "The Squeaky Door" for the first time. The show was later known as "Inner Sanctum."
07-01-1942 The World War II siege of Bataan began.
07-01-1949 The announcement of the first photograph of genes was shown at the University of Southern California in Los Angeles.
07-01-1953 U.S. President Harry Truman announced the development of the hydrogen bomb.
07-01-1954 The Duoscopic TV receiver was unveiled this day. The TV set allowed the watching of two different shows at the same time.
07-01-1959 The United States recognized Fidel Castro's new government in Cuba.
07-01-1968 The cost of a U.S. first class stamp was raised to 6 cents.
07-01-1975 OPEC agreed to raise crude oil prices by 10%, which began a time of world economic inflation.
07-01-1979 Vietnamese forces captured the Cambodian capital of Phnom Penh, overthrowing the Khmer Rouge government.
07-01-1980 U.S. President Jimmy Carter signed legislation that authorized $1.5 billion in loans for the bail out of Chrysler Corp.
07-01-1989 Crown Prince Akihito became the emperor of Japan following the death of his father, Emperor Hirohito.
07-01-1990 The Leaning Tower of Pisa was closed to the public. The accelerated rate of "leaning" raised fears for the safety of its visitors.
07-01-1996 Alvaro Arzu was elected president of Guatemala.
07-01-1996 One of the biggest blizzards in U.S. history hit the eastern states. More than 100 deaths were later blamed on the severe weather.
07-01-1998 Former White House intern Monica Lewinsky signed an affidavit denying that she had an affair with U.S. President Clinton.
07-01-1999 U.S. President Clinton went on trial before the Senate. It was only the second time in U.S. history that an impeached president had gone to trial. Clinton was later acquitted of perjury and obstruction of justice charges.
07-01-2002 Microsoft Corp. chairman Bill Gates introduced a new device code named Mira. The device was tablet-like and was a cross between a handheld computer and a TV remote control.
08-01-1642 Astronomer Galileo Galilei died in Arcetri, Italy.
08-01-1675 The first corporation was charted in the United States. The company was the New York Fishing Company.
08-01-1790 In the United States, George Washington delivered the first State of the Union address.
08-01-1815 The Battle of New Orleans began. The War of 1812 had officially ended on December 24, 1814, with the signing of the Treaty of Ghent. The news of the signing had not reached British troops in time to prevent their attack on New Orleans.
08-01-1838 Alfred Vail demonstrated a telegraph code he had devised using dots and dashes as letters. The code was the predecessor to Samuel Morse's code.
08-01-1853 A bronze statue of Andrew Jackson on a horse was unveiled in Lafayette Park in Washington, DC. The statue was the work of Clark Mills.
08-01-1856 Borax (hydrated sodium borate) was discovered by Dr. John Veatch.
08-01-1877 Crazy Horse (Tashunca-uitco) and his warriors fought their final battle against the U.S. Cavalry in Montana.
08-01-1886 The Severn Railway Tunnel, Britain's longest, was opened.
08-01-1889 The tabulating machine was patented by Dr. Herman Hollerith. His firm, Tabulating Machine Company, later became International Business Machines Corporation (IBM).
08-01-1894 Fire caused serious damage at the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, IL.
08-01-1900 U.S. President McKinley placed Alaska under military rule.
08-01-1900 In South Africa, General White turned back the Boers attack of Ladysmith.
08-01-1901 The first tournament sanctioned by the American Bowling Congress was held in Chicago, IL.
08-01-1908 A catastrophic train collision occurred in the smoke-filled Park Avenue Tunnel in New York City. Seventeen were killed and thirty-eight were injured. The accident caused a public outcry and increased demand for electric trains.
08-01-1916 During World War I, the final withdrawal of Allied troops from Gallipoli took place.
08-01-1918 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson announced his Fourteen Points as the basis for peace upon the end of World War I.
08-01-1921 David Lloyd George became the first prime minister tenant at Chequers Court, Buckinghamshire.
08-01-1929 William S. Paley appeared on CBS Radio for the first time to announce that CBS had become the largest regular chain of broadcasting chains in radio history.
08-01-1935 The spectrophotometer was patented by A.C. Hardy.
08-01-1952 Marie Wilson came to TV as "My Friend Irma".
08-01-1955 After 130 home basketball wins, Georgia Tech defeated Kentucky 59-58. It was the first Kentucky loss at home since January 2, 1943.
08-01-1957 Jackie Robinson announced his retirement from major league baseball in an article that appeared in "LOOK" magazine.
08-01-1958 Bobby Fisher, at the age of 14, won the United States Chess Championship for the first time.
08-01-1959 Charles De Gaulle was inaugurated as president of France's Fifth Republic.
08-01-1960 The NCAA met in New York and voted against reviving the unlimited substitution rule for college football.
08-01-1964 U.S. President Lyndon Johnson declared a "War on Poverty."
08-01-1961 Robert Goulet made his national TV debut this night on "The Ed Sullivan Show" on CBS.
08-01-1962 Leonardo da Vinci's Mona Lisa was exhibited in America for the first time at the National Gallery of Art in Washington, DC. The next day the exhibit opened to the public.
08-01-1973 Secret peace talks between the United States and North Vietnam resumed near Paris, France.
08-01-1973 The trial opened in Washington, of seven men accused of bugging Democratic Party headquarters in the Watergate apartment complex in Washington, DC.
08-01-1975 Ella Grasso became the governor of Connecticut. She was the first woman to become a governor of a state without a husband preceding her in the governor’s chair.
08-01-1982 American Telephone & Telegraph (AT&T) settled the Justice Department's antitrust lawsuit against it by agreeing to divest itself of the 22 Bell System companies.
08-01-1987 The Dow Jones industrial average closed over the 2000 mark for the first time at 2,002.25.
08-01-1992 U.S. President George Bush collapsed during a state dinner in Tokyo. White House officials said Bush was suffering from stomach flu.
08-01-1993 Bosnian President Izetbegovic visited the U.S. to plead his government's case for Western military aid and intervention to halt Serbian aggression.
08-01-1994 Tonya Harding won the ladies' U.S. Figure Skating Championship in Detroit, MI, a day after Nancy Kerrigan dropped out because of a clubbing attack that injured her right knee. The U.S. Figure Skating Association later took the title from Harding because of her involvement in the attack.
08-01-1997 Mister Rogers received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
08-01-1998 Ramzi Yousef was sentenced to life in prison for his role of mastermind behind the World Trade Center bombing in New York.
08-01-1998 Scientists announced that they had discovered that galaxies were accelerating and moving apart and at faster speeds.
08-01-1999 The top two executives of Salt Lake City's Olympic Organizing Committee resigned amid disclosures that civic boosters had given cash to members of the International Olympic Committee.
08-01-1999 British Prime Minister Tony Blair concluded a three-day visit to South Africa.
08-01-2005 The rate for U.S. First Class mail was raised to 39˘.
09-01-1793 Jean-Pierre Blanchard made the first successful balloon flight in the U.S.
09-01-1799 British prime minister William Pitt the Younger introduced income tax, at two shillings (10p) in the pound, to raise funds for the Napoleonic Wars.
09-01-1848 The first commercial bank was established in San Francisco, CA.
09-01-1861 The state of Mississippi seceded from the United States.
09-01-1894 The New England Telephone and Telegraph Company put the first battery-operated switchboard into operation in Lexington, MA.
09-01-1902 New York State introduced a bill to outlaw flirting in public.
09-01-1905 In Russia, the civil disturbances known as the Revolution of 1905 forced Czar Nicholas II to grant some civil rights.
09-01-1929 The Seeing Eye was incorporated in Nashville, TN. The company's purpose was to train dogs to guide the blind.
09-01-1936 The United States Army adopted the semi-automatic rifle.
09-01-1937 The first issue of "Look" went on sale. Within a month, "Look" became a biweekly magazine.
09-01-1940 Television was used for the first time to present a sales meeting to convention delegates in New York City.
09-01-1951 The United Nations headquarters officially opened in New York City.
09-01-1961 The play, "Rhinoceros," opened on Broadway.
09-01-1969 The supersonic aeroplane Concorde made its first trial flight, at Bristol.
09-01-1972 The ocean liner Queen Elizabeth was destroyed by fire in Hong Kong harbor.
09-01-1972 British miners went on strike for the first time since 1926.
09-01-1981 Hockey Hall of Famer, Phil Esposito, announced that he would retire as a hockey player after the New York Rangers-Buffalo Sabres hockey game. The game ended in a tie. (NHL)
09-01-1984 Clara Peller was first seen by TV viewers in the "Where's the Beef?" commercial campaign for Wendy's.
09-01-1986 Kodak got out of the instant camera business after 10 years due to a loss in a court battle that claimed that Kodak copied Polaroid patents.
09-01-1991 U.S. secretary of state Baker and Iraqi foreign minister Aziz met for 61/2 hours in Geneva, but failed to reach any agreement that would forestall war in the Persian Gulf.
09-01-1995 Russian cosmonaut Valeri Poliakov, 51, completed his 366th day in outer space aboard the Mir space station, breaking the record for the longest continuous time spent in outer space.
09-01-1997 Tamil rebels attact a military base in Sri Lanka. 200 soldiers and 140 rebels were killed.
09-01-2000 ABC-TV began airing "The Mole."
09-01-2002 Yasmine Bleeth was sentenced to two years of probation, regular drug tests, 100 hours of community service and pay the court costs in connection to a cocaine-possession charge.
09-01-2002 The U.S. Justice Department announced that it was pursuing a criminal investigation of Enron Corp. The company had filed for bankruptcy on December 2, 2001.
09-01-2003 Archaeologists announced that they had found five more chambers in the tomb of Qin Shihuang, China's first emperor. The rooms were believed to cover about 750,000 square feet.
09-01-2005 Matthew Broderick and Nathan Lane received stars on the Hollywood Walk of Fame in a dual ceremony.
10-01-1776 Thomas Paine published his pamphlet "Common Sense."
10-01-1840 The penny post, whereby mail was delivered at a standard charge rather than paid for by the recipient, began in Britain.
10-01-1861 Florida seceded from the United States.
10-01-1863 Prime Minister Gladstone opened the first section of the London Underground Railway system, from Paddington to Farringdon Street.
10-01-1870 John D. Rockefeller incorporated Standard Oil.
10-01-1901 Oil was discovered at the Spindletop oil field near Beaumont, TX.
10-01-1911 Major Jimmie Erickson took the first photograph from an airplane while flying over San Diego, CA.
10-01-1920 The League of Nations ratified the Treaty of Versailles, officially ending World War I with Germany.
10-01-1920 The League of Nations held its first meeting in Geneva.
10-01-1926 Fritz Lang's film "Metropolis" was first shown, in Berlin.
10-01-1928 The Soviet Union ordered the exile of Leon Trotsky.
10-01-1943 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt sailed from Miami, FL, to Trinidad thus becoming the first American President to visit a foreign country during wartime.
10-01-1943 The quiz show, "The Better Half," was heard for the first time on Mutual Radio.
10-01-1946 The first meeting of the United Nations General Assembly took place with 51 nations represented.
10-01-1949 Vinyl records were introduced by RCA (45 rpm) and Columbia (33.3 rpm).
10-01-1950 Ben Hogan appeared for the first time in a golf tournament since an auto accident a year earlier. He tied ‘Slammin’ Sammy Snead in the Los Angeles Open, however, Hogan lost in a playoff.
10-01-1951 Donald Howard Rogers piloted the first passenger jet on a trip from Chicago to New York City.
10-01-1957 Harold Macmillan became prime minister of Britain, following the resignation Anthony Eden.
10-01-1963 The Chicago Cubs became the first baseball club to hire an athletic director. He was Robert Whitlow. (MLB)
10-01-1969 The final issue of "The Saturday Evening Post" appeared after 147 years of publication.
10-01-1971 "Masterpiece Theatre" premiered on PBS with host Alistair Cooke. The introduction drama series was "The First Churchills."
10-01-1978 The Soviet Union launched two cosmonauts aboard a Soyuz capsule for a redezvous with the Salyut VI space laboratory.
10-01-1984 The United States and the Vatican established full diplomatic relations for the first time in more than a century.
10-01-1986 The uncut version of Jerome Kern’s musical, "Showboat", opened at the Kennedy Center in Washington, DC.
10-01-1990 Chinese Premier Li Peng ended martial law in Beijing after seven months. He said that crushing pro-democracy protests had saved China from "the abyss of misery."
10-01-1990 Time Inc. and Warner Communications Inc. completed a $14 billion merger. The new company, Time Warner, was the world's largest entertainment company.
10-01-1994 In Manassas, VA, Lorena Bobbitt went on trial. She had been charged with maliciously wounding her husband John. She was acquitted by reason of temporary insanity.
10-01-1997 Shelby Lynne Barrackman was strangled to death by her grand-father when she licked the icing off of cupcakes. He was convicted of the crime on September 15, 1998.
10-01-2000 It was announced that Time-Warner had agreed to buy America On-line (AOL). It was the largest-ever corporate merger priced at $162 billion. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) approved the deal on December 14, 2000.
10-01-2001 American Airlines agreed to acquire most of Trans World Airlines (TWA) assets for about $500 million. The deal brought an end to the financially troubled TWA.
10-01-2002 In France, the "Official Journal" reported that all women could get the morning-after conraception pill for free in pharmacies.
10-01-2003 North Korea announced that it was withdrawing from the global nuclear arms control treaty and that it had no plans to develop nuclear weapons.
11-01-1569 England's first state lottery was held.
11-01-1770 The first shipment of rhubarb was sent to the United States from London.
11-01-1805 The Michigan Territory was created.
11-01-1815 U.S. General Andrew Jackson achieved victory at the Battle of New Orleans. The War of 1812 had officially ended on December 24, 1814, with the signing of the Treaty of Ghent. The news of the signing had not reached British troops in time to prevent their attack on New Orleans.
11-01-1861 Alabama seceded from the United States.
11-01-1867 Benito Juarez returned to the Mexican presidency, following the withdrawal of French troops and the execution of Emperor Maximilian.
11-01-1878 In New York, milk was delivered in glass bottles for the first time by Alexander Campbell.
11-01-1902 "Popular Mechanics" magazine was published for the first time.
11-01-1913 The first sedan-type car was unveiled at the National Automobile Show in New York City. The car was manufactured by the Hudson Motor Company.
11-01-1922 At Toronto General Hospital, Leonard Thompson became the first person to be successfully treated with insulin.
11-01-1935 Amelia Earhart Putnam became the first woman to fly solo from Hawaii to California.
11-01-1938 In Limerick, ME, Frances Moulton assumed her duties as the first woman bank president.
11-01-1942 Japan declared war against the Netherlands. The same day, Japanese forces invaded the Dutch East Indies.
11-01-1943 The United States and Britain signed treaties relinquishing extraterritorial rights in China.
11-01-1947 "Murder and Mrs. Malone" debuted on ABC radio.
11-01-1958 "Seahunt" debuted on CBS-TV. The show was aired on the network for four years.
11-01-1964 U.S. Surgeon General Luther Terry released a report that said that smoking cigarettes was a definite health hazard.
11-01-1973 The Open University awarded its first degrees.
11-01-1973 Owners of American League baseball teams voted to adopt the designated-hitter rule on a trial basis.
11-01-1977 France released Abu Daoud, a Palestinian suspected of involvement in the massacre of Israeli athletes at the 1972 Munich Olympics.
11-01-1978 Two Soviet cosmonauts aboard the Soyuz 27 capsule linked up with the Salyut 6 orbiting space station, where the Soyuz 26 capsule was already docked.
11-01-1980 Nigel Short, age 14, from Bolton in Britain, became the youngest International Master in the history of chess.
11-01-1986 Author James Clavell signed a 5$ million deal with Morrow/Avon Publishing for the book "Whirlwind". The book is a 2,000 page novel.
11-01-1988 U.S. Vice President George Bush met with representatives of independent counsel Lawrence E. Walsh to answer questions about the Iran-Contra affair.
11-01-1991 An auction of silver and paintings that had been acquired by the late Ferdinand Marcos and his wife, Imelda, brought in a total of $20.29 million at Christie's in New York.
11-01-1996 Ryutaro Hashimoto become Japan's prime minister. He replaced Tomiichi Murayama who had resigned on January 5, 1996.
11-01-2000 The merger between AOL and Time Warner was approved by the U.S. government with restrictions.
11-01-2000 The U.S. Postal Service unveiled the second Vietnam Veterans Memorial commemorative stamp in a ceremony at The Wall.
11-01-2001 The Texas Board of Criminal Justice released a review of the escape of the "Texas 7." It stated that prison staff missed critical opportunities to prevent the escape by ignoring a fire alarm, not reporting unsupervised inmates and not demanding proper identification from inmates.
11-01-2002 Thomas Junta, 44, was convicted of involuntary manslaughter for beating another man to death at their son's hockey practice. The incident occurred on July 5, 2000.
12-01-1519 Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I died.
12-01-1773 The first public museum in America was established in Charleston, SC.
12-01-1866 The Royal Aeronautical Society was founded in London.
12-01-1875 Kwang-su was made emperor of China.
12-01-1882 Thomas Edison's central station on Holborn Viaduct in London began operation.
12-01-1895 The first performance of King Arthur took place at the Lyceum Theatre.
12-01-1896 At Davidson College, several students took x-ray photographs. They created the first X-ray photographs to be made in America.
12-01-1908 A wireless message was sent long-distance for the first time from the Eiffel Tower in Paris.
12-01-1915 The U.S. House of Representatives rejected a proposal to give women the right to vote.
12-01-1915 The U.S. Congress established the Rocky Mountain National Park.
12-01-1926 "Sam ‘n’ Henry" debuted on WGN Radio in Chicago, IL.
12-01-1932 Hattie W. Caraway became the first woman elected to the U.S. Senate.
12-01-1938 Austria recognized the Franco government in Spain.
12-01-1940 Soviet bombers raided cities in Finland.
12-01-1942 U.S. President Roosevelt created the National War Labor Board.
12-01-1943 The Office of Price Administration announced that standard frankfurters/hot dogs/wieners would be replaced by 'Victory Sausages.'
12-01-1945 During World War II, Soviet forces began a huge offensive against the Germans in Eastern Europe.
12-01-1948 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that states could not discriminate against law-school applicants because of race.
12-01-1949 "Arthur Godfrey and His Friends" was debuted on CBS-TV. The show stayed on the network for seven years.
12-01-1949 "Kukla, Fran and Ollie", the Chicago-based children’s show, made its national debut on NBC-TV.
12-01-1955 Rod Serling’s stellar career began with the TV production of "Patterns."
12-01-1960 Dolph Schayes of the Syracuse Nationals became the first pro basketball player in the NBA to score more than 15,000 points in his career.
12-01-1964 Leftist rebels in Zanzibar began their successful revolt against the government and a republic was proclaimed.
12-01-1966 U.S. President Johnson said in his State of the Union address that the United States should stay in South Vietnam until Communist aggression there was ended.
12-01-1966 "Batman" debuted on ABC-TV.
12-01-1967 "Dragnet" returned to NBC-TV after being off the network schedule for eight years.
12-01-1970 The breakaway state of Biafra capitulated and the Nigerian civil war came to an end.
12-01-1970 Nigeria's civil war ended.
12-01-1971 "All In the Family" debuted on CBS-TV.
12-01-1973 Yassar Arafat was re-elected as head of the Palestinian Liberation Organization.
12-01-1986 Space shuttle Columbia blasted off with a crew that included the first Hispanic-American in space, Dr. Franklin R. Chang-Diaz.
12-01-1991 The U.S. Congress passed a resolution authorizing President Bush to use military power to force Iraq out of Kuwait.
12-01-1995 Northern Ireland Secretary Patrick Mayhew announced that as of January 16 British troops would no longer carry out daylight street patrols in Belfast.
12-01-1998 Tyson Foods Inc. plead guilty to giving $12,000 to former Agriculture Secretary Mike Espy. Tyson was fined $6 million.
12-01-1998 19 European nations agreed to prohibit human cloning.
12-01-1998 Linda Tripp provided Independent Counsel Kenneth Starr's office with taped conversations between herself and former White House intern Monica Lewinsky.
12-01-1999 Mark McGwire's 70th home run ball was sold at auction in New York for $3 million to an anonymous bidder.
12-01-2000 The U.S. Supreme Court, in a 5-4 ruling, gave police broad authority to stop and question people who run at the sight of an officer.
12-01-2000 Charlotte Hornets guard Bobby Phills was killed in a crash during a drag race.
12-01-2005 NASA launched "Deep Impact". The spacecraft was planned to impact on Comet Tempel 1 after a six-month, 268 million-mile journey.
12-01-2006 The U.S. Mint began shipping new 5-cent coins to the 12 regional Federal Reserve Banks. The coin has an image of Thomas Jefferson taken from a 1800 Rembrandt Peale portrait in which the president is looking forward. Since 1909, when presidents were first depicted on circulating coins, all presidents had been shown in profile.
13-01-1794 U.S. President Washington approved a measure adding two stars and two stripes to the American flag, following the admission of Vermont and Kentucky to the union.
13-01-1854 Anthony Faas of Philadelphia, PA, patented the accordion.
13-01-1893 Britain's Independent Labor Party, a precursor to the current Labor Party, met for the first time.
13-01-1898 Emile Zola's "J'accuse" was published in Paris.
13-01-1900 In Austria-Hungary, Emperor Franz Joseph decreed that German would be the language of the imperial army to combat Czech nationalism.
13-01-1906 Hugh Gernsback, of the Electro Importing Company, advertised radio receivers for sale for the price of just $7.50 in "Scientific American" magazine.
13-01-1928 Ernst F. W. Alexanderson gave the first public demonstration of television.
13-01-1942 Henry Ford patented the plastic automobile, which allowed for a 30% decrease in car weight.
13-01-1957 Wham-O began producing "Pluto Platters." This marked the true beginning of production of the flying disc.
13-01-1962 Ernie Kovacs died in a car crash in west Los Angeles, CA.
13-01-1966 Elizabeth Montgomery’s character, Samantha, on "Bewitched," had a baby. The baby's name was Tabitha.
13-01-1966 Robert C. Weaver became the first black Cabinet member when he was appointed Secretary of Housing and Urban Development by U.S. President Johnson.
13-01-1982 An Air Florida 737 crashed into the capital's 14th Street Bridge after takeoff and fell into the Potomac River. 78 people were killed.
13-01-1984 Wayne Gretzky extended his NHL consecutive scoring streak to 45 games.
13-01-1986 The NCAA adopted the controversial "Proposal 48," which set standards for Division 1 freshman eligibility.
13-01-1986 "The Wall Street Journal" printed a real picture on its front page. The journal had not done this in nearly 10 years. The story was about artist, O. Winston Link and featured one of his works.
13-01-1989 Bernhard H. Goetz was sentenced to one year in prison for possession of an unlicensed gun that he used to shoot four youths he claimed were about to rob him. He was freed the following September.
13-01-1990 L. Douglas Wilder of Virginia, the nation's first elected black governor, took the oath of office in Richmond.
13-01-1992 Japan apologized for forcing tens of thousands of Korean women to serve as sex slaves for Japanese soldiers during World War II.
13-01-1997 Debbie Reynolds received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
13-01-1998 NBC agreed to pay almost $13 million for each episode of the TV show E.R. It was the highest amount ever paid for a TV show.
13-01-1998 ABC and ESPN negotiated to keep "Monday Night Football" for $1.15 billion a season.
13-01-1998 One of the 110 missing episodes of the British TV show "Doctor Who" was found in New Zealand.
13-01-1999 Michael Jordan (Chicago Bulls) announced his retirement from the NBA.
13-01-2002 The exhibit "In the Spirit of Martin: The Living Legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr." opened at the Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History. More than 100 artists supplied the collection of 120 works of art.
13-01-2002 Japan and Singapore signed a free trade pact that would remove tariffs on almost all goods traded between the two countries.
13-01-2002 U.S. President George W. Bush fainted after choking on a pretzel.
14-01-1639 Connecticut's first constitution, the "Fundamental Orders," was adopted.
14-01-1784 The United States ratified a peace treaty with England ending the Revolutionary War.
14-01-1858 French emperor Napoleon III escaped an attempt on his life.
14-01-1873 John Hyatt's 1869 invention ‘Celluloid’ was registered as a trademark.
14-01-1878 Alexander Graham Bell demonstrated the telephone for Britain's Queen Victoria.
14-01-1882 The Myopia Hunt Club, in Winchester, MA, became the first country club in the United States.
14-01-1907 An earthquake killed over 1,000 people in Kingston, Jamaica.
14-01-1939 "Honolulu Bound" was heard on CBS radio for the first time.
14-01-1943 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt became the first U.S. President to fly in an airplane while in office. He flew from Miami, FL, to French Morocco where he met with British Prime Minister Winston Churchill to discuss World War II.
14-01-1951 The first National Football League Pro Bowl All-Star Game was played in Los Angeles, CA.
14-01-1952 NBC's "Today" show premiered.
14-01-1953 Josip Broz Tito was elected president of Yugoslavia by the country's Parliament.
14-01-1954 Marilyn Monroe and Joe DiMaggio were married. The marriage only lasted nine months.
14-01-1954 The Hudson Motor Car Company merged with Nash-Kelvinator. The new company was called the American Motors Corporation.
14-01-1963 George C. Wallace was sworn in as governor of Alabama.
14-01-1969 An explosion aboard the U.S. aircraft carrier Enterprise off Hawaii killed 25 crew members.
14-01-1972 NBC-TV debuted "Sanford & Son."
14-01-1973 The Miami Dolphins defeated the Washington Redskins in Super Bowl VII and became the first NFL team to go undefeated in a season.
14-01-1985 Martina Navratilova won her 100th tournament. She joined Jimmy Connors and Chris Evert Lloyd as the only professional tennis players to win 100 tournaments.
14-01-1985 Former Miss America, Phyllis George, joined Bill Kurtis as host of "The CBS Morning News".
14-01-1986 "Rambo: First Blood, Part II" arrived at video stores. It broke the record set by "Ghostbusters", for first day orders. 435,000 copies of the video were sold.
14-01-1993 Television talk show host David Letterman announced he was moving from NBC to CBS.
14-01-1993 The British government pledged to introduce legislation to criminalize invasions of privacy by the press.
14-01-1994 U.S. President Clinton and Russian President Boris Yeltsin signed Kremlin accords to stop aiming missiles at any nation and to dismantle the nuclear arsenal of Ukraine.
14-01-1996 Jorge Sampaio was elected president of Portugal.
14-01-1996 Juan Garcia Abrego was arrested by Mexican agents. The alleged drug lord was handed over to the FBI the next day.
14-01-1998 Whitewater prosecutors questioned Hillary Rodham Clinton at the White House for 10 minutes about the gathering of FBI background files on past Republican political appointees.
14-01-1998 In Dallas, researchers report an enzyme that slows the aging process and cell death.
14-01-1999 The impeachment trial of U.S. President Clinton began in Washington, DC.
14-01-1999 The U.S. proposed the lifting of the U.N. ceilings on the sale of oil in Iraq. The restriction being that the money be used to buy medicine and food for the Iraqi people.
14-01-2000 A U.N. tribunal sentenced five Bosnian Croats to up to 25 years for the 1993 massacre of over 100 Muslims in a Bosnian village.
14-01-2000 The Dow Jones industrial average hit a new high when it closed at 11,722.98. Earlier in the session, the Dow had risen to 11,750.98. Both records stood until October 3, 2006.
14-01-2002 NBC's "Today" celebrated its 50th anniversary on television.
14-01-2002 Actor Brad Renfro, 19, was arrested after being stopped on a traffic violation. He was charged with public intoxication and driving without a license.
14-01-2004 In St. Louis, a Lewis and Clark Exhibition opened at the Missouri History Museum. The exhibit featured 500 rare and priceless objects used by the Corps of Discovery.
14-01-2005 A probe, from the Cassini-Huygens mission, sent back pictures during and after landing on Saturn's moon Titan. The mission was launched on October 15, 1997.
15-01-1559 England's Queen Elizabeth I (Elizabeth Tudor) was crowned in Westminster Abbey.
15-01-1777 The people of New Connecticut (now the state of Vermont) declared their independence.
15-01-1844 The University of Notre Dame received its charter from the state of Indiana.
15-01-1863 "The Boston Morning Journal" became the first paper in the U.S. to be published on wood pulp paper.
15-01-1870 A cartoon by Thomas Nast titled "A Live Jackass Kicking a Dead Lion" appeared in "Harper's Weekly." The cartoon used the donkey to symbolize the Democratic Party for the first time.
15-01-1892 "Triangle" magazine in Springfield, MA, published the rules for a brand new game. The original rules involved attaching a peach baskets to a suspended board. It is now known as basketball.
15-01-1899 Edwin Markham's poem, "The Man With a Hoe," was published for the first time.
15-01-1906 Willie Hoppe won the billiard championship of the world in Paris, France.
15-01-1908 Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority became America's first Greek-letter organization established by African-American college women.
15-01-1936 The first, all glass, windowless building was completed in Toledo, OH. The building was the new home of the Owens-Illinois Glass Company Laboratory.
15-01-1943 The Pentagon was dedicated as the world's largest office building just outside Washington, DC, in Arlington, VA. The structure covers 34 acres of land and has 17 miles of corridors.
15-01-1945 CBS Radio debuted "House Party". The show was on the air for 22 years.
15-01-1953 Harry S. Truman became the first U.S. President to use radio and television to give his farewell as he left office.
15-01-1955 The first solar-heated, radiation-cooled house was built by Raymond Bliss in Tucson, AZ.
15-01-1967 The first National Football League Super Bowl was played. The Green Bay Packers defeated the Kansas City Chiefs of the American Football League. The final score was 35-10.
15-01-1973 U.S. President Nixon announced the suspension of all U.S. offensive action in North Vietnam. He cited progress in peace negotiations as the reason.
15-01-1974 "Happy Days" premiered on ABC-TV.
15-01-1976 Sara Jane Moore was sentenced to life in prison for her attempt on the life of U.S. President Ford in San Francisco.
15-01-1978 Lisa Levy and Margaret Bowman, two students at Florida State University in Tallahassee, were murdered in their sorority house. Ted Bundy was later convicted of the crime and was executed.
15-01-1987 Paramount Home Video reported that it would place a commercial at the front of one of its video releases for the first time. It was a 30-second Diet Pepsi ad at the beginning of "Top Gun."
15-01-1998 Lance Carvin, a stalker of Howard Stern, was sentenced to 2 1/2 years in prison for threatening to kill Stern and his family.
15-01-2003 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the U.S. Congress had permission to repeatedly extend copyright protection.
16-01-1547 Ivan the Terrible was crowned Czar of Russia.
16-01-1572 The Duke of Norfolk was tried for treason for complicity in the Ridolfi plot to restore Catholicism in England. He was executed on June 2.
16-01-1759 The British Museum opened.
16-01-1809 The British defeated the French at the Battle of Corunna, in the Peninsular War.
16-01-1866 Mr. Everett Barney patented the metal screw, clamp skate.
16-01-1883 The United States Civil Service Commission was established as the Pendleton Act went into effect.
16-01-1896 The first five-player college basketball game was played at Iowa City, IA.
16-01-1900 The U.S. Senate consented to the Anglo-German treaty of 1899, by which the U.K. renounced rights to the Samoan islands.
16-01-1919 The 18th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which prohibited the sale or transportation of alcoholic beverages, was ratified. It was later repealed by the 21st Amendment.
16-01-1920 Prohibition went into effect in the U.S.
16-01-1920 The motion picture "The Kid" opened.
16-01-1925 Leon Trotsky was dismissed as Chairman of the Revolutionary Council of the USSR.
16-01-1939 The "I Love a Mystery" debuted on NBC’s West-Coast outlets.
16-01-1944 General Dwight D. Eisenhower took command of the Allied invasion force in London.
16-01-1961 Mickey Mantle signed a contract that made him the highest paid baseball player in the American League at $75,000 for the 1961 season.
16-01-1964 "Hello Dolly!" opened at the St. James Theatre in New York City.
16-01-1970 Colonel Muammar el-Quaddafi became virtual president of Libya.
16-01-1970 Buckminster Fuller, the designer of the geodesic dome, was awarded the Gold Medal of the American Institute of Architects.
16-01-1979 The Shah of Iran and his family fled Iran for Egypt.
16-01-1982 Britain and the Vatican resumed full diplomatic relations after a break of over 400 years.
16-01-1985 "Playboy" magazine announced its 30-year tradition of stapling centerfold models in the bellybutton and elsewhere would come to an immediate end.
16-01-1988 Jimmy "The Greek" Snyder was fired as a CBS sports commentator one day after telling a TV station in Washington, DC, that, during the era of slavery, blacks had been bred to produce stronger offspring.
16-01-1998 Researchers announce that an altered gene helped to defend against HIV.
16-01-1991 The White House announced the start of Operation Desert Storm. The operation was designed to drive Iraqi forces out of Kuwait.
16-01-1992 Officials of the government of El Salvador and rebel leaders signed a pact in Mexico City ending 12 years of civil war. At least 75,000 people were killed during the fighting.
16-01-1997 Bill Cosby's only son, Ennis, 27, was shot to death while changing a flat tire on a dark road in Los Angeles, CA.
16-01-1998 The first woman to enroll at Virginia Military Institute withdrew from the school.
16-01-1998 NASA officially announced that John Glenn would fly aboard the space shuttle Discovery in October.
16-01-1998 It was announced that Texas would receive $15.3 billion in a tobacco industry settlement. The payouts were planned to take place over 25 years.
16-01-1998 Three federal judges secretly granted Kenneth Starr authority to probe whether U.S. President Clinton or Vernon Jordan urged Monica Lewinsky to lie about her relationship with Clinton.
16-01-1999 At least 37 bodies were found mutilated of executed in southern Kosovo. They were all in civilian clothing. This occurred one day after a fierce attack by Serb forces.
16-01-2000 A truck loaded with evaporated milk was rammed into California's state capitol building in Sacramento, CA. The driver was killed in the incident.
16-01-2000 Ricardo Lagos was elected Chile's first socialist president since Salvador Allende.
16-01-2002 In Grundy, VA, student killed three and wounding three others at the Appalachian School of Law.
16-01-2002 U.S. Attorney General John Ashcroft announced that John Walker Lindh would be brought to the United States to face trial. He was charged in U.S. District Court in Alexandria, VA, with conspiracy to kill U.S. citizens, providing support to terrorist organizations, and engaging in prohibited transactions with the Taliban of Afghanistan.
16-01-2002 The U.N. Security Council unanimously adopted sanctions against Osama bin Laden, his terror network and the remnants of the Taliban. The sanctions required that all nations impose arms embargoes and freeze their finances.
17-01-1377 The Papal See was transferred from Avignon in France back to Rome.
17-01-1562 French Protestants were recognized under the Edict of St. Germain.
17-01-1773 Captain Cook's Resolution became the first ship to cross the Antarctic Circle.
17-01-1795 The Dudingston Curling Society was organized in Edinburgh, Scotland.
17-01-1806 James Madison Randolph, grandson of U.S. President Thomas Jefferson, was the first child born in the White House.
17-01-1852 The independence of the Transvaal Boers was recognized by Britain.
17-01-1871 Andrew S. Hallidie received a patent for a cable car system.
17-01-1882 Thomas Edison's exhibit opened the Crystal Palace Exhibition in London.
17-01-1893 Hawaii's monarchy was overthrown when a group of businessmen and sugar planters forced Queen Liliuokalani to abdicate.
17-01-1900 The U.S. took Wake Island where there was in important cable link between Hawaii and Manila.
17-01-1900 Yaqui Indians in Texas proclaimed their independence from Mexico.
17-01-1900 Mormon Brigham Roberts was denied a seat in the U.S. House of Representatives for his practicing of polygamy.
17-01-1905 Punchboards were patented by a manufacturing firm in Chicago, IL.
17-01-1912 English explorer Robert Falcon Scott reached the South Pole. Norwegian Roald Amundsen had beaten him there by one month. Scott and his party died during the return trip.
17-01-1913 All partner interests in 36 Golden Rule Stores were consolidated and incorporated in Utah into one company. The new corporation was the J.C. Penney Company.
17-01-1916 The Professional Golfers Association was formed in New York City.
17-01-1928 The fully automatic, film-developing machine was patented by A.M. Josepho.
17-01-1934 Ferdinand Porsche submitted a design for a people's car, a "Volkswagen," to the new German Reich government.
17-01-1938 "Stepmother" debuted on CBS radio.
17-01-1945 Soviet and Polish forces liberated Warsaw during World War II.
17-01-1945 Swedish diplomat Raoul Wallenberg disappeared in Hungary while in Soviet custody. Wallenberg was credited with saving tens of thousands of Jews.
17-01-1946 The United Nations Security Council held its first meeting.
17-01-1949 "The Goldbergs" debuted on CBS-TV. The program had been on radio since 1931. The TV version lasted for four years.
17-01-1959 Senegal and the French Sudan joined to form the Federal State of Mali.
17-01-1961 In his farewell address, U.S. President Eisenhower warned against the rise of "the military-industrial complex."
17-01-1966 A B-52 carrying four H-bombs collided with a refuelling tanker. The bombs were released and eight crewmembers were killed.
17-01-1977 Double murderer Gary Gilmore became the first to be executed in the U.S. in a decade. The firing squad took place at Utah State Prison.
17-01-1985 Leonard Nimoy got a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
17-01-1991 Coalition airstrikes began against Iraq after negotiations failed to get Iraq to retreat from the country of Kuwait.
17-01-1992 An IRA bomb, placed next to a remote country road in County Tyrone, Northern Ireland, killed seven building workers and injured seven others.
17-01-1994 The Northridge earthquake rocked Los Angeles, CA, registering a 6.7 on the Richter Scale. At least 61 people were killed and about $20 billion in damage was caused.
17-01-1995 More than 6,000 people were killed when an earthquake with a magnitude of 7.2 devastated the city of Kobe, Japan.
17-01-1997 A court in Ireland granted the first divorce in the Roman Catholic country's history.
17-01-1997 Israel gave over 80% of Hebron to Palestinian rule, but held the remainder where several hundred Jewish settlers lived among 20,000 Palestinians.
17-01-1998 U.S. President Clinton gave his deposition in the Paula Jones sexual harassment lawsuit against him. He was the first U.S. President to testify as a defendant in a criminal or civil lawsuit.
17-01-2000 British pharmaceutical companies Glaxo Wellcome PLC and SmithKline Beecham PLC agreed to a merger that created the world's largest drugmaker.
17-01-2001 Congo's President Laurent Kabila was shot and killed during a coup attempt. Congolese officials temporarily placed Kabila's son in charge of the government.
17-01-2001 The director of Palestinian TV, Hisham Miki, was killed at a restaurant when three masked gunmen walked up to his table and shot him more than 10 times.
17-01-2002 It was announced that Microsoft had signed a joint venture agreement to produce software with two partners in China. The two partners were Beijin Centergate Technologies (Holding) Co. and the Stone Group.
18-01-1803 Thomas Jefferson, in secret communication with Congress, seeked authorization for the first official exploration by the U.S. government.
18-01-1778 English navigator Captain James Cook discovered the Hawaiian Islands, which he called the "Sandwich Islands."
18-01-1788 The first English settlers arrived in Australia's Botany Bay to establish a penal colony. The group moved north eight days later and settled at Port Jackson.
18-01-1871 Wilhelm, King of Prussia from 1861, was proclaimed the first German Emperor.
18-01-1886 The Hockey Association was formed in England. This date is the birthday of modern field hockey.
18-01-1896 The x-ray machine was exhibited for the first time.
18-01-1911 For the first time an aircraft landed on a ship. Pilot Eugene B. Ely flew onto the deck of the USS Pennsylvania in San Francisco harbor.
18-01-1919 The World War I Peace Congress opened in Versailles, France.
18-01-1929 Walter Winchell made his debut on radio.
18-01-1937 CBS radio debuted "Aunt Jenny’s Real Life Stories".
18-01-1939 Louis Armstrong and his orchestra recorded "Jeepers Creepers."
18-01-1943 During World War II, the Soviets announced that they had broken the Nazi siege of Leningrad, which had began in September of 1941.
18-01-1943 U.S. commercial bakers stopped selling sliced bread. Only whole loaves were sold during the ban until the end of World War II.
18-01-1948 "The Original Amateur Hour" debuted. The show was on the air for 22 years.
18-01-1950 The federal tax on oleomargarine was repealed.
18-01-1951 Joan Blondell made her TV debut on "Pot of Gold" episode of "Airflyte Theatre" on CBS-TV.
18-01-1957 The first, non-stop, around-the-world, jet flight came to an end at Riverside, CA. The plane was refueled in mid-flight by huge aerial tankers.
18-01-1958 Willie O'Ree became made his NHL debut with the Boston Bruins. He was the first black player to enter the league.
18-01-1967 Albert DeSalvo, who claimed to be the "Boston Strangler," was convicted in Cambridge, MA, of armed robbery, assault and sex offenses. He was sentenced to life in prison. Desalvo was killed in 1973 by a fellow inmate.
18-01-1972 Former Rhodesian prime minister Garfield Todd and his daughter were placed under house arrest for campaigning against Rhodesian independence.
18-01-1975 "The Jeffersons" debuted on CBS-TV.
