18-01-1978 The European Court of Human Rights cleared the British government of torture but found it guilty of inhuman and degrading treatment of prisoners in Northern Ireland.
18-01-1985 Mary Decker broke a world, indoor record when she ran the women’s, 2,000-meter race in 5:34.2. She also ran the outdoor mile in 4:16.7.
18-01-1987 For the first time in history the Public Broadcasting System (PBS) was seen by over 100 million viewers. The audience was measured during the week of January 12-18.
18-01-1990 A jury in Los Angeles, CA, acquitted former preschool operators Raymond Buckey and his mother, Peggy McMartin Buckey, of 52 child molestation charges.
18-01-1990 In an FBI sting, Washington, DC, Mayor Marion Barry was arrested for drug possession. He was later convicted of a misdemeanor.
18-01-1991 Eastern Airlines shut down after 62 years in business due to financial problems.
18-01-1993 The Martin Luther King Jr. holiday was observed in all 50 U.S. states for the first time.
18-01-1995 A network of caves were discovered near the town of Vallon-Pont-d'Arc in southern France. The caves contained paintings and engravings that were 17,000 to 20,000 years old.
18-01-1997 Hutu militiamen killed three Spanish aid workers and three soldiers and seriously wound an American in a night attack in NW Rwanda.
18-01-2002 The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) announced the approval of a saliva-based ovulation test.
19-01-1419 Rouen surrendered to Henry V, completing his conquest of Normandy.
19-01-1764 John Wilkes was expelled from the British House of Commons for seditious libel.
19-01-1793 King Louis XVI was tried by the French Convention, found guilty of treason and sentenced to the guillotine.
19-01-1825 Ezra Daggett and Thomas Kensett of New York City patented a canning process to preserve salmon, oysters and lobsters.
19-01-1861 Georgia seceded from the Union.
19-01-1883 Thomas Edison's first village electric lighting system using overhead wires began operation in Roselle, NJ.
19-01-1907 The first film reviews appeared in "Variety" magazine.
19-01-1915 George Claude, of Paris, France, patented the neon discharge tube for use in advertising signs.
19-01-1915 More than 20 people were killed when German zeppelins bombed England for the first time. The bombs were dropped on Great Yarmouth and King's Lynn.
19-01-1937 Howard Hughes set a transcontinental air record. He flew from Los Angeles to New York City in 7 hours, 28 minutes and 25 seconds.
19-01-1942 The Japanese invaded Burma (later Myanmar).
19-01-1944 The U.S. federal government relinquished control of the nation's railroads after the settlement of a wage dispute.
19-01-1949 The salary of the President of the United States was increased from $75,000 to $100,000 with an additional $50,000 expense allowance for each year in office.
19-01-1952 The National Football League (NFL) bought the franchise of the New York Yankees from Ted Collins. The franchise was then awarded to a group in Dallas on January 24.
19-01-1953 Sixty-eight percent of all TV sets in the U.S. were tuned to CBS-TV, as Lucy Ricardo, of "I Love Lucy," gave birth to a baby boy.
19-01-1955 U.S. President Eisenhower allowed a filmed news conference to be used on television (and in movie newsreels) for the first time.
19-01-1957 Philadelphia comedian, Ernie Kovacs, did a half-hour TV show without saying a single word of dialogue.
19-01-1966 Indira Gandhi was elected prime minister of India.
19-01-1969 In protest against the Russian invasion of 1968, Czech student Jan Palach set himself on fire in Prague's Wenceslas Square.
19-01-1971 At the Charles Manson murder trial, the Beatles' "Helter Skelter" was played. At the scene of one of his gruesome murders, the words "helter skelter" were written on a mirror.
19-01-1971 "No, No Nanette" opened at the 46th Street Theatre in New York City.
19-01-1977 U.S. President Ford pardoned Iva Toguri D'Aquino (the "Tokyo Rose").
19-01-1979 Former U.S. Attorney General John N. Mitchell was released on parole after serving 19 months at a federal prison in Alabama.
19-01-1981 The U.S. and Iran signed an agreement paving the way for the release of 52 Americans held hostage for more than 14 months and for arrangements to unfreeze Iranian assets and to resolve all claims against Iran.
19-01-1993 IBM announced a loss of $4.97 billion for 1992. It was the largest single-year loss in U.S. corporate history.
19-01-1995 Russian forces overwhelmed the resistance forces in Chechnya.
19-01-1996 U.S. first lady Hillary Rodham Clinton was subpoenaed to appear before a federal grand jury. The investigation was concerning the discovery of billing records related to the Whitewater real estate investment venture.
19-01-1997 Yasser Arafat returned to Hebron for the first time in more than 30 years. He joined 60,000 Palestinians in celebration over the handover of the last West Bank city in Israeli control.
19-01-2000 In New York's Time Square, the first WWF restraunt opened.
19-01-2001 Texas officials demoted a warden and suspended three other prison workers in the wake of the escape of the "Texas 7."
20-01-1265 The first English parliament met in Westminster Hall.
20-01-1801 John Marshall was appointed chief justice of the United States.
20-01-1839 Chile defeated a confederation of Peru and Bolivia in the Battle of Yungay.
20-01-1841 The island of Hong Kong was ceded to Great Britain. It returned to Chinese control in July 1997.
20-01-1885 The roller coaster was patented by L.A. Thompson.
20-01-1886 The Mersey Railway Tunnel was officially opened by the Prince of Wales.
20-01-1887 The U.S. Senate approved an agreement to lease Pearl Harbor in Hawaii as a naval base.
20-01-1891 James Hogg took office as the first native-born governor of Texas.
20-01-1892 The first official basketball game was played by students at the Springfield, MA, YMCA Training School.
20-01-1929 The movie "In Old Arizona" was released. The film was the first full-length talking film to be filmed outdoors.
20-01-1937 Franklin Delano Roosevelt became the first U.S. President to be inaugurated on January 20th. The 20th Amendment of the U.S. Constitution officially set the date for the swearing in of the President and Vice President.
20-01-1942 Nazi officials held the Wannsee conference, during which they arrived at their "final solution" that called for exterminating Europe's Jews.
20-01-1944 The British RAF dropped 2,300 tons of bombs on Berlin.
20-01-1952 In Juarez, Mexico, Patricia McCormick debuted as the first professional woman bullfighter from the United States.
20-01-1953 "Studio One" became the first television show to be transmitted from the United States to Canada.
20-01-1954 The National Negro Network was formed on this date. Forty radio stations were charter members of the network.
20-01-1961 Marilyn Monroe and Arthur Miller were divorced. They were married on June 29, 1956.
20-01-1972 The number of unemployed in Britain exceeded 1 million.
20-01-1981 Iran released 52 Americans that had been held hostage for 444 days. The hostages were flown to Algeria and then to a U.S. base in Wiesbaden, West Germany. The release occurred minutes after the U.S. presidency had passed from Jimmy Carter to Ronald Reagan.
20-01-1985 The most-watched Super Bowl game in history was seen by an estimated 115.9 million people. The San Francisco 49ers downed the Miami Dolphins, 38-16. Super Bowl XIX marked the first time that TV commericals sold for a million dollars a minute.
20-01-1986 The U.S. observed the first federal holiday in honor of slain civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr.
20-01-1986 Britain and France announced their plans to build the Channel Tunnel.
20-01-1986 New footage of the 1931 "Frankenstein" was found. The footage was originally deleted because it was considered to be too shocking.
20-01-1987 Anglican Church envoy Terry Waite was kidnapped in Beirut, Lebanon. He was there attempting to negotiate the release of Western hostages. He was not freed until November 1991.
20-01-1994 Shannon Faulkner became the first woman to attend classes at The Citadel in South Carolina. Faulkner joined the cadet corps in August 1995 under court order but soon dropped out.
20-01-1996 Yasser Arafat was elected president of the Palestinian Authority and his supporters won two thirds of the 80 seats in the Legislative Council.
20-01-1997 Bill Clinton was inagurated for his second term as president of the United States.
20-01-1998 American researchers announced that they had cloned calves that may produce medicinal milk.
20-01-1998 In Chili, a judge agreed to hear a lawsuit that accused Chili's former dictator Augusto Pinochet with genocide.
20-01-1999 The China News Service announced that the Chinese government was tightening restrictions on internet use. The rules were aimed at 'Internet Bars.'
20-01-2000 Greece and Turkey signed five accords aimed to build confidence between the two nations.
20-01-2002 Michael Jordan (Washington Wizards) played his first game in Chicago as a visiting player. The Wizards beat the Bulls 77-69.
21-01-1789 W.H. Brown's "Power of Sympathy" was published. It was the first American novel to be published.
21-01-1793 During the French Revolution, King Louis XVI was executed on the guillotine. He had been condemned for treason.
21-01-1812 The Y-bridge in Zanesville, OH, was approved for construction.
21-01-1846 The first issue of the "Daily News," edited by Charles Dickens, was published.
21-01-1853 Dr. Russell L. Hawes patented the envelope folding machine.
21-01-1861 The future president of the Confederacy, Jefferson Davis of Mississippi, resigned from the U.S. Senate. Four other Southerners also resigned.
21-01-1865 An oil well was drilled by torpedoes for the first time.
21-01-1900 Canadian troops set sail to fight in South Africa. The Boers had attacked Ladysmith on January 8, 1900.
21-01-1908 The Sullivan Ordinance was passed in New York City making smoking by women became illegal. The measure was vetoed by Mayor George B. McClellan Jr.
21-01-1911 The first Monte Carlo car rally was held. Seven days later by French it was won by Henri Rougier.
21-01-1915 The first Kiwanis club was formed in Detroit, MI.
21-01-1924 Soviet leader Vladimir Llyich Lenin died. Joseph Stalin began a purge of his rivals for the leadership of the Soviet Union.
21-01-1927 The first opera broadcast over a national radio network was presented in Chicago, IL. The opera was "Faust".
21-01-1941 The British communist newspaper, the "Daily Worker," was banned due to wartime restrictions.
21-01-1946 "The Fat Man" debuted on ABC radio.
21-01-1954 The Nautilus was launched in Groton, CT. It was the first atomic-powered submarine. U.S. First Lady Mamie Eisenhower broke the traditional bottle of champagne across the bow.
21-01-1954 The gas turbine automobile was introduced in New York City.
21-01-1970 The Boeing 747 made its first commercial flight from New York to London for Pan American.
21-01-1970 ABC-TV presented "The Johnny Cash Show" in prime time.
21-01-1976 The French Concorde SST aircraft began regular commercial service for Air France and British Airways.
21-01-1977 U.S. President Carter pardoned almost all Vietnam War draft evaders.
21-01-1980 Gold was valued at $850 an ounce.
21-01-1986 Former major-league player, Randy Bass, became the highest-paid baseball player in Japanese history. Bass signed a three-year contract for $3.25 million. He played for the Hanshin Tigers.
21-01-1994 A jury in Manassas, VA, acquitted Lorena Bobbitt by reason of temporary insanity of maliciously wounding (severing his penis) her husband John. She accused him of sexually assaulting her.
21-01-1997 Newt Gingrich was fined as the U.S. House of Representatvies voted for first time in history to discipline its leader for ethical misconduct.
21-01-1998 A former White House intern said on tape that she had an affair with U.S. President Clinton.
21-01-1999 The U.S. Coast Guard intercepted a ship headed for Houston, TX, that had over 9,500 pounds of cocaine aboard. It was one of the largest drug busts in U.S. history.
21-01-2002 In Goma, Congo, about fifty people were killed when lava flow ignited a gas station. The people killed were trying to steal fuel from elevated tanks. The eruption of Mount Nyiragongo began on January 17, 2002.
21-01-2002 In London, a 17th century book by Capt. John Smith, founder of the English settlement at Jamestown, was sold at auction for $48,800. "The General History of Virginia, New England and the Summer Isles" was published in 1632.
21-01-2003 It was announced by the U.S. Census Bureau that estimates showed that the Hispanic population had passed the black population for the first time.
22-01-1666 Shah Jahan, a descendant of Genghis Khan and Timur, died at the age of 74. He was the Mogul emperor of India that built the Taj Mahal as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz-i-Mahal.
22-01-1771 The Falkland Islands were ceded to Britain by Spain.
22-01-1789 "The Power of Sympathy," by Philenia (Mrs. Sarah W.) Morton, was published in Boston, MA.
22-01-1824 The Asante army crushed British troops in the Gold Coast.
22-01-1879 James Shields began a term as a U.S. Senator from Missouri. He had previously served Illinois and Minnesota. He was the first Senator to serve three states.
22-01-1879 British troops were massacred by the Zulus at Isandhlwana.
22-01-1889 The Columbia Phonograph Company was formed in Washington, DC.
22-01-1895 The National Association of Manufacturers was organized in Cincinnati, OH.
22-01-1900 Off of South Africa, the British released the German steamer Herzog, which had been seized on January 6.
22-01-1901 Queen Victoria of England died after reigning for nearly 64 years. Edward VII, her son, succeeded her.
22-01-1905 Insurgent workers were fired on in St Petersburg, Russia, resulting in "Bloody Sunday." 500 people were killed.
22-01-1917 U.S. President Wilson pleaded for an end to war in Europe, calling for "peace without victory." America entered the war the following April.
22-01-1924 Ramsay MacDonald became Britain's first Labour Prime Minister.
22-01-1936 In Paris, Premier Pierre Laval resigned over diplomatic failure in the Ethiopian crisis.
22-01-1938 "Our Town," by Thornton Wilder, was performed publicly for the first time, in Princeton, NJ.
22-01-1941 Britain captured Tobruk from German forces.
22-01-1944 Allied forces began landing at Anzio, Italy, during World War II.
22-01-1947 KTLA, Channel 5, in Hollywood, CA, began operation as the first commercial television station west of the Mississippi River.
22-01-1950 Alger Hiss, a former adviser to U.S. President Franklin Roosevelt, was convicted of perjury for denying contacts with a Soviet agent. He was sentenced to five years in prison.
22-01-1951 Fidel Castro was ejected from a Winter League baseball game after hitting a batter. He later gave up baseball for politics.
22-01-1953 The Arthur Miller drama "The Crucible" opened on Broadway.
22-01-1956 Raymond Burr starred as Captain Lee Quince in the "Fort Laramie" debut on CBS radio.
22-01-1957 Suspected "Mad Bomber" was arrested in Waterbury, CT. George P. Metesky was accused of planting more than 30 explosive devices in the New York City area.
22-01-1957 The Israeli army withdrew from the Sinai. They had invaded Egypt on October 29, 1956.
22-01-1959 British world racing champion Mike Hawthorn was killed while driving on the Guildford bypass.
22-01-1961 Wilma Rudolph, set a world indoor record in the women’s 60-yard dash. She ran the race in 6.9 seconds.
22-01-1962 Cuba's membership in the Organization of American States (OAS) was suspended.
22-01-1964 Kenneth Kaunda was sworn in as the first Prime Minister of Northern Rhodesia.
22-01-1968 "Rowan & Martin's Laugh-In", debuted on NBC TV.
22-01-1970 The first regularly scheduled commercial flight of the Boeing 747 began in New York City and ended in London about 6 1/2 hours later.
22-01-1972 The United Kingdom, the Irish Republic, and Denmark joined the EEC.
22-01-1973 Joe Frazier lost the first fight of his professional career to George Foreman. He been the undefeated heavyweight world champion since February 16, 1970 when he knocked out Jimmy Ellis.
22-01-1973 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down state laws that had been restricting abortions during the first six months of pregnancy. The case (Roe vs. Wade) legalized abortion.
22-01-1983 Bjorn Borg retired from tennis. He had set a record by winning 5 consecutive Wimbledon championships.
22-01-1984 Apple introduced the Macintosh. It was the first computer to use point-and-click technology.
22-01-1987 Phil Donahue became the first talk show host to tape a show from inside the Soviet Union. The shows were shown later in the year.
22-01-1992 Rebel soldiers seized the national radio station in Kinshasa, Zaire's capital, and broadcast a demand for the government's resignation.
22-01-1995 Two Palestinian suicide bombers from the Gaza Strip detonated powerful explosives at a military transit point in central Israel, killing 19 Israelis.
22-01-1997 The U.S. Senate confirmed Madeleine Albright as the first female secretary of state.
22-01-1998 Theodore Kaczynski plead guilty to federal charges for his role as the Unabomber. He agreed to life in prison without parole.
22-01-2000 Elian Gonzalez's grandmothers met privately with U.S. Attorney General Janet Reno as they appealed for help in removing the boy from his Florida relatives and reuniting him with his father in Cuba.
22-01-2001 Former National Football League (NFL) player Rae Carruth was sentenced to a minimum 18 years and 11 months in prison for his role in the 1999 shooting death of his pregnant girlfriend, Cherica Adams. Adams died a month later from her wounds. The baby survived and lives with the victim's mother.
22-01-2001 Acting on a tip, authorities captured four of the "Texas 7" in Woodland Park, CO, at a convenience store. A fifth convict killed himself inside a motor home.
22-01-2002 In Calcutta, India, Heavily armed gunmen attacked the U.S. government cultural center. Five police officers were killed and twenty others, including one pedestrian and one private security guard, were wounded.
22-01-2002 Lawyers suing Enron Corp. asked a court to prevent further shredding of documents due to the pending federal investigation.
22-01-2002 Amazon.com announced that it had posted its first net profit in the fourth quarter (quarter ending December 31, 2001).
22-01-2002 AOL Time Warner filed suit against Microsoft in federal court seeking damages for harm done to AOL's Netscape Internet Browser when Microsoft began giving away its competing browser.
22-01-2002 Marc Chagall's work "Study for 'Over Vitebsk" was found at a postal installation in Topeka, KS. The 8x10 oil painting is valued at about $1 million. The work was stolen a year before form the Jewish Museum in New York City.
22-01-2002 Kmart Corp. filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy making it the largest retailer in history to seek legal protection from its creditors.
22-01-2003 In New York, the "Leonardo da Vinci, Master Draftsmen" exhibit opened at the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
22-01-2003 It was reported that scientists in China had found fossilized remains of a dinosaur with four feathered wings.
23-01-1556 An earthquake in Shanxi Province, China, was thought to have killed about 830,000 people.
23-01-1571 The Royal Exchange in London, founded by financier Thomas Gresham, was opened by Queen Elizabeth I.
23-01-1789 Georgetown College was established as the first Catholic college in the U.S. The school is in Washington, DC.
23-01-1845 The U.S. Congress decided all national elections would be held on the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November.
23-01-1849 English-born Elizabeth Blackwell became the first woman in America to receive medical degree. It was from the Medical Institution of Geneva, NY.
23-01-1907 Charles Curtis, of Kansas, began serving in the United States Senate. He was the first American Indian to become a U.S. Senator. He resigned in March of 1929 to become U.S. President Herbert Hoover’s Vice President.
23-01-1920 The Dutch government refused the demands from the Allies to hand over the ex-kaiser of Germany.
23-01-1924 The first Labour government was formed, under Ramsay MacDonald.
23-01-1937 In Moscow, seventeen people went on trial during Josef Stalin's "Great Purge."
23-01-1941 The play, "Lady in the Dark" premiered.
23-01-1943 Duke Ellington and the band played for a black-tie crowd at Carnegie Hall in New York City for the first time.
23-01-1943 The British captured Tripoli from the Germans.
23-01-1950 The Israeli Knesset approved a resolution proclaiming Jerusalem as the capital of Israel.
23-01-1960 The U.S. Navy bathyscaphe Trieste descended to a record depth of 35,820 feet (10,750 meters) in the Pacific Ocean.
23-01-1964 Ratification of the 24th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was completed. This amendment eliminated the poll tax in federal elections.
23-01-1968 North Korea seized the U.S. Navy ship Pueblo, charging it had intruded into the nation's territorial waters on a spying mission. The crew was released 11 months later.
23-01-1971 In Prospect Creek Camp, AK, the lowest temperature ever recorded in the U.S. was reported as minus 80 degrees.
23-01-1973 U.S. President Nixon announced that an accord had been reached to end the Vietnam War.
23-01-1974 Mike Oldfield’s "Tubular Bells" opened the credits of the movie, "The Exorcist".
23-01-1975 "Barney Miller" made his debut on ABC-TV.
23-01-1977 The TV mini-series "Roots," began airing on ABC. The show was based on the Alex Haley novel.
23-01-1978 Sweden banned aerosol sprays because of damage to environment. They were the first country to do so.
23-01-1983 "The A-Team" debuted on TV.
23-01-1985 O.J. Simpson became the first Heisman Trophy winner to be elected to pro football’s Hall of Fame in Canton, OH.
23-01-1985 The proceedings of the House of Lords were televised for the first time.
23-01-1989 Surrealist artist Salvador Dali died in Spain at age 84.
23-01-1997 A judge in Fairfax, VA, sentenced Mir Aimal Kasi to death for an assault rifle attack outside the CIA headquarters in 1993 that killed two men and wounded three other people.
23-01-1997 A British woman received a record Ł186,000 damages for Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI).
23-01-2001 A van used by the remaining two fugitives of the "Texas 7" was recovered in Colorado Springs, CO. A few hours later police surrounded a hotel where the convicts were hiding. Patrick Murphy Jr. and Donald Newbury were taken into custody the next morning without incident.
23-01-2002 John Walker Lindh returned to the U.S. under FBI custody. Lindh was charge with conspiring to kill U.S. citizens, providing support to terrorists and engaging in prohibited transactions with the Taliban while a member of the al-Quaida terrorist organization in Afghanistan.
23-01-2003 North Korea announced that it would consider sanctions an act of war for North Korea's reinstatement of its nuclear program.
24-01-1848 James W. Marshall discovered a gold nugget at Sutter's Mill in northern California. The discovery led to the gold rush of '49.
24-01-1888 The typewriter ribbon was patented by Jacob L. Wortman.
24-01-1899 Humphrey O’Sullivan patented the rubber heel.
24-01-1908 In England, the first Boy Scout troop was organized by Robert Baden-Powell.
24-01-1916 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that income tax was unconstitutional.
24-01-1916 Conscription was introduced in Britain.
24-01-1922 Christian K. Nelson patented the Eskimo Pie.
24-01-1924 The Russian city of St. Petersburg was renamed Leningrad. The name has since been changed back to St. Petersburg.
24-01-1930 Primo Carnera made his American boxing debut by knocking out Big Boy Patterson in one minute, ten seconds of the opening round.
24-01-1935 Krueger Brewing Company placed the first canned beer on sale in Richmond, VA.
24-01-1942 "Abie’s Irish Rose" was first heard on NBC radio.
24-01-1943 U.S. President Roosevelt and British Prime Minister Churchill concluded a wartime conference in Casablanca, Morocco.
24-01-1952 Vincent Massey was the first Canadian to be appointed governor-general of Canada.
24-01-1955 The rules committee of major league baseball announced a plan to strictly enforce the rule that required a pitcher to release the ball within 20 seconds after taking his position on the mound.
24-01-1964 CBS-TV acquired the rights to televise the National Football League’s 1964-1965 regular season. The move cost CBS $14.1 million a year. The NFL stayed on CBS for 30 years.
24-01-1965 Winston Churchill died at the age of 90.
24-01-1972 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down laws that denied welfare benefits to people who had resided in a state for less than a year.
24-01-1978 A nuclear-powered Soviet satellite plunged through Earth's atmosphere and disintegrated. The radioactive debris was scattered over parts of Canada's Northwest Territory.
24-01-1985 Penny Harrington became the first woman police chief of a major city. She assumed the duties as head of the Portland, Oregon, force of 940 officers and staff.
24-01-1986 The Voyager 2 space probe flew past Uranus. The probe came within 50,679 miles of the seventh planet of the solar system.
24-01-1987 In Lebanon, gunmen kidnapped educators Alann Steen, Jesse Turner, Robert Polhill and Mitheleshwar Singh. They were all later released.
24-01-1989 Ted Bundy, the confessed serial killer, was put to death in Florida's electric chair for the 1978 kidnap-murder of 12-year-old Kimberly Leach.
24-01-1990 Japan launched the first probe to be sent to the Moon since 1976. A small satellite was placed in lunar orbit.
24-01-1995 The prosecution gave its opening statement at the O.J. Simpson murder trial.
24-01-1996 Polish Premier Jozef Oleksy resigned due to allegations that he had spied for Moscow.
24-01-2000 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld a Missouri law that limited the contributions that individuals could donate to a candidate during a single election.
24-01-2001 In Colorado Springs, CO, Patrick Murphy Jr. and Donald Newbury were taken into custody after a 5-minute phone interview was granted with a TV station. They were the remaining fugitives of the "Texas 7."
24-01-2002 The U.S. Congress began a hearing on the collapse of Enron Corp.
24-01-2002 John Walker Lindh appeared in court for the first time concerning the charges that he conspired to kill Americans abroad and aided terrorist groups. Lindh had been taken into custody by U.S. Marines in Afghanistan.
24-01-2003 The U.S. Department of Homeland Security began operations under Tom Ridge.
25-01-1504 The English Parliament passed statutes against retainers and liveries, to curb private warfare.
25-01-1533 England's King Henry VIII secretly married his second wife Anne Boleyn. Boleyn later gave birth to Elizabeth I.
25-01-1579 The Treaty of Utrecht was signed marking the beginning of the Dutch Republic.
25-01-1799 Eliakim Spooner patented the seeding machine.
25-01-1858 Mendelssohn’s "Wedding March" was presented for the first time, as the daughter of Queen Victoria married the Crown Prince of Prussia.
25-01-1870 G.D. Dows patented the ornamental soda fountain.
25-01-1881 Thomas Edison, Alexander Graham Bell and others signed an agreement to organize the Oriental Telephone Company.
25-01-1890 The United Mine Workers of America was founded.
25-01-1915 In New York, Alexander Graham Bell spoke to his assistant in San Francisco, inaugurating the first transcontinental telephone service.
25-01-1924 The 1st Winter Olympic Games were inaugurated in Chamonix in the French Alps.
25-01-1927 Jack Benny married Sadye Marks on this day. Sadye changed her name to Mary Livingstone.
25-01-1937 NBC radio presented the first broadcast of "The Guiding Light." The show remained on radio until 1956 and began on CBS-TV in 1952.
25-01-1945 Richard Tucker debuted at the Metropolitan Opera House in New York City in the production of "La Gioconda".
25-01-1946 The United Mine Workers rejoined the American Federation of Labor.
25-01-1949 The first Emmys were presented at the Hollywood Athletic Club.
25-01-1950 A federal jury in New York City found former State Department official Alger Hiss guilty of perjury.
25-01-1959 In the U.S., American Airlines had the first scheduled transcontinental flight of a Boeing 707.
25-01-1961 John F. Kennedy presented the first live presidential news conference from Washington, DC. The event was carried on radio and television.
25-01-1971 Charles Manson and three female members of his "family" were found guilty of one count of conspiracy to commit murder and seven counts of murder in the first degree. They were all sentenced to life imprisonment for the 1969 killings.
25-01-1971 Maj. Gen. Idi Amin led a coup that deposed Milton Obote and became president of Uganda.
25-01-1981 Jiang Qing, Mao's widow, was tried for treason and received a death sentence, which was subsequently commuted to life imprisonment.
25-01-1981 The 52 Americans held hostage by Iran for 444 days arrived in the United States and were reunited with their families.
25-01-1987 The New York Giants defeated the Denver Broncos, 39-20, in Super Bowl XXI on NBC. The game featured TV commercials cost $550,000 for 30 seconds.
25-01-1993 A gunman shot and killed two CIA employees outside the agencies headquarters in Virginia. Mir Aimal Kansi, a Pakistani national, was later convicted of the shootings.
25-01-1995 The defense gave its opening statement in the O.J. Simpson trial.
25-01-1998 The Denver Broncos beat the Green Bay Packers 31-24 in Super Bowl XXXII. The Broncos had lost 3 previous Super Bowl appearances with quarterback John Elway.
25-01-1999 At least 1,000 people were killed when an earthquake hit western Columbia. The quake registered 6.0 on the Richter Scale.
25-01-1999 In Louisville, KY, man received the first hand transplant in the United States.
25-01-2001 A minor earthquake hit northeastern Ohio. The quake measured only 4.2 on the Richter Scale.
26-01-1500 Vicente Yáńez Pinzón discovered Brazil.
26-01-1736 Stanislaus I formally abdicated as King of Poland.
26-01-1784 In a letter to his daughter, Benjamin Franklin expressed unhappiness over the eagle as the symbol of America. He wanted the symbol to be the turkey.
26-01-1788 The first European settlers in Australia, led by Captain Arthur Phillip, landed in what became known as Sydney. The group had first settled at Botany Bay eight days before. This day is celebrated as Australia Day.
26-01-1802 The U.S. Congress passed an act calling for a library to be established within the U.S. Capitol.
26-01-1827 Peru seceded from Colombia in protest against Simón Bolívar's alleged tyranny.
26-01-1837 Michigan became the 26th state to join the United States.
26-01-1841 Britain formally occupied Hong Kong, which the Chinese had ceded to the British.
26-01-1861 In the U.S., Louisiana seceded from the Union.
26-01-1870 The state of Virgina rejoined the Union.
26-01-1875 George F. Green patented the electric dental drill for sawing, filing, dressing and polishing teeth.
26-01-1905 The Cullinan diamond, weighing 114 lbs, was found by Captain Wells at the Premier Mine, near Pretoria, South Africa.
26-01-1911 Inventor Glenn H. Curtiss flew the first successful seaplane.
26-01-1934 The Apollo Theatre opened in New York City.
26-01-1939 In the Spanish Civil War, Franco's forces, with Italian aid, took Barcelona.
26-01-1942 The first American expeditionary force to go to Europe during World War II went ashore in Northern Ireland.
26-01-1947 "The Greatest Story Ever Told" was first heard on ABC radio.
26-01-1950 India officially proclaimed itself a republic as Rajendra Prasad took the oath of office as president.
26-01-1950 The American Associated Insurance Companies, of St. Louis, MO, issued the first baby sitter’s insurance policy.
26-01-1959 "Alcoa Presents" debuted on ABC-TV. The show would later be renamed "One Step Beyond".
26-01-1961 U.S. President John F. Kennedy appointed Dr. Janet G. Travell as the first woman to be the "personal physician to the President".
26-01-1962 The U.S. launched Ranger 3 to land scientific instruments on the moon. The probe missed its target by about 22,000 miles.
26-01-1965 Hindi was made the official language of India.
26-01-1972 In Hermsdorf, Czechoslovakia, a JAT Yugoslav Airlines flight crashed after the detonation of a bomb in the forward cargo hold killing 27 people. The bomb was believed to have been placed on the plane by a Croatian extremist group. Vesna Vulovic, a stewardess, survived after falling 33,000 feet in the tail section. She broke both legs and became paralyzed from the waist down.
26-01-1979 The ‘Gizmo’ guitar synthesizer was first demonstrated.
26-01-1984 CBS television debuted Mickey Spillane's "Mike Hammer."
26-01-1992 Russian president Boris Yeltsin announced that his country would stop targeting U.S. cities with nuclear weapons.
26-01-1993 Former Czechoslovak President Vaclav Havel was elected president of the new Czech Republic.
26-01-1994 In Sydney, Australia, a young man lunged at and fired two blank shots at Britain's Prince Charles.
26-01-1996 U.S. first lady Hillary Rodham Clinton testified before a grand jury concerning the Whitewater probe.
26-01-1998 U.S. President Clinton denied having an affair with a former White House intern, saying "I did not have sexual relations with that woman, Miss Lewinsky."
26-01-1999 Saddam Hussein vowed revenge against the U.S. in response to air-strikes that reportedly killed civilians. The strikes were U.S. planes defending themselves against anti-aircraft fire.
26-01-1999 Gary Busey was released from jail after being arrested the night before for investigation of misdemeanor spousal abuse. Tiana Busey had no visible injuries.
26-01-2001 Near Ciudad Boliva, Venezuela, twenty four people were killed when a 50-year-old DC-3 crashed.
27-01-1606 The trial of Guy Fawkes and his fellow conspirators began. They were executed on January 31.
27-01-1870 Kappa Alpha Theta, the first women’s sorority, was founded at Indiana Asbury University (now DePauw University) in Greencastle, IN.
27-01-1880 Thomas Edison patented the electric incandescent lamp.
27-01-1888 The National Geographic Society was founded in Washington, DC.
27-01-1900 In China, foreign diplomats in Peking, fearing a revolt, demanded that the imperial government discipline the Boxer rebels.
27-01-1926 John Baird, a Scottish inventor, demonstrated a pictorial transmission machine called television.
27-01-1927 United Independent Broadcasters Inc. started a radio network with contracts with 16 stations. The company later became Columbia Broadcasting System (CBS).
27-01-1931 NBC radio debuted "Clara, Lu ’n’ Em" on its Blue network (later, ABC radio).
27-01-1943 During World War II, the first all American air raid against Germany took place when about 50 bombers attacked Wilhlemshaven.
27-01-1944 The Soviet Union announced that the two year German siege of Leningrad had come to an end.
27-01-1945 Soviet troops liberated the Nazi concentration camps Auschwitz and Birkenau in Poland.
27-01-1948 Wire Recording Corporation of America announced the first magnetic tape recorder. The ‘Wireway’ machine with a built-in oscillator sold for $149.50.
27-01-1951 In the U.S., atomic testing in the Nevada desert began as an Air Force plane dropped a one-kiloton bomb on Frenchman Flats.
27-01-1957 The "CBS Radio Workshop" was heard for the first time.
27-01-1967 At Cape Kennedy, FL, astronauts Virgil I. "Gus" Grissom, Edward H. White and Roger B. Chaffee died in a flash fire during a test aboard their Apollo I spacecraft.
27-01-1967 More than 60 nations signed the Outer Space Treaty which banned the orbiting of nuclear weapons and placing weapons on celestial bodies or space stations.
27-01-1973 The Vietnam peace accords were signed in Paris.
27-01-1977 The Vatican reaffirmed the Roman Catholic Church's ban on female priests.
27-01-1981 U.S. President Reagan greeted the 52 former American hostages released by Iran at the White House.
27-01-1984 Carl Lewis beat his own two-year-old record by 9-1/4 inches when he set a new indoor world record with a long-jump mark of 28 feet, 10-1/4 inches.
27-01-1984 Wayne Gretzky set a National Hockey League (NHL) record for consecutive game scoring. He ended the streak at 51 games.
27-01-1985 The Coca-Cola Company, of Atlanta, GA, announced a plan to sell its soft drinks in the Soviet Union.
27-01-1992 Former world boxing champion Mike Tyson went on trial for allegedly raping an 18-year-old contestant in the 1991 Miss Black America Contest.
27-01-1996 Mahamane Ousmane, the first democratically elected president of Niger, was overthrown by a military coup. Colonel Ibrahim Bare Mainassara declared himself head of state.
27-01-1997 It was revealed that French national museums were holding nearly 2,000 works of art stolen from Jews by the Nazis during World War II.
27-01-1998 U.S. First Lady Hillary Rodham Clinton appeared on NBC's "Today" show. She charged that the allegations against her husband were the work of a "vast right-wing conspiracy."
27-01-1999 The U.S. Senate blocked dismissal of the impeachment case against President Clinton and voted for new testimony from Monica Lewinsky and two other witnesses.
27-01-2002 A series of explosions occurred at a military dump in Lagos, Nigeria. More than 1,000 people were killed in the blast and in the attempt to escape.
27-01-2003 Altria Group, Inc. became the name of the parent company of Kraft Foods, Philip Morris USA, Philip Morris International and Philip Morris Capital Corporation.
28-01-1521 The Diet of Worms began, at which Protestant reformer Luther was declared an outlaw by the Roman Catholic church.
28-01-1547 England's King Henry VIII died. He was succeeded by his 9 year-old son, Edward VI.
28-01-1788 The first British penal settlement was founded at Botany Bay.
28-01-1807 London's Pall Mall became the first street lit by gaslight.
28-01-1871 France surrendered in the Franco-Prussian War.
28-01-1878 The first telephone switchboard was installed in New Haven, CT.
28-01-1878 "The Yale News" was published for the first time. It was the first, daily, collegiate newspaper in the U.S.
28-01-1902 The Carnegie Institution was established in Washington, DC. It began with a gift of $10 million from Andrew Carnegie.
28-01-1909 The United States ended direct control over Cuba.
28-01-1915 The Coast Guard was created by an act of the U.S. Congress.
28-01-1916 Louis D. Brandeis was appointed by President Wilson to the U.S. Supreme Court, becoming its first Jewish member.
28-01-1918 The Bolsheviks occupied Helsinki, Finland.
28-01-1922 The National Football League (NFL) franchise in Decatur, IL, transferred to Chicago. The team took the name Chicago Bears.
28-01-1935 Iceland became the first country to introduce legalized abortion.
28-01-1938 The first ski tow started operation in Vermont.
28-01-1945 During World War II, Allied supplies began reaching China over the newly reopened Burma Road.
28-01-1957 The Brooklyn Dodgers announced that circus clown Emmett Kelly had been hired to entertain fans at baseball games.
28-01-1958 Roy Campanella (Brooklyn Dodgers) was seriously injured in an auto accident in New York. He would never return to play again.
28-01-1958 Construction began on first private thorium-uranium nuclear reactor.
28-01-1960 The first photograph bounced off of the moon.
28-01-1965 General Motors reported the biggest profit of any U.S. company in history.
28-01-1973 CBS-TV debuted "Barnaby Jones."
28-01-1980 Six Americans who had fled the U.S. embassy in Tehran, Iran, on November 4, 1979, left Iran using false Canadian diplomatic passports. The Americans had been hidden at the Canadian embassy in Tehran.
28-01-1982 Italian anti-terrorism forces rescued U.S. Brigadier General James L. Dozier. 42 days before he had been kidnapped by the Red Brigades.
28-01-1986 The U.S. space shuttle Challenger exploded just after takeoff. All seven of its crewmembers were killed.
28-01-1994 In Los Angeles, Superior Court Judge Stanley Weisberg declared a mistrial in the case of Lyle Menendez in the murder of his parents. Lyle, and his brother Erik, were both retried later and were found guilty. They were sentenced to life in prison without parole.
28-01-1997 Clive Davis received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
28-01-1998 In Manilla, Philippines, gunmen held at least 400 children and teachers for several hours at an elementary school.
28-01-1999 Ford Motor company announced the purchase of Sweden's Volvo AB for $6.45 billion.
28-01-2002 Toys R Us Inc. announced that it would be closing 27 Toys R Us stores and 37 Kids R Us stores in otder to cut costs and boost operating profits.
29-01-1728 John Gay's The Beggar's Opera was first performed at Lincoln's Inn Fields Theatre, London.
29-01-1802 John Beckley became the first Librarian of Congress.
29-01-1820 Britain's King George III died insane at Windsor Castle.
29-01-1845 Edgar Allan Poe's "The Raven" was published for the first time in the "New York Evening Mirror."
29-01-1848 Greenwich Mean Time was adopted by Scotland.
29-01-1850 Henry Clay introduced in the Senate a compromise bill on slavery that included the admission of California into the Union as a free state.
29-01-1856 Britain's highest military decoration, the Victoria Cross, was founded by Queen Victoria.
29-01-1861 In America, Kansas became the 34th state of the Union.
29-01-1886 The first successful petrol-driven motorcar, built by Karl Benz, was patented.
29-01-1900 The American Baseball League was organized in Philadelphia, PA. It consisted of 8 teams.
29-01-1916 In World War I, Paris was bombed by German zeppelins for the first time.
29-01-1924 R. Taylor patented the ice cream cone rolling machine.
29-01-1936 The first members of major league baseball's Hall of Fame were named in Cooperstown, NY.
29-01-1940 The W. Atlee Burpee Seed Company displayed the first tetraploid flowers at the New York City Flower Show.
29-01-1949 "The Newport News" was commissioned as the first air-conditioned naval ship in Virginia.
29-01-1956 "Indictment" debuted on CBS radio and stayed on the air for three years.
29-01-1958 Paul Newman and Joanne Woodward were married.
29-01-1958 Charles Starkweather was captured by police in Wyoming.
29-01-1963 The first members to the NFL's Hall of Fame were named in Canton, OH.
29-01-1963 Britain was refused entry into the EEC.
29-01-1966 "Sweet Charity" opened at the Palace Theatre in New York City. It ran for 608 performances.
29-01-1979 U.S. President Carter formally welcomed Chinese Vice Premier Deng Xiaoping to the White House. The visit followed the establishment of diplomatic relations.
29-01-1985 The Dow Jones industrial average peaked at 1,292.62.
29-01-1987 "Physician’s Weekly" announced that the smile on the face of Leonardo DeVinci's Mona Lisa was caused by a "...facial paralysis resulting from a swollen nerve behind the ear."
29-01-1990 Joseph Hazelwood, the former skipper of the Exxon Valdez, went on trial in Anchorage, AK, on charges that stemmed from America's worst oil spill. Hazelwood was later acquitted of all the major charges and was convicted of a misdemeanor.
29-01-1995 The San Francisco 49ers became the first team in National Football League (NFL) history to win five Super Bowl titles. The 49ers defeated the San Diego Chargers 49-26.
29-01-1996 French President Jacques Chirac announced the "definitive end" to nuclear testing.
29-01-1996 La Fenice, the 204 year old opera house in Venice, was destroyed by fire. Arson was suspected.
29-01-1997 America Online agreed to give refunds to frustrated customers under threat of lawsuits across the country. Customers were unable to log on after AOL offered a flat $19.95-a-month rate.
29-01-1998 A bomb exploded at an abortion clinic in Birmingham, AL, killing an off-duty policeman and severely wounding a nurse. Eric Rudolph was charged with this bombing and three other attacks in Atlanta.
29-01-1999 Paris prosecutors announced the end of the investigation into the accident that killed Britain's Princess Diana.
29-01-1999 The U.S. Senate delivered subpoenas for Monica Lewinsky and two presidential advisers for private, videotaped testimony in the impeachment trial.
29-01-2001 In Indonesia, thousands of student protesters stormed the parliament property and demanded that President Abdurrahman Wahid quit due to his alleged involvement in two corruption scandals. Wahid announced that he would not resign.
30-01-1649 England's King Charles I was beheaded.
30-01-1790 The first purpose-built lifeboat was launched on the River Tyne.
30-01-1798 The first brawl in the U.S. House of Representatives took place. Congressmen Matthew Lyon and Roger Griswold fought on the House floor.
30-01-1847 The town of Yerba Buena was renamed San Francisco.
30-01-1862 The U.S. Navy's first ironclad warship, the "Monitor", was launched.
30-01-1889 Rudolph, crown prince of Austria, and his 17-year-old mistress, Baroness Marie Vetsera, were found shot in his hunting lodge at Mayerling, near Vienna.
30-01-1894 C.B. King received a patent for the pneumatic hammer.
30-01-1900 The British fighting the Boers in South Africa ask for a larger army.
30-01-1910 Work began on the first board-track automobile speedway. The track was built in Playa del Ray, CA.
30-01-1911 The first airplane rescue at sea was made by the destroyer "Terry." Pilot James McCurdy was forced to land in the ocean about 10 miles from Havana, Cuba.
30-01-1933 "The Lone Ranger" was heard on radio for the first time. The program ran for 2,956 episodes and ended in 1955.
30-01-1933 Adolf Hitler was named the German Chancellor.
30-01-1948 Indian political and spiritual leader Mahatma Gandhi was murdered by a Hindu extremist.
30-01-1950 NBC-TV debuted "Robert Montgomery Presents." The show lasted for seven seasons.
30-01-1958 Yves Saint Laurent, at age 22, held his first major fashion show in Paris.
30-01-1958 The first two-way moving sidewalk was put in service at Love Field in Dallas, TX. The length of the walkway through the airport was 1,435 feet.
30-01-1960 The women’s singles U.S. figure skating championship was won by Carol Heiss.
30-01-1962 Two members of the "Flying Wallendas" high-wire act were killed when their seven-person pyramid collapsed during a performance in Detroit, MI.
30-01-1964 January 30 - The U.S. launched Ranger 6. The unmanned spacecraft carried television cameras and was intentionally crash-landed on the moon. The cameras did not return any pictures to Earth.
30-01-1968 The Tet Offensive began as Communist forces launched surprise attacks against South Vietnamese provincial capitals.
30-01-1972 In Northern Ireland, British soldiers shot and killed thirteen Roman Catholic civil rights marchers. The day is known as "Bloody Sunday."
30-01-1979 The civilian government of Iran announced it had decided to allow Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini to return. He had been living in exile in France.
30-01-1989 The U.S. embassy in Kabul, Afghanistan was closed.
30-01-1994 Peter Leko became the world's youngest-ever grand master in chess.
30-01-1995 The U.N. Security Council authorized the deployment of a 6,000-member U.N. peace-keeping contingent to assume security responsibilities in Haiti from U.S. forces.
30-01-1995 Researchers from the U.S. National Institutes of Health announced that clinical trials had demonstrated the effectiveness of the first preventative treatment for sickle cell anaemia.
30-01-1996 Gino Gallagher, the reputed leader of the Irish National Liberation Army, was shot and killed as he queued for his unemployment benefit.
30-01-1997 A New Jersey judge ruled that the unborn child of a female prisoner must have legal representation. He denied the prisoner bail reduction to enable her to leave the jail and obtain an abortion.
30-01-2002 Slobodan Milosevic accused the U.N. war crimes tribunal of an "evil and hostile attack" against him. Milosevic was defending his actions during the Balkan wars.
30-01-2002 Japan's last coal mine was closed. The closures were due to high production costs and cheap imports.
30-01-2002 In Los Angeles, 15 students and 3 adults were injured when they were hit by a car.
31-01-1606 Guy Fawkes was executed after being convicted for his role in the "Gunpowder Plot" against the English Parliament and King James I.
31-01-1747 The first clinic specializing in the treatment of venereal diseases was opened at London Dock Hospital.
31-01-1858 The Great Eastern, the five-funnelled steamship designed by Brunel, was launched at Millwall.
31-01-1865 In America, General Robert E. Lee was named general-in-chief of the Confederate armies.
31-01-1865 The 13th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified by the U.S. Congress. It was ratified on December 6, 1865. The amendment abolished slavery in the United States.
31-01-1876 All Native American Indians were ordered to move into reservations.
31-01-1917 Germany announced its policy of unrestricted submarine warfare.
31-01-1929 The USSR exiled Leon Trotsky. He found asylum in Mexico.
31-01-1930 U.S. Navy Lt. Ralph S. Barnaby became the first glider pilot to have his craft released from a dirigible, a large blimp, at Lakehurst, NJ.
31-01-1934 Jim Londos defeated Joe Savoldi in a one-fall match in Chicago, IL. The crowd of 20,000 was one of the largest crowds to see a wrestling match.
31-01-1936 The radio show "The Green Hornet" debuted.
31-01-1940 The first Social Security check was issued by the U.S. Government.
31-01-1944 During World War II, U.S. forces invaded Kwajalein Atoll and other areas of the Japanese-held Marshall Islands.
31-01-1945 Private Eddie Slovik became the only U.S. soldier since the U.S. Civil War to be executed for desertion.
31-01-1946 A new constitution in Yugoslavia created six constituent republics (Serbia, Montenegro, Croatia, Slovenia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Macedonia) subordinated to a central authority, on the model of the USSR.
31-01-1949 The first TV daytime soap opera was broadcast from NBC's station in Chicago, IL. It was "These Are My Children."
31-01-1950 U.S. President Truman announced that he had ordered development of the hydrogen bomb.
31-01-1958 Explorer I was put into orbit around the earth. It was the first U.S. earth satellite.
31-01-1960 Julie Andrews, Henry Fonda, Rex Harrison and Jackie Gleason, appeared in a two-hour TV special entitled "The Fabulous ’50s".
31-01-1971 Astronauts Alan B. Shepard Jr., Edgar D. Mitchell and Stuart A. Roosa blasted off aboard Apollo 14 on a mission to the moon.
31-01-1971 Telephone service between East and West Berlin was re-established after 19 years.
31-01-1982 Sandy Duncan gave her final performance as "Peter Pan" in Los Angeles, CA. She completed 956 performances without missing a show.
31-01-1983 The wearing of seat belts in cars became compulsory in Britain.
31-01-1983 JCPenney announced plans to spend in excess of $1 billion over the next five years to modernize stores and to accelerate a repositioning program.
31-01-1985 The final Jeep rolled off the assembly line at the AMC plant in Toledo, OH.
31-01-1990 McDonald's Corp. opened its first fast-food restaurant in Moscow, Russia.
31-01-1995 U.S. President Clinton invoked presidential emergency authority to provide a $20 billion loan to Mexico to stabilize its economy.
31-01-1996 In Columbo, Sri Lanka, a truck was rammed into the gates of the Central Bank. The truck filled with explosives killed at least 86 and injured 1,400.
31-01-2000 John Rocker (Atlanta Braves) was suspended from major league baseball for disparaging foreigners, homosexuals and minorities in an interview published by Sports Illustrated.
31-01-2000 An Alaska Airlines jet crashed into the ocean off Southern California. All 88 people on board were killed.
31-01-2001 A Scottish court in the Netherlands convicted one Libyan and acquitted a second in the bombing of Pan Am Flight 103 over Lockerbie, Scotland, that occurred in 1988.
31-01-2005 Keanu Reeves received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
01-07-1996 Vespasian, a Roman Army leader, was hailed as a Roman Emperor by the Egyptian legions.
01-07-1543 England and Scotland signed the peace of Greenwich.
01-07-1596 An English fleet under the Earl of Essex, Lord Howard of Effingham and Francis Vere captured and sacked Cadiz, Spain.
01-07-1690 The French defeated the forces of the Grand Alliance at Fleurus in the Netherlands.
01-07-1798 Napoleon Bonaparte took Alexandria, Egypt.
01-07-1847 The U.S. Post Office issued its first adhesive stamps.
01-07-1862 The U.S. Congress established the Bureau of Internal Revenue.
01-07-1863 During the U.S. Civil War, the first day's fighting at Gettysburg began.
01-07-1867 Canada became an independent dominion.
01-07-1874 The Philadelphia Zoological Society zoo opened as the first zoo in the United States.
01-07-1876 Montenegro declared war on the Turks.
01-07-1893 The first bicycle race track in America to be made out of wood was opened in San Francisco, CA.
01-07-1897 Three years after the first issue of "Billboard Advertising" was published, the publication was renamed, "The Billboard".
01-07-1898 During the Spanish-American War, Theodore Roosevelt and his "Rough Riders" waged a victorious assault on San Juan Hill in Cuba.
01-07-1905 The USDA Forest Service was created within the Department of Agriculture. The agency was given the mission to sustain healthy, diverse, and productive forests and grasslands for present and future generations.
01-07-1909 Thomas Edison began commercially manufacturing his new "A" type alkaline storage batteries.
01-07-1916 The massive Allied offensive known as the Battle of the Somme began in France. The battle was the first to use tanks.
01-07-1934 The Federal Communications Commission replaced the Federal Radio Commission as the regulator of broadcasting in the United States.
01-07-1940 In Washington, the Tacoma Narrows Bridge was opened to traffic. The bridge collapsed during a wind storm on November 7, 1940.
01-07-1941 Bulova Watch Company sponsored the first TV commercial in New York City, NY.
01-07-1942 German troops captured Sevestpol, Crimea, in the Soviet Union.
01-07-1943 The U.S. Government began automatically withholding federal income tax from paychecks.
01-07-1945 New York established the New York State Commission Against Discrimination to prevent discrimination in employment because of race, creed or natural origin. It was the first such agency in the U.S.
01-07-1946 U.S. President Harry Truman signed Public Law 476 that incorporated the Civil Air Patrol as a benevolent, nonprofit organization. The Civil Air Patrol was created on December 1, 1941.
01-07-1946 The U.S. exploded a 20-kiloton atomic bomb near Bikini Atoll in the Pacific Ocean.
01-07-1948 The price of a subway ride in New York City was increased from 5 cents to 10.
01-07-1950 American ground troops arrived in South Korea to stem the tide of the advancing North Korean army.
01-07-1951 Bob Feller set a major league baseball record as he pitched his third no-hitter for the Cleveland Indians.
01-07-1960 Somalia gained its independence from Britain through the unification of Somaliland with Italian Somalia.
01-07-1961 British troops landed in Kuwait to aid against Iraqi threats.
01-07-1961 The first community air-raid shelter was built. The shelter in Boise, ID had a capacity of 1,000 people and family memberships sold for $100.
01-07-1963 The U.S. postmaster introduced the five-digit ZIP (Zoning Improvement Plan) code.
01-07-1966 The Medicare federal insurance program went into effect.
01-07-1968 The Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty was signed by 60 countries. It limited the spreading of nuclear material for military purposes. On May 11, 1995, the treaty was extended indefinitely.
01-07-1969 Britain's Prince Charles was invested as the Prince of Wales.
01-07-1974 Isavel Peron became the president of Argentina upon the death of her husband, Juan.
01-07-1979 Susan B. Anthony was commemorated on a U.S. coin, the Susan B. Anthony dollar.
01-07-1979 Sony introduced the Walkman.
01-07-1980 "O Canada" was proclaimed the national anthem of Canada.
01-07-1980 U.S. President Jimmy Carter signed legislation that provided for 2 acres of land near the Lincoln Memorial for the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
01-07-1981 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that candidates for federal office had an "affirmative right" to go on national television.
01-07-1985 Robin Yount (Milwaukee Brewers) got the 1,800th hit of his career.
01-07-1987 John Kevin Hill, at age 11, became the youngest to fly across the U.S. when he landed at National Airport in Washington, DC.
01-07-1989 The Montreal Protocol, an international treaty, went into effect. It limited the production of ozone-destroying chemicals.
01-07-1991 Court TV began airing.
01-07-1991 The Warsaw Pact dissolved.
01-07-1994 Yasser Arafat of the Palestinian Liberation Organization visited the Gaza Strip.
01-07-1996 Margaux Hemingway was found dead in her apartment. It was concluded that she had committed suicide.
01-07-1997 The sovereignty over Hong Kong was transferred from Great Britain to China. Britain had controlled Hong Kong as a colony for 156 years.
01-07-1999 The U.S. Justice Department released new regulations that granted the attorney general sole power to appoint and oversee special counsels. The 1978 independent-counsel statute expired on June 30.
01-07-2003 In Hong Kong, thousands of protesters marched to show their opposition to anti-subversion legislation.
02-07-1298 An army under Albert of Austria defeated and killed Adolf of Nassua near Worms, Germany.
02-07-1566 French astrologer, physician and prophet Nostradamus died.
02-07-1625 The Spanish army took Breda, Spain, after nearly a year of siege.
02-07-1644 Lord Cromwell crushed the Royalists at the Battle of Marston Moor near York, England.
02-07-1747 Marshall Saxe led the French forces to victory over an Anglo-Dutch force under the Duke of Cumberland at the Battle of Lauffeld.
02-07-1776 Richard Henry Lee’s resolution that the American colonies "are, and of right ought to be, free and independent States" was adopted by the Continental Congress.
02-07-1850 Prussia agreed to pull out of Schlewig and Holstein, Germany.
02-07-1850 B.J. Lane patented the gas mask.
02-07-1857 New York City’s first elevated railroad officially opened for business.
02-07-1858 Czar Alexander II freed the serfs working on imperial lands.
02-07-1881 Charles J. Guiteau fatally wounded U.S. President James A. Garfield in Washington, DC.
02-07-1890 The U.S. Congress passed the Sherman Antitrust Act.
02-07-1926 The U.S. Congress established the Army Air Corps.
02-07-1937 American aviation pioneer Amelia Earhart disappeared in the Central Pacific during an attempt to fly around the world at the equator.
02-07-1939 At Mount Rushmore, Theodore Roosevelt's face was dedicated.
02-07-1944 American bombers, as part of Operation Gardening, dropped land mines, leaflets and bombs on German-occupied Budapest.
02-07-1947 An object crashed near Roswell, NM. The U.S. Army Air Force insisted it was a weather balloon, but eyewitness accounts led to speculation that it might have been an alien spacecraft.
02-07-1961 Ernest Hemingway shot himself to death at his home in Ketchum, ID.
02-07-1964 U.S. President Johnson signed the "Civil Rights Act of 1964" into law. The act made it illegal in the U.S. to discriminate against others because of their race.
02-07-1967 The U.S. Marine Corps launched Operation Buffalo in response to the North Vietnamese Army's efforts to seize the Marine base at Con Thien.
02-07-1976 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled the death penalty was not inherently cruel or unusual.
02-07-1976 North Vietnam and South Vietnam were reunited.
02-07-1980 U.S. President Jimmy Carter reinstated draft registration for males 18 years of age.
02-07-1985 General Motors announced that it was installing electronic road maps as an option in some of its higher-priced cars.
02-07-1994 Colombian soccer player Andres Escobar was shot to death in Medellin. 10 days earlier he had accidentally scored a goal against his own team in World Cup competition.
02-07-1995 "Forbes" magazine reported that Microsoft's chairman, Bill Gates, was the worth $12.9 billion, making him the world's richest man. In 1999, he was worth about $77 billion.
02-07-1998 Cable News Network (CNN) retracted a story that alleged that U.S. commandos had used nerve gas to kill American defectors during the Vietnam War.
02-07-2000 In Mexico, Vicente Fox Quesada of the National Action Party (PAN) defeated Francisco Labastida Ochoa of the Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI) in the presidential election. The PRI had controlled the presidency in Mexico since the party was founded in 1929.
03-07-1608 The city of Quebec was founded by Samuel de Champlain.
03-07-1775 U.S. Gen. George Washington took command of the Continental Army at Cambridge, MA.
03-07-1790 In Paris, the marquis of Condorcet proposed granting civil rights to women.
03-07-1844 Ambassador Caleb Cushing successfully negotiated a commercial treaty with China that opened five Chinese ports to U.S. merchants and protected the rights of American citizens in China.
03-07-1863 The U.S. Civil War Battle of Gettysburg, PA, ended after three days. It was a major victory for the North as Confederate troops retreated.
03-07-1871 The Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad Company introduced the first narrow-gauge locomotive. It was called the "Montezuma."
03-07-1878 John Wise flew the first dirigible in Lancaster, PA.
03-07-1880 "Science" began publication. Thomas Edison had provided the principle funding.
03-07-1890 Idaho became the 43rd state to join the United States of America.
03-07-1898 During the Spanish American War, a fleet of Spanish ships in Cuba's Santiago Harbor attempted to run a blockade of U.S. naval forces. Nearly all of the Spanish ships were destroyed in the battle that followed.
03-07-1901 The Wild Bunch, led by Butch Cassidy, committed its last American robbery near Wagner, MT. They took $65,000 from a Great Northern train.
03-07-1903 The first cable across the Pacific Ocean was spliced between Honolulu, Midway, Guam and Manila.
03-07-1912 Rube Marquand of the New York Giants set a baseball pitching record when earned his 19th consecutive win.
03-07-1922 "Fruit Garden and Home" magazine was introduced. It was later renamed "Better Homes and Gardens."
03-07-1924 Clarence Birdseye founded the General Seafood Corp.
03-07-1930 The U.S. Congress created the U.S. Veterans Administration.
03-07-1934 U.S. Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) made its first payment to Lydia Losiger.
03-07-1937 Del Mar race track opened in Del Mar, CA.
03-07-1939 Chic Young’s comic strip character, "Blondie" was first heard on CBS radio.
03-07-1940 Bud Abbott and Lou Costello debuted on NBC radio.
03-07-1944 The U.S. First Army opened a general offensive to break out of the hedgerow area of Normandy, France.
03-07-1944 During World War II, Soviet forces recaptured Minsk.
03-07-1945 U.S. troops landed at Balikpapan and take Sepinggan airfield on Borneo in the Pacific.
03-07-1945 The first civilian passenger car built since February 1942 was driven off the assembly line at the Ford Motor Company plant in Detroit, MI. Production had been diverted due to World War II.
03-07-1950 U.S. carrier-based planes attacked airfields in the Pyongyang-Chinnampo area of North Korea in the first air-strike of the Korean War.
03-07-1954 Food rationing ended in Great Britain almost nine years after the end of World War II.
03-07-1962 Jackie Robinson became the first African American to be inducted into the National Baseball Hall of Fame.
03-07-1974 The Threshold Test Ban Treaty was signed, prohibiting underground nuclear weapons tests with yields greater than 150 kilotons.
03-07-1976 103 hostages were rescued by an Israeli commando unit at the raid on Entebbe airport in Uganda. The hostages had been taken from an Air France jetliner.
03-07-1981 The Associated Press ran its first story about two rare illnesses afflicting homosexual men. One of the diseases was later named AIDS.
03-07-1986 U.S. President Reagan presided over a ceremony in New York Harbor that saw the relighting of the renovated Statue of Liberty.
03-07-1986 Mikhail Baryshnikov became a U.S. citizen at Ellis Island, New York Harbor.
03-07-1988 The USS Vincennes shot down an Iran Air jetliner over the Persian Gulf, killing all 290 people aboard. The jetliner was misidentified as an Iranian F-14 fighter.
03-07-1991 U.S. President George Bush formally inaugurated the Mount Rushmore National Memorial in South Dakota.
03-07-1997 U.S. President Clinton made his first formal response to the charges of sexual harassment from Paula Jones. He denied all the charges and asked that the judge dismiss the case.
04-07-1712 Twelve slaves were executed for starting a slave uprising in New York that killed nine whites.
04-07-1776 The amended Declaration of Independence, prepared by Thomas Jefferson, was approved and signed by John Hancock, the President of the Continental Congress in America.
04-07-1802 The U.S. Military Academy officially opened at West Point, NY.
04-07-1803 The Louisiana Purchase was announced in newspapers. The property was purchased, by the U.S. from France, was for $15 million (or 3 cents an acre). The "Corps of Discovery," led by Meriwether Lewis and William Clark, began the exploration of the territory on May 14, 1804.
04-07-1817 Construction began on the Erie Canal, to connect Lake Erie and the Hudson River.
04-07-1845 American writer Henry David Thoreau began his two-year experiment in simple living at Walden Pond, near Concord, MA.
04-07-1848 In Washington, DC, the cornerstone for the Washington Monument was laid.
04-07-1855 The first edition of "Leaves of Grass," by Walt Whitman, was published in Brooklyn, NY.
04-07-1863 The Confederate town of Vicksburg, MS, surrendered to General Ulysses S. Grant.
04-07-1881 Tuskegee Institute opened in Alabama.
04-07-1884 Bullfighting was introduced in the U.S. in Dodge City, KS.
04-07-1886 The first rodeo in America was held at Prescott, AZ.
04-07-1892 The first double-decked street car service was inaugurated in San Diego, CA.
04-07-1894 After seizing power, Judge Stanford B. Dole declared Hawaii a republic.
04-07-1901 William H. Taft became the American governor of the Philippines.
04-07-1910 Race riots broke out all over the United States after African-American Jack Johnson knocked out Jim Jeffries in a heavyweight boxing match.
04-07-1934 Boxer Joe Louis won his first professional fight.
04-07-1934 At Mount Rushmore, George Washington's face was dedicated.
04-07-1939 Lou Gehrig retired from major league baseball.
04-07-1946 The Philippines achieved full independence for the first time in over four hundred years.
04-07-1955 The first king cobra snakes born in captivity in the U.S. hatched at the Bronx Zoo in New York City.
04-07-1957 The U.S. Postal Service issued the 4˘ Flag stamp.
04-07-1959 The 49-star U.S. flag was debuted.
04-07-1960 The 50-star U.S. flag made its debut in Philadelphia, PA.
04-07-1966 U.S. President Johnson signed the Freedom of Information Act, which went into effect the following year.
04-07-1976 The U.S. celebrated its Bicentennial.
04-07-1987 Klaus Barbie, the former Gestapo chief known as the "Butcher of Lyon," was convicted by a French court of crimes against humanity and sentenced to life in prison.
04-07-1997 The Mars Pathfinder, an unmanned spacecraft, landed on Mars. A rover named Sojourner was deployed to gather data about the surface of the planet.
04-07-1997 Ferry service between Manhattan and Staten Island was made free of charge. Previously, the charge had ranged from 5 cents to 50 cents.
04-07-2004 In New York, the cornerstone of the Freedom Tower was laid on the former World Trade Center site.
04-07-2005 NASA's Deep Impact spacecraft took pictures as a space probe smashed into the Tempel 1 comet. The mission was aimed at learning more about comets that formed from the leftover buidling blocks of the solar system. The Deep Impact mission launched on January 12, 2005.
05-07-1806 A Spanish army repelled the British during their attempt to retake Buenos Aires, Argentina.
05-07-1811 Venezuela became the first South American country to declare independence from Spain.
05-07-1814 U.S. troops under Jacob Brown defeated a superior British force at Chippewa, Canada.
05-07-1830 France occupied the North African city of Algiers.
05-07-1832 The German government began curtailing freedom of the press after German Democrats advocate a revolt against Austrian rule.
05-07-1839 British naval forces bombarded Dingai on Zhoushan Island in China and then occupied it.
05-07-1863 U.S. Federal troops occupied Vicksburg, MS, and distributed supplies to the citizens.
05-07-1865 William Booth founded the Salvation Army in London.
05-07-1892 Andrew Beard was issued a patent for the rotary engine.
05-07-1916 Adelina and August Van Buren started on the first successful transcontinental motorcycle tour to be attempted by two women. They started in New York City and arrived in San Diego, CA, on September 12, 1916.
05-07-1935 "Hawaii Call" was broadcast for the first time.
05-07-1935 U.S. President Roosevelt signed the National Labor Relations Act into law. The act authorized labor to organize for the purpose of collective bargaining.
05-07-1940 During World War II, Britain and the Vichy government in France broke diplomatic relations.
05-07-1941 German troops reached the Dnieper River in the Soviet Union.
05-07-1943 The battle of Kursk began as German tanks attack the Soviet salient. It was the largest tank battle in history.
05-07-1946 The bikini bathing suit, created by Louis Reard, made its debut during a fashion show at the Molitor Pool in Paris. Micheline Bernardini wore the two-piece outfit.
05-07-1947 Larry Doby signed a contract with the Cleveland Indians, becoming the first black player in the American League.
05-07-1948 Britain's National Health Service Act went into effect, providing government-financed medical and dental care.
05-07-1950 U.S. forces engaged the North Koreans for the first time at Osan, South Korea.
05-07-1951 Dr. William Shockley announced that he had invented the junction transistor.
05-07-1962 Algeria became independent after 132 years of French rule.
05-07-1975 Arthur Ashe became the first black man to win a Wimbledon singles title when he defeated Jimmy Connors.
05-07-1984 The U.S. Supreme Court weakened the 70-year-old "exclusionary rule," deciding that evidence seized with defective court warrants could be used against defendants in criminal trials.
05-07-1989 Former U.S. National Security Council aide Oliver North received a $150,000 fine and a suspended prison term for his part in the Iran-Contra affair. The convictions were later overturned.
05-07-1991 Regulators shut down the Pakistani-managed Bank of Credit and Commerce International (BCCI) in eight countries. The charge was fraud, drug money laundering and illegal infiltration into the U.S. banking system.
05-07-1995 The U.S. Justice Department decided not to take antitrust action against Ticketmaster.
05-07-1998 Japan joined U.S. and Russia in space exploration with the launching of the Planet-B probe to Mars.
05-07-2000 Jordanian security agents shot and killed a Syrian hijacker after he threw a grenade that exploded and wounded 15 passengers aboard a Royal Jordanian airliner.
05-07-2000 10 Bengal tigers, including 7 rare white tigers, died at the Nandankanan Zoo in India. The tigers died of trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness).
05-07-2000 Euan Blair, the oldest son of British prime minister Tony Blair, was arrested after police found him drunk and lying on the ground in London's Leicester Square.
05-07-2002 In Algeria, 35 people were killed in violent attacks on the day that the country celebrated its 40 years of independence from France.
05-07-2002 Former Nazi SS officer Friedrich Engel was convicted of 59 counts of murder stemming from massacre of Italian resistance fighters on May 19, 1944.
06-07-1483 King Richard III of England was crowned.
06-07-1535 Sir Thomas More was executed in England for treason.
06-07-1699 Captain William Kidd, the pirate, was captured in Boston, MA, and deported back to England.
06-07-1777 British forces captured Fort Ticonderoga during the American Revolution.
06-07-1854 In Jackson, MI, the Republican Party held its first convention.
06-07-1858 Lyman Blake patented the shoe manufacturing machine.
06-07-1885 Louis Pasteur successfully tested his anti-rabies vaccine. The child used in the test later became the director of the Pasteur Institute.
06-07-1893 In northwest Iowa 71 people were killed by a tornado.
06-07-1905 Fingerprints were exchanged for the first time between officials in Europe and the U.S. The person in question was John Walker.
06-07-1917 During World War I, Arab forces led by T.E. Lawrence captured the port of Aqaba from the Turks.
06-07-1919 A British dirigible landed in New York at Roosevelt Field. It completed the first crossing of the Atlantic Ocean by an airship.
06-07-1923 The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics was established.
06-07-1928 "The Lights of New York" was previewed in New York's Strand Theatre. It was the first all-talking movie.
06-07-1932 The postage rate for first class mail in the U.S. went from 2-cents to 3-cents.
06-07-1933 The first All-Star baseball game was held in Chicago. The American League beat the National League 4-2.
06-07-1942 Diarist Anne Frank and her family took refuge from the Nazis in Amsterdam.
06-07-1944 A fire broke out in the main tent of the Ringling Brother, Barnum and Bailey Circus. 169 people died.
06-07-1945 U.S. President Truman signed an order creating the Medal of Freedom.
06-07-1945 Nicaragua became the first nation to formally accept the United Nations Charter.
06-07-1947 "Candid Microphone" began airing on ABC radio.
06-07-1948 Frieda Hennok became the first woman to serve as the commissioner of the Federal Communications Commission.
06-07-1957 Althea Gibson won the Wimbledon women’s singles tennis title. She was the first black athlete to win the event.
06-07-1966 Malawi became a republic within the Commonwealth with Dr. Hastings Banda as its first president.
06-07-1967 The Biafran War erupted. The war lasted two-and-a-half years. About 600,000 people died.
06-07-1981 The Dupont Company announced an agreement to purchase Conoco, Inc. (Continental Oil Co.) for $7 billion. At the time it was the largest merger in corporate history.
06-07-1983 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that retirement plans could not pay women smaller monthly payments solely because of their gender.
06-07-1983 Fred Lynn of the California Angels hit the first grand slam in an All-Star game. The American League defeated the National League 13-3.
06-07-1985 Martina Navratilova won her 4th consecutive Wimbledon singles title.
06-07-1985 The submarine Nautilus arrived in Groton, Connecticut. The vessel had been towed from Mare Island Naval Shipyard.
06-07-1987 Sikh extremists made their first of three attacks over a two day period. The gunmen attacked a bus loaded with Hindu passengers. Over the two day period a total of 72 people were killed by the extremists.
06-07-1988 167 North Sea oil workers were killed by explosions and fires that destroyed the Piper Alpha drilling platform.
06-07-1988 Several popular beaches were closed in New York City due to medical waste and other debris began washing up on the seashores.
06-07-1989 The U.S. Army destroyed its last Pershing 1-A missiles at an ammunition plant in Karnack, TX. The dismantling was under the terms of the 1987 Intermediate-range Nuclear Forces Treaty.
06-07-1994 On Storm King Mountain, in Colorado, 14 firefighters were killed while fighting a several-day-old fire.
06-07-1995 In Los Angeles, the prosecution rested at the O.J. Simpson murder trial.
06-07-1996 Steffi Graf won her seventh Wimbledon title.
06-07-1997 The Mars Pathfinder released Sojourner, a robot rover on the surface of Mars. The spacecraft landed on the red planet on July 4th.
06-07-1997 In Cambodia, Second Prime Minister Hun Sen ousted First Prime Minister Norodom Ranariddh and claimed to have the capital under his control.
06-07-1998 Protestants rioted in many parts of Northern Ireland after British authorities blocked an Orange Order march in Portadown.
06-07-2000 In Orlando, FL, the body of Cory Erving was found in his vehicle in a pond near his families home. Julius "Dr. J" Erving had reported his son missing on June 4, 2000.
06-07-2000 A jury awarded former NHL player Tony Twist $24 million for the unauthorized use of his name in the comic book Spawn and the HBO cartoon series. Co-defendant HBO settled with Twist out of court for an undisclosed amount.
07-07-1754 Kings College opened in New York City. It was renamed Columbia College 30 years later.
07-07-1846 U.S. annexation of California was proclaimed at Monterey after the surrender of a Mexican garrison.
07-07-1862 The first railroad post office was tested on the Hannibal and St. Joseph Railroad in Missouri.
07-07-1865 Four people were hanged in Washington, DC, after being convicted of conspiring with John Wilkes Booth to assassinate U.S. President Lincoln.
07-07-1885 G. Moore Peters patented the cartridge-loading machine.
07-07-1898 The United States annexed Hawaii.
07-07-1917 Aleksandr Kerensky formed a provisional government in Russia.
07-07-1920 A device known as the radio compass was used for the first time on a U.S. Navy airplane near Norfolk, VA.
07-07-1930 Construction began on Boulder Dam, later Hoover Dam, on the Colorado River.
07-07-1937 Japanese forces invaded China.
07-07-1946 Mother Frances Xavier Cabrini was canonized as the first American saint.
07-07-1949 "Dragnet" was first heard on NBC radio.
07-07-1950 The UN Security Council authorized military aid for South Korea.
07-07-1969 Canada's House of Commons gave final approval to a measure that made the French language equal to English throughout the national government.
07-07-1981 U.S. President Reagan announced he was nominating Arizona Judge Sandra Day O'Connor to become the first female justice on the U.S. Supreme Court.
07-07-1983 Eleven-year-old Samantha Smith of Manchester, Maine, left for a visit to the Soviet Union at the personal invitation of Soviet leader Yuri V. Andropov.
07-07-1987 Public testimony at the Iran-Contra hearing began.
07-07-1998 A jury in Santa Monica, CA, convicted Mikail Markhasev of murdering Ennis Cosby, Bill Cosby's only son, during a roadside robbery.
07-07-1999 In Sierra Leone, President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah and rebel leader Foday Sankoh signed a pact to end the nation's civil war.
07-07-2000 Cisco Systems Inc. announced that it would buy Netiverse Inc. for $210 million in stock. It was the 13th time Cisco had purchased a company in 2000.
07-07-2000 Amazon.com announced that they had sold almost 400,000 copies of "Harry Potter and The Goblet of Fire," making it the biggest selling book in e-tailing history.
07-07-2003 In Liberia, a team of U.S. military experts arrived at the U.S. embassy compound to assess whether to deploy troops as part of a peacekeeping force in the country.
07-07-2005 In London, at least 66 people were killed and at least 700 were injured when several bombs were set off in subway cars and double-decker buses.
08-07-1099 Christian soldiers on the First Crusade march around Jerusalem.
08-07-1608 The first French settlement at Quebec was established by Samuel de Champlain.
08-07-1663 King Charles II of England granted a charter to Rhode Island.
08-07-1693 Uniforms for police in New York City were authorized.
