08-07-1709 Peter the Great defeated Charles XII at Poltava, in the Ukraine, The Swedish empire was effectively ended.
08-07-1755 Britain broke off diplomatic relations with France as their disputes in the New World intensified.
08-07-1776 Col. John Nixon gave the first public reading of the U.S. Declaration of Independence to a crowd at Independence Square in Philadelphia.
08-07-1794 French troops captured Brussels, Belgium.
08-07-1795 Kent County Free School changed its name to Washington College. It was the first college to be named after U.S. President George Washington. The school was established by an act of the Maryland Assembly in 1723.
08-07-1815 Louis XVIII returned to Paris after the defeat of Napoleon.
08-07-1865 C.E. Barnes patented the machine gun.
08-07-1879 The first ship to use electric lights departed from San Francisco, CA.
08-07-1881 Edward Berner, druggist in Two Rivers, WI, poured chocolate syrup on ice cream in a dish. To this time chocolate syrup had only been used for making ice-cream sodas.
08-07-1889 The Wall Street Journal was first published.
08-07-1889 John L. Sullivan defeated Jake Kilrain, in the last championship bare-knuckle fight. The fight lasted 75 rounds.
08-07-1907 Florenz Ziegfeld staged his first "Follies" on the roof of the New York Theater in New York City.
08-07-1919 U.S. President Wilson returned from the Versailles Peace Conference in France.
08-07-1947 Demolition work began in New York City for the new permanent headquarters of the United Nations.
08-07-1950 General Douglas MacArthur was named commander-in-chief of United Nations forces in Korea.
08-07-1953 Notre Dame announced that the next five years of its football games would be shown in theatres over closed circuit TV.
08-07-1960 The Soviet Union charged Gary Powers with espionage. He was shot down in a U-2 spy plane.
08-07-1963 All Cuban-owned assets in the United States were frozen.
08-07-1969 The U.S. Patent Office issued a patent for the game "Twister."
08-07-1970 The San Francisco Giant’s Jim Ray Hart became the first National League player in 59 seasons to collect six runs batted (RBI) during a single inning.
08-07-1986 Kurt Waldheim was inaugurated as president of Austria despite controversy over his alleged ties to Nazi war crimes.
08-07-1993 Charles Keating, chief of Lincoln Savings & Loan Association, was sentenced to 12 years and seven months in prison for violating California security and fraud laws.
08-07-1997 The Mayo Clinic and the U.S. government warned that the diet-drug combination known as "fen-phen" could cause serious heart and lung damage.
08-07-1997 NATO invited Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic to join the alliance in 1999.
08-07-2000 J.K. Rowling's "Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire" was released in the U.S. It was the fourth Harry Potter book.
09-07-0118 Hadrian, Rome's new emperor, made his entry into the city.
09-07-0455 Avitus, the Roman military commander in Gaul, became Emperor of the West.
09-07-1540 England's King Henry VIII had his 6-month-old marriage to his fourth wife, Anne of Cleves, annulled.
09-07-1553 Maurice of Saxony was mortally wounded at Sievershausen, Germany, while defeating Albert of Brandenburg-Kulmbach.
09-07-1609 In a letter to the crown, the emperor Rudolf II granted Bohemia freedom of worship.
09-07-1755 General Edward Braddock was killed when French and Indian troops ambushed his force of British regulars and colonial militia.
09-07-1776 The American Declaration of Independence was read aloud to Gen. George Washington's troops in New York.
09-07-1789 In Versailles, the French National Assembly declared itself the Constituent Assembly and began to prepare a French constitution.
09-07-1790 The Swedish navy captured one third of the Russian fleet at the naval battle of Svensksund in the Baltic Sea.
09-07-1792 S.L. Mitchell of Columbia College in New York City became the first Professor of Agriculture.
09-07-1808 The leather-splitting machine was patented by Samuel Parker.
09-07-1816 Argentina declared independence from Spain.
09-07-1847 A 10-hour work day was established for workers in the state of New Hampshire.
09-07-1850 U.S. President Zachary Taylor died in office at the age of 55. He was succeeded by Millard Fillmore. Taylor had only served 16 months.
09-07-1868 The 14th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. The amendment was designed to grant citizenship to and protect the civil liberties of recently freed slaves. It did this by prohibiting states from denying or abridging the privileges or immunities of citizens of the United States, depriving any person of his life, liberty, or property without due process of law, or denying to any person within their jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws.
09-07-1872 The doughnut cutter was patented by John F. Blondel.
09-07-1877 Alexander Graham Bell, Gardiner Greene Hubbard, Thomas Sanders and Thomas Watson formed the Bell Telephone Company.
09-07-1878 The corncob pipe was patented by Henry Tibbe.
09-07-1900 The Commonwealth of Australia was established by an act of the British Parliament, uniting the separate colonies under a federal government.
09-07-1910 W.R. Brookins became the first to fly an airplane a mile in the air.
09-07-1918 101 people were killed when an inbound local train collided with an outbound express in Nashville, TN.
09-07-1922 Johnny Weissmuller became the first person to swim the 100 meters freestyle in less than a minute.
09-07-1935 Norman Bright ran the two mile event in the record time of 9 minutes, 13.2 seconds at a meet in New York City.
09-07-1943 American and British forces made an amphibious landing on Sicily.
09-07-1947 The engagement of Britain's Princess Elizabeth to Lt. Philip Mountbatten was announced.
09-07-1951 U.S. President Truman asked Congress to formally end the state of war between the United States and Germany.
09-07-1953 New York Airways began the first commuter passenger service by helicopter.
09-07-1968 The first All-Star baseball game to be played indoors took place at the Astrodome in Houston, TX.
09-07-1971 The United States turned over complete responsibility of the Demilitarized Zone to South Vietnamese units.
09-07-1982 A Pan Am Boeing 727 crashed in Kenner, LA, all 146 people aboard and eight people on the ground were killed.
09-07-1985 Herschel Walker of the New Jersey Generals was named the Most Valuable Player in the United States Football League (USFL).
09-07-1985 Joe Namath, signed a five-year pact with ABC-TV to provide commentary for "Monday Night Football".
09-07-1997 Mike Tyson was banned from the boxing ring and fined $3 million for biting the ear of opponent Evander Holyfield.
09-07-2005 Danny Way, a daredevil skateboarder, rolled down a large ramp and jumped across the Great Wall of China. He was the first person to clear the wall without motorized aid.
10-07-1609 The Catholic states in Germany set up a league under the leadership of Maximillian of Bavaria.
10-07-1679 The British crown claimed New Hampshire as a royal colony.
10-07-1747 Persian ruler Nadir Shah was assassinated at Fathabad in Persia.
10-07-1776 The statue of King George III was pulled down in New York City.
10-07-1778 In support of the American Revolution, Louis XVI declared war on England.
10-07-1821 U.S. troops took possession of Florida. The territory was sold by Spain.
10-07-1832 U.S. President Andrew Jackson vetoed legislation to re-charter the Second Bank of the United States.
10-07-1866 Edison P. Clark patented his indelible pencil.
10-07-1890 Wyoming became the 44th state to join the United States.
10-07-1900 ‘His Master’s Voice’, was registered with the U.S. Patent Office. The logo of the Victor Recording Company, and later, RCA Victor, shows the dog, Nipper, looking into the horn of a gramophone machine.
10-07-1913 The highest temperature ever recorded in the U.S. was 134 degrees in Death Valley, CA.
10-07-1919 The Treaty of Versailles was hand delivered to the U.S. Senate by President Wilson.
10-07-1925 The official news agency of the Soviet Union, TASS, was established.
10-07-1928 George Eastman first demonstrated color motion pictures.
10-07-1929 The U.S. government began issuing paper money in the small size.
10-07-1938 Howard Hughes completed a 91 hour flight around the world.
10-07-1940 The 114-day Battle of Britain began during World War II.
10-07-1943 Arthur Ashe, the first African-American inducted into the International Tennis Hall of Fame, was born. He had won 33 career titles.
10-07-1949 The first practical rectangular television was presented. The picture tube measured 12 by 16 and sold for $12.
10-07-1951 Armistice talks aimed at ending the Korean conflict began at Kaesong.
10-07-1951 Sugar Ray Robinson was defeated for only the second time in 133 fights as Randy Turpin took the middleweight crown.
10-07-1953 American forces withdraw from Pork Chop Hill in Korea after heavy fighting.
10-07-1962 The Telstar Communications satellite was launched. The satellite relayed TV and telephone signals between Europe and the U.S.
10-07-1962 Fred Baldasare swam the English Channel underwater. It was a 42 miles and took 18 hours.
10-07-1969 The National League was divided up into two baseball divisions.
10-07-1973 Britain granted the Bahamas their independence after three centuries of British colonial rule.
10-07-1984 Dwight ‘Doc’ Gooden, of the New York Mets, became the youngest player to appear in an All-Star Game as a pitcher. He was 19 years, 7 months, and 24 days old.
10-07-1985 Coca-Cola resumed selling the old formula of Coke, it was renamed "Coca-Cola Classic." It was also announced that they would continue to sell "New" Coke.
10-07-1989 Mel Blanc, the "man of a thousand voices," died at age 81. He was known for such cartoon characters as Daffy Duck, Bugs Bunny and Porky Pig.
10-07-1990 Mikhail Gorbachev won re-election as the leader of the Soviet Communist Party.
10-07-1991 Boris Yeltsin took the oath of office as the first elected president of the Russian republic.
10-07-1991 U.S. President Bush lifted economic sanctions against South Africa, citing its "profound transformation" toward racial equality.
10-07-1992 In Miami, a federal judge sentenced former Panamanian leader Manuel Noriega to 40 years in prison. He was convicted of drug and racketeering charges.
10-07-1992 In New York, a jury found Pan Am responsible for allowing a terrorist to destroy Flight 103 in 1988, killing 270 people.
10-07-1993 Kenyan runner Yobes Ondieki became the first man to run 10,000 meters in less than 27 minutes.
10-07-1997 NATO forces captured one Serb war crimes suspect and killed another in a warning to Bosnia's most wanted.
10-07-1997 Scientists in London said DNA from a Neanderthal skeleton supported a theory that all humanity descended from an "African Eve" 100,000 to 200,000 years ago.
10-07-1998 The World Bank approved a $700 million loan to Thailand.
10-07-1998 The U.S. military delivered the remains of Air Force 1st Lt. Michael Blassie to his family in St. Louis. He had been placed in Arlington Cemetery's Tomb of the Unknown in 1984. His identity had been confirmed with DNA tests.
10-07-1998 The Diocese of Dallas agreed to pay $23.4 million to nine former altar boys who said they had been molested by a priest.
10-07-1999 The heads of six African nations that had troops in the Democratic Republic of the Congo signed a cease-fire agreement that would end the civil war in that nation.
10-07-2000 A pipeline explosion in southern Nigeria killed about 250 villagers.
10-07-2000 A woman was sentenced to nine years in prison for allowing three men to have sex with her 13-year-old daughter. The men involved were sentenced from six to seven years in prison.
10-07-2000 Justin Pierce commited suicide the day before the premiere of his last movie "Pigeonholed."
10-07-2000 Jean-Claude Van Damme was given three years probation and fined $1,200 for drunk driving and driving without a license. Van Damme had been arrested after he crashed his Mercedes-Benz into a restaurant on September 23, 1999.
10-07-2002 Peter Paul Rubens' painting "The Massacre of the Innocents" sold for $76.2 million at Sotheby's.
11-07-1346 Charles IV of Luxembourg was elected Holy Roman Emperor in Germany.
11-07-1533 Henry VIII, who divorced his wife and became head of the church of England, was excommunicated from the Catholic Church by Pope Clement VII.
11-07-1708 The French were defeated at Oudenarde, Malplaquet, in the Netherlands by the Duke of Marlborough and Eugene of Savoy.
11-07-1742 A papal decree was issued condemning the disciplining actions of the Jesuits in China.
11-07-1786 Morocco agreed to stop attacking American ships in the Mediterranean for a payment of $10,000.
11-07-1798 The U.S. Marine Corps was formally re-established by "An Act for Establishing a Marine Corps" passed by the U.S. Congress. The act also created the U.S. Marine Band. The Marines were first commissioned by the Continental Congress on November 10, 1775.
11-07-1804 The United States' first secretary of the treasury, Alexander Hamilton, was killed by Vice President Aaron Burr in a duel.
11-07-1864 In the U.S., Confederate forces led by Gen. Jubal Early began an invasion of Washington, DC. They turned back the next day.
11-07-1914 Babe Ruth debuted in the major leagues with the Boston Red Sox.
11-07-1918 Enrico Caruso recorded "Over There" written by George M. Cohan.
11-07-1934 The first appointments to the newly created Federal Communications Commission were made.
11-07-1934 U.S. President Franklin Delano Roosevelt became the first American chief executive to travel through the Panama Canal while in office.
11-07-1955 The U.S. Air Force Academy was dedicated in Colorado Springs, CO, at Lowry Air Base.
11-07-1960 In Honolulu, HI, the first tournament held outside the continental U.S., sanctioned by the U.S. Golf Association, began.
11-07-1962 The first transatlantic TV transmission was sent through the Telstar I satellite.
11-07-1972 U.S. forces broke the 95-day siege at An Loc in Vietnam.
11-07-1977 The Medal of Freedom was awarded posthumously to Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. in a White House ceremony.
11-07-1978 216 people were killed when a tanker truck overfilled with propylene gas exploded on a coastal highway south of Tarragona, Spain.
11-07-1979 The abandoned U.S. space station Skylab returned to Earth. It burned up in the atmosphere and showered debris over the Indian Ocean and Australia.
11-07-1980 Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini ordered the release of hostage Richard Queen due to illness. Queen was flown to Zurich, Switzerland. Queen had been taken hostage with 62 other Americans at the U.S. embassy in Tehran on November 4, 1979.
11-07-1985 Dr. H. Harlan Stone announced that he had used zippers for stitches on 28 patients. The zippers were used when he thought he may have to re-operate.
11-07-1985 Nolan Ryan of the Houston Astros became the first major league pitcher to earn 4,000 strikeouts in a career.
11-07-1987 Bo Jackson signed a contract to play football for the L.A. Raiders for 5 years. He was also continued to play baseball for the Kansas City Royals.
11-07-1994 Shawn Eckardt was sentenced in Portland, OR, to 18 months in prison for his role in the attack on figure skater Nancy Kerrigan.
11-07-1995 Full diplomatic relations were established between the United States and Vietnam.
11-07-1998 U.S. Air Force Lt. Michael Blassie, a casualty of the Vietnam War, was laid to rest near his Missouri home. He had been positively identified from his remains that had been enshrined in the Tomb of the Unknowns in Arlington, VA.
11-07-1999 A U.S. Air Force jet flew over the Antarctic and dropped off emergency medical supplies for Dr. Jerri Nelson after she had discovered a lump in her breast. Nelso was at the Amundsen-Scott South Pole Research Center.
11-07-2000 Comedian Jimmy Walker was cited for failing to maintain his lane after his car collided with another vehicle.
11-07-2000 Arkansas Judge Leon Johnson announced that he would preside over the disbarment case against U.S. President Clinton. Several other judges had stepped aside from the case citing the appearance of conflict of interest.
11-07-2000 The video "Jaws," the Anniversary Collector's Edition, was released.
11-07-2000 Liam Neeson broke his pelvis after hitting a deer with his Harley Davidson motorcycle.
12-07-1096 Crusaders under Peter the Hermit reached Sofia, Bulgaria. There they met their Byzantine escort, which brought them safely the rest of the way to Constantinople. by August 1.
12-07-1543 England's King Henry VIII married his sixth and last wife, Catherine Parr.
12-07-1690 Protestant forces led by William of Orange defeated the Roman Catholic army of James II.
12-07-1691 William III defeated the allied Irish and French armies at the Battle of Aughrim, Ireland.
12-07-1790 The French Assembly approved a Civil Constitution providing for the election of priests and bishops.
12-07-1806 The Confederation of the Rhine was established in Germany.
12-07-1862 The U.S. Congress authorized the Medal of Honor.
12-07-1864 U.S. President Abraham Lincoln witnessed the battle where Union forces repelled Jubal Early's army on the outskirts of Washington, DC.
12-07-1912 The first foreign-made film to premiere in America, "Queen Elizabeth", was shown.
12-07-1931 A major league baseball record for doubles was set as the St. Louis Cardinals and the Chicago Cubs combined for a total of 23.
12-07-1933 A minimum wage of 40 cents an hour was established in the U.S.
12-07-1941 Moscow was bombed by the German Luftwaffe for the first time.
12-07-1946 "The Adventures of Sam Spade" was heard on ABC radio for the first time.
12-07-1954 U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower proposed a highway modernization program, with costs to be shared by federal and state governments.
12-07-1954 The Major League Baseball Players Association was organized in Cleveland, OH.
12-07-1957 The U.S. surgeon general, Leroy E. Burney, reported that there was a direct link between smoking and lung cancer.
12-07-1960 The first Etch-A-Sketch went on sale.
12-07-1974 John Ehrlichman, a former aide to U.S. President Nixon, and three others were convicted of conspiring to violate the civil rights of Daniel Ellsberg's former psychiatrist.
12-07-1982 "E.T.: The Extra-Terrestrial" broke all box-office records by surpassing the $100-million mark of ticket sales in the first 31 days of its opening.
12-07-1982 The last of the distinctive-looking Checker taxicabs rolled off the assembly line in Kalamazoo, MI.
12-07-1984 Democratic presidential candidate Walter F. Mondale named U.S. Rep. Geraldine A. Ferraro of New York to be his running mate. Ferraro was the first woman to run for vice president on a major party ticket.
12-07-1990 Russian republic president Boris N. Yeltsin announced his resignation from the the Soviet Communist Party.
12-07-1993 196 people were killed when an earthquake with a magnitude of 7.8 on the Richter Scale struck northern Japan.
12-07-1998 In Ballymoney, Northern Ireland, three young brothers were burned to death in an arson attack while they slept.
12-07-1998 1.7 billion people watched soccer's World Cup finals between France and Brazil. France won 3-0.
12-07-1999 Walt Disney Co. announced that it was merging all of its Internet operations together with Infoseek into Go.com.
12-07-2000 Russia launched the Zvezda after two years of delays. The module was built to be the living quarters for the International Space Station (ISS.)
12-07-2000 A car bomb exploded in central Madrid injuring nine people. The attack was blamed on the Basque separatist group ETA.
12-07-2000 The movie "X-Men" premiered in New York.
13-07-1099 The Crusaders launched their final assault on Muslims in Jerusalem.
13-07-1534 The Ottoman armies captured Tabriz in northwestern Persia.
13-07-1558 Led by the court of Egmont, the Spanish army defeated the French at Gravelines, France.
13-07-1585 A group of 108 English colonists, led by Sir Richard Grenville, reached Roanoke Island, NC.
13-07-1643 In England, the Roundheads, led by Sir William Waller, were defeated by royalist troops under Lord Wilmot in the Battle of Roundway Down.
13-07-1754 At the beginning of the French and Indian War, George Washington surrendered the small, circular Fort Necessity in southwestern Pennsylvania to the French.
13-07-1787 The U.S. Congress, under the Articles of Confederation, enacted the Northwest Ordinance, which established the rules for governing the Northwest Territory, for admitting new states to the Union and limiting the expansion of slavery.
13-07-1793 French revolutionary writer Jean Paul Marat was stabbed to death in his bath by Charlotte Corday. She was executed four days later.
13-07-1812 The first pawnbroking ordinance was passed in New York City.
13-07-1832 Henry Schoolcraft discovered the source of the Mississippi River in Minnesota.
13-07-1835 John Ruggles received patent #1 from the U.S. Patent Office for a traction wheel used in locomotive steam engines. All 9,957 previous patents were not numbered.
13-07-1863 Opponents of the Civil War draft began three days of rioting in New York City, which resulted in more than 1,000 casualties.
13-07-1875 David Brown patented the first cash-carrier system.
13-07-1878 The Congress of Berlin divided the Balkans among European powers.
13-07-1896 Philadelphia’s Ed Delahanty became the second major league player to hit four home runs in a single game.
13-07-1931 A major German financial institution, Danabank, failed. This led to the closing of all banks in Germany until August 5.
13-07-1941 Britain and the Soviet Union signed a mutual aid pact, that provided the means for Britain to send war material to the Soviet Union.
13-07-1954 In Geneva, the United States, Great Britain and France reached an accord on Indochina which divided Vietnam into two countries, North and South, along the 17th parallel.
13-07-1967 Race-related rioting broke out in Newark, NJ. At the end of four days of violence 27 people had been killed.
13-07-1971 The Army of Morocco executed ten leaders accused of leading a revolt.
13-07-1972 Carroll Rosenbloom (owner of the Baltimore Colts) and Robert Irsay (owner of the Los Angeles Rams) traded teams.
13-07-1973 David Bedford set a new world record in the 10,000-meter race in London. His time was 27 minutes, 31 seconds.
13-07-1978 Lee Iacocca was fired as president of Ford Motor Co. by chairman Henry Ford II.
13-07-1979 A 45-hour siege began at the Egyptian Embassy in Ankara, Turkey. Four Palestinian guerrillas killed two security men and seized 20 hostages.
13-07-1982 The All-Star Game was played outside the United States for the first time. They played in Montreal, Canada.
13-07-1984 In Arkansas, Terry Wallis was injured in a car accident and was left comatose. He came out of the coma in June of 2003.
13-07-1994 Jeff Gillooly, Tonya Harding's ex-husband, was sentenced in Portland, OR, to two years in prison for his role in the attack on Nancy Kerrigan.
13-07-1998 "Image of an Assassination" went on sale. The video documentary is of Abraham Zapruder's home video of U.S. President Kennedy's assassination in Dallas.
13-07-1998 Four young cousins in Gallup, NM, died after becoming trapped in a car trunk.
13-07-1998 RealNetworks Inc. rolled out a test version of RealSystem G2. G2 is a streaming video and audio delivery system.
13-07-2000 The United States and Vietnam singed a major trade agreement. The pact still needed to be approved by the U.S. Congress.
13-07-2000 Sprint Corp. and WorldCom canceled their planned merger due to opposition by regulators in the United States and Europe.
14-07-1223 In France, Louis VIII succeeded his father, Philip Augustus.
14-07-1430 Joan of Arc, taken prisoner by the Burgundians in May, was handed over to Pierre Cauchon, the bishop of Beauvais.
14-07-1456 Hungarians defeated the Ottomans at the Battle of Belgrade.
14-07-1536 France and Portugal signed the naval treaty of Lyons, which aligned them against Spain.
14-07-1789 French Revolution began with Parisians stormed the Bastille prison and released the seven prisoners inside.
14-07-1798 The U.S. Congress passed the Sedition Act. The act made it a federal crime to write, publish, or utter false or malicious statements about the U.S. government.
14-07-1868 Alvin J. Fellows patented the tape measure.
14-07-1891 The primacy of Thomas Edison's lamp patents was upheld in the court decision Electric Light Company vs. U.S. Electric Lighting Company.
14-07-1900 European Allies retook Tientsin, China, from the rebelling Boxers.
14-07-1908 "The Adventures of Dolly" opened at the Union Square Theatre in New York City.
14-07-1911 Harry N. Atwood landed an airplane on the lawn of the White House to accept an award from U.S. President William Taft.
14-07-1914 Robert H. Goddard patented liquid rocket-fuel.
14-07-1933 All German political parties except the Nazi Party were outlawed.
14-07-1940 A force of German Ju-88 bombers attacked Suez, Egypt, from bases in Crete.
14-07-1941 Vichy French Foreign Legionaries signed an armistice in Damascus, which allowed them to join the Free French Foreign Legion.
14-07-1945 American battleships and cruisers bombarded the Japanese home islands for the first time.
14-07-1946 Dr. Benjamin Spock’s "The Common Sense Book of Baby and Child Care" was first published.
14-07-1951 The first sports event to be shown in color, on CBS-TV, was the Molly Pitcher Handicap at Oceanport, NJ.
14-07-1951 The George Washington Carver National Monument in Joplin, MO, became the first national park to honor an African American.
14-07-1958 The army of Iraq overthrew the monarchy.
14-07-1965 The American space probe Mariner 4 flew by Mars, and sent back photographs of the planet.
14-07-1966 In a Chicago dormitory, Richard Speck murdered eight student nurses.
14-07-1967 Eddie Mathews of the Houston Astros hit his 500th career home run.
14-07-1968 Hank Aaron, while with the Atlanta Braves, hit his 500th career home run.
14-07-1981 The All-Star Game was postponed because of a 33-day-old baseball players strike. The game was held on August 9.
14-07-1998 Los Angeles sued 15 tobacco companies for $2.5 billion over the dangers of secondhand smoke.
14-07-2001 Beijing was awarded the 2008 Olympics. It was the first time that the China had been awarded the games.
14-07-2003 Jerry Springer officially filed papers to run for the U.S. Senate from Ohio.
15-07-1099 Jerusalem fell to the Crusaders.
15-07-1410 Poles and Lithuanians defeated the Teutonic knights at Tannenburg, Prussia.
15-07-1685 The Duke of Monmouth was executed in Tower Hill in England, after his army was defeated at Sedgemore.
15-07-1788 Louis XVI jailed 12 deputies who protested new judicial reforms.
15-07-1789 The electors of Paris set up a "Commune" to live without the authority of the government.
15-07-1806 Lieutenant Zebulon Pike began his western expedition from Fort Belle Fountaine, near St. Louis, MS.
15-07-1813 Napoleon Bonaparte's representatives met with the Allies in Prague to discuss peace terms.
15-07-1834 Lord Napier of England arrived in Macao, China as the first chief superintendent of trade.
15-07-1857 British women and children were murdered in the second Cawnpore Massacre during the Indian Mutiny.
15-07-1863 Confederate raider Bill Anderson and his Bushwhackers attacked Huntsville, MO, where they stole $45,000 from the local bank.
15-07-1870 Georgia became the last of the Confederate states to be readmitted to the Union.
15-07-1876 George Washington Bradley of St. Louis pitched the first no-hitter in baseball in a 2-0 win over Hartford.
15-07-1888 "Printers’ Ink" was first sold.
15-07-1895 Ex-prime minister of Bulgaria, Stephen Stambulov, was murdered by Macedonian rebels.
15-07-1901 Over 74,000 Pittsburgh steel workers went on strike.
15-07-1904 The first Buddhist temple in the U.S. was established in Los Angeles, CA.
15-07-1916 In Seattle, WA, Pacific Aero Products was incorporated by William Boeing. The company was later renamed Boeing Co.
15-07-1918 The Second Battle of the Marne began during World War I.
15-07-1922 The duck-billed platypus arrived in America, direct from Australia. It was exhibited at the Bronx Zoo in New York City.
15-07-1940 Robert Wadlow died at the age of 22. At that time he was 8 feet, 11-1/10 inches tall and weighed 439 pounds.
15-07-1942 The first supply flight from India to China over the 'Hump' was carried to help China's war effort.
15-07-1958 Five thousand U.S. Marines landed in Beirut, Lebanon, to protect the pro-Western government. The troops withdrew October 25, 1958.
15-07-1965 The spacecraft Mariner IV sent back the first close-up pictures of the planet Mars.
15-07-1965 Joan Rivers and Edgar Rosenberg were married.
15-07-1968 ABC-TV premiered "One Life to Live".
15-07-1968 Commercial air travel began between the U.S. and the U.S.S.R., when the first plane, a Soviet Aeroflot jet, landed at Kennedy International Airport in New York.
15-07-1971 U.S. President Nixon announced he would visit the People's Republic of China to seek a "normalization of relations."
15-07-1973 Nolan Ryan of the California Angels became the first pitcher in two decades to win two no-hitters in a season.
15-07-1976 A 36-hour kidnap ordeal began for 26 schoolchildren and their bus driver when they were abducted by three gunmen near Chowchilla, CA. All of the captives escaped unharmed.
15-07-1981 Steven Ford, son of former President Gerald R. Ford, appeared in a seduction scene of "The Young and the Restless" on CBS-TV. Ford played the part of Andy.
15-07-1985 Baseball players voted to strike on August 6th if no contract was reached with baseball owners. The strike turned out to be just a one-day interruption.
15-07-1997 Gianni Versace was shot to death by Andrew Phillip Cunanan outside his home in Miami, FL. Cunanan was found dead eight days later.
15-07-1999 Harold Greene received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
15-07-2002 John Walker Lindh plead guilty to two felonies. The crimes were supplying services to Afghanistan's former Taliban government and for carrying explosives during the commission of a felony. Lindh agreed to spend 10 years in prison for each of the charges.
16-07-1765 Prime Minister of England Lord Greenville resigned and was replaced by Lord Rockingham.
16-07-1774 Russia and the Ottoman Empire signed the treaty of Kuchuk-Kainardji, ending their six-year war.
16-07-1779 American troops under General Anthony Wayne capture Stony Point, NY.
16-07-1790 The District of Columbia, or Washington, DC, was established as the permanent seat of the United States Government.
16-07-1791 Louis XVI was suspended from office until he agreed to ratify the constitution.
16-07-1845 The New York Yacht Club hosted the first American boating regatta.
16-07-1862 Two Union soldiers and their servant ransacked a house and raped a slave in Sperryville, VA.
16-07-1862 David G. Farragut became the first rear admiral in the U.S. Navy.
16-07-1875 The new French constitution was finalized.
16-07-1912 Bradley A. Fiske patented the airplane torpedo.
16-07-1918 Czar Nicholas II and his family were executed by Bolsheviks at Ekaterinburg, Russia.
16-07-1926 The first underwater color photographs appeared in "National Geographic" magazine. The pictures had been taken near the Florida Keys.
16-07-1935 Oklahoma City became the first city in the U.S. to make use of parking meters.
16-07-1940 Adolf Hitler ordered the preparations to begin on the invasion of England, known as Operation Sea Lion.
16-07-1942 French police officers rounded up 13,000 Jews and held them in the Winter Velodrome. The round-up was part of an agreement between Pierre Laval and the Nazis. Germany had agreed to not deport French Jews if France arrested foreign Jews.
16-07-1944 Soviet troops occupied Vilna, Lithuania, in their drive toward Germany.
16-07-1945 The United States detonated the first atomic bomb in a test at Alamogordo, NM.
16-07-1950 The largest crowd in sporting history was 199,854. They watched the Uruguay defeat Brazil in the World Cup soccer finals in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
16-07-1951 J.D. Salinger's novel, "The Catcher in the Rye," was first published.
16-07-1957 Marine Major John Glenn set a transcontinental speed record when he flew a jet from California to New York in 3 hours, 23 minutes and 8 seconds.
16-07-1964 Little League Baseball Incorporated was granted a Federal Charter unanimously by the United States Senate and House of Representatives.
16-07-1969 Apollo 11 blasted off from Cape Kennedy, FL, and began the first manned mission to land on the moon.
16-07-1970 The Pittsburgh Pirates played their first game at Three Rivers Stadium.
16-07-1973 Alexander P. Butterfield informed the Senate committee investigating the Watergate affair of the existence of recorded tapes.
16-07-1979 Saddam Hussein became president of Iraq after forcing Hasan al-Bakr to resign.
16-07-1981 After 23 years with the name Datsun, executives of Nissan changed the name of their cars to Nissan.
16-07-1985 The All-Star Game, televised on NBC-TV, was the first program broadcast in stereo by a TV network.
16-07-1990 An earthquake measuring 7.7 on the Richter Scale devastated the Philippines, killing over 1600 people.
16-07-1999 The plane of John F. Kennedy Jr. crashed off the coast of Martha's Vineyard, MA. His wife, Carolyn Bessette Kennedy, and her sister, Lauren Bessette, were also on board the plane. The body of John Kennedy was found on July 21, 1999.
16-07-2004 Martha Stewart was sentenced to five months in prison for lying about a stock sale. She was also ordered to spend five months confined to her home and fined $30,000. She was allowed to remain free pending her appeal.
16-07-2005 J.K. Rowling's book "Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince" was released. It was the sixth in the Harry Potter series. The book sold 6.9 million copies on its first day of release.
17-07-1212 The Moslems were crushed in the Spanish crusade.
17-07-1453 France defeated England at Castillon, France, which ended the 100 Years' War.
17-07-1762 Peter III of Russia was murdered. Catherine II the Great took the throne.
17-07-1785 France limited the importation of goods from Britain.
17-07-1815 Napoleon Bonaparte surrendered to the British at Rochefort, France.
17-07-1821 Spain ceded Florida to the U.S.
17-07-1862 National cemeteries were authorized by the U.S. government.
17-07-1866 Authorization was given to build a tunnel beneath the Chicago River. The three-year project cost $512,709.
17-07-1867 Harvard School of Dental Medicine was established in Boston, MA. It was the first dental school in the U.S.
17-07-1898 U.S. troops under General William R. Shafter took Santiago de Cuba during the Spanish-American War.
17-07-1917 The British royal family adopted the Windsor name.
17-07-1920 Sinclair Lewis finished his novel "Main Street".
17-07-1941 The longest hitting streak in baseball history ended when the Cleveland Indians pitchers held New York Yankee Joe DiMaggio hitless for the first time in 57 games.
17-07-1941 Brigadier General Soervell directed Architect G. Edwin Bergstrom to have basic plans and architectural perspectives for an office building that could house 40,000 War Department employees on his desk by the following Monday morning. The building became known as the Pentagon.
17-07-1944 232 people were killed when 2 ammunition ships exploded in Port Chicago, CA.
17-07-1945 U.S. President Truman, Soviet leader Josef Stalin and British Prime Minister Winston S. Churchill began meeting at Potsdam in the final Allied summit of World War II. During the meeting Stalin made the comment that "Hitler had escaped."
17-07-1946 Chinese communists opened a drive against the Nationalist army on the Yangtze River.
17-07-1950 The television show "The Colgate Comedy Hour" debuted featuring Dean Martin and Jerry Lewis.
17-07-1954 The Brooklyn Dodgers made history as the first team with a majority of black players.
17-07-1955 Disneyland opened in Anaheim, CA.
17-07-1960 Francis Gary Powers pled guilty to spying charges in a Moscow court after his U-2 spy plane was shot down over the Soviet Union.
17-07-1966 Ho Chi Minh ordered a partial mobilization of North Vietnam forces to defend against American air strikes.
17-07-1975 An Apollo spaceship docked with a Soyuz spacecraft in orbit. It was the first link up between the U.S. and Soviet Union.
17-07-1979 Nicaraguan President Anastasio Somoza resigned and fled to Miami in exile.
17-07-1981 Two skywalks suspended from the ceiling over the atrium lobby at the Hyatt Regency Hotel in Kansas City, MO, collapsed. 114 people were killed. Five years later two design engineers were convicted for their negligence.
17-07-1986 The largest bankruptcy filing in U.S. history took place when LTV Corporation asked for court protection from more than 20,000 creditors. LTV Corp. had debts in excess of $4 billion.
17-07-1987 Lieutenant Colonel Oliver North and rear Admiral John Poindexter begin testifying to Congress at the "Iran-Contra" hearings.
17-07-1995 The Nasdaq composite stock index rose above 1,000 for the first time.
17-07-1996 230 people were killed when TWA Flight 800 exploded and crashed off Long Island, NY.
17-07-1997 After 117 years, the Woolworth Corp. closed its last 400 stores.
17-07-1998 Nicholas II, the last of Romanov czars, was buried in Russia 80 years after he and his family were executed by the Bolsheviks.
17-07-1998 An entire village was swept away in Papua New Guinea by a 23-foot wave that was triggered by an undersea earthquake. Eight days later the government reported that 1,500 people were dead, 2,000 were missing and thousands were homeless.
17-07-1998 Biologists reported that they had deciphered the genome (genetic map) of the syphilis bacterium.
18-07-1964 The Great Fire of Rome began.
18-07-1536 The authority of the pope was declared void in England.
18-07-1743 "The New York Weekly Journal" published the first half-page newspaper ad.
18-07-1789 Robespierre, a deputy from Arras, France, decided to back the French Revolution.
18-07-1812 Great Britain signed the Treaty of Orebro, making peace with Russia and Sweden.
18-07-1830 Uruguay adopted a liberal constitution.
18-07-1872 The Ballot Act was passed in Great Britain, providing for secret election ballots.
18-07-1914 Six planes of the U.S. Army helped to form an aviation division called the Signal Corps.
18-07-1927 Ty Cobb set a major league baseball record by getting his 4,000th career hit. He hit 4,191 before he retired in 1928.
18-07-1932 The U.S. and Canada signed a treaty to develop the St. Lawrence Seaway.
18-07-1935 Ethiopian King Haile Selassie urged his countrymen to fight to the last man against the invading Italian army.
18-07-1936 The first Oscar Meyer Wienermobile rolled out of General Body Company’s factory in Chicago, IL.
18-07-1936 The Spanish Civil War began as Gen. Francisco Franco led an uprising of army troops based in Spanish North Africa.
18-07-1936 "The Columbia Workshop" debuted on CBS radio.
18-07-1942 The German Me-262, the first jet-propelled aircraft to fly in combat, made its first flight.
18-07-1944 U.S. troops captured Saint-Lo, France, ending the battle of the hedgerows.
18-07-1944 Hideki Tojo was removed as Japanese premier and war minister due to setbacks suffered by his country in World War II.
18-07-1947 U.S. President Truman signed the Presidential Succession Act, which placed the Speaker of the House and the Senate President Pro Tempore next in the line of succession after the vice president.
18-07-1964 Pete Rose of the Cincinnati Reds hit the only grand slam home run of his career.
18-07-1970 Ron Hunt of the San Francisco Giants was hit by a pitch for the 119th time in his career.
18-07-1971 New Zealand and Australia announced they would pull their troops out of Vietnam.
18-07-1984 A gunman opened fire at a McDonald's fast-food restaurant in San Ysidro, CA. He killed 21 people before being shot dead by police.
18-07-1985 Jack Nicklaus II, at age 23 years old, made his playing debut on the pro golf tour at the Quad Cities Open in Coal Valley, IL.
18-07-1994 In Buenos Aires, a massive car bomb killed 96 people belonging to Argentinean Jewish organizations.
18-07-2000 It was announced that Christopher Reeve would direct and serve as executive producer on the TV movie "Rescuing Jeffrey."
18-07-2000 Kareem Abdul-Jabbar was arrested on suspicion of driving under the influence of marijuana. He was stopped for speeding and then failed to pass a sobriety test. Abdul-Jabbar was the leading scorer in National Basketball Association (NBA) history at the time.
18-07-2001 A train derailed, involving 60 cars, in a Baltimore train tunnel. The fire that resulted lasted for six days and virtually closed down downtown Baltimore for several days.
19-07-1525 The Catholic princes of Germany formed the Dessau League to fight against the Reformation.
19-07-1553 Fifteen-year-old Lady Jane Grey was deposed as Queen of England after claiming the crown for nine days. Mary, the daughter of King Henry VIII, was proclaimed Queen.
19-07-1788 Prices plunged on the Paris stock market.
19-07-1799 The Rosetta Stone, a tablet with hieroglyphic translations into Greek, was found in Egypt.
19-07-1848 The Women's Rights Convention took place in Seneca Fall, NY. Bloomers were introduced at the convention.
19-07-1870 France declared war on Prussia.
19-07-1909 The first unassisted triple play in major-league baseball was made by Cleveland Indians shortstop Neal Ball in a game against Boston.
19-07-1939 Dr. Roy P. Scholz became the first surgeon to use fiberglass sutures.
19-07-1942 German U-boats were withdrawn from positions off the U.S. Atlantic coast due to effective American anti-submarine countermeasures.
19-07-1943 During World War II, more than 150 B-17 and 112 B-24 bombers attacked Rome for the first time.
19-07-1946 Marilyn Monroe acted in her first screen test.
19-07-1960 Juan Marichal of the San Francisco Giants became the first pitcher to get a one-hitter in his major league debut.
19-07-1974 The House Judiciary Committee recommended that U.S. President Richard Nixon should stand trial in the Senate for any of the five impeachment charges against him.
19-07-1975 The Apollo and Soyuz spacecrafts separated after being linked in orbit for two days.
19-07-1979 The Marxist Sandinistas seized control of Nicaragua.
19-07-1984 Geraldine Ferraro was nominated by the Democratic Party to become the first woman from a major political party to run for the office of U.S. Vice-President.
19-07-1985 George Bell won first place in a biggest feet contest with a shoe size of 28-1/2. Bell, at age 26, stood 7 feet 10 inches tall.
19-07-1985 Christa McAuliffe of New Hampshire was chosen to be the first schoolteacher to ride aboard the space shuttle. She died with six others when the Challenger exploded the following year.
19-07-1989 112 people were killed when a United Airline DC-10 airplane crashed in Sioux City, Iowa. 184 people did survive the accident.
20-07-1801 A 1,235 pound cheese ball was pressed at the farm of Elisha Brown, Jr. The ball of cheese was later loaded on a horse-driven wagon and presented to U.S. President Thomas Jefferson at the White House.
20-07-1810 Colombia declared independence from Spain.
20-07-1859 Brooklyn and New York played baseball at Fashion Park Race Course on Long Island, NY. The game marked the first time that admission had been charged for to see a ball game. It cost $.50 to get in and the players on the field did not receive a salary (until 1863).
20-07-1861 The Congress of the Confederate States began holding sessions in Richmond, VA.
20-07-1868 Legislation that ordered U.S. tax stamps to be placed on all cigarette packs was passed.
20-07-1871 British Columbia joined Confederation as a Canadian province.
20-07-1881 Sioux Indian leader Sitting Bull, a fugitive since the Battle of the Little Big Horn, surrendered to federal troops.
20-07-1917 The draft lottery in World War I went into operation.
20-07-1935 NBC radio debuted "G-men." The show was later renamed "Gangbusters."
20-07-1942 The first detachment of the Women's Army Auxiliary Corps, (WACS) began basic training at Fort Des Moines, Iowa.
20-07-1944 An attempt by a group of German officials to assassinate Adolf Hitler failed. The bomb exploded at Hitler's Rastenburg headquarters. Hitler was only wounded.
20-07-1944 U.S. President Roosevelt was nominated for an unprecedented fourth term of office at the Democratic National Convention in Chicago.
20-07-1947 The National Football League (NFL) ruled that no professional team could sign a player who had college eligibility remaining.
20-07-1951 Jordan's King Abdullah Ibn Hussein was assassinated in Jerusalem.
20-07-1961 "Stop the World, I Want to Get Off" opened in London.
20-07-1969 Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Edwin E. Aldrin, Jr. became the first men to walk on the moon.
20-07-1974 Turkish forces invaded Cyprus.
20-07-1976 America's Viking I robot spacecraft made a successful landing on Mars.
20-07-1977 A flash flood hit Johnstown, PA, killing 80 people and causing $350 million worth of damage.
20-07-1982 U.S. President Ronald Reagan pulled the U.S. out of comprehensive test ban negotiations indefinitely.
20-07-1984 Hank Aaron broke Ty Cobb’s record, as he appeared in the 3,034th game of his career.
20-07-1985 Treasure hunters began raising $400 million in coins and silver from the Spanish galleon "Nuestra Senora de Atocha." The ship sank in 1622 40 miles of the coast of Key West, FL.
20-07-1992 Vaclav Havel, the playwright who led the Velvet Revolution against communism, stepped down as president of Czechoslovakia.
20-07-1993 White House deputy counsel Vincent Foster Jr. was found shot to death, a suicide, in a park near Washington, DC.
20-07-1997 Seven people were arrested after New York City police found scores of deaf Mexicans kept in slave-like conditions and forced to peddle trinkets for the smugglers who had brought them to the U.S.
20-07-1998 Russia won a $11.2 billion loan from the International Monetary Fund to help avert the devaluation of its currency.
20-07-2003 In India, elephants used for commercial work began wearing reflectors to avoid being hit by cars during night work.
21-07-1733 John Winthrop was granted the first honorary Doctor of Law Degree in the U.S. The honor was given by Harvard College in Cambridge, MA.
21-07-1831 Belgium became independent as Leopold I was proclaimed King of the Belgians.
21-07-1861 The first major battle of the U.S. Civil War began. It was the Battle of Bull Run at Manassas Junction, VA. The Confederates won the battle.
21-07-1873 Jesse James and his gang pulled off the first train robbery in the U.S. They took $3,000 from the Rock Island Express at Adair, IA.
21-07-1925 The "Monkey Trial" ended in Dayton, TN. John T. Scopes was convicted of violating the state law for teaching Darwin's theory of evolution. The conviction was later overturned.
21-07-1930 The Veterans’ Administration of the United States was established.
21-07-1931 CBS aired the first regularly scheduled program to be simulcast on radio and television. The show featured singer Kate Smith, composer George Gershwin and New York City Mayor Jimmy Walker.
21-07-1931 The Reno Race Track inaugurated the daily double in the U.S.
21-07-1940 Lithuania, Estonia, and Latvia were annexed by the Soviet Union.
21-07-1944 American forces landed on Guam during World War II.
21-07-1947 Loren MacIver’s portrait of Emmett Kelly as Willie the Clown appeared on the cover of "LIFE" magazine.
21-07-1949 The U.S. Senate ratified the North Atlantic Treaty.
21-07-1954 The Geneva Conference partitioned Vietnam into North Vietnam and South Vietnam.
21-07-1957 Althea Gibson became the first black woman to win a major U.S. tennis title when she won the Women’s National clay-court singles competition.
21-07-1958 The last of "Arthur Godfrey’s Talent Scouts" programs aired on CBS-TV.
21-07-1959 A U.S. District Court judge in New York City ruled that "Lady Chatterley’s Lover" was not a dirty book.
21-07-1961 Capt. Virgil "Gus" Grissom became the second American to rocket into a sub-orbital pattern around the Earth. He was flying on the Liberty Bell 7.
21-07-1968 Arnold Palmer became the first golfer to make a million dollars in career earnings after he tied for second place at the PGA Championship.
21-07-1980 Draft registration began in the United States for 19 and 20-year-old men.
21-07-1987 Mary Hart, of "Entertainment Tonight", had her legs insured by Lloyd’s of London for $2 million.
21-07-1997 The U.S.S. Constitution, which defended the United States during the War of 1812, set sail under its own power for the first time in 116 years.
21-07-1998 Chinese gymnast Sang Lan, 17, was paralyzed after a fall while practicing for the women's vault competition at the Goodwill Games in New York. Spinal surgery 4 days later failed to restore sensation below her upper chest.
21-07-1999 The missing plane of John F. Kennedy Jr. was found off of the coast of Martha's Vineyard, MA. The bodies of Kennedy, his wife Carolyn Bessette and her sister Lauren Bessette were found on board. The plane had crashed on July 16, 1999.
21-07-2000 NBC announced that they had found nearly all of Milton Berle's kinescopes. The filmed recordings of Berle's early TV shows had been the subject of a $30 million lawsuit filed by Berle the previous May.
21-07-2002 WorldCom Inc. filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection. At the time it was the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history.
21-07-2004 White House officials were briefed on the September 11 commission's final report. The 575-page report concluded that hijackers exploited "deep institutional failings within our government." The report was released to the public the next day.
22-07-1376 The legend of the Pied Piper of Hamelin leading rats out of town is said to have occurred on this date.
22-07-1587 A second English colony was established on Roanoke Island off North Carolina. The colony vanished under mysterious circumstances.
22-07-1796 Cleveland was founded by Gen. Moses Cleaveland.
22-07-1798 The USS Constitution was underway and out to sea for the firs time since being launched on October 21, 1797.
22-07-1812 English troops under the Duke of Wellington defeated the French at the Battle of Salamanca in Spain.
22-07-1916 10 people were killed when a bomb went off during a Preparedness Day parade in San Francisco, CA.
22-07-1926 Babe Ruth caught a baseball at Mitchell Field in New York. The ball had been dropped from an airplane flying at 250 feet.
22-07-1933 Wiley Post ended his around-the-world flight. He had traveled 15,596 miles in 7 days, 18 hours and 45 minutes.
22-07-1934 John Dillinger was mortally wounded by FBI agents at the Biograph Theatre in Chicago, IL.
22-07-1937 The U.S. Senate rejected President Roosevelt's proposal to add more justices to the Supreme Court.
22-07-1943 American forces led by Gen. George S. Patton captured Palermo, Sicily.
22-07-1941 Plans for the Pentagon were presented to the House Subcommittee on Appropriations.
22-07-1946 90 people were killed when Jewish extremists blew up a wing of the King David Hotel in Jerusalem.
22-07-1955 U.S. Vice-President Richard M. Nixon chaired a cabinet meeting in Washington, DC. It was the first time that a Vice-President had carried out the task.
22-07-1965 "Till Death Us Do Part" debuted on England’s BBC-TV.
22-07-1975 Confederate General Robert E. Lee had his U.S. citizenship restored by the U.S. Congress.
22-07-1991 Desiree Washington, a Miss Black America contestant, charged she'd been raped by boxer Mike Tyson in an Indianapolis hotel room. Tyson was later convicted of rape and served 3 years in prison.
22-07-1991 Police arrested Jeffrey Dahmer after finding the remains of 11 victims in his apartment in Milwaukee. Dahmer confessed to 17 murders and was sentenced to life in prison.
22-07-1992 Colombian drug lord Pablo Escobar escaped from his luxury prison near Medellin. He was killed by security forces in December 1993.
22-07-1998 Iran tested medium-range missile, capable of reaching Israel or Saudi Arabia.
22-07-2000 Astronomers at the University of Arizona announced that they had found a 17th moon orbiting Jupiter.
22-07-2003 In northern Iraq, Saddam Hussein's sons Odai and Qusai died after a gunfight with U.S. forces.
22-07-2003 In Paris, France, a fire broke out near the top of the Eiffel Tower. About 4,000 visitors were evacuated and no injuries were reported.
22-07-2004 The September 11 commission's final report was released. The 575-page report concluded that hijackers exploited "deep institutional failings within our government." The report was released to White House officials the day before.
23-07-1715 The first lighthouse in America was authorized for construction at Little Brewster Island, Massachusetts.
23-07-1827 The first swimming school in the U.S. opened in Boston, MA.
23-07-1829 William Burt patented the typographer, which was the first typewriter.
23-07-1877 The first municipal railroad passenger service began in Cincinnati, Ohio.
23-07-1886 Steve Brodie, a New York saloonkeeper, claimed to have made a daredevil plunge from the Brooklyn Bridge into the East River.
23-07-1904 The ice cream cone was invented by Charles E. Menches during the Louisiana Purchase Exposition in St. Louis, MO.
23-07-1914 Austria-Hungary issued an ultimatum to Serbia following the killing of Archduke Francis Ferdinand by a Serb assassin. The dispute led to World War I.
23-07-1938 The first federal game preserve was approved by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. The area was 2,000 acres in Utah.
23-07-1945 The first passenger train observation car was placed in service by the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy Railroad.
23-07-1952 Egyptian military officers led by Gamal Abdel Nasser overthrew King Farouk I.
23-07-1954 A law is passed that states that "The Secretary of the Navy is authorized to repair, equip, and restore the United States Ship Constitution, as far as may be practicable, to her original appearance, but not for active service, and thereafter to maintain the United States Ship Constitution at Boston, Massachusetts."
23-07-1958 The submarine Nautilus departed from Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, under orders to conduct "Operation Sunshine." The mission was to be the first vessel to cross the north pole by ship. The Nautils achieved the goal on August 3, 1958.
23-07-1962 The "Telstar" communications satellite sent the first live TV broadcast to Europe.
23-07-1967 In Detroit, MI, rioting that claimed some 43 lives.
23-07-1972 Eddie Merckx of Belgium won his fourth consecutive Tour de France bicycling competition.
23-07-1972 The U.S. launched Landsat 1 (ERTS-1). It was the first Earth-resources satellite.
23-07-1977 A jury in Washington, DC, convicted 12 Hanafi Muslims of charges stemming from the hostage siege at three buildings the previous March.
23-07-1984 Miss America, Vanessa Williams, turned in her crown after it had been discovered that nude photos of her had appeared in "Penthouse" magazine. She was the first to resign the title.
23-07-1986 Britain's Prince Andrew married Sarah Ferguson at Westminster Abbey in London. They divorced in 1996.
23-07-1997 Police in Miami Beach, FL, found the body of Andrew Cunanan. He was the suspected killer of Gianni Versace.
23-07-1998 U.S. scientists at the University of Hawaii turned out more than 50 "carbon-copy" mice, with a cloning technique.
23-07-2000 Lance Armstrong won his second Tour de France.
24-07-1847 Mormon leader Brigham Young and his followers arrived in the valley of the Great Salt Lake in present-day Utah.
24-07-1847 Richard M. Hoe patented the rotary-type printing press.
24-07-1849 Georgetown University in Washington, DC. presented its first Doctor of Music Degree. It was given to Professor Henry Dielman.
24-07-1866 Tennessee became the first state to be readmitted to the Union after the U.S. Civil War.
24-07-1923 The Treaty of Lausanne, which settled the boundaries of modern Turkey, was concluded in Switzerland.
24-07-1929 U.S. President Hoover proclaimed the Kellogg-Briand Pact, which renounced war as an instrument of foreign policy.
24-07-1933 The first broadcast of "The Romance of Helen Trent" was heard on radio. 7,222 episodes were aired.
24-07-1933 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt gave his fourth "Fireside Chat."
24-07-1937 The state of Alabama dropped charges against five black men accused of raping two white women in the so-called Scottsboro case.
24-07-1948 Soviet occupation forces in Germany blockaded West Berlin. The U.S.-British airlift began the following day.
24-07-1956 Dean Martin and Jerry Lewis ended their team. They ended the partnership a decade after it began on July 25, 1946.
24-07-1969 The Apollo 11 astronauts splashed down safely in the Pacific Ocean.
24-07-1974 The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled that President Nixon had to turn over subpoenaed White House tape recordings to the Watergate special prosecutor.
24-07-1978 Billy Martin was fired for the first of three times as the manager of the New York Yankees baseball team.
24-07-1984 Terry Bradshaw retired from the National Football League.
24-07-1985 Walt Disney released their 25th full-length cartoon. The work was "The Black Cauldron."
24-07-1987 Hulda Crooks, at 91 years of age, climbed Mt. Fuji. Hulda became the oldest person to climb Japan’s highest peak.
24-07-1998 A gunman burst into U.S. Capitol and opened fire killing two police officers. Russel Weston Jr., was later ruled incompetent to stand trial.
24-07-1998 Roy O. Disney received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
24-07-2003 The U.S. released pictures of the bodies of Odai and Qusai Hussein. The two died during a battle with U.S. forces near Mosul, Iraq.
25-07-0326 Constantine refused to carry out the traditional pagan sacrifices.
25-07-1394 Charles VI of France issued a decree for the general expulsion of Jews from France.
25-07-1564 Maximillian II became emperor of the Holy Roman Empire.
25-07-1587 Japanese strong-man Hideyoshi banned Christianity in Japan and ordered all Christians to leave.
25-07-1593 France's King Henry IV converted from Protestantism to Roman Catholicism.
25-07-1759 British forces defeated a French army at Fort Niagara in Canada.
25-07-1799 Napoleon Bonaparte defeated the Ottomans at Aboukir, Egypt.
25-07-1805 Aaron Burr visited New Orleans with plans to establish a new country, with New Orleans as the capital city.
25-07-1845 China granted Belgium equal trading rights with Britain, France and the United States.
25-07-1850 In Worcester, MA, Harvard and Yale University freshmen met in the first intercollegiate billiards match.
25-07-1850 Gold was discovered in the Rogue River in Oregon.
25-07-1854 The paper collar was patented by Walter Hunt.
25-07-1861 The Crittenden Resolution, which called for the American Civil War to be fought to preserve the Union and not for slavery, was passed by the U.S. Congress.
25-07-1866 Ulysses S. Grant was named General of the Army. He was the first American officer to hold the rank.
25-07-1868 The U.S. Congress passed an act creating the Wyoming Territory.
25-07-1871 Seth Wheeler patented perforated wrapping paper.
25-07-1907 Korea became a protectorate of Japan.
25-07-1909 French aviator Louis Bleriot flew across the English Channel in a monoplane. He traveled from Calais to Dover in 37 minutes. He was the first man to fly across the channel.
25-07-1914 Russia declared that it would act to protect Serbian sovereignty.
25-07-1924 Greece announced the deportation of 50,000 Armenians.
25-07-1934 Austrian chancellor Engelbert Dollfuss was shot and killed by Nazis.
25-07-1939 W2XBS TV in New York City presented the first musical comedy seen on TV. The show was "Topsy and Eva".
25-07-1941 The U.S. government froze all Japanese and Chinese assets.
25-07-1943 Italian Fascist dictator Benito Mussolini was overthrown in a coup.
25-07-1946 The U.S. detonated an atomic bomb at Bikini Atoll in the Pacific. It was the first underwater test of the device.
25-07-1946 Dean Martin and Jerry Lewis staged their first show as a team at Club 500 in Atlantic City, NJ.
25-07-1947 Fortune Gordien of Oslo, Norway set a world record discus throw of 178.47 feet.
25-07-1952 Puerto Rico became a self-governing commonwealth of the U.S.
25-07-1956 The Italian liner Andrea Doria sank after colliding with the Swedish ship Stockholm off the New England coast. 51 people were killed.
25-07-1978 Louise Joy Brown, the first test-tube baby, was born in Oldham, England. She had been conceived through in-vitro fertilization.
25-07-1978 Pete Rose of the Cincinnati Red's broke the National League record for consecutive base hits as he got a hit in 38 straight games.
25-07-1984 Soviet cosmonaut Svetlana Savitskaya became the first woman to walk in space. She was aboard the orbiting space station Salyut 7.
25-07-1987 The Salt Lake City Trappers set a professional baseball record as the team won its 29th game in a row.
25-07-1994 Israel and Jordan formally ended the state of war that had existed between them since 1948.
25-07-1997 K.R. Narayanan became India's president. He was the first member of the Dalits caste to do so.
25-07-1998 The USS Harry S. Truman was commissioned and put into service by the U.S. Navy.
25-07-1998 U.S. President Clinton was subpoenaed to appear before a federal grand jury regarding the Monica Lewinsky case. The subpoena was withdrawn when Clinton agreed to give videotaped testimony with his lawyers present.
25-07-1999 Lance Armstrong won the Tour de France. He was only the second American to win the race. He won the race again in 2000.
25-07-2000 A supersonic Concorde crashed outside Paris, France, killing all 109 people aboard and 5 on the ground.
26-07-1775 A postal system was established by the 2nd Continental Congress of the United States. The first Postmaster General was Benjamin Franklin.
26-07-1788 New York became the 11th state to ratify the U.S. Constitution.
26-07-1881 Thomas Edison and Patrick Kenny execute a patent application for a facsimile telegraph (U.S. Pat. 479,184).
26-07-1893 Commercial production of the Addressograph started in Chicago, IL.
26-07-1907 The Chester was launched. It was the first turbine-propelled ship.
26-07-1908 U.S. Attorney General Charles J. Bonaparte issued an order that created an investigative agency that was a forerunner of the FBI.
26-07-1945 Winston Churchill resigned as Britain's prime minister.
26-07-1947 U.S. President Truman signed The National Security Act. The act created the National Security Council, the Department of Defense, the Central Intelligence Agency and the Joint Chiefs of Staff.
26-07-1948 Babe Ruth was seen by the public for the last time, when he attended the New York City premiere of the motion picture, "The Babe Ruth Story".
26-07-1948 U.S. President Truman signed executive orders that prohibited discrimination in the U.S. armed forces and federal employment.
26-07-1952 King Farouk I of Egypt abdicated in the wake of a coup led by Gamal Abdel Nasser.
26-07-1953 Fidel Castro began his revolt against Fulgencio Batista with an unsuccessful attack on an army barracks in eastern Cuba. Castro eventually ousted Batista six years later.
26-07-1956 Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal.
26-07-1964 Teamsters president Jimmy Hoffa and six others were convicted of fraud and conspiracy in the handling of a union pension fund.
26-07-1971 Apollo 15 was launched from Cape Kennedy, FL.
26-07-1998 AT&T and British Telecommunications PLC announced they were forming a joint venture to combine international operations and develop a new Internet system.
26-07-1999 1,500 pieces of Marilyn Monroe's personal items went on display at Christie's in New York, NY. The items went on sale later in 1999.
27-07-1214 At the Battle of Bouvines in France, Philip Augustus of France defeated John of England.
27-07-1245 Frederick II of France was deposed by a council at Lyons, which found him guilty of sacrilege.
27-07-1663 The British Parliament passed a second Navigation Act, which required all goods bound for the colonies be sent in British ships from British ports.
27-07-1689 Government forces defeated the Scottish Jacobites at the Battle of Killiecrankie.
27-07-1694 The Bank of England received a royal charter as a commercial institution.
27-07-1775 Benjamin Rush began his service as the first Surgeon General of the Continental Army.
27-07-1784 "Courier De L’Amerique" became the first French newspaper to be published in the United States. It was printed in Philadelphia, PA.
27-07-1777 The marquis of Lafayette arrived in New England to help the rebellious American colonists fight the British.
27-07-1778 The British and French fleets fought to a standoff in the first Battle of Ushant.
27-07-1789 The Department of Foreign Affairs was established by the U.S. Congress. The agency was later known as the Department of State.
27-07-1804 The 12th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. With the amendment Electors were directed to vote for a President and for a Vice-President rather than for two choices for President.
27-07-1866 Cyrus Field successfully completed the Atlantic Cable. It was an underwater telegraph from North America to Europe.
27-07-1909 Orville Wright set a record for the longest airplane flight. He was testing the first Army airplane and kept it in the air for 1 hour 12 minutes and 40 seconds.
27-07-1914 British troops invaded the streets of Dublin, Ireland, and began to disarm Irish rebels.
27-07-1918 The Socony 200 was launched. It was the first concrete barge and was used to carry oil.
27-07-1921 Canadian biochemist Frederick Banting and associates announced the discovery of the hormone insulin.
27-07-1940 Bugs Bunny made his official debut in the Warner Bros. animated cartoon "A Wild Hare."
27-07-1944 U.S. troops completed the liberation of Guam.
27-07-1947 The World Water Ski Organization was founded in Geneva, Switzerland.
27-07-1953 The armistice agreement that ended the Korean War was signed at Panmunjon, Korea.
27-07-1955 The Allied occupation of Austria ended.
27-07-1964 U.S. President Lyndon Johnson sent an additional 5,000 advisers to South Vietnam.
27-07-1965 In the U.S., the Federal Cigarette Labeling and Advertising Act was signed into law. The law required health warnings on all cigarette packages.
27-07-1967 U.S. President Johnson appointed the Kerner Commission to assess the causes of the violence in the wake of urban rioting.
27-07-1974 NBC-TV took "Dinah's Place" off of its daytime programming roster.
27-07-1974 The U.S. Congress asked for impeachment procedures against President Richard Nixon.
27-07-1980 The deposed shah of Iran, Muhammad Riza Pahlavi, died in a hospital near Cairo, Egypt.
27-07-1984 Pete Rose passed Ty Cobb’s record for most singles in a career when he got his 3,503rd base hit.
27-07-1992 Boston Celtics star Reggie Lewis died after collapsing on a Brandeis University basketball court during practice. He was 27 years old.
27-07-1993 IBM's new chairman, Louis V. Gerstner, Jr., announced an $8.9 billion plan to cut the company's costs.
27-07-1995 The Korean War Veterans Memorial was dedicated in Washington, DC, by U.S. President Clinton and South Korean President Kim Young-sam.
27-07-1996 At the Atlanta Olympics a pipe bomb exploded at the public Centennial Olympic Park. One person was killed and more than 100 were injured.
27-07-1998 Robert Vaughn received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
27-07-1999 The U.S. space shuttle Discovery completed a five-day mission commanded by Air Force Col. Eileen Collins. It was the first shuttle mission to be commanded by a woman.
27-07-2001 The ribbon cutting ceremony was held for American Airlines Center in Dallas, TX. The event set two new world records, one for the 3 mile long ribbon and one for the 2,000 people that cut it.
27-07-2003 It was reported by the BBC (British Broadcasting Corp.) that there was no monster in Loch Ness. The investigation used 600 separate sonar beams and satellite navigation technology to trawl the loch. Reports of sightings of the "Loch Ness Monster" began in the 6th century.
27-07-2006 Intel Corp introduced its Core 2 Duo microprocessors.
28-07-1540 King Henry VIII's chief minister, Thomas Cromwell, was executed. The same day, Henry married his fifth wife, Catherine Howard.
28-07-1794 Maximilien Robespierre was sent to the guillotine. He was a leading figure in the French Revolution.
28-07-1821 Peru declared its independence from Spain.
28-07-1865 The American Dental Association proposed its first code of ethics.
28-07-1866 The metric system was legalized by the U.S. Congress for the standardization of weights and measures throughout the United States.
28-07-1868 The Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was declared in effect. The amendment guaranteed due process of law.
28-07-1896 The city of Miami, FL, was incorporated.
28-07-1914 World War I officially began when Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia.
28-07-1932 Federal troops forcibly dispersed the "Bonus Army" of World War I veterans who had gathered in Washington, DC. They were demanding money they were not scheduled to receive until 1945.
28-07-1941 Plans for the Pentagon were approved by the U.S. House of Representatives.
28-07-1942 L.A. Thatcher received a patent for a coin-operated mailbox. The device stamped envelopes when money was inserted.
28-07-1945 A U.S. Army bomber crashed into the 79th floor of New York City's Empire State Building. 14 people were killed and 26 were injured.
28-07-1951 The Walt Disney film "Alice in Wonderland" was released.
28-07-1965 U.S. President Johnson announced he was increasing the number of American troops in South Vietnam from 75,000 to 125,000.
28-07-1973 Lee Majors and Farrah Fawcett were married.
28-07-1976 An earthquake northern China, killed at least 242,000 people.
28-07-1991 Dennis Martinez (Montreal Expos) pitched the 13th perfect game in major league baseball history.
28-07-1994 Kenny Rogers (Texas Rangers) pitched the 14th perfect game in major league baseball history.
28-07-1998 Bell Atlantic and GTE announced $52 billion deal that created the second-largest phone company.
28-07-1998 Serbian military forces seized the Kosovo town of Malisevo.
28-07-1998 Monica Lewinsky received blanket immunity from prosecution to testify before a grand jury about her relationship with U.S. President Clinton.
28-07-2000 Kathie Lee Gifford made her final appearance as co-host of the ABC talk show "Live with Regis and Kathie Lee."
28-07-2006 Researchers announced that two ancient reptiles had been found off Australia. The Umoonasaurus and Opallionectes were the first of their kind to be found in the period soon after the Jurassic era.
29-07-1588 The English defeated the Spanish Armada in the Battle of Gravelines.
29-07-1754 The first international boxing match was held. The 25-minute match was won when Jack Slack of Britain knocked out Jean Petit from France.
29-07-1773 The first schoolhouse to be located west of the Allegheny Mountains was built in Schoenbrunn, OH.
29-07-1786 "The Pittsburgh Gazette" became the first newspaper west of the Alleghenies to be published. The paper's name was later changed to "The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette".
29-07-1874 Major Walter Copton Winfield of England received U.S. patent for the lawn-tennis court.
29-07-1890 Artist Vincent van Gogh died of a self-inflicted gunshot wound in Auvers, France.
29-07-1914 The first transcontinental telephone service was inaugurated when two people held a conversation between New York, NY and San Francisco, CA.
29-07-1940 John Sigmund of St. Louis, MO completed a 292-mile swim down the Mississippi River. The swim from St. Louis to Caruthersville, MO took him 89 hours and 48 minutes.
29-07-1950 Disney's adaptation of Robert Louis Stevenson's "Treasure Island" was released.
29-07-1957 Jack Paar began hosting the "Tonight" show on NBC-TV. The name of the show was changed to "The Jack Paar Show". Paar was host for five years.
29-07-1957 The International Atomic Energy Agency was established.
29-07-1958 The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) was authorized by the U.S. Congress.
29-07-1967 Fire swept the USS Forrestal in the Gulf of Tonkin. 134 U.S. servicemen were killed.
29-07-1968 Pope Paul VI reaffirmed the Roman Catholic Church's stance against artificial methods of birth control.
29-07-1975 OAS (Organization of American States) members voted to lift collective sanctions against Cuba. The U.S. government welcomed the action and announced its intention to open serious discussions with Cuba on normalization.
29-07-1981 England's Prince Charles and Lady Diana Spencer were married.
29-07-1983 Steve Garvey of the Los Angeles Dodgers set the National League consecutive game record at 1,207.
29-07-1985 General Motors announced that Spring Hill, TN, would be the home of the Saturn automobile assembly plant.
29-07-1993 The Israeli Supreme Court acquitted retired Ohio autoworker John Demjanjuk of being Nazi death camp guard "Ivan the Terrible." His death sentence was thrown out and he was set free.
29-07-1997 Minamata Bay in Japan was declared free of mercury 40 years after contaminated food fish were blamed for deaths and birth defects.
29-07-1998 The United Auto Workers union ended a 54-day strike against General Motors. The strike caused $2.8 billion in lost revenues.
29-07-1999 Mark O. Barton killed nine people and wounded 13 others in a shooting rampage in Atlanta, GA. He wife and two children had been found bludgeoned to death earlier in the day.
29-07-2005 Astronomers announced that they had discovered a new planet larger than Pluto in orbit around the sun.
30-07-1619 The first representative assembly in America convened in Jamestown, VA. (House of Burgesses)
30-07-1729 The city of Baltimore was founded in Maryland.
30-07-1889 Vladimir Zworykin, called the "Father of Television" was born in Russia. He invented the iconoscope.
30-07-1898 "Scientific America" carried the first magazine automobile ad. The ad was for the Winton Motor Car Company of Cleveland, OH.
30-07-1937 The American Federation of Radio Artists (AFRA) was organized as a part of the American Federation of Labor.
30-07-1942 The WAVES were created by legislation signed by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt. The members of the Women's Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service were a part of the U.S. Navy.
30-07-1945 The USS Indianapolis was torpedoed by a Japanese submarine. The ship had just delivered key components of the Hiroshima atomic bomb to the Pacific island of Tinian. Only 316 out of 1,196 men aboard survived the attack.
30-07-1956 The phrase "In God We Trust" was adopted as the U.S. national motto.
30-07-1965 U.S. President Johnson signed into law Social Security Act that established Medicare and Medicaid. It went into effect the following year.
30-07-1968 Ron Hansen of the Washington Senators made the first unassisted triple play in the major leagues in 41 years.
30-07-1974 The U.S. House of Representatives Judiciary Committee voted to impeach President Nixon for blocking the Watergate investigation and for abuse of power.
30-07-1975 Jimmy Hoffa, former Teamsters union president, disappeared in Michigan. His remains were never found.
30-07-1996 A federal law enforcement source said that security guard Richard Jewell had become the focus of the investigation into the bombing at Centennial Olympic Park. Jewell was later cleared as a suspect.
30-07-1997 14 Israelis were killed in a double suicide bombing in a Jerusalem marketplace. The Islamist group Hamas claimed responsibility for the bombings.
30-07-1998 A group of Ohio machine-shop workers (who call themselves the Lucky 13) won the $295.7 million Powerball jackpot. It was the largest-ever American lottery.
30-07-2000 Jennifer Aniston and Brad Pitt were married.
30-07-2001 Lance Armstrong became the first American to win three consecutive Tours de France.
30-07-2003 In Mexico, the last 'old style' Volkswagon Beetle rolled off an assembly line.
31-07-1498 Christopher Columbus, on his third voyage to the Western Hemisphere, arrived at the island of Trinidad.
31-07-1790 The first U.S. patent was issued to Samuel Hopkins for his process for making potash and pearl ashes. The substance was used in fertilizer.
31-07-1792 The cornerstone of the U.S. Mint in Philadelphia, PA, was laid. It was the first building to be used only as a U.S. government building.
31-07-1919 Germany's Weimar Constitution was adopted.
31-07-1928 MGM’s Leo the lion roared for the first time. He introduced MGM’s first talking picture, "White Shadows on the South Seas."
31-07-1932 Enzo Ferrari retired from racing. In 1950 he launched a series of cars under his name.
31-07-1945 Pierre Laval of France surrendered to Americans in Austria.
31-07-1948 U.S. President Truman helped dedicate New York International Airport (later John F. Kennedy International Airport) at Idlewild Field.
31-07-1955 Marilyn Bell of Toronto, Canada, at age 17, became the youngest person to swim the English Channel.
31-07-1961 The first tie in All-Star Game major league baseball history was recorded when it was stopped in the 9th inning due to rain at Boston's Fenway Park.
31-07-1964 The American space probe Ranger 7 transmitted pictures of the moon's surface.
31-07-1971 Men rode in a vehicle on the moon for the first time in a lunar rover vehicle (LRV).
31-07-1981 The seven-week baseball players’ strike came to an end when the players and owners agreed on the issue of free agent compensation.
31-07-1989 A pro-Iranian group in Lebanon released a videotape reportedly showing the hanged body of American hostage William R. Higgins.
31-07-1991 U.S. President Bush and Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev signed the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty.
31-07-1995 The Walt Disney Company agreed to acquire Capital Cities/ABC in a $19 billion deal.
31-07-1997 In New York City, NY, police seized five bombs believed to be bound for terrorist attacks on city subways.
31-07-1998 More than 50 people died in Kashmir due to crossfire between India and Pakistan.
31-07-1998 Nicolas Cage received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
31-07-1999 The spacecraft Lunar Prospect crashed into the moon. It was a mission to detect frozen water on the moon's surface. The craft had been launched on January 6, 1998.
01-06-0193 The Roman Emperor, Marcus Didius, was murdered in his palace.
01-06-1533 Anne Boleyn, Henry VIII’s new queen, was crowned.
01-06-1774 The British government ordered the Port of Boston closed.
01-06-1789 The first U.S. congressional act on administering oaths became law.
01-06-1792 Kentucky became the 15th state of the U.S.
01-06-1796 Tennessee became the 16th state of the U.S.
01-06-1861 The first skirmish of the U.S. Civil War took place at the Fairfax Court House, Virginia.
01-06-1869 Thomas Edison received a patent for his electric voting machine.
01-06-1877 U.S. troops were authorized to pursue bandits into Mexico.
01-06-1915 Germany conducted the first zeppelin air raid over England.
01-06-1916 The National Defense Act increased the strength of the U.S. National Guard by 450,000 men.
01-06-1921 A race riot erupted in Tulsa, Oklahoma. 85 people were killed.
01-06-1935 The Ingersoll-Waterbury Company reported that it had produced 2.5 million Mickey Mouse watches during its 2-year association with Disney.
01-06-1938 Baseball helmets were worn for the first time.
01-06-1938 Superman, the world's first super hero, appeared in the first issue of Action Comics.
01-06-1939 The Douglas DC-4 made its first passenger flight from Chicago to New York.
01-06-1941 The German Army completed the capture of Crete as the Allied evacuation ended.
