01-06-1942 The U.S. began sending Lend-Lease materials to the Soviet Union.
01-06-1943 During World War II, Germans shot down a civilian flight from Lisbon to London.
01-06-1944 The French resistance was warned by a coded message from the British that the D-Day invasion was imminent.
01-06-1944 Siesta was abolished by the government of Mexico.
01-06-1953 Raymond Burr mad his network-TV acting debut. It was in "The Mask of Medusa" on ABC-TV's "Twilight Theater."
01-06-1954 In the Peanuts comic strip, Linus' security blanket made its debut.
01-06-1958 Charles de Gaulle became the premier of France.
01-06-1961 Radio listeners in New York, California, and Illinois were introduced to FM multiplex stereo broadcasting. A year later the FCC made this a standard.
01-06-1963 Governor George Wallace vowed to defy an injunction that ordered the integration of the University of Alabama.
01-06-1968 Helen Keller died. She had been deaf and blind since the age of 18 months. During her life she learned to speak, ride horses, and the waltz. She also graduated from Radcliffe cum laude.
01-06-1970 Zimbabwe came into existence. It was formerly known as Rhodesia.
01-06-1973 The James Bond movie "Live and Let Die" opened.
01-06-1977 The Soviet Union formally charged Jewish human rights activist Anatoly Shcharansky with treason. He was imprisoned until 1986.
01-06-1978 The U.S. reported the finding of wiretaps in the American embassy in Moscow.
01-06-1980 Cable News Network (CNN) made its debut as the first all-news station.
01-06-1989 Disney World's "Typhoon Lagoon" opened.
01-06-1995 At Disneyland Paris, the attraction "Space Mountain: From The Earth to the Moon" opened.
01-06-1998 In the U.S., the FDA approved a urine-only test for the AIDS virus.
01-06-1998 A $124 million suit was brought against Goodyear Tire & Rubber that alleged discrimination towards black workers.
02-06-1537 Pope Paul III banned the enslavement of Indians.
02-06-1774 The Quartering Act, which required American colonists to allow British soldiers into their houses, was reenacted.
02-06-1793 Maximillian Robespierre initiated the "Reign of Terror". It was an effort to purge those suspected of treason against the French Republic.
02-06-1818 The British army defeated the Maratha alliance in Bombay, India.
02-06-1851 Maine became the first U.S. state to enact a law prohibiting alcohol.
02-06-1883 The first baseball game under electric lights was played in Fort Wayne, Indiana.
02-06-1886 Grover Cleveland became the first U.S. president to get married while in office.
02-06-1896 Guglieimo Marconi's radio was patented in the U.S.
02-06-1897 Mark Twain, at age 61, was quoted by the New York Journal as saying "the report of my death was an exaggeration." He was responding to the rumors that he had died.
02-06-1910 Charles Stewart Roll became the first person to fly across the English Channel.
02-06-1924 All American Indians were granted U.S. citizenship by the U.S. Congress.
02-06-1928 Nationalist Chiang Kai-shek captured Peking, China.
02-06-1930 Mrs. M. Niezes of Panama gave birth to the first baby to be born on a ship while passing through the Panama Canal.
02-06-1933 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt accepted the first swimming pool to be built inside the White House.
02-06-1935 George Herman "Babe" Ruth announced that he was retiring from baseball.
02-06-1937 "The Fabulous Dr. Tweedy" was broadcast on NBC radio for the first time.
02-06-1941 Lou Gehrig died in New York of the degenerative disease amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
02-06-1946 Italians voted by referendum to form a republic instead of of a monarchy.
02-06-1953 Elizabeth was crowned queen of England at Westminster Abbey.
02-06-1954 U.S. Senator Joseph McCarthy charged that there were communists working in the CIA and atomic weapons plants.
02-06-1957 Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev was interviewed by CBS-TV.
02-06-1966 Surveyor 1, the U.S. space probe, landed on the moon and started sending photographs back to Earth of the Moon's surface. It was the first soft landing on the Moon.
02-06-1969 The National Arts Center in Canada opened its doors to the public.
02-06-1969 Australian aircraft carrier Melbourne sliced the destroyer USS Frank E. Evans in half off the shore of South Vietnam.
02-06-1979 Pope John Paul II arrived in his native Poland on the first visit by a pope to a Communist country.
02-06-1985 The R.J. Reynolds Company proposed a major merger with Nabisco that would create a $4.9 billion conglomerate.
02-06-1985 Tommy Sandt was ejected from a major-league baseball game before the national anthem was played. He had complained to the umpire about a call against his team the night before.
02-06-1995 Captain Scott F. O'Grady's U.S. Air Force F-16C was shot down by Bosnian Serbs. He was rescued six days later.
02-06-1997 Timothy McVeigh was found guilty of the bombing of a federal building in Oklahoma City in which 168 people were killed.
02-06-1998 Royal Caribbean Cruises agreed to pay $9 million to settle charges of dumping waste at sea.
02-06-1998 Voters in California passed Proposition 227. The act abolished the state's 30-year-old bilingual education program by requiring that all children be taught in English.
02-06-1999 In South Africa, the African National Congress (ANC) won a major victory. ANC leader Thabo Mbeki was to succeed Nelson Mandela as the nation's president.
02-06-2003 In the U.S., federal regulators voted to allow companies to buy more television stations and newspaper-broadcasting combinations in the same city. The previous ownership restrictions had not been altered since 1975.
02-06-2003 In Seville, Spain, a chest containing the supposed remains of Christopher Columbus were exhumed for DNA tests to determine whether the bones were really those of the explorer. The tests were aimed at determining if Colombus was currently buried in Spain's Seville Cathedral or in Santo Domingo in the Dominican Republic.
02-06-2003 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that companies could not be sued under a trademark law for using information in the public domain without giving credit to the originator. The case had originated with 20th Century Fox against suing Dastar Corp. over their use of World War II footage.
02-06-2003 William Baily was reunited with two paintings he had left on a subway platform. One of the works was an original Picasso rendering of two male figures and a recreation of Picasso's "Guernica" by Sophie Matisse. Sophie Matisse was the great-granddaughter of Henri Matisse.
03-06-1098 Christian Crusaders of the First Crusade seized Antioch, Turkey.
03-06-1539 Hernando De Soto claimed Florida for Spain.
03-06-1621 The Dutch West India Company received a charter for New Netherlands (now known as New York).
03-06-1784 The United States Congress created the United States Army.
03-06-1800 John Adams moved to Washington, DC. He was the first President to live in what later became the capital of the United States.
03-06-1805 A peace treaty between the U.S. and Tripoli was completed in the captain's cabin on board the USS Constitution.
03-06-1851 The New York Knickerbockers became the first baseball team to wear uniforms.
03-06-1856 Cullen Whipple patented the screw machine.
03-06-1864 About 7,000 Union troops were killed within 30 minutes during the Battle of Cold Harbor in Virginia during the U.S. Civil War.
03-06-1871 Jesse James, then 24, and his gang robbed the Obocock bank in Corydon, Iowa. They stole $15,000.
03-06-1888 "Casey at the Bat" the poem by Ernest Lawrence Thayer was first published.
03-06-1918 The Finnish Parliament ratified its treaty with Germany.
03-06-1923 In Italy, Benito Mussolini granted women the right to vote.
03-06-1928 Manchurian warlord Chian Tso-Lin died as a result of a bomb blast set off by the Japanese.
03-06-1932 Lou Gehrig set a major league baseball record when he hit four consecutive home runs.
03-06-1937 The Duke of Windsor, who had abdicated the British throne, married Wallis Warfield Simpson.
03-06-1938 The German Reich voted to confiscate so-called "degenerate art."
03-06-1940 German bombed Paris, killing 254 people. Most of the people killed were civilians and school children.
03-06-1952 A rebellion by North Korean prisoners in the Koje prison camp in South Korea was put down by American troops.
03-06-1959 The first class graduated from the Air Force Academy in Colorado Springs, CO.
03-06-1965 Edward White became the first American astronaut to do a "space walk" when he left the Gemini 4 capsule.
03-06-1968 Andy Warhol was shot and critically wounded in his New York film studio by Valerie Solanas.
03-06-1970 Har Gobind Khorana and colleagues announced the first synthesis of a gene from chemical components.
03-06-1974 Charles Colson, an aide to U.S. President Richard Nixon, pled guilty to obstruction of justice.
03-06-1983 Gordon Kahl was killed in a gun battle with law enforcement officials near Smithville, AR. Kahl was wanted for the slayings of two U.S. marshals in North Dakota.
03-06-1985 After five years, the characters of Nancy and Chris Hughes returned to CBS-TV's "As the World Turns."
03-06-1989 Iran's Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini died.
03-06-1989 Chinese army troops positioned themselves to began a sweep of Beijing to crush student-led pro-democracy demonstrations in Tiananmen Square.
03-06-1991 Mount Unzen in southern Japan erupted killing 40 people.
03-06-1998 In Germany, a train veered off its tracks and hit a road bridge. 101 people were killed and 80 were injured.
03-06-1999 Slobodan Milosevic's government accepted an international peace plan concerning Kosovo. NATO announced that airstrikes would continue until 40,000 Serb forces were withdrawn from Kosovo.
03-06-1999 Dennis Muren received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
03-06-2003 Sammy Sosa (Chicago Cubs) broke a bat when he grounded out against the Tampa Bay Devil Rays. The bat he was using was a corked bat.
03-06-2003 Toys "R" Us, Inc. announced that it had signed a multi-year agreement with Albertson to become the exclusive toy provider for all of all of Albertson's food and drug stores.
04-06-1615 The fortress of Osaka, Japan, fell to shogun Ieyasu after a six month siege.
04-06-1647 The British army seized King Charles I and held him as a hostage.
04-06-1674 Horse racing was prohibited in Massachusetts.
04-06-1717 The Freemasons were founded in London.
04-06-1784 Marie Thible became the first woman to fly in a hot-air balloon. The flight was 45 minutes long and reached a height of 8,500 feet.
04-06-1792 Captain George Vancouver claimed Puget Sound for Britain.
04-06-1794 British troops captured Port-au-Prince, Haiti.
04-06-1805 Tripoli was forced to conclude peace with U.S. after conflicts over tribute.
04-06-1812 The Louisiana Territory had its name changed to the Missouri Territory.
04-06-1816 The Washington was launched at Wheeling, WV. It was the first stately, double-decker steamboat.
04-06-1878 Turkey turned Cyprus over to Britain.
04-06-1892 The Sierra Club was incorporated in San Francisco.
04-06-1896 Henry Ford made a successful test drive of his new car in Detroit, MI. The vehicle was called a quadricycle.
04-06-1911 Gold was discovered in Alaska's Indian Creek.
04-06-1918 French and American troops halted Germany's offensive at Chateau-Thierry, France.
04-06-1919 The U.S. Senate passed the Women's Suffrage bill.
04-06-1924 An eternal light was dedicated at Madison Square in New York City in memory of all New York soldiers who died in World War I.
04-06-1931 The first rocket-glider flight was made by William Swan in Atlantic City, NJ.
04-06-1935 "Invisible" glass was patented by Gerald Brown and Edward Pollard.
04-06-1939 The first shopping cart was introduced by Sylvan Goldman in Oklahoma City, OK. It was actually a folding chair that had been mounted on wheels.
04-06-1940 The British completed the evacuation of 300,000 troops at Dunkirk, France.
04-06-1942 The Battle of Midway began. It was the first major victory for America over Japan during World War II. The battle ended on June 6 and ended Japanese expansion in the Pacific.
04-06-1943 In Argentina, Juan Peron took part in the military coup that overthrew Ramon S. Castillo.
04-06-1944 The U-505 became the first enemy submarine captured by the U.S. Navy.
04-06-1944 During World War II, the U.S. Fifth Army entered Rome, which began the liberation of the Italian capital.
04-06-1944 "Leonidas Witherall" was first broadcast on the Mutual Broadcasting System.
04-06-1946 Juan Peron was installed as Argentina's president.
04-06-1947 The House of Representatives approved the Taft-Hartley Act. The legislation allowed the President of the United States to intervene in labor disputes.
04-06-1954 French Premier Joseph Laniel and Vietnamese Premier Buu Loc initialed treaties in Paris giving "complete independence" to Vietnam.
04-06-1960 The Taiwan island of Quemoy was hit by 500 artillery shells fired from the coast of Communist China.
04-06-1972 Angela Davis was found not guilty of murder, kidnapping, and criminal conspiracy.
04-06-1974 The Cleveland Indians had "Ten Cent Beer Night". Due to the drunken and unruly fans the Indians forfeited to the Texas Rangers.
04-06-1974 Sally Murphy became the first woman to qualify as an aviator with the U.S. Army.
04-06-1984 For the first time in 32 years, Arnold Palmer failed to make the cut for the U.S. Open golf tournament.
04-06-1985 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld a lower court ruling striking down an Alabama law that provided for a daily minute of silence in public schools.
04-06-1986 Jonathan Jay Pollard, a former Navy intelligence analyst, pled guilty in Washington to spying for Israel. He was sentenced to life in prison.
04-06-1989 645 people were killed in the Soviet Union when a gas explosion engulfed two passing trains.
04-06-1989 In Beijing, Chinese army troops stormed Tiananmen Square to crush the pro-democracy movement. It is believed that hundreds, possibly thousands, of demonstrators were killed.
04-06-1992 The U.S. Postal Service announced that people preferred the "younger Elvis" stamp design in a nationwide vote.
04-06-1998 Terry Nichols received a life sentence for his role in the bombing of an Oklahoma City Federal Building.
04-06-1998 George and Ira Gershwin received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
04-06-2000 Julius "Dr. J" Erving reported his 19-year-old son, Cory, missing. His body was found on July 6, 2000.
04-06-2001 Nepal's King Dipendra died. Three days earlier, he had reportedly shot and killed most members of the royal family before turning the gun on himself.
04-06-2003 Martha Stewart was indicted on federal charges of using illegal privileged information and then obrstructing an investigation. She resigned as chairman and chief executive officer of her company the same day.
04-06-2003 The U.S. House of Representatives passed a bill that would ban "partial birth" abortions with a 282-139 vote.
04-06-2003 Amazon.com announced that it had received more than 1 million orders for the book "Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix." The released date was planned for June 21.
05-06-1595 Henry IV's army defeated the Spanish at the Battle of Fontaine-Francaise.
05-06-1637 American settlers in New England massacred a Pequot Indian village.
05-06-1752 Benjamin Franklin flew a kite for the first time to demonstrate that lightning was a form of electricity.
05-06-1783 A hot-air balloon was demonstrated by Joseph and Jacques Montgolfier. It reached a height of 1,500 feet.
05-06-1794 The U.S. Congress prohibited citizens from serving in any foreign armed forces.
05-06-1827 Athens fell to the Ottomans.
05-06-1851 Harriet Beecher Stow published the first installment of "Uncle Tom's Cabin" in "The National Era."
05-06-1865 The first safe deposit vault was opened in New York. The charge was $1.50 a year for every $1,000 that was stored.
05-06-1884 U.S. Civil War General William T. Sherman refused the Republican presidential nomination, saying, "I will not accept if nominated and will not serve if elected."
05-06-1917 American men began registering for the World War I draft.
05-06-1924 Ernst F. W. Alexanderson transmitted the first facsimile message across the Atlantic Ocean.
05-06-1927 Johnny Weissmuller set two world records in swimming events. Weissmuller set marks in the 100-yard, and 200-yard, free-style swimming competition.
05-06-1933 President Roosevelt signed the bill that took the U.S. off of the gold standard.
05-06-1940 During World War II, the Battle of France began when Germany began an offensive in Southern France.
05-06-1942 In France, Pierre Laval congratulated French volunteers that were fighting in the U.S.S.R. with Germans.
05-06-1944 The first B-29 bombing raid hit the Japanese rail line in Bangkok, Thailand.
05-06-1946 The first medical sponges were first offered for sale in Detroit, MI.
05-06-1947 U.S. Secretary of State George C. Marshall gave a speech at Harvard University in which he outlined the Marshall Plan.
05-06-1956 Premier Nikita Khrushchev denounced Josef Stalin to the Soviet Communist Party Congress.
05-06-1967 The National Hockey League (NHL) awarded three new franchises. The Minnesota North Stars (later the Dallas Stars), the California Golden Seals (no longer in existence) and the Los Angeles Kings.
05-06-1967 The Six Day War between Israel and Egypt, Syria and Jordan began.
05-06-1968 U.S. Senator Robert F. Kennedy was mortally shot in Los Angeles by Sirhan Sirhan. Kennedy died early the next morning.
05-06-1973 The first hole-in-one in the British Amateur golf championship was made by Jim Crowford.
05-06-1975 Egypt reopened the Suez Canal to international shipping, eight years after it was closed because of the 1967 war with Israel.
05-06-1981 In the U.S., the Center for Disease Control and Prevention reported that five men in Los Angeles were suffering from a rare pneumonia found in patients with weakened immune systems. They were the first recognized cases of what came to be known as AIDS.
05-06-1986 A federal jury in Baltimore convicted Ronald W. Pelton of selling secrets to the Soviet Union. Pelton was sentenced to three life prison terms plus 10 years.
05-06-1987 Ted Koppel and guests discussed the topic of AIDS for four hours on ABC-TV’s "Nightline".
05-06-1994 An earthquake in East Java killed 264 people.
05-06-1998 A strike began at a General Motors Corp. parts factory near Detroit, MI, that closed five assembly plants and idled workers across the U.S. for seven weeks.
05-06-1998 Volkswagen AG won approval to buy Rolls-Royce Motor Cars for $700 million, outbidding BMW's $554 million offer.
05-06-1998 C-Span reported that Bob Hope had died. The report was false and had begun with an inaccurate obituary on the Associated Press Web site.
05-06-1998 A strike at a General Motors parts factory began. It lasted for seven weeks.
05-06-2001 Amazon.com announced that it would begin selling personal computers later in the year.
05-06-2004 The U.S.S. Jimmy Carter was christened in the U.S. Navy in Groton, CT.
06-06-1674 Sivaji crowned himself King of India.
06-06-1813 The U.S. invasion of Canada was halted at Stony Creek, Ontario.
06-06-1833 Andrew Jackson became the first U.S. president to ride in a train. It was a B&O passenger train.
06-06-1844 The Young Men's Christian Association was founded in London.
06-06-1865 Confederate raider Wiliam Quantrill died from shot in the back that he received while escaping from a Union patrol near Taylorsville, KY.
06-06-1882 The first electric iron was patented by H.W. Seely.
06-06-1890 The United States Polo Association was formed in New York City, NY.
06-06-1904 The National Tuberculosis Association was formed in Atlantic City, NJ.
06-06-1924 The German Reichtag accepted the Dawes Plan. It was an American plan to help Germany pay off its war debts.
06-06-1925 Chrysler Corporation was founded by Walter Percy Chrysler.
06-06-1932 In the U.S., the first federal tax on gasoline went into effect. It was a penny per gallon.
06-06-1933 In Camden, NJ, the first drive-in movie theater opened.
06-06-1934 U.S. President Franklin Roosevelt signed the Securities Exchange Act, which established the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).
06-06-1936 The first helicopter was tested in a building in Berlin, Germany.
06-06-1941 The U.S. government authorized the seizure of foreign ships in U.S. ports.
06-06-1942 The first nylon parachute jump was made by Adeline Gray in Hartford, CT.
06-06-1942 Japanese forces retreated in the World War II Battle of Midway. The battle had begun on June 4.
06-06-1944 The D-Day invasion of Europe took place on the beaches of Normandy, France. 400,000 Allied American, British and Canadian troops were involved.
06-06-1946 The Basketball Association of America was formed in New York City, NY.
06-06-1966 James Meridith was shot and wounded while on a solo march in Mississippi to promote voter registration among blacks.
06-06-1968 U.S. Senator Robert F. Kennedy died at 1:44am in Los Angeles after being shot by Sirhan Sirhan. Kennedy was was shot the evening before while campaigning for the Democratic presidential nomination.
06-06-1971 "The Ed Sullivan Show" aired for the last time. It was canceled after 23 years on the air. Gladys Knight and the Pips were the musical guests on show.
06-06-1978 "20/20" debuted on ABC.
06-06-1982 Israel invaded southern Lebanon in an effort to drive PLO guerrillas out of Beirut.
06-06-1985 The body of Nazi war criminal Dr. Josef Mengele was located and exhumed near Sao Paolo, Brazil. Mengele was known as the "Angel of Death."
06-06-1993 Mongolia held its first direct presidential elections.
06-06-2001 U.S. District Court Judge Matsch rejected a request to delay the execution of convicted Oklahoma City bomber Timothy McVeigh. The date was left at June 11.
06-06-2005 The United States Supreme Court ruled that federal authorities could prosecute sick people who smoke marijuana on doctor's orders. The ruling concluded that state medical marijuana laws did not protect uses from the federal ban on the drug.
07-06-1494 Spain and Portugal divided the new lands they had discovered between themselves.
07-06-1498 Christopher Columbus left on his third voyage of exploration.
07-06-1546 Peace of Ardes ended the war between France and England.
07-06-1654 Louis XIV was crowned king of France.
07-06-1712 The Pennsylvania Assembly banned the importation of slaves.
07-06-1775 The United Colonies changed their name to the United States.
07-06-1776 Richard Henry Lee of Virginia proposed to the Continental Congress a resolution calling for a Declaration of Independence.
07-06-1863 Mexico City was captured by French troops.
07-06-1892 J.F. Palmer patented the cord bicycle tire.
07-06-1892 John Joseph Doyle became the first pinch-hitter in baseball when he was used in a game.
07-06-1900 Boxer rebels cut the rail links between Peking and Tientsin in China.
07-06-1903 Professor Pierre Curie revealed the discovery of Polonium.
07-06-1909 Mary Pickford made her motion picture debut in "The Violin Maker of Cremona."
07-06-1929 The sovereign state of Vatican City came into existence as copies of the Lateran Treaty were exchanged in Rome.
07-06-1932 Over 7,000 war veterans marched on Washington, DC, demanding their bonuses.
07-06-1935 Pierre Laval received emergency powers to save the franc.
07-06-1937 The cover of "LIFE" magazine showed the latest in campus fashions of the times, which included saddle shoes.
07-06-1939 King George VI and his wife, Queen Elizabeth, arrived in the U.S. It was the first visit to the U.S. by a reigning British monarch.
07-06-1942 The Battle of Midway ended. The sea and air battle lasted 4 days. Japan lost four carriers, a cruiser, and 292 aircraft, and suffered 2,500 casualties. The U.S. lost the Yorktown, the destroyer USS Hammann, 145 aircraft, and suffered 307 casualties.
07-06-1942 Japan landed troops on the islands of Attu and Kiska in the Aleutians. The U.S. invaded and recaptured the Alutians one year later.
07-06-1944 Off of the coast of Normandy, France, the Susan B. Anthony sank. All 2,689 people aboard survived.
07-06-1948 The Communists completed their takeover of Czechoslovakia.
07-06-1955 "The $64,000 Question" premiered.
07-06-1965 Sony Corporation unveiled its brand new consumer home videotape recorder. The black and white only unit sold for $995.
07-06-1965 In the U.S., the Gemini 4 mission was completed. The mission featured the first spacewalk by an American.
07-06-1968 In Operation Swift Saber, U.S. Marines swept an area 10 miles northwest of Danang in South Vietnam.
07-06-1976 "The NBC Nightly News", with John Chancellor and David Brinkley, aired for the first time.
07-06-1981 Israeli F-16 fighter-bombers destroyed Iraq’s only nuclear reactor.
07-06-1993 Woody Allen lost his custody battle against Mia Farrow.
07-06-1994 The United States District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia declared the RMS Titanic, Inc. (RMST) salvor-in-possession of the wreck and the wreck site of the RMS Titanic.
07-06-1998 James Byrd Jr., at age 49, was murdered in Jasper, TX. Byrd had been dragged to death behind a pickup truck. On February 25, 1999 William King was sentenced to the death penalty for the racial crime while two other men charged awaited trial.
07-06-2000 U.S. Federal Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson ordered the breakup of Microsoft Corporation.
07-06-2002 Michael Skakel was convicted of beating his neighbor Martha Moxley to death in 1975. The two were 15 years old at the time.
08-06-0452 Italy was invaded by Attila the Hun.
08-06-0632 The prophet Mohammed died.
08-06-0793 The Vikings raided the Northumbrian coast of England.
08-06-1783 Iceland’s Laki volcano erupted and continued to spew lava for eight months. 9,350 people were killed and famine started and lasted until 1790.
08-06-1786 In New York City, commercial ice cream was manufactured for the first time.
08-06-1790 The first loan for the U.S. was repaid. The Temporary Loan of 1789 was negotiated and secured on September 18, 1789 by Alexander Hamilton.
08-06-1861 Tennessee voted to secede from the Union and joined the Confederacy.
08-06-1866 Prussia annexed the region of Holstein.
08-06-1869 Ives W. McGaffey received a U.S. patent for the suction vacuum cleaner.
08-06-1872 The penny postcard was authorized by the U.S. Congress.
08-06-1904 U.S. Marines landed in Tangiers, Morocco, to protect U.S. citizens.
08-06-1915 U.S. Secretary of State William Jennings Bryan resigned in a disagreement over U.S. handling of the sinking of the Lusitania.
08-06-1947 "Lassie" debuted on ABC radio. It was a 15-minute show.
08-06-1948 Milton Berle hosted "Texaco Star Theater" NBC-TV. It was the show's debut.
08-06-1953 The U.S. Supreme Court outlawed segregated restaurants in Washington, DC.
08-06-1961 The Milwaukee Braves set a major league baseball record when four consecutive home runs in the seventh inning.
08-06-1965 U.S. troops in South Vietnam were given orders to begin fighting offensively.
08-06-1967 Israeli airplanes attacked the USS Liberty in the Mediterranean during the 6-Day War between Israel and its Arab neighbors. 34 U.S. Navy crewmen were killed. Israel later called the incident a tragic mistake due to the mis-identification of the ship. The U.S. has never publicly investigated the incident.
08-06-1968 James Earl Ray was captured at the London Airport. He was suspected of assassinating Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
08-06-1969 The New York Yankees retired Mickey Mantle's number (7).
08-06-1969 It was announced that there would be a single schedule for both the NFL and AFL.
08-06-1969 U.S. President Richard Nixon met with President Thieu of South Vietnam to tell him 25,000 U.S. troops would pull out by August.
08-06-1978 A jury in Clark County, Nevada, ruled that the "Mormon will," was a forgery. The work was supposedly written by Howard Hughes.
08-06-1982 U.S. President Reagan became the first American chief executive to address a joint session of the British Parliament.
08-06-1986 The Boston Celtics won their 16th NBA championship.
08-06-1987 Fawn Hill began testifying in the Iran-Contra hearings. She said that she had helped to shred some documents.
08-06-1991 A victory parade was held in Washington, DC, to honor veterans of the Persian Gulf War.
08-06-1994 The warring factions in Bosnia agreed to a one-month cease-fire.
08-06-1995 U.S. Air Force pilot Captain Scott O'Grady was rescued by U.S. Marines after surviving alone in Bosnia after his F-16 fighter was shot down on June 2.
08-06-1996 China set off an underground nuclear test blast.
08-06-1998 The National Rifle Association elected Charlton Heston to be its president.
08-06-1998 In the U.S., the FTC brought an antitrust complaint against Intel Corp., alleging its policies punished other developers of microprocessor chips.
08-06-1998 Honda agreed to pay $17.1 million for disconnecting anti-pollution devices in 1.6 million cars.
08-06-1998 The space shuttle Discovery pulled away from Mir, ending America's three-year partnership with Russia.
08-06-2000 The Dallas Stars and the New Jersey Devils played the NHL's longest scoreless game in Stanley Cup finals history. The fifth game of the series lasted 106 minutes and 21 seconds. The game ended with a goal by Mike Madano that allowed the Stars to play a game six back in Dallas.
08-06-2001 Marc Chagall's painting "Study for 'Over Vitebsk" was stolen from the Jewish Museum in New York City. The 8x10 painting was valued at about $1 million. A group called the International Committee for Art and Peace later announced that they would return the painting after the Israelis and Palestinians made peace.
08-06-2001 In Japan, a knife-wielding man murdered eight children at an elementary school.
08-06-2004 Nate Olive and Sarah Jones began the first known continuous hike of the 1,800-mile trail down the U.S. Pacific Coast. They completed the trek at the U.S.-Mexico border on September 28.
09-06-1064 Coimbra, Portugal fell to Ferdinand, the King of Castile.
09-06-1534 Jacques Cartier became the first to sail into the river he named Saint Lawrence.
09-06-1790 John Barry copyrighted "Philadelphia Spelling Book." It was the first American book to be copyrighted.
09-06-1790 Civil war broke out in Martinique.
09-06-1860 The book, "Malaeska, the Indian Wife of the White Hunter" by Mrs. Ann Stevens, was offered for sale for a dime. It was the first published "dime novel."
09-06-1861 Mary Ann "Mother" Bickerdyke began working in Union hospitals.
09-06-1923 Bulgaria’s government was overthrown by the military.
09-06-1931 Robert H. Goddard patented a rocket-fueled aircraft design.
09-06-1934 Donald Duck made his debut in the Silly Symphonies cartoon "The Wise Little Hen."
09-06-1940 Norway surrendered to the Nazis during World War II.
09-06-1943 The withholding tax on payrolls was authorized by the U.S. Congress.
09-06-1945 Japanese Premier Kantaro Suzuki declared that Japan would fight to the last rather than accept unconditional surrender.
09-06-1946 Mel Ott (with the New York Giants) became the first manager to be ejected from a doubleheader (both games).
09-06-1953 A tornado struck Worcester, Massachusetts, killing about 100 people.
09-06-1959 The first ballistic missile carrying submarine, the USS George Washington, was launched.
09-06-1965 Michel Jazy ran the mile in 3 minutes, 53.6 seconds. He broke the record set by Peter Snell in 1964.
09-06-1972 American advisor John Paul Vann was killed in a helicopter accident in Vietnam.
09-06-1978 Leaders of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints struck down a 148-year-old policy of excluding black men from the Mormon priesthood.
09-06-1980 Richard Pryor was severely burned by a "free-base" mixture that exploded. He was hospitalized more than two months.
09-06-1985 Thomas Sutherland, an American educator, was kidnapped in Lebanon. He was not released until November 1991.
09-06-1985 The Los Angeles Lakers won the NBA title by defeating the Boston Celtics.
09-06-1986 The Rogers Commission released a report on the Challenger disaster. The report explained that the spacecraft blew up as a result of a failure in a solid rocket booster joint.
09-06-1998 In Jasper, TX, three white men were charged in the dragging death of African-American James Byrd Jr.
09-06-1999 NATO and Yugoslavia signed a peace agreement over Kosovo.
09-06-2000 The U.S. Justice Department announced that it had not uncovered reliable evidence of conspiracy behind 1968 assissination of Martin Luther King Jr.
09-06-2000 Canada and the United States signed a border security agreement. The agreement called for the establishment of a border-enforcement team.
09-06-2000 The U.S. House of Representatives voted to repeal gift and estate taxes. The bill called for the taxes to be phased out over 10 years.
09-06-2001 Patrick Roy (Colorado Avalanche) became the first National Hockey League (NHL) player to win three Conn Smythe Trophies. The award is given to the playoff's Most Valuable Player.
10-06-1190 Roman Emperor Frederick I Barbarossa drowned in the Saleph River while leading an army of the Third Crusade to free Jerusalem.
10-06-1776 The Continental Congress appointed a committee to write a Declaration of Independence.
10-06-1793 The Jardin des Plantes zoo opened in Paris. It was the first public zoo.
10-06-1801 The North African State of Tripoli declared war on the U.S. The dispute was over merchant vessels being able to travel safely through the Mediterranean.
10-06-1806 New York's "Commercial Advertiser" became the first U.S. newspapter to cover the sport of harness racing.
10-06-1854 The U.S. Naval Academy in Annapolis, MD, held its first graduation.
10-06-1889 Hattie McDaniel was born. She, for her role in "Gone With the Wind," was the first African-American to win an Academy Award.
10-06-1898 U.S. Marines landed in Cuba during the Spanish-American War.
10-06-1902 The "outlook" or "see-through" envelope was patented by Americus F. Callahan.
10-06-1909 The SOS distress signal was used for the first time. The Cunard liner SS Slavonia used the signal when it wrecked off the Azores.
10-06-1916 Mecca, under control of the Turks, fell to the Arabs during the Great Arab Revolt.
10-06-1920 The Republican convention in Chicago endorsed woman suffrage.
10-06-1924 The Italian socialist leader Giacomo Matteotti was kidnapped and murdered by Fascists in Rome.
10-06-1924 The Republican National Convention was broadcast by NBC radio. It was the first political convention to be on radio.
10-06-1925 The state of Tennessee adopted a new biology text book that denied the theory of evolution.
10-06-1935 Alcoholic Anonymous was founded by William G. Wilson and Dr. Robert Smith.
10-06-1940 Italy declared war on France and Britain. In addition, Canada declared war on Italy.
10-06-1942 The Gestapo massacred 173 male residents of Lidice, Czechoslovakia, in retaliation for the killing of a Nazi official.
10-06-1943 Laszlo Biro patented his ballpoint pen. Biro was a Hungarian journalist.
10-06-1943 The Allies began bombing Germany around the clock.
10-06-1944 The youngest pitcher in major league baseball pitched his first game. Joe Nuxhall was 15 years old (and 10 months and 11 days).
10-06-1946 Italy established a republic replacing its monarchy.
10-06-1948 Chuck Yeager exceeded the speed of sound in the Bell XS-1.
10-06-1954 General Motors announced the gas turbine bus had been produced successfully.
10-06-1967 Israel and Syria agreed to a cease-fire that ended the Six-Day War.
10-06-1970 A fifteen-man group of special forces troops began training for Operation Kingpin. The operation was a POW rescue mission in North Vietnam.
10-06-1971 The U.S. ended a 21-year trade embargo of China.
10-06-1977 James Earl Ray escaped with 6 others from Brushy Mountain State Prison in Tennessee. Ray was recaptured June 13, 1977.
10-06-1983 Johnny Bench announced his plans to retire. He was a catcher in the major leagues for 16 years.
10-06-1984 The U.S. Army successfully tested an antiballistic missile.
10-06-1985 Frank Sinatra was portrayed as a friend of organized crime in a "Doonesbury" comic strip. Over 800 newspapers carried the panel.
10-06-1985 The Israeli army pulled out of Lebanon after 1,099 days of occupation.
10-06-1987 An earthquake hit 15 states from Iowa to South Carolina.
10-06-1988 Author Louis L'Amour died at age 80.
10-06-1990 The Civic Forum movement won Czechoslovakia's first free elections since 1946. The movement was founded by President Vaclav Havel.
10-06-1990 Bulgaria's former Communist Party won the country's first free elections in more than four decades.
10-06-1993 It was announced by scientists that genetic material was extracted from an insect that lived when dinosaurs roamed the Earth.
10-06-1994 U.S. President Clinton intensified sanctions against Haiti's military leaders. U.S. commercial air travel was suspended along with most financial transactions between Haiti and the U.S.
10-06-1995 26 people were killed in Medellin, Columbia, by a bomb blast that was blamed on drug traffickers.
10-06-1996 The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Florida Panthers in a 1-0 triple overtime game. The win ended a four-game sweep for the Stanley Cup.
10-06-1996 Britain and Ireland opened Northern Ireland peace talks. The IRA's political arm Sinn Fein was excluded.
10-06-1997 Khmer Rouge leader Pol Pot killed his defense chief Son Sen and 11 members of his family. He then fled his northern stronghold. The news did not emerge for three days.
10-06-1998 The Wisconsin Supreme Court ruled that poor children in Milwaukee could attend religious schools at taxpayer expense.
10-06-1999 NATO suspended air strikes in Yugoslavia after Slobodan Milosevic agreed to withdraw his forces from Kosovo.
11-06-1346 Charles IV of Luxembourg was elected Holy Roman Emperor in Germany.
11-06-1488 James III of Scotland was murdered after his defeat at the Battle of Sauchieburn, Stirling. He was succeeded by his son James IV.
11-06-1509 King Henry VIII married his first of six wives, Catherine of Aragon.
11-06-1770 Captain James Cook discovered the Great Barrier Reef off of Australia when he ran aground.
11-06-1776 In America, the Continental Congress formed a committee to draft a Declaration of Independence from Britain.
11-06-1793 Robert Haeterick was issued the first patent for a stove.
11-06-1798 Napoleon Bonaparte took the island of Malta.
11-06-1847 Sir John Franklin died in Canada while attempting to discover the Northwest Passage. Franklin was an English naval officer and an Arctic explorer.
11-06-1880 Jeanette Rankin was born. She became the first woman elected to the U.S. Congress.
11-06-1889 The Washington Business High School opened in Washington, DC. It was the first school devoted to business in the U.S.
11-06-1895 Charles E. Duryea received the first U.S. patent granted to an American inventor for a gasoline-driven automobile.
11-06-1903 King Alexander and Queen Draga of Serbia were murdered in a coup by members of the Serbian army.
11-06-1910 Jacques-Yves Cousteau was born. He was the French underwater explorer that invented the Aqua-Lung diving apparatus.
11-06-1912 Silas Christoferson became the first pilot to take off from the roof of a hotel.
11-06-1915 British troops took Cameroon in Africa.
11-06-1919 Sir Barton became the first horse to capture the Triple Crown when he won the Belmont Stakes in New York City.
11-06-1927 Charles A. Lindberg was presented the first Distinguished Flying Cross.
11-06-1930 William Beebe dove to a record-setting depth of 1,426 feet off the coast of Bermuda. He used a diving chamber called a bathysphere.
11-06-1934 The Disarmament Conference in Geneva ended in failure.
11-06-1936 The Presbyterian Church of America was formed in Philadelphia, PA.
11-06-1937 Soviet leader Josef Stalin began a purge of Red Army generals.
11-06-1940 The Italian Air Force bombed the British fortress at Malta in the Mediterranean.
11-06-1942 The U.S. and the Soviet Union signed a lend lease agreement to aid the Soviets in their effort in World War II.
11-06-1943 During World War II, the Italian island of Pantelleria surrendered after a heavy air bombardment.
11-06-1947 The U.S. government announced an end sugar rationing.
11-06-1950 Ben Hogan returned to tournament play after a near fatal car accident. He won the U.S. Open.
11-06-1955 In France, 80 people were killed and more than 100 were injured when three cars crashed on the Le Mans racetrack. The cars had ploughed into the spectator's grandstand.
11-06-1963 Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. was arrested in Florida for trying to integrate restaurants.
11-06-1963 Buddhist monk Quang Duc immolated himself on a Saigon street to protest the government of South Vietnamese President Ngo Dinh Diem.
11-06-1963 Alabama Gov. George Wallace allowed two black students to enroll at the University of Alabama.
11-06-1967 Israel and Syria accepted a U.N. cease-fire.
11-06-1972 Hank Aaron tied the National League record for 14 grand-slam home runs in a career.
11-06-1973 After a ruling by the Justice Department of the State of Pennsylvania, women were licensed to box or wrestle.
11-06-1977 In the Netherlands, a 19-day hostage situation came to an end when Dutch marines stormed a train and a school being held by South Moluccan extremist. Two hostages and the six terrorists were killed.
11-06-1981 The first major league baseball player's strike began. It would last for two months.
11-06-1981 In Iran, more than 1,000 people were killed in an earthquake that measured 6.8 on the Richter ScaleRichter Scale. The town of Golbaf in the Kermin province was destroyed.
11-06-1982 Steven Spielberg's movie "E.T." opened.
11-06-1985 Karen Ann Quinlan died at age 31. Quinlan was a comatose patient whose case prompted a historic right-to-die court decision.
11-06-1987 Margaret Thatcher became the first British prime minister in 160 years to win a third consecutive term of office.
11-06-1990 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down a law that would prohibit the desecration of the American Flag.
11-06-1991 Mount Pinatubo in the Philippines erupted. The eruption of ash and gas could be seen for more than 60 miles.
11-06-1993 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that people who commit "hate crimes" could be sentenced to extra punishment. The court also ruled in favor of religious groups saying that they indeed had a constitutional right to sacrifice animals during worship services.
11-06-1993 Steven Spielberg's movie "Jurassic Park" opened.
11-06-1994 A car bomb blew up in Guadalajara, Mexico killing five people. The bombing was believed to be drug related.
11-06-1998 Mitsubishi of America agreed to pay $34 million to end the largest sexual harassment case filed by the U.S. government. The federal lawsuit claimed that hundreds of women at a plant in Normal, IL, had endured groping and crude jokes from male workers.
11-06-1998 Pakistan announced moratorium on nuclear testing and offered to talk with India over disputed Kashmir.
11-06-2001 Timothy McVeigh was executed by the U.S. federal government for his role in the bombing of a federal building in Oklahoma City.
12-06-1099 Crusade leaders visited the Mount of Olives where they met a hermit who urged them to assault Jerusalem.
12-06-1442 Alfonso V of Aragon was crowned King of Naples.
12-06-1665 England installed a municipal government in New York. The was the former Dutch settlement of New Amsterdam.
12-06-1667 The first human blood transfusion was administered by Dr. Jean Baptiste. He successfully transfused the blood of a sheep to a 15-year old boy.
12-06-1812 Napoleon's invasion of Russia began.
12-06-1838 The Iowa Territory was organized.
12-06-1839 Abner Doubleday created the game of baseball, according to the legend. However, evidence has surfaced that indicates that the game of baseball was played before 1800.
12-06-1849 The gas mask was patented by L.P. Haslett.
12-06-1897 Carl Elsener patented his penknife. The object later became known as the Swiss army knife.
12-06-1898 Philippine nationalists declared their independence from Spain.
12-06-1900 The Reichstag approved a second law that would allow the expansion of the German navy.
12-06-1901 Cuba agreed to become an American protectorate by accepting the Platt Amendment.
12-06-1912 Lillian Russel retired from the stage and was married for the fourth time.
12-06-1918 The first airplane bombing raid by an American unit occurred on World War I's Western Front in France.
12-06-1921 U.S. President Warren Harding urged every young man to attend military training camp.
12-06-1923 Harry Houdini, while suspended upside down 40 feet above the ground, escaped from a strait jacket.
12-06-1926 Brazil quit the League of Nations in protest over plans to admit Germany.
12-06-1929 Anne Frank was born in Germany. She wrote in her diary about growing up in occupied Amsterdam during World War II. She died in the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp in March 1945.
12-06-1931 Al Capone and 68 of his henchmen were indicted for violating U.S. Prohibition laws.
12-06-1935 U.S. Senator Huey Long of Louisiana made the longest speech on Senate record. The speech took 15 1/2 hours and was filled by 150,000 words.
12-06-1935 The Chaco War was ended with a truce. Bolivia and Paraguay had been fighting since 1932.
12-06-1937 The Soviet Union executed eight army leaders under Joseph Stalin.
12-06-1939 The National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum was dedicated in Cooperstown, New York. This was exactly one hundred years to the day on which the game was invented by Abner Doubleday.
12-06-1941 In London, the Inter-Allied Declaration was signed. It was the first step towards the establishment of the United Nations.
12-06-1944 Chinese Communist leader Mao Tse-tung announced that he would support Nationalist leader Chiang Kai-shek in the war against Japan.
12-06-1948 Ben Hogan won his first U.S. Open golf classic.
12-06-1963 "Cleopatra" starring Elizabeth Taylor, Rex Harrison, and Richard Burton premiered at the Rivoli Theatre in New York City.
12-06-1963 Civil rights leader Medgar Evers was fatally shot in front of his home in Jackson, MS.
12-06-1967 State laws which prohibited interracial marriages were ruled unconstitutional by the U.S. Supreme Court.
12-06-1971 Tricia Nixon and Edward F. Cox were married in the White House Rose Garden.
12-06-1975 Indian Prime Minister Indira Gandhi was found guilty of corrupt election practices in 1971.
12-06-1978 David Berkowitz, the "Son of Sam" killer in New York, was sentenced to 25 years to life in prison for six killings.
12-06-1979 Bryan Allen flew the Gossamer Albatross, man powered, across the English Channel.
12-06-1981 Major league baseball players began a 49 day strike. The issue was free-agent compensation.
12-06-1982 75,000 people rallied against nuclear weapons in New York City's Central Park. Jackson Browne, James Taylor, Bruce Springsteen, and Linda Ronstadt were in attendance.
12-06-1985 Wayne "The Great One" Gretsky was named winner of the NHL's Hart Trophy. The award is given to the the league Most Valuable Player.
12-06-1985 The U.S. House of Representatives approved $27 million in aid to the Nicaraguan contras.
12-06-1986 South Africa declared a national state of emergency. Virtually unlimited power was given to security forces and restrictions were put on news coverage of the unrest.
12-06-1987 Central African Republic's former emperor Jean-Bedel Bokassa was sentenced to death for crimes he had committed during his 13-year rule.
12-06-1987 U.S. President Reagan publicly challenged Mikhail Gorbachev to tear down the Berlin Wall.
12-06-1990 The parliament of the Russian Federation formally declared its sovereignty.
12-06-1991 Russians went to the election polls and elected Boris N. Yeltsin as the president of their republic.
12-06-1991 The Chicago Bulls won their first NBA championship. The Bulls beat the Los Angeles Lakers four games to one.
12-06-1992 In a letter to the U.S. Senate, Russian Boris Yeltsin stated that in the early 1950's the Soviet Union had shot down nine U.S. planes and held 12 American survivors.
12-06-1994 Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald Goldman were murdered outside her home in Los Angeles. O.J. Simpson was later acquitted of the killings, but he was held liable in a civil suit.
12-06-1996 In Philadelphia a panel of federal judges blocked a law against indecency on the internet. The panel said that the 1996 Communications Decency Act would infringe upon the free speech rights of adults.
12-06-1997 Interleague play began in baseball, ending a 126-year tradition of separating the major leagues until the World Series.
12-06-1997 The U.S. Treasury Department unveiled a new $50 bill meant to be more counterfeit-resistant.
12-06-1998 Compaq Computer paid $9 billion for Digital Equipment Corp. in largest high-tech acquisition.
12-06-1998 A jury in Hattiesburg, MS, convicted 17-year-old Luke Woodham of killing two students and wounding seven others at Pearl High School.
12-06-1999 NATO peacekeeping forces entered the province of Kosovo in Yugoslavia.
12-06-2003 In Arkansas, Terry Wallis spoke for the first time in nearly 19 years. Wallis had been in a coma since July 13, 1984, after being injured in a car accident.
13-06-1415 Henry the Navigator, the prince of Portugal, embarked on an expedition to Africa.
13-06-1777 The Marquis de Lafayette arrived in the American colonies to help with their rebellion against the British.
13-06-1789 Ice cream was served to General George Washington by Mrs. Alexander Hamilton.
13-06-1825 Walter Hunt patented the safety pin. Hunt then then sold the rights for $400.
13-06-1866 The 14th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was passed by the U.S. Congress. It was ratified on July 9, 1868. The amendment was designed to grant citizenship to and protect the civil liberties of recently freed slaves. It did this by prohibiting states from denying or abridging the privileges or immunities of citizens of the United States, depriving any person of his life, liberty, or property without due process of law, or denying to any person within their jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws.
13-06-1886 King Ludwig II of Bavaria drowned in Lake Starnberg.
13-06-1888 The U.S. Congress created the Department of Labor.
13-06-1898 The Canadian Yukon Territory was organized.
13-06-1900 China's Boxer Rebellion against foreigners and Chinese Christians erupted into violence.
13-06-1912 Captain Albert Berry made the first successful parachute jump from an airplane in Jefferson, Mississippi.
13-06-1913 Ralph Edwards, the host of "This is Your Life" and "Truth or Consequences" was born. Ronald Reagan was the only person to ever substitute for him.
13-06-1920 The U.S. Post Office Department ruled that children may not be sent by parcel post.
13-06-1922 Charlie Osborne started the longest attack on hiccups. He hiccuped over 435 million times before stopping. He died in 1991, 11 months after his hiccups ended.
13-06-1923 The French set a trade barrier between the occupied Ruhr and the rest of Germany.
13-06-1927 Charles Lindbergh was honored with a ticker-tape parade in New York City.
13-06-1927 For the first time an American Flag was displayed from the right hand of the Statue of Liberty.
13-06-1940 Paris was evacuated before the German advance on the city.
13-06-1943 German spies landed on Long Island, New York. They were soon captured.
13-06-1944 Germany launched 10 of its new V1 rockets against Britain from a position near the Channel coast. Of the 10 rockets only 5 landed in Britain and only one managed to kill (6 people in London).
13-06-1944 Marvin Camras patented the wire recorder.
13-06-1949 Bao Dai entered Saigon to rule Vietnam. He had been installed by the French.
13-06-1951 U.N. troops seized Pyongyang, North Korea.
13-06-1966 The landmark "Miranda vs. Arizona" decision was issued by the U.S. Supreme Court. The decision ruled that criminal suspects had to be informed of their constitutional rights before being questioned by police.
13-06-1967 Solicitor General Thurgood Marshall was nominated by President Lyndon B. Johnson to become the first black justice on the U.S. Supreme Court.
13-06-1971 The New York Times began publishing the "Pentagon Papers". The articles were a secret study of America's involvement in Vietnam.
13-06-1977 James Earl Ray was recaptured after his escape from prison 3 days earlier.
13-06-1978 Israelis withdrew the last of their invading forces from Lebanon.
13-06-1979 Sioux Indians were awarded $105 million in compensation for the U.S. seizure in 1877 of their Black Hills in South Dakota.
13-06-1981 At a parade in London a teen-ager fired six-blank shots at Queen Elizabeth II.
13-06-1983 The unmanned U.S. space probe Pioneer 10 became the first spacecraft to leave the solar system. It was launched in March 1972. The first up-close images of the planet Jupiter were provided by Pioneer 10.
13-06-1988 The Ligger Group, a cigarette manufacturer, was found liable for a lung-cancer death. They were, however, found innocent by the federal jury of misrepresenting the risks of smoking.
13-06-1989 The Detroit Pistons won their first National Basketball Association title. They beat the L.A. Lakers in four games.
13-06-1989 U.S. President George Bush exercised his first Presidential veto on a bill dealing with minimum wage.
13-06-1991 In the first round of the U.S. Open golf tournament a spectator was killed when lightning struck.
13-06-1992 Future U.S. President Bill Clinton criticized rap singer Sister Souljah for making remarks "filled with hatred" towards whites.
13-06-1994 A jury in Anchorage, Alaska, found Exxon Corp. and Captain Joseph Hazelwood to be reckless in the Exxon Valdez oil spill.
13-06-1994 O.J. Simpson was questioned by Los Angeles police concerning the deaths of his ex-wife and her friend, Ronald Goldman.
13-06-1995 France announced that they would conduct eight more nuclear tests in the South Pacific.
13-06-1996 In Montana, the 81-day standoff between the Freemen and the FBI ended when the anti-government group surrendered.
13-06-1997 The same Denver jury that convicted Timothy McVeigh of the 1995 bombing of a federal building in Oklahama City recommended the death penalty for his crime.
13-06-2000 Julius "Dr. J." Erving issued a public appeal for help finding his 19-year-old son, Cory. Cory had been missing since May 28, 2000. His body was found July 6, 2000.
13-06-2000 In Pyongyang, North Korea's leader Kim Jong Il welcomed South Korea's President Kim Dae for a three-day summit. It was the first such meeting between the leaders of North and South Korea.
14-06-1381 The Peasant’s Revolt, led by Wat Tyler, climaxed when rebels marched on London. They plundered, burned and captured the Tower of London and killed the Archbishop of Canterbury. The revolt was in response to a statute intended to hold down wages during a labor shortage.
14-06-1775 The Continental Army was founded by the Continental Congress for purposes of common defense. This event is considered to be the birth of the United States Army. On June 15, George Washington was appointed commander-in-chief.
14-06-1777 The Continental Congress in Philadelphia adopted the "Stars and Stripes" as the national flag of the United States.
14-06-1789 Captain William Bligh of the HMS Bounty arrived in Timor in a small boat.
14-06-1834 Cyrus Hall McCormick received a patent for his reaping machine.
14-06-1834 Isaac Fischer Jr. patented sandpaper.
14-06-1841 The first Canadian parliament opened in Kingston.
14-06-1846 A group of U.S. settlers in Sonoma proclaimed the Republic of California.
14-06-1864 Alois Alzheimer was born. He was a psychiatrist/pathologist, and in 1907 he wrote an article describing the disease that is named for him.
14-06-1893 Philadelphia observed the first Flag Day.
14-06-1900 Hawaii became a U.S. territory.
14-06-1907 Women in Norway won the right to vote.
14-06-1917 General John Pershing arrived in Paris during World War I.
14-06-1919 The first non-stop trans-Atlantic flight began. Captain John Alcot and Lt. Arthur Brown flew from Newfoundland to Ireland.
14-06-1922 Warren G. Harding became the first U.S. president to be heard on radio. The event was the dedication of the Francis Scott Key memorial at Fort McHenry.
14-06-1927 Nicaraguan President Adolfo Diaz signed a treaty with the U.S. allowing American intervention in his country.
14-06-1932 U.S. Representative Edward Eslick died on the floor of the House of Representatives while pleading for the passage of the bonus bill.
14-06-1940 The Nazis opened their concentration camp at Auschwitz in German-occupied Poland.
14-06-1940 German troops entered Paris. As Paris became occupied loud speakers announced the implementation of a curfew being imposed for 8 p.m.
14-06-1943 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that schoolchildren could not be made to salute the U.S. flag if doing so conflicted with their religious beliefs.
14-06-1944 Sixty U.S. B-29 Superfortress' attacked an iron and steel works factory on Honshu Island. It was the first U.S. raid against mainland Japan.
14-06-1945 Burma was liberated by Britain.
14-06-1949 The state of Vietnam was formed.
14-06-1951 "Univac I" was unveiled. It was a computer designed for the U.S. Census Bureau and billed as the world's first commercial computer.
14-06-1952 The Nautilus was dedicated. It was the first nuclear powered submarine.
14-06-1954 U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower signed an order adding the words "under God" to the Pledge of Allegiance.
14-06-1954 Americans took part in the first nation-wide civil defense test against atomic attack.
14-06-1965 A military triumvirate took control in Saigon, South Vietnam.
14-06-1967 Mariner 5 was launched from Cape Kennedy, FL. The space probe's flight took it past Venus.
14-06-1982 Argentine forces surrendered to British troops on the Falkland Islands.
14-06-1983 Five people were killed in a wing of a Ramada Inn in Fort Worth, TX. The fire began in stacked rolls of carpet and padding, the fumes that ensued were toxic.
14-06-1985 The 17-day hijacking of TWA flight 847 began. The hijackers were Lebanese Shiite Muslim extremists.
14-06-1987 The Los Angeles Lakers won the NBA title by defeating the defending Boston Celtics.
14-06-1989 Former U.S. President Reagan received an honorary knighthood from Britain's Queen Elizabeth II.
14-06-1989 Zsa Zsa Gabor was arrested in Beverly Hills for slapping a motorcycle policeman.
14-06-1990 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld police checkpoints that are used to examine drivers for signs of intoxication.
14-06-1992 The Chicago Bulls won the NBA championship beating the Portland Trailblazers.
14-06-1994 The New York Rangers won the Stanley Cup by defeating the Vancouver Canucks. It was the first time the Rangers had won the cup in 54 years.
14-06-1995 Chechen rebels took 2,000 people hostage in a hospital in Russia.
14-06-1996 The FBI released that the White House had done bureau background reports on at leat 408 people without justification.
14-06-2002 Twelve people were killed and 50 were injured when a car bomb was used to attack the U.S. consulate in Karachi, Pakistan.
14-06-2002 Actor Kirk Douglas received the UCLA Medal. The award is presented to people for cultural, political and humanitarian achievements.
15-06-1215 King John of England put his seal on the Magna Carta.
15-06-1381 The English peasant revolt was crushed in London.
15-06-1389 Ottoman Turks crushed Serbia in the Battle of Kosovo.
15-06-1607 Colonists in North America completed James Fort in Jamestown.
15-06-1752 Benjamin Franklin experimented by flying a kite during a thunderstorm. The result was a little spark that showed the relationship between lightning and electricity.
15-06-1775 George Washington was appointed head of the Continental Army by the Second Continental Congress.
15-06-1836 Arkansas became the 25th U.S. state.
15-06-1844 Charles Goodyear was granted a patent for the process that strengthens rubber.
15-06-1846 The United States and Britain settled a boundary dispute concerning the boundary between the U.S. and Canada, by signing a treaty.
15-06-1864 An order to establish a military burial ground was signed by Secretary of War Edwin M. Stanton. The location later became known as Arlington National Cemetery.
15-06-1866 Prussia attacked Austria.
15-06-1877 Henry O. Flipper became the first African American to graduate from the U.S. Military Academy at West Point.
15-06-1898 The U.S. House of representatives approved the annexation of Hawaii.
15-06-1904 The steamboat General Slocum erupted in fire killing more than 1,000 in New York City's East River.
15-06-1909 Benjamin Shibe patented the cork center baseball.
15-06-1911 The Computing-Tabulating-Recording Co. was incorporated in the state of New York. The company was later renamed International Business Machines (IBM) Corp.
15-06-1916 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson signed a bill incorporating the Boy Scouts of America.
15-06-1917 Great Britain pledged the release of all the Irish captured during the Easter Rebellion of 1916.
15-06-1919 Captain John Alcock and Lt. Arthur W. Brown won $50,000 for successfully completing the first, non-stop trans-Atlantic plane flight.
15-06-1932 Gaston Means was sentenced to 15 years for fraud in the Lindbergh baby kidnapping.
15-06-1938 Johnny Vandemeer, of the Cincinnati Reds, pitched his second straight no-hitter.
15-06-1940 The French fortress of Verdun was captured by Germans.
15-06-1944 American forces began their successful invasion of Saipan during World War II.
15-06-1947 The All-Indian Congress accepted a British plan for the partition of India.
15-06-1958 Greece severed military ties to Turkey because of the Cypress issue.
15-06-1964 The last French troops left Algeria.
15-06-1978 King Hussein of Jordan married 26-year-old American Lisa Halaby, who became Queen Noor.
15-06-1983 The U.S. Supreme Court reinforced its position on abortion by striking down state and local restriction on abortions.
15-06-1985 U.S. Navy diver Robert D. Stethem was killed by the hijackers of Flight 847.
15-06-1986 Pravda, the Communist Party newspaper, reported that the chief engineer of the Chernobyl nuclear plant was dismissed for mishandling the incident at the plant.
15-06-1989 In Shanghai three Chinese workers were sentenced to death for setting fire to a train during a pro-democracy protest.
15-06-1992 It was ruled by the U.S. Supreme Court that the government could kidnap criminal suspects from foreign countries for prosecution.
15-06-1992 U.S. Vice President Dan Quayle instructed a student to spell "potato" with an "e" on the end during a spelling bee. He had relied on a faulty flash card that had been written by the student's teacher.
15-06-1994 Israel and the Vatican established full diplomatic relations.
15-06-1995 During the O.J. Simpson murder trial, O.J. was asked to put on a pair of gloves. The gloves were said to have been worn by the killer on the night of the murders of Nicole Brown and Ronald Goldman. The gloves appeared not to fit.
15-06-1996 The Irish Republican Army set of a truck bomb in a retail district in Manchester England. The explosion wounded more than 200 people.
15-06-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that state prison inmates are protected by the Americans with Disabilities Act.
15-06-1999 South Korean naval forces sank a North Korean torpedo boat during an exchange in the disputed Yellow Sea.
15-06-2003 In northeast London, a trailer was stolen that contained thousands of copies of J.K. Rowling's book "Harry Potter And The Order Of The Phoenix." The empty trailer was discovered two days later.
15-06-2006 The U.S. Supreme Court said that judges cannot throw out evidence collected by police who have search warrants but do not properly announced their arrival.
16-06-0455 Rome was sacked by the Vandal army.
16-06-1487 The War of the Roses ended with the Battle of Stoke.
16-06-1567 Mary, Queen of Scots, was imprisoned in Lochleven Castle in Scotland.
16-06-1815 Napoleon defeated the Prussians at the Battle of Ligny, Netherlands.
16-06-1858 In a speech in Springfield, IL, U.S. Senate candidate Abraham Lincoln said the slavery issue had to be resolved. He declared, "A house divided against itself cannot stand."
16-06-1890 The second Madison Square Gardens opened.
16-06-1883 The New York Giants baseball team admitted all ladies for free to the ballpark. It was the first Ladies Day.
16-06-1897 The U.S. government signed a treaty of annexation with Hawaii.
16-06-1903 Ford Motor Company was incorporated.
16-06-1904 The novel "Ulysses" by James Joyce took place. The main character of the book was Leopold Bloom.
16-06-1907 The Russian czar dissolved the Duma in St. Petersburg.
16-06-1909 Glenn Hammond Curtiss sold his first airplane, the "Gold Bug" to the New York Aeronautical Society for $5,000.
16-06-1910 The first Father's Day was celebrated in Spokane, Washington.
16-06-1922 Henry Berliner accomplished the first helicopter flight at College Park, MD.
16-06-1925 France accepted a German proposal for a security pact.
16-06-1932 The ban on Nazi storm troopers was lifted by the von Papen government in Germany.
16-06-1940 Marshal Henri-Philippe Petain became the prime minister of the Vichy government of occupied France.
16-06-1941 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt orded the closure of all German consulates in the United States. The deadline was set as July 10.
16-06-1952 "My Little Margie" debuted on CBS-TV.
16-06-1952 "Anne Frank: Diary of a Young Girl" was published in the United States.
16-06-1952 A Swedish rescue plane was shot down by Soviet fighters over Swedish territorial waters. The rescue plane was searching for a lost aircraft.
16-06-1955 The U.S. House of Representatives voted to extend Selective Service until 1959.
16-06-1955 Pope Pius XII excommunicated Argentine President Juan Peron. The ban was lifted eight years later.
16-06-1955 Argentine naval officers launched an attack on President Juan Peron's headquarters. The revolt was suppressed by the army.
16-06-1958 Hungarian prime minister Imre Nagy was hanged for treason. He had been the prime minister during the 1956 uprising that was crushed by Soviet tanks.
16-06-1961 Rudolf Nureyev defected from the Soviet Union while in Paris, traveling with the Leningrad Kirov Ballet.
16-06-1963 26-year-old Valentina Tereshkova went into orbit aboard the Vostok 6 spacecraft for three days. She was the first female space traveler.
16-06-1971 An El Greco sketch, "The Immaculate Conception," was recovered in New York City by the FBI. The work had been stolen 35 years earlier.
16-06-1972 Ulrike Meinhof was captured by West German police in Hanover. He was co-founder of the Baader-Meinhof terrorist group.
16-06-1975 The Simonstown agreement on naval cooperation between Britain and South Africa ended. The agreement was formally ended by mutual agreement after 169 years.
16-06-1976 In Soweto, thousands of school children revolted against the South African government's plan to enforce Afrikaans as the language for instruction in black schools.
16-06-1977 Leonid Brezhnev was named the first Soviet president of the USSR. He was the first person to hold the post of president and Communist Party General Secretary. He replaced Nikolai Podgorny.
16-06-1978 U.S. President Carter and Panamanian leader Omar Torrijos ratified the Panama Canal treaties.
16-06-1978 The film adaptation of "Grease" premiered in New York City.
16-06-1979 General Ignatius Kutu Acheampong was executed for corruption. He was the former military ruler of Ghana from 1972-1978.
16-06-1980 The movie "The Blues Brothers" opened in Chicago, IL.
16-06-1981 The "Chicago Tribune" purchased the Chicago Cubs baseball team from the P.K. Wrigley Chewing Gum Company for $20.5 million.
16-06-1983 Yuri Andropov was elected chairman of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet. The position was the equivalent of president.
16-06-1984 Wilson Ferreira Aldunate was arrested upon his return from an eleven year exile. Aldunate had been a popular Uruguayan opposition leader.
16-06-1985 Willie Banks broke the world record for the triple jump with a leap of 58 feet, 11-1/2 inches in the U.S.A. championships in Indianapolis, IN.
16-06-1987 A jury in New York acquitted Bernhard Goetz of attempted murder in the subway shooting of four young blacks he said were going to rob him. He was convicted of illegal possession of a weapon. Also, in 1996 a civil jury ordered Goetz to pay $43 million to one of the people he shot.
16-06-1989 Hungarian prime minister Imre Nagy was reburied. The funeral brought at least a quarter of a million people to the streets of Budapest. Nagy had been prime minister during the 1956 uprising that was crushed by Soviet tanks. He was hanged for treason on June 16, 1958.
16-06-1992 U.S. President George Bush welcomed Russian President Boris Yeltsin to a meeting in Washington, DC. The two agreed in principle to reduce strategic weapon arsenals by about two-thirds by the year 2003.
16-06-1993 The U.S. Postal Service released a set of seven stamps that featured Bill Haley, Buddy Holly, Clyde McPhatter, Otis Redding, Ritchie Valens, Dinah Washington and Elvis Presley.
16-06-1996 Russian voters had their first independent presidential election. Boris Yeltsin was the winner after a run-off.
16-06-1996 "Batman Forever" opened in the U.S.
16-06-1999 Kathleen Ann Soliah was arrested by the FBI in St. Paul, MN. She had been wanted since 1976 after being indicted on murder conspiracy and explosives charges.
16-06-1999 The U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals said that a 1992 federal music piracy law does not prohibit a palm-sized device that can download high-quality digital music files from the Internet and play them at home.
16-06-2000 U.S. federal regulators approved the merger of Bell Atlantic and GTE Corp. The merger created the nation's largest local phone company.
16-06-2000 U.S. Secretary of Energy Bill Richardson reported that an employee at the Los Alamos National Laboratory in New Mexico had discovered that two computer hard drives were missing.
17-06-0362 Emperor Julian issued an edict banning Christians from teaching in Syria.
17-06-1579 Sir Francis Drake claimed San Francisco Bay for England.
17-06-1775 The British took Bunker Hill outside of Boston.
17-06-1789 The Third Estate in France declared itself a national assembly, and began to frame a constitution.
17-06-1799 Napoleon Bonaparte incorporated Italy into his empire.
17-06-1837 Charles Goodyear received his first patent. The patent was for a process that made rubber easier to work with.
17-06-1848 Austrian General Alfred Windischgratz crushed a Czech uprising in Prague.
17-06-1854 The Red Turban revolt broke out in Guangdong, China.
17-06-1856 The Republican Party opened its first national convention in Philadelphia.
17-06-1861 U.S. President Abraham Lincoln witnessed Dr. Thaddeus Lowe demonstrate the use of a hydrogen balloon.
17-06-1872 George M. Hoover began selling whiskey in Dodge City, Kansas. The town had been dry up until this point.
17-06-1876 General George Crook’s command was attacked and bested on the Rosebud River by 1,500 Sioux and Cheyenne under the leadership of Crazy Horse.
17-06-1879 Thomas Edison received an honorary degree of Doctor of Philosophy from the trustees of Rutgers College in New Brunswick, NJ.
17-06-1885 The Statue of Liberty arrived in New York City aboard the French ship Isere.
17-06-1912 The German Zeppelin SZ 111 burned in its hanger in Friedrichshafen.
17-06-1913 U.S. Marines set sail from San Diego to protect American interests in Mexico.
17-06-1917 The Russian Duma met in a secret session in Petrograd and voted for an immediate Russian offensive against the German Army.
17-06-1924 The Fascist militia marched into Rome.
17-06-1926 Spain threatened to quit the League of Nations if Germany was allowed to join.
17-06-1928 Amelia Earhart began the flight that made her the first woman to successfully fly across the Atlantic Ocean.
17-06-1930 The Smoot-Hawley Tariff Bill became law. It placed the highest tariff on imports to the U.S.
17-06-1931 British authorities in China arrested Indochinese Communist leader Ho Chi Minh.
17-06-1932 The U.S. Senate defeated the bonus bill as 10,000 veterans massed around the Capitol.
17-06-1940 The Soviet Union occupied Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia.
17-06-1940 France asked Germany for terms of surrender in World War II.
17-06-1941 WNBT-TV in New York City, NY, was granted the first construction permit to operate a commercial TV station in the U.S.
17-06-1942 Yank, a weekly magazine for the U.S. armed services, began publication. The term "G.I. Joe" was first used in a comic strip by Dave Breger.
17-06-1942 "Suspense" debuted on CBS Radio.
17-06-1944 French troops landed on the island of Elba in the Mediterranean.
17-06-1944 The republic of Iceland was established.
17-06-1950 Dr. Richard H. Lawler performed the first kidney transplant in a 45-minute operation in Chicago, IL.
17-06-1953 Soviet tanks fought thousands of Berlin workers that were rioting against the East German government.
17-06-1963 The U.S. Supreme Court banned the required reading of the Lord's prayer and Bible in public schools.
17-06-1965 Twenty-seven B-52’s hit Viet Cong outposts but lost two planes in South Vietnam.
17-06-1969 Boris Spasky became chess champion of the world after checkmating former champion Tigran Petrosian in Moscow.
17-06-1970 North Vietnamese troops cut the last operating rail line in Cambodia.
17-06-1972 Five men were arrested for burglarizing the Democratic Party Headquarters in the Watergate complex in Washington, DC. The men all worked for the reelection of President Nixon. The event was the beginning of the Watergate affair.
17-06-1982 Former U.S. President Richard M. Nixon was interviewed by Diane Sawyer on "The CBS Morning News."
17-06-1985 Judy Norton-Taylor was photographed for "Playboy" magazine.
17-06-1987 American journalist Charles Glass was kidnapped. He was held captive for 62 days until he escaped on August 18, 1987.
17-06-1991 The Parliament of South Africa repealed the Population Registration Act. The act had required that all South Africans for classified by race at birth.
17-06-1994 O.J. Simpson drove his Ford Bronco across Los Angeles with police in pursuit and millions of people watching live on television. After the slow speed chase ended Simpson was arrested and charged with the murders of Nicole Simpson and Ronald Goldman.
18-06-1155 Frederick I Barbarossa was crowned emperor of Rome.
18-06-1429 French forces defeated the English at the battle of Patay. The English had been retreating after the siege of Orleans.
18-06-1621 The first duel in America took place in the Plymouth Colony in Massachusetts.
18-06-1667 The Dutch fleet sailed up the Thames toward London.
18-06-1778 Britain evacuated Philadelphia during the U.S. Revolutionary War.
18-06-1812 The War of 1812 began as the U.S. declared war against Great Britain. The conflict began over trade restrictions.
18-06-1815 At the Battle of Waterloo Napoleon was defeated by an international army under the Duke of Wellington. Napoleon abdicated on June 22.
18-06-1817 London's Waterloo Bridge opened. The bridge, designed by John Rennie, was built over the River Thames.
18-06-1861 The first American fly-casting tournament was held in Utica, NY.
18-06-1873 Susan B. Anthony was fined $100 for attempting to vote for a U.S. President.
18-06-1898 Atlantic City, NJ, opened its Steel Pier.
18-06-1915 During World War I, the second battle of Artois ended.
18-06-1918 Allied forces on the Western Front began their largest counter-attack against the German army.
18-06-1925 The first degree in landscape architecture was granted by Harvard University.
18-06-1927 The U.S. Post Office offered a special 10-cent postage stamp for sale. The stamp was of Charles Lindbergh’s "Spirit of St. Louis."
18-06-1928 Amelia Earhart became the first woman to fly across the Atlantic Ocean as she completed a flight from Newfoundland to Wales.
18-06-1936 Charles ‘Lucky’ Luciano was found guilty on 62 counts of compulsory prostitution.
18-06-1936 The first bicycle traffic court was established in Racine, WI.
18-06-1939 The CBS radio network aired "Ellery Queen" for the first time.
18-06-1942 The U.S. Navy commissioned its first black officer, Harvard University medical student Bernard Whitfield Robinson.
18-06-1948 The United Nations Commission on Human Rights adopted its International Declaration of Human Rights.
18-06-1951 General Vo Nguyen Giap ended his Red River Campaign against the French in Indochina.
18-06-1953 Seventeen major league baseball records were tied or broken in a game between the Boston Red Sox and the Detroit Tigers.
18-06-1953 Egypt was proclaimed to be a republic with General Neguib as its first president.
18-06-1959 A Federal Court annulled the Arkansas law allowing school closings to prevent integration.
18-06-1959 The first telecast received from England was broadcast in the U.S. over NBC-TV.
18-06-1961 "Gunsmoke" was broadcast for the last time on CBS radio.
18-06-1966 Samuel Nabrit became the first African American to serve on the Atomic Energy Commission.
18-06-1972 A BEA Trident crashed just after takeoff from London Airport. All 118 people on board were killed.
18-06-1975 Fred Lynn of the Boston Red Sox hit three home runs, a triple and a single in a game against the Detroit Tigers.
18-06-1979 In Vienna, U.S. President Jimmy Carter and Leonid Brezhnev signed the Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty (SALT) 2.
18-06-1983 Dr. Sally Ride became the first American woman in space aboard the space shuttle Challenger.
18-06-1984 Alan Berg was shot to death outside his home. Two white supremacists were convicted of civil rights violations in the murder.
18-06-1996 Richard Allen Davis was convicted in San Jose, CA, of the 1993 kidnap-murder of 12-year-old Polly Klaas.
18-06-1997 Sirhan Sirhan was denied parole for the 10th time. He had assissinated presidential candidate Robert Kennedy in 1968.
18-06-1998 The Walt Disney Co. purchased a 43% stake in the Web search engine company Infoseek Corp.
18-06-1998 Nine commemorative U.S. postage stamps were reissued. The stamps were considered to be classically beautiful examples of stamp engraving.
18-06-1998 "The Boston Globe" asked Patricia Smith to resign after she admitted to inventing people and quotes in four of her recent columns.
18-06-1999 Walt Disney's "Tarzan" opened.
18-06-2000 In Algiers, Algeria, the foreign ministers of Ethiopia and Eritrea signed a preliminary cease-fire accord and agreed to work toward a permanent settlement of their two-year border war.
18-06-2002 In Jerusalem, a suicide bomber killed 19 people and injured at least 50 more on a city bus. The Islamic militant group Hamas claimed responsibility for the attack.
19-06-1586 English colonists sailed away from Roanoke Island, NC, after failing to establish England's first permanent settlement in America.
19-06-1778 U.S. General George Washington's troops finally left Valley Forge after a winter of training.
19-06-1821 The Ottomans defeated the Greeks at the Battle of Dragasani.
19-06-1846 The New York Knickerbocker Club played the New York Club in the first baseball game at the Elysian Field, Hoboken, NJ. It was the first organized baseball game.
19-06-1862 U.S. President Abraham Lincoln outlined his Emancipation Proclamation, which outlawed slavery in U.S. territories.
19-06-1864 The USS Kearsarge sank the CSS Alabama off of Cherbourg, France.
19-06-1865 The emancipation of slaves was proclaimed in Texas.
19-06-1867 Mexican Emperor Maximillian was executed.
19-06-1867 In New York, the Belmont Stakes was run for the first time.
19-06-1903 The young school teacher, Benito Mussolini, was placed under investigation by police in Bern, Switzerland.
