19-06-1910 Father's Day was celebrated for the first time, in Spokane, WA.
19-06-1911 In Pennsylvania, the first motion-picture censorship board was established.
19-06-1912 The U.S. government established the 8-hour work day.
19-06-1917 During World War I, King George V ordered the British royal family to dispense with German titles and surnames. On July 17, 1917, the family took the name "Windsor".
19-06-1933 France granted Leon Trotsky political asylum.
19-06-1934 The U.S. National Archives and Records Administration was established.
19-06-1934 The U.S. Congress established the Federal Communications Commission (FCC). The commission was to regulate radio and TV broadcasting (later).
19-06-1937 The town of Bilbao, Spain, fell to the Nationalist forces.
19-06-1939 In Atlanta, GA, legislation was enacted that disallowed pinball machines in the city.
19-06-1942 Norma Jeane Mortenson (Marilyn Monroe) and her 21-year-old neighbor Jimmy Dougherty were married. They were divorced in June of 1946.
19-06-1942 British Prime Minister Winston Churchill arrived in Washington, DC. to discuss the invasion of North Africa with U.S. President Roosevelt.
19-06-1943 Henry Kissinger became a naturalized United States citizen.
19-06-1943 The National Football League approved the merger of the Philadelphia Eagles and the Pittsburgh Steelers.
19-06-1944 The U.S. won the battle of the Philippine Sea against the Imperial Japanese fleet.
19-06-1951 U.S. President Harry S. Truman signed the Universal Military Training and Service Act, which extended Selective Service until July 1, 1955 and lowered the draft age to 18.
19-06-1952 "I’ve Got a Secret" debuted on CBS-TV.
19-06-1953 Julius and Ethel Rosenberg were executed at Sing Sing Prison in Ossining, NY. They had been convicted of conspiring to pass U.S. atomic secrets to the Soviet Union.
19-06-1958 In Washington, DC, nine entertainers refused to answer a congressional committee's questions on communism.
19-06-1961 Kuwait regained complete independence from Britain.
19-06-1961 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down a provision in Maryland's constitution that required state officeholders to profess a belief in God.
19-06-1964 The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was approved after surviving an 83-day filibuster in the U.S. Senate.
19-06-1965 Air Marshall Nguyen Cao Ky became South Vietnam's youngest premier at age 34.
19-06-1968 50,000 people marched on Washington, DC. to support the Poor People's Campaign.
19-06-1973 The Case-Church Amendment prevented further U.S. involvement in Southeast Asia.
19-06-1973 Pete Rose (Cincinnati Reds) got his 2,000th career hit.
19-06-1973 The stage production of "The Rocky Horror Picture Show" opened in London.
19-06-1973 Gordie Howe left the NHL to join his sons Mark and Marty in the WHA (World Hockey League).
19-06-1976 During three days of violence, black student protestors were massacred in Soweto, South Africa.
19-06-1978 Garfield was in newspapers around the U.S. for the first time.
19-06-1981 "Superman II" set the all-time, one-day record for theater box-office receipts when it took in $5.5 million.
19-06-1981 The European Space Agency sent two satellites into orbit from Kourou, French Guiana.
19-06-1983 Lixian-nian was chosen to be China's first president since 1969.
19-06-1986 University of Maryland basketball star Len Bias died of a cocaine-induced seizure.
19-06-1987 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down the Louisiana law that required that schools teach creationism.
19-06-1989 The movie "Batman" premiered.
19-06-1997 William Hague became the youngest leader of Britain's Conservative party in nearly 200 years.
19-06-1998 Gateway was fined more than $400,000 for illegally shipping personal computers to 16 countries subject to U.S. export controls.
19-06-1998 A study released said that smoking more than doubles risks of developing dementia and Alzheimer's.
19-06-1998 Switzerland's three largest banks offered $600 million to settle claims they'd stolen the assets of Holocaust victims during World War II. Jewish leaders called the offer insultingly low.
19-06-1999 Stephen King was struck from behind by a mini-van while walking along a road in Maine.
19-06-1999 The Dallas Stars won their first NHL Stanley Cup by defeating the Buffalo Sabres in the third overtime of game six.
19-06-2000 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that a group prayer led by students at public-school football games violated the 1st Amendment's principle that called for the separation of church and state.
20-06-0451 Roman and Barbarian warriors brought Attila's army to a halt at the Catalaunian Plains in eastern France.
20-06-1397 The Union of Kalmar united Denmark, Sweden, and Norway under one monarch.
20-06-1756 In India, 150 British soldiers were imprisoned in a cell that became known as the "Black Hole of Calcutta."
20-06-1782 The U.S. Congress approved the Great Seal of the United States.
20-06-1791 King Louis XVI of France was captured while attempting to flee the country in the so-called Flight to Varennes.
20-06-1793 Eli Whitney applied for a cotton gin patent. He received the patent on March 14. The cotton gin initiated the American mass-production concept.
20-06-1837 Queen Victoria ascended the British throne following the death of her uncle, King William IV.
20-06-1863 West Virginia became the 35th state to join the U.S.
20-06-1863 The National Bank of Philadelphia in Philadelphia, PA, became the first bank to receive a charter from the U.S. Congress.
20-06-1893 A jury in New Bedford, MA, found Lizzie Borden innocent of the ax murders of her father and stepmother.
20-06-1898 The U.S. Navy seized the island of Guam enroute to the Phillipines to fight the Spanish.
20-06-1910 Mexican President Porfirio Diaz proclaimed martial law and arrested hundreds.
20-06-1910 Fanny Brice debuted in the New York production of the "Ziegfeld Follies".
20-06-1923 France announced it would seize the Rhineland to assist Germany in paying its war debts.
20-06-1941 The U.S. Army Air Force was established, replacing the Army Air Corps.
20-06-1943 Race-related rioting erupted in Detroit. Federal troops were sent in two days later to end the violence that left more than 30 dead.
20-06-1947 Benjamin "Bugsy" Siegel was murdered in Beverly Hills, CA, at the order of mob associates angered over the soaring costs of his project, the Flamingo resort in Las Vegas, NV.
20-06-1948 "Toast of the Town" debuted on CBS-TV. The show was hosted by Ed Sullivan.
20-06-1950 Willie Mays graduated from high school and immediately signed with the New York Giants.
20-06-1955 The AFL and CIO agreed to combine names and a merge into a single group.
20-06-1963 The United States and Soviet Union signed an agreement to set up a hot line communication link between the two countries.
20-06-1966 The U.S. Open golf tournament was broadcast in color for the first time.
20-06-1967 Muhammad Ali was convicted in Houston of violating Selective Service laws by refusing to be drafted. The U.S. Supreme Court later overturned the conviction.
20-06-1977 The Trans-Alaska Pipeline began operation.
20-06-1979 ABC News correspondent Bill Stewart was shot to death in Managua, Nicaragua, by a member of President Anastasio Somoza's national guard.
20-06-1994 In Los Angeles, O.J. Simpson pled innocent to the killing of Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald Goldman.
20-06-1997 The tobacco industry agreed to a massive settlement in exchange for major relief from mounting lawsuits and legal bills.
20-06-2001 Barry Bonds, of the San Francisco Giants, hit his 38th home run of the season. The home run broke the major league baseball record for homers before the midseason All-Star break.
20-06-2001 In Texas, Andrea Yates was arrested for drowning her five children in a bathtub.
20-06-2002 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the execution of mentally retarded murderers was unconstitutionally cruel. The vote was 6 in favor and 3 against.
21-06-1404 Owain Glyndwr established a Welsh Parliament at Machynlleth and was crowned Prince of Wales.
21-06-1788 The U.S. Constitution went into effect when New Hampshire became the ninth state to ratify it.
21-06-1834 Cyrus McCormick patented the first practical mechanical reaper for farming. His invention allowed farmers to more than double their crop size.
21-06-1859 Andrew Lanergan received the first rocket patent.
21-06-1913 Georgia Broadwick became the first woman to jump from an airplane.
21-06-1937 In Paris, Leon Blum's Popular Front Cabinet resigned.
21-06-1938 In Washington, U.S. President Roosevelt signed the $3.75 billion Emergency Relief Appropriation Act.
21-06-1939 Lou Gehrig quit baseball due to illness.
21-06-1940 Richard M. Nixon and Thelma Catherine ‘Pat’ Ryan were married.
21-06-1941 German troops entered Russia on a front from the Arctic to Black Sea.
21-06-1942 Ben Hogan recorded the lowest score (to that time) in a major golf tournament. Hogan shot a 271 for 72 holes in Chicago, IL.
21-06-1945 Pan Am announced an 88-hour round-the-world flight at a cost of $700.
21-06-1954 The American Cancer Society reported significantly higher death rates among cigarette smokers than among non-smokers.
21-06-1954 NBC radio presented the final broadcast of "The Railroad Hour."
21-06-1954 Australian John Landy ran the mile in 3:58. He was the second person to achieve the feat.
21-06-1958 In Arkansas, a federal judge let Little Rock delay school integration.
21-06-1958 Linus Pauling and Detlev Bronke, both Americans, were elected to the Soviet Academy of Science.
21-06-1960 In Zurich, German, Armin Hary ran 100-meters in a record 10.0 seconds.
21-06-1963 In St. Louis, Bob Hayes set a record when he ran the 100-yard dash in 0:09.1.
21-06-1963 France announced that they were withdrawing from the North Atlantic NATO fleet.
21-06-1964 Three civil rights workers disappeared in Philadelphia, MS. Their bodies were found on August 4, 1964 in an earthen dam. Eight Ku Klux Klan members later went to federal prison on conspiracy charges.
21-06-1969 In South Carolina, civil rights leader Rev. Ralph Abernathy was jailed on riot charges.
21-06-1970 Tony Jacklin became the second British golfer in 50 years to win the U.S. Open golf tournament.
21-06-1973 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that states may ban materials found to be obscene according to local standards.
21-06-1981 "Raiders of the Lost Ark" opened.
21-06-1982 A jury in Washington, DC, found John Hinckley Jr. innocent by reason of insanity in the shootings of U.S. President Reagan and three other men.
21-06-1985 Scientists announced that skeletal remains exhumed in Brazil were those of Nazi war criminal Josef Mengele.
21-06-1989 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that burning the American flag as a form of political protest was protected by the First Amendment.
21-06-1993 In Madrid, five senior military officers and two civilians were killed by a car bomb. 24 people were injured. The Basque terror group claimed responsibility.
21-06-2001 Former Haitian Army colonel Carl Dorelien taken into custody in Port St. Lucie. Dorelien had been in exile since 1994 when he was sentenced to life in prison for his role in a 1994 massacre.
21-06-2001 In Alexandria, VA, a U.S. federal grand jury indicted 13 Saudis and a Lebanese in the 1996 bombing of the Khobar Towers in Saudi Arabia that killed 19 American servicemen.
21-06-2003 The fifth Harry Potter book, "Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix," was published by J.K. Rowling. Amazon.com shipped out more than one million copies on this day making the day the largest distribution day of a single item in e-commerce history. The book set sales records around the world with an estimated 5 million copies were sold on the first day.
21-06-2004 SpaceShipOne, designed by Burt Rutan and piloted by Mike Melvill, reached 328,491 feet above Earth in a 90 minute flight. The height is about 400 feet above the distance scientists consider to be the boundary of space.
22-06-1558 The French take the French town of Thioville from the English.
22-06-1611 English explorer Henry Hudson, his son and several other people were set adrift in present-day Hudson Bay by mutineers.
22-06-1772 Slavery was outlawed in England.
22-06-1807 British seamen board the USS Chesapeake, a provocation leading to the War of 1812.
22-06-1815 Napoleon Bonaparte abdicated a second time.
22-06-1832 J.I. Howe patented the pin machine.
22-06-1868 Arkansas was re-admitted to the Union.
22-06-1870 The U.S. Congress created the Department of Justice.
22-06-1874 Dr. Andrew Taylor Still began the first known practice of osteopathy.
22-06-1909 The first transcontinental auto race ended in Seattle, WA.
22-06-1911 King George V of England was crowned.
22-06-1915 Austro-German forces occupied Lemberg on the Eastern Front as the Russians retreat.
22-06-1925 France and Spain agreed to join forces against Abd el Krim in Morocco.
22-06-1933 Germany became a one political party country when Hitler banned parties other than the Nazis.
22-06-1939 The first U.S. water-ski tournament was held at Jones Beach, on Long Island, New York.
22-06-1940 France and Germany signed an armistice at Compiegne, on terms dictated by the Nazis.
22-06-1941 Under the codename Barbarossa, Germany invaded the Soviet Union.
22-06-1942 A Japanese submarine shelled Fort Stevens at the mouth of the Columbia River.
22-06-1942 In France, Pierre Laval declared "I wish for a German vitory".
22-06-1942 V-Mail, or Victory-Mail, was sent for the first time.
22-06-1944 U.S. President Franklin Roosevelt signed the "GI Bill of Rights" to provide broad benefits for veterans of the war.
22-06-1945 During World War II, the battle for Okinawa officially ended after 81 days.
22-06-1946 Jet airplanes were used to transport mail for the first time.
22-06-1956 The battle for Algiers began as three buildings in Casbah were blown up.
22-06-1959 Eddie Lubanski rolled 24 consecutive strikes in a bowling tournament in Miami, FL.
22-06-1964 The U.S. Supreme Court voted that Henry Miller’s book, "Tropic of Cancer", could not be banned.
22-06-1969 Judy Garland died from an accidental overdose of prescription sleeping aids. She was 47.
22-06-1970 U.S. President Richard Nixon signed 26th amendment, lowering the voting age to 18.
22-06-1973 Skylab astronauts splashed down safely in the Pacific after a record 28 days in space.
22-06-1977 John N. Mitchell became the first former U.S. Attorney General to go to prison as he began serving a sentence for his role in the Watergate cover-up. He served 19 months.
22-06-1978 James W. Christy and Robert S. Harrington discovered the only known moon of Pluto. The moon is named Charon.
22-06-1980 The Soviet Union announceed a partial withdrawal of its forces from Afghanistan.
22-06-1981 Mark David Chapman pled guilty to killing John Lennon.
22-06-1989 The government of Angola and the anti-Communist rebels of the UNITA movement agreed to a formal truce in their 14-year-old civil war.
22-06-1992 The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled that hate-crime laws that ban cross-burning and similar expressions of racial bias violated free-speech rights.
22-06-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that evidence illegally obtained by authorities could be used at revocation hearings for a convicted criminal's parole.
22-06-1998 The 75th National Marbles Tournament begins in Wildwood, NJ.
22-06-1999 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that persons with remediable handicaps cannot claim discrimination in employment under the Americans with Disability Act.
23-06-1683 William Penn signed a friendship treaty with Lenni Lenape Indians in Pennsylvania.
23-06-1700 Russia gave up its Black Sea fleet as part of a truce with the Ottoman Empire.
23-06-1758 British and Hanoverian armies defeated the French at Krefeld in Germany.
23-06-1760 The Austrians defeated the Prussians at Landshut, Germany.
23-06-1757 Robert Clive defeated the Indians at Plassey and won control of Bengal.
23-06-1836 The U.S. Congress approved the Deposit Act, which contained a provision for turning over surplus federal revenue to the states.
23-06-1848 A bloody insurrection of workers in Paris erupted.
23-06-1860 The U.S. Secret Service was created to arrest counterfeiters.
23-06-1865 Confederate General Stand Watie, who was also a Cherokee chief, surrendered the last sizable Confederate army at Fort Towson, in the Oklahoma Territory.
23-06-1868 Christopher Latham Sholes received a patent for an invention that he called a "Type-Writer."
23-06-1884 A Chinese Army defeated the French at Bacle, Indochina.
23-06-1902 Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Italy renewed the Triple Alliance for a 12 year duration.
23-06-1904 The first American motorboat race got underway on the Hudson River in New York.
23-06-1926 The first lip reading tournament in America was held in Philadelphia, PA.
23-06-1931 Wiley Post and Harold Gatty took off from New York on the first round-the-world flight in a single-engine plane.
23-06-1934 Italy gained the right to colonize Albania after defeating the country.
23-06-1938 The Civil Aeronautics Authority was established.
23-06-1938 Marineland opened near St. Augustine, Florida.
23-06-1947 The U.S. Senate joined the House in overriding President Truman's veto of the Taft-Hartley Act.
23-06-1951 Soviet U.N. delegate Jacob Malik proposed cease-fire discussions in the Korean War.
23-06-1952 The U.S. Air Force bombed power plants on Yalu River, Korea.
23-06-1956 Gamal Abdel Nasser was elected president of Egypt.
23-06-1964 Henry Cabot Lodge resigned as the U.S. envoy to Vietnam and was succeeded by Maxwell Taylor.
23-06-1964 The burned car of three civil rights workers was found prompting the FBI to begin a search. The men had been missing since June 21, 1964. Their bodies were found on August 4, 1964.
23-06-1966 Civil Rights marchers in Mississippi were dispersed by tear gas.
23-06-1972 U.S. President Nixon and White House chief of staff H.R. Haldeman discussed a plan to use the CIA to obstruct the FBI's Watergate investigation.
23-06-1985 All 329 people aboard an Air-India Boeing 747 were killed when the plane crashed into the Atlantic Ocean near Ireland. The cause was thought to be a bomb.
23-06-1989 The movie "Batman" was released nationwide.
23-06-1992 John Gotti was sentenced in New York to life in prison after being convicted of racketeering charges.
23-06-1993 Lorena Bobbitt of Prince William County, VA, sexually mutilated her husband, John, after he allegedly raped her.
23-06-1997 Betty Shabazz, the widow of Malcolm X, died in New York of burns suffered in a fire set by her 12-year old grandson. She was 61.
23-06-2003 Apple Computer Inc. unveiled the new Power Mac desktop computer.
23-06-2004 The U.S. proposed that North Korea agree to a series of nuclear disarmament measures over a three-month period in exchange for economic benefits.
23-06-2005 Roger Ebert received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
24-06-1314 Scottish forces led by Robert the Bruce won over Edward II of England at the Battle of Bannockburn in Scotland.
24-06-1340 The English fleet defeated the French fleet at Sluys, off the Flemish coast.
24-06-1497 Italian explorer John Cabot, sailing in the service of England, landed in North America on what is now Newfoundland.
24-06-1509 Henry VIII was crowned King of England.
24-06-1664 New Jersey, named after the Isle of Jersey, was founded.
24-06-1675 King Philip's War began when Indians massacre colonists at Swansee, Plymouth colony.
24-06-1793 The first republican constitution in France was adopted.
24-06-1812 Napoleon crossed the Nieman River and invaded Russia.
24-06-1844 Charles Goodyear was granted U.S. patent #3,633 for vulcanized rubber.
24-06-1859 At the Battle of Solferino, also known as the Battle of the Three Sovereigns, the French army led by Napoleon III defeated the Austrian army under Franz Joseph I in northern Italy.
24-06-1861 Federal gunboats attacked Confederate batteries at Mathias Point, Virginia.
24-06-1862 U.S. intervention saved the British and French at the Dagu forts in China.
24-06-1869 Mary Ellen "Mammy" Pleasant officially became the Vodoo Queen in San Francisco, CA.
24-06-1896 Booker T. Washington became the first African American to receive an honorary MA degree from Howard University.
24-06-1910 The Japanese army invaded Korea.
24-06-1913 Greece and Serbia annulled their alliance with Bulgaria following border disputes over Macedonia and Thrace.
24-06-1922 The American Professional Football Association took the name of The National Football League.
24-06-1931 The Soviet Union and Afghanistan signed a treaty of neutrality.
24-06-1940 France signed an armistice with Italy.
24-06-1940 TV cameras were used for the first time in a political convention as the Republicans convened in Philadelphia, PA.
24-06-1941 U.S. President Franklin Roosevelt pledged all possible support to the Soviet Union.
24-06-1947 Kenneth Arnold reported seeing flying saucers over Mt. Rainier, Washington.
24-06-1948 The Soviet Union began the Berlin Blockade.
24-06-1953 John F. Kennedy and Jacqueline Bouvier announced their engagement.
24-06-1955 Soviet MIG's down a U.S. Navy patrol plane over the Bering Strait.
24-06-1962 The New York Yankees beat the Detroit Tigers, 9-7, after 22 innings.
24-06-1964 The Federal Trade Commission announced that starting in 1965, cigarette manufactures would be required to include warnings on their packaging about the harmful effects of smoking.
24-06-1968 "Resurrection City," a shantytown constructed as part of the Poor People's March on Washington D.C., was closed down by authorities.
24-06-1970 The U.S. Senate voted overwhelmingly to repeal the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution.
24-06-1970 The movie "Myra Breckinridge" premiered.
24-06-1971 The National Basketball Association modified its four-year eligibility rule to allow for collegiate hardship cases.
24-06-1975 113 people were killed when an Eastern Airlines Boeing 727 crashed while attempting to land during a thunderstorm at New York's John F. Kennedy International Airport.
24-06-1985 Natalia Solzhenitsyn the wife of exiled, Soviet author Alexander Solzhenitsyn, became a U.S. citizen.
24-06-1997 18-year-old Melissa Drexler was charged with murder in the death of her baby. Drexler had given birth during her prom.
24-06-1997 The U.S. Air Force released a report on the "Roswell Incident," suggesting the alien bodies witnesses reported seeing in 1947 were actually life-sized dummies.
24-06-1998 AT&T Corp. struck a deal to buy cable TV giant Tele-Communications Inc. for $31.7 billion.
24-06-1998 Walt Disney World Resort admitted its 600-millionth guest.
24-06-2002 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that juries, not judges, must make the decision to give a convicted killer the death penalty.
24-06-2002 A painting from Monet's Waterlilies series sold for $20.2 million.
24-06-2003 In Paris, France, manuscripts by novelist Georges Simenon brought in $325,579. The original manuscript of "La Mort de Belle" raised $81,705.
25-06-0841 Charles the Bald and Louis the German defeated Lothar at Fontenay.
25-06-1080 At Brixen, a council of bishops declared Pope Gregory to be deposed and Archbishop Guibert as antipope Clement III.
25-06-1580 The Book of Concord was first published. The book is a collection of doctrinal standards of the Lutheran Church.
25-06-1658 Aurangzeb proclaimed himself emperor of the Moghuls in India.
25-06-1767 Mexican Indians rioted as Jesuit priests were ordered home.
25-06-1788 Virginia ratified the U.S. Constitution and became the 10th state of the United States.
25-06-1864 Union troops surrounding Petersburg, VA, began building a mine tunnel underneath the Confederate lines.
25-06-1867 Lucien B. Smith patented the first barbed wire.
25-06-1868 The U.S. Congress enacted legislation granting an eight-hour day to workers employed by the Federal government.
25-06-1868 Florida, Alabama, Louisiana, Georgia, North Carolina and South Carolina were readmitted to the Union.
25-06-1870 In Spain, Queen Isabella abdicated in favor of Alfonso XII.
25-06-1876 Lt. Col. Custer and the 210 men of U.S. 7th Cavalry were killed by Sioux and Cheyenne Indians at Little Big Horn in Montana. The event is known as "Custer's Last Stand."
25-06-1877 In Philadelphia, PA, Alexander Graham Bell demonstated the telephone for Sir William Thomson (Baron Kelvin) and Emperor Pedro II of Brazil at the Centennial Exhibition.
25-06-1906 Pittsburgh millionaire Harry Kendall Thaw, the son of coal and railroad baron William Thaw, shot and killed Stanford White. White, a prominent architect, had a tryst with Florence Evelyn Nesbit before she married Thaw. The shooting took place at the premeire of Mamzelle Champagne in New York.
25-06-1910 The U.S. Congress authorized the use of postal savings stamps.
25-06-1917 The first American fighting troops landed in France.
25-06-1920 The Greeks took 8,000 Turkish prisoners in Smyrna.
25-06-1921 Samuel Gompers was elected head of the AFL for the 40th time.
25-06-1938 Gaelic scholar Douglas Hyde was inaugurated as the first president of the Irish Republic.
25-06-1941 Finland declared war on the Soviet Union.
25-06-1946 Ho Chi Minh traveled to France for talks on Vietnamese independence.
25-06-1948 The Soviet Union tightened its blockade of Berlin by intercepting river barges heading for the city.
25-06-1950 North Korea invaded South Korea initiating the Korean War.
25-06-1951 In New York, the first regular commercial color TV transmissions were presented on CBS using the FCC-approved CBS Color System. The public did not own color TV's at the time.
25-06-1952 John Christie, the British murderer of 10 Rillington Place, was sentenced to death for killing six women.
25-06-1959 The Cuban government seized 2.35 million acres under a new agrarian reform law.
25-06-1959 Eamon De Valera became president of Ireland at the age of 76.
25-06-1962 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the use of unofficial non-denominational prayer in public schools was unconstitutional.
25-06-1964 U.S. President Lyndon Johnson ordered 200 naval personnel to Mississippi to assist in finding three missing civil rights workers.
25-06-1966 "Dark Shadows" began running on ABC-TV.
25-06-1968 Bobby Bonds (San Francisco Giants) hit a grand-slam home run in his first game with the Giants. He was the first player to debut with a grand-slam.
25-06-1970 The U.S. Federal Communications Commission handed down a ruling (35 FR 7732), making it illegal for radio stations to put telephone calls on the air without the permission of the person being called.
25-06-1973 Erskine Childers Jr. became president of Ireland after the retirement of Eamon De Valera.
25-06-1973 White House Counsel John Dean admitted that U.S. President Nixon took part in the Watergate cover-up.
25-06-1975 Mozambique became independent. Samora Machel was sworn in as president after 477 years of Portuguese rule.
25-06-1981 The U.S. Supreme Court decided that male-only draft registration was constitutional.
25-06-1985 ABC’s "Monday Night Football" began with a new line-up. The trio was Frank Gifford, Joe Namath and O.J. Simpson.
25-06-1985 New York Yankees officials enacted the rule that mandated that the team’s bat boys were to wear protective helmets during all games.
25-06-1986 The U.S. Congress approved $100 million in aid to the Contras fighting in Nicaragua.
25-06-1987 Austrian President Kurt Waldheim visited Pope John Paul II at the Vatican. The meeting was controversial due to allegations that Waldheim had hidden his Nazi past.
25-06-1990 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld the right of an individual, whose wishes are clearly made, to refuse life-sustaining medical treatment. "The right to die" decision was made in the Curzan vs. Missouri case.
25-06-1991 The last Soviet troops left Czechoslovakia 23 years after the Warsaw Pact invasion.
25-06-1991 The Yugoslav republics of Slovenia and Croatia declared their independence from Yugoslavia.
25-06-1993 Kim Campbell took office as Canada's first woman prime minister. She assumed power upon the resignation of Brian Mulroney.
25-06-1996 Outside the Khobar Towers near Dhahran, Saudi Arabia a truck bomb exploded. The bomb killed 19 Americans and injured over 500 Saudis and Americans.
25-06-1997 The Russian space station Mir was hit by an unmanned cargo vessel. Much of the power supply was knocked out and the station's Spektr module was severely damaged.
25-06-1997 U.S. air pollution standards were significantly tightened by U.S. President Clinton.
25-06-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court rejected the line-item veto thereby striking down presidential power to cancel specific items in tax and spending legislation.
25-06-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that those infected with HIV are protected by the Americans With Disabilities Act.
25-06-1998 Microsoft's "Windows 98" was released to the public.
25-06-1999 Germany's parliament approved a national Holocaust memorial to be built in Berlin.
25-06-2000 U.S. and British researchers announced that they had completed a rough draft of a map of the genetic makeup of human beings. The project was 10 years old at the time of the announcement.
25-06-2000 A Florida judge approved a class-action lawsuit to be filed against American Online (AOL) on behalf of hourly subscribers who were forced to view "pop-up" advertisements.
26-06-1096 Peter the Hermit's crusaders forced their way across Sava, Hungary.
26-06-1243 The Seljuk Turkish army in Asia Minor was wiped out by the Mongols.
26-06-1483 Richard III usurped himself to the English throne.
26-06-1541 Francisco Pizarro, the Spanish Conqueror of Peru, was murdered by his former followers.
26-06-1794 The French defeated an Austrian army at the Battle of Fleurus.
26-06-1804 The Lewis and Clark Expedition reached the mouth of the Kansas River after completing a westward trek of nearly 400 river miles.

26-06-1844 John Tyler took Julia Gardiner as his bride, thus becoming the first U.S. President to marry while in office.
26-06-1870 The first section of the boardwalk in Atlantic City, NJ, was opened to the public.
26-06-1894 The American Railway Union called a general strike in sympathy with Pullman workers.
26-06-1900 The United States announced that it would send troops to fight against the Boxer rebellion in China.
26-06-1900 A commission that included Dr. Walter Reed began the fight against the deadly disease yellow fever.
26-06-1907 Russia's nobility demanded drastic measures to be taken against revolutionaries.
26-06-1908 Shah Muhammad Ali's forces squelched the reform elements of Parliament in Persia.
26-06-1917 General John "Black Jack" Pershing arrived in France with the American Expeditionary Force.
26-06-1925 Charlie Chaplin's comedy, "The Gold Rush," premiered in Hollywood.
26-06-1926 A memorial to the first U.S. troops in France was unveiled at St. Nazaire.
26-06-1924 After eight years of occupation, American troops left the Dominican Republic.
26-06-1942 The Grumman F6F Hellcat fighter was flown for the first time.
26-06-1945 The U.N. Charter was signed by 50 nations in San Francisco, CA.
26-06-1948 The Berlin Airlift began as the U.S., Britain and France started ferrying supplies to the isolated western sector of Berlin.
26-06-1951 The Soviet Union proposed a cease-fire in the Korean War.
26-06-1959 CBS journalist Edward R. Murrow interviewed Lee Remick. It was his 500th and final guest on "Person to Person."
26-06-1959 U.S. President Eisenhower joined Britain's Queen Elizabeth II in ceremonies officially opening the St. Lawrence Seaway.
26-06-1961 A Kuwaiti vote opposed Iraq's annexation plans.
26-06-1963 U.S. President John Kennedy announced "Ich bin ein Berliner" (I am a Berliner) at the Berlin Wall.
26-06-1971 The U.S. Justice Department issued a warrant for Daniel Ellsberg, accusing him of giving away the Pentagon Papers.
26-06-1975 Indian Prime Minister Indira Gandhi declared a state of emergency due to "deep and widespread conspiracy."
26-06-1976 The CN (Canadian National) Tower in Toronto, Canada, opened.
26-06-1979 Muhammad Ali, at 37 years old, announced that he was retiring as world heavyweight boxing champion.
26-06-1981 In Mountain Home, Idaho, Virginia Campbell took her coupons and rebates and bought $26,460 worth of groceries. She only paid 67 cents after all the discounts.
26-06-1985 Wilbur Snapp was ejected after playing "Three Blind Mice" during a baseball game. The incident followed a call made by umpire Keith O'Connor.
26-06-1987 The movie "Dragnet" opened in the U.S.
26-06-1996 The U.S. Supreme Court ordered the Virginia Military Institute to admit women or forgo state support.
26-06-1997 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down the Communications Decency Act of 1996 that made it illegal to distribute indecent material on the Internet.
26-06-1997 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld state laws that allow for a ban on doctor-assisted suicides.
26-06-1998 The U.S. and Peru open school to train commandos to patrol Peru's rivers for drug traffickers.
26-06-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that employers are always potentially liable for supervisor's sexual misconduct toward an employee.
26-06-2000 The Human Genome Project and Celera Genomics Corp. jointly announced that they had created a working draft of the human genome.
26-06-2000 Indonesia's President Abdurrahman Wahid declared a state of emergency in the Moluccas due to the escalation of fighting between Christians and Muslims.
26-06-2001 Ray Bourque (Colorado Avalanche) announced his retirement just 17 days after winning his first Stanley Cup. Bouque retired after 22 years and held the NHL record for highest-scoring defenseman and playing in 19 consecutive All-Star games.
26-06-2002 David Hasseloff checked into The Betty Ford Center for treatment of alcoholism.
26-06-2002 WorldCom Inc. filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection.
27-06-0363 The death of Roman Emperor Julian brought an end to the Pagan Revival.
27-06-1693 "The Ladies' Mercury" was published by John Dunton in London. It was the first women's magazine and contained a "question and answer" column that became known as a "problem page."
27-06-1743 King George II of England defeated the French at Dettingen, Bavaria, in the War of the Austrian Succession.
27-06-1787 Edward Gibbon completed "The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire." It was published the following May.
27-06-1801 British forces defeated the French and took control of Cairo, Egypt.
27-06-1844 Joseph Smith and his brother Hyrum were killed by mob in Carthage, IL.
27-06-1847 New York and Boston were linked by telegraph wires.
27-06-1871 The yen became the new form of currency in Japan.
27-06-1885 Chichester Bell and Charles S. Tainter applied for a patent for the gramophone. It was granted on May 4, 1886.
27-06-1893 The New York stock market crashed. By the end of the year 600 banks and 74 railroads had gone out of business.
27-06-1905 The battleship Potemkin succumbed to a mutiny on the Black Sea.
27-06-1918 Two German pilots were saved by parachutes for the first time.
27-06-1923 Yugoslav Premier Nikola Pachitch was wounded by Serb attackers in Belgrade.
27-06-1924 Democrats offered Mrs. Leroy Springs for vice presidential nomination. She was the first woman considered for the job.
27-06-1927 The U.S. Marines adopted the English bulldog as their mascot.
27-06-1929 Scientists at Bell Laboratories in New York revealed a system for transmitting television pictures.
27-06-1931 Igor Sikorsky filed U.S. Patent 1,994,488, which marked the breakthrough in helicopter technology.
27-06-1940 Robert Pershing Wadlow was measured by Dr. Cyril MacBryde and Dr. C. M. Charles. They recorded his height at 8' 11.1." He was only 22 at the time of his death on July 15, 1940.
27-06-1942 The FBI announced the capture of eight Nazi saboteurs who had been put ashore from a submarine on New York's Long Island.
27-06-1944 During World War II, American forces completed their capture of the French port of Cherbourg from the German army.
27-06-1949 "Captain Video and His Video Rangers" premiered on the Dumont Television Network.
27-06-1950 Two days after North Korea invaded South Korea, U.S. President Truman ordered the Air Force and Navy into the Korean conflict. The United Nations Security Council had asked for member nations to help South Korea repel an invasion from the North.
27-06-1954 The world's first atomic power station opened at Obninsk, near Moscow.
27-06-1955 The first "Wide Wide World" was broadcast on NBC-TV.
27-06-1955 The state of Illinois enacted the first automobile seat belt legislation.
27-06-1957 More than 500 people were killed when Hurricane Audrey hit the coastal area of Louisiana and Texas.
27-06-1958 NBC's "Matinee Theatre" was seen for the final time.
27-06-1959 The play, "West Side Story," with music by Leonard Bernstein, closed after 734 performances on Broadway.
27-06-1961 Arthur Michael Ramsey was enthroned as the 100th Archbishop of Canterbury.
27-06-1964 Ernest Borgnine and Ethel Merman were married. It only lasted 38 days.
27-06-1967 The world's first cash dispenser was installed at Barclays Bank in Enfield, England. The device was invented by John Sheppard-Barron. The machine operated on a voucher system and the maximum withdrawal was $28.
27-06-1967 Two hundred people were arrested during a race riot in Buffalo, NY.
27-06-1969 Patrons at the Stonewall Inn, a gay bar in New York City's Greenwich Village, clashed with police. This incident is considered to be the birth of the homosexual rights movement.
27-06-1972 Bobby Hull signed a 10-year hockey contract for $2,500,000. He became a player and coach of the Winnipeg Jets of the World Hockey Association.
27-06-1973 Former White House counsel John W. Dean told the Senate Watergate Committee about an "enemies list" that was kept by the Nixon White House.
27-06-1973 Nixon vetoed a Senate ban on bombing Cambodia.
27-06-1976 Palestinian extremists hijacked an Air France plane in Greece. There were 246 passengers and 12 crew onboard. The plane eventually was taken to Entebbe, Uganda where Israeli commandos stormed it on July 4. The raid resulted in the deaths of seven pasengers.
27-06-1980 U.S. President Carter signed legislation reviving draft registration.
27-06-1984 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that individual colleges could make their own TV package deals.
27-06-1984 The Federal Communications Commission moved to deregulate U.S. commercial TV by lifting most programming requirements and ending day-part restrictions on advertising.
27-06-1985 Officials decertified Route 66.
27-06-1985 The U.S. House of Representatives voted to limit the use of combat troops in Nicaragua.
27-06-1986 The World Court ruled that the U.S. had broken international law by aiding Nicaraguan rebels.
27-06-1991 Associate Justice Thurgood Marshall resigned from the U.S. Supreme Court. He had been appointed in 1967 by President Lyndon Johnson.
27-06-1992 The body of kidnapped Exxon executive Sidney J. Reso was found buried in a makeshift grave in a state park in New Jersey. Arthur and Irene Seale were later convicted and sentenced to prison for the crime.
27-06-1995 Qatar's Crown Prince Sheik Hamad bin Khalifa al-Thani ousted his father in a bloodless palace coup.
27-06-1995 Actor Hugh Grant was arrested in Los Angeles for engaging in "lewd behavior" with a prostitute in a rented BMW.
27-06-1998 An English woman was impregnated with her dead husband's sperm after two-year legal battle over her right to the sperm.
27-06-1998 In a live joint news conference in China U.S. President Clinton and President Jiang Zemin offered an uncensored airing of differences on human rights, freedom, trade and Tibet.
27-06-2002 In the U.S., the Securities and Exchange Commission required companies with annual sales of more than $1.2 billion to submit sworn statements backing up the accuracy of their financial reports.
27-06-2005 In Alaska's Denali National Park, a roughly 70-million year old dinosaur track was discovered. The track was form a three-toed Cretaceous period dinosaur.

28-06-1675 Frederick William of Brandenburg crushed the Swedes.

28-06-1776 American Colonists repulsed a British sea attack on Charleston, SC.

28-06-1894 The U.S. Congress made Labor Day a U.S. national holiday.

28-06-1911 Samuel J. Battle became the first African-American policeman in New York City.
28-06-1914 Archduke Francis Ferdinand and the Mrs. Archduke were assassinated by Serb nationalist in (what is now known as) Sarajevo, Bosnia.
28-06-1919 The Treaty of Versailles was signed ending World War I exactly five years after it began. The treaty also established the League of Nations.
28-06-1921 A coal strike in Great Britain was settled after three months.
28-06-1930 More than 1,000 communists were routed during an assault on the British consulate in London.
28-06-1939 Pan American Airways began the first transatlantic passenger service.
28-06-1938 The U.S. Congress created the Federal Housing Administration (FHA) to insure construction loans.
28-06-1940 The "Quiz Kids" was heard on NBC radio for the first time.
28-06-1942 German troops launched an offensive to seize Soviet oil fields in the Caucasus and the city of Stalingrad.
28-06-1943 "The Dreft Star Playhouse" debuted on NBC radio.
28-06-1944 "The Alan Young Show" debuted on NBC radio.
28-06-1945 U.S. General Douglas MacArthur announced the end of Japanese resistance in the Philippines.
28-06-1949 The last U.S. combat troops were called home from Korea, leaving only 500 advisers.
28-06-1950 North Korean forces captured Seoul, South Korea.
28-06-1951 "Amos ’n’ Andy" moved to CBS-TV from radio.
28-06-1954 French troops began to pull out of Vietnam’s Tonkin Province.
28-06-1960 In Cuba, Fidel Castro confiscated American-owned oil refineries without compensation.
28-06-1964 Malcolm X founded the Organization for Afro American Unity to seek independence for blacks in the Western Hemisphere.
28-06-1965 The first commercial satellite began communications service. It was Early Bird (Intelsat II).
28-06-1967 Fourteen people were shot in race riots in Buffalo, New York.
28-06-1967 Israel formally declared Jerusalem reunified under its sovereignty following its capture of the Arab sector in the June 1967 war.
28-06-1971 The U.S. Supreme Court overturned the draft evasion conviction of Muhammad Ali.
28-06-1972 U.S. President Nixon announced that no new draftees would be sent to Vietnam.
28-06-1976 The first women entered the U.S. Air Force Academy.
28-06-1978 The U.S. Supreme Court ordered the medical school at the University of California at Davis to admit Allan Bakke. Bakke, a white man, argued he had been a victim of reverse racial discrimination.
28-06-1996 The Citadel voted to admit women, ending a 153-year-old men-only policy at the South Carolina military school.
28-06-1996 Charles M. Schulz got a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
28-06-1997 Mike Tyson was disqualified for biting Evander Holyfield's ear after three rounds of their WBA heavyweight title fight in Las Vegas, NV.
28-06-1998 Poland, due to shortage of funds, is allowed to lease, U.S. aircraft to bring military force up to NATO standards.
28-06-1998 The Cincinnati Enquirer apologized to Chiquita banana company and retracted their stories that questioned company's business practices. They also agreed to pay more than $10 million to settle legal claims.
28-06-2000 The U.S. Supreme Court declared that a Nebraska law that outlawed "partial birth abortions" was unconstitutional. About 30 U.S. states had similar laws at the time of the ruling.
28-06-2000 Darva Conger announced that she had done a layout for Playboy magazine. Conger had married Rick Rockwell on Fox-TV's "Who Wants to Marry a Multimillionaire."
28-06-2000 The European Commission announced that they had blocked the planned merger between the U.S. companies WorldCom Inc. and Sprint due to competition concerns.
28-06-2000 Six-year-old Elián González returned to Cuba from the U.S. with his father. The child had been the center of an international custody dispute.
28-06-2001 Slobodan Milosevic was taken into custody and was handed over to the U.N. war crimes tribunal in The Hague, Netherlands. The indictment charged Milosevic and four other senior officials, with crimes against humanity and violations of the laws and customs of war in Kosovo.
28-06-2001 The U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit set aside an order that would break up Microsoft for antitrust violations. However, the judges did agree that the company was in violation of antitrust laws.
28-06-2004 The U.S. turned over official sovereignty to Iraq's interim leadership. The event took place two days earlier than previously announced to thwart insurgents' attempts at undermining the transfer. 2004- The U.S. resumed diplomatic ties with Libya after a 24-year break.
29-06-1236 Ferdinand III of Castile and Leon took Cordoba in Spain.
29-06-1652 Massachusetts declared itself an independent commonwealth.
29-06-1767 The British Parliament approved the Townshend Revenue Acts. The acts imposed import duties on glass, lead, paint, paper and tea shipped to America.
29-06-1776 The Virginia constitution was adopted and Patrick Henry was made governor.
29-06-1804 Privates John Collins and Hugh Hall of the Lewis and Clark Expedition were found guilty by a court-martial consisting of members of the Corps of Discovery for getting drunk on duty. Collins received 100 lashes on his back and Hall received 50.
29-06-1860 The first iron-pile lighthouse was completed at Minot’s Ledge, MA.
29-06-1880 France annexed Tahiti.
29-06-1888 Professor Frederick Treves performed the first appendectomy in England.
29-06-1897 The Chicago Cubs scored 36 runs in a game against Louisville, setting a record for runs scored by a team in a single game.
29-06-1901 The first edition of "Editor & Publisher" was issued.
29-06-1903 The British government officially protested Belgian atrocities in the Congo.
29-06-1905 Russian troops intervened as riots erupted in ports all over the country. Many ships were looted.
29-06-1917 The Ukraine proclaimed independence from Russia.
29-06-1925 Marvin Pipkin filed for a patent for the frosted electric light bulb.
29-06-1926 Fascists in Rome added an hour to the work day in an economic efficiency measure.
29-06-1932 Siam’s army seized Bangkok and announced an end to the absolute monarchy.
29-06-1932 "Vic and Sade" debuted on NBC radio.
29-06-1941 Joe DiMaggio got a base hit in his 42nd consecutive game. He broke George Sisler's record from 1922.
29-06-1946 British authorities arrested more than 2,700 Jews in Palestine in an attempt to end alleged terrorism.
29-06-1950 U.S. President Harry S. Truman authorized a sea blockade of Korea.
29-06-1951 The United States invited the Soviet Union to the Korean peace talks on a ship in Wonson Harbor.
29-06-1953 The Federal Highway Act authorized the construction of 42,500 miles of freeway from coast to coast.
29-06-1954 The Atomic Energy Commission voted against reinstating Dr. J. Robert Oppenheimer's access to classified information.
29-06-1955 The Soviet Union sent tanks to Pozan, Poland, to put down anti-Communist demonstrations.
29-06-1956 Marilyn Monroe and Arthur Miller were married. They were divorced on January 20, 1961.
29-06-1966 The U.S. bombed fuel storage facilities near the North Vietnamese cities of Hanoi and Haiphong.
29-06-1967 Jayne Mansfield, at age 34, and two male companions died when their car struck a trailer truck east of New Orleans.
29-06-1967 Israel removed barricades, re-unifying Jerusalem.
29-06-1972 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the death penalty could constitute "cruel and unusual punishment." The ruling prompted states to revise their capital punishment laws.
29-06-1982 Israel invaded Lebanon.
29-06-1987 Vincent Van Gogh’s "Le Pont de Trinquetaille" was bought for $20.4 million at an auction in London, England.
29-06-1995 The shuttle Atlantis and the Russian space station Mir docked, forming the largest man-made satellite ever to orbit the Earth.
29-06-1995 501 people were killed when a department store in Seoul, South Korea collapsed. 900 others were injured.
29-06-1998 With negotiations on a new labor agreement at a standstill, the National Basketball Association (NBA) announced that a lockout would be imposed at midnight.
29-06-2000 In Santa Rosa, CA, the official groundbreaking ceremony took place for the Charles M. Schulz Museum.
29-06-2007 The Apple iPhone went on sale.
30-06-1097 The Crusaders defeated the Turks at Dorylaeum.
30-06-1841 The Erie Railroad rolled out its first passenger train.
30-06-1859 Charles Blondin crossed Niagara Falls on a tightrope.
30-06-1894 Korea declared independence from China and asked for Japanese aid.
30-06-1908 An explosion in Siberia, which knocked down trees in a 40-mile radius and struck people unconscious some 40 miles away. It was believed by some scientists to be caused by a falling fragment from a meteorite.
30-06-1912 Belgian workers went on strike to demand universal suffrage.
30-06-1913 Fighting broke out between Bulgaria and Greece and Spain. It was the beginning of the Second Balkan War.
30-06-1915 During World War I, the Second Battle Artois ended when the French failed to take Vimy Ridge.
30-06-1921 The Radio Corporation of America (RCA) was formed.
30-06-1922 Irish rebels in London assassinate Sir Henry Wilson, the British deputy for Northern Ireland.
30-06-1930 France pulled its troops out of Germany’s Rhineland.
30-06-1934 Adolf Hitler purged the Nazi Party by destroying the SA and bringing to power the SS in the "Night of the Long Knives."
30-06-1935 Fascists caused an uproar at the League of Nations when Haile Selassie of Ethiopia speaks.
30-06-1936 Margaret Mitchell’s book, "Gone with the Wind," was published in New York City.
30-06-1950 U.S. President Harry Truman ordered U.S. troops into Korea and authorizes the draft.
30-06-1951 On orders from Washington, General Matthew Ridgeway broadcasts that the United Nations was willing to discuss an armistice with North Korea.
30-06-1952 CBS-TV debuted "The Guiding Light."
30-06-1953 The first Corvette rolled off the Chevrolet assembly line in Flint, MI. It sold for $3,250.
30-06-1955 The U.S. began funding West Germany’s rearmament.
30-06-1957 The American occupation headquarters in Japan was dissolved.
30-06-1958 The U.S. Congress passed a law authorizing the admission of Alaska as the 49th state in the Union.
30-06-1960 The Katanga province seceded from Congo (upon Congo's independence from Belgium).
30-06-1962 Los Angeles Dodger Sandy Koufax pitched his first no-hitter in a game with the New York Mets.
30-06-1964 The last of U.N. troops left Congo after a four-year effort to bring stability to the country.
30-06-1970 The Cincinnati Reds moved to their new home at Riverfront Stadium.
30-06-1971 The U.S. Supreme Court allowed the New York Times to continue publishing the Pentagon Papers.
30-06-1971 The Soviet spacecraft Soyuz 11 returned to Earth. The three cosmonauts were found dead inside.
30-06-1971 The 26th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified when Ohio became the 38th state to approve it. The amendment lowered the minimum voting age to 18.
30-06-1974 Russian ballet dancer Mikhail Baryshnikov defected in Toronto, Canada.
30-06-1974 The July 4th scene from the Steven Spielberg movie "Jaws" was filmed.
30-06-1977 U.S. President Jimmy Carter announced his opposition to the B-1 bomber.
30-06-1984 The longest professional football game took place in the United States Football League (USFL). The Los Angeles Express beat the Michigan Panthers 27-21 after 93 minutes and 33 seconds.
30-06-1985 Thirty-nine American hostages were freed from a hijacked TWA jetliner in Beirut after being held for 17 days.
30-06-1985 Yul Brynner left his role as the King of Siam after 4,600 performances in "The King and I."
30-06-1986 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 5-4 that states could outlaw homosexual acts between consenting adults.
30-06-1994 The U.S. Figure Skating Association stripped Tonya Harding of the 1994 national championship and banned her from the organization for life for an attack on rival Nancy Kerrigan.
30-06-1998 Officials confirmed that the remains of a Vietnam War serviceman buried in the Tomb of the Unknowns at Arlington National Cemetery were identified as those of Air Force pilot Michael J. Blassie.
30-06-2000 U.S. President Clinton signed the E-Signature bill to give the same legal validity to an electronic signature as a signature in pen and ink.
01-03-1498 Vasco de Gama landed at what is now Mozambique on his way to India.
01-03-1562 In Vassy, France, Catholics massacred over 1,000 Huguenots. The event started the First War of Religion.
01-03-1692 In Salem Village, in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, the Salem witch trials began. Four women were the first to be charged.
01-03-1781 In America, the Continental Congress adopted the Articles of Confederation.
01-03-1784 In Great Britain, E. Kidner opened the first cooking school.
01-03-1790 The U.S. Congress authorized the first U.S. census.
01-03-1803 Ohio became the 17th U.S. state.
01-03-1810 Sweden became the first country to appoint an Ombudsman, Lars August Mannerheim.
01-03-1811 Egyptian ruler Mohammed Ali massacred the leaders of the Mameluke dynasty.
01-03-1815 Napoleon returned to France from the island of Elba. He had been forced to abdicate in April of 1814.
01-03-1845 U.S. President Tyler signed the congressional resolution to annex the Republic of Texas.
01-03-1862 Prussia formally recognized the Kingdom of Italy.
01-03-1864 Louis Ducos de Hauron patented a machine for taking and projecting motion pictures. The machine was never built.
01-03-1867 Nebraska became the 37th U.S. state.
01-03-1869 Postage stamps with scenes were issued for the first time.
01-03-1872 The U.S. Congress authorized the creation of Yellowstone National Park. It was the world's first national park.
01-03-1873 E. Remington and Sons of Ilion, NY, began the manufacturing the first practical typewriter.
01-03-1879 The library of Hawaii was established.
01-03-1890 "Literary Digest" was available for the first time.
01-03-1896 The Battle of Adowa began in Ethiopia between the forces of Emperor Menelik II and Italian troops. The Italians were defeated.
01-03-1900 In South Africa, Ladysmith was relieved by British troops after being under siege by the Boers for more than four months.
01-03-1907 In Odessa, Russia, there were only about 15,000 Jews left due to evacuations.
01-03-1907 In Spain, a royal decree abolished civil marriages.
01-03-1907 In New York, the Salvation Army opened an anti-suicide bureau.
01-03-1911 Industrialist Henry Frick acquired Velasquez's "Portrait of King Philip IV."
01-03-1911 Jose Ordonez was elected President of Uraguay.
01-03-1912 Captain Albert Berry made the first parachute jump from a moving airplane.
01-03-1927 The Bank of Italy became a National Bank.
01-03-1932 The 22-month-old son of Charles and Anne Lindbergh was kidnapped. The child was found dead in May.
01-03-1937 U.S. Steel raised workers’ wages to $5 a day.
01-03-1937 In Connecticut, the first permanent automobile license plates were issued.
01-03-1941 FM Radio began in Nashville, TN, when station W47NV began operations.
01-03-1941 Bulgaria joined the Axis powers by signing the Tripartite Pact.
01-03-1941 "Duffy’s Tavern" debuted on CBS Radio.
01-03-1947 The International Monetary Fund began operations.
01-03-1947 Chinese Premier T.V. Soong resigned.
01-03-1949 Joe Louis announced that he was retiring from boxing as world heavyweight boxing champion.
01-03-1950 Klaus Fuchs was convicted of giving U.S. atomic secrets to the Soviet Union.
01-03-1954 The United States announced that it had conducted a hydrogen bomb test on the Bikini Atoll in the Pacific Ocean.
01-03-1954 Five U.S. congressmen were wounded when four Puerto Rican nationalists opened fire from the gallery of the U.S. House of Representatives.
01-03-1959 Archbishop Makarios returned to Cyprus from exile.
01-03-1961 The Peace Corps was established by U.S. President Kennedy.
01-03-1962 Pakistan announced that it had a new constitution that set up a presidential system of government.
01-03-1966 The Soviet probe, Venera 3 crashed on the planet Venus. It was the first unmanned spacecraft to land on the surface of another planet.
01-03-1966 Ghana ordered all Soviet, East German and Chinese technicians to leave the country.
01-03-1969 Mickey Mantle announced his retirement from major league baseball.
01-03-1971 A bomb exploded in a restroom in the Senate wing of the U.S. Capitol. There were no injuries. A U.S. group protesting the Vietnam War claimed responsibility.
01-03-1974 Seven people were indicted in connection with the Watergate break-in. The charge was conspiring to obstruct justice.
01-03-1987 The Boston Celtics defeated Detroit 112-102 to post their 2,235th NBA win.
01-03-1987 S&H Green Stamps became S&H Green Seals. The stamps were introduced 90 years earlier.
01-03-1988 Soviet troops were sent into Azerbaijan after ethnic riots between Armenians and Azerbaijanis.
01-03-1989 In Washington, DC, Mayor Barry and the City council imposed a curfew on minors.
01-03-1990 In Cairo, 16 people were killed in a fire at the Sheraton Hotel.
01-03-1992 Bosnian Serb snipers fired upon civilians after a majority of the Moslem and Croatian communities voted in favor of Bosnia's independence.
01-03-1992 King Fahd of Saudi Arabia announced major political reforms that ceded some powers after 10 years of disciplined rule.
01-03-1992 Bosnian Muslims and Croats voted to secede from Yugoslavia.
01-03-1993 The U.S. government announced that the number of food stamp recipients had reached a record number of 26.6 million.
01-03-1994 Israel released about 500 Arab prisoners in an effort to placate Palestinians over the Hebron massacre.
01-03-1995 The European Parliament rejected legislation that would have allowed biotechnology companies to patent new life forms.
01-03-1999 The Angolan Embassy in Lusaka, Zambia, exploded. Four other bombs went off in the capital.
01-03-1999 In Uganda, eight tourists were brutally murdered by Hutu rebels.
01-03-2002 Operation Anaconda began in eastern Afghanistan. Allied forces were fighting against Taliban and Al Quaida fighters.
01-03-2003 In New York, a $250,000 Salvador Dali sketch was stolen from a display case in the lobby at Rikers Island jail. On June 17, 2003, it was announced that four corrections officers had surrendered and plead innocent in connection to the theft. The mixed-media composition was a sketch of the crucifixion.
01-03-2003 In the U.S., approximately 180,000 personnel from 22 different organizations around the government became part of the Department of Homeland Security. This completed the largest government reorganization since the beginning of the Cold War.
02-03-1807 The U.S. Congress passed an act to "prohibit the importation of slaves into any port or place within the jurisdiction of the United States... from any foreign kingdom, place, or country."
02-03-1836 Texas declared its independence from Mexico and an ad interim government was formed.
02-03-1861 The U.S. Congress created the Territory of Nevada.
02-03-1866 Excelsior Needle Company began making sewing machine needles.
02-03-1877 In the U.S., Rutherford B. Hayes was declared the winner of the 1876 presidential election by the U.S. Congress. Samuel J. Tilden, however, had won the popular vote on November 7, 1876.
02-03-1887 The American Trotting Association was organized in Detroit, MI.
02-03-1897 U.S. President Cleveland vetoed legislation that would have required a literacy test for immigrants entering the country.
02-03-1899 Mount Rainier National Park in Washington was established by the U.S. Congress.
02-03-1899 U.S. President McKinley signed a measure that created the rank of Admiral for the U.S. Navy. The first admiral was George Dewey.
02-03-1900 The U.S. Congress voted to give $2 million in aid to Puerto Rico.
02-03-1901 The first telegraph company in Hawaii opened.
02-03-1901 The U.S. Congress passed the Platt amendment, which limited Cuban autonomy as a condition for withdrawal of U.S. troops.
02-03-1903 The Martha Washington Hotel opened for business in New York City. The hotel had 416 rooms and was the first hotel exclusively for women.
02-03-1906 A tornado in Missouri killed 33 and did $5 million in damage.
02-03-1907 In Hamburg, Germany, dock workers went on strike after the end of the night shift. British strike breakers were brought in. The issue was settled on April 22, 1907.
02-03-1908 In New York, the Committee of the Russian Republican Administration was founded.
02-03-1908 In Paris, Gabriel Lippmann introduced three-dimensional color photography at the Academy of Sciences.
02-03-1911 Maurice Maeterlinck's "The Bluebird" opened in Paris.
02-03-1917 The Russian Revolution began with Czar Nicholas II abdicating.
02-03-1917 Citizens of Puerto Rico were granted U.S. citizenship with the enactment of the Jones Act.
02-03-1925 State and federal highway officials developed a nationwide route-numbering system and adopted the familiar U.S. shield-shaped, numbered marker.
02-03-1929 The U.S. Court of Customs & Patent Appeals was created by the U.S. Congress.
02-03-1933 The motion picture King Kong had its world premiere in New York.
02-03-1939 The Massachusetts legislature voted to ratify the Bill of Rights to the U.S. Constitution. These first ten amendments had gone into effect 147 years before.
02-03-1946 Ho Chi Minh was elected President of Vietnam.
02-03-1949 The B-50 Superfortress Lucky Lady II landed in Fort Worth, TX. The American plane had completed the first non-stop around-the-world flight.
02-03-1962 Wilt 'The Stilt' Chamberlain scored 100 points against the New York Knicks 169-147. Chamberlain broke several NBA records in the game.
02-03-1969 In Toulouse, France, the supersonic transport Concorde made its first test flight.
02-03-1974 Postage stamps jumped from 8 to 10 cents for first-class mail.
02-03-1984 The first McDonald's franchise was closed. A new location was opened across the street from the old location in Des Plaines, IL.
02-03-1985 The U.S. government approved a screening test for AIDS that detected antibodies to the virus that allowed possibly contaminated blood to be kept out of the blood supply.
02-03-1986 Corazon Aquino was sworn into office as president of the Philippines. Her first public declaration was to restore the civil rights of the citizens of her country.
02-03-1989 Representatives from the 12 European Community nations all agreed to ban all production of CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) by the end of the 20th century.
02-03-1995 Russian anti-corruption journalist Vladislav Listyev was killed by a gunman in Moscow.
02-03-1995 Nick Leeson was arrested for his role in the collapse of Britain's Barings Bank.
02-03-1998 The U.N. Security Council endorses U.N. chief Kofi Annan's deal to open Iraq's presidential palaces to arms inspectors.
02-03-1998 Images from the American spacecraft Galileo indicated that the Jupiter moon Europa has a liquid ocean and a source of interior heat.
02-03-2000 In Great Britain, Chile's former President Augusto Pinochet Ugarte was freed from house arrest and allowed to return to Chile. Britain's Home Secretary Jack Straw had concluded that Pinochet was mentally and physically unable to stand trial. Belgium, France, Spain and Switzerland had sought the former Chilean leader on human-rights violations.
02-03-2003 Over the Sea of Japan, there was a confrontation between four armed North Korean fighter jets and a U.S. RC-135S Cobra Ball. No shots were fired in the encounted in international airspace about 150 miles off North Korea's coast. The U.S. Air Force announced that it would resume reconnaissance flights on March 12.
02-03-2004 NASA announced that the Mars rover Opportunity had discovered evidence that water had existed on Mars in the past.
03-03-1791 The U.S. Congress passed a resolution that created the U.S. Mint.
03-03-1803 The first impeachment trial of a U.S. Judge, John Pickering, began.
03-03-1812 The U.S. Congress passed the first foreign aid bill.
03-03-1817 The first commercial steamboat route from Louisville to New Orleans was opened.
03-03-1845 Florida became the 27th U.S. state.
03-03-1845 The U.S. Congress passed legislation overriding a U.S. President’s veto. It was the first time the Congress had achieved this.
03-03-1849 The U.S. Department of the Interior was established.
03-03-1849 The Gold Coinage Act was passed by the U.S. Congress. It allowed the minting of gold coins.
03-03-1849 The U.S. Congress created the territory of Minnesota.
03-03-1851 The U.S. Congress authorized the 3-cent piece. It was the smallest U.S. silver coin.
03-03-1857 Britain and France declared war on China.
03-03-1863 Free city delivery of mail was authorized by the U.S. Postal Service.
03-03-1875 The U.S. Congress authorized the 20-cent piece. It was only used for 3 years.
03-03-1878 Russia and the Ottomans signed the treaty of Stenafano. The treaty granted independence to Serbia.
03-03-1885 The American Telephone and Telegraph (AT&T) was incorporated in New York as a subsidiary of the American Bell Telephone Company.
03-03-1885 The U.S. Post Office began offering special delivery for first-class mail.
03-03-1894 The "Atlantis" was first published. It was the first Greek newspaper in America.
03-03-1900 Striking miners in Germany returned to work.
03-03-1903 In St. Louis, MO, Barney Gilmore was arrested for spitting.
03-03-1903 The U.S. imposed a $2 head tax on immigrants.
03-03-1904 Wilhelm II of Germany made the first recording of a political document with Thomas Edison's cylinder.
03-03-1905 The Russian Czar agreed to create an elected assembly.
03-03-1906 A Frenchman tried the first flight in an airplane with tires.
03-03-1908 The U.S. government declared open war on on U.S. anarchists.
03-03-1909 Aviators Herring, Curtiss and Bishop announced that airplanes would be made commercially in the U.S.
03-03-1910 J.D. Rockefeller Jr. announced his withdrawal from business to administer his fathers fortune for an "uplift in humanity". He also appealed to the U.S. Congress for the creation of the Rockefeller Foundation.
03-03-1910 In New York, Robert Forest founded the National Housing Association to fight deteriorating urban living conditions.
03-03-1910 Nicaraguan rebels admitted defeat in open war and resorted to guerrilla tactics in the hope of U.S. intervention.
03-03-1915 The motion picture "Birth of a Nation" debuted in New York City.
03-03-1918 The Treaty of Brest Litovsky was signed by Germany, Austria and Russia. The treaty ended Russia's participation in World War I.
03-03-1923 The first issue of Time magazine was published.
03-03-1930 "Flying High" opened at the Apollo Theatre in New York City.
03-03-1931 The "Star Spangled Banner," written by Francis Scott Key, was adopted as the American national anthem. The song was originally a poem known as "Defense of Fort McHenry."
03-03-1938 A world record for the indoor mile run was set by Glenn Cunningham. He ran the distance in 4 minutes, 4.4 seconds.
03-03-1939 In Bombay, Ghandi began a fast to protest the state's autocratic rule.
03-03-1941 Moscow denounced the Axis rule in Bulgaria.
03-03-1945 Superman encountered Batman and Robin for the first time on the Mutual Broadcasting System.
03-03-1945 During World War II, Finland declared war on the Axis.
03-03-1952 "Whispering Streets" debuted on ABC Radio.
03-03-1952 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld New York's Feinberg Law that banned Communist teachers in the U.S.
03-03-1956 Morocco gained its independence.
03-03-1959 The San Francisco Giants had their new stadium officially named Candlestick Park.
03-03-1969 Apollo 9 was launched by NASA to test a lunar module.
03-03-1969 Sirhan Sirhan testified in a Los Angeles court that he killed Robert Kennedy.
03-03-1973 Japan disclosed its first defense plan since World War II.
03-03-1974 About 350 people died when a Turkish Airlines DC-10 crashed just after takeoff from Orly Airport in Paris.
03-03-1978 The remains of Charles Chaplin were stolen from his grave in Cosier-sur-Vevey, Switzerland. The body was recovered 11 weeks later near Lake Geneva.
03-03-1980 The submarine Nautilus was decommissioned. The vessels final voyage had ended on May 26, 1979.
03-03-1985 Women Against Pornography awarded its ‘Pig Award’ to Huggies Diapers. The activists claimed that the TV ads for diapers had "crossed the line between eye-catching and porn."
03-03-1985 The TV show "Moonlighting" premiered.
03-03-1991 25 people were killed when a United Airlines Boeing 737-200 crashed while on approach to the Colorado Springs airport.
03-03-1991 Rodney King was severely beaten by Los Angeles police officers. The scene was captured on amateur video.
03-03-1994 The Mexican government reached a peace agreement with the Chiapas rebels.
03-03-1995 A U.N. peacekeeping mission in Somalia ended. Several gunmen were killed by U.S. Marines in Mogadishu while overseeing the pull out of peacekeepers.
03-03-1999 In Egypt, 19 people were killed when a bus plunged into a Nile canal.
03-03-1999 Bertrand Piccard and Brian Jones began their attempt to circumnavigate the Earth in a hot air balloon non-stop. They succeeded on March 20, 1999.
04-03-1634 Samuel Cole opened the first tavern in Boston, MA.
04-03-1681 England's King Charles II granted a charter to William Penn for an area that later became the state of Pennsylvania.
04-03-1766 The British Parliament repealed the Stamp Act, which had caused bitter and violent opposition in the U.S. colonies.
04-03-1778 The Continental Congress voted to ratify the Treaty of Amity and Commerce and the Treaty of Alliance. The two treaties were the first entered into by the U.S. government.
04-03-1789 The first Congress of the United States met in New York and declared that the U.S. Constitution was in effect.
04-03-1791 Vermont was admitted as the 14th U.S. state. It was the first addition to the original 13 American colonies.
04-03-1794 The 11th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was passed by the U.S. Congress. The Amendment limited the jurisdiction of the federal courts to automatically hear cases brought against a state by the citizens of another state. Later interpretations expanded this to include citizens of the state being sued, as well.
04-03-1813 The Russians fighting against Napoleon reached Berlin. The French garrison evacuated the city without a fight.
04-03-1826 The first railroad in the U.S. was chartered. It was the Granite Railway in Quincy, MA.
04-03-1837 The state of Illinois granted a city charter to Chicago.
04-03-1861 The Confederate States of America adopted the "Stars and Bars" flag.
04-03-1877 Emile Berliner invented the microphone.
04-03-1880 Halftone engraving was used for the first time when the "Daily Graphic" was published in New York City.
04-03-1881 Eliza Ballou Garfield became the first mother of a U.S. President to live in the executive mansion.
04-03-1902 The American Automobile Association was founded in Chicago.
04-03-1904 In Korea, Russian troops retreated toward the Manchurian border as 100,000 Japanese troops advanced.
04-03-1908 The New York board of education banned the act of whipping students in school.
04-03-1908 France notified signatories of Algeciras that it would send troops to Chaouia, Morocco.
04-03-1914 Doctor Fillatre successfully separated Siamese twins.
04-03-1917 Jeanette Rankin of Montana took her seat as the first woman elected to the House of Representatives.
04-03-1925 Calvin Coolidge took the oath of office in Washington, DC. The presidential inauguration was broadcast on radio for the first time.
04-03-1930 Emma Fahning became the first woman bowler to bowl a perfect game in competition run by the Women’s International Bowling Congress in Buffalo, NY.
04-03-1933 U.S. President Roosevelt gave his inauguration speech in which he said "We have nothing to fear, but fear itself."
04-03-1933 Labor Secretary Frances Perkins became the first woman to serve in a Presidential administrative cabinet.
04-03-1942 "Junior Miss" starring Shirley Temple aired on CBS radio for the first time.
04-03-1942 The Stage Door Canteen opened on West 44th Street in New York City.
04-03-1944 Louis "Lepke" Buchalter, the head of Murder, Inc., was executed for murder at Sing Sing Prison in Ossining, NY. He was the leader of U.S. organized crime during the 1930's.
04-03-1946 Canada reported that it had uncovered a spy ring that had been organized by the Soviet Embassy in Ottawa. All four people accused admitted to being involved.
04-03-1947 France and Britain signed an alliance treaty.
04-03-1950 Walt Disney’s "Cinderella" was released.
04-03-1952 U.S. President Harry Truman dedicated the "Courier," the first seagoing radio broadcasting station.
04-03-1952 Ronald Reagan and Nancy Davis were married.
04-03-1954 In Boston, Peter Bent Brigham Hospital reported the first successful kidney transplant.
04-03-1963 Six people received a death sentence in Paris for plotting to kill French President Charles de Gaulle.
04-03-1975 Queen Elizabeth knighted Charlie Chaplin.
04-03-1977 More than 1,500 people were killed in an earthquake that affected southern and eastern Europe.
04-03-1986 "Today" debuted in London as England’s newest, national, daily newspaper.
04-03-1989 Time, Inc. and Warner Communications Inc. announced a plan to merge.
04-03-1991 Sheik Saad al-Jaber al-Sabah, the prime minister of Kuwait, returned to his country for the first time since Iraq's invasion.
04-03-1993 Authorities announced the arrest of Mohammad Salameh. He was later convicted for his role in the World Trade Center Bombing in New York City.
04-03-1994 Bosnia's Croats and Moslems signed an agreement to form a federation in a loose economic union with Croatia.
04-03-1994 Four extremists were convicted in the World Trade Center bombing in which six people were killed and more than a thousand were injured.
04-03-1997 U.S. President Clinton barred federal spending on human cloning.
04-03-1998 Microsoft repaired software that apparently allowed hackers to shut down computers in government and university offices nationwide.
04-03-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court said that federal law banned on-the-job sexual harassment even when both parties are the same sex.
04-03-1999 Monica Lewinsky's book about her affair with U.S. President Clinton went on sale in the U.S.
04-03-1999 U.S. Marine Captain Richard Ashby was acquitted in a military court of the charge of recklessly flying his jet. 20 people were killed in Italy when his jet hit a gondola cable.
04-03-2002 Canada banned human embryo cloning but permitted government-funded scientists to use embryos left over from fertility treatment or abortions.
04-03-2003 In the southern Philippines, a bomb hidden in a backpack exploded and killed at least 19 people at an airport.
04-03-2003 In the Indian-controlled portion of Kashmir, at least 9 people were killed and 52 were injured when a bus fell into a deep gorge.
05-03-1623 The first alcohol temperance law in the colonies was enacted in Virginia.
05-03-1624 In the American colony of Virginia, the upper class was exempted from whipping by legislation.
05-03-1750 "King Richard III" was performed in New York City. It was the first Shakespearean play to be presented in America.
05-03-1766 The first Spanish governor of Louisiana, Antonio de Ulloa, arrived in New Orleans.
05-03-1770 "The Boston Massacre" took place when British troops fired on a crowd in Boston killing five people. Two British troops were later convicted of manslaughter.
05-03-1793 Austrian troops defeated the French and recaptured Liege.
05-03-1836 Samuel Colt manufactured the first pistol (.34-caliber).
05-03-1842 A Mexican force of over 500 men under Rafael Vasquez invaded Texas for the first time since the revolution. They briefly occupied San Antonio, but soon headed back to the Rio Grande.
05-03-1845 The U.S. Congress appropriated $30,000 to ship camels to the western U.S.
05-03-1864 For the first time, Oxford met Cambridge in track and field competition in England.
05-03-1867 An abortive Fenian uprising against English rule took place in Ireland.
05-03-1868 The U.S. Senate was organized into a court of impeachment to decide charges against President Andrew Johnson.
05-03-1872 George Westinghouse patented the air brake.
05-03-1900 The American Hall of Fame was founded.
05-03-1900 Two U.S. battleships leave for Nicaragua to halt revolutionary disturbances.
05-03-1901 Germany and Britain began negotiations with hopes of creating an alliance.
05-03-1902 In France, the National Congress of Miners decided to call for a general strike for an 8-hour day.
05-03-1905 Russian troops began their retreat from Mukden in Manchuria, China. Over 100,000 had been killed in 3 days of fighting.
05-03-1907 In St. Petersburg, Russia, the new Duma opened. 40,000 demonstrators were dispersed by troops.
05-03-1910 In Philadelphia, PA, 60,000 people left their jobs to show support for striking transit workers.
05-03-1910 The Moroccan envoy signed the 1909 agreement with France.
05-03-1912 The Italians became the first to use dirigibles for military purposes. They used them for reconnaissance flights behind Turkish lines west of Tripoli.
05-03-1918 The Soviets moved the capital of Russia from Petrograd to Moscow.
05-03-1922 Phoebe Anne Oakley Mozee broke all existing records for women's trap shooting. She hit 98 out of 100 targets.
05-03-1923 Old-age pension laws were enacted in the states of Montana and Nevada.
05-03-1924 Frank Caruana of Buffalo, NY, became the first bowler to roll two perfect games in a row.
05-03-1933 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt ordered a four-day bank holiday in order to stop large amounts of money from being withdrawn from banks.
05-03-1933 The Nazi Party won 44 percent of the vote in German parliamentary elections.
05-03-1934 In Amarillo, TX, the first Mother's-In-Law Day was celebrated.
05-03-1943 Germany called fifteen and sixteen year olds for military service due to war losses.
05-03-1946 Winston Churchill delivered his "Iron Curtain Speech".
05-03-1946 The U.S. sent protests to the U.S.S.R. on incursions into Manchuria and Iran.
05-03-1953 Soviet dictator Joseph Stalin died. He had been in power for 29 years.
05-03-1956 The U.S. Supreme Court affirmed the ban on segregation in public schools.
05-03-1969 Gustav Heinemann was elected West German President.
05-03-1970 A nuclear non-proliferation treaty went into effect after 43 nations ratified it.
05-03-1976 The British pound fell below the equivalent of $2 for the first time in history.
05-03-1977 U.S. President Jimmy Carter appeared on CBS News with Walter Cronkite for the first "Dial-a-President" radio talk show.
05-03-1982 John Belushi died in Los Angeles of a drug overdose at the age of 33.
05-03-1984 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that cities had the right to display the Nativity scene as part of their Christmas display.
05-03-1985 Mike Bossy, of the New York Islanders, became the first National Hockey League player to score 50 goals in eight consecutive seasons.
05-03-1993 Cuban President Fidel Castro said that Hillary Clinton is "a beautiful woman."
05-03-1993 Sprinter Ben Johnson was banned from racing for life by the Amateur Athletic Association after testing positive for banned performance-enhancing substances for a second time.
05-03-1997 North Korea and South Korea met for first time in 25 years for peace talks.
05-03-1997 Chuck Niles received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
05-03-1998 NASA announced that an orbiting craft had found enough water on the moon to support a human colony and rocket fueling station.
