05-03-1998 It was announced that Air Force Lt. Col. Eileen Collins would lead crew of Columbia on a mission to launch a large X-ray telescope. She was the first woman to command a space shuttle mission.
05-03-2004 Martha Stewart was found guilty of lying about the reason for selling 3,298 shares of ImClone Systems stock, conspiracy, making false statement and obstruction of justice.
06-03-1521 Ferdinand Magellan discovered Guam.
06-03-1808 At Harvard University, the first college orchestra was founded.
06-03-1820 The Missouri Compromise was enacted by the U.S. Congress and signed by U.S. President James Monroe. The act admitted Missouri into the Union as a slave state, but prohibited slavery in the rest of the northern Louisiana Purchase territory.
06-03-1834 The city of York in Upper Canada was incorporated as Toronto.
06-03-1836 The thirteen-day siege of the Alamo by Santa Anna and his army ended. The Mexican army of three thousand men defeated the 189 Texas volunteers.
06-03-1854 At the Washington Monument, several men stole the Pope's Stone from the lapidarium.
06-03-1857 The U.S. Supreme Court's Dred Scott decision ruled that blacks could not sue in federal court to be citizens.
06-03-1886 "The Nightingale" was first published. It was the first magazine for nurses.
06-03-1899 Aspirin was patented by German researchers Felix Hoffman and Hermann Dreser.
06-03-1900 In West Virginia, an explosion trapped 50 coal miners underground.
06-03-1901 An assassin tried to kill Wilhelm II of Germany in Bremen.
06-03-1907 British creditors of the Dominican Republic claimed that the U.S. had failed to collect debts.
06-03-1928 A Communist attack on Peking, China resulted in 3,000 dead and 50,000 fled to Swatow.
06-03-1939 In Spain, Jose Miaja took over the Madrid government after a military coup and vowed to seek "peace with honor."
06-03-1941 Les Hite and his orchestra recorded "The World is Waiting for the Sunrise".
06-03-1944 During World War II, U.S. heavy bombers began the first American raid on Berlin. Allied planes dropped 2000 tons of bombs.
06-03-1946 Ho Chi Minh, the President of Vietnam, struck an agreement with France that recognized his country as an autonomous state within the Indochinese Federation and the French Union.
06-03-1947 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld the contempt conviction of John L. Lewis.
06-03-1947 Winston Churchill announced that he opposed British troop withdrawals from India.
06-03-1947 The first air-conditioned naval ship, "The Newport News," was launched from Newport News, VA.
06-03-1957 The British African colonies of the Gold Coast and Togoland became the independent state of Ghana.
06-03-1960 Switzerland granted women the right to vote in municipal elections.
06-03-1960 The United States announced that it would send 3,500 troops to Vietnam.
06-03-1964 Tom O’Hara set a new world indoor record when he ran the mile in 3 minutes, 56.4 seconds.
06-03-1967 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson announced his plan to establish a draft lottery.
06-03-1970 Charles Manson released his album "Lies" to finance his defense against murder charges.
06-03-1973 U.S. President Richard Nixon imposed price controls on oil and gas.
06-03-1975 Iran and Iraq announced that they had settled their border dispute.
06-03-1980 Islamic militants in Tehran said that they would turn over American hostages to the Revolutionary Council.
06-03-1981 Walter Cronkite appeared on his last episode of "CBS Evening News with Walter Cronkite." He had been on the job 19 years.
06-03-1981 U.S. President Reagan announced a plan to cut 37,000 federal jobs.
06-03-1982 National Basketball Association history was made when San Antonio beat Milwaukee 171-166 in three overtime periods to set the record for most points by two teams in a game. The record was beaten on December 13, 1983 by the Pistons and the Nuggets when they played to a final score of 186-184
06-03-1983 The United States Football League began its first season of pro football competition.
06-03-1985 Yul Brynner played his his 4,500th performance in the musical "The King and I."
06-03-1987 The British ferry Herald of Free Enterprise capsized in the Channel off the coast of Belgium. 189 people died.
06-03-1990 In Afghanistan, an attempted coup to remove President Najibullah from office failed.
06-03-1990 The Russian Parliament passed a law that sanctioned the ownership of private property.
06-03-1991 In Paris, five men were jailed for plotting to smuggle Libyan arms to the Irish Republican Army.
06-03-1992 The last episode of "The Cosby Show" aired. The show had been on since September of 1984.
06-03-1992 The computer virus "Michelangelo" went into effect.
06-03-1997 A gunman stole "Tete de Femme," a million-dollar Picasso portrait, from a London gallery. The painting was recovered a week later.
06-03-1997 Britain's Queen Elizabeth II launched the first official royal Web site.
06-03-1998 A Connecticut state lottery accountant gunned down three supervisors and the lottery chief before killing himself.
07-03-1774 The British closed the port of Boston to all commerce.
07-03-1799 In Palestine, Napoleon captured Jaffa and his men massacred more than 2,000 Albanian prisoners.
07-03-1848 In Hawaii, the Great Mahele was signed.
07-03-1849 The Austrian Reichstag was dissolved.
07-03-1850 U.S. Senator Daniel Webster endorsed the Compromise of 1850 as a method of preserving the Union.
07-03-1854 Charles Miller received a patent for the sewing machine.
07-03-1876 Alexander Graham Bell received a patent (U.S. Patent No. 174,465) for his telephone.
07-03-1901 It was announced that blacks had been found enslaved in parts of South Carolina.
07-03-1904 The Japanese bombed the Russian town of Vladivostok.
07-03-1904 In Springfield, OH, a mob broke into a jail and shot a black man accused of murder.
07-03-1906 Finland granted women the right to vote.
07-03-1908 Cincinnati's mayor, Mark Breith announced before the city council that, "Women are not physically fit to operate automobiles."
07-03-1911 Willis Farnworth patented the coin-operated locker.
07-03-1911 In the wake of the Mexican Revolution, the U.S. sent 20,000 troops to the border of Mexico.
07-03-1918 Finland signed an alliance treaty with Germany.
07-03-1925 The Soviet Red Army occupied Outer Mongolia.
07-03-1927 A Texas law that banned Negroes from voting was ruled unconstitutional by the U.S. Supreme Court.
07-03-1933 CBS radio debuted "Marie The Little French Princess." It was the first daytime radio serial.
07-03-1933 The board game Monopoly was invented.
07-03-1935 Malcolm Campbell set an auto speed record of 276.8 mph in Florida.
07-03-1936 Hitler sent German troops into the Rhineland in violation of the Locarno Pact and the Treaty of Versailles.
07-03-1942 Japanese troops landed on New Guinea.
07-03-1945 During World War II, U.S. forces crossed the Rhine River at Remagen, Germany.
07-03-1947 John L. Lewis declared that only a totalitarian regime could prevent strikes.
07-03-1951 U.N. forces in Korea under General Matthew Ridgeway launched Operation Ripper against the Chinese.
07-03-1954 Russia appeared for the first time in ice-hockey competition. Russia defeated Canada 7-2 to win the world ice-hockey title in Stockholm, Sweden.
07-03-1955 "Peter Pan" was presented as a television special for the first time.
07-03-1955 Baseball commissioner Ford Frick said that he was in favor of legalizing the spitball.
07-03-1955 Phyllis Diller made her debut at the Purple Onion in San Francisco, CA.
07-03-1959 Melvin C. Garlow became the first pilot to fly over a million miles in jet airplanes.
07-03-1965 State troopers and a sheriff's posse broke up a march by civil rights demonstrators in Selma, AL.
07-03-1968 The Battle of Saigon came to an end.
07-03-1971 A thousand U.S. planes bombed Cambodia and Laos.
07-03-1975 The U.S. Senate revised the filibuster rule. The new rule allowed 60 senators to limit debate instead of the previous two-thirds.
07-03-1981 Anti-government guerrillas in Colombia executed the kidnapped American Bible translator Chester Allen Bitterman. The guerrillas accused Bitterman of being a CIA agent.
07-03-1983 TNN (The Nashville Network) began broadcasting.
07-03-1985 "Commonwealth" magazine ceased publication after five decades.
07-03-1985 The first AIDS antibody test, an ELISA-type test, was released.
07-03-1987 Mike Tyson became the youngest heavyweight titleholder when he beat James Smith in a decision during a 12-round fight in Las Vegas, NV.
07-03-1989 Poland accused the Soviet Union of a World War II massacre in Katyn.
07-03-1994 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that parodies that poke fun at an original work can be considered "fair use" that does not require permission from the copyright holder.
07-03-1994 In Moldova, a referendum was rejected by 90% of voters to form a union with Rumania.
07-03-1999 In El Salvador, Francisco Flores Pérez of the ruling Nationalist Republican Alliance (Arena) was elected president.
07-03-2002 A federal judge awarded Anna Nicole Smith more than $88 million in damages. The ruling was the latest in a legal battle over the estate of Smith's late husband, J. Howard Marshall II.
07-03-2003 Scientists at the Stanford Linear Accelerator Center announced that they had transferred 6.7 gigabytes of uncompressed data from Sunnvale, CA, to Amsterdam, Netherlands, in 58 seconds. The data was sent via fiber-optic cables and traveled 6,800 miles.
08-03-1618 Johann Kepler discovered the third Law of Planetary Motion.
08-03-1702 England's Queen Anne took the throne upon the death of King William III.
08-03-1782 The Gnadenhutten massacre took place. About 90 Indians were killed by militiamen in Ohio in retaliation for raids carried out by other Indians.
08-03-1853 The first bronze statue of Andrew Jackson is unveiled in Washington, DC.
08-03-1855 A train passed over the first railway suspension bridge at Niagara Falls, NY.
08-03-1862 The Confederate ironclad "Merrimack" was launched.
08-03-1880 U.S. President Rutherford B. Hays declared that the United States would have jurisdiction over any canal built across the isthmus of Panama.
08-03-1887 The telescopic fishing rod was patented by Everett Horton.
08-03-1894 A dog license law was enacted in the state of New York. It was the first animal control law in the U.S.
08-03-1904 The Bundestag in Germany lifted the ban on the Jesuit order of priests.
08-03-1905 In Russia, it was reported that the peasant revolt was spreading to Georgia.
08-03-1907 The British House of Commons turned down a women's suffrage bill.
08-03-1909 Pope Pius X lifted the church ban on interfaith marriages in Hungary.
08-03-1910 In France, Baroness de Laroche became the first woman to obtain a pilot's license.
08-03-1910 The King of Spain authorized women to attend universities.
08-03-1911 In Europe, International Women's Day was celebrated for the first time.
08-03-1911 British Minister of Foreign Affairs Edward Gray declared that Britain would not support France in the event of a military conflict.
08-03-1917 Russia's "February Revolution" began with rioting and strikes in St. Petersburg. The revolution was called the "February Revolution" due to Russia's use of the Old Style calendar.
08-03-1917 The U.S. Senate voted to limit filibusters by adopting the cloture rule.
08-03-1921 Spanish Premier Eduardo Dato was assassinated while leaving the Parliament in Madrid.
08-03-1921 French troops occupied Dusseldorf.
08-03-1933 Self-liquidating scrip money was issued for the first time at Franklin, IN.
08-03-1941 Martial law was proclaimed in Holland in order to extinguish any anti-Nazi protests.
08-03-1942 During World War II, Japanese forces captured Rangoon, Burma.
08-03-1943 Japanese forces attacked American troops on Hill 700 in Bougainville. The battle lasted five days.
08-03-1945 Phyllis Mae Daley received a commission in the U.S. Navy Nurse Corps. She later became the first African-American nurse to serve duty in World War II.
08-03-1946 In New York City, the "Journal American" became the first commercial business to receive a helicopter license.
08-03-1946 The French naval fleet arrived at Haiphong, Vietnam.
08-03-1948 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that religious instruction in public schools was unconstitutional.
08-03-1953 A census bureau report indicated that 239,000 farmers had quit farming over the last 2 years.
08-03-1954 France and Vietnam opened talks in Paris on a treaty to form the state of Indochina.
08-03-1954 Herb McKenley set a world record for the quarter mile when he ran the distance in 46.8 seconds.
08-03-1957 The International Boxing Club was ruled a monopoly putting it in violation of the Sherman Anti-Trust Law.
08-03-1959 Groucho, Chico and Harpo made their final TV appearance together.
08-03-1961 Max Conrad circled the globe in a record time of eight days, 18 hours and 49 minutes in the Piper Aztec.
08-03-1965 The U.S. landed about 3,500 Marines in South Vietnam. They were the first U.S. combat troops to land in Vietnam.
08-03-1966 Australia announced that it would triple the number of troops in Vietnam.
08-03-1973 Two bombs exploded near Trafalgar Square in Great Britain. 234 people were injured.
08-03-1982 The U.S. accused the Soviets of killing 3,000 Afghans with poison gas.
08-03-1985 The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) reported that 407,700 Americans were millionaires. That was more than double the total from just five years before.
08-03-1986 Four French television crewmembers were abducted in west Beirut. All four were eventually released.
08-03-1988 In Fort Campbell, KY, 17 U.S. soldiers were killed when two Army helicopters collided in midair.
08-03-1989 In Lhasa, Tibet, martial law was declared after three days of protest against Chinese rule.
08-03-1999 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld the conviction of Timothy McVeigh for the bombing of a federal building in Oklahoma City in 1995.
08-03-1999 The White House, under President Bill Clinton, directed the firing of nuclear scientist Wen Ho Lee from his job at the Los Alamos National Laboratory. The firing was a result of alleged security violations.
08-03-2001 The U.S. House of Representatives voted for an across-the-board tax cut of nearly $1 trillion over the next decade.
08-03-2005 In norther Chechnya, Chechen rebel leader Aslan Maskhadov was killed during a raid by Russian forces.
09-03-1454 Amerigo Vespucci was born in Florence, Italy. Matthias Ringmann, a German mapmaker, named the American continent in his honor.
09-03-1617 The Treaty of Stolbovo ended the occupation of Northern Russia by Swedish troops.
09-03-1734 The Russians took Danzig (Gdansk) in Poland.
09-03-1745 The first carillon was shipped from England to Boston, MA.
09-03-1788 Connecticut became the 5th state to join the United States.
09-03-1793 Jean Pierre Blanchard made the first balloon flight in North America. The event was witnessed by U.S. President George Washington.
09-03-1796 Napoleon Bonaparte and Josephine de Beauharnais were married. They were divorced in 1809.
09-03-1799 The U.S. Congress contracted with Simeon North, of Berlin, CT, for 500 horse pistols at the price of $6.50 each.
09-03-1812 Swedish Pomerania was seized by Napoleon.
09-03-1820 The U.S. Congress passed the Land Act that paved the way for westward expansion of North America.
09-03-1822 Charles M. Graham received the first patent for artificial teeth.
09-03-1832 Abraham Lincoln announced that he would run for a political office for the first time. He was unsuccessful in his run for a seat in the Illinois state legislature.
09-03-1839 The French Academy of Science announced the Daguerreotype photo process.
09-03-1858 Albert Potts was awarded a patent for the letter box.
09-03-1859 The National Association of Baseball Players adopted the rule that limited the size of bats to no more than 2-1/2 inches in diameter.
09-03-1860 The first Japanese ambassador to the U.S. was appointed.
09-03-1862 During the U.S. Civil War, the ironclads Monitor and Virginia fought to a draw in a five-hour battle at Hampton Roads, Virginia.
09-03-1863 General Ulysses Grant was appointed commander-in-chief of the Union forces.
09-03-1900 In Germany, women petition Reichstag for the right to take university entrance exams.
09-03-1905 In Egypt, U.S. archeologist Davies discovered the royal tombs of Tua and Yua.
09-03-1905 In Manchuria, Japanese troops surrounded 200,000 Russian troops that were retreating from Mudken.
09-03-1905 In Congo, Belgian Vice Gov. Costermans committed suicide following an investigation of colonial policy.
09-03-1906 In the Philippines, fifteen Americans and 600 Moros were killed in the last two days of fighting.
09-03-1909 The French National Assembly passed an income tax bill.
09-03-1910 Union men urged for a national sympathy strike for miners in Pennsylvania.
09-03-1911 The funding for five new battleships was added to the British military defense budget.
09-03-1916 Mexican raiders led by Pancho Villa attacked Columbus, New Mexico. 17 people were killed by the 1,500 horsemen.
09-03-1929 Eric Krenz became the first athlete to toss the discus over 160 feet.
09-03-1932 Eamon De Valera was elected president of the Irish Free State and pledged to abolish all loyalty to the British Crown.
09-03-1933 The U.S. Congress began its 100 days of enacting New Deal legislation.
09-03-1936 The German press warned that all Jews who vote in the upcoming elections would be arrested.
09-03-1945 "Those Websters" debuted on CBS radio.
09-03-1945 During World War II, U.S. B-29 bombers launched incendiary bomb attacks against Japan.
09-03-1946 The A.F.L. accused Juan Peron of using the army to establish a dictatorship over Argentine labor.
09-03-1949 The first all-electric dining car was placed in service on the Illinois Central Railroad.
09-03-1954 WNBT-TV (now WNBC-TV), in New York, broadcast the first local color television commercials. The ad was Castro Decorators of New York City.
09-03-1956 British authorities arrested and deported Archbishop Makarios from Cyprus. He was accused of supporting terrorists.
09-03-1957 Egyptian leader Nasser barred U.N. plans to share the tolls for the use of the Suez Canal.
09-03-1964 The first Ford Mustang rolled off of the Ford assembly line.
09-03-1965 The first U.S. combat troops arrived in South Vietnam.
09-03-1967 Svetlana Alliluyeva, Josef Stalin's daughter defected to the United States.
09-03-1969 "The Smothers Brothers' Comedy Hour" was canceled by CBS-TV.
09-03-1975 Work began on the Alaskan oil pipeline.
09-03-1975 Iraq launched an offensive against the rebel Kurds.
09-03-1977 About a dozen armed Hanafi Muslims invaded three buildings in Washington, DC. They killed one person and took more than 130 hostages. The siege ended two days later.
09-03-1985 "Gone With The Wind" went on sale in video stores across the U.S. for the first time.
09-03-1986 U.S. Navy divers found the crew compartment of the space shuttle Challenger along with the remains of the astronauts.
09-03-1987 Chrysler Corporation offered to buy American Motors Corporation.
09-03-1989 The U.S. Senate rejected John Tower as a choice for a cabinet member. It was the first rejection in 30 years.
09-03-1989 In Maylasia, 30 Asian nations conferred on the issue of "boat people".
09-03-1989 In the U.S., a strike forced Eastern Airlines into bankruptcy.
09-03-1989 In the U.S., President Bush urged for a mandatory death penalty in drug-related killings.
09-03-1990 Dr. Antonia Novello was sworn in as the first female and Hispanic surgeon general.
09-03-1993 Rodney King testified at the federal trial of four Los Angeles police officers accused of violating his civil rights.
09-03-1995 The Canadian Navy arrested a Spanish trawler for illegally fishing off of Newfoundland.
09-03-2000 In Norway, the coalition government of Kjell Magne Bondevik resigned as a result of an environmental dispute.
10-03-1496 Christopher Columbus concluded his second visit to the Western Hemisphere when he left Hispaniola for Spain.
10-03-1629 England's King Charles I dissolved Parliament and did not call it back for 11 years.
10-03-1656 In the American colony of Virginia, suffrage was extended to all free men regardless of their religion.
10-03-1776 "Common Sense" by Thomas Paine was published.
10-03-1785 Thomas Jefferson was appointed minister to France. He succeeded Benjamin Franklin.
10-03-1792 John Stone patented the pile driver.
10-03-1804 The formal ceremonies transferring the Louisiana Purchase from France to the U.S. took place in St. Louis.
10-03-1806 The Dutch in Cape Town, South Africa surrendered to the British.
10-03-1814 In France, Napoleon Bonaparte was defeated by a combined Allied Army at the battle of Laon.
10-03-1848 The U.S. Senate ratified the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, which ended the war with Mexico.
10-03-1849 Abraham Lincoln applied for a patent for a device to lift vessels over shoals by means of inflated cylinders.
10-03-1864 Ulysses S. Grant became commander of the Union armies in the U.S. Civil War.
10-03-1876 Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful call with the telephone. He spoke the words "Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you."
10-03-1880 The Salvation Army arrived in the U.S. from England.
10-03-1893 New Mexico State University canceled its first graduation ceremony because the only graduate was robbed and killed the night before.
10-03-1894 New York Gov. Roswell P. Flower signed the nation's first dog-licensing law.
10-03-1902 The Boers of South Africa scored their last victory over the British, when they captured British General Methuen and 200 men.
10-03-1902 Tochangri, Turkey, was entirely wiped out by an earthquake.
10-03-1902 U.S. Attorney General Philander Knox announced that a suit was being brought against Morgan and Harriman's Northern Securities Company. The suit was enforcement of the Sherman Antitrust Act. Northern Securities loss in court was upheld by the U.S. Supreme Court on March 14, 1904.
10-03-1903 Harry C. Gammeter patented the multigraph duplicating machine.
10-03-1903 In New York's harbor, the disease-stricken ship Karmania was quarantined with six dead from cholera.
10-03-1906 In France, 1,200 miners were buried in an explosion at Courrieres.
10-03-1909 Britain extracted territorial concessions from Siam and Malaya.
10-03-1910 Slavery was abolished in China.
10-03-1912 China became a republic after the overthrow of the Manchu Ch'ing Dynasty.
10-03-1913 William Knox rolled the first perfect 300 game in tournament competition.
10-03-1924 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld a New York state law forbidding late-night work for women.
10-03-1927 Prussia lifted its Nazi ban allowing Adolf Hitler to speak in public.
10-03-1933 Nevada became the first U.S. state to regulate drugs.
10-03-1940 W2XBS-TV in New York City aired the first televised opera as it presented scenes from "I Pagliacci".
10-03-1941 The Brooklyn Dodgers announced that their players would begin wearing batting helmets during the 1941 season.
10-03-1941 Vichy France threatened to use its navy unless Britain allowed food to reach France.
10-03-1944 The Irish refused to oust all Axis envoys and denied the accusation of spying on Allied troops.
10-03-1945 American B-29 bombers attacked Tokyo, Japan, 100,000 were killed.
10-03-1947 The Big Four met in Moscow to discuss the future of Germany.
10-03-1947 Poland and Czechoslovakia signed a 20-year mutual aid pact.
10-03-1949 Nazi wartime broadcaster Mildred E. Gillars, also known as "Axis Sally," was convicted in Washington, DC. Gillars was convicted of treason and served 12 years in prison.
10-03-1953 North Korean gunners at Wonsan fired upon the USS Missouri. The ship responded by firing 998 rounds at the enemy position.
10-03-1955 The last broadcast of "The Silver Eagle" was heard on radio.
10-03-1956 Julie Andrews at the age of 23 made her TV debut in "High Tor" with Bing Crosby and Nancy Olson.
10-03-1959 "Sweet Bird of Youth", a play by Tennessee Williams, opened in New York City.
10-03-1965 Walter Matthau and Art Carney opened in "The Odd Couple". It later became a hit on television.
10-03-1966 The North Vietnamese captured a Green Beret camp at Ashau Valley.
10-03-1966 France withdrew from NATO's military command to protest U.S. dominance of the alliance and asked NATO to move its headquarters from Paris.
10-03-1969 James Earl Ray plead guilty in Memphis, TN, to the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. Ray later repudiated the guilty plea and maintained his innocence until his death in April of 1998.
10-03-1971 The U.S. Senate approved an amendment to lower the voting age to 18.
10-03-1975 The North Vietnamese Army attacked the South Vietnamese town of Ban Me Thout.
10-03-1980 Iran's leader, Ayatollah Khomeini, lent his support to the militants holding American hostages in Tehran.
10-03-1982 The U.S. banned Libyan oil imports due to their continued support of terrorism.
10-03-1986 The Wrigley Company, of Chicago, raised the price of its seven-stick pack of Wrigley’s chewing gum from a quarter to 30 cents.
10-03-1987 The Vatican condemned surrogate parenting as well as test-tube and artificial insemination.
10-03-1990 Haitian President Prosper Avril was ousted 18 months after seizing power in a coup.
10-03-1991 "Phase Echo" began. It was the operation to withdraw 540,000 U.S. troops from the Persian Gulf region.
10-03-1994 White House officials began testifying before a federal grand jury about the Whitewater controversy.
10-03-1995 U.S. Secretary of State Warren Christopher told Yasser Arafat that he must do more to curb Palestinian terrorists.
10-03-1998 U.S. troops in the Persian Gulf began receiving the first vaccinations against anthrax.
10-03-2002 The Associated Press reported that the Pentagon informed the U.S. Congress in January that it was making contingency plans for the possible use of nuclear weapons against countries that threaten the U.S. with weapons of mass destruction, including Iraq and North Korea.
10-03-2003 North Korea test-fired a short-range missile. The event was one of several in a patter of unusual military maneuvers.
11-03-1302 The characters Romeo and Juliet were married this day according to William Shakespeare.
11-03-1649 The peace of Rueil was signed between the Frondeurs (rebels) and the French government.
11-03-1665 A new legal code was approved for the Dutch and English towns, guaranteeing religious observances unhindered.
11-03-1702 The Daily Courant, the first regular English newspaper was published.
11-03-1791 Samuel Mulliken became the first person to receive more than one patent from the U.S. Patent Office.
11-03-1810 The Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte was married by proxy to Archduchess Marie Louise of Austria.
11-03-1824 The U.S. War Department created the Bureau of Indian Affairs. Seneca Indian Ely Parker became the first Indian to lead the Bureau.
11-03-1845 Seven hundred Maoris led by their chief, Hone-Heke, burned the small town of Kororareka. The act was in protest to the settlement of Maoriland by Europeans, which was a breach of the 1840 Treaty of Waitangi.
11-03-1847 John Chapman 'Johnny Appleseed' died in Allen County, Indiana. This day became known as Johnny Appleseed Day.
11-03-1861 A Confederate Convention was held in Montgomery, Alabama, where a new constitution was adopted.
11-03-1865 Union General William Sherman and his forces occupied Fayetteville, NC.
11-03-1867 In Hawaii, the volcano Great Mauna Loa erupted.
11-03-1882 The Intercollegiate Lacrosse Association was formed in Princeton, NJ.
11-03-1888 The "Blizzard of '88" began along the U.S. Atlantic Seaboard shutting down communication and transportation lines. More than 400 people died.(March 11-14)
11-03-1900 British Prime Minister Lord Salisbury rejected the peace overtures offered from the Boer leader Paul Kruger.
11-03-1901 Britain rejected an amended treaty to the canal agreement with Nicaragua.
11-03-1901 U.S. Steel was formed when industrialist J.P. Morgan purchased Carnegie Steep Corp. The event made Andrew Carnegie the world's richest man.
11-03-1905 The Parisian subway was officially inaugurated.
11-03-1907 U.S. President Roosevelt induced California to revoke its anti-Japanese legislation.
11-03-1907 In Bulgaria, Premier Nicolas Petkov was killed by an anarchist.
11-03-1909 The first gold medal to a perfect-score bowler was awarded to A.C. Jellison by the American Bowling Congress.
11-03-1927 Samuel Roxy Rothafel opened the famous Roxy Theatre in New York City.
11-03-1927 The Flatheads Gang stole $104,250 in the first armored-car robbery near Pittsburgh, PA.
11-03-1930 Babe Ruth signed a two-year contract with the New York Yankees for the sum of $80,000.
11-03-1930 U.S. President Howard Taft became the first U.S. president to be buried in the National Cemetery in Arlington, VA.
11-03-1935 The German Air Force became an official organ of the Reich.
11-03-1941 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt authorized the Lend-Lease Act, which authorized the act of providing war supplies to the Allies.
11-03-1946 Communists and Nationalists began fighting as the Soviets pulled out of Mukden, Manchuria.
11-03-1946 Pravda denounced Winston Churchill as anti-Soviet and a warmonger.
11-03-1947 The DuMont network aired "Movies For Small Fry." It was network television's first successful children's program.
11-03-1948 Reginald Weir became the first black tennis player to participate in a U.S. Indoor Lawn Tennis Association tournament.
11-03-1959 The Lorraine Hansberry drama A Raisin in the Sun opened at New York's Ethel Barrymore Theater.
11-03-1964 U.S. Senator Carl Hayden broke the record for continuous service in the U.S. Senate. He had worked 37 years and seven days.
11-03-1965 The American navy began inspecting Vietnamese junks in an effort to end arms smuggling to the South.
11-03-1965 The Rev. James J. Reeb, a white minister from Boston, died after being beaten by whites during a civil rights disturbances in Selma, Alabama.
11-03-1966 Three men were convicted of the murder of Malcolm X.
11-03-1969 Levi-Strauss started selling bell-bottomed jeans.
11-03-1977 More than 130 hostages held in Washington, DC, by Hanafi Muslims were freed after ambassadors from three Islamic nations joined the negotiations.
11-03-1978 Bobby Hull (Winnipeg Jets) joined Gordie Howe by getting his 1,000th career goal.
11-03-1978 Palestinian guerrillas on the Tel Aviv Haifa highway killed 34 Israelis.
11-03-1985 Mikhail Gorbachev was named the new chairman of the Soviet Communist Party.
11-03-1986 Popsicle announced its plan to end the traditional twin-stick frozen treat for a one-stick model.
11-03-1988 A cease-fire was declared in the war between Iran and Iraq.
11-03-1990 Lithuania declared its independence from the Soviet Union. It was the first Soviet republic to break away from Communist control.
11-03-1990 In Chile, Patricio Aylwin was sworn in as the first democratically elected president since 1973.
11-03-1991 In South Africa a curfew was imposed on black townships after fighting between political gangs had left 49 dead.
11-03-1992 Former U.S. President Nixon said that the Bush administration was not giving enough economic aid to Russia.
11-03-1993 Janet Reno was unanimously confirmed by the U.S. Senate to become the first female attorney general.
11-03-1993 North Korea withdrew from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty refusing to open sites for inspection.
11-03-1994 In Chile, Eduardo Frei was sworn in as President. It was the first peaceful transfer of power in Chile since 1970.
11-03-1997 An explosion at a nuclear waste reprocessing plant caused 35 workers to be exposed to low levels of radioactivity. The incident was the worst in Japan's history.
11-03-1998 The International Astronomical Union issued an alert that said that a mile-wide asteroid could come very close to, and possibly hit, Earth on Oct. 26, 2028. The next day NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory announced that there was no chance the asteroid would hit Earth.
11-03-2002 Two columns of light were pointed skyward from ground zero in New York as a temporary memorial to the victims of the terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001.
11-03-2003 Fort Drum, NY, 11 troops were killed and two were injured during a training mission when a Black Hawk helicopter crashed.
11-03-2004 In Madrid, Spain, several coordinated bombing attacks on commuter trains killed at least 190 people and injured more than 2,000.
12-03-1496 Jews were expelled from Syria.
12-03-1507 Cesare Borgia died while fighting alongside his brother, the king of Navarre in Spain.
12-03-1609 The Bermuda Islands became an English colony.
12-03-1664 New Jersey became a British colony. King Charles II granted land in the New World to his brother James (The Duke of York).
12-03-1755 In North Arlington, NJ, the steam engine was used for the first time.
12-03-1789 The U.S. Post Office was established.
12-03-1809 Britain signed a treaty with Persia forcing the French to leave the country.
12-03-1857 "Simon Boccanegra" by Verdi debuted in Venice.
12-03-1884 The State of Mississippi authorized the first state-supported college for women. It was called the Mississippi Industrial Institute and College.
12-03-1863 President Jefferson Davis delivered his State of the Confederacy address.
12-03-1879 The British Zulu War began.
12-03-1889 Almon B. Stowger applied for a patent for his automatic telephone system.
12-03-1894 Coca-Cola was sold in bottles for the first time.
12-03-1903 The Czar of Russia issued a decree providing for nominal freedom of religion throughout his territory.
12-03-1904 After 30 years of drilling, the tunnel under the Hudson River was completed. The link was between Jersey City, NJ, and New York, NY.
12-03-1905 In Rome, Premier Giovanni Giolliwas forced out of office by continued civil strife.
12-03-1906 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that corporations must yield incriminating evidence in anti-trust suits.
12-03-1909 The British Parliament increased naval appropriations for Britain.
12-03-1909 Three U.S. warships were ordered to Nicaragua to stem the conflict with El Salvador.
12-03-1911 Dr. Fletcher of Rockefeller Institute discovered the cause of infantile paralysis.
12-03-1912 The Girl Scout organization was founded. The original name was Girl Guides.
12-03-1923 Dr. Lee DeForest demonstrated phonofilm. It was his technique for putting sound on motion picture film.
12-03-1930 Ghandi began his 200-mile march to the sea that symbolized his defiance of British rule over India.
12-03-1933 President Paul von Hindenburg dropped the flag of the German Republic and ordered that the swastika and empire banner be flown side by side.
12-03-1933 U.S. President Roosevelt presented his first presidential address to the nation. It was the first of the "Fireside Chats."
12-03-1935 Parimutuel betting became legal in the State of Nebraska.
12-03-1938 The "Anschluss" took place as German troops entered Austria.
12-03-1940 Finland surrendered to Russia ending the Russo-Finnish War.
12-03-1944 Britain barred all travel to Ireland.
12-03-1947 U.S. President Truman established the "Truman Doctrine" to help Greece and Turkey resist Communism.
12-03-1959 The U.S. House joined the U.S. Senate in approving the statehood of Hawaii.
12-03-1966 Bobby Hull, of the Chicago Blackhawks, became the first National Hockey League (NHL) player to score 51 points in a single season.
12-03-1974 "Wonder Woman" debuted on ABC-TV. The show later went to CBS-TV.
12-03-1980 In Chicago, IL, a jury found John Wayne Gacy Jr. guilty of the murders of 33 men and boys.
12-03-1984 Lebanese President Gemayel opened the second meeting in five years calling for the end to nine-years of war.
12-03-1985 The U.S. and the U.S.S.R. began arms control talks in Geneva.
12-03-1985 Larry Bird, of the NBA’s Boston Celtics, scored a club-record 60 points against the Atlanta Hawks.
12-03-1985 Former U.S. President Richard M. Nixon announced that he planned to drop Secret Service protection and hire his own bodyguards in an effort to lower the deficit by $3 million.
12-03-1987 "Les Miserables" opened on Broadway.
12-03-1989 Prime Minister Sadiq al Mahdi of Sudan formed a new cabinet to end civil war.
12-03-1989 About 2,500 veterans and supporters marched at the Art Institute of Chicago to demand that officials remove an American flag placed on the floor as part of an exhibit.
12-03-1992 Mauritius became a republic but remained a member of the British Commonwealth.
12-03-1993 In the U.S., the Pentagon called for the closure of 31 major military bases.
12-03-1993 Several bombs were set of in Bombay, India. About 300 were killed and hundreds more were injured.
12-03-1993 Janet Reno was sworn in as the first female U.S. attorney general.
12-03-1994 A photo by Marmaduke Wetherell of the Loch Ness monster was confirmed to be a hoax. The photo was taken of a toy submarine with a head and neck attached.
12-03-1994 The Church of England ordained its first women priests.
12-03-1997 Police in Los Angeles arrested Mikail Markhasev for the shooting of Bill Cosby's 27-year-old son, Ennis. Markhasev was later convicted and sentenced to life in prison without parole.
12-03-1998 Astronomers cancelled a warning that a mile-wide asteroid might collide with Earth saying that calculations had been off by 600,000 miles.
12-03-1999 Hungary, Poland and the Czech Republic became members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). All three countries were members of the former Warsaw Pact.
12-03-2002 In Houston, Andrea Yates was convicted of murdering her five children in the family bathtub.
12-03-2002 U.S. homeland security chief Tom Ridge unveiled a color-coded system for terror warnings.
12-03-2002 Conoco and Phillips Petroleum stockholders approved a proposed merger worth $15.6 billion.
12-03-2003 In Utah, Elizabeth Smart was reunited with her family nine months after she was abducted from her home. She had been taken on June 5, 2002, by a drifter that had previously worked at the Smart home.
12-03-2003 In Belgrade, Serbia-Montenegro, Prime Minister Zoran Djindjic was assassinated as he walked into government headquarters. Djindjic had helped to topple Slobodan Milosevic and had declared war on organized crime.
12-03-2003 The U.S. Air Force announced that it would resume reconnaissance flights off the coast of North Korea. The flights had stopped on March 2 after an encounter with four armed North Korean jets.
12-03-2004 In Spain, millions of people marched to protest train bombings in Madrid that killed 191 people the day before.
13-03-0483 St. Felix III began his reign as Pope.
13-03-0607 The 12th recorded passage of Halley's Comet occurred.
13-03-1519 Cortez landed in Mexico.
13-03-1639 Harvard University was named for clergyman John Harvard.
13-03-1660 A statute was passed limiting the sale of slaves in the colony of Virginia.
13-03-1777 The U.S. Congress ordered its European envoys to appeal to high-ranking foreign officers to send troops to reinforce the American army.
13-03-1781 Sir William Herschel discovered the planet Uranus.
13-03-1852 The New York "Lantern" newspaper published the first "Uncle Sam cartoon". It was drawn by Frank Henry Bellew.
13-03-1861 Jefferson Davis signed a bill authorizing slaves to be used as soldiers for the Confederacy.
13-03-1868 The U.S. Senate began the impeachment trial of President Andrew Johnson.
13-03-1877 Chester Greenwood patented the earmuff.
13-03-1878 The first collegiate golf match was played between Oxford and Cambridge.
13-03-1881 Tsar Alexander II was assassinated when a bomb was thrown at him near his palace.
13-03-1884 Standard time was adopted throughout the U.S.
13-03-1900 In South Africa, British Gen. Roberts took Bloemfontein.
13-03-1901 Andrew Carnegie announced that he was retiring from business and that he would spend the rest of his days giving away his fortune. His net worth was estimated at $300 million.
13-03-1902 In Poland, schools were shut down across the country when students refused to sing the Russian hymn "God Protect the Czar."
13-03-1902 Andrew Carnegie approved 40 applications from libraries for donations.
13-03-1908 The people of Jerusalem saw an automobile for the first time. The owner was Charles Glidden of Boston.
13-03-1911 The U.S. Supreme Court approved corporate tax law.
13-03-1915 The Germans repelled a British expeditionary force attack in France.
13-03-1918 Women were scheduled to march in the St. Patrick's Day Parade in New York due to a shortage of men due to wartime.
13-03-1925 A law in Tennessee prohibited the teaching of evolution.
13-03-1928 The St. Francis Dam in California burst and killing 400 people.
13-03-1930 It was announced that the planet Pluto had been discovered by scientist Clyde Tombaugh at the Lowell Observatory.
13-03-1933 U.S. banks began to re-open after a "holiday" that had been declared by President Roosevelt.
13-03-1935 Three-thousand-year-old archives were found in Jerusalem confirming some biblical history.
13-03-1940 The war between Russia and Finland ended with the signing of a treaty in Moscow.
13-03-1941 Adolf Hitler issued an edict calling for an invasion of the U.S.S.R.
13-03-1942 Julia Flikke of the Nurse Corps became the first woman colonel in the U.S. Army.
13-03-1943 Japanese forces ended their attack on the American troops on Hill 700 in Bougainville.
13-03-1946 Reports from Iran indicated that Soviet tanks units were stationed 20 miles from Tehran.
13-03-1946 Premier Tito seized wartime collaborator General Draja Mikhailovich in a cave in Yugoslavia.
13-03-1951 Israel demanded $1.5 billion in German reparations for the cost of caring for war refugees.
13-03-1951 The comic strip "Dennis the Menace" appeared for the first time in newspapers across the country.
13-03-1957 Jimmy Hoffa was arrested by the FBI on bribery charges.
13-03-1963 China invited Soviet President Khrushchev to visit Peking.
13-03-1964 38 residents of a New York City neighborhood failed to respond to the screams of Kitty Genovese, 28 years old, as she was stabbed to death.
13-03-1969 The Apollo 9 astronauts returned to Earth after the conclusion of a mission that included the successful testing of the Lunar Module.
13-03-1970 A group calling itself "Revolutionary Force 9" took credit for 3 bombs that exploded in New York City.
13-03-1970 Cambodia ordered Hanoi and Viet Cong troops to leave.
13-03-1970 Digital Equipment Corp. introduced the PDP-11 minicomputer.
13-03-1972 "The Merv Griffin Show" debuted in syndication for Metromedia Television.
13-03-1974 The U.S. Senate voted 54-33 to restore the death penalty.
13-03-1974 An embargo imposed by Arab oil-producing countries was lifted.
13-03-1980 A jury in Winamac, IN, found Ford Motor Company innocent of reckless homicide in the deaths of three young women that had been riding in a Ford Pinto.
13-03-1988 The board of trustees off Gallaudet University in Washington, DC, chose I. King Jordan to be its first deaf president. The college is a liberal arts college for the hearing-impaired.
13-03-1990 The U.S. lifted economic sanctions against Nicaragua.
13-03-1991 Exxon paid $1 billion in fines and for the clean-up of the Alaskan oil spill.
13-03-1992 An earthquake in eastern Turkey killed more than 1,000.
13-03-1995 The first United Nations World Summit on Social Development concluded in Copenhagen, Denmark.
13-03-1997 Sister Nirmala was chosen by India's Missionaries of Charity to succeed Mother Teresa as leader of the Catholic order.
13-03-1998 Sgt. Maj. Gene McKinney, at one time the U.S. Army's top enlisted man, was acquitted of pressuring military women for sex. He was convicted of trying to persuade the chief accuser to lie. He was reprimanded and had his rank reduced.
13-03-2002 Fox aired "Celebrity Boxing." Tonya Harding beat Paula Jones, Danny Banaduce beat Barry Williams and Todd Bridges defeated Vanilla Ice.
13-03-2003 Japan sent a destroyer to the Sea of Japan amid reports that North Korea was planning to test an intermediate-range ballistic missile.
13-03-2003 A report in the journal "Nature" reported that scientists had found 350,000-year-old human footprints in Italy. The 56 prints were made by three early, upright-walking humans that were descending the side of a volcano.
14-03-1489 Catherine Cornaro, Queen of Cyprus, sold her kingdom to Venice. She was the last of the Lusignan dynasty.
14-03-1629 A Royal charter was granted to the Massachusetts Bay Colony.
14-03-1647 During the Thirty Years War, France, Sweden, Bavaria and Cologne signed a Treaty of Neutrality.
14-03-1743 First American town meeting was held at Boston's Faneuil Hall.
14-03-1757 British Admiral John Byng was executed by a firing squad on board HMS Monarch for neglect of duty.
14-03-1794 Eli Whitney received a patent for his cotton gin.
14-03-1864 Samuel Baker discovered another source of the Nile in East Africa. He named it Lake Albert Nyanza.
14-03-1891 The submarine Monarch laid telephone cable along the bottom of the English Channel to prepare for the first telephone links across the Channel.
14-03-1900 U.S. currency went on the gold standard with the ratification of the Gold Standard Act.
14-03-1900 In Holland, Botanist Hugo de Vries rediscovered Mendel's laws of heredity.
14-03-1901 Utah Governor Heber M. Wells vetoed a bill that would have relaxed restrictions on polygamy.
14-03-1903 The U.S. Senate ratified the Hay-Herran Treaty that guaranteed the U.S. the right to build a canal at Panama. The Columbian Senate rejected the treaty.
14-03-1904 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld the governments claim that the Northern Securities Company was an illegal merger between the Great Northern and Northern Pacific Railway companies.
14-03-1905 French bankers refused to lend money to Russia until after their war.
14-03-1905 The British House of Commons cited a need to compete with Germany in naval strength.
14-03-1906 The island of Ustica was devastated by an earthquake.
14-03-1907 Acapulco, Mexico, was hit by an earthquake.
14-03-1912 An anarchist named Antonio Dalba unsuccessfully attempted to kill Italy's King Victor Emmanuel III in Rome.
14-03-1914 Henry Ford announced the new continuous motion method to assemble cars. The process decreased the time to make a car from 12˝ hours to 93 minutes.
14-03-1915 The British Navy sank the German battleship Dresden off the Chilean coast.
14-03-1918 An all-Russian Congress of Soviets ratified a peace treaty with the Central Powers.
14-03-1923 President Harding became the first U.S. President to file an income tax report.
14-03-1932 George Eastman, the founder of the Kodak company, committed suicide.
14-03-1936 Adolf Hitler told a crowd of 300,000 that Germany's only judge is God and itself.
14-03-1938 Germany invaded Austria. A union of Austria and Germany was proclaimed by Adolf Hitler.
14-03-1939 Hungary occupied the Carpatho-Ukraine. Slovakia declared its independence.
14-03-1943 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt became the first U.S. President to fly in an airplane while in office.
14-03-1945 In Germany, a 22,000 pound "Grand Slam" bomb was dropped by the Royal Air Force Dumbuster Squad on the Beilefeld railway viaduct. It was the heaviest bomb used during World War II.
14-03-1947 The U.S. signed a 99-year lease on naval bases in the Philippines.
14-03-1947 Moscow announced that 890,532 German POWs were held in the U.S.S.R.
14-03-1951 U.N. forces recaptured Seoul for the second time during the Korean War.
14-03-1954 The Viet Minh launched an assault on Dien Bien Phu in Saigon.
14-03-1958 The U.S. government suspended arms shipments to the Batista government of Cuba.
14-03-1964 A Dallas jury found Jack Ruby guilty of the murder of Lee Harvey Oswald.
14-03-1967 John F. Kennedy's body was moved from a temporary grave to a permanent one.
14-03-1976 Egypt formally abrogated the 1971 Treaty Friendship and Cooperation with the Soviet Union.
14-03-1978 An Israeli force of 22,000 invaded south Lebanon. The PLO bases were hit.
14-03-1979 The Census Bureau reported that 95% of all Americans were married or would get married.
14-03-1979 Near Peking, China, at least 200 people died when a Trident aircraft crashed into a factory.
14-03-1980 A Polish airliner crashed while making an emergency landing near Warsaw. 87 people were killed. A 14-man U.S. boxing team was aboard the plane.
14-03-1983 OPEC agreed to cut its oil prices by 15% for the first time in its 23-year history.
14-03-1989 Imported assault guns were banned in the U.S. under President Bush.
14-03-1991 The "Birmingham Six," imprisoned for 16 years for their alleged part in an IRA pub bombing, were set free after a court agreed that the police fabricated evidence.
14-03-1991 Bolivian interior minister Guillermo Capobianco resigned after U.S. officials accused him of receiving money from drug traffickers.
14-03-1995 American astronaut Norman Thagard became the first American to enter space aboard a Russian rocket.
14-03-1996 U.S. President Bill Clinton committed $100 million for an anti-terrorism pact with Israel to track down and root out Islamic militants.
14-03-1998 An earthquake left 10,000 homeless in southeastern Iran.
14-03-2002 A Scottish appeals court upheld the conviction of a Libyan intelligence agent for the 1988 bombing of Pan Am Flight 103. A five-judge court ruled unanimously that Abdel Basset Ali al-Megrahi was guilty of bringing down the plane over Lockerbie, Scotland.
14-03-2003 Robert Blake was released from jail on $1.5 million bail. Blake had been jailed for the murder of his wife Bonny Lee Bakley.
15-03-1341 During the Hundred Years War, an alliance was signed between Roman Emperor Louis IV and France's Philip VI.
15-03-1493 Christopher Columbus returned to Spain after his first New World voyage.
15-03-1778 In command of two frigates, the Frenchman la Perouse sailed east from Botany Bay for the last lap of his voyage around the world.
15-03-1781 During the American Revolution, the Battle of Guilford Courthouse took place in North Carolina. British General Cornwallis' 1,900 soldiers defeated an American force of 4,400.
15-03-1820 Maine was admitted as the 23rd state of the Union.
15-03-1862 General John Hunt Morgan began four days of raids near the city of Gallatin, TN.
15-03-1864 Red River Campaign began as the Union forces reach Alexandria, LA.
15-03-1875 The Roman Catholic Archbishop of New York, John McCloskey, was named the first American cardinal.
15-03-1877 The first cricket test between Australia and England was played in Melbourne. Australia won by 45 runs.
15-03-1892 New York State unveiled the new automatic ballot voting machine.
15-03-1892 Jesse W. Reno patented the Reno Inclined Elevator. It was the first escalator.
15-03-1900 In Paris, Sarah Bernhardt starred in the premiere of Edmond Rostand's "L'Aiglon."
15-03-1901 German Chancellor von Bulow declared that an agreement between Russia and China over Manchuria would violate the Anglo-German accord of October 1900.
15-03-1902 In Boston, MA, 10,000 freight handlers went back to work after a weeklong strike.
15-03-1903 The British conquest of Nigeria was completed. 500,000 square miles were now controlled by the U.K.
15-03-1904 Three hundred Russians were killed as the Japanese shelled Port Arthur in Korea.
15-03-1907 In Finland, woman won their first seats in the Finnish Parliament. They took their seats on May 23.
15-03-1909 Italy proposed a European conference on the Balkans.
15-03-1910 Otto Kahn offered $500,000 for a family portrait by Dutch artist Frans Hals. Kahn had outbid J.P. Morgan for the work.
15-03-1913 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson held the first open presidential news conference.
15-03-1916 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson sent 12,000 troops, under General Pershing, over the border of Mexico to pursue bandit Pancho Villa. The mission failed.
15-03-1917 Russian Czar Nicholas II abdicated himself and his son. His brother Grand Duke succeeded as czar.
15-03-1919 The American Legion was founded in Paris.
15-03-1922 Fuad I assumed the title of king of Egypt after the country gained nominal independence from Britain.
15-03-1934 Henry Ford restored the $5 a day wage.
15-03-1935 Joseph Goebbels, German Minister of Propaganda banned four Berlin newspapers.
15-03-1937 In Chicago, IL, the first blood bank to preserve blood for transfusion by refrigeration was established at the Cook County Hospital.
15-03-1938 Oil was discovered in Saudi Arabia.
15-03-1939 German forces occupied Bohemia and Moravia, and part of Czechoslovakia.
15-03-1944 Cassino, Italy, was destroyed by Allied bombing.
15-03-1946 British Premier Attlee offered India full independence after agreement on a constitution.
15-03-1948 Sir Laurence Olivier was on the cover of "LIFE" magazine for his starring role in Shakespeare’s "Hamlet."
15-03-1949 Clothes rationing in Great Britain ended nearly four years after the end of World War II.
15-03-1951 General de Lattre demanded that Paris send him more troops for the fight in Vietnam.
15-03-1951 The Persian parliament voted to nationalize the oil industry.
15-03-1954 CBS television debuted its "Morning Show."
15-03-1955 The U.S. Air Force unveiled a self-guided missile.
15-03-1956 The musical "My Fair Lady" opened on Broadway.
15-03-1960 Ten nations met in Geneva to discuss disarmament.
15-03-1960 The first underwater park was established as Key Largo Coral Reef Preserve.
15-03-1964 In Montreal, Richard Burton and Elizabeth Taylor were married.
15-03-1968 The U.S. mint halted the practice of buying and selling gold.
15-03-1970 The musical "Purlie" opened on Broadway in New York City.
15-03-1971 CBS television announced it was going to drop "The Ed Sullivan Show."
15-03-1977 The first episode of "Eight is Enough" was aired on ABC-TV.
15-03-1977 The U.S. House of Representatives began a 90-day test to determine the feasibility of showing its sessions on television.
15-03-1979 Pope John Paul II published his first encyclical "Redemptor Hominis." In the work he warned of the growing gap between the rich and poor.
15-03-1989 The U.S. Food and Drug administration decided to impound all fruit imported from Chili after two cyanide-tainted grapes were found in Philadelphia, PA.
15-03-1989 The U.S. Department of Veteran's Affairs became the 14th Department in the President's Cabinet.
15-03-1990 In Iraq, British journalist Farzad Bazoft was hanged for spying.
15-03-1990 Mikhail Gorbachev was elected the first executive president of the Soviet Union.
15-03-1990 The Ford Explorer was introduced to the public.
15-03-1990 The Soviet parliament ruled that Lithuania's declaration of independence was invalid and that Soviet law was still in force in the Baltic republic.
15-03-1991 Four Los Angeles police officers were indicted in the beating of Rodney King on March 3, 1991.
15-03-1991 Yugoslav President Borisav Jovic resigned after about a week of anit-communist protests.
15-03-1994 U.S. President Clinton extended the moratorium on nuclear testing until September of 1995.
15-03-1996 The aviation firm Fokker NV collapsed.
15-03-1998 More than 15,000 ethnic Albanians marched in Yugoslavia to demand independence for Kosovo.
15-03-1998 CBS' "60 Minutes" aired an interview with former White House employee Kathleen Willey. Wiley said U.S. President Clinton made unwelcome sexual advances toward her in the Oval Office in 1993.
15-03-2002 Libyan Abdel Baset Ali Mohmed Al-Megrahi began his life sentence in a Scottish jail for his role in the bombing of Pan Am Flight 103 on December 21, 1988.
15-03-2002 In the U.S., Burger King began selling a veggie burger. The event was billed as the first veggie burger to be sold nationally by a fast food chain.
15-03-2002 In Texas, Andrea Yates received a life sentence for drowning her five children on June 20, 2001.
15-03-2002 U.S. Secretary of State Colin Powell told the Associated Press that the U.S. would stand by a 24-year pledge not to use nuclear arms against states that don't have them.
15-03-2004 Clive Woodall's novel "One for Sorrow: Two for Joy" was published. Two days later Woodall sold the film rights to Walt Disney Co. for $1 million.
16-03-1190 The Crusaders began the massacre of Jews in York, England.
16-03-1521 Portuguese navigator Ferdinand Magellan reached the Philippines. He was killed the next month by natives.
16-03-1527 The Emperor Babur defeated the Rajputs at the Battle of Kanvaha in India.
16-03-1621 Samoset walked into the settlement of Plymouth Colony, later Plymouth, MA. Samoset was a native from the Monhegan tribe in Maine who spoke English. He greeted the Pilgrims by saying, "Welcome, Englishmen! My name is Samoset."
16-03-1802 The U.S. Congress established the West Point Military Academy in New York.
16-03-1836 The Republic of Texas approved a constitution.
16-03-1850 The novel "The Scarlet Letter," by Nathaniel Hawthorne, was published for the first time.
16-03-1871 The State of Delaware enacted the first fertilizer law.
16-03-1882 The U.S. Senate approved a treaty allowing the United States to join the Red Cross.
16-03-1883 Susan Hayhurst graduated from the Philadelphia College of Pharmacy. She was the first woman pharmacy graduate.
16-03-1907 The world's largest cruiser, the British Invincible was completed at Glasgow.
16-03-1908 China released the Japanese steamship Tatsu Maru.
16-03-1909 Cuba suffered its first revolt only six weeks after the inauguration of Gomez.
16-03-1913 The 15,000-ton battleship Pennsylvania was launched at Newport News, VA.
16-03-1915 The Federal Trade Commission began operation.
16-03-1917 Russian Czar Nicholas II abdicated his throne.
16-03-1918 Tallulah Bankhead made her New York acting debut with a role in "The Squab Farm."
16-03-1926 Physicist Robert H. Goddard launched the first liquid-fuel rocket.
16-03-1928 The U.S. planned to send 1,000 more Marines to Nicaragua.
16-03-1935 Adolf Hitler ordered a German rearmament and violated the Versailles Treaty.
16-03-1939 Germany occupied the rest of Czechoslovakia.
16-03-1945 Iwo Jima was declared secure by the Allies. However, small pockets of Japanese resistance still existed.
16-03-1946 Algerian nationalist leader Ferhat Abbas was freed after spending a year in jail.
16-03-1946 India called British Premier Attlee's independence off contradictory and a propaganda move.
16-03-1947 Martial law was withdrawn in Tel Aviv.
16-03-1949 The first museum to be devoted solely to atomic energy opened in Oak Ridge, TN.
16-03-1950 Congress voted to remove federal taxes on oleomargarine.
16-03-1964 Paul Hornung and Alex Karras were reinstated to the NFL after an 11-month suspension for betting on football games.
16-03-1964 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson submitted a $1 billion war on poverty program to Congress.
16-03-1968 U.S. troops in Vietnam destroyed a village consisting mostly of women and children. The event is known as the My-Lai massacre.
16-03-1978 Italian politician Aldo Moro was kidnapped by left-wing urban guerrillas. Moro was later murdered by the group.
16-03-1984 Mozambique and South Africa signed a pact banning the support for one another's internal enemies.
16-03-1984 William Buckley, the CIA station chief in Beirut, was kidnapped by gunmen. He died while in captivity.
16-03-1985 "A Chorus Line" played its 4,000 performance.
16-03-1985 Terry Anderson, an Associated Press newsman, was taken hostage in Beirut. He was released in December 4, 1991.
16-03-1987 "Bostonia" magazine printed an English translation of Albert Einstein’s last high school report card.
16-03-1988 Indictments were issued for Lt. Colonel Oliver North and Vice Admiral John Poindexter of the National Security Council for their involvement in the Iran-Contra affair.
16-03-1988 Mickey Thompson and his wife Trudy were shot to death in their driveway. Thompson, known as the "Speed King," set nearly 500 auto speed endurance records including being the first person to travel more than 400 mph on land.
16-03-1989 In the U.S.S.R., the Central Committee approved Gorbachev's agrarian reform plan.
16-03-1989 The Soviet Communist Party's Central Committee approved large-scale agricultural reforms and elected the party's 100 members to the Congress of People's Deputies.
16-03-1993 In France, ostrich meat was officially declared fit for human consumption.
16-03-1994 Tonya Harding plead guilty in Portland, OR, to conspiracy to hinder prosecution for covering up the attack on her skating rival Nancy Kerrigan. She was fined $100,000. She was also banned from amateur figure skating.
16-03-1994 Russia agreed to phase out production of weapons-grade plutonium.
16-03-1995 NASA astronaut Norman Thagard became the first American to visit the Russian space station Mir.
16-03-1998 Rwanda began mass trials for 1994 genocide with 125,000 suspects for 500,000 murders.
16-03-1999 The 20 members of the European Union's European Commission announced their resignations amid allegations of corruption and financial mismanagement.
17-03-0461 Bishop Patrick, St. Patrick, died in Saul. Ireland celebrates this day in his honor.
17-03-1756 St. Patrick's Day was celebrated in New York City for the first time. The event took place at the Crown and Thistle Tavern.
17-03-1766 Britain repealed the Stamp Act that had caused resentment in the North American colonies.
17-03-1776 British forces evacuated Boston to Nova Scotia during the Revolutionary War.
17-03-1868 Postage stamp canceling machine patent was issued.
17-03-1870 Wellesley College was incorporated by the Massachusetts legislature under its first name, Wellesley Female Seminary.
17-03-1884 John Joseph Montgomery made the first glider flight in Otay, California.
17-03-1886 20 Blacks were killed in the Carrollton Massacre in Mississippi.
17-03-1891 The British steamer Utopia sank off the coast of Gibraltar.
17-03-1901 In Paris, Vincent Van Gogh's paintings were shown at the Bernheim Gallery.
17-03-1909 In France, the communications industry was paralyzed by strikes.
17-03-1910 The Camp Fire Girls organization was founded by Luther and Charlotte Gulick. It was formally presented to the public exactly 2 years later.
17-03-1914 Russia increased the number of active duty military from 460,000 to 1,700,000.
17-03-1917 America’s first bowling tournament for ladies began in St. Louis, MO. Almost 100 women participated in the event.
17-03-1930 Al Capone was released from jail.
17-03-1941 The National Gallery of Art was officially opened by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt in Washington, DC.
17-03-1942 Douglas MacArthur became the Supreme Commander of the United Nations forces in the Southwestern Pacific.
17-03-1944 During World War II, the U.S. bombed Vienna.
17-03-1950 Scientists at the University of California at Berkeley announced that they had created a new radioactive element. They named it "californium". It is also known as element 98.
17-03-1958 The Vanguard 1 satellite was launched by the U.S.
17-03-1959 The Dalai Lama (Lhama Dhondrub, Tenzin Gyatso) fled Tibet and went to India.
17-03-1961 The U.S. increased military aid and technicians to Laos.
17-03-1962 Moscow asked the U.S. to pull out of South Vietnam.
17-03-1966 A U.S. submarine found a missing H-bomb in the Mediterranean off of Spain.
17-03-1967 Snoopy and Charlie Brown of "Peanuts" were on the cover of "LIFE" magazine.
17-03-1969 Golda Meir was sworn in as the fourth premier of Israel.
17-03-1970 The U.S. Army charged 14 officers with suppression of facts in the My Lai massacre case.
17-03-1972 U.S. President Nixon asked Congress to halt busing in order to achieve desegregation.
17-03-1973 Twenty were killed in Cambodia when a bomb went off that was meant for the Cambodian President Lon Nol.
17-03-1973 The first American prisoners of war (POWs) were released from the "Hanoi Hilton" in Hanoi, North Vietnam.
17-03-1982 Four Dutch television crewmembers were killed in El Salvador.
17-03-1985 U.S. President Reagan agreed to a joint study with Canada on acid rain.
17-03-1989 A series of solar flares caused a violent magnetic storm that brought power outages over large regions of Canada.
17-03-1992 In Buenos Aires, 10 people were killed in a suicide car-bomb attack against the Israeli embassy.
17-03-1992 White South Africans approved constitutional reforms to give legal equality to blacks.
17-03-1995 Gerry Adams became the first leader of Sinn Fein to be received at the White House.
17-03-1998 Washington Mutual announced it had agreed to buy H.F. Ahmanson and Co. for $9.9 billion dollars. The deal created the nation's seventh-largest banking company.
17-03-1999 A panel of medical experts concluded that marijuana had medical benefits for people suffering from cancer and AIDS.
17-03-1999 The International Olympic Committee expelled six of its members in the wake of a bribery scandal.
17-03-2000 In Norway, Jens Stotenberg and the Labour Party took office as Prime Minister. The coalition government of Kjell Magne Bondevik resigned on March 9 as a result of an environmental dispute.
17-03-2000 In Kanungu, Uganda, a fire at a church linked to the cult known as the Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments killed more than 530. On March 31, officials set the number of deaths linked to the cult at more than 900 after authorities subsequently found mass graves at various sites linked to the cult.
17-03-2007 Mike Modano (Dallas Stars) scored his 502nd and 503rd career goals making him the all-time U.S. leader in goal-scoring.

18-03-1903 France dissolved the Catholic religious orders.

18-03-1910 The first opera by a U.S. composer performed at the Met in New York City.
18-03-1911 Theodore Roosevelt opened the Roosevelt Dam in Phoenix, AZ. It was the largest dam in the U.S. at the time.
18-03-1911 North Dakota enacted a hail insurance law.
18-03-1913 Greek King George I was killed by an assassin. Constantine I succeeded him.
18-03-1916 Russia countered the Verdun assault with an attack at Lake Naroch. The Russians lost 100,000 men and the Germans lost 20,000.
18-03-1917 The Germans sank the U.S. ships, City of Memphis, Vigilante and the Illinois, without any warning.
18-03-1919 The Order of DeMolay was established in Kansas City.
18-03-1920 Greece adopted the Gregorian calendar.
18-03-1921 Poland was enlarged with the second Peace of Riga.
18-03-1921 The steamer "Hong Koh" ran aground off of Swatow China. Over 1,000 people were killed.
18-03-1922 Mohandas K. Gandhi was sentenced to six years in prison for civil disobedience in India. He served only 2 years of the sentence.
18-03-1922 Princeton and Yale played the first intercollegiate indoor polo championship.
18-03-1931 Schick Inc. displayed the first electric shaver.
18-03-1937 More than 400 people, mostly children, were killed in a gas explosion at a school in New London, TX.
18-03-1938 Mexico took control of all foreign-owned oil properties on its soil.
18-03-1938 New York first required serological blood tests of pregnant women.

18-03-1940 The soap opera "Light of the World" was first heard on NBC radio.
18-03-1940 Adolf Hitler and Benito Mussolini held a meeting at the Brenner Pass. The Italian dictator agreed to join in Germany's war against France and Britain during the meeting.
18-03-1942 The third military draft began in the U.S. because of World War II.
18-03-1943 The Reich called off its offensive in Caucasus.
18-03-1943 American forces took Gafsa in Tunisia.
18-03-1944 The Russians reached the Rumanian border in the Balkans during World War II.

18-03-1952 In Philadelphia, PA, the first plastic lenses were fitted for a cataract patient.
18-03-1953 An earthquake hit West Turkey killing 250 people.
18-03-1954 RKO Pictures was sold for $23,489,478. It became the first motion picture studio to be owned by an individual. The person was Howard Hughes.

18-03-1965 Cosmonaut Alexei Leonov became the first man to spacewalk when he left the Voskhod II space capsule while in orbit around the Earth. He was outside the spacecraft for about 20 minutes.

18-03-1966 Scott Paper began selling paper dresses for $1.
18-03-1968 The U.S. Congress repealed the requirement for a gold reserve.

18-03-1977 Vietnam turned over an MIA to a U.S. delegation.

19-03-2002 Operation Anaconda, the largest U.S.-led ground offensive since the Gulf War, ended in eastern Afghanistan. During the operation, which began on March 2, it was reported that at least 500 Taliban and al Qaeda fighters were killed. Eleven allied troops were killed during the same operation.

20-03-1943 The Allies attacked Field Marshall Erwin Rommel's forces on the Mareth Line in North Africa.
20-03-1947 A blue whale weighing 180-metric tons was caught in the South Atlantic.
20-03-1952 The U.S. Senate ratified a peace treaty with Japan.
20-03-1956 Mount Bezymianny on Kamchatka Peninsula (USSR) exploded.
20-03-1956 Tunisia gained independence from France.

20-03-1967 Twiggy arrived in the U.S. for a one-week stay.
20-03-1969 U.S. Senator Edward Kennedy called on the U.S. to close all bases in Taiwan.
20-03-1972 19 mountain climbers were killed on Japan's Mount Fuji during an avalanche.
20-03-1976 Patricia Hearst was convicted of armed robbery for her role in the hold up of a San Francisco Bank.
20-03-1980 The U.S. made an appeal to the International Court concerning the American Hostages in Iran.
20-03-1982 U.S. scientists' return from Antarctica with the first land mammal fossils found there.
20-03-1984 The U.S. Senate rejected an amendment to permit spoken prayer in public schools.
20-03-1985 For the first time in its 99-year history, Avon representatives received a salary. Up to that time they had been paid solely on commissions.
20-03-1985 CBS-TV presented "The Romance of Betty Boop."
20-03-1985 Libby Riddles won the 1,135-mile Anchorage-to-Nome dog race becoming the first woman to win the Iditarod.
20-03-1986 Fallon Carrington and Jeff Colby were wed on the TV drama "The Colby’s". "The Colby’s" was an offshoot of "Dynasty".
20-03-1987 The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved AZT. The drug was proven to slow the progress of AIDS (Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome).
20-03-1989 A Washington, DC, district court judge blocked a curfew imposed by Mayor Barry and the City Council.
20-03-1989 In Belfast, two policemen were killed. The IRA claimed responsibility.
20-03-1989 It was announced that Cincinnati Reds manager Pete Rose was under investigation.
20-03-1990 The Los Angeles Lakers retired Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's #33.
20-03-1990 Namibia became an independent nation ending 75 years of South African rule.
20-03-1990 Imelda Marcos, widow of ex-Philippines dictator Ferdinand Marcos, went on trial for racketeering, embezzlement and bribery.
20-03-1990 In Rumania, tanks were sent to the town of Tirgu Mures to quell ethnic riots.
20-03-1991 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled unanimously that employers could not exclude women from jobs where exposure to toxic chemicals could potentially damage a fetus.
20-03-1991 The U.S. forgave $2 billion in loans to Poland.
20-03-1992 Janice Pennington was awarded $1.3 million for accident on the set of the "Price is Right" TV show.
20-03-1993 Russian President Boris Yeltsin declared emergency rule. He set a referendum on whether the people trusted him or the hard-line Congress to govern.
20-03-1993 An Irish Republican Army bomb was detonated in Warrington, England. A 3-year-old boy and a 12-year-old boy were killed.
20-03-1995 About 35,000 Turkish troops crossed the northern border of Iraq in pursuit of the separatist rebels of the Kurdistan Workers Party (PKK).
20-03-1995 In Tokyo, 12 people were killed and more than 5,500 others were sickened when packages containing the nerve gas Sarin was released on five separate subway trains. The terrorists belonged to a doomsday cult in Japan.
20-03-1996 In Los Angeles, Erik and Lyle Menendez were found guilty of first-degree murder in the killing of their parents.
20-03-1996 The U.K. announced that humans could catch CJD (Mad Cow Disease).
20-03-1997 Brian Grazer received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
20-03-1997 Liggett Group, the maker of Chesterfield cigarettes, settled 22 state lawsuits by admitting the industry marketed cigarettes to teenagers and agreed to warn on every pack that smoking is addictive.
20-03-1998 India's new Hindu nationalist-led government pledges to "exercise the option to induct nuclear weapons."
20-03-1999 Bertrand Piccard and Brian Jones became the first men to circumnavigate the Earth in a hot air balloon. The non-stop trip began on March 3 and covered 26,500 miles.
20-03-2000 Former Black Panther Jamil Abdullah Al-Amin, once known as H. Rap Brown, was captured following a shootout that left a sherriff's deputy dead.
20-03-2002 Actress Pamela Anderson disclosed that she had hepatitis C.
20-03-2002 Arthur Andersen plead innocent to charges that it had shredded documents and deleted computer files related to the energy company Enron.
20-03-2003 Cisco Systems Inc. announced it was buying The Linksys Group INc. for $500 million in stock.
20-03-2003 U.S. and British forces invaded Iraq from Kuwait.
21-03-1349 3,000 Jews were killed in Black Death riots in Efurt Germany.
21-03-1556 Thomas Cranmer, the Archbishop of Canterbury, was burned at the stake at Oxford after retracting the last of seven recantations that same day.
21-03-1788 Almost the entire city of New Orleans, LA, was destroyed by fire. 856 buildings were destroyed.
21-03-1790 Thomas Jefferson reported to U.S. President George Washington as the new secretary of state.

21-03-1824 A fire at a Cairo ammunitions dump killed 4,000 horses.

21-03-1851 Emperor Tu Duc ordered that Christian priests be put to death.
