21-03-1868 The Sorosos club for professional women was formed in New York City by Jennie June. It was the first of its kind.
21-03-1871 Journalist Henry M Stanley began his famous expedition to Africa.
21-03-1902 Romain Roland's play "The 4th of July" premiered in Paris.
21-03-1902 In New York, three Park Avenue mansions were destroyed when a subway tunnel roof caved in.
21-03-1904 The British Parliament vetoed a proposal to send Chinese workers to Transvaal.
21-03-1905 Sterilization legislation was passed in the State of Pennsylvania. The governor vetoed the measure.
21-03-1906 Ohio passed a law that prohibited hazing by fraternities after two fatalities.
21-03-1907 The U.S. Marines landed in Honduras to protect American interests in the war with Nicaragua.
21-03-1907 The first Parliament of Transvaal met in Pretoria.
21-03-1908 A passenger was carried in a bi-plane for the first time by Henri Farman of France.
21-03-1909 Russia withdrew its support for Serbia and recognized the Austrian annexation of Bosnia-Herzegovina. Serbia accepted Austrian control over Bosnia-Herzegovina on March 31, 1909.
21-03-1910 The U.S. Senate granted ex-President Teddy Roosevelt a yearly pension of $10,000.
21-03-1918 During World War I, the Germans launched the Somme Offensive.
21-03-1928 U.S. President Calvin Coolidge gave the Congressional Medal of Honor to Charles Lindbergh for his first trans-Atlantic flight.
21-03-1934 A fire destroyed Hakodate, Japan, killing about 1,500.
21-03-1935 Incubator ambulance service began in Chicago, IL.
21-03-1941 The last Italian post in East Libya, North Africa, fell to the British.
21-03-1945 During World War II, Allied bombers began four days of raids over Germany.
21-03-1946 The Los Angeles Rams signed Kenny Washington. Washington was the first black player to join a National Football League team since 1933.
21-03-1946 The United Nations set up a temporary headquarters at Hunter College in New York City.
21-03-1953 The Boston Celtics beat Syracuse Nationals (111-105) in four overtimes to eliminate them from the Eastern Division Semifinals. A total of seven players (both teams combined) fouled out of the game.
21-03-1955 NBC-TV presented the first "Colgate Comedy Hour".
21-03-1957 Shirley Booth made her TV acting debut in "The Hostess with the Mostest" on CBS.
21-03-1960 About 70 people were killed in Sharpeville, South Africa, when police fired upon demonstrators.
21-03-1963 Alcatraz Island, the federal penitentiary in San Francisco Bay, CA, closed.
21-03-1965 The U.S. launched Ranger 9. It was the last in a series of unmanned lunar explorations.
21-03-1965 More than 3,000 civil rights demonstrators led by the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. began a march from Selma to Montgomery, AL.
21-03-1971 Two U.S. platoons in Vietnam refused their orders to advance.
21-03-1972 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that states could not require one year of residency for voting eligibility.
21-03-1974 An attempt was made to kidnap Princess Anne in London's Pall Mall.
21-03-1980 U.S. President Jimmy Carter announced to the U.S. Olympic Team that they would not participate in the 1980 Summer Games in Moscow as a boycott against Soviet intervention in Afghanistan.
21-03-1980 On the TV show Dallas, J.R. Ewing was shot.
21-03-1982 The movie "Annie" premiered.
21-03-1984 A Soviet submarine crashed into the USS Kitty Hawk off the coast of Japan.
21-03-1985 In Langa, South Africa, at least 21 demonstrators were killed at a march to mark the 25th anniversary of the Sharpeville shootings.
21-03-1985 Larry Flynt offered to sell his pornography empire for $26 million or "Hustler" magazine alone for $18 million.
21-03-1985 Police in Langa, South Africa, opened fire on blacks marching to mark the 25th anniversary of the Sharpeville shootings. At least 21 demonstrators were killed.
21-03-1989 Randall Dale Adams was released from a Texas prison after his conviction was overturned. The documentary "The Thin Blue Line" had challenged evidence of Adams' conviction for killing a police officer.
21-03-1990 "Normal Life" with Moon Unit & Dweezil Zappa premiered on CBS-TV.
21-03-1990 Australian businessman Alan Bond sold Van Gogh's "Irises" to the Gerry Museum. Bond had purchased the painting for $53.9 million in 1987.
21-03-1990 "Sydney" starring Valerie Bertinelli premiered on CBS-TV.
21-03-1990 Namibia became independent of South Africa.
21-03-1991 27 people were lost at sea when two U.S. Navy anti-submarine planes collided.
21-03-1991 The U.N. Security Council lifted the food embargo against Iraq.
21-03-1994 Dudley Moore was arrested for hitting his girlfriend.
21-03-1994 Steven Spielberg won his first Oscars. They were for best picture and best director for "Schindler's List."
21-03-1994 Wayne Gretzky tied Gordie Howe's NHL record of 801 goals.
21-03-1994 Bill Gates of Microsoft and Craig McCaw of McCaw Cellular Communications announced a $9 billion plan that would send 840 satellites into orbit to relay information around the globe.
21-03-1995 New Jersey officially dedicated the Howard Stern Rest Area along Route 295.
21-03-1995 Tokyo police raided the headquarters of Aum Shinrikyo in search of evidence to link the cult to the Sarin gas released on five Tokyo subway trains.
21-03-1999 Israel's Supreme Court rejected the final effort to have American Samuel Sheinbein returned to the U.S. to face murder charges for killing Alfred Tello, Jr. Under a plea bargain Sheinbein was sentenced to 24 years in prison.
21-03-2000 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 5-4 that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration had overstepped its regulatory authority when it attempted to restrict the marketing of cigarettes to youngsters.
21-03-2001 Nintendo released Game Boy Advance.
21-03-2002 In Pakistan, Ahmed Omar Saeed Sheikh was charged with murder for his role in the kidnapping of Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pear. Three other Islamic militants that were in custody were also charged along with seven more accomplices that were still at large.
21-03-2002 In Paris, an 1825 print by French inventor Joseph Nicephore Niepce was sold for $443,220. The print, of a man leading a horse, was the earliest recorded image taken by photographic means.
21-03-2003 It was reported that the Dow Jones Industrial Average closed up 235.27 (2.8%) at 8,521.97. It was the strongest weekly gain in more than 20 years.

22-03-1954 The London gold market reopened for the first time since 1939.
22-03-1956 Perry Como became the first major TV variety-show host to book a rock and roll act on his program. The act was Carl Perkins.

22-03-1965 U.S. confirmed that its troops used chemical warfare against the Vietcong.
22-03-1972 The U.S. Senate passed the Equal Rights Amendment. It was not ratified by the states.

22-03-1977 The Dutch Den Uyl government fell.

22-03-1980 People for Ethical Treatment of Animals (PETA) was founded by Ingrid Newkirk and Alex Pacheco.

22-03-1981 RCA put its Selectra Vision laser disc players on the market.
22-03-1981 The first Mongolian entered space aboard the Russian Soyuz 39.
22-03-1987 A barge loaded with 32,000 tons of refuse left Islip, NY, to find a place to unload. After being refused by several states and three countries space was found back in Islip.
22-03-1988 The Congress overrode U.S. President Reagan's veto of a sweeping civil rights bill.
22-03-1989 Oliver North began two days of testimony at his Iran-Contra trial in Washington, DC.
22-03-1989 The U.S. House Ways and Means Committee reported the class gap was widening.
22-03-1990 A jury in Anchorage, Alaska, found Captain Hazelwood not guilty in the Valdez oil spill.
22-03-1991 Pamela Smart, a high school teacher, was found guilty in New Hampshire of manipulating her student-lover to kill her husband.
22-03-1992 A Fokker F-28 veered off a runway at New York's LaGuardia airport and into Flushing Bay, killing 27 people.
22-03-1993 Cleveland Indians pitchers Steve Olin and Tim Crews were killed in a boating accident in Florida. Bob Ojeda was seriously injured in the accident.
22-03-1993 Intel introduced the Pentium-processor (80586) 64 bits-60 MHz-100+ MIPS.
22-03-1995 Russian cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov returned to Earth after setting a record for 438 days in space.
22-03-1997 Tara Lipinski, at 14 years and 10 months, became the youngest women's world figure skating champion.
22-03-2002 The U.S. Postal Rate Commission approved a request for a postal rate increase of first-class stamps from 34 cents to 37 cents by June 30. It was the first time a postal rate case was resolved through a settlement between various groups. The groups included the U.S. Postal Service, postal employees, mailer groups and competitors.
22-03-2002 A collection of letters and cards sent by Princess Diana of Wales sold for $33,000. The letters and cards were written to a former housekeeper at Diana's teenage home.

23-03-1983 Dr. Barney Clark died after 112 days with a permanent artificial heart.
23-03-1989 A 1,000-foot diameter asteroid missed Earth by 500,000 miles.
23-03-1989 Joel Steinberg was sentenced to 25 years for killing his adopted daughter.

23-03-1993 U.N. experts announced that record ozone lows had been registered over a large area of the Western Hemisphere.
23-03-1994 Luis Donaldo Colosio, Mexico's leading presidential candidate, was assassinated in Tijuana. Mario Aburto Martinez was arrested at the scene and confessed to the killing.
23-03-1994 Wayne Gretzky broke Gordie Howe's National Hockey League (NHL) career record with his 802nd goal.

23-03-1996 Taiwan held its first democratic presidential elections.
23-03-1998 Germany's largest bank pledged $3.1 million to Jewish foundations as restitution for Nazi looting.
23-03-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that term limits for state lawmakers were constitutional.
23-03-1998 Russian President Boris Yeltsin fired his Cabinet.
23-03-1998 The movie "Titanic" won 11 Oscars at the Academy Awards.
23-03-1998 The German company Bertelsmann AG agreed to purchase the American publisher Random House for $1.4 billion. The merger created the largest English-language book-publishing company in the world.

23-03-1999 NATO Secretary-General Javier Solana gave formal approval for air strikes against Serbian targets.

24-03-1934 U.S. President Roosevelt signed a bill granting future independence to the Philippines.
24-03-1938 The U.S. asked that all powers help refugees fleeing from the Nazis.
24-03-1944 In Rome, The Gestapo rounded up innocent Italians and shot them to death in response to a bomb attack that killed 32 German policemen. Over 300 civilians were executed.
24-03-1946 The Soviet Union announced that it was withdrawing its troops from Iran.
24-03-1947 The U.S. Congress proposed the limitation of the presidency to two terms.
24-03-1954 Britain opened trade talks with Hungary.
24-03-1955 Tennessee Williams' play "Cat on a Hot Tin Roof" debuted on Broadway.
24-03-1955 The first oil drill seagoing rig was put into service.
24-03-1960 A U.S. appeals court ruled that the novel, "Lady Chatterly’s Lover", was not obscene and could be sent through the mail.
24-03-1972 Great Britain imposed direct rule over Northern Ireland.
24-03-1976 The president of Argentina, Isabel Peron, was deposed by her country's military.
24-03-1980 In San Salvador, Archbishop Oscar Arnulfo Romero was shot to death by gunmen as he celebrated Mass.
24-03-1981 "Nightline" with Ted Koppel premiered.
24-03-1985 Thousands demonstrated in Madrid against the NATO presence in Spain.
24-03-1988 Former national security aides Oliver L. North and John M. Poindexter and businessmen Richard V. Secord and Albert Hakim pleaded innocent to Iran-Contra charges.
24-03-1989 The Exxon Valdez spilled 240,000 barrels (11 million gallons) of oil in Alaska's Prince William Sound after it ran aground.
24-03-1989 The U.S. decided to send humanitarian aid to the Contras.
24-03-1990 Indian troops left Sri Lanka.
24-03-1991 The African nation of Benin held its first presidential elections in about 30 years.
24-03-1993 In Israel, Ezer Weizman, an advocate of peace with neighboring Arab nations, was elected President.
24-03-1995 Russian forces surrounded Achkoi-Martan. It was one of the few remaining strongholds of rebels in Chechenia.
24-03-1995 The U.S. House of Representatives passed a welfare reform package that made the most changes in social programs since the New Deal.
24-03-1997 The Australian parliament overturned the world's first and only euthanasia law.
24-03-1998 In Jonesboro, AR, two young boys open fire at students from woods near a school. Four students and a teacher were killed and 10 others were injured. The two boys were 11 and 13 years old cousins.
24-03-1998 A former FBI agent said papers found in James Earl Ray's car supports a conspiracy theory in the assassination of Rev. Martin Luther King Jr.
24-03-1999 In Kenya, at least 31 people were killed when a passenger train derailed. Hundreds were injured.
24-03-1999 NATO launched air strikes against Yugoslavia (Serbia, Montenegro, Kosovo and Vojvodina). The attacks marked the first time in its 50-year history that NATO attacked a sovereign country. The bombings were in response to Serbia's refusal to sign a peace treaty with ethnic Albanians who were seeking independence for the province of Kosovo.
24-03-1999 The 7-mile tunnel under Mont Blanc in France was an inferno after a truck carrying flour and margarine caught on fire. At least 30 people were killed.
24-03-2002 Thieves stole five 17th century paintings from the Frans Hals Museum in the Dutch city of Haarlem. The paintings were worth about $2.6 million. The paintings were works by Jan Steen, Cornelis Bega, Adriaan van Ostade and Cornelis Dusart.
24-03-2005 The government of Kyrgyzstan collapsed after opposition protesters took over President Askar Akayev's presidential compound and government offices.
24-03-2005 Sandra Bullock received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
24-03-2006 In Spain, the Basque separatist group ETA announced a permanent cease-fire.
25-03-0421 The city of Venice was founded.
25-03-0708 Constantine began his reign as Catholic Pope.
25-03-1133 William the Conqueror ordered the first Domesday Survey of England.
25-03-1306 Robert the Bruce was crowned king of Scotland.
25-03-1409 The Council of Pisa opened.
25-03-1609 Henry Hudson left on an exploration for Dutch East India Co.
25-03-1634 Lord Baltimore founded the Catholic colony of Maryland.
25-03-1655 Puritans jailed Governor Stone after a military victory over Catholic forces in the colony of Maryland.
25-03-1655 Christian Huygens discovered Titan. Titan is Saturn's largest satellite.
25-03-1668 The first horse race in America took place.
25-03-1669 Mount Etna in Sicily erupted destroying Nicolosi. 20,000 people were killed.
25-03-1700 England, France and Netherlands ratify the 2nd Extermination Treaty.
25-03-1753 Voltaire left the court of Frederik II of Prussia.
25-03-1774 English Parliament passed the Boston Port Bill.
25-03-1776 The Continental Congress authorized a medal for General George Washington.
25-03-1802 France, Netherlands, Spain and England signed the Peace of Amiens.
25-03-1807 The first railway passenger service began in England.
25-03-1807 British Parliament abolished the slave trade.
25-03-1813 The frigate USS Essex flew the first U.S. flag in battle in the Pacific.
25-03-1814 The Netherlands Bank was established.
25-03-1820 Greece freedom revolt against anti Ottoman attack
25-03-1821 Greece gained independence from Turkey.
25-03-1856 A. E. Burnside patented Burnside carbine.
25-03-1857 Frederick Laggenheim took the first photo of a solar eclipse.
25-03-1865 The SS General Lyon at Cape Hatteras caught fire and sank. 400 people were killed.
25-03-1865 During the American Civil War, Confederate forces captured Fort Stedman in Virginia.
25-03-1879 Japan invaded the kingdom of Liuqiu (Ryukyu) Islands, formerly a vassal of China.
25-03-1895 Italian troops invaded Abyssinia (Ethiopia).
25-03-1898 The Intercollegiate Trapshooting Association was formed in New York City.
25-03-1900 The U.S. Socialist Party was formed in Indianapolis.
25-03-1901 55 people died when a Rock Island train derailed near Marshalltown, IA.
25-03-1901 The Mercedes was introduced by Daimler at the five-day "Week of Nice" in Nice, France.
25-03-1901 It was reported in Washington, DC, that Cubans were beginning to fear annexation.
25-03-1902 Irving W. Colburn patented the sheet glass drawing machine.
25-03-1902 In Russia, 567 students were found guilty of "political disaffection." 95 students were exiled to Siberia.
25-03-1904 E.D. Morel and Roger Casement formed the Congo Reform Association in Liverpool.
25-03-1905 Rebel battle flags that were captured during the American Civil War were returned to the South.
25-03-1905 Russia received Japan's terms for peace.
25-03-1907 Nicaraguan troops took Tegucigalpa, the capital of Honduras.
25-03-1908 Wilhelm II paid an official visit to Italy's king in Venice.
25-03-1909 In Russia, revolutionary Popova was arrested on 300 murder charges.
25-03-1911 In New York City, 146 women were killed in fire at the Triangle Shirtwaist Company in New York City. The owners of the company were indicted on manslaughter charges because some of the employees had been behind locked doors in the factory. The owners were later acquitted and in 1914 they were ordered to pay damages to each of the twenty-three families that had sued.
25-03-1913 The Palace Theatre opened in New York City.
25-03-1915 21 people died when a U.S. F-4 submarine sank off the Hawaiian coast.
25-03-1919 The Paris Peace Commission adopted a plan to protect nations from the influx of foreign labor.
25-03-1923 The British government granted Trans-Jordan autonomy.
25-03-1931 Fifty people were killed in riots that broke out in India. Gandhi was one of many people assaulted.
25-03-1931 The Scottsboro Boys were arrested in Alabama.
25-03-1936 The Detroit Red Wings defeated the Montreal Maroons in the longest hockey game to date. The game lasted for 2 hours and 56 minutes.
25-03-1940 The U.S. agreed to give Britain and France access to all American warplanes.
25-03-1941 Yugoslavia joined the Axis powers.
25-03-1941 The first paprika mill was incorporated in Dollon, SC.
25-03-1947 A coalmine explosion in Centralia, IL, killed 111 people.
25-03-1947 John D. Rockefeller III presented a check for $8.5 million to the United Nations for the purchase of land for the site of the U.N. center.
25-03-1953 The USS Missouri fired on targets at Kojo, North Korea.
25-03-1954 RCA manufactured its first color TV set and began mass production.
25-03-1957 The European Economic Community was established with the signing of the Treaty of Rome.
25-03-1960 A guided missile was launched from a nuclear powered submarine for the first time.
25-03-1965 Martin Luther King Jr. led a group of 25,000 to the state capital in Montgomery, AL.
25-03-1966 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the "poll tax" was unconstitutional.
25-03-1970 The Concorde made its first supersonic flight.
25-03-1971 The Boston Patriots became the New England Patriots.
25-03-1972 Bobby Hull joined Gordie Howe to become only the second National Hockey League player to score 600 career goals.
25-03-1975 King Faisal of Saudi Arabia was shot to death by a nephew. The nephew, with a history of mental illness, was beheaded the following June.
25-03-1981 The U.S. Embassy in San Salvador was damaged when gunmen attacked using rocket propelled grenades and machine guns.
25-03-1982 Wayne Gretzky became the first player in the NHL to score 200 points in a season.
25-03-1986 U.S. President Ronald Reagan ordered emergency aid for the Honduran army. U.S. helicopters took Honduran troops to the Nicaraguan border.
25-03-1988 Robert E. Chambers Jr. pled guilty to first-degree manslaughter in the death of 18-year-old Jennifer Levin. The case was known as New York City's "preppie murder case."
25-03-1989 In Paris, the Louvre reopened with I.M. Pei's new courtyard pyramid.
25-03-1990 A fire in Happy Land, an illegal New York City social club, killed 87 people.
25-03-1990 Estonia voted for independence from the Soviet Union.
25-03-1991 Iraqi President Saddam Hussein launched a major counter-offensive to recapture key towns from Kurds in northern Iraq.
25-03-1992 Soviet cosmonaut Sergei Krikalev returned to Earth after spending 10 months aboard the orbiting Mir space station.
25-03-1993 President de Klerk admitted that South Africa had built six nuclear bombs, but said that they had since been dismantled.
25-03-1994 United States troops completed their withdrawal from Somalia.
25-03-1995 Boxer Mike Tyson was released from jail after serving 3 years.
25-03-1996 An 81-day standoff by the antigovernment Freemen began at a ranch near Jordan, MT.
25-03-1996 The U.S. issued a newly redesigned $100 bill for circulation.
25-03-1998 A cancer patient was the first known to die under Oregon's doctor-assisted suicide law.
25-03-1998 The FCC nets $578.6 million at auction for licenses for new wireless technology.
25-03-1998 Quinn Pletcher was found guilty on charges of extortion. He had threatened to kill Bill Gates unless he was paid $5 million.
25-03-2002 The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) dismissed complaints against Walt Disney Co.'s ABC network broadcast of a Victoria's Secret fashion show in November 2001.
25-03-2004 The U.S. Senate voted (61-38) on the Unborn Victims of Violence Act (H.R. 1997) to make it a separate crime to harm a fetus during the commission of a violent federal crime.
26-03-1026 Conrad II was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope John XIX.
26-03-1799 Napoleon captured Jaffa Palestine.
26-03-1780 The British Gazette and Sunday Monitor was published for the first time. It was the first Sunday newspaper in Britain.
26-03-1793 The Holy Roman Emperor formally declared war on France.
26-03-1804 The U.S. Congress ordered the removal of Indians east of the Mississippi to Louisiana.
26-03-1804 The Louisiana Purchase was divided into the District of Louisiana and the Territory of Orleans.
26-03-1854 Charles III, duke of Parma, was attacked by an assassin. He died the next day.
26-03-1871 The Paris Commune was formally set up.
26-03-1878 Hastings College of Law was founded.
26-03-1885 Eastman Kodak (Eastman Dry Plate and Film Co.) produced the first commercial motion picture film in Rochester, NY.
26-03-1898 In South Africa, the world's first game reserve, the Sabi Game reserve, was designated.
26-03-1909 Russian troops invaded Persia to support Muhammad Ali as shah in place of the constitutional government.
26-03-1910 The U.S. Congress passed an amendment to the 1907 Immigration Act that barred criminals, paupers, anarchists and carriers of disease from settling in the U.S.
26-03-1913 During the Balkan War, the Bulgarians took Adrianople.
26-03-1917 At the start of the battle of Gaza, the British cavalry withdrew when 17,000 Turks blocked their advance.
26-03-1937 Spinach growers in Crystal City, TX, erected a statue of Popeye.
26-03-1938 Herman Goering warned all Jews to leave Austria.
26-03-1942 The Germans began sending Jews to Auschwitz in Poland.
26-03-1945 The battle of Iwo Jima ended.
26-03-1945 In the Aleutians, the battle of Komandorski began when the Japanese attempted to reinforce a garrison at Kiska and were intercepted by a U.S. naval force.
26-03-1951 The U.S. Air Force flag was approved. The flag included the coat of arms, 13 white stars and the Air Force seal on a blue background.
26-03-1953 Dr. Jonas Salk announced a new vaccine that would prevent poliomyelitis.
26-03-1956 Red Buttons made his debut as a television actor in "Studio One" on CBS television.
26-03-1958 The U.S. Army launched America's third successful satellite, Explorer III.
26-03-1962 The U.S. Supreme Court supported the 1-man-1-vote apportionment of seats in the State Legislature.
26-03-1969 The TV movie "Marcus Welby" was seen on ABC-TV. It was later turned into a series.
26-03-1971 Sheikh Mujibur Rahman declared East Pakistan to be the independent republic of Bangladesh.
26-03-1971 "Cannon" premiered on CBS-TV as a movie. It was turned into a series later in the year.
26-03-1972 The Los Angeles Lakers broke a National Basketball Association (NBA) record by winning 69 of their 82 games.
26-03-1973 Egyptian President Anwar Sadat took over the premiership and said "the stage of total confrontation (with Israel) has become inevitable."
26-03-1973 Women were allowed on the floor of the London Stock Exchange for the first time.
26-03-1979 The Camp David treaty was signed by Israel and Egypt that ended the 31-year state of war between the countries.
26-03-1982 Ground breaking ceremonies were held in Washington, DC, for the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
26-03-1989 The first free elections took place in the Soviet Union. Boris Yeltsin was elected.
26-03-1991 The presidents of Argentina, Paraguay, Brazil and Uruguay signed an agreement that established the Southern Cone Common Market, a free-trade zone, by January 1, 1995.
26-03-1992 In Indianapolis, heavyweight boxing champion Mike Tyson was found guilty of rape. He was sentenced to 6 years in prison. He only served three.
26-03-1995 Seven of the 15 European Union states abolished border controls.
26-03-1996 The International Monetary Fund approved a $10.2 billion loan for Russia to help the country transform its economy.
26-03-1997 The 39 bodies of Heaven's Gate members are found in a mansion in Rancho Santa Fe, CA. The group had committed suicide thinking that they would be picked up by a spaceship following behind the comet Hale-Bopp.
26-03-1998 In the U.S., the Federal government endorses new HIV test that yields instant results.
26-03-1998 Unisys Corp. and Lockheed Martin Corp. pay a $3.15 million fine for selling spare parts at inflated prices to the U.S. federal government.
26-03-1999 The macro virus "Melissa" was reported for the first.
26-03-1999 In Michigan, Dr. Jack Kevorkian was convicted of second-degree murder for giving a terminally ill man a lethal injection and putting it all on videotape on September 17, 1998 for "60 Minutes."
26-03-2000 The Seattle Kingdome was imploded to make room for a new football arena.
26-03-2000 In Russia, acting President Vladimir Putin was elected president outright. He won a sufficient number of votes to avoid a runoff election.
26-03-2007 The design for the "Forever Stamp" was unveiled by the U.S. Postal Service.
27-03-1350 While besieging Gibraltar, Alfonso XI of Castile died of the Black Death.
27-03-1512 Spanish explorer Juan Ponce de Leon sighted Florida.
27-03-1794 The U.S. Congress and President Washington authorized the creation of the U.S. Navy.
27-03-1802 The Treaty of Amiens was signed ending the French Revolutionary War.
27-03-1814 U.S. troops under Gen. Andrew Jackson defeated the Creek Indians at Horshoe Bend in Northern Alabama.
27-03-1836 The Mexican army massacred about 400 Texan rebels at Goliad, TX, under the order of Santa Anna.
27-03-1836 The first Mormon temple was dedicated in Kirtland, OH.
27-03-1841 The first steam fire engine was tested in New York City.
27-03-1860 The corkscrew was patented by M.L. Byrn.
27-03-1866 U.S. President Andrew Johnson vetoed the civil rights bill, which later became the 14th amendment.
27-03-1884 The first long-distance telephone call was made from Boston to New York.
27-03-1899 The first international radio transmission between England and France was achieved by the Italian inventor G. Marconi.
27-03-1900 The London Parliament passed the War Loan Act that gave 35 million pounds to the Boer War cause in South Africa.
27-03-1900 The Russian army mobilized 250,000 troops for active duty.
27-03-1901 Filipino rebel leader Emilio Aguinaldo was captured by the U.S.
27-03-1904 Mary Jarris "Mother" Jones was ordered by Colorado state authorities to leave the state. She was accused of stirring up striking coal miners.
27-03-1907 French troops occupied Oudja, Morocco, as a punitive action for the murder of French Dr. Muchamp.
27-03-1912 The first cherry blossom trees were planted in Washington, DC. The trees were a gift from Japan.
27-03-1917 The Seattle Metropolitans, of the Pacific Coast League of Canada, defeated the Montreal Canadiens and became the first U.S. hockey team to win the Stanley Cup.
27-03-1931 Actor Charlie Chaplin received France’s Legion of Honor decoration.
27-03-1933 About 55,000 people staged a protest against Hitler in New York City.
27-03-1933 In the U.S., the Farm Credit Administration was authorized.
27-03-1941 Tokeo Yoshikawa arrived in Oahu, HI, and began spying for Japan on the U.S. Fleet at Pearl Harbor.
27-03-1942 The British raided the Nazi submarine base at St. Nazaire, France.
27-03-1944 One-thousand Jews left Drancy, France, for the Auschwitz concentration camp.
27-03-1944 Thousands of Jews were murdered in Kaunas, Lithuania.
27-03-1946 Four-month long strikes at both General Electric and General Motors ended with a wage increase.
27-03-1952 The U.S. Eighth Army reached the 38th parallel in Korea, the original dividing line between the two Koreas.
27-03-1955 Steve McQueen made his network TV debut on "Goodyear Playhouse."
27-03-1958 Nikita Khrushchev became the chairman of the Soviet Council of Ministers in addition to First Secretary of the Communist Party.
27-03-1958 The U.S. announced a plan to explore space near the moon.
27-03-1964 An earthquake in Alaska killed 114 people and registered 8.4 on the Richter Scale.
27-03-1968 Yuri Gagarin, the first man to orbit the earth, died in a plane crash.
27-03-1976 Washington, DC, opened its subway system.
27-03-1977 About 570 people died when a KLM 747 and a Pan Am 747 collided with each other on a foggy runway on the Canary Island of Tenerife.
27-03-1985 Billy Dee Williams received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
27-03-1988 The U.S. Senate ratified the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty.
27-03-1989 The U.S. anti-missile satellite failed the first test in space.
27-03-1992 Police in Philadelphia, PA, arrested a man with AIDS on charges that he may have infected several hundred teenage boys with HIV through sexual relations.
27-03-1993 In China, Communist Party leader Jiang Zemin was appointed President.
27-03-1995 Maurizo Gucci was shot to death outside his office in Milan.
27-03-1997 Russian workers, nearly 2 million, held a nationwide strike to protest unpaid wages.
27-03-1997 In Australia, Governor-General William Deane signed a bill to overturn a 1996 Northern Territory act to legalize assisted suicides. The 1996 act was the first in the world to permit assisted suicides.
27-03-1997 Dexter King met with James Earl Ray. Ray was in prison for the assassination of Dr. Martin Luther King. Dexter King believes that Ray had nothing to do with the assassination.
27-03-1998 In the U.S., the FDA approved the prescription drug Viagra. It was the first pill for male impotence.
27-03-1998 Top civilian aircraft makers in France, Spain, Germany and Britain agreed to create single European aerospace and defense company.
27-03-1998 Ax-wielders killed at least 52 people in southern Algeria, most of which were toddlers.
27-03-2002 Rodney Dangerfield received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
27-03-2004 NASA successfully launched an unpiloted X-43A jet that hit Mach 7 (about 5,000 mph).
28-03-1774 Britain passed the Coercive Act against Massachusetts.
28-03-1797 Nathaniel Briggs patented a washing machine.
28-03-1834 The U.S. Senate voted to censure President Jackson for the removal of federal deposits from the Bank of the United States.
28-03-1854 The Crimean War began with Britain and France declaring war on Russia.
28-03-1864 A group of Copperheads attack Federal soldiers in Charleston, IL. Five were killed and twenty were wounded.
28-03-1865 Outdoor advertising legislation was enacted in New York. The law banned "painting on stones, rocks and trees."
28-03-1885 The Salvation Army was officially organized in the U.S.
28-03-1898 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that a child born in the U.S. to Chinese immigrants was a U.S. citizen. This meant that they could not be deported under the Chinese Exclusion Act.
28-03-1903 Anatole France's "Crainquebille" premiered in Paris.
28-03-1905 The U.S. took full control over Dominican revenues.
28-03-1908 Automobile owners lobbied the U.S. Congress, supporting a bill that called for vehicle licensing and federal registration.
28-03-1910 The first seaplane took off from water at Martinques, France. The pilot was Henri Fabre.
28-03-1911 In New York, suffragists performed the political play "Pageant of Protest."
28-03-1917 During World War I the Women’s Army Auxiliary Corps (WAAC) was founded.
28-03-1921 U.S. President Warren Harding named William Howard Taft as chief justice of the United States Supreme Court.
28-03-1922 Bradley A. Fiske patented a microfilm reading device.
28-03-1930 Constantinople and Angora changed their names to Istanbul and Ankara respectively.
28-03-1933 In Germany, the Nazis ordered a ban on all Jews in businesses, professions and schools.
28-03-1938 In Italy, psychiatrists demonstrated the use of electric-shock therapy for treatment of certain mental illnesses.
28-03-1939 The Spanish Civil War ended as Madrid fell to Francisco Franco.
28-03-1941 The Italian fleet was defeated by the British at the Battle of Matapan.
28-03-1942 British naval forces raided the Nazi occupied French port of St. Nazaire.
28-03-1945 Germany launched the last of the V-2 rockets against England.
28-03-1947 The American Helicopter Society revealed a flying device that could be strapped to a person's body.
28-03-1962 The U.S. Air Force announced research into the use of lasers to intercept missiles and satellites.
28-03-1963 Sonny Werblin announced that the New York Titans of the American Football League was changing its name to the New York Jets. (NFL)
28-03-1967 Raymond Burr starred in a TV movie titled "Ironside." The movie was later turned into a television series.
28-03-1968 The U.S. lost its first F-111 aircraft in Vietnam when it vanished while on a combat mission. North Vietnam claimed that they had shot it down.
28-03-1974 A streaker ran onto the set of "The Tonight Show starring Johnny Carson."
28-03-1979 A major accident occurred at Pennsylvania's Three Mile Island nuclear power plant. A nuclear power reactor overheated and suffered a partial meltdown.
28-03-1986 The U.S. Senate passed $100 million aid package for the Nicaraguan contras.
28-03-1986 More than 6,000 radio stations of all format varieties played "We are the World" simultaneously at 10:15 a.m. EST.
28-03-1990 Jesse Owens received the Congressional Gold Medal from U.S. President George Bush.
28-03-1990 In Britain, a joint Anglo-U.S. "sting" operation ended with the seizure of 40 capacitors, which can be used in the trigger mechanism of a nuclear weapon.
28-03-1991 The U.S. embassy in Moscow was severely damaged by fire.
28-03-1994 Violence between Zulus and African National Congress supporters took the lives of 18 in Johannesburg.
28-03-1999 Paraguay's President Raúl Cubas Grau resigned after protests inspired by the assassination of Vice-President Luis María Argańa on March 23. The nation's Congress had accused Cubas and his political associate, Gen. Lino César Oviedo, for Cubas' murder. Senate President Luis González Macchi took office as Paraguay's new chief executive.
28-03-2002 The exhibit "The Italians: Three Centuries of Italian Art" opened at the National Gallery of Australia.
29-03-1461 Edward IV secured his claim to the English thrown by defeating Henry VI’s Lancastrians at the battle of Towdon.
29-03-1638 First permanent European settlement in Delaware was established.
29-03-1847 U.S. troops under General Winfield Scott took possession of the Mexican stronghold at Vera Cruz.
29-03-1848 Niagara Falls stopped flowing for one day due to an ice jam.
29-03-1867 The British Parliament passed the North America Act to create the Dominion of Canada.
29-03-1882 The Knights of Columbus organization was granted a charter by the State of Connecticut.
29-03-1901 The first federal elections were held in Australia.
29-03-1903 A regular news service began between New York and London on Marconi's wireless.
29-03-1906 In the U.S., 500,000 coal miners walked off the job seeking higher wages.
29-03-1913 The Reichstag announced a raise in taxes in order to finance the new military budget.
29-03-1916 The Italians call off the fifth attack on Isonzo.
29-03-1932 Jack Benny made his radio debut.
29-03-1936 Italy firebombed the Ethiopian city of Harar.
29-03-1941 The British sink five Italian warships off the Peloponnesus coast in the Mediterranean.
29-03-1943 In the U.S. rationing of meat, butter and cheese began during World War II.
29-03-1946 Fiorella LaGuardia became the director general of the United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Organization.
29-03-1946 Gold Coast became the first British colony to hold an African parliamentary majority.
29-03-1951 The Chinese reject MacArthur's offer for a truce in Korea.
29-03-1951 In the United States, Julius and Ethel Rosenberg were convicted of conspiracy to commit espionage. They were executed in June 19, 1953.
29-03-1961 The 23rd amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified. The amendment allowed residents of Washington, DC, to vote for president.
29-03-1962 Cuba opened the trial of the Bay of Pigs invaders.
29-03-1962 Jack Paar made his final appearance on the "Tonight" show.
29-03-1966 Leonid Brezhnev became the First Secretary of the Soviet Communist Party. He denounced the American policy in Vietnam and called it one of aggression.
29-03-1967 France launched its first nuclear submarine.
29-03-1971 Lt. William Calley Jr., of the U.S. Army, was found guilty of the premeditated murder of at least 22 Vietnamese civilians. He was sentenced to life imprisonment. The trial was the result of the My Lai massacre in Vietnam on March 16, 1968.
29-03-1971 A jury in Los Angeles recommended the death penalty for Charles Manson and three female followers for the 1969 Tate-La Bianca murders. The death sentences were later commuted to live in prison.
29-03-1973 "Hommy," the Puerto Rican version of the rock opera "Tommy," opened in New York City.
29-03-1973 The last U.S. troops left South Vietnam.
29-03-1974 Mariner 10, the U.S. space probe became the first spacecraft to reach the planet Mercury. It had been launched on November 3, 1973.
29-03-1974 Eight Ohio National Guardsmen were indicted on charges stemming from the shooting deaths of four students at Kent State University on May 4, 1970. All the guardsmen were later acquitted.
29-03-1975 Egyptian president Anwar Sadat declared that he would reopen the Suez Canal on June 5, 1975.
29-03-1979 The Committee on Assassinations Report issued by U.S. House of Representatives stated the assassination of President John F. Kennedy was the result of a conspiracy.
29-03-1982 The soap opera "Search for Tomorrow" changed from CBS to NBC.
29-03-1986 A court in Rome acquitted six men in a plot to kill the Pope.
29-03-1987 Hulk Hogan took 11 minutes, 43 seconds to pin Andre the Giant in front of 93,136 at Wrestlemania III fans at the Silverdome in Pontiac, MI.
29-03-1992 Democratic presidential front-runner Bill Clinton said "I didn't inhale and I didn't try it again" in reference to when he had experimented with marijuana.
29-03-1993 The South Korean government agreed to pay financial support to women who had been forced to have sex with Japanese troops during World War II.
29-03-1993 Clint Eastwood won his first Oscars. He won them for best film and best director for the film "Unforgiven."
29-03-1995 The U.S. House of Representatives rejected a constitutional amendment that would have limited terms to 12 years in the U.S. House and Senate.
29-03-1998 Tennessee won the woman's college basketball championship over Louisiana. Tennessee had set a NCAA record with regular season record or 39-0.
29-03-1999 At least 87 people died in an earthquake in India's Himalayan foothills.
29-03-1999 The Dow Jones industrial average closed above the 10,000 mark for the first time.
29-03-2004 Bulgaria, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia and Slovenia became members of NATO.
30-03-1492 King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella signed a decree expelling all Jews from Spain.
30-03-1533 Henry VIII divorced his first wife, Catherine of Aragon.
30-03-1814 The allied European nations against Napoleon marched into Paris.
30-03-1822 Florida became a U.S. territory.
30-03-1842 Dr. Crawford W. Long performed the first operation while his patient was anesthetized by ether.
30-03-1855 About 5,000 "Border Ruffians" from western Missouri invaded the territory of Kansas and forced the election of a pro-slavery legislature. It was the first election in Kansas.
30-03-1858 Hyman L. Lipman of Philadelphia patented the pencil.
30-03-1867 The U.S. purchased Alaska from Russia for $7.2 million dollars.
30-03-1870 The 15th amendment, guaranteeing the right to vote regardless of race, was passed by the U.S. Congress.
30-03-1870 Texas was readmitted to the Union.
30-03-1903 Revolutionary activity in the Dominican Republic brought U.S. troops to Santo Domingo to protect American interests.
30-03-1905 U.S. President Roosevelt was chosen to mediate in the Russo-Japanese peace talks.
30-03-1909 The Queensboro bridge in New York opened linking Manhattan and Queens. It was the first double decker bridge.
30-03-1909 In Oklahoma, Seminole Indians revolted against meager pay for government jobs.
30-03-1916 Pancho Villa killed 172 at the Guerrero garrison in Mexico.
30-03-1936 Britain announced a naval construction program of 38 warships.
30-03-1940 The Japanese set up a puppet government called Manchuko in Nanking, China.
30-03-1941 The German Afrika Korps under General Erwin Rommel began its first offensive against British forces in Libya.
30-03-1944 The U.S. fleet attacked Palau, near the Philippines.
30-03-1945 The U.S.S.R. invaded Austria during World War II.
30-03-1946 The Allies seized 1,000 Nazis attempting to revive the Nazi party in Frankfurt.
30-03-1947 Lord Mountbatten arrived in India as the new Viceroy.
30-03-1950 The invention of the phototransistor was announced.
30-03-1950 U.S. President Truman denounced Senator Joe McCarthy as a saboteur of U.S. foreign policy.
30-03-1957 Tunisia and Morocco signed a friendship treaty in Rabat.
30-03-1958 The Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater gave its initial performance.
30-03-1964 "Jeopardy" debuted on NBC-TV.
30-03-1964 John Glenn withdrew from the Ohio race for U.S. Senate because of injuries suffered in a fall.
30-03-1970 "Applause" opened on Broadway.
30-03-1970 "Another World - Somerset" debuted on NBC-TV.
30-03-1972 The British government assumed direct rule over Northern Ireland.
30-03-1972 The Eastertide Offensive began when North Vietnamese troops crossed into the Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) in the northern portion of South Vietnam.
30-03-1975 As the North Vietnamese forces moved toward Saigon South Vietnamese soldiers mob rescue jets in desperation.
30-03-1981 U.S. President Ronald Reagan was shot and wounded in Washington, DC, by John W. Hinckley Jr. Two police officers and Press Secretary James Brady were also wounded.
30-03-1984 The U.S. ended its participation in the multinational peace force in Lebanon.
30-03-1987 Vincent Van Gogh's "Sunflowers" was bought for $39.85 million.
30-03-1993 In Sarajevo, two Serb militiamen were sentenced to death for war crimes committed in Bosnia.
30-03-1993 In the Peanuts comic strip, Charlie Brown hit his first home run.
30-03-1994 Serbs and Croats signed a cease-fire to end their war in Croatia while Bosnian Muslims and Serbs continued to fight each other.
30-03-1998 Rolls-Royce was purchased by BMW in a $570 million deal.
30-03-2002 An unmanned U.S. spy plan crashed at sea in the Southern Philippines.
30-03-2002 Suspected Islamic militants set off several grenades at a temple in Indian-controlled Kashmir. Four civilians, four policemen and two attackers were killed and 20 people were injured.
31-03-1492 King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain issued an edict expelling Jews who were unwilling to convert to Christianity.
31-03-1776 Abigail Adams wrote to her husband John that women were "determined to foment a rebellion" if the new Declaration of Independence failed to guarantee their rights.
31-03-1779 Russia and Turkey signed a treaty concerning military action in Crimea.
31-03-1831 Quebec and Montreal were incorporated as cities.
31-03-1854 The U.S. government signed the Treaty of Kanagawa with Japan. The act opened the ports of Shimoda and Hakotade to American trade.
31-03-1862 Skirmishing between Rebels and Union forces took place at Island 10 on the Mississippi River.
31-03-1870 In Perth Amboy, NJ, Thomas P. Munday became the first black to vote in the U.S.
31-03-1880 Wabash, IN, became the first town to be completely illuminated with electric light.
31-03-1889 In Paris, the Eiffel Tower officially opened.
31-03-1900 The W.E. Roach Company was the first automobile company to put an advertisement in a national magazine. The magazine was the "Saturday Evening Post".
31-03-1900 In France, the National Assembly passed a law reducing the workday for women and children to 11 hours.
31-03-1901 In Russia, the Czar lashed out at Socialist-Revolutionaries with the arrests of 72 people and the seizing of two printing presses.
31-03-1902 In Tennessee, 22 coal miners were killed by an explosion.
31-03-1904 In India, hundreds of Tibetans were slaughtered by the British.
31-03-1905 Kaiser Wilhelm arrived in Tangier proclaiming to support for an independent state of Morocco.
31-03-1906 The Conference on Moroccan Reforms in Algerciras ended after two months with France and Germany in agreement.
31-03-1906 The Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States was founded to set rules in amateur sports. The organization became the National Collegiate Athletic Association in 1910.
31-03-1908 250,000 coal miners in Indianapolis, IN, went on strike to await a wage adjustment.
31-03-1909 Serbia accepted Austrian control over Bosnia-Herzegovina.
31-03-1917 The U.S. purchased and took possession of the Virgin Islands from Denmark for $25 million.
31-03-1918 For the first time in the U.S., Daylight Saving Time went into effect.
31-03-1921 Great Britain declared a state of emergency because of the thousands of coal miners on strike.
31-03-1923 In New York City, the first U.S. dance marathon was held. Alma Cummings set a new world record of 27 hours.
31-03-1932 The Ford Motor Co. debuted its V-8 engine.
31-03-1933 The U.S. Congress authorized the Civilian Conservation Corps to relieve rampant unemployment.
31-03-1933 The "Soperton News" in Georgia became the first newspaper to publish using a pine pulp paper.
31-03-1939 Britain and France agreed to support Poland if Germany threatened invasion.
31-03-1940 La Guardia airport in New York officially opened to the public.
31-03-1941 Germany began a counter offensive in North Africa.
31-03-1945 "The Glass Menagerie" by Tennessee Williams opened on Broadway.
31-03-1946 Monarchists won the elections in Greece.
31-03-1947 John L. Lewis called a strike in sympathy for the miners killed in an explosion in Centralia, IL, on March 25, 1947.
31-03-1948 The Soviets in Germany began controlling the Western trains headed toward Berlin.
31-03-1949 Winston Churchill declared that the A-bomb was the only thing that kept the U.S.S.R. from taking over Europe.
31-03-1949 Newfoundland entered the Canadian confederation as its 10th province.
31-03-1958 The U.S. Navy formed the atomic submarine division.
31-03-1959 The Dalai Lama (Lhama Dhondrub, Tenzin Gyatso) began exile by crossing the border into India where he was granted political asylum. Gyatso was the 14th Daila Lama.
31-03-1960 The South African government declared a state of emergency after demonstrations lead to the death of more than 50 Africans.
31-03-1966 An estimated 200,000 anti-war demonstrators march in New York City.
31-03-1966 The Soviet Union launched Luna 10, which became the first spacecraft to enter a lunar orbit.
31-03-1967 U.S. President Lyndon Johnson signed the Consular Treaty, the first bi-lateral pact with the Soviet Union since the Bolshevik Revolution.
31-03-1970 The U.S. forces in Vietnam down a MIG-21, it was the first since September 1968.
31-03-1976 The New Jersey Supreme Court ruled that Karen Anne Quinlan could be disconnected from a respirator. Quinlan remained comatose until 1985 when she died.
31-03-1980 U.S. President Carter deregulated the banking industry.
31-03-1985 ABC-TV aired the 200th episode of "The Love Boat."
31-03-1986 167 people died when a Mexicana Airlines Boeing 727 crashed in Los Angeles.
31-03-1987 HBO (Home Box Office) earned its first Oscar for "Down and Out in America".
31-03-1989 Canada and France signed a fishing rights pact.
31-03-1991 Albania offered a multi-party election for the first time in 50 years. Incumbent President Ramiz Alia won.
31-03-1991 Iraqi forces recaptured the northern city of Kirkuk from Kurdish guerillas.
31-03-1993 Brandon Lee was killed accidentally while filming a movie.
31-03-1994 "Nature" magazine announced that a complete skull of Australppithecus afarensis had been found in Ethiopia. The finding is of humankind's earliest ancestor.
31-03-1998 U.N. Security Council imposed arms embargo on Yugoslavia.
31-03-1998 Buddy Hackett received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
31-03-1998 For the first time in U.S. history the federal government's detailed financial statement was released. This occurred under the Clinton administration.
31-03-1999 Three U.S. soldiers were captured by Yugoslav soldiers three miles from the Yugoslav border in Macedonia.
31-03-1999 Fabio was hit in the face by a bird during a promotional ride of a new roller coaster at the Busch Gardens theme park in Williamsburg, VA. Fabio received a one-inch cut across his nose.
31-03-2000 In Uganda, officials set the number of deaths linked to a doomsday religious cult, the Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments, at more than 900. In Kanungu, a March 17 fire at the cult's church killed more than 530 and authorities subsequently found mass graves at various sites linked to the cult.
31-03-2004 Air America Radio launched five stations around the U.S.
31-03-2004 Google Inc. announced that it would be introducing a free e-mail service called Gmail.
01-05-0408 Theodosius II succeeded to the throne of Constantinople.
01-05-1308 King Albert was murdered by his nephew John, because he refused his share of the Habsburg lands.
01-05-1486 Christopher Columbus convinced Queen Isabella to fund an expedition to the West Indies.
01-05-1707 England, Wales and Scotland were united to form Great Britain.
01-05-1751 America’s first cricket tournament was held in New York City.
01-05-1805 The state of Virginia passed a law requiring all freed slaves to leave the state, or risk either imprisonment or deportation.
01-05-1863 In Virginia, the Battle of Chancellorsville began. General Robert E. Lee's forces began fighting with Union troops under General Joseph Hooker. Confederate General Stonewall Jackson was mortally wounded by his own soldiers in this battle. (May 1-4)
01-05-1867 Reconstruction in the South began with black voter registration.
01-05-1877 U.S. President Rutherford B. Hayes withdrew all Federal troops from the South, ending Reconstruction.
01-05-1883 William F. Cody (Buffalo Bill) had his first Wild West Show.
01-05-1884 The construction of the firt American 10-story building began in Chicago, IL.
01-05-1898 The U.S. Navy under Dewey defeated the Spanish fleet at Manila Bay in the Philippines.
01-05-1905 In New York, radium was tested as a cure for cancer.
01-05-1912 In London's Kensington Gardens, a statue of Peter Pan was erected.
01-05-1915 A German submarine sank the U.S. ship Gulflight.
01-05-1922 Charlie Robertson of the Chicago White Sox pitched a perfect no-hit, no-run game against the Detroit Tigers. The Sox won 3-0. Another perfect game did not come along until 46 years later.
01-05-1927 Adolf Hitler held his first Nazi meeting in Berlin.
01-05-1931 The Empire State Building in New York was dedicated and opened. It was 102 stories tall and was the tallest building in the world at the time.
01-05-1934 The Philippine legislature accepted a U.S. proposal for independence.
01-05-1937 U.S. President Franklin Roosevelt signed an act of neutrality, keeping the United States out of World War II.
01-05-1941 "Citizen Kane," directed and starring Orson Welles, premiered in New York.
01-05-1944 The Messerschmitt Me 262, the first combat jet, made its first flight.
01-05-1945 Martin Bormann, private secretary to Adolf Hitler, escaped from the Fuehrerbunker as the Red Army advanced on Berlin.
01-05-1945 Admiral Karl Doenitz succeeded Hitler as leader of the Third Reich. This was one day after Hitler committed suicide.
01-05-1948 The People's Democratic Republic of Korea (North Korea) was proclaimed.
01-05-1950 Gwendolyn Brooks became the first African American to win the Pulitzer Prize for her book of poetry called Annie Allen.
01-05-1958 James Van Allen reported that two radiation belts encircled Earth.
01-05-1960 Francis Gary Powers' U-2 spy plane was shot down over the Soviet Union. Powers was taken prisoner.
01-05-1961 Fidel Castro announced there would be no more elections in Cuba.
01-05-1967 Anastasio Somoza Debayle became president of Nicaragua.
01-05-1968 In the second day of battle, U.S. Marines, with the support of naval fire, continue their attack on a North Vietnamese Division at Dai Do.
01-05-1969 Leonard Tose bought the NFL's Philadelphia Eagles for $16,155,000.
01-05-1970 Students at Kent State University riot in downtown Kent, OH, in protest of the American invasion of Cambodia.
01-05-1971 The National Railroad Passenger Copr. (Amtrak) went into service. It was established by the U.S. Congress to run the nation's intercity railroads.
01-05-1986 The Tass News Agency reported the Chernobyl nuclear power plant accident.
01-05-1986 Bill Elliott set a stock car speed record with his Ford Thunderbird in Talladega, AL. Elliott reached a speed of 212.229 mph.
01-05-1989 Disney-MGM Studios opened.
01-05-1992 On the third day of the Los Angeles riots resulting from the Rodney King beating trial. King appeared in public to appeal for calm, he asked, "Can we all get along?"
01-05-1998 Arrow Air was fined $5 million for using spare parts that lacked federal approval in the U.S.
01-05-1999 On Mount Everest, a group of U.S. mountain climbers discovered the body of George Mallory. Mallory had died in June of 1924 while trying to become the first person to reach the summit of Everest. At the time of the discovery it was unclear whether or not Mallory had actually reached the summit.
01-05-2000 ABC aired the first celebrity "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire."
01-05-2000 The "Barbie for President" doll was released in stores.
01-05-2001 In Washington, DC, Chandra Levy disappeared. She was an intern at the U.S. Bureau of Prisons. California Representative Gary Condit was named in the investigation. Her body was found on May 22, 2002 in Rock Creek Park.
02-05-1519 Leonardo da Vinci died.
02-05-1670 The Hudson Bay Company was founded by England's King Charles II.
02-05-1776 France and Spain agreed to donate arms to American rebels fighting the British.
02-05-1797 A mutiny in the British navy spread from Spithead to the rest of the fleet.
02-05-1798 The black General Toussaint L’ouverture forced British troops to agree to evacuate the port of Santo Domingo.
02-05-1808 The citizens of Madrid rose up against Napoleon.
02-05-1813 Napoleon defeated a Russian and Prussian army at Grossgorschen.
02-05-1853 Franconi’s Hippodrome opened at Broadway and 23rd Street in New York City.
02-05-1863 Confederate Gen. Thomas "Stonewall" Jackson was wounded by his own men in the battle of Chancellorsville, VA. He died 8 days later.
02-05-1865 U.S. President Andrew Johnson offered $100,000 reward for the capture of Confederate President Jefferson Davis.
02-05-1885 The Congo Free State was established by King Leopold II of Belgium.
02-05-1885 "Good Housekeeping" was first published.
02-05-1887 Hannibal W. Goodwin applied for a patent on celluloid photographic film. This is the film from which movies are shown.
02-05-1890 The Oklahoma Territory was organized.
02-05-1902 "A Trip To The Moon," the first science fiction film was released. It was created by magician George Melies.
02-05-1919 The first U.S. air passenger service started.
02-05-1922 WBAP-AM bean broadcasting in north Texas.
02-05-1926 In India, Hindu women gained the right to seek elected office.
02-05-1926 U.S. Marines landed in Nicaragua to put down a revolt and to protect U.S. interests. They did not depart until 1933.
02-05-1932 Jack Benny's first radio show debuted on NBC Radio.
02-05-1933 Hitler banned trade unions in Germany.
02-05-1939 Lou Gehrig set a new major league baseball record when he played in his 2,130th game. The streak began on June 1, 1925.
02-05-1941 Hostilities broke out between British forces in Iraq and that country’s pro-German faction.
02-05-1941 The Federal Communications Commission agreed to let regular scheduling of TV broadcasts by commercial TV stations begin on July 1, 1941. This was the start of network television.
02-05-1945 Russians took Berlin after 12 days of fierce house-to-house fighting. The Allies announced the surrender of Nazi troops in Italy and parts of Austria.
02-05-1946 Prisoners revolted at California's Alcatraz prison.
02-05-1954 Stan Musial of the St. Louis Cardinals set a new major league record when he hit 5 home runs against the New York Giants.
02-05-1960 Caryl Chessman was executed. He was a convicted sex offender and had become a best selling author while on death row.
02-05-1965 The "Early Bird" satellite was used to transmit television pictures across the Atlantic.
02-05-1970 Student anti-war protesters at Ohio's Kent State University burn down the campus ROTC building. The National Guard took control of the campus.
02-05-1974 Former U.S. Vice President Spiro T. Agnew was disbarred by the Maryland Court of Appeals.
02-05-1974 The filming of "Jaws" began in Martha's Vineyard, MA.
02-05-1982 The British submarine HMS Conqueror sank Argentina's only cruiser, the General Belgrano during the Falkland Islands War. More than 350 people died.
02-05-1993 At Washington's National Gallery of Art, an exhibit of 80 paintings from the collection of Dr. Albert C. Barnes opened.
02-05-1993 Authorities said that they had recovered the remains of David Koresh from the Branch Davidian compound near Waco, TX.
02-05-1994 Nelson Mandela claimed victory after South Africa's first democratic elections.
02-05-1999 In Panama, Mireya Moscoso de Grubar, of the Armulfista Party, was elected president.
02-05-2002 It was reported that Phyllis Diller had retired from touring.
03-05-1568 French forces in Florida slaughtered hundreds of Spanish.
03-05-1802 Washington, DC, was incorporated as a city.
03-05-1855 Macon B. Allen became the first African American to be admitted to the Bar in Massachusetts.
03-05-1859 France declared war on Austria.
03-05-1888 Thomas Edison organized the Edison Phonograph Works.
03-05-1916 Irish nationalist Padraic Pearse and two others were executed by the British for their roles in the Easter Rising.
03-05-1921 West Virginia imposed the first state sales tax.
03-05-1926 The revival of Wilde's "The Importance of Being Earnest" opened in New York.
03-05-1926 U.S. Marines landed in Nicaragua and stayed until 1933.
03-05-1926 In Britain, trade unions began a general strike.
03-05-1927 Francis E.J. Wilde of Meadowmere Park, NY, patented the electric sign flasher.
03-05-1933 The U.S. Mint was under the direction of a woman for the first time when Nellie Ross took the position.
03-05-1937 Margaret Mitchell won a Pulitzer Prize for "Gone With The Wind."
03-05-1944 Wartime rationing of most grades of meats ended in the U.S.
03-05-1944 Dr. Robert Woodward and Dr. William Doering produced the first synthetic quinine at Harvard University.
03-05-1945 Indian forces captured Rangoon, Burma, from the Japanese.
03-05-1948 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that covenants prohibiting the sale of real estate to blacks and other minorities were legally unenforceable.
03-05-1952 The first airplane landed at the geographic North Pole.
03-05-1966 The game "Twister" was featured on the "Tonight Show" with Johnny Carson.
03-05-1968 After three days of battle, the U.S. Marines retook Dai Do complex in Vietnam. They found that the North Vietnamese had evacuated the area.
03-05-1971 Anti-war protesters began four days of demonstrations in Washington, DC.
03-05-1971 National Public Radio broadcast for the first time.
03-05-1971 James Earl Ray, Martin Luther King's assassin, was caught in a jailbreak attempt.
03-05-1986 In NASA's first post-Challenger launch, an unmanned Delta rocket lost power in its main engine shortly after liftoff. Safety officers destroyed it by remote control.
03-05-1988 The White House acknowledged that first lady Nancy Reagan had used astrological advice to help schedule her husband's activities.
03-05-1992 Five days of rioting and looting ended in Los Angeles, CA. The riots, that killed 53 people, began after the acquittal of police officers in the beating of Rodney King.
03-05-1997 The "Republic of Texas" surrendered to authorities ending an armed standoff where two people were held hostage. The group asserts the independence of Texas from the U.S.
03-05-1998 "The Sevres Road," by 18-century landscape painter Camille Corot, stolen from the Louvre in France.
03-05-1999 Mark Manes, at age 22, was arrested for supplying a gun to Eric Harris and Dylan Kleibold, who later killed 13 people at Columbine High School in Colorado.
03-05-1999 Hasbro released the first collection of toys for the Star Wars movie "Episode I: The Phantom Menace."
03-05-1999 The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed above 11,000 for the first time.
03-05-2000 The trial of two Libyans accused of killing 270 people in the bombing of Pan Am flight 103 (over Lockerbie) opened.
03-05-2006 In Alexandria, VA, Al-Quaida conspirator Zacarias Moussaoui was given a sentence of life in prison for his role in the terrorist attack on the U.S. on September 11, 2001.
04-05-1471 In England, the Yorkists defeated the Landcastrians at the battle of Tewkesbury in the War of the Roses.
04-05-1493 Alexander VI divided non-Christian world between Spain and Portugal.
04-05-1626 Dutch explorer Peter Minuit landed on Manhattan Island. Native Americans later sold the island (20,000 acres) for $24 in cloth and buttons.
04-05-1715 A French manufacturer debuted the first folding umbrella.
04-05-1776 Rhode Island declared its freedom from England two months before the Declaration of Independence was adopted.
04-05-1795 Thousands of rioters entered jails in Lyons, France, and massacre 99 Jacobin prisoners.
04-05-1814 Napoleon Bonaparte disembarked at Portoferraio on the island of Elba in the Mediterranean.
04-05-1863 The Battle of Chancellorsville ended when the Union Army retreated.
04-05-1864 Ulysses S Grant crossed Rapidan and began his duel with Robert E Lee.
04-05-1886 Chichester Bell and Charles S. Tainter patented the gramophone. It was the first practical phonograph.
04-05-1905 Belmont Park opened in surburban Long Island. It opened as the largest race track in the world.
04-05-1916 Germany agreed to limit its submarine warfare after a demand from U.S. President Wilson.
04-05-1930 Mahatma Gandhi was arrested by the British.
04-05-1932 Al Capone entered the Atlanta Penitentiary federal prison for income-tax evasion.
04-05-1942 The Battle of the Coral Sea commenced as American and Japanese carriers launched their attacks at each other.
04-05-1942 The United States began food rationing.
04-05-1946 A two-day riot at Alcatraz prison in San Francisco Bay ended. Five people were killed.
04-05-1954 The first intercollegiate court tennis match in the U.S. It was between Yale and Princeton.
04-05-1961 Thirteen civil rights activists, dubbed "Freedom Riders," began a bus trip through the South.
04-05-1964 "Another World" premiered on NBC-TV.
04-05-1970 The Ohio National Guardsmen opened fire on students during an anti-Vietnam war protest at Kent State University. Four students were killed and nine others were wounded.
04-05-1979 Margaret Thatcher became Britain's first woman prime minister.
04-05-1987 Live models were used for the first time in Playtex bra ads.
04-05-1989 Oliver North, a former White House aide was convicted of shredding documents and two other crimes. He was acquitted of nine other charges stemming from the Iran-Contra affair. The three convictions were later overturned on appeal.
04-05-1994 Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin and PLO leader Yasser Arafat signed a historic accord on Palestinian autonomy that granted self-rule in the Gaza Strip and Jericho.
04-05-1998 Unabomber Theodore Kaczynski was given four life sentences plus 30 years by a federal judge in Sacramento, CA. The sentence was under a plea agreement that spared Kaczynski the death penalty.
04-05-1999 Several severe tornadoes hit the Midwest U.S. overnight. At least 45 people were killed.
04-05-1999 Manuel Babbitt was executed for killing Leah Schendel in 1980. Babbitt had received a purple heart for his injuries in Vietnam while on death row.
04-05-2000 Londeners elected their mayor for the first time.
04-05-2003 Idaho Gem was born. He was the first member of the horse family to be cloned.
05-05-1494 Christopher Columbus sighted Jamaica on his second trip to the Western Hemisphere. He named the island Santa Gloria.
05-05-1798 U.S. Secretary of War William McHenry ordered that the USS Constitution be made ready for sea. The frigate was launched on October 21, 1797, but had never been put to sea.
05-05-1809 Mary Kies was awarded the first patent to go to a woman. It was for technique for weaving straw with silk and thread.
05-05-1814 The British attack the American forces at Ft. Ontario, Oswego, NY.
05-05-1821 Napoleon Bonaparte died on the island of St. Helena, where he had been in exile.
05-05-1834 The first mainland railway line opened in Belgium.
05-05-1847 The AMA (American Medical Association) was organized in Philadelphia, PA.
05-05-1862 The Battle of Puebla took place. It is celebrated as Cinco de Mayo Day.
05-05-1865 The Thirteenth Amendment was ratified, abolishing slavery in the U.S.
05-05-1886 A bomb exploded on the fourth day of a workers' strike in Chicago, IL.
05-05-1891 Music Hall was dedicated in New York City. It was later renamed Carnegie Hall.
05-05-1892 The U.S. Congress extended the Geary Chinese Exclusion Act for 10 more years. The act required Chinese in the U.S. to be registered or face deportation.
05-05-1901 The first Catholic mass for night workers was held at the Church of St. Andrew in New York City.
05-05-1904 The third perfect game of the major leagues was thrown by Cy Young (Boston Red Sox) against the Philadelphia Athletics. It was the first perfect game under modern rules.
05-05-1912 Soviet Communist Party newspaper Pravda began publishing.
05-05-1916 U.S. Marines invaded the Dominican Republic.
05-05-1917 Eugene Jacques Bullard becomes the first African-American aviator when he earned his flying certificate with the French Air Service.
05-05-1920 Anarchists Nicola Sacco and Bartolomeo Vanzetti were arrested for murder.
05-05-1925 John T. Scopes, a biology teacher in Dayton, TN, was arrested for teaching Darwin's theory of evolution.
05-05-1926 Eisenstein's film "Battleship Potemkin" was shown in Germany for the first time.
05-05-1926 Sinclair Lewis refused a 1925 Pulitzer for "Arrowsmith."
05-05-1936 Edward Ravenscroft received a patent for the screw-on bottle cap with a pour lip.
05-05-1942 General Joseph Stilwell learned that the Japanese had cut his railway out of China and was forced to lead his troops into India.
05-05-1945 The Netherlands and Denmark were liberated from Nazi control.
05-05-1945 A Japanese balloon bomb exploded on Gearhart Mountain in Oregon. A pregnant woman was killed in the only fatal attack of its kind during World War II.
05-05-1955 "Damn Yankees" opened on Broadway.
05-05-1955 The Federal Republic of Germany (West Germany) became a sovereign state.
05-05-1956 Jim Bailey became the first runner to break the four-minute mile in the U.S. He was clocked at 3:58.5.
05-05-1961 Alan Shepard became the first American in space when he made a 15 minute suborbital flight.
05-05-1966 Willie Mays broke the National League record for home runs when he hit his 512th.
05-05-1978 Pete Rose of the Cincinnati Reds registered his 3,000th major league hit.
05-05-1981 Irish Republican Army hunger-striker Bobby Sands died at the Maze Prison in Northern Ireland. It was his 66th day without food.
05-05-1987 The U.S. congressional Iran-Contra hearings opened.
05-05-1991 In New York, Carnegie Hall marked its 100th anniversary.
05-05-1994 Michael Fay was caned in Singapore for vandalism. He received four lashes.
05-05-1997 Dolores Hope received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
05-05-1997 Ivan Reitman received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
05-05-2000 The final episode of "Boy Meets World" aired on ABC.
06-05-1527 German troops began sacking Rome, bringing about the end of the Renaissance.
06-05-1529 Babur defeated the Afghan Chiefs in the Battle of Ghagra, India.
06-05-1576 The peace treaty of Chastenoy ended the fifth war of religion.
06-05-1682 King Louis XIV moved his court to Versailles, France.
06-05-1835 James Gordon Bennett published the "New York Herald" for the first time.
06-05-1840 The first adhesive postage stamps went on sale in Great Britain.
06-05-1851 The mechanical refrigerator was patented by Dr. John Gorrie.
06-05-1851 Linus Yale patented the clock-type lock.
06-05-1861 Arkansas became the ninth state to secede from the Union.
06-05-1877 Chief Crazy Horse surrendered to U.S. troops in Nebraska.
06-05-1882 The U.S. Congress passed the Chinese Exclusion Act. The act barred Chinese immigrants from the U.S. for 10 years.
06-05-1889 The Universal Exposition opened in Paris, France, marking the dedication of the Eiffel Tower. Also at the exposition was the first automobile in Paris, the Mercedes-Benz.
06-05-1910 Kind Edward VII of England died. He was succeeded by his second son, George V.
06-05-1915 Babe Ruth hit his first major league home run while playing for the Boston Red Sox.
06-05-1937 The German airship Hindenburg crashed and burned in Lakehurst, NJ. Thirty-six people (of the 97 on board) were killed.
06-05-1941 Joseph Stalin assumed the Soviet premiership.
06-05-1941 Bob Hope gave his first USO show at California's March Field.
06-05-1942 During World War II, the Japanese seized control of the Philippines. About 15,000 Americans and Filipinos on Corregidor surrendered to the Japanese.
06-05-1945 Axis Sally made her final propaganda broadcast to Allied troops.
06-05-1946 The New York Yankees became the first major league baseball team to travel by plane.
06-05-1954 British runner Roger Banister broke the four minute mile.
06-05-1957 U.S. Senator John Fitzgerald Kennedy was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for his book "Profiles in Courage".
06-05-1959 The Pablo Picasso painting of a Dutch girl was sold for $154,000 in London. It was the highest price paid (at the time) for a painting by a living artist.
06-05-1960 Britain's Princess Margaret married Anthony Armstrong Jones. They were divorced in 1978.
06-05-1960 U.S. President Eisenhower signed the Civil Rights Act of 1960.
06-05-1962 The first nuclear warhead was fired from the Polaris submarine.
06-05-1981 A jury of international architects and sculptors unanimously selected Maya Ying Lin's entry for the design of the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
06-05-1994 The Chunnel officially opened. The tunnel under the English Channel links England and France.
06-05-1994 Former Arkansas state worker Paula Jones filed suit against U.S. President Clinton. The case alleged that he had sexually harassed her in 1991.
06-05-1997 Army Staff Sgt. Delmar G. Simpson was sentenced to 25 years in prison for raping six trainees at Aberdeen Proving Ground in Maryland.
06-05-1997 Four health-care companies agreed to a settlement of $600 million to hemophiliacs who had contracted AIDS from tainted blood between 1978-1985.
06-05-1999 Britain's Labour Party won the largest number of seats in the first elections for Scotland's new Parliament and Wales' new Assembly.
06-05-1999 A parole board in New York voted to release Amy Fisher. She had been in jail for 7 years for shooting her lover's wife, Mary Jo Buttafuoco, in the face.
06-05-2001 Chandra Levy's parents reported her missing to police in Washington, DC. Levy's body was found on May 22, 2002 in Rock Creek Park.
07-05-0558 The dome of the church of St. Sophia in Constantinople collapsed. It was immediately rebuilt as ordered by Justinian.
07-05-1274 The Second Council of Lyons opened in France to regulate the election of the pope.
07-05-1429 The English siege of Orleans was broken by Joan of Arc.
07-05-1525 The German peasants' revolt was crushed by the ruling class and church.
07-05-1663 The first Theatre Royal was opened in London.
07-05-1763 Indian chief Pontiac began all out war on the British in New York.
07-05-1789 The first U.S. Presidential Inaugural Ball was held in New York City.
07-05-1800 The U.S. Congress divided the Northwest Territory into two parts. The western part became the Indiana Territory and the eastern section remained the Northwest Territory.
07-05-1847 The AMA (American Medical Association) was founded in Philadelphia.
07-05-1898 The first Intercollegiate Trapshooting Association meet was held in New Haven, CT.
07-05-1912 Columbia University approved final plans for awarding the Pulitzer Prize in several categories.
07-05-1912 The first airplane equipped with a machine gun flew over College Park, MD.
07-05-1915 The Lusitania, a civilian ship, was sunk by a German submarine. 1,198 people were killed.
07-05-1926 A U.S. report showed that one-third of the nation's exports were motors.
07-05-1937 The German Condor Legion arrived in Spain to assist Franco’s forces.
07-05-1939 Germany and Italy announced a military and political alliance known as the Rome-Berlin Axis.
07-05-1940 Winston Churchill became British Prime Minister.
07-05-1942 In the Battle of the Coral Sea, Japanese and American navies attacked each other with carrier planes. It was the first time in the history of naval warfare where two enemy fleets fought without seeing each other.
07-05-1943 The last major German strongholds in North Africa, Tunis and Bizerte, fell to Allied forces.
07-05-1945 Baseball owner Branch Rickey announced the organization of the United States Negro Baseball League. There were 6 teams.
07-05-1945 Germany signed unconditional surrender ending World War II. It would take effect the next day.
07-05-1946 Tokyo Telecommunications Engineering Corp. was founded. The company was later renamed Sony.
07-05-1951 Russia was admitted to participate in the 1952 Olympic Games by the International Olympic Committee.
07-05-1954 French Colonial Forces surrendered to the Vietminh at Dien Bien Phu after 55 days of fighting.
07-05-1954 The United States and the United Kingdom rejected the Soviet Union's bid to join NATO.
07-05-1958 Howard Johnson set an aircraft altitude record in F-104.
07-05-1960 Leonid Brezhnev became president of the Soviet Union.
07-05-1975 U.S. President Ford declared an end to the Vietnam War.
07-05-1977 Rookie Janet Guthrie set the fastest time on opening day of practice for the Indianapolis 500. Her time was 185.607.
07-05-1984 A $180 million out-of-court settlement was announced in the Agent Orange class-action suit brought by Vietnam veterans who claimed they had suffered injury from exposure to the defoliant while serving in the armed forces.
07-05-1987 Shelly Long, as Diane Chambers, made her last appearance as a regular on the TV show "Cheers."
07-05-1992 A 203-year-old proposed constitutional amendment barring the U.S. Congress from giving itself a midterm pay raise was ratified as the 27th Amendment.
07-05-1994 The Edvard Munch painting "The Scream" was recovered after being stolen 3 months earlier from an Oslo Museum. This version of "The Scream", one of four different versions, was painted on paper.
07-05-1996 The trial of Serbian police officer Dusan Tadic opened in the Netherlands. He was later convicted on murder-torture charges and was sentenced to 20 years in prison.
07-05-1997 A report released by the U.S. government said that Switzerland provided Nazi Germany with equipment and credit during World War II. Germany exchanged for gold what had been plundered or stolen. Switzerland did not comply with postwar agreements to return the gold.
07-05-1998 Daimler-Benz bought Chrysler Corp. for close to $40 billion. It was the largest industrial merger on record.
07-05-1998 Residents of London voted to elect their own mayor for the first time in history. The vote would take place in May 2000.
07-05-1998 Leeza Gibbons received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
07-05-1999 A jury ruled that "The Jenny Jones Show" and Warner Bros. were liable in the shooting death of Scott Amedure. He was killed by another guest on the show. The jury's award was $25 million.
07-05-1999 Jerry Moss received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
07-05-1999 In Belgrade, Yugoslavia, three Chinese citizens were killed and 20 were wounded when a NATO plane mistakenly bombed the Chinese embassy.
07-05-1999 In Guinea-Bissau, the government of President Joăo Bernardo Vieira was ousted in a military coup.
07-05-2000 Russian President Vladimir V. Putin named First Deputy Premier Mikhail Kasyanov as premier.
07-05-2003 In Washington, DC, General Motors Corp. delivered six fuel cell vehicles to Capitol Hill for lawmakers and others to test drive during the next two years.
07-05-2003 Roger Moore collapsed during a matinee performance of the Broadway comedy "The Play What I Wrote." He finished the show after a 10-minute break. He was fitted with a pacemaker the following day.
08-05-1096 Peter the Hermit and his army reached Hungary. They passed through without incident.
08-05-1450 Jack Cade's Rebellion-Kentishmen revolted against King Henry VI.
08-05-1541 Hernando de Soto reached the Mississippi River. He called it Rio de Espiritu Santo.
08-05-1794 Antoine Lavoisier was executed by guillotine. He was the French chemist that discovered oxygen.
08-05-1794 The United States Post Office was established.
08-05-1846 The first major battle of the Mexican War was fought. The battle occurred in Palo Alto, TX.
08-05-1847 The rubber tire was patented by Robert W. Thompson.
08-05-1879 George Selden applied for the first automobile patent.
08-05-1886 Pharmacist Dr. John Styth Pemberton invented what would later be called "Coca-Cola."
08-05-1902 Mount Pelee on Martinique erupted and killed over 30,000 people and destroyed the town of St. Pierre.
08-05-1904 U.S. Marines landed in Tangier to protect the Belgian legation.
08-05-1914 The U.S. Congress passed a Joint Resolution that designated the second Sunday in May as Mother's Day.
08-05-1915 H.P. Whitney's Regret became the first filly to win the Kentucky Derby.
08-05-1919 The first transatlantic flight took-off by a navy seaplane.
08-05-1921 Sweden abolished capital punishment.
08-05-1933 Gandhi began a hunger strike to protest British oppression in India.
08-05-1939 Clay Puett's electric starting gate was used for the first time.
08-05-1943 The Germans suppressed a revolt by Polish Jews and destroyed the Warsaw Ghetto.
