08-05-1945 U.S. President Harry Truman announced that World War II had ended in Europe.
08-05-1954 Parry O'Brien became the first to toss a shot put over 60 feet. O'Brien achieved a distance of 60 feet 5 1/4 inches.
08-05-1956 Alfred E. Neuman appeared on the cover of "Mad Magazine" for the first time.
08-05-1958 U.S. President Eisenhower ordered the National Guard out of Little Rock as Ernest Green became the first black to graduate from an Arkansas public school.
08-05-1960 Diplomatic relations between Cuba and the Soviet Union resumed.
08-05-1961 New Yorkers selected a new name for their new National League baseball franchise. They chose the Mets.
08-05-1967 Muhammad Ali was indicted for refusing induction in U.S. Army.
08-05-1970 Construction workers broke up an anti-war protest on New York City's Wall Street.
08-05-1973 Militant American Indians who had held the South Dakota hamlet of Wounded Knee for 10 weeks surrendered.
08-05-1978 David R. Berkowitz, known as the "Son of Sam," pled guilty to six murder charges.
08-05-1984 The Soviet Union announced that they would not participate in the 1984 Summer Olympics Games in Los Angeles.
08-05-1984 Joanie (Erin Moran) and Chachi (Scott Baio) got married on ABC-TV's "Happy Days."
08-05-1985 "New Coke" was released to the public on the 99th anniversary of Coca-Cola.
08-05-1986 Reporters were told that 84,000 people had been evacuated from areas near the Chernobyl nuclear plant in Soviet Ukraine.
08-05-1997 Larry King received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
08-05-1998 A pipe burst leaving a million residents without water in Malaysia's capital area. This added to four days of shortages that 2 million already faced.
08-05-1999 The first female cadet graduated from The Citadel military college.
09-05-1429 Joan of Arc defeated the besieging English at Orleans.
09-05-1502 Christopher Columbus left Spain for his final trip to the Western Hemisphere.
09-05-1671 Thomas "Captain" Blood stole the crown jewels from the Tower of London.
09-05-1754 The first newspaper cartoon in America showed a divided snake "Join or die" in "The Pennsylvania Gazette."
09-05-1785 Joseph Bramah patented the beer-pump handle.
09-05-1825 The Chatham Theatre opened in New York City. It was the first gas-lit theater in America.
09-05-1901 In Australia, the Duke of Cornwall and York declared the First Commonwealth Parliament open.
09-05-1915 German and French forces fought the Battle of Artois.
09-05-1926 Americans Richard Byrd and Floyd Bennett became the first men to fly an airplane over the North Pole.
09-05-1930 A starting gate was used to start a Triple Crown race for the first time.
09-05-1936 Fascist Italy took Addis Abba and annexed Ethiopia.
09-05-1936 The first sheet of postage stamps of more than one variety went on sale in New York City.
09-05-1940 Vivien Leigh debuted in America on stage in "Romeo and Juliet" with Lawrence Olivier.
09-05-1941 The German submarine U-110 was captured at sea by Britain's Royal navy.
09-05-1945 U.S. officials announced that the midnight entertainment curfew was being lifted immediately.
09-05-1946 King Victor Emmanuel II of Italy abdicated and was replaced by Umberto.
09-05-1955 West Germany joined NATO.
09-05-1958 Richard Burton made his network television debut in the presentation of "Wuthering Heights" on CBS-TV.
09-05-1960 The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved for sale an oral birth-control pill for the first time.
09-05-1961 Jim Gentile (Baltimore Orioles) set a major league baseball record when he hit a grand slam home run in two consecutive innings. The game was against the Minnesota Twins.
09-05-1962 A laser beam was successfully bounced off Moon for the first time.
09-05-1974 The House Judiciary Committee began formal hearings on the Nixon impeachment.
09-05-1978 The bullet-riddled body of former Italian Prime Minister Aldo Moro was found in an automobile in the center of Rome. The Red Brigades had abducted him.
09-05-1980 A Liberian freighter hit the Sunshine Skyway Bridge over Tampa Bay in Florida. 35 motorists were killed and a 1,400-foot section of the bridge collapsed.
09-05-1987 Tom Cruise and Mimi Rogers were married.
09-05-1994 Nelson Mandela was chosen to be South Africa's first black president.
09-05-1996 In video testimony to a courtroom in Little Rock, AR, U.S. President Clinton insisted that he had nothing to do with a $300,000 loan in the criminal case against his former Whitewater partners.
09-05-2002 In Bethlehem, West Bank, a deal was reached that would end the 38-day standoff at the Church of the Nativity. Thirteen suspected militants were to be deported to several different countries. The standoff had begun on April 2, 2002.
09-05-2002 In Kaspiisk, Russia, 39 people were killed and at least 130 were injurde when a remote-controlled bomb exploded during a holiday parade.
09-05-2002 In Bahrain, people were allowed to vote for representatives for the first time in nearly 30 years. Women were allowed to vote for the first time in the country's history.
10-05-1503 Christopher Columbus discovered the Cayman Islands.
10-05-1676 Bacon's Rebellion, which pits frontiersmen against the government, began.
10-05-1768 The imprisonment of the journalist John Wilkes as an outlaw provoked violence in London. Wilkes was returned to parliament as a member for Middlesex.
10-05-1773 The English Parliament passed the Tea Act, which taxed all tea in the U.S. colonies.
10-05-1774 Louis XVI ascended the throne of France.
10-05-1775 Ethan Allen and Colonel Benedict Arnold led an attack on the British Fort Ticonderoga and captured it from the British.
10-05-1794 Elizabeth, the sister of King Louis XVI, was beheaded.
10-05-1796 Napoleon Bonaparte won a brilliant victory against the Austrians at Lodi bridge in Italy.
10-05-1840 Mormon leader Joseph Smith moved his band of followers to Illinois to escape the hostilities they had experienced in Missouri.
10-05-1857 The Seepoys of India revolted against the British Army.
10-05-1865 Jefferson Davis was captured by Union troops near Irvinville, GA.
10-05-1869 Central Pacific and Union Pacific Rail Roads meet in Promontory, UT. A golden spike was driven in at the celebration of the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S.
10-05-1872 Victoria Woodhull became the first woman nominated for the U.S. presidency.
10-05-1876 Richard Wagner’s "Centennial Inaugural March" was heard for the first time at the Centennial Exposition in Philadelphia, PA.
10-05-1898 A vending machine law was enacted in Omaha, NE. It cost $5,000 for a permit.
10-05-1908 The first Mother's Day observance took place during a church service in Grafton, West Virginia.
10-05-1924 J. Edgar Hoover was appointed head of the Federal Bureau of Investigation.
10-05-1927 The Hotel Statler in Boston, MA. became the first hotel to install radio headsets in each of its 1,300 rooms.
10-05-1928 WGY-TV in Schenectady, NY, began regular television programming.
10-05-1930 The Adler Planetarium opened to the public in Chicago, IL.
10-05-1933 The Nazis staged massive public book burnings in Germany.
10-05-1940 Germany invaded Belgium, France, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg.
10-05-1941 England's House of Commons was destroyed by a German air raid.
10-05-1941 Rudolf Hess, Adolf Hitler's deputy, parachuted into Scotland on what he claimed was a peace mission.
10-05-1942 U.S. forces in the Philippines began to surrender to the Japanese.
10-05-1943 U.S. troops invaded Attu in the Aleutian Islands to expel the Japanese.
10-05-1960 The U.S.S. Triton completed the first circumnavigation of the globe under water. The trip started on February 16.
10-05-1968 Preliminary Vietnam peace talks began in Paris.
10-05-1969 The National and American Football Leagues announced their plans to merge for the 1970-71 season.
10-05-1978 Britain's Princess Margaret and the Earl of Snowdon announced they were divorcing after 18 years of marriage.
10-05-1982 Elliott Gould made his dramatic television debut after 30 movies in 17 years. He starred in "The Rules of Marriage" on CBS-TV.
10-05-1986 Navy Lt. Commander Donnie Cochran became the first black pilot to fly with the Blue Angels team.
10-05-1994 The state of Illinois executed convicted serial killer John Wayne Gacy for the murders of 33 young men and boys.
10-05-1994 Nelson Mandela was sworn in as South Africa’s first black president.
10-05-1997 An earthquake in northeastern Iran killed at least 2,400 people.
10-05-1999 China broke off talks on human rights with the U.S. in response to NATO's accidental bombing of the Chinese Embassy in Yugoslavia.
10-05-1999 The Cezanne painting "Still Life With Curtain, Pitcher and Bowl of Fruit" sold for 60.5 million.
10-05-2000 11,000 residents were evacuated in Los Alamos, NM, due to a fire that was blown into a canyon. The fire had been deliberately set to clear brush.
10-05-2001 Boeing Co. announced that it would be moving its headquarters to Chicago, IL.
10-05-2001 In Ghana, 121 people were killed in a stampede at a soccer game.
10-05-2002 Robert Hanssen was sentenced to life in prison with no chance for parole. Hanssen, an FBI agent, had sold U.S. secrets to Moscow for $1.4 million in cash and diamonds.
10-05-2002 Taiwan test fired a locally made Sky Bow II surface-to-air missile for the first time. They also fired three U.S.-made Hawk missiles.
10-05-2002 Dr. Pepper announced that it would be introducing a new flavor, Red Fusion, for the first time in 117 years.
11-05-0330 Constantinople, previously the town of Byzantium, was founded.
11-05-1573 Henry of Anjou became the first elected king of Poland.
11-05-1647 Peter Stuyvesant arrived in New Amsterdam to become governor.
11-05-1689 French and English naval battle takes place at Bantry Bay.
11-05-1745 French forces defeat an Anglo-Dutch-Hanoverian army at Fontenoy.
11-05-1792 The Columbia River was discovered by Captain Robert Gray.
11-05-1812 British prime Minster Spencer Perceval was shot by a bankrupt banker in the lobby of the House of Commons.
11-05-1816 The American Bible Society was formed in New York City.
11-05-1857 Indian mutineers seized Delhi from the British.
11-05-1858 Minnesota was admitted as the 32nd U.S. state.
11-05-1860 Giuseppe Garibaldi landed at Marsala, Sicily.
11-05-1889 Major Joseph Washington Wham takes charge of $28,000 in gold and silver to pay troops at various points in the Arizona Territory. The money was stolen in a train robbery.
11-05-1894 Workers at the Pullman Palace Car Company in Illinois went on strike.
11-05-1910 Glacier National Park in Montana was established.
11-05-1927 The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences was founded.
11-05-1934 A severe two-day dust storm stripped the topsoil from the great plains of the U.S. and created a "Dust Bowl." The storm was one of many.
11-05-1944 A major offensive was launched by the allied forces in central Italy.
11-05-1947 The creation of the tubeless tire was announced by the B.F. Goodrich Company.
11-05-1949 Siam changed its name to Thailand.
11-05-1960 Israeli soldiers captured Adolf Eichmann in Buenos Aires.
11-05-1967 The siege of Khe Sanh ended.
11-05-1985 More than 50 people died when a flash fire swept a soccer stadium in Bradford, England.
11-05-1995 The Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty was extended indefinitely. The treaty limited the spread of nuclear material for military purposes.
11-05-1996 An Atlanta-bound ValuJet DC-9 caught fire shortly after takeoff from Miami and crashed into the Florida Everglades. All 110 people on board were killed.
11-05-1997 Garry Kasparov, world chess champion, lost his first ever multi-game match. He lost to IBM's chess computer Deep Blue. It was the first time a computer had beat a world-champion player.
11-05-1998 India conducted its first underground nuclear tests, three of them, in 24 years. The tests were in violation of a global ban on nuclear testing.
11-05-1998 A French mint produced the first coins of Europe's single currency. The coin is known as the euro.
11-05-2001 U.S. Attorney General John Ashcroft announced his decision to approve a 30-day delay of the execution of convicted Oklahoma City bomber Timothy McVeigh. McVeigh had been scheduled to be executed on May 16, 2001. The delay was because the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) had failed to disclose thousands of documents to McVeigh's defense team.
12-05-1588 King Henry III fled Paris after Henry of Guise triumphantly entered the city.
12-05-1780 Charleston, South Carolina fell to British forces.
12-05-1831 Edward Smith became the first indicted bank robber in the U.S.
12-05-1847 William Clayton invented the odometer.
12-05-1870 Manitoba entered the Confederation as a Canadian province.
12-05-1881 Tunisia, in North Africa became a French protectorate.
12-05-1885 In the Battle of Batoche, French Canadians rebelled against the Canadian government.
12-05-1888 Charles Sherrill of the Yale track team became the first runner to use the crouching start for a fast break in a foot race.
12-05-1926 The airship Norge became the first vessel to fly over the North Pole.
12-05-1926 In Britain, a general strike by trade unions ended. The strike began on May 3, 1926.
12-05-1932 The infant body of Charles and Anna Lindbergh's son was found just a few miles from the Lindbergh home near Hopewell, NJ.
12-05-1937 Britain's King George VI was crowned at Westminster Abbey.
12-05-1940 The Nazi conquest of France began with the German army crossing Muese River.
12-05-1942 The Soviet Army launched its first major offensive of World War II and took Kharkov in the eastern Ukraine from the German army.
12-05-1943 The Axis forces in North Africa surrendered during World War II.
12-05-1948 The state of Israel and its provisional government was established.
12-05-1949 The Soviet Union announced an end to the Berlin Blockade.
12-05-1950 The American Bowling Congress abolished its white males-only membership restriction after 34 years.
12-05-1957 A.J. Foyt won his first auto racing victory in Kansas City, MO.
12-05-1965 West Germany and Israel exchanged letters establishing diplomatic relations.
12-05-1970 Ernie Banks, of the Chicago Cubs, hit his 500th home run.
12-05-1975 U.S. merchant ship Mayaguez was seized by Cambodian forces in international waters.
12-05-1978 The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration announced that they would no longer exclusively name hurricanes after women.
12-05-1982 In Fatima, Portugal, security guards overpowered a Spanish priest armed with a bayonet who was trying to reach Pope John Paul II.
12-05-1992 Four suspects were arrested in the beating of trucker Reginald Denny at the start of the Los Angeles riots.
12-05-1999 Russian President Boris Yeltsin dismissed Prime Minister Yevgeny Primakov and named Interior Minister Sergei Stepashin as his successor.
12-05-2002 Former U.S. President Carter arrived in Cuba for a visit with Fidel Castro. It was the first time a U.S. head of state, in or out of office, had gone to the island since Castro's 1959 revolution.
12-05-2003 In Texas, fifty-nine Democratic lawmakers went into hiding over a dispute with Republican's over a congressional redistricting plan.
13-05-1607 Jamestown, Virginia, was settled as a colony of England.
13-05-1648 Margaret Jones of Plymouth was found guilty of witchcraft and was sentenced to be hanged by the neck.
13-05-1779 The War of Bavarian Succession ended.
13-05-1787 Captain Arthur Phillip left Britain for Australia. He successfully landed eleven ships full of convicts on January 18, 1788, at Botany Bay. The group moved north eight days later and settled at Port Jackson.
13-05-1821 The first practical printing press was patented in the U.S. by Samuel Rust.
13-05-1846 The U.S. declared that war already existed with Mexico.
13-05-1854 The first big American billiards match was held at Malcolm Hall in Syracuse, NY.
13-05-1861 Britain declared its neutrality in the American Civil War.
13-05-1864 The Battle of Resaca commenced as Union General Sherman fought towards Atlanta during the American Civil War.
13-05-1865 The last land engagement of the American Civil War was fought at the Battle of Palmito Ranch in far south Texas, more than a month after Gen. Lee's surrender at Appomattox, VA.
13-05-1867 Confederate President Jefferson Davis became a free man after spending two years in prison for his role in the American Civil War.
13-05-1873 Ludwig M. Wolf patented the sewing machine lamp holder.
13-05-1880 Thomas Edison tested his experimental electric railway in Menlo Park.
13-05-1888 Slavery was abolished in Brazil.
13-05-1911 The New York Giants set a major league baseball record. Ten runners crossed home plate before the first out of the game against St. Louis.
13-05-1912 Royal Flying Corps was established in England.
13-05-1913 Igor Sikorsky flew the first four engine aircraft.
13-05-1917 Three peasant children near Fatima, Portugal, reported seeing a vision of the Virgin Mary.
13-05-1918 The first airmail postage stamps were issued with airplanes on them. The denominations were 6, 16, and 24 cents.
13-05-1926 In Warsaw, Joseph Pilsudski had President Wojciechowski arrested.
13-05-1927 "Black Friday" occurred in Germany.
13-05-1940 Winston Churchill made his first speech as the prime minister of Britain.
13-05-1949 The first gas turbine to pump natural gas was installed in Wilmar, AR.
13-05-1954 U.S. President Eisenhower signed into law the St. Lawrence Seaway Development Act.
13-05-1958 French troops took control of Algiers.
13-05-1958 U.S. Vice President Nixon's limousine was battered by rocks thrown by anti-U.S. demonstrators in Caracas, Venezuela.
13-05-1967 Mickey Mantle hit his 500th homerun.
13-05-1968 Peace talks between the U.S. and North Vietnam began in Paris.
13-05-1975 Hailstones the size of tennis balls hit Wenerville, TN.
13-05-1981 Pope John Paul II was shot and seriously wounded in St. Peter's Square by Turkish assailant Mehmet Ali Agca.
13-05-1982 The Chicago Cubs became the first major league baseball team to win 8,000 games.
13-05-1985 Tony Perez became the oldest major league baseball player to hit a grand slam home run at the age of 42 and 11 months.
13-05-1985 A confrontation between Philadelphia authorities and the radical group MOVE ended as police dropped an explosive onto the group's headquarters. 11 people died in the fire that resulted.
13-05-1996 In Bangladesh 600 people were killed by a tornado.
13-05-1998 India did a second round of nuclear tests. The first round had been done 2 days earlier. Within hours the U.S. and Japan imposed tough economic sanctions. India claimed that the tests were necessary to maintain India's national security.
13-05-1999 In Moscow, the impeachment of Russian President Boris Yeltsin began.
14-05-1264 King Henry III was captured by his brother in law Simon deMontfort at the Battle of Lewes in France.
14-05-1509 In the Battle of Agnadello, French defeated Venitians in Northern Italy.
14-05-1610 French King Henri IV (Henri de Navarre) was assassinated by a fanatical monk, François Ravillac.
14-05-1643 Louis XIV became King of France at age 4 upon the death of his father, Louis XIII.
14-05-1727 Thomas Gainsborough was born. He was an English painter.
14-05-1787 Delegates began gathering in Philadelphia for a convention to draw up the U.S. Constitution.
14-05-1796 The first smallpox vaccination was given by Edward Jenner.
14-05-1804 William Clark set off the famous expedition from Camp Dubois. A few days later, in St. Louis, Meriwether Lewis joined the group. The group was known as the "Corps of Discovery."
14-05-1811 Paraguay gained independence from Spain.
14-05-1853 Gail Borden applied for a patent for condensed milk.
14-05-1862 The chronograph was patented by Adolphe Nicole.
14-05-1874 McGill University and Harvard met at Cambridge, MA, for the first college football game to charge admission.
14-05-1878 The name Vaseline was registered by Robert A. Chesebrough.
14-05-1879 Thomas Edison incorporated the Edison Telephone Company of Europe.
14-05-1897 "The Stars and Stripes Forever" by John Phillip Sousa was performed for the first time. It was at a ceremony where a statue of George Washington was unveiled.
14-05-1897 Guglielmo Marconi made the first communication by wireless telegraph.
14-05-1904 In St. Louis, the Olympic games were held. It was the first time for the games to be played in the U.S.
14-05-1913 The Rockefeller Foundation was created by John D. Rockefeller with a gift of $100,000,000.
14-05-1935 The Philippines ratified an independence agreement.
14-05-1940 The Netherlands surrendered to Nazi Germany.
14-05-1942 The Women's Auxiliary Army Corps (WAAC) was established by an act of the U.S. Congress.
14-05-1942 "Lincoln Portrait" by Aaron Copland was performed for the first time by the Cincinnati Symphony Orchestra.
14-05-1942 The British, while retreating from Burma, reached India.
14-05-1948 Prime Minister David Ben-Gurion proclaimed the independent State of Israel as British rule in in Palestine came to an end.
14-05-1955 The Warsaw Pact, a Easter European mutual-defense treaty, was signed in Poland by eight communist bloc countries including the Soviet Union.
14-05-1961 A bus carrying Freedom Riders was bombed and burned in Alabama.
14-05-1969 Jacqueline Susann’s second novel, "The Love Machine," was published by Simon and Schuster.
14-05-1973 Skylab One was launched into orbit around Earth as the first U.S. manned space station.
14-05-1975 U.S. forces raided the Cambodian island of Koh Tang and recaptured the American merchant ship Mayaguez. All 40 crew members were released safely by Cambodia. About 40 U.S. servicemen were killed in the military operation.
14-05-1980 U.S. President Carter inaugurated the Department of Health and Human Services.
14-05-1985 The first McDonald's restaurant became the first fast-food business museum. It is located in Des Plaines, Illinois.
14-05-1989 The final episode of "Family Ties" aired.
14-05-1992 Former Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev addressed members of the U.S. Congress, appealing to them to pass a bill to aid the people of the former Soviet Union.
14-05-1996 A tornado hit 80 villages in nothern Bangladesh. More than 440 people were killed.
14-05-1998 The Associated Press marked its 150th anniversary.
14-05-1998 The final episode of the TV series "Seinfeld" aired after nine years on NBC.
14-05-1999 North Korea returned the remains of six U.S. soldiers that had been killed during the Korean War.
14-05-1999 Jess Marlow received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
14-05-2005 The art exhibit "Gumby and Friends: The First 50 Years" opened at the Lynn House Gallery in Antioch, CA.
15-05-1602 Cape Cod was discovered by Bartholomew Gosnold.
15-05-1614 An aristocratic uprising in France ended with the treaty of St.Menehould.
15-05-1618 Johannes Kepler discovered his harmonics law.
15-05-1702 The War of Spanish Succession began.
15-05-1768 Under the Treaty of Versailles, France purchased Corsica from Genoa.
15-05-1795 Napoleon entered the Lombardian capital of Milan.
15-05-1849 Neapolitan troops entered Palermo, and were in possession of Sicily.
15-05-1856 Lyman Frank Baum, author of "The Wonderful Wizard of Oz," was born.
15-05-1862 The U.S. Congress created the U.S. Department of Agriculture.
15-05-1911 The U.S. Supreme Court ordered the dissolution of Standard Oil Company, ruling it was in violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act.
15-05-1916 U.S. Marines landed in Santo Domingo to quell civil disorder.
15-05-1918 Regular airmail service between New York City, Philadelphia and Washington, DC, began under the direction of the Post Office Department, which later became the U.S. Postal Service.
15-05-1926 Roald Amundsen and Lincoln Ellsworth were forced down in Alaska after a four-day flight over an icecap. Ice had begun to form on the dirigible Norge.
15-05-1926 The New York Rangers were officially granted a franchise in the NHL. The NHL also announced that Chicago and Detroit would be joining the league in November.
15-05-1930 Ellen Church became the first airline stewardess.
15-05-1940 Nylon stockings went on sale for the first time in the U.S.
15-05-1941 Joe DiMaggio began his historic major league baseball hitting streak of 56 games.
15-05-1942 Gasoline rationing began in the U.S. The limit was 3 gallons a week for nonessential vehicles.
15-05-1948 Israel was attacked by Transjordan, Egypt, Syria, Iraq and Lebanon only hours after declaring its independence.
15-05-1951 AT&T became the first corporation to have one million stockholders.
15-05-1957 Britain dropped its first hydrogen bomb on Christmas Island in the Pacific Ocean.
15-05-1958 Sputnik III, the first space laboratory, was launched in the Soviet Union.
15-05-1963 The last Project Mercury space flight was launched.
15-05-1964 The Smothers Brothers, Dick and Tom, gave their first concert in Carnegie Hall in New York City.
15-05-1970 U.S. President Nixon appointed America's first two female generals.
15-05-1970 Phillip Lafayette Gibbs and James Earl Green, two black students at Jackson State University in Mississippi, were killed when police opened fire during student protests.
15-05-1972 Alabama Gov. George C. Wallace was shot by Arthur Bremer in Laurel, MD while campaigning for the U.S. presidency. Wallace was paralyzed by the shot.
15-05-1975 The merchant ship U.S. Mayaguez was recaptured from Cambodia's Khmer Rouge.
15-05-1980 The first transcontinental balloon crossing of the United States took place.
15-05-1988 Soviet forces began their withdrawal from Afghanistan. Soviet forces had been there for more than eight years.
15-05-1990 Vincent Van Gogh's "Portrait of Doctor Gachet" was sold for $82.5 million. The sale set a new world record.
15-05-1997 The Space shuttle Atlantis blasted off on a mission to deliver urgently needed repair equipment and a fresh American astronaut to Russia's orbiting Mir station.
15-05-1999 The Russian parliament was unable a attain enough votes to impeach President Boris Yeltsin.
16-05-1770 Marie Antoinette, at age 14, married the future King Louis XVI of France, who was 15.
16-05-1866 The U.S. Congress authorized the first 5-cent piece to be minted.
16-05-1868 U.S. President Andrew Johnson was acquitted during the Senate impeachment, by one vote.
16-05-1879 The Treaty of Gandamak between Russia and England set up the Afghan state.
16-05-1881 In Germany the first electric tram for the public started service.
16-05-1888 The first demonstration of recording on a flat disc was demonstrated by Emile Berliner.
16-05-1888 The capitol of Texas was dedicated in Austin.
16-05-1910 The U.S. Bureau of Mines was authorized by the U.S. Congress.
16-05-1914 The American Horseshoe Pitchers Association (AHPA) was formed in Kansas City, Kansas.
16-05-1920 Joan of Arc was canonized in Rome.
16-05-1929 The first Academy Awards were held in Hollywood.
16-05-1939 The Philadelphia Athletics and the Cleveland Indians met at Shibe Park in Philadelphia for the first baseball game to be played under the lights in the American League.
16-05-1946 "Annie Get Your Gun" opened on Broadway.
16-05-1946 Jack Mullin showed the world the first magnetic tape recorder.
16-05-1948 The body of CBS News correspondent George Polk was found in Solonika Bay in Greece. It had been a week after he'd disappeared.
16-05-1960 A Big Four summit in Paris collapsed due to the American U-2 spy plane incident.
16-05-1963 After 22 Earth orbits Gordon Cooper returned to Earth, ending Project Mercury.
16-05-1965 Spaghetti-O's went on sale.
16-05-1969 Venus 5, a Russian spacecraft, landed on the planet Venus.
16-05-1971 U.S. postage for a one-ounce first class stamp was increased from 6 to 8 cents.
16-05-1975 Japanese climber Junko Tabei became the first woman to reach the summit of Mount Everest.
16-05-1977 Five people were killed when a New York Airways helicopter, idling on top of the Pan Am Building in Manhattan, toppled over, sending a huge rotor blade flying.
16-05-1985 Michael Jordan was named Rookie of the Year in the NBA.
16-05-1987 The Bobro 400 set sail from New York Harbor with 3,200 tons of garbage. The barge travelled 6,000 miles in search of a place to dump its load. It returned to New York Harbor after 8 weeks with the same load.
16-05-1988 A report released by Surgeon General C. Everett Koop declared that nicotine was addictive in similar was as heroin and cocaine.
16-05-1988 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that police do not have to have a search warrant to search discarded garbage.
16-05-1991 Queen Elizabeth II became the first British monarch to address the U.S. Congress.
16-05-1992 The Endeavour space shuttle landed safely after its maiden voyage.
16-05-1996 Admiral Jeremy "Mike" Boorda, the nation's top Navy officer, died from a self-inflicted gunshot wound after some of his military awards were called into question.
16-05-1997 In Zaire, President Mobutu Sese Seko gave control of the country to rebel forces ending 32 years of autocratic rule.
16-05-2000 U.S. First Lady Hillary Rodham Clinton was nominated to run for U.S. Senator in New York. She was the first U.S. first lady to run for public office.
16-05-2003 Adam Rich was placed on three years probation after he plead no contest to misdemeanor charges of driving under the influence and being under the influence of a controlled substance. He was also ordered to take part ina 60-day treatment program and pay about $1,200 in fines.
16-05-2005 Sony Corp. unveiled three styles of its new PlayStation 3 video game machine.
17-05-1540 Afghan chief Sher Khan defeated Mongul Emperor Humayun at Kanauj.
17-05-1630 Italian Jesuit Niccolo Zucchi saw the belts on Jupiter's surface.
17-05-1681 Louis XIV sent an expedition to aid James II in Ireland. As a result, England declares war on France.
17-05-1756 Britain declared war on France, beginning the French and Indian War.
17-05-1792 The New York Stock Exchange was founded at 70 Wall Street by 24 brokers.
17-05-1814 Denmark ceded Norway to Sweden. Norway's constitution, which provided a limited monarchy, was signed.
17-05-1875 The first Kentucky Derby was run at Louisville, KY.
17-05-1877 The first telephone switchboard burglar alarm was installed by Edwin T. Holmes.
17-05-1881 Frederick Douglass was appointed recorder of deeds for Washington, DC.
17-05-1926 The U.S. Embassy in Buenos Aires was damaged by bombs that were believed set by sympathizers of Sacco and Vanzetti.
17-05-1932 The U.S. Congress changed the name "Porto Rico" to "Puerto Rico."
17-05-1939 The first fashion to be shown on television was broadcast in New York from the Ritz-Carleton Hotel.
17-05-1940 Germany occupied Brussels, Belgium and began the invasion of France.
17-05-1946 U.S. President Truman seized control of the nation's railroads, delaying a threatened strike by engineers and trainmen.
17-05-1948 The Soviet Union recognized the new state of Israel.
17-05-1954 The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled for school integration in Brown vs. Board of Education of Topeka. The ruling declared that racially segregated schools were inherently unequal.
17-05-1956 The first synthetic mica (synthamica) was offered for sale in Caldwell Township, NJ.
17-05-1973 The U.S. Senate Watergate Committee began its hearings.
17-05-1975 NBC TV bought the rights to show "Gone With the Wind." The one time rights cost NBC $5,000,000.
17-05-1980 Rioting erupted in Miami's Liberty City neighborhood after an all-white jury in Tampa acquitted four former Miami police officers of fatally beating black insurance executive Arthur McDuffie. Eight people were killed in the rioting.
17-05-1985 Bobby Ewing died on the season finale of "Dallas" on CBS-TV. He returned the following season.
17-05-1987 Eric ‘Sleepy’ Floyd of the Golden State Warriors set a playoff record for points in a single quarter with 29.
17-05-1987 An Iraqi warplane attacked the U.S. Navy frigate Stark in the Persian Gulf, killing 37 American sailors. Iraq and the United States called the attack a mistake.
17-05-1990 Kelsey Grammer was sentenced to 30 days in jail for DWI.
17-05-1996 U.S. President Clinton signed a measure requiring neighborhood notification when sex offenders move in. Megan's Law was named for 7-year-old Megan Kanka, who was raped and killed in 1994.
17-05-1997 Rebel leader Kabila declared himself president of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, formerly Zaire.
17-05-1997 Sylvester Stallone and Jennifer Flavin were married in London.
17-05-1998 New York Yankees pitcher David Wells became the 13th player in modern major league baseball history to throw a perfect game.
17-05-1999 Eric Ford, a tabloid photographer, was sentenced to 6 months at a halfway house, 3 years probation and 150 hours of community service. The sentence stemmed from a charge that Ford had eavesdropped on a call between Tom Cruise and Nicole Kidman and then sold a recording of the conversation.
17-05-1999 Alex Trebek received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
17-05-2000 Thomas E. Blanton Jr. and David Luker surrendered to police in Birmingham, AL. The two former Ku Klux Klan members were arrested on charges from the bombing of a church in 1963 that killed four young black girls.
17-05-2000 Austria, the U.S. and six other countries agreed on the broad outline of a plan that would compensate Nazi-Era forced labor.
17-05-2000 It was announced that Terra Networks SA and Lycos would be merging with the new name to be Terra Lycos. Terra made the deal happen with the purchase of $12.5 billion in stock.
17-05-2001 The U.S. Postal Service issued a stamp based on Charles M. Schulz's "Peanuts" comic strip.
17-05-2006 The U.S. aircraft carrier Oriskany was sunk about 24 miles off Pensacola Beach. It was the first vessel sunk under a Navy program to dispose of old warships by turning them into diving attractions. It was the largest man-made reef at the time of the sinking.
18-05-1302 The weaver Peter de Coningk led a massacre of the Flemish oligarchs.
18-05-1642 Montreal, Canada, was founded.
18-05-1643 Queen Anne, the widow of Louis XIII, was granted sole and absolute power as regent by the Paris parliament, overriding the late king's will.
18-05-1652 In Rhode Island, a law was passed that made slavery illegal in North America. It was the first law of its kind.
18-05-1792 Russian troops invaded Poland.
18-05-1798 The first Secretary of the U.S. Navy was appointed. He was Benjamin Stoddert.
18-05-1802 Great Britain declared war on Napoleon's France.
18-05-1804 Napoleon Bonaparte was proclaimed emperor by the French Senate.
18-05-1828 Battle of Las Piedras ended the conflict between Uruguay and Brazil.
18-05-1896 The U.S. Supreme court upheld the "separate but equal" policy in the Plessy vs. Ferguson decision. The ruling was overturned 58 years later with Brown vs. Board of Education.
18-05-1897 A public reading of Bram Stoker's new novel, "Dracula, or, The Un-dead," was performed in London.
18-05-1904 Brigand Raizuli kidnapped American Ion H. Perdicaris in Morocco.
18-05-1917 The U.S. Congress passed the Selective Service act, which called up soldiers to fight in World War I.
18-05-1926 Evangelist Aimee Semple McPherson vanished while visiting a beach in Venice, CA. She reappeared a month later with the claim that she had been kidnapped.
18-05-1931 Japanese pilot Seiji Yoshihara crashed his plane in the Pacific Ocean while trying to be the first to cross the ocean nonstop. He was picked up seven hours later by a passing ship.
18-05-1933 The Tennessee Valley Authority was created.
18-05-1934 The U.S. Congress approved an act, known as the "Lindberg Act," that called for the death penalty in interstate kidnapping cases.
18-05-1942 New York ended night baseball games for the duration of World War II.
18-05-1944 Monte Cassino, Europe's oldest Monastic house, was finally captured by the Allies in Italy.
18-05-1949 Antiquarian Booksellers Association of America was incorporated
18-05-1951 The United Nations moved its headquarters to New York City.
18-05-1953 The first woman to fly faster than the speed of sound, Jacqueline Cochran, piloted an F-86 Sabrejet over California at an average speed of 652.337 miles-per-hour.
18-05-1974 India became the sixth nation to explode an atomic bomb.
18-05-1980 Mt. Saint Helens erupted in Washington state. 57 people were killed and 3 billion in damage was done.
18-05-1994 Israel's three decades of occupation in the Gaza Strip ended as Israeli troops completed their withdrawal and Palestinian authorities took over.
18-05-1998 The U.S. federal government and 20 states filed a sweeping antitrust case against Microsoft Corp., saying the computer software company had a "choke hold" on competitors which denied consumer choices by controlling 90% of the software market.
18-05-1998 U.S. federal officials arrested more than 130 people and seized $35 million. This was the end to an investigation of money laundering being done by a dozen Mexican banks and two drug-smuggling cartels.
19-05-1535 French explorer Jacques Cartier set sail for North America.
19-05-1536 Anne Boleyn, the second wife of England's King Henry VIII, was beheaded after she was convicted of adultery.
19-05-1568 After being defeated by the Protestants, Mary the Queen of Scots, fled to England where she was imprisoned by Queen Elizabeth.
19-05-1588 The Spanish Armada set sail from Lisbon, bound for England.
19-05-1608 The Protestant states formed the Evangelical Union of Lutherans and Calvinists.
19-05-1643 Delegates from four New England colonies met in Boston to form a confederation.
19-05-1643 The French army defeated a Spanish army at Rocroi, France.
19-05-1796 The first U.S. game law was approved. The measure called for penalties for hunting or destroying game within Indian territory.
19-05-1847 The first English-style railroad coach was placed in service on the Fall River Line in Massachusetts.
19-05-1856 U.S. Senator Charles Sumner spoke out against slavery.
19-05-1857 The electric fire alarm system was patented by William F. Channing and Moses G. Farmer.
19-05-1858 A pro-slavery band led by Charles Hameton executed unarmed Free State men near Marais des Cygnes on the Kansas-Missouri border.
19-05-1864 The Union and Confederate armies launched thier last attacks against each other at Spotsylvania in Virginia.
19-05-1865 Confederate President Jefferson Davis was captured by the Union Cavalry in Georgia.
19-05-1906 The Federated Boys' Clubs, forerunner of the Boys' Clubs of America, were organized.
19-05-1911 The first American criminal conviction that was based on fingerprint evidence occurred in New York City.
19-05-1912 The Associated Advertising Clubs of America held its first convention in Dallas, TX.
19-05-1921 The U.S. Congress passed the Emergency Quota Act, which established national quotas for immigrants.
19-05-1926 Thomas Edison spoke on the radio for the first time.
19-05-1926 Benito Mussolini announced that democracy was deceased. Rome became a fascist state.
19-05-1926 In Damascus, Syria, French shells killed 600 people.
19-05-1928 The first frog-jumping jubilee held in Calaveras County, CA.
19-05-1935 T.E. Lawrence "Lawrence of Arabia" died from injuries in a motorcycle crash in England.
19-05-1935 The National Football League (NFL) adopted an annual college draft to begin in 1936.
19-05-1943 Winston Churchill told the U.S. Congress that his country was pledging their full support in the war against Japan.
19-05-1958 Canada and the U.S. formally established the North American Air Defense Command.
19-05-1962 Marilyn Monroe performed a sultry rendition of "Happy Birthday" for U.S. President John F. Kennedy. The event was a fund-raiser at New York's Madison Square Garden.
19-05-1964 The U.S. State Department reported that diplomats had found about 40 microphones planted in the U.S. Embassy in Moscow.
19-05-1967 The Soviet Union ratified a treaty with the United States and Britain that banned nuclear weapons from outer space.
19-05-1967 U.S. planes bombed Hanoi for the first time.
19-05-1984 The Edmonton Oilers won their first Stanley Cup. They defeated the New York Islanders in five games.
19-05-1989 The Dow Jones Industrial Average passed 2,500 for the first time. The close for the day was 2,501.1.
19-05-1992 U.S. Vice President Dan Quayle criticized the CBS sitcom "Murphy Brown" for having its title character decide to bear a child out of wedlock.
19-05-1992 In Massapequa, NY, Mary Jo Buttafuoco was shot and seriously wounded by Amy Fisher. Fisher was her husband Joey's teen-age lover.
19-05-1992 The 27th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution went into effect. The amendment prohibits Congress from giving itself midterm pay raises.
19-05-1993 The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed about 3,500 (3,500.03) for the first time.
19-05-1998 In Russia, strikes broke out over unpaid wages.
19-05-1998 Bandits stole three of Rome's most important paintings from the National Gallery of Modern Art.
19-05-1999 "Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace" was released in the U.S. It set a new record for opening day sales at 28.5 million.
19-05-1999 Rosie O'Donnell and Tom Selleck got into an uncomfortable verbal issue concerning gun control on O'Donnell's talk show.
19-05-2000 The bones of the most complete and best-preserved Tyrannosaurus rex skeleton went on display in Chicago.
19-05-2000 Disney released the movie "Dinosaur."
19-05-2003 It was announced that Worldcom Inc. would pay investors $500 million to settle civil fraud charges over its $11 billion accounting scandal.
19-05-2003 Hundreds of Albert Einstein's scientific papers, personal letters and humanist essays were make available on the Internet. Einstein had given the papers to the Hebrew Universtiy of Jerusalem in his will.
19-05-2005 "Star Wars: Episode III - Revenge of the Sith" brought in 50.0 million in its opening day.
20-05-0325 The Ecumenical council was inaugurated by Emperor Constantine in Nicea, Asia Minor.
20-05-1303 A peace treaty was signed between England and France over the town of Gascony.
20-05-1347 Cola di Rienzo took the title of tribune in Rome.
20-05-1506 In Spain, Christopher Columbus died in poverty.
20-05-1520 Hernando Cortez defeated Spanish troops that had been sent to punish him in Mexico.
20-05-1690 England passed the Act of Grace, forgiving followers of James II.
20-05-1674 John Sobieski became Poland’s first King.
20-05-1774 Britain's Parliament passed the Coercive Acts to punish the American colonists for their increasingly anti-British behavior
20-05-1775 North Carolina became the first colony to declare its independence.
20-05-1784 The Peace of Versailles ended a war between France, England, and Holland.
20-05-1830 The fountain pen was patented by H.D. Hyde.
20-05-1861 North Carolina became the elevent state to secede from the Union.
20-05-1861 During the American Civil War, the capital of the Confederacy was moved from Montgomery, AL, to Richmond, VA.
20-05-1874 Levi Strauss began marketing blue jeans with copper rivets.
20-05-1875 The International Bureau of Weights and Measures was established.
20-05-1899 Jacob German of New York City became the first driver to be arrested for speeding. The posted speed limit was 12 miles per hour.
20-05-1902 The U.S. military occupation of Cuba ended.
20-05-1902 Cuba gained its independence from Spain.
20-05-1916 Norman Rockwell’s first cover on "The Saturday Evening Post" appeared.
20-05-1926 The U.S. Congress passed the Air Commerce Act. The act gave the Department of Commerce the right to license pilots and planes.
20-05-1927 Charles Lindbergh took off from New York to cross the Atlantic for Paris aboard his airplane the "Spirit of St. Louis." The trip took 33 1/2 hours.
20-05-1930 The first airplane was catapulted from a dirigible.
20-05-1932 Amelia Earhart took off to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean. She became the first woman to achieve the feat.
20-05-1933 "Charlie Chan" was heard for the final time on the NBC Blue radio network, after only six months on the air.
20-05-1939 The first telecast over telephone wires was sent from Madison Square Garden to the NBC-TV studios at 30 Rockefeller Center in Manhattan. The event was a bicycle race.
20-05-1939 The first regular air-passenger service across the Atlantic Ocean began with the take-off of the "Yankee Clipper" from Port Washington, New York.
20-05-1941 Germany invaded Crete by air.
20-05-1942 Japan completed the conquest of Burma.
20-05-1961 A white mob attacked the Freedom Riders in Montgomery, AL. The event prompted the federal government to send U.S. marshals.
20-05-1969 U.S. and South Vietnamese forces captured Apbia Mountain, which was referred to as Hamburger Hill.
20-05-1970 100,000 people marched in New York supporting U.S. policies in Vietnam.
20-05-1978 Mavis Hutchinson, at age 53, became the first woman to run across America. It took Hutchinson 69 days to run the 3,000 miles.
20-05-1980 The submarine Nautilus was designated as a National Historic Landmark by the U.S. Secretary of the Interior.
20-05-1982 TV’s "Barney Miller" was seen for the last time on ABC-TV.
20-05-1985 The Dow Jones industrial average broke the 1300 mark for the first time. The Dow closed at 1304.88.
20-05-1990 The Hubble Space Telescope sent back its first photographs.
20-05-1993 The final episode of "Cheers" was aired on NBC-TV.
20-05-1996 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down a Colorado measure banning laws that would protect homosexuals from discrimination.
20-05-1999 At Heritage High School in Conyers, GA, a 15-year-old student shot and injured six students. He then surrendered to an assistant principal at the school.
21-05-0996 Sixteen year old Otto III was crowned the Roman Emperor.
21-05-1471 King Henry VI was killed in the tower of London. Edward IV took the throne.
21-05-1536 The Reformation was officially adopted in Geneva, Switzerland.
21-05-1542 Hernando de Soto died along the Mississippi River while searching for gold.
21-05-1602 Martha's Vineyard was first sighted by Captain Bartholomew Gosnold.
21-05-1688 The English poet Alexander Pope was born.
21-05-1790 Paris was divided into 48 zones.
21-05-1819 Bicycles were first seen in the U.S. in New York City. They were originally known as "swift walkers."
21-05-1832 In the U.S., the Democratic Party held its first national convention.
21-05-1840 New Zealand was declared a British colony.
21-05-1856 Lawrence, Kansas was captured by pro-slavery forces.
21-05-1863 The siege of the Confederate Port Hudson, LA, began.
21-05-1881 The American branch of the Red Cross was founded by Clara Barton.
21-05-1881 The United States Lawn Tennis Association was formed in New York City.
21-05-1891 Peter Jackson and Jim Corbett fought for 61 rounds only to end in a draw.
21-05-1906 Louis H. Perlman received his patent for the demountable tire-carrying rim.
21-05-1922 The cartoon, "On the Road to Moscow," by Rollin Kirby won a Pulitzer Prize. It was the first cartoon awarded the Pulitzer.
21-05-1924 Fourteen-year-old Bobby Franks was murdered in a "thrill killing" committed by Nathan Leopold Jr. and Richard Loeb. The killers were students at the University of Chicago.
21-05-1927 Charles A. Lindberg completed the first solo nonstop airplane flight across the Atlantic Ocean. The trip began May 20.
21-05-1929 The first automatic electric stock quotation board was used by Sutro and Company of New York City.
21-05-1934 Oskaloosa, IA, became the first city in the U.S. to fingerprint all of its citizens.
21-05-1941 The first U.S. ship, the SS Robin Moor, was sunk by a U-boat.
21-05-1945 Lauren Bacall and Humphrey Bogart were married.
21-05-1947 Joe DiMaggio and five of his New York Yankee teammates were fined $100 because they had not fulfilled contract requirements to do promotional duties for the team.
21-05-1956 The U.S. exploded the first airborne hydrogen bomb in the Pacific Ocean over Bikini Atoll.
21-05-1961 Governor Patterson declared martial law in Montgomery, AL.
21-05-1968 The nuclear-powered U.S. submarine Scorpion, with 99 men aboard, was last heard from. The remains of the sub were later found on the ocean floor 400 miles southwest of the Azores.
21-05-1970 The National Guard was mobilized to quell disturbances at Ohio State University.
21-05-1980 The movie "The Empire Strikes Back" was released.
21-05-1991 In Madras, India, the former prime minister, Rajiv Gandhi was killed by a bouquet of flowers that contained a bomb.
21-05-1998 An expelled student, Kipland Kinkel, in Springfield, OR, killed 2 people and wounded 25 others with a semi-automatic rifle. Police also discovered that the boy had killed his parents before the rampage.
21-05-1998 Microsoft and Sega announced that they are collaborating on a home video game system.
21-05-1998 In Miami, FL, five abortion clinics were hit by an butyric acid-attacker.
22-05-1246 Henry Raspe was elected anti-king by the Rhenish prelates in France.
22-05-1455 King Henry VI was taken prisoner by the Yorkists at the Battle of St. Albans, during the War of the Roses.
22-05-1570 Abraham Ortelius published the first modern atlas in Belgium.
22-05-1761 In Philadelphia, the first life insurance policy was issued in the U.S.
22-05-1819 The steamship Savannah became the first to cross the Atlantic Ocean.
22-05-1841 Henry Kennedy received a patent for the first reclining chair.
22-05-1849 Abraham Lincoln received a patent for the floating dry dock.
22-05-1859 The creator of "Sherlock Holmes," Sir Arthur Conan Doyle was born.
22-05-1868 Near Marshfield, IN, The "Great Train Robbery" took place. The robbery was worth $96,000 in cash, gold and bonds to the seven members of the Reno gang.
22-05-1872 The Amnesty Act restored civil rights to Southerners.
22-05-1882 The U.S. formally recognized Korea.
22-05-1891 The first public motion picture was given in Thomas Edison's lab.
22-05-1892 Dr. Sheffield, a British dentist, invented the toothpaste tube.
22-05-1900 The Associated Press was incorporated as a non-profit news cooperative in New York.
22-05-1900 A. DeVilbiss, Jr. patented his pendulum-type computing scale.
22-05-1900 Edwin S. Votey received a patent for the pianola (a pneumatic piano player). It could be attached to any piano.
22-05-1908 The Wright brothers registered their flying machine for a U.S. patent.
22-05-1939 Adolf Hitler and Benito Mussolini signed a military alliance between Germany and Italy known as the "Pact of Steel."
22-05-1947 The Truman Doctrine was enacted by the U.S. Congress to appropriate military and economic aid Turkey and Greece.
22-05-1955 A scheduled dance to be headlined by Fats Domino was canceled by police in Bridgeport, Connecticut because "rock and roll dances might be featured."
22-05-1955 Jack Benny did his last live network radio broadcast after a run of 23 years. He devoted his time fully to TV.
22-05-1967 "Mister Rogers' Neighborhood" premiered on PBS.
22-05-1967 The final "To Tell the Truth" program was seen on CBS-TV.
22-05-1969 A lunar module of Apollo 10 flew within nine miles of the moon's surface. The event was a rehearsal for the first lunar landing.
22-05-1972 U.S. President Nixon became the first U.S. president to visit Russia. He met with Soviet leader Leonid Brezhnev.
22-05-1972 The island Ceylon adopted a new constitution and became the republic of Sri Lanka.
22-05-1977 Janet Guthrie set the fastest time of the second weekend of qualifying, becoming the first woman to earn a starting spot in the Indianapolis 500 since its inception in 1911.
22-05-1985 Pete Rose passed Hank Aaron as National League run scoring leader with 2,108.
22-05-1986 Sylvester Stallone agreed to a 10-picture, six-year deal with United Artists. He signed for a reported $15 million for each film.
22-05-1990 In the Middle East, North and South Yemen merged to become a single state known as the Republic of Yemen.
22-05-1990 Microsoft released Windows 3.0.
22-05-1992 Johnny Carson hosted NBC's "Tonight Show" for the last time. He had been host for 30 years.
22-05-1997 Kelly Flinn, the U.S. Air Force's first female bomber pilot certified for combat, accepted a general discharge. She thereby avoided court-martial on charges of adultery, lying and disobeying an order.
22-05-1998 Bolivia was hit with a series of powerful earthquakes. At least 18 were killed. The quakes ranged in magnitude from 5.9 to 6.8.
22-05-1998 New information came to light about the June 1996 bombing that killed 19 American airmen. The information indicated that Saudi citizens had been responsible and not Iranians as once believed.
22-05-1998 A federal judge said that Secret Service agents could be compelled to testify before a grand jury in Monica Lewinsky investigation concerning U.S. President Clinton.
22-05-1998 Voters in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland turned out to cast ballots giving approval to a Northern Ireland peace accord.
22-05-2002 Chandra Levy's remains were found in Washington, DC's Rock Creek Park. She was last seen on April 30, 2001. California Congressman Gary Condit was questioned in the case due to his relationship with Levy.
22-05-2002 In Birmingham, AL, a jury convicted former Ku Klux Klansman Bobby Frank Cherry of murder in the 1963 church bombing that killed four girls.
22-05-2002 Barry Bonds (San Francisco Giants) hit his 583rd career home run. He tied Mark McGwire for fifth on the all-time list.
22-05-2003 At the Colonial in Fort Worth, TX, Annika Sorentam became the first woman to play on the PGA tour in 58 years. She ended the day at 1-over par.
23-05-1430 Joan of Arc was captured by Burgundians. She was then sold to the English.
23-05-1533 Henry VIII’s marriage to Catherine of Aragon was declared null and void.
23-05-1618 The Thirty Years War began when three opponents of the Reformation were thrown through a window.
23-05-1701 In London, Captain William Kidd was hanged after being convicted of murder and piracy.
23-05-1785 Benjamin Franklin wrote in a letter that he had invented bifocals.
23-05-1788 South Carolina became the eighth state to ratify U.S. Constitution.
23-05-1827 The first nursery school in the U.S. was established in New York City.
23-05-1846 Arabella Mansfield (Belle Aurelia Babb) was born. She was the first woman in the U.S. to pass the bar exam, though she never used her law degree.
23-05-1873 Canada's North West Mounted Police force was established. The organization's name was changed to Royal Canadian Mounted Police in 1920.
23-05-1876 Boston’s Joe Borden pitched the very first no-hitter in the history of the National League.
23-05-1879 The first U.S. veterinary school was established by Iowa State University.
23-05-1895 The New York Public Library was created with an agreement that combined the city's existing Astor and Lenox libraries.
23-05-1900 Civil War hero Sgt. William H. Carney became the first African American to receive the Medal of Honor, 37 years after the Battle of Fort Wagner.
23-05-1901 American forces captured Filipino rebel leader Emilio Aguinaldo.
23-05-1908 Part of the Great White Fleet arrived in Puget Sound, WA.
23-05-1915 During World War I, Italy joined the Allies as they declared war on Austria-Hungary.
23-05-1922 The play "Abie's Irish Rose" opened in New York City.
23-05-1922 "Daylight Saving Time" was debated in the first debate ever to be heard on radio in Washington, DC.
23-05-1926 The French captured the Moroccan Rif capital.
23-05-1934 In Bienville Parish, LA, Bonnie Parker and Clyde Barrow were ambushed and killed by Texas Rangers. The bank robbers were riding in a stolen Ford Deluxe.
23-05-1937 Industrialist John D. Rockefeller died.
23-05-1938 "LIFE" magazine’s cover pictured Errol Flynn as a glamour boy.
23-05-1945 In Luneburg Germany, Heinrich Himmler, the head of the Nazi Gestapo, committed suicide while imprisoned by the Allied forces.
23-05-1949 The Republic of West Germany was established.
23-05-1960 Israel announced the capture of Nazi Adolf Eichmann in Argentina.
23-05-1962 The National Basketball Association (NBA) agreed to transfer the Philadelphia Warriors to San Francisco, CA. The team became the San Francisco Warriors (and later the Golden State Warriors).
23-05-1962 Joe Pepitone of the New York Yankees set a major league baseball record by hitting two home runs in one inning.
23-05-1985 Thomas Patrick Cavanagh was sentenced to life in prison for trying to sell Stealth bomber secrets to the Soviet Union.
23-05-1992 In Lisbon, Portugal , the U.S. and four former Soviet republics signed an agreement to implement the START missile reduction treaty that had been agreed to by the Soviet Union before it was dissolved.
23-05-1994 "Pulp Fiction" won the "Golden Palm" for best film at the 47th Cannes Film Festival.
23-05-1995 The Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City was demolished.
23-05-1998 British Protestants and Irish Catholics of Northern Ireland approved a peace accord.
23-05-1999 In Kansas City, MO, Owen Hart (Blue Blazer) died when he fell 90 feet while being lowered into a WWF wrestling ring. He was 33 years old.
23-05-1999 Gerry Bloch, at age 81, became the oldest climber to scale El Capitan in Yosemite National Park. He broke his own record that he set in 1986 when he was 68 years old.
24-05-1543 Nicolaus Copernicus published proof of a sun-centered solar system.
24-05-1607 Captain Christopher Newport and 105 followers found the colony of Jamestown at the mouth of the James River on the coast of Virginia.
24-05-1610 Sir Thomas Gates institutes "laws divine moral and marshal," a harsh civil code for Jamestown.
24-05-1624 After years of unprofitable operation Virginia’s charter was revoked and it became a royal colony.
24-05-1689 The English Parliament passed Act of Toleration, protecting Protestants. Roman Catholics were specifically excluded from exemption.
24-05-1738 The Methodist Church was established.
24-05-1764 Bostonian lawyer James Otis denounced "taxation without representation" and called for the colonies to unite in demonstrating their opposition to Britain’s new tax measures.
24-05-1798 Believing that a French invasion of Ireland was imminent, Irish nationalists rose up against the British occupation.
24-05-1816 Emamual Leutze was born in Germany. He was most famous for his paintings "Washington Crossing the Delaware" and "Columbus Before the Queen".
24-05-1822 At the Battle of Pichincha, Bolivar secured independence of the Quito.
24-05-1830 The first passenger railroad service in the U.S. began service.
24-05-1844 Samuel F.B. Morse formally opened America's first telegraph line. The first message was sent from Washington, DC, to Baltimore, MD. The message was "What hath God wrought?"
24-05-1859 Charles Gounod's "Ave Maria" was performed by Madame Caroline Miolan-Carvalho for the first time in public.
24-05-1863 Bushwackers led by Captain William Marchbanks attacked a U.S. Federal militia party in Nevada, Missouri.
24-05-1878 The first American bicycle race was held in Boston.
24-05-1881 About 200 people died when the Canadian ferry Princess Victoria sank near London, Ontario.
24-05-1883 After 14 years of construction the Brooklyn Bridge was opened to traffic.
24-05-1899 The first public garage was opened by W.T. McCullough.
24-05-1913 The U.S. Department of Labor entered into its first strike mediation. The dispute was between the Railroad Clerks of the New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad.
24-05-1930 Amy Johnson became the first woman to fly from England to Australia.
24-05-1931 B&O Railroad began service with the first passenger train to have air conditioning throughout. The run was between New York City and Washington, DC.
24-05-1935 The Cincinnati Reds played the Philadelphia Phillies in the first major league baseball game at night. The switch for the floodlights was thrown by President F.D. Roosevelt.
24-05-1941 The HMS Hood was sunk by the German battleship Bismarck in the North Atlantic. Only three people survived.
24-05-1950 ‘Sweetwater’ (Nat) Clifton’s contract was purchased by the New York Knicks. Sweetwater played for the Harlem Globetrotters and became the first black player in the NBA.
24-05-1954 The first moving sidewalk in a railroad station was opened in Jersey City, NJ.
24-05-1958 United Press International was formed through a merger of the United Press and the International News Service.
24-05-1961 The Freedom Riders were arrested in Jackson, Mississippi.
24-05-1962 The officials of the National Football League ruled that halftime of regular season games would be cut to 15 minutes.
24-05-1967 California Governor Ronald Reagan greeted Charles M. Schulz at the state capitol in observance of the legislature-proclaimed "Charles Schulz Day."
24-05-1974 The last "Dean Martin Show" was seen on NBC. The show had been aired for 9 years.
24-05-1976 Britain and France opened trans-Atlantic Concorde service to Washington.
24-05-1980 The International Court of Justice issued a final decision calling for the release of the hostages taken at the U.S. embassy in Tehran on November 4, 1979.
24-05-1983 The Brooklyn Bridge's 100th birthday was celebrated.
24-05-1983 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the federal government had the right to deny tax breaks to schools that racially discriminate.
24-05-1986 Montreal won its 23rd National Hockey League (NHL) Stanley Cup championship.
24-05-1990 The Edmonton Oilers won their fifth National Hockey League (NHL) Stanley Cup.
24-05-1993 Roman Catholic Cardinal Juan Jesus Posada Ocampo and six other people were killed at the Guadalajara, Mexico, airport in a shootout that involved drug gangs.
24-05-1993 The Ethiopian province of Eritrea declared itself an independent nation.
24-05-1994 The four men convicted of bombing the New York's World Trade Center were each sentenced to 240 years in prison.
24-05-1999 39 miners were killed in an underground gas explosion in the Ukraine.
24-05-2000 Five people were killed and two others wounded when two gunmen entered a Wendy's restaurant in Flushing, Queens, New York. The gunmen tied up the victims in the basement and then shot them.
24-05-2000 The U.S. House of Representatives approved permanent normal trade relations with China. China was not happy about some of the human rights conditions that had been attached by the U.S. lawmakers.
24-05-2000 A Democratic Party event for Al Gore in Washington brought in $26.5 million. The amount set a new record, which had just been set the previous month by Republicans for Texas Gov. George W. Bush.
24-05-2001 Temba Tsheri, 15, became the youngest person to reach the summit of Mount Everest.
25-05-1085 Alfonso VI took Toledo, Spain from the Moslems.
25-05-1787 The Constitutional convention opened in Philadelphia with George Washington presiding.
25-05-1810 Argentina declared independence from Napoleonic Spain.
25-05-1844 The gasoline engine was patented by Stuart Perry.
25-05-1844 The first telegraphed news dispatch, sent from Washington, DC, to Baltimore, MD, appeared in the Baltimore "Patriot."
25-05-1895 Oscar Wilde, a playwright, poet and novelist, was convicted of a morals charge and sentenced to prison in London.
25-05-1895 James P. Lee first published "Gold in America -- A Practical Manual."
25-05-1911 President of Mexico, Porfolio Diaz, resigned his office.
25-05-1925 John Scopes was indicted for teaching the Darwinian theory in school.
25-05-1927 Ford Motor Company announced that the Model A would replace the Model T.
25-05-1927 The "Movietone News" was shown for the first time at the Sam Harris Theatre in New York City.
25-05-1935 Babe Ruth hit his final homerun, his 714th, and set a record that would stand for 39 years.
25-05-1935 Jesse Owens tied the world record for the 100-yard dash. He ran it in 9.4 seconds. He also broke three other world track records.
25-05-1946 Jordan gained independence from Britain.
25-05-1953 In Nevada, the first atomic cannon was fired.
25-05-1961 America was asked by U.S. President Kennedy to work toward putting a man on the moon before the end of the decade.
25-05-1963 The Organization of African Unity was founded, in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia.
25-05-1968 The Gateway Arch, part of the Jefferson National Expansion Memorial in St. Louis, MO, was dedicated.
25-05-1970 Boeing Computer Services was founded.
25-05-1977 "Star Wars Episode IV: A New Hope" opened and became the largest grossing film to date.
25-05-1977 An opinion piece by Vietnam verteran Jan Scruggs appeared in "The Washington Post." The article called for a national memorial to "remind an ungrateful nation of what it has done to its sons" that had served in the Vietnam War.
25-05-1979 An American Airlines DC-10 crashed during takeoff at Chicago's O'Hare International Airport. 275 people were killed.
25-05-1981 Daredevil Daniel Goodwin scaled Chicago's Sears Tower, while wearing a "Spiderman" costume, in 7 1/2 hours.
25-05-1983 "The Return of the Jedi" opened nationwide. It set a new record in opening day box office sales. The gross was $6,219,629.
25-05-1985 Bangladesh was hit with a hurricane and tidal wave that killed more than 11,000 people.
25-05-1986 Approximately 7 million Americans participated in "Hands Across America."
25-05-1989 The Calgary Flames won their first NHL Stanley Cup by defeating the Montreal Canadiens.
25-05-1992 Jay Leno debuted as the new permanent host of NBC's "Tonight Show."
25-05-1996 In Nimes, France, Christina Sanchez became the first woman to achieve the rank of matadore in Europe.
25-05-1997 In Sierra Leone a military coup overthrew the popularly elected President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah. He was replaced with Major Johnny Paul Koromah.
25-05-1997 U.S. Senator Strom Thurmond became the longest-serving senator in U.S. history (41 years and 10 months).
25-05-1997 Poland adopted a constitution that removed all traces of communism.
25-05-1999 A report by the U.S. House of Representatives Select Committee on U.S. National Security and Military/Commercial Concerns with the People's Republic of China concluded that China had "stolen design information on the U.S. most-advanced thermonuclear weapons" and that China's penetration of U.S. weapons laboratories "spans at least the past several decades and almost certainly continues today."
25-05-2000 The Walt Disney Co. and Time Warner Inc. signed a long-term deal that ended a dispute over the airing policies of Time Warner. Time Warner had blacked out Disney programs for a 39 hour period the previous month due to the lack of an agreement.
25-05-2001 Erik Weihenmeyer, 32, of Golder, CO, became the first blind climber to reach the summit of Mount Everest.
25-05-2001 Sherman Bull, 64, of New Canaan, CT, became the oldest climber to reach the summit of Mount Everest.
26-05-2017 Germanicus of Rome celebrated his victory over the Germans.
26-05-1328 William of Ockham was forced to flee from Avignon by Pope John XXII.
26-05-1521 Martin Luther was banned by the Edict of Worms because of his religious beliefs and writings.
26-05-1647 A new law banned Catholic priests from the colony of Massachusetts. The penalty was banishment or death for a second offense.
26-05-1660 King Charles II of England landed at Dover after being exiled for nine years.
26-05-1670 A treaty was signed in secret in Dover, England, between Charles II and Louis XIV ending the hostilities between them.
26-05-1691 Jacob Leiser, leader of the popular uprising in support of William and Mary’s accession to the English throne, was executed for treason.
26-05-1736 The British and Chickasaw Indians defeated the French at the Battle of Ackia.
26-05-1791 The French Assembly forced King Louis XVI to hand over the crown and state assets.
26-05-1805 Napoleon Bonaparte was crowned King of Italy in Milan Cathedral.
26-05-1831 Russians defeated the Poles at battle of Ostrolenska.
26-05-1835 A resolution was passed in the U.S. Congress stating that Congress has no authority over state slavery laws.
26-05-1836 The U.S. House of Representatives adopted what has been called the Gag Rule.
26-05-1864 The Territory of Montana was organized.
26-05-1865 Arrangements were made in New Orleans for the surrender of Confederate forces west of the Mississippi.
26-05-1868 U.S. President Andrew Johnson was acquitted, by one vote, of all charges in his impeachment trial.
26-05-1896 The Dow Jones Industrial Average appeared for the first time in the "Wall Street Journal."
26-05-1896 The last czar of Russia, Nicholas II, was crowned.
26-05-1908 In Persia, the first oil strike was made in the Middle East.
26-05-1913 Actors’ Equity Association was organized in New York City.
26-05-1926 In Morocco, rebel leader Abd el Krim surrendered.
26-05-1938 The House Committee on Un-American Activities began its work of searching for subversives in the United States.
26-05-1940 The evacuation of Allied troops from Dunkirk, France, began during World War II.
26-05-1946 A patent was filed in the United States for an H-bomb.
26-05-1946 British Prime Minister Winston Churchill signed a military pact with Russian leader Joseph Stalin. Stalin promised a "close collaboration after the war."
26-05-1948 The U.S. Congress passed Public Law 557 which permanently established the Civil Air Patrol as the Auxiliary of the new U.S. Air Force.
26-05-1956 The first trailer bank opened for business in Locust Grove, Long Island, NY. The 46-foot-long trailer took in $100,000 in deposits its first day.
26-05-1958 Union Square, San Francisco became a state historical landmark.
26-05-1959 The word "Frisbee" became a registered trademark of Wham-O.
26-05-1961 Civil rights activist group Freedom Ride Coordinating Committee was established in Atlanta, GA.
26-05-1961 A U.S. Air Force bomber flew across the Atlantic in a record time of just over three hours.
26-05-1969 The Apollo 10 astronauts returned to Earth after a successful eight-day dress rehearsal for the first manned moon landing.
26-05-1972 The Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty (SALT I) was signed by the U.S. and USSR. The short-term agreement put a freeze on the testing and deployment of intercontinental and submarine-launched ballistic missiles for a 5-year period.
26-05-1973 Kathy Schmidt set an American women’s javelin record with a throw of 207 feet, 10 inches.
26-05-1975 American stuntman Evel Knievel suffered severe spinal injuries in Britain when he crashed while attempting to jump 13 buses in his car.
26-05-1977 George H. Willig was arrested after he scaled the South Tower of New York's World Trade Center. It took him 3 1/2 hours.
26-05-1978 The first legal casino in the Eastern U.S. opened in Atlantic City, NJ.
26-05-1988 The Edmonton Oilers won their fourth NHL Stanley Cup in five seasons. They swept the series 4 games to 0 against the Boston Bruins.
26-05-1991 A Lauda Air Boeing 767 crashed in Thailand, killing all 223 people aboard.
26-05-1994 U.S. President Clinton renewed trade privileges for China, and announced that his administration would no longer link China's trade status with its human rights record.
26-05-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that Ellis Island was mainly in New Jersey, not New York.
26-05-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that police officers in high-speed chases are liable for bystander injuries only if their "actions shock the conscience."
26-05-1998 The Grand Princess cruise ship made its inaugural cruise. The ship measured 109,000 tons and cost approximately $450 million, making it the largest and most expensive cruise ship ever built.
27-05-1647 Achsah Young, a resident of Windsor, CT, was executed for being a "witch." It was the first recorded American execution of a "witch."
27-05-1668 Three colonists were expelled from Massachusetts for being Baptists.
27-05-1813 Americans captured Fort George, Canada.
27-05-1896 255 people were killed in St. Louis, MO, when a tornado struck.
27-05-1901 The Edison Storage Battery Company was organized.
27-05-1907 The Bubonic Plague broke out in San Francisco.
27-05-1919 A U.S. Navy seaplane completed the first transatlantic flight.
27-05-1926 Bronze figures of Huck Finn and Tom Sawyer were erected in Hannibal, MO.
27-05-1929 Colonel Charles Lindbergh and Anne Spencer Murrow were married.
27-05-1931 Piccard and Knipfer made the first flight into the stratosphere, by balloon.
27-05-1933 Walt Disney's "Three Little Pigs" was first released.
27-05-1933 In the U.S., the Federal Securities Act was signed. The act required the registration of securities with the Federal Trade Commission.
27-05-1935 The U.S. Supreme Court declared that President Franklin Roosevelt's National Industrial Recovery Act was unconstitutional.
27-05-1937 In California, the Golden Gate Bridge was opened to pedestrian traffic. The bridge connected San Francisco and Marin County.
27-05-1941 U.S. President Roosevelt proclaimed an "unlimited national emergency" amid rising world tensions.
27-05-1941 The German battleship Bismarck was sunk by British naval and air forces. 2,300 people were killed.
27-05-1942 German General Erwin Rommel began a major offensive in Libya with his Afrika Korps.
27-05-1944 U.S. General MacArthur landed on Biak Island in New Guinea.
27-05-1960 A military coup overthrew the democratic government of Turkey.
27-05-1964 Indian Prime Minister Jawaharla Nehru died.
27-05-1968 After 48 years as coach of the Chicago Bears, George Halas retired.
27-05-1969 Construction of Walt Disney World began in Florida.
27-05-1977 George H. Willig was fined for scaling the World Trade Center in New York on May 26. He was fined $1.10.
27-05-1985 In Beijing, representatives of Britain and China exchanged instruments of ratification on the pact returning Hong Kong to the Chinese in 1997.
27-05-1986 Mel Fisher recovered a jar that contained 2,300 emeralds from the Spanish ship Atocha. The ship sank in the 17th century.
27-05-1994 Nobel Prize-winning author Alexander Solzhenitsyn returned to Russia. He had been in exile for two decades.
27-05-1995 In Charlottesville, VA, Christopher Reeve was paralyzed after being thrown from his horse during a jumping event.
27-05-1996 Russian President Boris Yeltsin negotiated a cease-fire to the war in Chechnya in his first meeting with the leader of the rebels.
27-05-1997 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the sexual harassment suit filed by Paula Jones could continue while President Clinton was in office.
27-05-1998 Charlie Sheen was admitted to a hospital in Los Angeles for a drug overdose.
27-05-1998 Michael Fortier was sentenced to 12 years in prison for not warning anyone about the plot to bomb an Oklahoma City federal building.
27-05-1999 In The Hague, Netherlands, a war crimes tribunal indicted Slobodan Milosevic and four others for atrocities in Kosovo. It was the first time that a sitting head of state had been charged with such a crime.
28-05-1533 England's Archbishop declared the marriage of King Henry VIII to Anne Boleyn valid.
28-05-1774 The First Continental Congress convened in Virginia.
28-05-1805 Napoleon was crowned in Milan, Italy.
28-05-1830 The U.S. Congress authorized Indian removal from all states to western prairie.
28-05-1863 The first black regiment left Boston to fight in the U.S. Civil War.
28-05-1892 The Sierra club was organized in San Francisco, CA.
28-05-1900 Britain annexed the Orange Free State.
28-05-1908 Ian Fleming, who created the character James Bond, was born.
28-05-1918 Azerbaijan, in Russian Caucasus, declared independence.
28-05-1926 Portuguese General da Costa took over in a coup.
28-05-1928 Chrysler Corporation merged with Dodge Brothers, Inc.
28-05-1929 Warner Brothers debuted "On With The Show" in New York City. It was the first all-color-talking picture.
28-05-1934 The Dionne quintuplets were born near Callender, Ontario, to Olivia and Elzire Dionne. The babies were the first quintuplets to survive infancy.
28-05-1937 U.S. President Roosevelt pushed a button in Washington, DC, signaling that vehicular traffic could cross the newly opened Golden Gate Bridge in California.
28-05-1940 During World War II, Belgium surrendered to Germany.
28-05-1953 The Walt Disney film "Melody" premiered in the Paramount Theatre in Hollywood. The picture was the first 3-D cartoon.
28-05-1957 National League club owners voted to allow the Brooklyn Dodgers to move to Los Angeles and that the New York Giants could move to San Francisco.
28-05-1961 Amnesty International, a human rights organization, was founded.
28-05-1976 The Peaceful Nuclear Explosion Treaty was signed, limiting any nuclear explosion - regardless of its purpose - to a yield of 150 kilotons.
28-05-1977 Fire raced through the Beverly Hills Supper Club in Southgate, KY. 165 people were killed.
28-05-1985 The first issue of "Vanity Fair" magazine went on sale. The issue had a picture of U.S. President Ronald Reagan and First Lady Nancy smooching on the cover.
28-05-1985 David Jacobsen, director of the American University Hospital in Beirut, Lebanon, was abducted by pro-Iranian kidnappers. He was freed 17 months later.
28-05-1987 Mathias Rust, a 19-year-old West German pilot, landed a private plane in Moscow's Red Square after evading Soviet air defenses. He was released August 3, 1988.
28-05-1995 An earthquake in the Russian town Neftegorsk killed at least 2000 people. It had a magnitude of 7.5.
28-05-1996 U.S. President Clinton's former business partners in the Whitewater land deal were convicted of fraud.
28-05-1998 Pakistan matched India with five nuclear test blasts. The U.S., Japan and other nations imposed economic sanctions. Pakistani Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif said "Today, we have settled the score with India."
28-05-1998 Dr. Susan Terebey discoved a planet outside of our solar system with the use of photos taken by the Hubble Space Telescope.
28-05-1998 Phil Hartman was shot to death at his home by his wife, Brynn, who then killed herself.
28-05-1999 In Milan, Italy, Leonardo de Vinci's "The Last Supper" was put back on display after 22 years of restoration work.
28-05-2002 Russia became a limited partner in NATO with the creation of the NATO-Russia Council.
29-05-1453 Constantinople fell to Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II, ending the Byzantine Empire.
29-05-1660 Charles II was restored to the English throne after the Puritan Commonwealth.
29-05-1721 South Carolina was formally incorporated as a royal colony.
29-05-1765 Patrick Henry denounced the Stamp Act before Virginia's House of Burgesses.
29-05-1790 Rhode Island became the last of the original thirteen colonies to ratify the U.S. Constitution.
29-05-1827 The first nautical school opened in Nantucket, MA, under the name Admiral Sir Isaac Coffin’s Lancasterian School.
29-05-1848 Wisconsin became the 30th state to join the United States.
29-05-1849 A patent for lifting vessels was granted to Abraham Lincoln.
29-05-1910 An airplane raced a train from Albany, NY, to New York City. The airplane pilot Glenn Curtiss won the $10,000 prize.
29-05-1911 The first running of the Indianapolis 500 took place.
29-05-1912 Fifteen women were dismissed from their jobs at the Curtis Publishing Company in Philadelphia, PA, for dancing the Turkey Trot while on the job.
29-05-1916 The official flag of the president of the United States was adopted.
29-05-1916 U.S. forces invaded Dominican Republic and remained until 1924.
29-05-1922 Ecuador became independent.
29-05-1922 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that organized baseball was a sport, not subject to antitrust laws.
29-05-1932 World War I veterans began arriving in Washington, DC. to demand cash bonuses they were not scheduled to receive for another 13 years.
29-05-1951 C.F. Blair became the first man to fly over the North Pole in single engine plane.
29-05-1953 Edmund Hillary and Sherpa Tenzing Norgay became first men to reach the top of Mount Everest.
29-05-1962 Buck (John) O’Neil became the first black coach in major league baseball when he accepted the job with the Chicago Cubs.
29-05-1965 Ralph Boston set a world record in the broad jump at 27-feet, 4-3/4 inches, at a meet held in Modesto, CA.
