29-05-1973 Tom Bradley was elected the first black mayor of Los Angeles.
29-05-1974 U.S. President Nixon agreed to turn over 1,200 pages of edited Watergate transcripts.
29-05-1978 In the U.S., postage stamps were raised from 13 cents to 15 cents.
29-05-1985 39 people were killed and 400 were injured in a riot at a European Cup soccer match in Brussels, Belgium.
29-05-1988 U.S. President Reagan began his first visit to the Soviet Union in Moscow.
29-05-1988 NBC aired "To Heal A Nation," the story of Jan Scruggs' effort to build the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
29-05-1990 Boris Yeltsin was elected president of the Russian republic by the Russian parliament.
29-05-1995 The last 3 bodies were recovered from the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City.
29-05-1997 The ruling party in Indonesia, Golkar, won the Parliament election by a record margin. There was a boycott movement and rioting that killed 200 people.
29-05-1999 Space shuttle Discovery completed the first docking with the International Space Station.
29-05-2000 Fiji's military took control of the nation and declared martial law following a coup attempt by indigenous Fijians in mid-May.
29-05-2001 In New York, four followers of Osama bin Laden were convicted of a global conspiracy to murder Americans. The crimes included the 1998 bombings of two U.S. embassies in Africa that killed 224 people.
29-05-2001 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that disabled golfer Casey Martin could use a cart to ride in tournaments.
30-05-1416 Jerome of Prague was burned as a heretic by the Church.
30-05-1431 Joan of Arc was burned at the stake in Rouen, France, at the age of 19.
30-05-1527 The University of Marburg was founded in Germany.
30-05-1539 Hernando de Soto, the Spanish explorer, landed in Florida with 600 soldiers to search for gold.
30-05-1783 The first daily newspaper was published in the U.S. by Benjamin Towner called "The Pennsylvania Evening Post"
30-05-1814 The First Treaty of Paris was declared, which returned France to its 1792 borders.
30-05-1848 W.G. Young patented the ice cream freezer.
30-05-1854 The U.S. territories of Nebraska and Kansas were established.
30-05-1868 Memorial Day was observed for the first time in the U.S.
30-05-1879 William Vanderbilt renamed New York City's Gilmore’s Garden to Madison Square Garden.
30-05-1883 Twelve people were trampled to death in New York City in a stampede when a rumor that the Brooklyn Bridge was in danger of collapsing occurred.
30-05-1889 The brassiere was invented.
30-05-1896 The first automobile accident occurred in New York City.
30-05-1903 In Riverdale, NY, the first American motorcycle hill climb was held.
30-05-1911 Ray Harroun won the first Indianapolis Sweepstakes. The 500-mile auto race later became known as the Indianapolis 500. Harroun's average speed was 74.59 miles per hour.
30-05-1912 The U.S. Marines were sent to Nicaragua to protect American interests.
30-05-1913 The First Balkan War ended.
30-05-1921 The U.S. Navy transferred the Teapot Dome oil reserves to the Department of the Interior.
30-05-1922 The Lincoln Memorial was dedicated in Washington, DC.
30-05-1933 Sally Rand introduced her exotic and erotic fan dance to audiences at Chicago’s Century of Progress Exposition.
30-05-1943 American forces secured the Aleutian island of Attu from the Japanese during World War II.
30-05-1958 Unidentified soldiers killed in World War II and the Korean conflicts were buried at Arlington National Cemetery.
30-05-1967 Daredevil Evel Knievel jumped 16 automobiles in a row in a motorcycle stunt at Ascot Speedway in Gardena, CA.
30-05-1967 The state of Biafra seceded from Nigeria and Civil war erupted.
30-05-1971 Mariner 9, the American deep space probe blasted off on a journey to Mars.
30-05-1982 Spain became the 16th NATO member. Spain was the first country to enter the Western alliance since West Germany in 1955.
30-05-1989 The "Goddess of Democracy" statue (33 feet height) was erected in Tiananmen Square by student demonstrators.
30-05-1996 Britain's Prince Andrew and the former Sarah Ferguson were granted an uncontested decree ending their 10-year marriage.
30-05-1997 Jesse K. Timmendequas was convicted in Trenton, NJ, of raping and strangling a 7-year-old neighbor, Megan Kanka. The 1994 murder inspired "Megan's Law," requiring that communities be notified when sex offenders move in.
30-05-1998 A powerful earthquake hit northern Afghanistan killing up to 5,000.
30-05-2003 Peter Jenning was sworn in as a U.S. citizen.
31-05-1433 Sigismund was crowned emperor of Rome.
31-05-1854 The Kansas-Nebraska Act passed by the U.S. Congress.
31-05-1859 The Philadelphia Athletics were formally organized to play the game of Town Ball.
31-05-1870 E.J. DeSemdt patented asphalt.
31-05-1879 New York's Madison Square Garden opened.
31-05-1880 The first U.S. national bicycle society was formed in Newport, RI. It was known as the League of American Wheelman.
31-05-1884 Dr. John Harvey Kellogg patented "flaked cereal."
31-05-1889 In Johnstown, PA, more than 2,200 people died after the South Fork Dam collapsed.
31-05-1900 U.S. troops arrived in Peking to help put down the Boxer Rebellion.
31-05-1902 The Boer War ended between the Boers of South Africa and Great Britain with the Treaty of Vereeniging.
31-05-1907 The first taxis arrived in New York City. They were the first in the United States.
31-05-1909 The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) held its first conference.
31-05-1910 The Union of South Africa was founded.
31-05-1913 The 17th Amendment went into effect. It provided for popular election of U.S. senators.
31-05-1915 A German zeppelin made an air raid on London.
31-05-1926 Frank Lockhart won the 14th Indy 500. He averaged 95.9 mph.
31-05-1927 Ford Motor Company produced the last "Tin Lizzie" in order to begin production of the Model A.
31-05-1929 In Beverly, MA, the first U.S. born reindeer were born.
31-05-1941 The first issue of the still popular "Parade: The Weekly Picture Newspaper" went on sale.
31-05-1943 "Archie" was aired on the Mutual Broadcasting System for the first time.
31-05-1947 Communists seized control of Hungary.
31-05-1955 The U.S. Supreme Court ordered that all states must end racial segregation "with all deliberate speed."
31-05-1961 South Africa became an independent republic.
31-05-1962 Adolf Eichmann was hanged in Israel. Eichmann was a Gestapo official and was executed for his actions in the Nazi Holocaust.
31-05-1970 An earthquake in Peru killed tens of thousands of people.
31-05-1974 Israel and Syria signed an agreement on the Golan Heights.
31-05-1977 The trans-Alaska oil pipeline was finished after 3 years of construction.
31-05-1979 Zimbabwe proclaimed its independence.
31-05-1988 U.S. President Ronald Reagan arrived in Moscow in an effort to relieve Cold War tensions. He was the first president to do so in 14 years.
31-05-1994 The U.S. announced it was no longer aiming long-range nuclear missiles at targets in the former Soviet Union.
31-05-1995 Bob Dole singled out Time Warner for "the marketing of evil" in movies and music. Dole later admitted that he had not seen or heard much of what he had been criticizing.
31-05-2003 In North Carolina, Eric Robert Rudolph was captured. He had been on the FBI's 10 Most Wanted list for five years for several bombings including the 1996 Olympic bombing.
01-11-1512 Michelangelo's paintings on the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel were first exhibited to the public.
01-11-1604 "Othello," the tragedy by William Shakespeare, was first presented at Whitehall Palace in London.
01-11-1611 "The Tempest," Shakespeare's romantic comedy, was first presented at Whitehall Palace in London.
01-11-1755 At least 60,000 people were killed in Lisbon, Portugal by an earthquake, its aftershocks and the ensuing tsunami.
01-11-1765 The British Parliament enacted The Stamp Act in the American colonies. The act was repealed in March of 1766 on the same day that the Parliament passed the Declaratory Acts which asserted that the British government had free and total legislative power of the colonies.
01-11-1800 U.S. President John Adams became the first president to live in the White House when he moved in.
01-11-1848 The first medical school for women, founded by Samuel Gregory, opened in Boston, MA. The Boston Female Medical School later merged with Boston University School of Medicine.
01-11-1856 The first photography magazine, Daguerreian Journal, was published in New York City, NY.
01-11-1861 Gen. George B. McClellan was made the general-in-chief of the American Union armies.
01-11-1864 The U.S. Post Office started selling money orders. The money orders provided a safe way to payments by mail.
01-11-1870 The U.S. Weather Bureau made its first meteorological observations using 24 locations that provided reports via telegraph.
01-11-1879 Thomas Edison executed his first patent application for a high-resistance carbon filament (U.S. Pat. 223,898).
01-11-1894 "Billboard Advertising" was published for the first time. It later became known as "Billboard."
01-11-1894 Russian Emperor Alexander III died.
01-11-1904 The Army War College in Washington, DC, enrolled the first class.
01-11-1911 Italy used planes to drop bombs on the Tanguira oasis in Libya. It was the first aerial bombing.
01-11-1936 Benito Mussolini made a speech in Milan, Italy, in which he described the alliance between Italy and Nazi Germany as an "axis" running between Berlin and Rome.
01-11-1937 "Hilltop House" was aired for the first time on CBS Radio.
01-11-1937 "Terry and the Pirates" debuted on NBC Radio.
01-11-1940 "A Night in the Tropics" was released. It was the first movie for Abbott and Costello.
01-11-1944 "Harvey," by Mary Chase, opened on Broadway.
01-11-1947 The famous racehorse Man o' War died.
01-11-1949 In Washington, 55 people were killed when a fighter plane hit an airliner.
01-11-1950 Two Puerto Rican nationalists tried to assassinate U.S. President Harry Truman. One of the men was killed when they tried to force their way into Blair House in Washington, DC.
01-11-1950 Charles Cooper became the first black man to play in the National Basketball Association (NBA).
01-11-1952 The United States exploded the first hydrogen bomb on Eniwetok Atoll in the Marshall Islands.
01-11-1954 Algeria began to rebel against French rule.
01-11-1959 Jacques Plante, of the Montreal Canadiens, became the first goalie in the NHL to wear a mask.
01-11-1962 "The Lucy Show" premiered.
01-11-1963 The USSR launched Polyot I. It was the first satellite capable of maneuvering in all directions and able to change its orbit.
01-11-1968 The movie rating system of G, M, R, X, followed by PG-13 and NC-17 went into effect.
01-11-1973 Leon Jaworski was appointed the new Watergate special prosecutor in the Watergate case.
01-11-1979 Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini urged all Iranians to demonstrate on November 4 and to expand their attacks against the U.S. and Israel. On November 4, Iranian militants seized the U.S. embassy in Tehran and took 63 Americans hostage.
01-11-1985 In the village of Ignacio Aldama, 22 members of a Mexican anti-narcotics squad were killed by alleged drug traffickers.
01-11-1987 Deng Xiaoping retired from China's Communist Party's Central Committee.
01-11-1989 Tens of thousands of refugees to fled to the West when East Germany reopened its border with Czechoslovakia.
01-11-1989 Nicaraguan President Daniel Ortega announced the end of a cease-fire with the Contra rebels.
01-11-1993 The European Community's treaty on European unity took effect.
01-11-1995 In Dayton, OH, the Bosnian peace talks opened with the leaders of Bosnia, Serbia and Croatia present.
01-11-1998 Nicaraguan Vice President Enrique Bolanos announced that between 1,000 and 1,500 people were buried in a 32-square mile area below the slopes of the Casita volcano in northern Nicaragua by a mudslide caused by Hurricane Mitch.
01-11-1998 Iridium inaugurated the first handheld, global satellite phone and paging system.
02-11-1721 Peter the Great (Peter I), ruler of Russia, changed his title to emperor.
02-11-1776 During the American Revolutionary War, William Demont, became the first traitor of the American Revolution when he deserted.
02-11-1783 U.S. Gen. George Washington gave his "Farewell Address to the Army" near Princeton, NJ.
02-11-1867 "Harpers Bazaar" magazine was founded.
02-11-1883 Thomas Edison executed a patent application for an electrical indicator using the Edison effect lamp (U.S. Pat. 307,031).
02-11-1889 North Dakota and South Dakota were admitted into the union as the 39th and 40th states.
02-11-1895 In Chicago, IL, the first gasoline powered contest took place in America.
02-11-1917 British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour expressed support for a "national home" for the Jews of Palestine.
02-11-1920 The first commercial radio station in the U.S., KDKA of Pittsburgh, PA, began regular broadcasting.
02-11-1921 Margaret Sander's National Birth Control League combined with Mary Ware Denetts Voluntary Parenthood League to form the American Birth Control League.
02-11-1930 Haile Selassie was crowned emperor of Ethiopia.
02-11-1930 The DuPont Company announced the first synthetic rubber. It was named DuPrene.
02-11-1937 The play "I'd Rather be Right" opened in New York City.
02-11-1947 Howard Hughes flew his "Spruce Goose," a huge wooden airplane, for eight minutes in California. It was the plane's first and only flight. The "Spruce Goose," nicknamed because of the white-gray color of the spruce used to build it, never when into production.
02-11-1948 Harry S. Truman defeated Thomas E. Dewey for the U.S. presidency. The Chicago Tribune published an early edition that had the headline "DEWEY DEFEATS TRUMAN." The Truman victory surprised many polls and newspapers.
02-11-1959 Charles Van Doren, a game show contestant on the NBC-TV program "Twenty-One" admitted that he had been given questions and answers in advance.
02-11-1960 In London, the novel "Lady Chatterly's Lover," was found not guilty of obscenity.
02-11-1962 U.S. President Kennedy announced that the U.S.S.R. was dismantling the missile sites in Cuba.
02-11-1963 South Vietnamese President Ngo Dihn Diem was assassinated in a military coup.
02-11-1966 The Cuban Adjustment Act allows 123,000 Cubans to apply for permanent residence in the U.S.
02-11-1979 Joanna Chesimard, a black militant escaped from a New Jersey prison, where she'd been serving a life sentence for the 1973 murder of a New Jersey state trooper.
02-11-1983 U.S. President Ronald Reagan signed a bill establishing a federal holiday on the third Monday of January in honor of civil rights leader Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
02-11-1984 Velma Barfield became the first woman to be executed in the U.S. since 1962. She had been convicted of the poisoning death of her boyfriend.
02-11-1985 The South African government imposed severe restrictions on television, radio and newspaper coverage of unrest by both local and foreign journalists.
02-11-1986 The 12-by-16-inch celluloid of a poison apple from Walt Disney's "Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs"" was purchased for $30,800.
02-11-1986 American hostage David Jacobson was released after being held in Lebanon for 17 months by Shiite Muslims kidnappers.
02-11-1989 Carmen Fasanella retired after 68 years and 243 days of taxicab service in Princeton, NJ.
02-11-1992 Magic Johnson retired from the NBA again, this time for good because of fear due to his HIV infection.
02-11-1993 The U.S. Senate called for full disclosure of Senator Bob Packwood's diaries in a sexual harassment probe.
02-11-1993 Christie Todd Whitman was elected the first woman governor of New Jersey.
02-11-1995 The play "Sacrilege" opened.
02-11-1995 The U.S. expelled Daiwa Bank Ltd. for allegedly covering up $1.1 billion in trading losses.
02-11-1998 U.S. President Clinton gave his first in-depth interview since the White House sex scandal to Black Entertainment Television talk show host and political commentator Tavis Smiley on the network's "BET Tonight with Tavis Smiley."
02-11-2001 The computer-animated movie "Monsters, Inc." opened. The film recorded the best debut ever for an animated film and the 6th best of all time.
02-11-2003 In the U.S., the Episcopal Church diocese consecrated the church's first openly gay bishop.
03-11-1507 Leonardo DaVinci was commissioned by the husband of Lisa Gherardini to paint her. The work is known as the Mona Lisa.
03-11-1631 The Reverend John Eliot arrived in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. He was the first Protestant minister to dedicate himself to the conversion of Native Americans to Christianity.
03-11-1793 Stephen F. Austin was born. He was the principle founder of Texas.
03-11-1796 John Adams was elected the 2nd U.S. President.
03-11-1839 The first Opium War between China and Britain erupted.
03-11-1892 The first automatic telephone went into service at LaPorte, IN. The device was invented by Almon Strowger.
03-11-1900 The first automobile show in the United States opened at New York's Madison Square Garden.
03-11-1903 Panama proclaimed its independence from Columbia.
03-11-1934 The first race track in California opened under a new pari-mutuel betting law.
03-11-1941 Japanese Ambassador John Grew warned that the Japanese may be planning a sudden attack on the U.S.
03-11-1952 Frozen bread was offered for sale for the first time in a supermarket in Chester, NY.
03-11-1953 The Rules Committee of organized baseball restored the sacrifice fly. The rule had not been used since 1939.
03-11-1957 Sputnik II was launched by the Soviet Union. It was the second manmade satellite to be put into orbit and was the first to put an animal into space, a dog named Laika.
03-11-1973 The U.S. launched the Mariner 10 spacecraft. On March 29, 1974 it became the first spacecraft to reach the planet Mercury.
03-11-1975 "Good Morning America" premiered on ABC-TV.
03-11-1979 Five members of the Communist Workers' Party are shot to death in broad daylight at an anti-Ku Klux Klan rally in Greensboro, NC. Eight others were wounded.
03-11-1986 The Ash-Shiraa, pro-Syrian Lebanese magazine, first broke the story of U.S. arms sales to Iran to secure the release of seven American hostages. The story turned into the Iran-Contra affair.
03-11-1987 China told the U.S. that it would halt the sale of arms to Iran.
03-11-1991 Israeli and Palestinian representatives held their first-ever face-to-face talks in Madrid, Spain.
03-11-1992 Carol Moseley-Braun became the first African-American woman U.S. senator.
03-11-1994 Susan Smith of Union, SC, was arrested for drowning her two sons. Nine days earlier Smith had claimed that the children had been abducted by a black carjacker.
03-11-1995 U.S. President Clinton dedicated a memorial at Arlington National Cemetery to the 270 victims of the bombing of Pan Am Flight 103.
03-11-1998 Bob Kane, the creator of Batman, died at the age of 83.
03-11-1998 A state-run newspaper in Iraq urged the country to prepare for to battle "the U.S. monster."
03-11-1998 Minnesota elected Jesse "The Body" Ventura, a former pro wrestler, as its governor.
03-11-2003 In Kabul, Afghanistan, a post-Taliban draft constitution was unveiled.
04-11-1842 Abraham Lincoln married Mary Todd in Springfield, IL.
04-11-1846 The patent for the artificial leg is granted to Benjamin Palmer.
04-11-1880 James and John Ritty patented the first cash register.
04-11-1922 In Egypt, Howard Carter discovered the entry of the lost tomb of Pharaoh Tutankhamen.
04-11-1924 Nellie T. Ross of Wyoming was elected America's first woman governor so she could serve out the remaining term of her late husband, William B. Ross.
04-11-1939 During World War II, the U.S. modified its neutrality stance with the Neutrality Act of 1939. The new policy allowed cash-and-carry purchases of arms by belligerents.
04-11-1939 At the 40th National Automobile Show the first air-conditioned car was put on display.
04-11-1942 During World War II, Axis forces retreated from El Alamein in North Africa. It was a major victory for the British.
04-11-1956 Soviet forces enter Hungary in order to supress the uprising that had begun on October 23, 1956.
04-11-1965 Lee Ann Roberts Breedlove became the first woman to exceed 300 mph when she went 308.5 mph.
04-11-1970 Former King Peter II of Yugoslavia died in Denver, CO. He was the first European king or queen to die and to be buried in the U.S.
04-11-1979 Iranian militants seized the U.S. embassy in Tehran and took 63 Americans hostage (90 total hostages). The militants, mostly students, demanded that the U.S. send the former shah back to Iran to stand trial. Many hostages were later released, but 52 were held for the next 14 months.
04-11-1985 Soviet defector Vitaly Yurchenko announced he was returning to the Soviet Union. He had charged that he had been kidnapped by the CIA.
04-11-1989 About a million East Germans filled the streets of East Berlin in a pro-democracy rally.
04-11-1990 Iraq issued a statement saying it was prepared to fight a "dangerous war" rather than give up Kuwait.
04-11-1991 Ronald Reagan opened his presidential library in Simi Valley, CA. The dedication ceremony was attended by President Bush and former U.S. presidents Jimmy Carter, Gerald R. Ford and Richard M. Nixon. It was the 1st gathering of 5 U.S. chief executives.
04-11-1995 Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin, 73 years old, was assassinated by right-wing Israeli Yigal Amir after attending a peace rally.
04-11-1999 Cristina Saralegui received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
04-11-1999 The United Nations imposed economic sanctions against the Taliban that controlled most of Afghanistan. The sanctions were imposed because the Taliban had refused to turn over Osama bin Laden, who had been charged with masterminding the 1998 bombings of the U.S. embassies in Kenya and Tanzania.
04-11-2001 Hurrican Michelle hit Cuba destroying crops and thousands of homes. The United States made the gesture of sending humanitarian aid. On December 16, 2001, Cuba received the first commercial food shipment from the U.S. in nearly 40 years.
05-11-1605 The "Gunpowder Plot" attempted by Guy Fawkes failed when he was captured before he could blow up the English Parliament. Guy Fawkes Day is celebrated every November 5th in Britain to celebrate his failure to blow up all the members of Parliament and King James I.
05-11-1844 In California, a grizzly bear underwent a successful cataract operation at the Zoological Garden.
05-11-1872 In the U.S., Susan B. Anthony was fined $100 for attempting to vote in the presidential election. She never paid the fine.
05-11-1895 George B. Selden received the first U.S. patent for an automobile. He sold the rights for $200,000 four years later.
05-11-1911 Italy officially annexed Tripoli.
05-11-1935 The game "Monopoly" was introduced by Parker Brothers Company.
05-11-1940 U.S. President Roosevelt won an unprecedented third term in office.
05-11-1944 Lord Moyne, a British official, was assassinated by the Zionist Stern gang in Cairo, Egypt.
05-11-1946 John F. Kennedy was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives at the age of 29.
05-11-1955 The Vienna State Opera House in Austria formally opened.
05-11-1956 British and French forces began landing in Egypt during the Suez Canal Crisis. A cease-fire was declared 2 days later.
05-11-1959 The American Football League was formed.
05-11-1963 Archaeologists found the remains of a Viking settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows, Newfoundland.
05-11-1974 Ella T. Grasso was elected governor of Connecticut. She was the first woman in the U.S. to win a governorship without succeeding her husband.
05-11-1984 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the NFL had exceeded antitrust limits in attempting to stop the Oakland Raiders from moving to Los Angeles.
05-11-1986 The White House reaffirmed the U.S. ban on the sale of weapons to Iran.
05-11-1987 In South Africa, Goban Mbeki was released after serving 24 years in the Robben Island prison. He had been sentenced to life for treason against the white minority government of South Africa.
05-11-1998 Scientists published a genetic study that showed strong evidence that Thomas Jefferson fathered at least one child (Eston Hemings) of his slave, Sally Hemings. (for more information)
05-11-1990 Rabbi Meir Kahane, founder of the Kach movement, was shot to death after a speech at a New York Hotel. His assassin, Egyptian El Sayyid, was later convicted of the murder and was sentenced to life in prison for his part in the World Trade Center bombing.
05-11-1992 Malice Green, a black motorist, was beaten to death in Detroit during a struggle with police. Two officers were later convicted in his death and sentenced to prison.
05-11-1994 Former U.S. President Reagan announced that he had Alzheimer's disease.
05-11-1994 George Foreman, 45, became boxing's oldest heavyweight champion when he knocked out Michael Moorer in the 10th round of their WBA fight in Las Vegas, NV.
05-11-1998 In the U.S., Chairman Henry Hyde of the Judiciary Committee asked President Clinton to answer 81 questions for the House impeachment inquiry.
05-11-1998 The U.N. announced that the Taliban militia had killed up to 5,000 civilians in a takeover of an Afghani town.
05-11-1999 A 12-day conference on global warming, attended by delegates from 170 nations, ended in Bonn, Germany.
05-11-1999 Dennis Rodman (NBA) and Carmen Electra were both arrested and charged with battery and domestic violence in a hotel in Miami Beach, FL.
05-11-1999 U.S. District Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson ruled that Microsoft Corp. enjoyed "monopoly power".
05-11-2001 It was announced that European aircraft manufacturer Airbus and Dubai-based Emirates airlines set up a joint venture specializing in airline services.
06-11-1789 Father John Carroll was appointed as the first Roman Catholic bishop in the United States of America.
06-11-1832 Joseph Smith, III, was born. He was the first president of the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints. He was also the son of Joseph Smith, the founder of Mormonism.
06-11-1851 Charles Henry Dow was born. He was the founder of Dow Jones & Company.
06-11-1860 Abraham Lincoln was elected to be the sixteenth president of the United States.
06-11-1861 Jefferson Davis was elected as the president of the Confederacy in the U.S.
06-11-1861 The inventor of basketball, James Naismith, was born.
06-11-1869 The first official intercollegiate football game was played in New Brunswick, NJ.
06-11-1913 Mohandas K. Gandhi was arrested as he led a march of Indian miners in South Africa.
06-11-1917 During World War I, Candian forces take the village of Passchendaele, Belgium, in the Third Battle of Ypres.
06-11-1923 Jacob Schick was granted a patent for the electric shaver.
06-11-1935 Edwin H. Armstrong announced his development of FM broadcasting.
06-11-1952 The first hydrogen bomb was exploded at Eniwetok Atoll in the Pacific Ocean.
06-11-1962 The U.N. General Assembly adopts a resolution that condemned South Africa's racist apartheid policies. The resolution also called for all member states to terminate military and economic relations with South Africa.
06-11-1965 The Freedom Flights program began which would allow 250,000 Cubans to come to the United States by 1971.
06-11-1967 Phil Donahue began a TV talk show in Dayton, OH. The show was on the air for 29 years.
06-11-1975 King Hassan II of Morocco launches the Green March, a mass migration of 300,000 unarmed Moroccans, that march into the nation of Western Sahara.
06-11-1977 39 people were killed when an earthen dam burst, sending a wall of water through the campus of Toccoa Falls Bible College in Georgia.
06-11-1983 U.S. Army choppers dropped hundreds of leaflets over northern and central Grenada. The leaflets urged residents to cooperate in locating any Grenadian army or Cuban resisters to the U.S-led invasion.
06-11-1984 For the first time in 193 years, the New York Stock Exchange remained open during a presidential election day.
06-11-1985 Leftist guerrillas belonging to Columbia's April 19 Movement seized control of the Palace of Justice in Bogota.
06-11-1986 Former Navy radioman John A. Walker Jr., was sentenced in Baltimore to life imprisonment. Walker had admitted to being the head of a family spy ring.
06-11-1986 U.S. intelligence sources confirmed a story run by the Lebanese magazine Ash Shiraa that reported the U.S. had been secretly selling arms to Iran in an effort to secure the release of seven American hostages.
06-11-1989 In the hopes of freeing U.S. hostages held in Iran, the U.S. announced that it would unfreeze $567 million in Iranian assets that had been held since 1979.
06-11-1990 About 20% of the Universal Studios backlot in southern California was destroyed in an arson fire.
06-11-1991 Kuwait celebrated the dousing of the last of the oil fires ignited by Iraq during the Persian Gulf War.
06-11-1995 Art Modell, the owner of the Cleveland Browns, announced plans to move his team to Baltimore. (NFL)
06-11-1995 Mark Messier scored his 500th NHL goal.
06-11-1996 Michael Jordan scored 50 points for the 29th time in his NBA career.
06-11-1998 The Islamic militant group Hamas exploded a car bomb killing the two attackers and injuring 21 civilians.
06-11-1999 Australian voters rejected a referendum to drop Britain's queen as their head of state.
06-11-2001 In London, the "Lest We Forget" exhibit opened at the National Memorial Arboretum. Fred Seiker was the creator of the 24 watercolors. Seiker was a prisoner of war that had been forced to build the Burma Railroad, the "railway of death," for the Japanese during World War II.
06-11-2001 In Madrid, Spain, a car bomb injured about 60 people. The bomb was blamed on Basque separatists.
06-11-2001 Ten people were executed in Beijing, China. The state newspaper of China said that all of the people executed were robbers and killers aged 20-23.
06-11-2001 Disney's "Mickey's Magical Christmas - Snowed In at the House Of Mouse" was released on video and DVD.
07-11-1637 Anne Hutchinson, the first female religious leader in the American colonies, was banished from the Massachusetts Bay Colony for heresy.
07-11-1665 "The London Gazette" was first published.
07-11-1811 The Shawnee Indians of chief Tecumseh were defeated by William Henry Harrison at the Battle of Wabash (or (Tippecanoe).
07-11-1837 In Alton, IL, abolitionist printer Elijah P. Lovejoy was shot to death by a mob (supporters of slavery) while trying to protect his printing shop from a third destruction.
07-11-1874 The Republican party of the U.S. was first symbolized as an elephant in a cartoon by Thomas Nast in Harper's Weekly.
07-11-1876 The cigarette manufacturing machine was patented by Albert H. Hook.
07-11-1877 "The Sorcerer" was performed for the first time of 178 total performances.
07-11-1893 The state of Colorado granted its women the right to vote.
07-11-1895 The last spike was driven into Canada's first transcontinental railway in the mountains of British Columbia.
07-11-1914 The "New Republic" magazine was printed for the first time.
07-11-1916 Jeanette Rankin of Montana became the first woman elected to the U.S. Congress.
07-11-1917 Russia's Bolshevik Revolution took place. The provisional government of Alexander Kerensky was overthrown by forces led by Vladimir Ilyich Lenin.
07-11-1918 During World War I, a false report through the United Press announced that an armistice had been signed.
07-11-1929 The Museum of Modern Art in New York City opened to the public.
07-11-1932 "Buck Rogers in the 25th Century" was broadcast for the first on CBS Radio.
07-11-1933 Voters in Pennsylvania eliminated sports from Pennsylvanian "Blue Laws."
07-11-1940 The middle section of the Tacoma Narrows Bridge in Washington state collapsed during a windstorm. The suspension bridge had opened to traffic on July 1, 1940.
07-11-1944 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt became the first person to win a fourth term as president.
07-11-1963 The comedy "It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World" premiered in Hollywood.
07-11-1963 Elston Howard, of the New York Yankees, became the first black player to be named the American League's Most Valuable Player.
07-11-1965 The "Pillsbury Dough Boy" debuted in television commercials.
07-11-1967 Carl Stokes was elected the first black mayor Cleveland, OH, becoming the first black mayor of a major city.
07-11-1967 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson signed a bill establishing the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.
07-11-1967 The U.S. Selective Service Commission announced that college students arrested in anti-war demonstrations would lose their draft deferments.
07-11-1973 New Jersey became the first U.S. state to permit girls to play on Little League baseball teams.
07-11-1973 The U.S. Congress over-rode President Nixon's veto of the War Powers Act, which limits a chief executive's power to wage war without congressional approval.
07-11-1983 A bomb exploded in the U.S. Capitol. No one was injured.
07-11-1985 The Colombian army stormed the country's Palace of Justice. The siege claimed the lives of 100 people, including 11 Supreme Court Justices. The Palace had been seized by leftist guerrillas belonging to the April 19 Movement.
07-11-1987 Tunisia's president Habib Bourguiba was overthrown. He had been president since the country's independence in 1956.
07-11-1988 Sugar Ray Leonard knocked out Donnie LaLonde.
07-11-1989 L. Douglas Wilder won the governor's race in Virginia, becoming the first elected African-American state governor in U.S. history.
07-11-1989 David Dinkins was elected and become New York City's first African-American mayor.
07-11-1989 Richard Ramirez, convicted of California's "Night Stalker" killings, was sentenced to death.
07-11-1991 Magic Johnson (NBA) announced that he had tested positive for the virus that causes AIDS, and that he was retiring from basketball.
07-11-1991 Pro- and anti-Communists rallies took place in Moscow on the 74th anniversary of the Bolshevik Revolution.
07-11-1991 Actor Paul Reubens, a.k.a. Pee Wee Herman, pled no contest to charges of indecent exposure. Reubens had been arrested in Sarasota, FL, for exposing himself in a theater.
07-11-1995 In a Japanese courtroom, three U.S. military men admitted to the rape of a 12-year-old Okinawan schoolgirl.
07-11-1999 Tiger Woods became the first golfer since Ben Hogan in 1953 to win four straight tournaments.
07-11-2000 Hillary Rodham Clinton made history as the first president's wife to win public office. The state of New York elected her to the U.S. Senate.
07-11-2001 The new .BIZ domain extension was officially launched.
07-11-2001 After a 16-month stoppage the Concorde resumed flying commercially.
08-11-1656 Edmond Halley was born. Halley, an astronomer-mathmatician, was the first to calculate the orbit that was named after him. The comet makes an appearance every 76 years.
08-11-1793 The Louvre Museum, in Paris, opened to the public for the first time.
08-11-1805 The "Corps of Discovery" reached the Pacific Ocean. The expedition was lead by William Clark and Meriwether Lewis. The journey had begun on May 14, 1804, with the goal of exploring the Louisiana Purchase territory.
08-11-1880 French actress Sarah Bernhardt made her American stage debut in "Adrienne Lecouvreur" in New York City.
08-11-1887 Doc Holliday died at the age of 35. The gun fighting dentist died from tuberculosis in a sanitarium in Glenwood Springs, CO.
08-11-1889 Montana became the 41st U.S. state.
08-11-1895 Wilhelm Roentgen while experimenting with electricity discovered the scientific principle involved and took the first X-ray pictures.
08-11-1910 William H. Frost patented the insect exterminator.
08-11-1923 Adolf Hitler made his first attempt at seizing power in Germany with a failed coup in Munich that came to be known as the "Beer-Hall Putsch."
08-11-1933 The Civil Works Administration was created by executive order by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt. The organization was designed to create jobs for more than 4 million unemployed people in the U.S.
08-11-1939 "Life With Father" premiered on Broadway in New York City.
08-11-1942 The U.S. invaded Morocco and Algeria.
08-11-1942 During World War II, Operation Torch began as U.S. and British forces landed in French North Africa.
08-11-1950 During the Korean conflict, the first jet-plane battle took place as U.S. Air Force Lt. Russell J. Brown shot down a North Korean MiG-15.
08-11-1954 The American League approved the transfer of the Philadelphia Athletics baseball team to Kansas City, MO.
08-11-1956 After turning down 18,000 names, the Ford Motor Company decided to name their new car the "Edsel," after Henry Ford's only son.
08-11-1959 The 'Big E', Elgin Baylor of the Minneapolis Lakers, scored 64 points and set a National Basketball Association scoring record.
08-11-1965 The soap opera "Days of Our Lives" debuted on NBC-TV.
08-11-1966 Edward W. Brooke of Massachusetts became the first African-American elected to the U.S. Senate by popular vote.
08-11-1966 Ronald Reagan was elected governor of California.
08-11-1979 The program, "The Iran Crisis: America Held Hostage", premiered on ABC-TV. The show was planned to be temporary, but it evolved into "Nightline" in March of 1980.
08-11-1979 U.S. Senators John Warner (R-VA) and Mac Mathias (R-MD) introduced legislation to provide a site on the National Mall for the building of the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
08-11-1980 Scientists at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory in California announced that they had discovered a 15th moon orbiting the planet Saturn.
08-11-1985 A letter signed by four American hostages in Lebanon was delivered to The Associated Press in Beirut. The letter, contained pleas from Terry Anderson, Rev. Lawrence Jenco, David Jacobsen and Thomas Sutherland to President Reagan to negotiate a release.
08-11-1986 Vyacheslav M. Molotov died at age 96. During World War II, Molotov ordered the mass production of bottles filled with flammable liquid later called the "Molotov cocktail."
08-11-1987 A bomb planted by the Irish Republican Army exploded in Enniskillen, Northern Ireland, at a ceremony honoring Britain's war dead. Eleven people were killed.
08-11-1990 U.S. President Bush ordered more troop deployments in the Persian Gulf, adding about 150,000 soldiers to the multi-national force fighting against Iraq.
08-11-1991 The European Community and Canada imposed economic sanctions on Yugoslavia in an attempt to stop the Balkan civil war.
08-11-1992 About 350,000 people rallied in Berlin against racist violence.
08-11-1993 Five Picasso paintings and other artwork were stolen from the Museum of Modern Art in Stockholm, Sweden. The works were valued at $52 million.
08-11-1997 Chinese engineers diverted the Yangtze River to make way for the Three Gorges Dam.
08-11-2000 In Florida, a statewide recount began to decide the winner of the 2000 U.S. presidential election.
08-11-2000 Waco special counsel John C. Danforth released his final report that absolved the government of wrongdoing in the 1993 seige of the Branch Davidian compound in Texas.
08-11-2001 The "Homage to Van Gogh: International Artists Pay Tribute to a Legend" exhibit opened at the Appleton Museum of Art in Florida.
09-11-1857 The "Atlantic Monthly" first appeared on newsstands and featured the first installment of "The Autocrat of the Breakfast Table" by Oliver Wendell Holmes.
09-11-1872 A fire destroyed about 800 buildings in Boston, MA.
09-11-1906 U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt left for Panama to see the progress on the new canal. It was the first foreign trip by a U.S. president.
09-11-1911 George Claude of Paris, France, applied for a patent on neon advertising signs.
09-11-1918 Germany's Kaiser Wilhelm II announced he would abdicate. He then fled to the Netherlands.
09-11-1923 In Munich, the Beer Hall Putsch was crushed by German troops that were loyal to the democratic government. The event began the evening before when Adolf Hitler took control of a beer hall full of Bavarian government leaders at gunpoint.
09-11-1935 United Mine Workers president John L. Lewis and other labor leaders formed the Committee for Industrial Organization.
09-11-1938 Nazi troops and sympathizers destroyed and looted 7,500 Jewish businesses, burned 267 synagogues, killed 91 Jews, and rounded up over 25,000 Jewish men in an event that became known as Kristallnacht or "Night of Broken Glass."
09-11-1953 The U.S. Supreme Court upheld a 1922 ruling that major league baseball did not come within the scope of federal antitrust laws.
09-11-1961 Major Robert White flew an X-15 rocket plane at a world record speed of 4,093 mph.
09-11-1961 The Professional Golfer's Association (PGA) eliminated is "caucasians only" rule.
09-11-1963 In Japan, about 450 miners were killed in a coal-dust explosion.
09-11-1963 In Japan, 160 people died in a train crash.
09-11-1965 The great Northeast blackout occurred as several states and parts of Canada were hit by a series of power failures lasting up to 13 1/2 hours.
09-11-1967 A Saturn V rocket carrying an unmanned Apollo spacecraft blasted off from Cape Kennedy on a successful test flight.
09-11-1976 The U.N. General Assembly approved ten resolutions condemning the apartheid government in South Africa.
09-11-1979 The United Nations Security Council unanimously called upon Iran to release all American hostages "without delay." Militants, mostly students had taken 63 Americans hostage at the U.S. embassy in Tehran, Iran, on November 4.
09-11-1982 Sugar Ray Leonard retired from boxing. In 1984 Leonard came out of retirement to fight one more time before becoming a boxing commentator for NBC.
09-11-1984 A bronze statue titled "Three Servicemen," by Frederick Hart, was unveiled at the site of the Vietnam Veterans Memorial in Washington, DC.
09-11-1989 Communist East Germany opened its borders, allowing its citizens to travel freely to West Germany.
09-11-1990 Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev signed a non-aggression treaty with Germany.
09-11-1992 Russian President Boris Yeltsin, visiting London, appealed for assistance in rescheduling his country's debt, and asked British businesses to invest.
09-11-1997 Barry Sanders (Detroit Lions) became the first player in NFL history to rush for over 1,000 yards in nine straight seasons. In the same game Sanders passed former Dallas Cowboy Tony Dorsett for third place on the all-time rushing list.
09-11-1998 A federal judge in New York approved the richest antitrust settlement in U.S. history. A leading brokerage firm was ordered to pay $1.03 billion to investors who had sued over price-rigging of Nasdaq stocks.
09-11-1998 PBS aired its documentary special "Chihuly Over Venice."
09-11-2004 U.S. First Lady Laura Bush officially reopened Pennsylvania Avenue in front of the White House to pedestrians.
10-11-1775 The U.S. Marines were organized under authority of the Continental Congress. The Marines went out of existence after the end of the Revolutionary War in April of 1783. The Marine Corps were formally re-established on July 11, 1798. This day is observed as the birth date of the United States Marine Corps.
10-11-1801 The U.S. state of Tennessee outlawed the practice of dueling.
10-11-1871 Henry M. Stanley, journalist and explorer, found David Livingstone. Livingston was a missing Scottish missionary in central Africa. Stanley delivered his famous greeting: "Dr. Livingstone, I presume?"
10-11-1879 Western Union and the National Bell Telephone Company reached a settlement over various telephone patents.
10-11-1917 41 suffragists were arrested in front of the White House.
10-11-1919 The American Legion held its first national convention, in Minneapolis, MN.
10-11-1928 Michinomiya Hirohito was enthroned as Emperor of Japan.
10-11-1951 Direct-dial, coast-to-coast telephone service began when Mayor M. Leslie Denning of Englewood, NJ, called his counterpart in Alameda, CA.
10-11-1954 The Iwo Jima Memorial was dedicated in Arlington, VA.
10-11-1957 102,368 people attended the San Francisco 49ers and Los Angeles Rams game. The crowd was the largest regular-season crowd in NFL history.
10-11-1969 "Sesame Street" made its debut on PBS.
10-11-1970 The Great Wall of China opened for tourism.
10-11-1975 The U.N. General Assembly approved a resolution that equated Zionism with racism. The resolution was repealed in December of 1991.
10-11-1975 The Edmund Fitzgerald, an ore-hauling ship, and its crew of 29 vanished during a storm in Lake Superior.
10-11-1976 The Utah Supreme Court gave approval for Gary Gilmore to be executed, according to his wishes. The convicted murderer was put to death the following January.
10-11-1977 The Major Indoor Soccer League was officially organized in New York City.
10-11-1980 CBS News anchor Dan Rather claimed he had been kidnapped in a cab. It turned out that Rather had refused to pay the cab fare.
10-11-1982 Soviet leader Leonid I. Brezhnev died of a heart attack at age 75. He was suceeded by Yuri V. Andropov.
10-11-1982 In Washington, DC, the Vietnam Veterans Memorial was opened to visitors.
10-11-1984 The U.S. Postal Service issued a commemorative stamp of the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
10-11-1986 Camille Sontag and Marcel Coudari, two Frenchmen were released by the captors that held them in Lebanon.
10-11-1988 The U.S. Department of Energy announced that Texas would be the home of the atom-smashing super-collider. The project was cancelled by a vote of the U.S. Congress in Oct. 1993.
10-11-1990 Chandra Shekhar was sworn in as India's new prime minister.
10-11-1991 Robert Maxwell was buried in Israel, five days after his body was recovered off the Canary Islands.
10-11-1993 John Wayne Bobbitt was acquitted on the charge of marital sexual assault against his wife who sexually mutilated him. Lorena Bobbitt was later acquitted of malicious wounding her husband.
10-11-1993 The U.S. House of Representatives passed the Brady Bill, which called for a five-day waiting period for handgun purchases.
10-11-1994 U.S. officials that it planned to stop enforcing the arms embargo against the Bosnian government the following week. The U.N. Security Council was opposed to lifting the ban.
10-11-1994 Iraq recognized Kuwait's borders in the hope that the action would end trade sanctions.
10-11-1995 Nigeria's military rulers hanged playwright Ken Saro-Wiwa along with several other anti-government activists.
10-11-1995 In Katmandu, Nepal, searchers rescued 549 hikers after a massive avalanche struck the Himalayan foothills. The disaster left 24 tourists and 32 Nepalese dead.
10-11-1996 Dan Marino (Miami Dolphins) became the first quarterback in NFL history to pass for more than 50,000 yards.
10-11-1997 WorldCom Inc. acquired MCI Communication Corporation. It was the largest merger in U.S. history valued at $37 billion.
10-11-1997 A jury in Virginia convicted Mir Aimal Kasi of the murder of two CIA employees in 1993.
10-11-1997 A judge in Cambridge, MA, reduced Louise Woodward's murder conviction to manslaughter and sentenced the English au pair to time served. She had served 279 days in the death of 8-month-old Matthew Eappen.
10-11-1998 At the White House, U.S. Vice President Al Gore unveiled "The Virtual Wall" website (www.thevirtualwall.org) that enables visitors to experience The Wall through the Internet.
10-11-1999 Ted Danson received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
10-11-2001 The World Trade Organization approved China's membership.
10-11-2001 The musical "Lady Diana - A Smile Charms the World" opened in Germany.
10-11-2004 Yusuf Islam (formerly known as Cat Stevens) was awarded the "Man for Peace" prize in Rome at the opening of a meeting of Nobel Peace Prize laureates.
11-11-1620 The Mayflower Compact was signed by the 41 men on the Mayflower when they landed in what is now Provincetown Harbor near Cape Cod. The compact called for "just and equal laws.
11-11-1831 Nat Turner, a slave and educated minister, was hanged in Jerusalem, VA, after inciting a violent slave uprising.
11-11-1851 The telescope was patented by Alvan Clark.
11-11-1868 The first indoor amateur track and field meet was held by the New York Athletic Club.
11-11-1880 Australian outlaw and bank robber Ned Kelly was hanged at the Melbourne jail at age 25.
11-11-1887 Labor Activists were hanged in Illinois after being convicted of being connected to a bombing that killed eight police officers.
11-11-1889 Washington became the 42nd state of the United States.
11-11-1918 World War I came to an end when the Allies and Germany signed an armistice. This day became recognized as Veteran's Day in the United States.
11-11-1918 Poland was reestablished shortly after the surrender of Germnay.
11-11-1920 The body of an unknown British soldier was buried in Westminster Abbey. The service was recorded with the first electronic recording process developed by Lionel Guest and H.O. Merriman.
11-11-1921 The Tomb of the Unknowns was dedicated at Arlington Cemetery in Virginia by U.S. President Harding.
11-11-1938 Kate Smith first sang Irving Berlin's "God Bless America" on network radio.
11-11-1940 The Jeep made its debut.
11-11-1942 During World War II, Germany completed its occupation of France.
11-11-1946 The New York Knickerbockers (now the Knicks) played their first game at Madison Square Garden.
11-11-1952 The first video recorder was demonstrated by John Mullin and Wayne Johnson in Beverly Hills, CA.
11-11-1965 The government of Rhodesia declared its independence from Britain. The country later became known as Zimbabwe.
11-11-1965 Walt Disney announced a project in Florida.
11-11-1966 The U.S. launched Gemini 12 from Cape Kennedy, FL. The craft circled the Earth 59 times before returning.
11-11-1972 The U.S. Army turned over its base at Long Bihn to the South Vietnamese army. The event symbolized the end of direct involvement in the Vietnam War by the U.S. military.
11-11-1975 Civil war broke out when Angola gained independence from Portugal.
11-11-1981 Stuntman Dan Goodwin scaled the outside of the 100-story John Hancock Center in Chicago in about six hours.
11-11-1984 The Reverend Martin Luther King Sr. died in Atlanta at age 84.
11-11-1984 U.S. President Ronald Reagan accepted the Vietnam Veterans Memorial as a gift to the nation from the Vietnam Veterans Memorial Fund.
11-11-1984 Gary Coleman, at age 13, underwent his second kidney transplant in Los Angeles. He had his first transplant at age 5.
11-11-1986 Sperry Rand and Burroughs merged to form "Unisys," becoming the second largest computer company.
11-11-1987 Vincent Van Gogh's "Irises" was sold for a then record 53.9 million dollars in New York.
11-11-1988 Police in Sacramento, CA, found the first of seven bodies buried on the grounds of a boardinghouse. Dorothea Puente was later charged in the deaths of nine people, convicted of three murders and sentenced to life in prison.
11-11-1990 Stormie Jones, the world's first heart-liver transplant recipient, died at a Pittsburgh hospital at age 13.
11-11-1991 The U.S. stationed its first diplomat in Cambodia in 16 years to help the nation arrange democratic elections.
11-11-1992 Russian President Boris Yeltsin told U.S. senators in a letter that Americans had been held in prison camps after World War II. Some were "summarily executed," but others were still living in his country voluntarily.
11-11-1992 The Church of England voted to ordain women as priests.
11-11-1993 Walt Disney Co. announced plans to build a U.S. history theme park in a Virginia suburb of Washington. The plan was halted later due to local opposition.
11-11-1993 In Washington, DC, the Vietnam Women's Memorial was dedicated to honor the more than 11,000 women who had served in the Vietnam War.
11-11-1994 In Gaza, a suicide bomber detonated his explosives at an Israeli military checkpoint killing three soldiers.
11-11-1996 The Vietnam Veterans Memorial Fund unveiled "The Wall That Heals." The work was a half-scale replica of the Vietnam Veterans Memorial that would tour communities throughout the United States.
11-11-1997 The Eastman Kodak Company announced that they were laying off 10,000 employees.
11-11-1997 Roger Clemens (Toronto Blue Jays) became the third major league player to win the Cy Young Award four times.
11-11-1998 Jay Cochrane set a record for the longest blindfolded skywalk. He walked on a tightrope between the towers of the Flamingo Hilton in Las Vegas, NV. The towers are 600 feet apart.
11-11-1998 Vincente Fernandez received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
11-11-1998 Israel's Cabinet ratified a land-for-peace agreement with the Palestinians.
11-11-2002 Microsoft Chairman Bill Gates pledged $100 million to fight AIDS in India.
12-11-1799 Andrew Ellicott Douglass witnesses the Leonids meteor shower from a ship off the Florida Keys.
12-11-1815 American suffragist Elizabeth Cady Stanton was born in Johnstown, NY.
12-11-1840 Sculptor Auguste Rodin was born in Paris. His most widely known works are "The Kiss" and "The Thinker."
12-11-1859 The first flying trapeze act was performed by Jules Leotard at Cirque Napoleon in Paris, France. He was also the designer of the garment that is named after him.
12-11-1892 William "Pudge" Heffelfinger became the first professional football player when he was paid a $500 bonus for helping the Allegheny Athletic Association beat the Pittsburgh Athletic Club.
12-11-1915 Theodore W. Richards, of Harvard University, became the first American to be awarded the Nobel Prize in chemistry.
12-11-1918 Austria was declared an independent republic only one day after the end of World War I.
12-11-1920 Judge Keneshaw Mountain Landis was elected the first commissioner of the American and National Leagues.
12-11-1921 Representatives of nine nations gathered for the start of the Washington Conference for Limitation of Armaments.
12-11-1927 Joseph Stalin became the undisputed ruler of the Soviet Union. Leon Trotsky was expelled from the Communist Party leading to Stalin coming to power.
12-11-1931 Maple Leaf Gardens opened in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. It was to be the new home of the Toronto Maple Leafs in the National Hockey League (NHL).
12-11-1933 In Philadelphia, the first Sunday football game was played.
12-11-1940 Walt Disney released "Fantasia."
12-11-1942 During World War II, naval battle of Guadalcanal began between Japanese and American forces. The Americans won a major victory.
12-11-1944 During World War II, the German battleship "Tirpitz" was sunk off the coast of Norway.
12-11-1946 The first drive-up banking facility opened at the Exchange National Bank in Chicago, IL.
12-11-1948 The war crimes tribunal sentenced Japanese Premier Hideki Tojo and six other World War II Japanese leaders to death.
12-11-1953 The National Football League (NFL) policy of blacking out home games was upheld by Judge Allan K. Grim of the U.S. District Court in Philadelphia.
12-11-1954 Ellis Island, the immigration station in New York Harbor, closed after processing more than 20 million immigrants since 1892.
12-11-1964 Paula Murphy set the female land speed record 226.37 MPH.
12-11-1972 Don Shula, coach of the Miami Dolphins, became the first NFL head coach to win 100 regular season games in 10 seasons.
12-11-1975 U.S. Supreme Court Justice William O. Douglas retired because of failing health, ending a record 36˝-year term.
12-11-1979 U.S. President Carter ordered a halt to all oil imports from Iran in response to 63 Americans being taken hostage at the U.S. embassy in Tehran, Iran on November 4.
12-11-1980 The U.S. space probe Voyager I came within 77,000 miles of Saturn while transmitting data back to Earth.
12-11-1982 Yuri V. Andropov was elected to succeed the late Leonid I. Brezhnev as general secretary of the Soviet Communist Party's Central Committee.
12-11-1984 Space shuttle astronauts Dale Gardner and Joe Allen snared the Palapa B-2 satellite in history's first space salvage.
12-11-1985 In Norfolk, VA, Arthur James Walker was sentenced to life in prison for his role in a spy ring run by his brother, John A. Walker Jr.
12-11-1987 The American Medical Association issued a policy statement that said it was unethical for a doctor to refuse to treat someone solely because that person had AIDS or was HIV-positive.
12-11-1990 Japanese Emperor Akihito formally assumed the Chrysanthemum Throne.
12-11-1991 In the U.S., Robert Gates was sworn in as CIA director.
12-11-1995 The space shuttle Atlantis blasted off on a mission to dock with the Russian space station Mir.
12-11-1997 Four Americans and their Pakistani driver were shot to death in Karachi, Pakistan. The Americans were oil company employees.
12-11-1997 The UN Security Council imposed new sanctions on Iraq for constraints being placed on UN arms inspectors.
12-11-1997 Ramzi Yousef was found guilty of masterminding the 1993 bombing of the World Trade Center.
12-11-1998 Daimler-Benz completed a merger with Chrysler to form Daimler-Chrysler AG.
12-11-2001 American Airlines flight 587 crashed just minutes after take off from Kennedy Airport in New York. The Airbus A300 crashed into the Rockaway Beach section of Queens. All 260 people aboard were killed.
12-11-2001 It was reported that the Northern Alliance had taken the Kabul, Afghanistan, from the ruling Taliban. The Norther Alliance at this point was reported to have control over most of the northern areas of Afghanistan.
12-11-2002 Stan Lee filed a lawsuit against Marvel Entertainment Inc. that claimed the company had cheated him out of millions of dollars in movie profits related to the 2002 movie "Spider-Man." Lee was the creator of Spider-Man, the Incredible Hulk and Daredevil.
13-11-1775 During the American Revolution, U.S. forces captured Montreal.
13-11-1789 Benjamin Franklin wrote a letter to a friend in which he said, "In this world nothing can be said to be certain, except death and taxes."
13-11-1805 Johann George Lehner, a Viennese butcher, invented a recipe and called it the "frankfurter."
13-11-1850 Robert Louis Stevenson was born in Edinburgh, Scotland. He is best known for Treasure Island and The Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde.
13-11-1909 250 miners were killed in a fire and explosion at the St. Paul Mine at Cherry, IL.
13-11-1927 The Holland Tunnel opened to the public, providing access between New York City and New Jersey beneath the Hudson River.
13-11-1933 In Austin, MN, the first sit-down labor strike in America took place.
13-11-1940 The Walt Disney movie "Fantasia" had its world premiere at New York's Broadway Theater.
13-11-1942 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed a measure lowering the minimum draft age from 21 to 18.
13-11-1956 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down laws calling for racial segregation on public buses.
13-11-1971 The U.S. spacecraft Mariner 9 became the first spacecraft to orbit another planet, Mars.
13-11-1977 The comic strip "Li'l Abner" by Al Capp appeared in newspapers for the last time.
13-11-1982 The Vietnam Veterans Memorial was dedicated in Washington, DC.
13-11-1984 A libel suit against Time, Inc. by former Israeli Defense Minister Ariel Sharon went to trial in New York.
13-11-1985 About 23,000 residents of Armero, Colombia, died when a gigantic mudslide buried the city. The slide was triggered by a mild eruption of the Nevado del Ruiz volcano.
13-11-1986 U.S. President Ronald Reagan publicly acknowledged that the U.S. had sent "defensive weapons and spare parts" to Iran. He denied that the shipments were sent to free hostages, but that they had been sent to improve relations.
13-11-1991 Roger Clemens won his third Cy Young Award for the American League.
13-11-1994 In San Francisco, CA, a heavily armed gunman traded fire with police, hitting two police officers, a paramedic and another person before being killed.
13-11-1994 Sweden voted to join the European Union.
13-11-1995 Greg Maddox (Atlanta Braves) became the first major league pitcher to win four consecutive Cy Young Awards.
13-11-1995 Seven people, including five Americans are killed in a car bomb attack at a U.S. military headquarters in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
13-11-1997 Iraq expelled six U.N. arms inspectors that were U.S. citizens.
13-11-1998 "The Wizard of Oz" was released on the big screen by Warner Bros. 59 years after its original release.
13-11-1998 Monica Lewinsky signed a deal with St. Martin's Press for the North American rights to her story about her affair with U.S. President Bill Clinton.
13-11-1998 U.S. President Clinton agreed to pay Paula Jones $850,000, without an apology or admission of guilt, to throw out her sexual harassment lawsuit.
13-11-2001 U.S. President George W. Bush signed an executive order that would allow for military tribunals to try any foreigners captured with connections to the terrorist attacks on the United States on September 11, 2001. It was the first time since World War II that a president had taken such action.
14-11-1832 The first streetcar went into operation in New York City, NY. The vehicle was horse-drawn and had room for 30 people.
14-11-1851 Herman Melville's novel "Moby Dick" was first published in the U.S.
14-11-1881 Charles J. Guiteau's trial began for the assassination of U.S. President Garfield. Guiteau was convicted and hanged the following year.
14-11-1889 New York World reporter Nellie Bly (Elizabeth Cochrane) began an attempt to surpass the fictitious journey of Jules Verne's Phileas Fogg by traveling around the world in less than 80 days. Bly succeeded by finishing the journey the following January in 72 days, 6 hours and 11 minutes.
14-11-1922 The British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) began domestic radio service.
14-11-1935 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt proclaimed the Philippine Islands a free commonwealth after its new constitution was approved. The Tydings-McDuffie Act planned for the Phillipines to be completely independent by July 4, 1946.
14-11-1940 During World War II, German war planes destroyed most of the English town of Coventry when about 500 Luftwaffe bombers attacked.
14-11-1943 Ernie Nevers of the St. Louis Cardinals became the first professional football player to score six touchdowns in a single game.
14-11-1956 The USSR crushed the Hungarian uprising.
14-11-1968 Yale University announced it was going co-educational.
14-11-1969 Apollo 12 blasted off for the moon from Cape Kennedy, FL.
14-11-1969 During the Vietnam War, Major General Bruno Arthur Hochmuth, commander of the Third Marine Division, became the first general to be killed in Vietnam by enemy fire.
14-11-1972 The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed above the 1,000 (1,003.16) level for the first time.
14-11-1972 Blue Ribbon Sports became Nike.
14-11-1973 Britain's Princess Anne married a commoner, Capt. Mark Phillips, in Westminster Abbey. They divorced in 1992, and Princess Anne re-married.
14-11-1979 U.S. President Carter froze all Iranian assets in the United States and U.S. banks abroad in response to the taking of 63 American hostages at the U.S. embassy in Tehran, Iran.
14-11-1983 The British government announced that U.S.-made cruise missiles had arrived at the Greenham Common air base amid protests.
14-11-1986 The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission imposed a record $100 million penalty against Ivan F. Boesky for insider-trading and barred him from working again in the securities industry.
14-11-1987 In the lobby of Beirut's American University Hospital a bomb hidden in a box of chocolates exploded. Seven people were killed including the woman carrying the box.
14-11-1988 Israeli President Chaim Herzog formally asked Prime Minister Yitzhak Shamir to form a new government.
14-11-1989 The U.S. Navy ordered an unprecedented 48-hour stand-down in the wake of a recent string of serious accidents.
14-11-1990 Simon and Schuster announced it had dropped plans to publish Bret Easton Ellis novel "American Psycho."
14-11-1991 U.S. and British authorities announced indictments against two Libyan intelligence officials in connection with the bombing of Pan Am Flight 103.
14-11-1991 Thomas McIlvane fatally shot four workers at the Royal Oak, MI, Post Office before killing himself. He had been fired from the location.
14-11-1991 After 13 years in exile Cambodian Prince Norodom Sihanouk returned to his homeland.
14-11-1994 U.S. experts visited North Korea's main nuclear complex for the first time under an accord that opened such sites to outside inspections.
14-11-1995 The U.S. government instituted a partial shutdown, closing national parks and museums while most government offices operated with skeleton crews.
14-11-1998 Carmen Electra and Dennis Rodman were married in Las Vegas, NV.
15-11-1777 The Continental Congress approved the Articles of Confederation, precursor to the U.S. Constitution.
15-11-1806 Explorer Zebulon Pike spotted the mountaintop that became known as Pikes Peak.
15-11-1889 Brazil's monarchy was overthrown.
15-11-1901 Miller Reese patented an electrical hearing aid.
15-11-1902 Anarchist Gennaro Rubin failed in his attempt to murder King Leopold II of Belgium.
15-11-1920 The League of Nations met for the first time in Geneva, Switzerland.
15-11-1926 The National Broadcasting Co. (NBC) debuted with a radio network of 24 stations. The first network radio broadcast was a four-hour "spectacular."
15-11-1939 U.S. President Roosevelt laid the cornerstone of the Jefferson Memorial in Washington, DC.
15-11-1940 The first 75,000 men were called to Armed Forces duty under peacetime conscription.
15-11-1965 The Soviet probe, Venera 3, was launched from Baikonur, Kazakhstan. On March 1, 1966, it became the first unmanned spacecraft to reach the surface of another planet when it crashed on Venus.
15-11-1966 The flight of Gemini 12 ended successfully as astronauts James A. Lovell and Edwin "Buzz" Aldrin Jr. splashed down safely in the Atlantic Ocean.
15-11-1969 In Washington, DC, a quarter of a million protesters staged a peaceful demonstration against the Vietnam War.
15-11-1985 Britain and Ireland signed an accord giving Dublin an official consultative role in governing Northern Ireland.
15-11-1986 A government tribunal in Nicaragua convicted American Eugene Hasenfus of charges related to his role in delivering arms to Contra rebels. He was sentenced to 30 years in prison and was pardoned a month later.
15-11-1986 Ivan F. Boesky, reputed to be the highest-paid person on Wall Street, faced penalties of $100 million for insider stock trading. It was the highest penalty ever imposed by the SEC.
15-11-1988 The Palestine National Council, the legislative body of the PLO, proclaimed the establishment of an independent Palestinian state at the close of a four-day conference in Algiers.
15-11-1992 Richard Petty drove in the final race of his 35-year career.
15-11-1993 A judge in Mineola, NY, sentenced Joey Buttafuoco to six months in jail for the statutory rape of Amy Fisher. Fisher was serving a prison sentence for shooting and wounding Buttafuoco's wife, Mary Jo.
15-11-1995 Texaco agreed to pay $176 million to settle a race-discrimination lawsuit.
15-11-1999 Representatives from China and the United States signed a major trade agreement that involved China's membership in the World Trade Organization (WTO).
15-11-2000 Three police officers from the Rampart division of the Los Angeles police department were convicted on several counts of conspiracy to obstruct justice. One other officer was acquitted. The case was the first major case against the anti-gang unit.
15-11-2005 In Amiens, France, Isabelle Dinoire became the first person to undergo a partial face transplant. She had been attacked by a dog earlier in the year.
15-11-2006 Andy Warhol's painting of Communist Party Chairman Mao Zedong sold for $17.4 million. At the same auction "Orange Marilyn" sold for $16.2 million and "Sixteen Jackies" sold for $15.6 million.
16-11-1776 British troops captured Fort Washington during the American Revolution.
16-11-1864 Union Gen. William T. Sherman and his troops began their "March to the Sea" during the U.S. Civil War.
16-11-1885 Canadian rebel Louis Riel was executed for high treason.
16-11-1907 Oklahoma was admitted as the 46th state.
16-11-1915 Coca-Cola had its prototype for a countoured bottle patented. The bottle made its commercial debut the next year.
16-11-1933 The United States and the Soviet Union established diplomatic relations for the first time.
16-11-1952 In the Peanuts comic strip, Lucy first held a football for Charlie Brown.
16-11-1957 Jim Brown (Cleveland Browns) set an NFL season rushing record of 1163 yards after only eight games.
16-11-1966 Dr. Samuel H. Sheppard was acquitted in his second trial of charges he had murdered his pregnant wife, Marilyn, in 1954.
16-11-1969 The U.S. Army announced that several had been charged with massacre and the subsequent cover-up in the My Lai massacre in Vietnam on March 16, 1968.
16-11-1973 Skylab 3 carrying a crew of three astronauts, was launched from Cape Canaveral, FL, on an 84-day mission.
16-11-1973 U.S. President Nixon signed the Alaska Pipeline measure into law.
16-11-1981 A vaccine for hepatitis B was approved. The vaccine had been developed at Merck Institute for Therapeutic Research.
16-11-1982 An agreement was announced on the 57th day of a strike by National Football League (NFL) players.
16-11-1988 Estonia's parliament declared that the Baltic republic "sovereign," but stopped short of complete independence.
16-11-1994 Major League Soccer announced that it would start its inaugural season in 1996.
16-11-1997 China released Wei Jingsheng, a pro-democracy dissident from jail for medical reasons. He had been incarcerated for almost 18 years.
16-11-1998 In Burlington, Wisconsin, five high school students, aged 15 to 16, were arrested in an alleged plot to kill a carefully selected group of teachers and students.
16-11-1998 It was announced that Monica Lewinsky had signed a deal for the North American rights to a book about her affair with U.S. President Clinton.
16-11-1998 The U.S. Supreme Court said that union members could file discrimination lawsuits against employers even when labor contracts require arbitration.
16-11-1999 Johnny Depp received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
16-11-1999 Chrica Adams, the pregnant girlfriend of Rae Carruth, was shot four times in her car. She died a month later from her wounds. The baby survived. Carruth was sentenced to a minimum of 18 years and 11 months in prison for his role in the murder.
16-11-2000 Bill Clinton became the first serving U.S. president to visit Communist Vietnam.
16-11-2004 A NASA unmanned "scramjet" (X-43A) reached a speed of nearly 10 times the speed of sound above the Pacific Ocean.
17-11-1558 Elizabeth I ascended the English throne upon the death of Queen Mary Tudor.
17-11-1603 Sir Walter Raleigh went on trial for treason.
17-11-1796 Catherine the Great of Russia died at the age of 67.
17-11-1798 Irish nationalist leader Wolfe Tone committed suicide while in jail awaiting execution.
17-11-1800 The U.S. Congress held its first session in Washington, DC, in the partially completed Capitol building.
17-11-1869 The Suez Canal opened in Egypt, linking the Mediterranean and the Red seas.
17-11-1880 The first three British female graduates received their Bachelor of Arts degrees from London University.
17-11-1903 Russia's Social Democrats officially split into two groups - Bolsheviks and Mensheviks.
17-11-1904 The first underwater submarine journey was taken, from Southampton, England, to the Isle of Wight.
17-11-1913 The steamship Louise became the first ship to travel through the Panama Canal.
17-11-1913 In Germany, Kaiser Wilhelm banned the armed forces from dancing the tango.
17-11-1922 Siberia voted for union with the U.S.S.R.
17-11-1962 Washington's Dulles International Airport was dedicated by U.S. President Kennedy.
17-11-1968 NBC cut away from the final minutes of a New York Jets-Oakland Raiders game to begin a TV special, "Heidi," on schedule. The Raiders came from behind to beat the Jets 43-32.
17-11-1970 The Soviet Union landed an unmanned, remote-controlled vehicle on the moon, the Lunokhod 1. The vehicle was released by Luna 17.
17-11-1973 U.S. President Nixon told an Associated Press managing editors meeting in Orlando, FL, "people have got to know whether or not their president is a crook. Well, I'm not a crook."
17-11-1979 Iran's Ayatollah Khomeini ordered the release of 13 female and black American hostages being held at the U.S. Embassy in Tehran.
17-11-1988 Benazir Bhutto became the first woman leader of an Islamic country. She was elected in the first democratic elections in Pakistan in 11 years.
17-11-1990 A mass grave was discovered by the bridge over the River Kwai in Thailand. The bodies were believed to be those of World War II prisoners of war.
17-11-1990 The Soviet government agreed to change the country's constitution.
17-11-1997 62 people were killed by 6 Islamic militants outside the Temple of Hatshepsut in Luxor, Egypt. The attackers were killed by police.
17-11-1997 Mario Lemieux was voted into the NHL Hall of Fame.
17-11-2001 "Toys "R" Us Times Square - The Center of the Toy Universe" opened in New York City.
17-11-2006 Sony's PlayStation 3 went on sale in the United States.
18-11-1477 William Caxton produced "Dictes or Sayengis of the Philosophres," which was the first book to be printed in England.
18-11-1820 Captain Nathaniel Palmer became the first American to sight the continent of Antarctica.
18-11-1865 Samuel L. Clemens published "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County" under the pen name "Mark Twain" in the New York "Saturday Press."
18-11-1883 The U.S. and Canada adopted a system of standard time zones.
18-11-1903 The U.S. and Panama signed a treaty that granted the U.S. rights to build the Panama Canal.
18-11-1928 The first successful sound-synchronized animated cartoon premiered in New York. It was Walt Disney's "Steamboat Willie," starring Mickey Mouse.
18-11-1916 Douglas Haig, commander of the British Expeditionary Force in World War I, calls off the Battle of the Somme in France. The offensive began on July 1, 1916.
18-11-1936 Germany and Italy recognized the Spanish government of Francisco Franco.
18-11-1942 "The Skin of Our Teeth," by Thornton Wilder opened on Broadway.
18-11-1951 Chuck Connors (Los Angeles Angels) became the first player to oppose the major league draft. Connors later became the star of the television show "The Rifleman."
18-11-1959 William Wyler's "Ben-Hur" premiered at Loew's Theater in New York City's Times Square.
18-11-1966 U.S. Roman Catholic bishops did away with the rule against eating meat on Fridays.
18-11-1976 The parliament of Spain approved a bill that established a democracy after 37 years of dictatorship.
18-11-1978 In Jonestown, Guyana, Reverend Jim Jones persuaded his followers to commit suicide by drinking a death potion. Some people were shot to death. 914 cult members were left dead including over 200 children.
18-11-1985 Joe Theismann (Washington Redskins) broke his leg after being hit by Lawrence Taylor (New York Giants). The injury ended Theismann's 12 year National Football League (NFL) career.
18-11-1987 The U.S. Congress issued the Iran-Contra Affair report. The report said that President Ronald Reagan bore "ultimate responsibility" for wrongdoing by his aides.
18-11-1987 31 people died in a fire at King's Cross, London's busiest subway station.
18-11-1987 CBS Inc. announced it had agreed to sell its record division to Sony Corp. for about $2 billion.
18-11-1988 U.S. President Reagan signed major legislation provided the death penalty for drug traffickers who kill.
18-11-1991 Shiite Muslim kidnappers in Lebanon freed Anglican Church envoy Terry Waite and Thomas Sutherland.
18-11-1993 The U.S. House of Representatives joined the U.S. Senate in approving legislation aimed at protecting abortion facilities, staff and patients.
18-11-1993 American Airlines flight attendants went on strike. They ended their strike only 4 days later.
18-11-1993 Representatives from 21 South African political parties approved a new constitution.
18-11-1994 Outside a mosque in the Gaza Strip, 15 people were killed and more than 150 wounded when Palestinian police opened fire on rioting worshipers.
18-11-1997 The FBI officially pulled out of the probe into the TWA Flight 800 disaster. They said the explosion that destroyed the Boeing 747 was not caused by a criminal act. 230 people were killed.
18-11-1997 First Union Corp. announced its purchase of CoreStates Financial Corp. for $16.1 billion. To date it was the largest banking deal in U.S. history.
18-11-1999 12 people were killed and 28 injured when a huge bonfire under construction collapsed at Texas A&M in College Station, TX.
18-11-1999 In Jasper, TX, Shawn Allen Berry was sentenced to life in prison for his role in the racial murder of James Byrd Jr. John William King and Lawrence Russell Brewer both received the death penalty earlier in the year for their roles in the crime.
18-11-2001 Nintendo released the GameCube home video game console in the United States.
19-11-1794 The U.S. and Britain signed the Jay Treaty, which resolved the issues left over from the Revolutionary War.
19-11-1850 The first life insurance policy for a woman was issued. Carolyn Ingraham, 36 years old, bought the policy in Madison, NJ.
19-11-1863 U.S. President Lincoln delivered his Gettysburg Address as he dedicated a national cemetery at the site of the Civil War battlefield in Pennsylvania.
19-11-1893 The first newspaper color supplement was published in the Sunday New York World.
19-11-1895 The "paper pencil" was patented by Frederick E. Blaisdell.
19-11-1919 The U.S. Senate rejected the Treaty of Versailles with a vote of 55 in favor to 39 against. A two-thirds majority was needed for ratification.
19-11-1928 "Time" magazine presented its cover portrait for the first time. Japanese Emperor Hirohito was the magazine's first cover subject.
19-11-1942 During World War II, Russian forces launched their winter offensive against the Germans along the Don front.
19-11-1954 Two automatic toll collectors were placed in service on the Garden State Parkway in New Jersey.
19-11-1959 Ford Motor Co. announced it was ending the production of the unpopular Edsel.
19-11-1966 Sandy Koufax (Los Angeles Dodgers) announced his retirement from major league baseball.
19-11-1969 Apollo 12 astronauts Charles Conrad and Alan Bean made man's second landing on the moon.
19-11-1970 Hafiz al-Assad seized power in Syria.
19-11-1977 Egyptian President Anwar Sadat became the first Arab leader to set foot in Israel on an official visit.
19-11-1979 Nolan Ryan (Houston Astros) signed a four-year contract for $4.5 million. At the time, Ryan was the highest paid player in major league baseball.
19-11-1984 Almost 500 people died in a firestorm after a series of explosions at a Mexico City petroleum storage plant.
19-11-1984 Dwight Gooden, 20-year-old, of the New York Mets, became the youngest major-league pitcher to be named Rookie of the Year in the National League. (MLB)
19-11-1985 U.S. President Reagan and Soviet leader Mikhail S. Gorbachev met for the first time as they began their summit in Geneva.
19-11-1990 NATO and the Warsaw Pact signed a treaty of nonaggression.
19-11-1993 The U.S. Senate approved a sweeping $22.3 billion anti-crime measure.
19-11-1994 The U.N. Security Council authorized NATO to bomb rebel Serb forces striking from neighboring Croatia.
19-11-1997 In Carlisle, IA, septuplets were born to Bobbi McCaughey. It was only the second known case where all seven were born alive.
19-11-1998 The impeachment inquiry of U.S. President Clinton began.
19-11-1998 Vincent van Gogh's "Portrait of the Artist Without Beard" sold at auction for more than $71 million.
