19-11-1998 Michelle Lee received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
19-11-1999 In Istanbul, Turkey, the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) concluded a two-day summit after adopting a new arms accord. During the conference, Russia was criticized for its military campaign against Chechnya's separatist movement.
19-11-2001 U.S. President George W. Bush signed the most comprehensive air security bill in U.S. history.
19-11-2002 The oil tanker Prestige broke into two pieces and sank off northwest Spain. The tanker lost about 2 million gallons of fuel oil when it ruptured November 13th and was towed about 150 miles out to sea.
19-11-2002 The U.S. government completed its takeover of security at 424 airports nationwide.
19-11-2003 Eight competing designs for a memorial to the victims of the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks at the World Trade Center were unveiled. One design would be built at the site of the World Trade Center.
20-11-1620 Peregrine White was born aboard the Mayflower in Massachusetts Bay. White was the first child to be born of English parents in present-day New England.
20-11-1789 New Jersey became the first state to ratify the Bill of Rights.
20-11-1818 Simon Bolivar formally declared Venezuela independent of Spain.
20-11-1873 Budapest was formed when the rival cities of Buda and Pest were united to form the capital of Hungary.
20-11-1889 Astronomer Edwin Hubble was born. Hubble discovered and developed the concept of an expanding universe. In 1924 he proved the existence of galaxies other than our own.
20-11-1901 The second Hay-Pauncefoot Treaty provided for construction of the Panama Canal by the U.S.
20-11-1910 Francisco I. Madero led a revolution that broke out in Mexico.
20-11-1925 Robert Francis Kennedy was born in Brookline, MA.
20-11-1929 The radio program "The Rise of the Goldbergs," later known as "The Goldbergs," made its debut on the NBC Blue Network.
20-11-1943 During World War II, U.S. Marines began their landing on Tarawa and Makin atolls in the Gilbert Islands.
20-11-1945 24 Nazi leaders went before an international war crimes tribunal in Nuremberg, Germany.
20-11-1947 Britain's Princess Elizabeth married Philip Mountbatten, Duke of Edinburgh in Westminster Abbey.
20-11-1959 Britain, Norway, Portugal, Switzerland, Austria, Denmark and Sweden met to create the European Free Trade Association.
20-11-1962 The Cuban Missile Crisis ended. The Soviet Union removed its missiles and bombers from Cuba and the U.S. ended its blockade of the island.
20-11-1962 Mickey Mantle was named the American League Most Valuable Player for the third time.
20-11-1967 The Census Clock at the Department of Commerce in Washington, DC, went past 200 million.
20-11-1969 The Nixon administration announced a halt to residential use of the pesticide DDT as part of a total phase out of the substance.
20-11-1970 The majority in U.N. General Assembly voted to give China a seat, but two-thirds majority required for admission was not met.
20-11-1975 After nearly 40 years of absolute rule Spain's General Francisco Franco died.
20-11-1977 Egyptian President Anwar Sadat became the first Arab leader to address Israel's parliament.
20-11-1983 An estimated 100 million people watched the controversial ABC-TV movie "The Day After." The movie depicted the outbreak of nuclear war.
20-11-1986 Dr. Halfdan Maher, the director of the World Health Organization, announced the first coordinated global effort to fight the disease AIDS.
20-11-1987 Police investigating the fire at King's Cross, London's busiest subway station, said that arson was unlikely to be the cause of the event that took 31 lives.
20-11-1988 Egypt and China announced that they would recognize the Palestinian state proclaimed by the Palestine National Council.
20-11-1989 Over 200,000 people rallied peacefully in Prague, Czechoslovakia, demanding democratic reforms.
20-11-1990 Saddam Hussein ordered another 250,000 Iraqi troops into the country of Kuwait.
20-11-1990 The space shuttle Atlantis landed at Cape Canaveral, FL, after completing a secret military mission.
20-11-1992 A fire seriously damaged the northwest side of Windsor Castle in England.
20-11-1993 The U.S. Senate passed the Brady Bill and legislation implementing NAFTA.
20-11-1994 The Angolan government and rebels signed a treaty in Zambia to end 19 years of war.
20-11-1995 Princess Diana admitted being unfaithful to Prince Charles in an interview that was broadcast on BBC Television.
20-11-1998 Afghanistan's Taliban militia offered Osama bin Laden safe haven. Osama bin Laden had been accused of orchestrating two U.S. embassy bombings in Africa and later terrorist attacks on New York City and the Pentagon.
20-11-1998 Forty-six states agreed to a $206 billion settlement of health claims against the tobacco industry. The industry also agreed to give up billboard advertising of cigarettes.
20-11-2001 The U.S. Justice Department headquarters building was renamed the Robert F. Kennedy building by President George W. Bush. The event was held on what would have been Kennedy's 76th birthday.
21-11-1620 The Mayflower reached Provincetown, MA. The ship discharged the Pilgrims at Plymouth, MA, on December 26, 1620.
21-11-1694 French author and philosopher Jean Francois Voltaire was born. At age 65 he spent only three days writing "Candide."
21-11-1783 The first successful flight was made in a hot air balloon. The pilots, Francois Pilatre de Rosier and Francois Laurent, Marquis d'Arlandes, flew for 25 minutes and 5˝ miles over Paris.
21-11-1789 North Carolina became the 12th state to ratify the U.S. Constitution.
21-11-1871 M.F. Galethe patented the cigar lighter.
21-11-1877 Thomas A. Edison announced the invention of his phonograph.
21-11-1922 Rebecca L. Felton of Georgia was sworn in as the first woman to serve as a member of the U.S. Senate.
21-11-1929 Spanish surrealist Salvador Dali had his first art exhibit.
21-11-1934 The New York Yankees purchased the contract of Joe DiMaggio from San Francisco of the Pacific Coast League.
21-11-1942 The Alaska highway across Canada was formally opened.
21-11-1953 British Natural History Museum authorities announced that "Piltdown Man" was a hoax.
21-11-1962 U.S. President Kennedy terminated the quaratine measures against Cuba.
21-11-1963 U.S. President John F. Kennedy and his wife, Jacqueline, arrived in San Antonio, TX. They were beginning an ill-fated, two-day tour of Texas that would end in Dallas.
21-11-1973 U.S. President Richard M. Nixon's attorney, J. Fred Buzhardt, announced the presence of an 18˝-minute gap in one of the White House tape recordings related to the Watergate case.
21-11-1979 The U.S. Embassy in Islamabad, Pakistan, was attacked by a mob that set the building afire and killed two Americans.
21-11-1980 An estimated 83 million viewers tuned in to find out "who shot J.R." on the CBS prime-time soap opera Dallas. Kristin was the character that fired the gun.
21-11-1980 87 people died in a fire at the MGM Grand Hotel-Casino in Las Vegas, NV.
21-11-1982 The National Football League (NFL) resumed its season following a 57-day player's strike.
21-11-1985 Former U.S. Navy intelligence analyst Jonathan Jay Pollard was arrested after being accused of spying for Israel. He was later sentenced to life in prison.
21-11-1987 An eight-day siege began at a detention center in Oakdale, LA, as Cuban detainees seized the facility and took hostages.
21-11-1989 The proceedings of Britain's House of Commons were televised live for the first time.
21-11-1992 U.S. Senator Bob Packwood, issued an apology but refused to discuss allegations that he'd made unwelcome sexual advances toward 10 women in past years.
21-11-1993 The U.S. House of Representatives voted against making the District of Columbia the 51st state.
21-11-1994 NATO warplanes bombed an air base in Serb-held Croatia that was being used by Serb planes to raid the Bosnian "safe area" of Bihac.
21-11-1995 France detonated its fourth underground nuclear blast at a test site in the South Pacific.
21-11-1995 The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed above the 5,000-mark (5,023.55) for the first time.
21-11-1999 China announced that it had test-launched an unmanned space capsule that was designed for manned spaceflight.
21-11-2000 The Florida Supreme Court granted Al Gore's request to keep the presidential recounts going.
21-11-2001 Microsoft Corp. proposed giving $1 billion in computers, software, training and cash to more than 12,500 of the poorest schools in the U.S. The offer was intended as part of a deal to settle most of the company's private antitrust lawsuits.
21-11-2002 NATO invited Latvia, Estonia, Lithuania, Bulgaria, Romania, Slovakia and Slovenia to become members.
22-11-1699 A treaty was signed by Denmark, Russia, Saxony and Poland for the partitioning of the Swedish Empire.
22-11-1718 English pirate Edward Teach (a.k.a. "Blackbeard") was killed during a battle off the coast of North Carolina. British soldiers cornered him aboard his ship and killed him. He was shot and stabbed more than 25 times.
22-11-1880 Lillian Russell made her vaudeville debut in New York City.
22-11-1899 The Marconi Wireless Company of America was incorporated in New Jersey.
22-11-1906 The International Radio Telegraphic Convention in Berlin adopted the SOS distress signal.
22-11-1909 Helen Hayes appeared on stage for the first time. She was a member of the cast of "In Old Dutch."
22-11-1910 Arthur F. Knight patented a steel shaft to replace wood shafts in golf clubs.
22-11-1917 The National Hockey League (NHL) was officially formed in Montreal, Canada.
22-11-1928 In Paris, "Bolero" by Maurice Ravel was first performed publicly.
22-11-1935 The first trans-Pacific airmail flight began in Alameda, CA, when the flying boat known as the China Clipper left for Manila. The craft was carrying over 110,000 pieces of mail.
22-11-1942 During World War II, the Battle of Stalingrad began.
22-11-1943 U.S. President Roosevelt, British Prime Minister Winston Churchill and Chinese leader Chiang Kai-shek met in Cairo to discuss the measures for defeating Japan.
22-11-1950 The lowest scoring game in the NBA was played. The Fort Wayne Pistons (later the Detroit Pistons) defeated the Minneapolis Lakers (later the Los Angeles Lakers) 19-18.
22-11-1961 The film, "A Man for All Seasons", opened in New York City.
22-11-1963 U.S. President Kennedy was assassinated while riding in a motorcade in Dallas, TX. Texas Governor John B. Connally was also seriously wounded. Vice-President Lyndon B. Johnson was inaugurated as the 36th U.S. President.
22-11-1967 The U.N. Security Council approved resolution 242. The resolution called for Israel to withdraw from territories it had captured in 1967 and called on adversaries to recognize Israel's right to exist.
22-11-1972 U.S. President Richard M. Nixon lifted a ban on American travel to Cuba. The ban had been put in place on February 8, 1963.
22-11-1974 The U.N. General Assembly gave the Palestine Liberation Organization observer status.
22-11-1975 Juan Carlos I was proclaimed King of Spain upon the death of Gen. Francisco Franco.
22-11-1975 "Dr. Zhivago" appeared on TV for the first time. NBC paid $4 million for the broadcast rights.
22-11-1977 Regular passenger service on the Concorde began between New York and Europe.
22-11-1983 The Bundestag approved NATO's plan to deploy new U.S. nuclear missiles in West Germany.
22-11-1984 Fred Rogers of PBS' "Mr. Rogers' Neighborhood" presented a sweater to the Smithsonian Institution.
22-11-1985 Anne Henderson-Pollard was taken into custody a day after her husband Jonathon Jay Pollard was arrested for spying for Israel.
22-11-1985 38,648 immigrants became citizens of the United States. It was the largest swearing-in ceremony.
22-11-1986 An Iranian surface-to-surface missile hit a residential area in the Iraqi capital of Baghdad, wounding 20 civilians.
22-11-1986 Mike Tyson became the youngest to wear the world heavyweight-boxing crown. He was only 20 years and 4 months old.
22-11-1988 The South African government announced it had joined Cuba and Angola in endorsing a plan to remove Cuban troops from Angola.
22-11-1989 Rene Moawad, the president of Lebanon, was assassinated less than three weeks after taking office by a bomb that exploded next to his motorcade in West Beirut.
22-11-1990 U.S. President Bush, his wife, Barbara, and other congressional leaders shared Thanksgiving dinner with U.S. troops in Saudi Arabia.
22-11-1990 British Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher announced she would resign.
22-11-1993 Mexico's Senate overwhelmingly approved the North American Free Trade Agreement.
22-11-1993 American Airlines flight attendants ended their strike that only lasted four days.
22-11-1994 Inside the District of Columbia's police headquarters a gunman opened fire. Two FBI agents, a city detective and the gunman were killed in the gun battle.
22-11-1994 In northwest Bosnia, Serb fighters set villages on fire in response to a retaliatory air strikes by NATO.
22-11-1998 CBS's "60 Minutes" aired a tape of Jack Kevorkian giving lethal drugs in an assisted suicide of a terminally ill patient. Kevorkian was later sentenced to 25 years in prison for second-degree murder.
22-11-2005 Angela Merkel was elected as Germany's first female chancellor.
22-11-2005 Microsoft's XBOX 360 went on sale.
23-11-1765 Frederick County, MD, repudiated the British Stamp Act.
23-11-1835 Henry Burden patented the horseshoe manufacturing machine.
23-11-1889 The first jukebox made its debut in San Francisco, at the Palais Royale Saloon.
23-11-1890 Princess Wilhelmina became Queen of the Netherlands at the age of 10 when her father William III died.
23-11-1936 The first edition of "Life" was published.
23-11-1943 During World War II, U.S. forces seized control of Tarawa and Makin from the Japanese during the Central Pacific offensive in the Gilbert Islands.
23-11-1945 The U.S. wartime rationing of most foods ended.
23-11-1948 Dr. Frank G. Back patented the "Zoomar" lens.
23-11-1961 The Dominican Republic changed the name of its capital from Ciudad Trujillo to Santo Domingo.
23-11-1971 The People's Republic of China was seated in the United Nations Security Council.
23-11-1979 In Dublin, Ireland, Thomas McMahon was sentenced to life imprisonment for the assassination of Earl Mountbatten.
23-11-1980 In southern Italy, approximately 4,800 people were killed in a series of earthquakes.
23-11-1985 Larry Wu-tai Chin, a retired CIA analyst, was arrested and accused of spying for China. He committed suicide a year after his conviction.
23-11-1985 Gunmen hijacked an Egyptian jetliner en route from Athens to Cairo. The plane was forced to land in Malta.
23-11-1986 In Manila, President Aquino dismissed Defense Minister Enrile.
23-11-1988 Wayne Gretzky scored his 600th National Hockey League (NHL) goal.
23-11-1989 Lucia Barrera de Cerna, a housekeeper who claimed she had witnessed the slaying of six Jesuit priests and two other people at the Jose Simeon Canas University in El Salvador, was flown to the U.S.
23-11-1991 Yugoslavia's rival leaders agreed to a new cease-fire, the 14th of the Balkan civil war.
23-11-1991 The Sacramento Kings ended the NBA's longest road losing streak at 43 games.
23-11-1992 The play "Someone Who'll Watch Over Me" opened.
23-11-1994 About 111 people, mostly women and children, were killed in a stampede after Indian police baton-charged tribal protesters in the western city of Nagpur.
23-11-1995 Charles Rathbun, free-lance photographer, was booked in Hermosa Beach, CA, for investigation of murder in the disappearance of model Linda Sobek. He was later convicted.
23-11-1998 Dennis Rodman filed for an annulment from Carmen Electra. The two had been married on November 14, 1998.
23-11-1998 The tobacco industry signed the biggest U.S. civil settlement. It was a $206-billion deal to resolve remaining state claims for treating sick smokers.
23-11-1998 A U.S. federal judge rejected a Virginia county's effort to block pornography on library computer calling the attempt unconstitutional.
23-11-2001 A crowd of 87,555 people watched the Texas Longhorns beat the Texas A&M Aggies 21-7. The crowd was the largest to see a football game in Texas.
24-11-1615 French King Louis XIII married Ann of Austria. They were both 14 years old.
24-11-1859 Charles Darwin, a British naturalist, published "On the Origin of Species." It was the paper in which he explained his theory of evolution through the process of natural selection.
24-11-1863 During the Civil War, the battle for Lookout Mountain began in Tennessee.
24-11-1871 The National Rifle Association was incorporated in the U.S.
24-11-1903 Clyde J. Coleman received the patent for an electric self-starter for an automobile.
24-11-1940 Nazis closed off the Jewish ghetto in Warsaw, Poland. Over the next three years the population dropped from 350,000 to 70,000 due to starvation, disease and deportations to concentration camps.
24-11-1944 During World War II, the first raid against the Japanese capital of Tokyo was made by land-based U.S. bombers.
24-11-1947 The "Hollywood 10," were cited for contempt of Congress for refusing to answer questions about alleged Communist influence in their industry.
24-11-1947 John Steinbeck's novel "The Pearl" was published for the first time.
24-11-1963 Dallas nightclub owner Jack Ruby shot and killed Lee Harvey Oswald live on national television.
24-11-1969 Apollo 12 landed safely in the Pacific Ocean bringing an end to the second manned mission to the moon.
24-11-1971 Hijacker Dan Cooper, known as D.B. Cooper, parachuted from a Northwest Airlines 727 over Washington state with $200,000 in ransom.
24-11-1983 The Palestine Liberation Organization released six Israeli prisoners in exchange for the release of 4,500 Palestinians and Lebanese held by the Israelis.
24-11-1985 In Malta, Egyptian commandos stormed an Egyptian jetliner. 60 people died in the raid.
24-11-1987 The U.S. and the Soviet Union agreed to scrap short- and medium-range missiles. It was the first superpower treaty to eliminate an entire class of nuclear weapons.
24-11-1989 Czechoslovakia's hard-line party leadership resigned after more than a week of protests against its policies.
24-11-1992 In China, a domestic jetliner crashed, killing 141 people.
24-11-1993 The U.S. Congress gave its final approval to the Brady handgun control bill.
24-11-1993 Robert Thompson and Jon Venables (both 11 years old) were convicted of murdering 2-year-old James Bulger of Liverpool, England. They were both sentenced to "indefinite detention."
24-11-1995 In Ireland, the voters narrowly approved a constitutional amendment legalizing divorce.
24-11-1996 Rusty Wallace won the first NASCAR event to be held in Japan.
24-11-1996 Barry Sanders (Detroit Lions) set an NFL record when he recorded his eighth straight 1,000-yard season.
24-11-1998 AOL (America Online) announced a deal for their purchase of Netscape for $4.21 billion.
25-11-1715 Sybilla Thomas Masters became the first American to be granted an English patent for cleaning and curing Indian corn.
25-11-1758 During the French and Indian War, the British captured Fort Duquesne at what is now known as Pittsburgh.
25-11-1783 During the Revolutionary War, the British evacuated New York. New York was their last military position in the U.S.
25-11-1837 William Crompton patented the silk power loom.
25-11-1850 Texas relinquished one-third of its territory in exchange for $10 million from the U.S. to pay its public debts and settle border disputes.
25-11-1867 Alfred Nobel patented dynamite.
25-11-1882 The first of 400 performances of "lolnathe" took place.
25-11-1884 J.B. Meyenberg received the patent for evaporated milk.
25-11-1920 The first play-by-play broadcast of a football game was aired in College Station, TX. The game was between the University of Texas and Texas A&M.
25-11-1936 The Anti-Comintern Pact, an agreement between Japan and Germany, was signed.
25-11-1947 Movie studio executives meeting in New York agreed to blacklist the "Hollywood 10," who were cited a day earlier and jailed for contempt of Congress when they failed to cooperate with the House Un-American Activities Committee.
25-11-1952 Agatha Christie's "The Mousetrap" opened in London.
25-11-1955 In the U.S., the Interstate Commerce Commission banned racial segregation on interstate trains and buses.
25-11-1957 U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower suffered a stroke.
25-11-1970 Japanese author Yukio Mishima committed ritual suicide after giving a speech attacking Japan's post-war constitution.
25-11-1973 Greek President George Papadapoulos was ousted in military coup.
25-11-1976 O.J. Simpson (Buffalo Bills) ran for 273 yards against the Detroit Lions.
25-11-1983 Mediators from Syria and Saudi Arabia announced a cease-fire in the PLO civil war in Tripoli, Lebanon.
25-11-1985 Ronald W. Pelton was arrested on espionage charges. Pelton was a former employee of the National Security Agency. He was later convicted of 'selling secrets' to Soviet agents.
25-11-1986 U.S. President Reagan and Attorney Gen. Edwin Meese revealed that profits from secret arms sales to Iran had been diverted to rebels in Nicaragua.
25-11-1990 Poland held its first popular presidential election.
25-11-1992 The Czech parliament voted to split the country into separate Czech and Slovak republics beginning January 1, 1993.
25-11-1993 Egyptian Prime Minister Atef Sedki escaped an attempt on his life when a bomb was detonated by Islamic militants near his motorcade.
25-11-1995 Serbs protested in the streets of the Bosnian capital Sarajevo The protest was against a peace plan.
25-11-1998 Britain's highest court ruled that former Chilean dictator Augusto Pinochet, whose extradition was being sought by Spain, could not claim immunity from prosecution for the crimes he committed during his rule.
25-11-1998 President Jiang Zemin arrived in Tokyo for the first visit to Japan by a Chinese head of state since World War II.
25-11-1998 The IMF (International Monetary Fund) approved a $5.5 billion bailout for Pakistan.
26-11-1716 The first lion to be exhibited in America went on display in Boston, MA.
26-11-1731 English poet William Cowper was born. He is best known for "The Poplar Trees" and "The Task."
26-11-1789 U.S. President Washington set aside this day to observe the adoption of the Constitution of the United States.
26-11-1825 The first college social fraternity, Kappa Alpha, was formed at Union College in Schenectady, NY.
26-11-1832 Public streetcar service began in New York City.
26-11-1861 West Virginia was created (out of Virginia) over a dispute of slavery. West Virginia was against slavery.
26-11-1867 J.B. Sutherland patented the refrigerated railroad car.
26-11-1922 In Egypt, Howard Carter peered into the tomb of King Tutankhamen.
26-11-1940 The Nazis forced 500,000 Jews of Warsaw, Poland to live within a walled ghetto.
26-11-1941 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed a bill establishing the fourth Thursday in November as Thanksgiving Day. In 1939 Roosevelt had signed a bill that changed the celebration of Thanksgiving to the third Thursday of November.
26-11-1942 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt ordered nationwide gasoline rationing to begin December 1.
26-11-1942 The motion picture "Casablanca" had its world premiere at the Hollywood Theater in New York City.
26-11-1943 The HMS Rohna became the first ship to be sunk by a guided missile. The German missile attack led to the death of 1,015 U.S. troops.
26-11-1949 India's Constituent Assembly adopted the country's constitution The country became republic within the British Commonwealth two months later.
26-11-1950 China entered the Korean conflict forcing UN forces to retreat.
26-11-1958 Maurice Richard (Montreal Canadiens) scored his 600th NHL career goal.
26-11-1965 France became the third country to enter space when it launched its first satellite the Diamant-A.
26-11-1973 Rose Mary Woods, told a federal court that she was responsible for the 18-1/2 minute gap in a key Watergate tape. Woods was U.S. President Nixon's personal secretary.
26-11-1975 Lynette"Squeaky" Fromme was found guilty by a federal jury in Sacramento, CA, for trying to assassinate U.S. President Ford on September 5.
26-11-1979 The International Olympic Committee voted to re-admit China after a 21-year absence.
26-11-1983 A Brinks Mat Ltd. vault at London's Heathrow Airport was robbed by gunmen. The men made off with 6,800 gold bars worth nearly $40 million. Only a fraction of the gold has ever been recovered and only two men have been convicted in the heist.
26-11-1985 The rights to Ronald Reagan's autobiography were acquired by Random House for $3,000,000.
26-11-1986 U.S. President Reagan appointed a commission headed by former Sen. John Tower to investigate his National Security Council staff after the Iran-Contra affair.
26-11-1988 The U.S. denied an entry visa to PLO chairman Yasser Arafat, who was seeking permission to travel to New York to address the U.N. General Assembly.
26-11-1990 Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev met with Iraqi Foreign Minister Tariq Aziz at the Kremlin to demand that Iraq withdraw from Kuwait.
26-11-1990 Matsushita Electric Industrial Co. agreed to acquire MCA Inc. for $6.6 billion.
26-11-1992 The British government announced that Queen Elizabeth II had volunteered to start paying taxes on her personal income. She also took her children off the public payroll.
26-11-1995 Two men set fire to a subway token booth in the Brooklyn borough of New York City. The clerk inside was fatally burned.
26-11-1997 The U.S. and North Korea held high-level discussions at the State Department for the first time.
26-11-1998 British Prime Minister Toney Blair made a speech to the Irish Parliament. It was a first time event for a British Prime Minister.
26-11-1998 Hulk Hogan announced that he was retiring from pro wrestling and would run for president in 2000.
26-11-2003 The U.N. atomic agency adopted a resolution that censured Iran for past nuclear cover-ups and warning that it would be policed to put to rest suspicions that the country had a weapons agenda.
27-11-1684 Japan's shogun Yoshimune Tokugawa was born.
27-11-1701 Anders Celsius was born in Sweden. He was the inventor of the Celsius thermometer.
27-11-1779 The College of Pennsylvania became the University of Pennsylvania. It was the first legally recognized university in America.
27-11-1839 The American Statistical Association was founded in Boston.
27-11-1889 Curtis P. Brady was issued the first permit to drive an automobile through Central Park in New York City.
27-11-1901 The Army War College was established in Washington, DC.
27-11-1910 New York's Pennsylvania Station opened.
27-11-1939 The play "Key Largo," by Maxwell Anderson, opened in New York.
27-11-1951 Hosea Richardson became the first black horse racing jockey to be licensed in Florida.
27-11-1963 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson delivered his first address to a joint session of Congress.
27-11-1970 Pope Paul VI, visiting the Philippines, was attacked at the Manila airport by a Bolivian painter disguised as a priest.
27-11-1973 The U.S. Senate voted to confirm Gerald R. Ford as vice president after the resignation of Spiro T. Agnew.
27-11-1978 San Francisco Mayor George Moscone and City Supervisor Harvey Milk, a gay-rights activist, were shot to death inside City Hall by Dan White, a former supervisor.
27-11-1980 Dave Williams (Chicago Bears) became the first player in NFL history to return a kick for touchdown in overtime.
27-11-1983 183 people were killed when a Colombian Avianca Airlines Boeing 747 crashed near Barajas airport in Madrid.
27-11-1985 The British House of Commons approved the Anglo-Irish accord giving Dublin a consulting role in the governing of British-ruled Northern Ireland.
27-11-1987 French hostages Jean-Louis Normandin and Roger Auque were set free by their pro-Iranian captors in West Beirut, Lebanon.
27-11-1989 107 people were killed when a bomb destroyed a Colombian jetliner minutes after the plane had taken off from Bogota's international airport. Police blamed the incident on drug traffickers.
27-11-1991 The UN Security Council unanimously adopted a resolution that led the way for the establishment of a UN peacekeeping operation in Yugoslavia.
27-11-1992 In Venezuela, rebel forces tried but failed to overthrow President Carlos Andres Perez for the second time in ten months.
28-11-1520 Portuguese navigator Ferdinand Magellan reached the Pacific Ocean after passing through the South American strait. The strait was named after him. He was the first European to sail the Pacific from the east.
28-11-1582 William Shakespeare and Anne Hathaway were married.
28-11-1757 English poet, painter and engraver William Blake was born. Two of his best known works are "Songs of Innocence" and "Songs of Experience."
28-11-1919 American-born Lady Astor was elected the first female member of the British Parliament.
28-11-1922 Capt. Cyril Turner of the Royal Air Force gave the first public exhibition of skywriting. He spelled out, "Hello USA. Call Vanderbilt 7200" over New York's Times Square.
28-11-1925 The Grand Ole Opry made its radio debut on station WSM.
28-11-1934 The U.S. bank robber George "Baby Face" Nelson was killed by FBI agents near Barrington, IL.
28-11-1942 491 people died in a fire that destroyed the Coconut Grove in Boston.
28-11-1943 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt, British Prime Minister Winston Churchill and Soviet Leader Joseph Stalin met in Tehran to map out strategy concerning World War II.
28-11-1953 New York City began 11 days without newspapers due to a strike of photoengravers.
28-11-1958 The African nation of Chad became an autonomous republic within the French community.
28-11-1963 U.S. President Johnson announced that Cape Canaveral would be renamed Cape Kennedy in honor of his assassinated predecessor. The name was changed back to Cape Canaveral in 1973 by a vote of residents.
28-11-1964 The U.S. launched the space probe Mariner IV from Cape Kennedy on a course set for Mars.
28-11-1977 Larry Bird was introduced as "College Basketball's Secret Weapon" with a cover story in Sports Illustrated. (NBA)
28-11-1978 The Iranian government banned religious marches.
28-11-1979 An Air New Zealand DC-10 flying to the South Pole crashed in Antarctica killing all 257 people aboard.
28-11-1985 The Irish Senate approved the Anglo-Irish accord concerning Northern Ireland.
28-11-1987 A South African Airways Boeing 747 crashed into the Indian Ocean. All 159 people aboard were killed.
28-11-1989 Romanian gymnast Nadia Comaneci arrived in New York after escaping her homeland through Hungary.
28-11-1990 Margaret Thatcher resigned as prime minister of Britain.
28-11-1992 In Bosnia-Herzegovina, 137 tons of food and supplies were to be delivered to the isolated town of Srebrenica.
28-11-1992 In King William's Town, South Africa, black militant gunmen attacked a country club killing four people and injuring 20.
28-11-1993 The play "Mixed Emotions" closed after 48 performances.
28-11-1994 Jeffrey Dahmer, a convicted serial killer, was clubbed to death in a Wisconsin prison by a fellow inmate.
28-11-1994 Norwegian voters rejected European Union membership.
28-11-1995 U.S. President Clinton signed a $6 billion road bill that ended the federal 55 mph speed limit.
29-11-1530 Cardinal Thomas Wolsey, former adviser to England's King Henry VIII, died.
29-11-1864 The Sand Creek Massacre occurred in Colorado when a militia led by Colonel John Chivington, killed at least 400 peaceful Cheyenne and Arapaho Indians who had surrendered and had been given permission to camp.
29-11-1890 Navy defeated Army by a score of 24-0 in the first Army-Navy football game. The game was played at West Point, NY.
29-11-1929 The first airplane flight over the South Pole was made by U.S. Navy Lt. Comdr. Richard E. Byrd.
29-11-1939 The USSR broke off diplomatic relations with Finland prior to a Soviet attack.
29-11-1945 The monarchy was abolished in Yugoslavia and a republic proclaimed.
29-11-1947 The U.N. General Assembly passed a resolution that called for the division of Palestine between Arabs and Jews.
29-11-1961 The Mercury-Atlas 5 spacecraft was launched by the U.S. with Enos the chimp on board. The craft orbited the earth twice before landing off Puerto Rico.
29-11-1963 A Trans-Canada Airlines DC-8F with 111 passengers and 7 crew members crashed in woods north of Montreal 4 minutes after takeoff from Dorval Airport. All aboard were killed. The crash was the worst in Canada's history.
29-11-1963 U.S. President Johnson named a commission headed by Earl Warren to investigate the assassination of President Kennedy.
29-11-1967 U.S. Secretary of Defense Robert S. McNamara announed that he was leaving the Johnson administration to become president of the World Bank.
29-11-1971 The Professional Golf Championship was held at Walt Disney World for the first time.
29-11-1974 In Britain, a bill that outlawed the Irish Republican Army became effective.
29-11-1975 Bill Gates adopted the name Microsoft for the company he and Paul Allen had formed to write the BASIC computer language for the Altair.
29-11-1981 Actress Natalie Wood drowned in a boating accident off Santa Catalina Island, CA, at the age 43.
29-11-1982 The U.N. General Assembly voted that the Soviet Union should withdraw its troops from Afghanistan.1986- Actor Cary Grant died at the age of 82.
29-11-1987 A Korean jetliner disappeared off Burma, with 115 people aboard.
29-11-1987 Cuban detainees released 26 hostages they'd been holding for more than a week at the Federal Detention Center in Oakdale, LA.
29-11-1988 The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the rights of criminal defendants are not violated when police unintentionally fail to preserve potentially vital evidence.
29-11-1989 In Czechoslovakia, the Communist-run parliament ended the party's 40-year monopoly on power.
29-11-1990 The U.N. Security Council voted to authorize military action if Iraq did not withdraw its troops from Kuwait and release all foreign hostages by January 15, 1991.
29-11-1991 17 people were killed in a 164-vehicle wreck during a dust storm near Coalinga, CA, on Interstate 5.
29-11-1992 Dennis Byrd (New York Jets) was paralyzed after a neck injury in a game against the Kansas City Chiefs.
29-11-1994 The U.S. House passed the revised General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade.
29-11-1994 Fighter jets attacked the capital of Chechnya and its airport only hours after Russian President Boris Yeltsin demanded the breakaway republic end its civil war.
29-11-1996 A U.N. court sentenced Bosnian Serb army soldier Drazen Erdemovic to 10 years in prison for his role in the massacre of 1,200 Muslims. The sentence was the first international war crimes sentence since World War II.
29-11-1998 Swiss voters overwhelmingly rejected legalizing heroin and other narcotics.
29-11-2004 The French government announced plans to build the Louvre II in northern France. The 236,808 square foot museum was the planned home for 500-600 works from the Louvre's reserves.
29-11-2004 Godzilla received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
30-11-1016 English King Edmund II died.
30-11-1700 8,000 Swedish troops under King Charles XII defeated an army of at least 50,000 Russians at the Battle of Narva. King Charles XII died on this day.
30-11-1782 The United States and Britain signed preliminary peace articles in Paris, ending the Revolutionary War.
30-11-1803 Spain completed the process of ceding Louisiana to France.
30-11-1804 U.S. Supreme Court Justice Samuel Chase went on trial accused of political bias. He was later acquitted by the U.S. Senate.
30-11-1835 Samuel Langhorne Clemens was born. He wrote "Tom Sawyer" and "Huckleberry Finn" under the name Mark Twain.
30-11-1838 Three days after the French occupation of Vera Cruz Mexico declared war on France.
30-11-1853 During the Crimean War, the Russian fleet attacked and destroyed the Turkish fleet at the battle of Sinope.
30-11-1875 A.J. Ehrichson patented the oat-crushing machine.
30-11-1897 Thomas Edison's own motion picture projector had its first commercial exhibition.
30-11-1936 London's famed Crystal Palace was destroyed in a fire. The structure had been constructed for the International Exhibition of 1851.
30-11-1939 The Russo-Finnish War began when 20 divisions of Soviet troops invaded Finland.
30-11-1940 Lucille Ball and Cuban musician Desi Arnaz were married.
30-11-1949 Chinese Communists captured Chungking.
30-11-1954 In Sylacauga, AL, Elizabeth Hodges was injured when a meteorite crashed through the roof of her house. The rock weighed 8˝-pounds.
30-11-1956 CBS replayed the program "Douglas Edward and the News" three hours after it was received on the West Coast. It was the world's first broadcast via videotape.
30-11-1962 U Thant of Burma was elected secretary-general of the United Nations, succeeding the late Dag Hammarskjold.
30-11-1966 The former British colony of Barbados became independent.
30-11-1967 Julie Nixon and David Eisenhower announced their engagement.
30-11-1971 ABC-TV aired "Brian's Song." The movie was about Chicago Bears' Brian Picolo and his friendship with Gayle Sayers.
30-11-1981 The U.S. and the Soviet Union opened negotiations in Geneva that were aimed at reducing nuclear weapons in Europe.
30-11-1982 The motion picture "Ghandi" had its world premiere in New Delhi.
30-11-1986 "Time" magazine published an interview with U.S. President Reagan. In the article, Reagan described fired national security staffer Oliver North as a "national hero."
30-11-1988 Kohlberg Kravis Roberts and Co. took over RJR Nabisco Inc. with a bid of $24.53 billion.
30-11-1989 Alfred Herrhausen was killed in a bombing. The Red Army Faction claimed responsibility of killing Herrhausen the chairman of West Germany's largest bank.
30-11-1989 PLO leader Yasser Arafat was refused a visa to enter the United States in order to address the U.N. General Assebly in New York City.
30-11-1993 U.S. President Clinton signed into law the Brady Bill. The bill required a five-day waiting period for handgun purchases and background checks of prospective buyers.
30-11-1993 Richard Allen Davis was arrested by authorities in California. Davis confessed to abducting and slaying 12-year-old Polly Klaas of Petaluma.
30-11-1995 President Clinton became the first U.S. chief executive to visit Northern Ireland.
30-11-1998 The Deutsche Bank AG announced that it would acquire Bankers Trust Corp. for $10.1 billion creating the world's largest financial institution.
30-11-2000 David Spade was assaulted with a stun gun by his longtime personal assistant, David Warren Malloy. Malloy attacked Spade during a burglary of Spade's home in Beverly Hills.
30-11-2001 For the first time in its history, McDonald's teamed up with a retail partner on its Happy Meal promotions. Toys R Us provided plush figures from its Animal Alley.
30-11-2001 In Seattle, WA, Gary Leon Ridgeway was arrested for four of the Green River serial killings. He was pled innocent on December 18, 2001.
30-11-2004 In Stockholm, Sweden, the Carl Larsson painting "Boenskoerd" ("Bean Harvest") was sold at auction for $730,000. The work had been in a private collection for more than a century. The Larsson work "Vid Kattegatt" ("By Kattegatt") sold for $640,000 at the same auction.
01-10-1596 The Duke of Norfolk was imprisoned by Britain's Queen Elizabeth for trying to marry Mary the Queen of Scots.
01-10-1781 James Lawrence was born. He was the American naval officer whose dying words were "Don't give up the ship."
01-10-1800 Spain ceded the territory of Louisiana back to France. Later the property would be purchased by the U.S. effectively doubling its size.
01-10-1880 Thomas Edison began the commercial production of electric lamps at Edison Lamp Works in Menlo Park.
01-10-1885 Special delivery mail service began in the United States. The first routes were in West Virginia.
01-10-1890 The U.S. Congress passed the McKinley Tariff Act. The act raised tariffs to a record level.
01-10-1896 Rural Free Delivery was established by the U.S. Post Office.
01-10-1903 The first modern World Series took place between the Boston Pilgrims and the Pittsburgh Pirates.
01-10-1908 The Model T automobile was introduced by Henry Ford. The purchase price of the car was $850.
01-10-1918 Damascus was captured from the Turks during World War I by a force made up of British and Arab forces.
01-10-1933 Babe Ruth made his final pitching appearance. He pitched all nine innings and hit a home run in the 5th inning.
01-10-1936 General Francisco Franco was proclaimed the head of the Spanish state.
01-10-1938 German forces enter Czechoslovakia and seized control of the Sudetenland. The Munich Pact had been signed two days before.
01-10-1940 The Pennsylvania Turnpike opened as the first toll superhighway in the United States.
01-10-1943 Naples was captured by the Allied forces during World War II.
01-10-1946 The International War Crimes Tribunal in Nuremberg sentenced 12 Nazi officials to death. Seven others were sentenced to prison terms and 3 were acquitted.
01-10-1946 The first baseball play-off game for a league championship was played. The St. Louis Cardinals defeated the Brooklyn Dodgers, 4-2.
01-10-1949 Mao Tse-tung raised the first flag of the People's Republic of China when the communist forces had defeated the Nationalists. The Nationalist forces fled to Taiwan.
01-10-1952 "This is Your Life" began airing on NBC-TV.
01-10-1961 Roger Maris of the New York Yankees hit his 61st home run of the season to beat Babe Ruth's major league record of 60.
01-10-1962 Johnny Carson began hosting the "Tonight" show on NBC-TV. He stayed with the show for 29 years. Jack Paar was the previous host.
01-10-1964 The Free Speech Movement was started at the University of California at Berkeley.
01-10-1968 "Night of the Living Dead" premiered in Pittsburgh, PA.
01-10-1971 Walt Disney World opened in Orlando, FL.
01-10-1972 The Chinese government approved friendly relations with the United States.
01-10-1979 The United States handed control of the Canal Zone over to Panama.
01-10-1980 Robert Redford became the first male to appear alone on the cover of "Ladies' Home Journal." He was the only male to achieve this in 97 years.
01-10-1981 EPCOT (Experimental Prototype Community of Tomorrow) Center opened in Florida. The concept was planned by Walt Disney.
01-10-1984 U.S. Labor Secretary Raymond Donovan announced that he was taking a leave of absence following his indictment on charges of larceny and fraud. He was later acquitted.
01-10-1985 The PLO's headquarters in Tunisia was raided by Israeli jet fighters.
01-10-1987 Eight people were killed in Los Angeles when an earthquake measuring 5.9 on the Richter Scale hit the area.
01-10-1988 Mikhail Gorbachev assumed the Soviet presidency.
01-10-1989 The authorized Charles Schulz biography, Good Grief, was published.
01-10-1989 7,000 East Germans were welcomed into West Germany after they were allowed to leave by the communist government.
01-10-1990 U.S. President Bush addressed the U.N. General Assembly and once again condemned Iraq's takeover of Kuwait.
01-10-1990 In Croatia, minority Serbs proclaimed autonomy.
01-10-1991 U.S. President Bush condemned the military coup in Haiti that removed President Jean-Bertrand Aristide from power. U.S. economic and military aid was suspended.
01-10-1991 The U.S. trust territory of Palau became independent.
01-10-1992 The USS Saratoga accidentally fired missiles at a Turkish destroyer in the Aegean Sea. Five people were killed in the incident.
01-10-1992 The Strategic Arm Reduction Treaty was approved by the U.S. Senate.
01-10-1993 12-year-old Polly Klaas was abducted from her home in Petaluma, CA, by an intruder.
01-10-1994 The U.S. and Japan avoided a trade war by reaching a series of trade agreements.
01-10-1994 The National Hockey League (NHL) team owners began a lockout of the players that lasted 103 days.
01-10-1995 Sheik Omar Abdel-Rahman and nine other defendants were convicted in New York of conspiring to attack the U.S. through bombings, kidnappings and assassinations.
01-10-1995 Southwestern Turkey experienced an earthquake that killed about 90 people.
01-10-1996 Lucent Technologies became an independent company.
01-10-1996 A federal grand jury indicted Unabomber suspect Theodore Kaczynski in the 1994 mail bomb murder of an ad executive.
01-10-1998 The U.S. government posted a $2.2 million reward for the capture of Augustin Vasquez Mendoza. He is accused of killing an undercover U.S. agent during a drug purchase in 1994.
01-10-1999 The 50th anniversary of the founding of the Peoples Republic of China was celebrated in Beijing.
01-10-2001 San Francisco's Board of Supervisors voted unanimously to ban Internet filters designed to keep pornography away from children at city libraries. The board left the decision up to the Library Commission to decide whether to install filtering software in children's areas. A federal law in the U.S. mandated the use of the filters.
02-10-1452 Richard III was born. He married the widow of the Prince of Wales and then imprisoned his mother-in-law for life.
02-10-1492 King Henry VII of England invaded France.
02-10-1780 British army major John Andre was hanged as a spy. He was carrying information about the actions of Benedict Arnold.
02-10-1835 The first battle of the Texas Revolution took place near the Guadalupe River when American settlers defeated a Mexican cavalry unit.
02-10-1836 Charles Darwin returned to England after 5 years of acquiring knowledge around the world about fauna, flora, wildlife and geology. He used the information to develop his "theory of evolution" which he unveiled in his 1859 book entitled The Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection.
02-10-1869 Mahatma (Mohandas) K Gandhi was born. He was known for his advocacy of non-violent resistance to fight tyranny.
02-10-1870 Rome was made the capital of Italy.
02-10-1876 The Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas opened. It was the state's first venture into public higher education. The school was formally dedicated 2 days later by Texas Gov. Richard Coke.
02-10-1889 The first international Conference of American States began in Washington, DC.
02-10-1890 Groucho Marx was born in New York. He is known for the "Marx Brothers" movies and his quiz show "You Bet Your Life."
02-10-1895 Ruth Cheney Streeter was born. She became the first director of the U.S. Marine Corps Women's Reserve.
02-10-1908 Addie Joss of Cleveland pitched the fourth perfect game in major league baseball history.
02-10-1919 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson suffered a stroke that left him partially paralyzed.
02-10-1920 The Cincinnati Reds and the Pittsburgh Pirates played the only triple-header in baseball history. The Reds won 2 of the 3 games.
02-10-1924 The Geneva Protocol adopted the League of Nations.
02-10-1925 Scottish inventor John Logie Baird completed the first transmission of moving images.
02-10-1929 "The National Farm and Home Hour" debuted on NBC radio.
02-10-1933 "Red Adams" debuted on NBC radio.
02-10-1937 Warner Bros. released "Love Is on the Air." Ronald Reagan made his acting debut in the motion picture. He was 26 years old.
02-10-1940 During World War II, the HMS Empress was sunk while carrying child refugees from Britain to Canada.
02-10-1941 Operation Typhoon was launched by Nazi Germany. The plan was an all-out offensive against Moscow.
02-10-1944 The Nazis crushed the Warsaw Uprising.
02-10-1947 The Federatino Internationale de l'Automobile (FIA) formally established Formula One racing in Grand Prix competition.
02-10-1948 The first automobile race to use asphalt, cement and dirt roads took place in Watkins Glen in New York. It was the first road race in the U.S. following World War II.
02-10-1949 "The Aldrich Family" debuted on NBC-TV.
02-10-1950 "Peanuts," the comic strip created by Charles M. Schulz, was published for the first time in seven newspapers.
02-10-1953 "Person to Person" debuted on CBS-TV.
02-10-1955 "Alfred Hitchcock Presents" debuted on CBS-TV.
02-10-1958 Guinea, the French colony in West Africa, proclaimed its independence. Sekou Toure was the first president of the Republic of Guinea.
02-10-1959 "The Twilight Zone" debuted on CBS-TV. The show ran for 5 years for a total of 154 episodes.
02-10-1962 U.S. ports were closed to nations that allowed their ships to carry arms to Cuba, ships that had docked in a socialist country were prohibited from docking in the United States during that voyage, and the transport of U.S. goods was banned on ships owned by companies that traded with Cuba.
02-10-1967 Thurgood Marshall was sworn in. He was the first African-American member of the U.S. Supreme Court.
02-10-1985 Rock Hudson died from the AIDS virus at the age of 59.
02-10-1988 Pakistan's Supreme Court ordered free elections.
02-10-1989 In Leipzig, East Germany a protest took place demanding the legalization of opposition groups and the adoption of democratic reforms.
02-10-1990 The Allies ceded their rights to areas they occupied in Germany.
02-10-1993 Opponents of Russian President Boris Yeltsin fought police and set up burning barricades.
02-10-1996 Mark Fuhrman was given three years' probation and fined $200 after pleading no contest to perjury at O.J. Simpson's trial.
02-10-1998 Hawaii sued petroleum companies, claiming state drivers were overcharged by about $73 million a year in price-fixing.
02-10-1998 About 10,000 Turkish soldiers cross into northern Iraq and attacked Kurdish rebels.
02-10-2001 The U.S. Postmaster unveiled the "Tribute to America" stamp. The stamp was planned for release the next month.
02-10-2001 NATO, for the first time, invoked a treaty clause that stated that an attack on one member is an attack on all members. The act was in response to the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks in the United States.
03-10-1226 St. Francis of Assisi died. He was the founder of the Franciscan order.
03-10-1863 U.S. President Lincoln declared that the last Thursday of November would be recognized as Thanksgiving Day.
03-10-1888 "The Yeomen of the Guard" was performed for the first time. It was the first of 423 shows.
03-10-1893 The motor-driven vacuum cleaner was patented by J.S. Thurman.
03-10-1901 The Victor Talking Machine Company was incorporated. After a merger with Radio Corporation of America the company became RCA-Victor.
03-10-1902 Harvey Kurtzman, founder of "Mad" magazine, was born.
03-10-1906 W.T. Grant opened a 25-cent department store.
03-10-1922 Rebecca L. Felton became the first female to hold office of U.S. Senator. She was appointed by Governor Thomas W. Hardwick of Georgia to fill a vacancy.
03-10-1929 The Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes officially changed its name to the Kingdom of Yugoslavia.
03-10-1932 Iraq was admitted into the League of Nations leading Britain to terminate their mandate over the nation. Britain had ruled Iraq since taking it from Turkey during World War I.
03-10-1935 Italian forces invaded Abyssinia (now Ethiopia).
03-10-1941 Adolf Hitler stated in a speech that Russia was "broken" and they "would never rise again."
03-10-1942 The Office of Economic Stabilization was established by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt. He also authorized controls on rents, wages, salaries and farm prices.
03-10-1944 During World War II, U.S. troops broke through the Siegfried Line.
03-10-1946 "A Day in the Life of Dennis Day" began airing on NBC-TV.
03-10-1951 CBS-TV aired the first coast-to-coast telecast of a prizefight. Dave Sands defeated Carl Olson at Soldier Field in Chicago.
03-10-1952 Britain became the third nuclear power in the world when they successfully detonated their first atomic bomb. The U.S. and Russia were the only other nuclear powers.
03-10-1954 "Father Knows Best" began airing on CBS-TV.
03-10-1955 "Captain Kangaroo" premiered on CBS-TV.
03-10-1955 Rock Hudson was featured on the cover of "LIFE" magazine.
03-10-1955 "The Mickey Mouse Club" premiered on ABC-TV.
03-10-1961 "The Dick Van Dyke Show" debuted on CBS-TV.
03-10-1962 The Sigma VII blasted off from Cape Canaveral for a nine-hour flight.
03-10-1962 The play, "Stop the World, I Want to Get Off!" opened on Broadway.
03-10-1974 Frank Robinson took over the management position of the Cleveland Indians baseball team. He was the first black manager in major league baseball.
03-10-1981 Irish Nationalist in Maze Prison in Belfast, Northern Ireland called off their hunger strike. The strike had lasted 7 months and ten people had died.
03-10-1986 "Tough Guys" was released. It was the first comedy to feature Burt Lancaster and Kirk Douglas. It was, however, their seventh movie together.
03-10-1988 The space shuttle Discovery landed safely after its four-day mission. It was the first American shuttle mission since the Challenger disaster.
03-10-1988 Mithileshwar Singh, an Indian educator, was released by kidnappers in Lebanon. He had been held captive for almost two years with three Americans.
03-10-1989 An unsuccessful coup was attempted against Panamanian Gen. Manuel Antonio Noriega.
03-10-1989 East Germany suspended unrestricted travel to Czechoslovakia in an effort to slow the flow of refugees to the West.
03-10-1989 Art Shell became the first African-American head coach in the modern NFL when he took over the Los Angeles Raiders.
03-10-1990 The Berlin Wall was dismantled eleven months after the borders between East and West Germany were dissolved. The unification of Germany ended 45 years of division.
03-10-1990 Iraqi President Saddam Hussein made a visit to Kuwait since his country had seized control of the oil-rich nation.
03-10-1994 The headquarters of the Haitian pro-army militia was raided by U.S. soldiers.
03-10-1995 O.J. Simpson was acquitted of the 1994 murder of Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald L. Goldman. Simpson was later found liable in a civil trial.
03-10-1997 U.S. Attorney General Janet Reno said she had found no evidence that U.S. President Clinton had broken the law with White House coffees and overnight stays for big contributors.
03-10-2001 Near Manchester, TN, a passenger on a Greyhound bus slashed the throat of the driver. The resulting wreck killed six and injured 34 others. The driver survived his injuries. The attacker was killed in the accident and was identified as Damir Igric.
03-10-2001 ESPN began its 10th season of National Hockey League (NHL) coverage.
03-10-2001 Barry Bonds (San Francisco Giants) broke Babe Ruth's major league single-season record for walks at 171.
03-10-2003 Ray Horn, of the duo "Siegfried & Roy," was attacked by tiger during a performance. Roy survived the attack after being dragged offstage. The tiger, a 7-year-old male named Montecore, was debuting in his first show.
03-10-2006 North Korea announced that it would conduct a nuclear test as a key step in the manufacture of atomic bombs that it viewed as a deterrent against a U.S. attack. A date for the test was not announced.
03-10-2006 The Dow Jones industrial average closed at a new high ending the day at 11,727.34. Earlier in the session, the Dow had risen to 11,758.95. Both previous records had been set on January 14, 2000.
04-10-1535 The first complete English translation of the Bible was printed in Zurich, Switzerland.
04-10-1648 The first volunteer fire department was established in New York by Peter Stuyvesant.
04-10-1777 At Germantown, PA, Patriot forces and British forces both suffer heavy losses in battle. The battle was seen as British victory, which actually served as a moral boost to the Americans.
04-10-1876 The Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas formally dedicated by Texas Gov. Richard Coke. It was the state's first venture into public higher education. The college opened for classed two days earlier.
04-10-1881 Edward Leveaux received a patent for the player piano.
04-10-1887 The Paris Herald Tribune was published for the first time. It was later known as the International Herald Tribune.
04-10-1893 The first professional football contract was signed by Grant Dibert for the Pittsburgh AC.
04-10-1895 The first U.S. Open golf tournament took place in Newport, RI. Horace Rawlins, 19 years old, won the tournament.
04-10-1909 The first airship race in the U.S. took place in St. Louis, MO.
04-10-1915 The Dinosaur National Monument was established. The area covered part of Utah and Colorado.
04-10-1927 The first actual work of carving began on Mount Rushmore.
04-10-1931 The comic strip "Dick Tracy" made its debut in the Detroit Daily Mirror. The strip was created by Chester Gould.
04-10-1933 "Esquire" magazine was published for the first time.
04-10-1940 Adolf Hitler and Benito Mussolini met in the Alps at Brenner Pass. Hitler was seeking help from Italy to fight the British.
04-10-1948 The Railroad Hour" debuted on ABC radio.
04-10-1953 "I Led Three Lives" was first seen in syndication. The TV show was never on network.
04-10-1954 "December Bride" debuted on CBS-TV.
04-10-1956 "Playhouse 90" debuted on CBS-TV.
04-10-1957 "Leave it to Beaver" debuted on CBS-TV.
04-10-1957 The Soviet Union launched Sputnik I into orbit around the Earth. Sputnik was the first manmade satellite to enter space. Sputnik I fell out of orbit on January 4, 1958.
04-10-1958 British Overseas Airways Corporation became the first jetliner to offer trans-Atlantic service to passengers with flights between London, England and New York.
04-10-1959 The first World Series to be played west of St. Louis began in Los Angeles, CA.
04-10-1965 Pope Paul VI addressed the U.N. General Assembly and became the first reigning pontiff to visit the Western Hemisphere.
04-10-1976 Barbara Walters joined Harry Reasoner at the anchor desk of the "ABC Evening News" for the first time.
04-10-1981 Bruce Jenner and Harry Belafonte debuted in their first dramatic roles in NBC-TV's "Grambling's White Tiger".
04-10-1985 The Shiite Muslim group Islamic Jihad announced that they had killed American hostage William Buckley. Later another American hostage said that he believed that Buckley had died four months earlier from torture.
04-10-1986 Two men mugged Dan Rather in New York City, NY.
04-10-1987 NFL owners used replacement personnel to play games despite the player's strike.
04-10-1989 Fawaz Younis, a Lebanese hijacker, was sentenced in Washington for commandeering a Jordanian jetliner with two Americans aboard in 1985.
04-10-1990 The German parliament had its first meeting since reunification.
04-10-1992 The 16-year civil war in Mozambique ended.
04-10-1993 Russian Vice-President Alexander Rutskoi and Chairman Ruslan Khasbulatov surrendered to Boris Yeltsin after a ten-hour tank assault on the Russian White House. The two men had barricaded themselves in after Yeltsin called for general elections and dissolved the legislative body.
04-10-1993 Dozens of Somalis dragged an American soldier through the streets of Mogadishu. A videotape showed Michael Durant being taken prisoner by Somali militants.
04-10-1994 South African President Nelson Mandela was welcomed to the White House by U.S. President Clinton.
04-10-1997 Hundreds of thousands of men attended a Promise Keepers rally on the Mall in Washington, DC.
04-10-1998 The Vincent Van Gogh exhibit opened in Washington, DC. The exhibit featured 70 paintings.
04-10-1998 Davis Gaines performed as the Phantom in the show "Phantom of the Opera" for the 2,000th time.
04-10-2001 NATO granted the United States open access to their airfields and seaports and agreed to deploy ships and early-warning radar planes in the war on terrorism.
04-10-2001 A Russian airliner blew up as it flew over the Black Sea. There were no survivors of the 76 people on the plane. U.S. intelligence sources stated that they likely cause of the accident was a missile strike from a Ukrainian military exercise.
04-10-2001 Barry Bonds (San Francisco Giants) hit his 70th home run of the season to tie Mark McGwire's major league record. Bonds also moved past Reggie Jackson on the all-time list with his 564th career home run.
04-10-2001 Rickey Henderson (San Diego Padres) scored his 2,246th career run to break Ty Cobb's major league record.
04-10-2001 Shannen Doherty was sentenced to serve five days in a work-release program for a drunken driving arrest on December 28, 2000. The sentence came after Doherty had given lectures to teens about the dangers of driving under the influence.
04-10-2001 In Washington, DC, Reagan National Airport re-opened. The airport had been closed since the terrorist attacks on the United States on September 11, 2001.
04-10-2004 SpaceShipOne reached an altitude of 368,000 feet. It was the first privately built, manned rocket ship to fly in space twice within a two week window. The ship won the Ansari X Prize of $10 million dollars for their success.
05-10-1813 Chief Tecumseh of the Shawnee Indians was killed at the Battle of Thames when American forced defeated the British and the allied Indian warriors.
05-10-1877 Chief Joseph of the Nez Perce Indians surrendered to the U.S. Army after a 1,000-mile retreat towards the Canadian border.
05-10-1882 Robert H. Goddard , known as the "Father of the Space Age", was born.
05-10-1892 The Dalton gang was nearly wiped out while attempting to rob two banks simultaneously in Coffeyville, KS. Four members of the gang and four citizens were killed. The only survivor of the gang, Emmett Dawson, was sentenced to life after surviving his wounds.
05-10-1902 Ray Kroc, the founder of McDonald's, was born.
05-10-1919 Enzo Ferrari debuted in his first race. He later founded the Auto Avio Construzioni Ferrari, an independent manufacturing company.
05-10-1921 The World Series was broadcast on the radio for the first time. The game was between the New York Giants and the New York Yankees.
05-10-1930 Laura Ingalls became the first woman to make a transcontinental airplane flight.
05-10-1930 "The Fighting Priest" began airing on CBS radio.
05-10-1931 Clyde Pangborn and Hugh Herndon landed in Washington after flying non-stop across the Pacific Ocean. The flight originated in Japan and took about 41 hours.
05-10-1934 "Hollywood Hotel" became the first major network radio to originate from Hollywood, CA.
05-10-1937 U.S. President Roosevelt called for a "quarantine" of aggressor nations.
05-10-1947 U.S. President Harry S. Truman held the first televised presidential address from the White House. The subject was the current international food crisis.
05-10-1952 "Inner Sanctum" was heard for the last time on ABC radio.
05-10-1955 The play "The Diary of Anne Frank" opened at the Cort Theatre in New York.
05-10-1969 Dianne Linkletter jumps to her death from her apartment in West Hollywood. Art Linkletter, her father, claimed that she was under the influence of LSD at the time of her death.
05-10-1969 A Cuban defector landed a Soviet-made MiG-17 at Homestead Air Force Base in Florida. The plane entered U.S. air space and landed without being detected.
05-10-1969 "Monty Python's Flying Circus" debuted on BBC television.
05-10-1970 Anwar Sadat took office as President of Egypt replacing Gamal Abdel Nassar. Sadat was assassinated in 1981.
05-10-1974 American David Kunst completed the first journey around the world on foot. It took four years and 21 pairs of shoes. He crossed four continents and walked 14,450 miles.
05-10-1985 An Egyptian policeman went on a shooting rampage at a Sinai beach. Seven Israeli tourists were killed. The policeman died in prison the following January of an apparent suicide.
05-10-1986 "Business World" began airing on ABC-TV.
05-10-1986 Sandinista soldiers captured American Eugene Hasenfus after shooting him down over southern Nicaragua.
05-10-1988 In a debate between candidates for vice president of the U.S., Democratic Lloyd Bentsen told Republican Dan Quayle, "You're no Jack Kennedy."
05-10-1989 Jim Bakker was convicted of using his television show to defraud his viewers.
05-10-1989 The Dalai Lama (Lhama Dhondrub, Tenzin Gyatso) was named the winner of the Nobel Peace Prize for his nonviolent campaign to end the Chinese domination of Tibet. Gyatso was the 15th Dalai Lama.
05-10-1990 The Glasgow Royal Concert Hall opened.
05-10-1990 A jury in Cincinnati, OH, acquitted an art gallery and its director of obscenity charges stemming from an exhibit of sexually graphic photographs by Robert Mapplethorpe.
05-10-1991 Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev announced that his country would cut its nuclear arsenal in response to the arms reduction that was initiated by U.S. President George Bush.
05-10-1993 China set off an underground nuclear explosion.
05-10-1994 48 people found dead in two Swiss villages. The people were members of a secret religious doomsday cult. Five other people were found in Montreal, Canada.
05-10-1995 A 60-day cease-fire was agreed upon by Bonsian combatants. The civil war had lasted 3 1/2.
05-10-1997 In London, the Express Newspapers printed an article claiming that Tom Cruise and Nicole Kidman were homosexual and that their marriage was a sham to cover the truth. The paper paid damages in a settlement on October 29, 1998.
05-10-1998 The U.S. paid $60 million for Russia's research time on the international space station to keep the cash-strapped Russian space agency afloat.
05-10-1999 Kevin Spacey received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
05-10-1999 MCI Worldcom Inc. and Sprint Corp. announced plans to merge.
05-10-2006 Wal-Mart Stores Inc. rolled out its $4 generic drug program to the entire state of Florida after a successful test in the Tampa area.
06-10-1536 Anglican priest William Tyndale was captured at Antwerp where he was strangled and burnt. He is credited with making the first English translation of the Bible.
06-10-1683 The first Mennonites arrived in America aboard the Concord. The German and Dutch families settled in an area that is now a neighborhood in Philadelphia, PA.
06-10-1846 Inventor George Westinghouse was born. He was the founder of Westinghouse Electric Company and invented railway braking systems.
06-10-1847 "Jane Eyre" by Charlotte Bronte was first published in London.
06-10-1848 The steamboat SS California left New York Harbor for San Francisco via Cape Horn. The steamboat service arrived on February 28, 1849. The trip took 4 months and 21 days.
06-10-1857 The American Chess Congress held their first national chess tournament in New York City.
06-10-1863 The first Turkish bath was opened in Brooklyn, NY, by Dr. Charles Shepard.
06-10-1866 The Reno Brothers pulled the first train robbery in America near Seymour, IN. The got away with $10,000.
06-10-1880 The National League kicked the Cincinnati Reds out for selling beer.
06-10-1884 The Naval War College was established in Newport, RI.
06-10-1889 In Paris, the Moulin Rouge opened its doors to the public for the first time.
06-10-1889 The Kinescope was exhibited by Thomas Edison. He had patented the moving picture machine in 1887.
06-10-1890 Polygamy was outlawed by the Mormon Church.
06-10-1927 "The Jazz Singer" opened in New York starring Al Jolson. The film was based on the short story "The Day of Atonement" by Sampson Raphaelson.
06-10-1928 War-torn China was reunited under the Nationalist leader Chiang Kai-Shek.
06-10-1937 "Hobby Lobby" debuted on CBS radio.
06-10-1939 Adolf Hitler denied any intention to wage war against Britain and France in an address to Reichstag.
06-10-1948 "Summer and Smoke" by Tennessee Williams opened on Broadway.
06-10-1949 Iva Toguri D'Aquino was sentenced to 10 years in prison and fined $10,000 for war crimes. The conviction was for being Japanese wartime broadcaster "Tokyo Rose."
06-10-1949 U.S. president Harry Truman signed the Mutual Defense Assistance Act. The act provided $1.3 billion in the form of military aid to NATO countries.
06-10-1954 E.L. Lyon became the first male nurse for the U.S. Army.
06-10-1961 U.S. president John F. Kennedy advised American families to build or buy bomb shelters to protect them in the event of a nuclear exchange with the Soviet Union.
06-10-1962 Robert Goulet began the role of Sir Lancelot in "Camelot".
06-10-1972 South of Saltillo, Mexico, a train derailed killing 208 people and injuring 1,200.
06-10-1973 Egypt and Syria attacked Israel in an attempt to win back territory that had been lost in the third Arab-Israel war. Support for Israel led to a devastating oil embargo against many nations including the U.S. and Great Britain on October 17, 1973. The war lasted 2 weeks.
06-10-1979 Pope John Paul II became the first pontiff to visit the White House.
06-10-1981 Egyptian president Anwar-el Sadat was assassinated at a military rally in Cairo. Muslim extremists were responsible the other eight deaths that occurred during the attack. Hosni Mubarak became president.
06-10-1986 A Soviet nuclear submarine sank in the Atlantic Ocean about 1,200 miles from New York.
06-10-1989 Two workers for the Swiss Red Cross were kidnapped by terrorists in Lebanon.
06-10-1991 Elizabeth Taylor married Larry Fortensky. The ceremony was held at Michael Jackson's estate near Los Angeles, CA. It was Taylor's 8th marriage and Fortensky's 3rd.
06-10-1991 Cable News Network aired a videotape of American hostage Terry Anderson that had been made in Beirut, Lebanon.
06-10-1992 Ross Perot appeared in his first paid broadcast on CBS-TV after entering the U.S. presidential race.
06-10-1998 Imelda Marcos was acquitted by the Philippine Supreme Court on the charge of graft. The ruling overturned the guilty verdict that had been found in 1993.
07-10-1765 Nine American colonies sent a total of 28 delegates to New York City for the Stamp Act Congress. The delegates adopted the "Declaration of Rights and Grievances."
07-10-1777 During the American Revolution the second Battle of Saratoga began.
07-10-1868 Cornell University was inaugurated in Ithaca, NY.
07-10-1913 For the first time, Henry Ford's entire Highland Park automobile factory was run on a continuously moving assembly line when the chassis was added to the process.
07-10-1918 The Georgia Tech football team defeated Cumberland College 222-0. Georgia Tech carried the ball 978 yards and never threw a pass.
07-10-1939 "Kate Hopkins, Angel of Mercy" was heard for the first time on CBS radio.
07-10-1940 "Portia Faces Life" debuted on the NBC Red network.
07-10-1949 The German Democratic Republic (East Germany) was formed.
07-10-1950 The U.S.-led U.N. forces crossed the 38th parallel and entered North Korea. China in November proved their threat to enter the war by sending several hundred thousand troops over the border into North Korea.
07-10-1951 The Western Hills Hotel in Fort Worth, TX, became the first hotel to feature all foam-rubber mattresses and pillows.
07-10-1956 A U.S. House subcommittee began investigations of allegedly rigged TV quiz shows.
07-10-1963 U.S. President Kennedy signed a nuclear test ban treaty with Britain and the Soviet Union.
07-10-1968 The Motion Picture Association of America adopted the film-rating system that ranged for "G" to "X."
07-10-1981 The Egyptian parliament, after the assassination of Anwar Sadat, named Vice President Hosni Mubarak the next president of Egypt.
07-10-1985 Four Palestinian terrorists hijacked the Italian cruise ship Achille Lauro off the coast of Egypt. There were 440 people onboard. They surrendered after two days and killing American passenger Leon Klinghoffer.
07-10-1985 91 people were killed in Ponce, Puerto Rico, by a mudslide.
07-10-1989 In Budapest, Hungary's Communist Party renounced Marxism in favor of democratic socialism.
07-10-1992 In Peru, a secret military tribunal sentenced Abimael Guzman to life in prison without parole. He was the leader of the Shining Path guerrilla movement.
07-10-1993 U.S. President Clinton sent more troops, heavy armor, and naval firepower to Somalia.
07-10-1994 U.S. President Clinton dispatched an aircraft carrier to the Persian Gulf when Iraqi troops were spotted moving toward Kuwait. The U.S. Army was also put on alert.
07-10-1995 More than 80 people were killed in Indonesia when an earthquake with a magnitude of 7 on the Richter Scale hit.
07-10-1998 The U.S. government filed an antitrust suit that alleged Visa and MasterCard inhibit competition by preventing banks from offering other cards.
07-10-1999 American Home Products Corp. agreed to pay up to $4.83 billion to settle claims that the fen-phen diet drug caused dangerous problems with heart valves.
07-10-2000 Vojislav Kostunica took the oath of office as Yugoslavia's first popularly elected president.
07-10-2001 Barry Bonds (San Francisco Giants) hit his 73rd home run of the season and set a new major league record.
07-10-2001 The U.S. and Great Britain began airstrikes in Afghanistan in response to that state's support of terrorism and Osama bin Laden. The act was the first military action taken in response to the terrorist attacks on the U.S. on September 11, 2001.
07-10-2003 In California, Arnold Schwarzenegger was elected governor in the recall election of Governor Gray Davis.
07-10-2003 Randy Quaid received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
07-10-2004 Billy Bob Thornton got a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
08-10-1871 The Great Fire of Chicago broke out destroying about 17,450 buildings. About 250 people were killed and 90,000 were left homeless.
08-10-1871 Peshtigo, WI, was destroyed by a forest fire. Over 1,100 people were killed by the fire that eventually burned across 6 counties.
08-10-1895 The Berliner Gramophone Company was founded in Philadelphia, PA.
08-10-1904 "Little Johnny Jones" opened in Hartford, CT.
08-10-1915 During World War I, the Battle of Loos concluded.
08-10-1918 U.S. Corporal Alvin C. York almost single-handedly killed 25 German soldiers and captured 132 in the Argonne Forest in France. York had originally tried to avoid being drafted as a conscientious objector. After this event his was promoted to sergeant and was awarded the Congressional Medal of Honor.
08-10-1919 The first transcontinental air race in the U.S. began.
08-10-1934 Bruno Hauptmann was indicted for the murder of the infant son of Charles A. Lindbergh.
08-10-1935 "The O’Neills" debuted on CBS radio.
08-10-1938 The cover of "The Saturday Evening Post" portrayed Norman Rockwell.
08-10-1944 "The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet" debuted on CBS radio.
08-10-1945 U.S. President Truman announced that only Britain and Canada would be given the secret to the atomic bomb.
08-10-1950 U.N. forces crossed into North Korea from South Korea.
08-10-1952 "The Complete Book of Etiquette" was published for the first time.
08-10-1956 Donald James Larsen (New York Yankees) pitched the first perfect game in the history of the World Series.
08-10-1957 Jack Soble, a confessed Soviet spy, was sentenced to seven years in prison for espionage.
08-10-1957 The Brooklyn Baseball Club announced that it had accepted a deal to move the Dodgers to Los Angeles.
08-10-1966 The U.S. Government declares that LSD is dangerous and an illegal substance.
08-10-1970 Soviet author Alexander Solzhenitsyn won the Nobel Prize for literature.
08-10-1979 "Sugar Babies" opened at the Mark Hellinger Theatre on Broadway.
08-10-1981 U.S. President Reagan greeted former Presidents Carter, Ford and Nixon to the White House. The group was preparing to leave for Egypt to attend the funeral of Anwar Sadat.
08-10-1982 In Poland, all labor organizations, including Solidarity, were banned.
08-10-1991 A slave burial site was found by construction workers in lower Manhattan. The "Negro Burial Ground" had been closed in 1790. Over a dozen skeletons were found.
08-10-1993 The U.S. government issued a report absolving the FBI of any wrongdoing in its final assault in Waco, TX, on the Branch Davidian compound. The fire that ended the siege killed as many as 85 people.
08-10-1996 Pope John Paul II underwent a successful operation to remove his inflamed appendix.
08-10-1998 Taliban forces attacked Iranian border posts. Iran said that three border posts were destroyed before the Taliban forces were forced to retreat. The Taliban of Afghanistan denied the event occurred.
08-10-1998 Canada and Netherlands were voted into the U.N. Security Council.
08-10-2001 Tom Ridge, former Governor of Pennsylvania, was sworn in as director of the new U.S. department of Homeland Security.
08-10-2001 Rush Limbaugh announced to his listeners that he was totally deaf in his left ear and had only partial hearing in his right ear. The condition had happened in a three month period.
08-10-2001 Two Russian cosmonauts made the first spacewalk to be conducted outside of the international space station without a shuttle present.
08-10-2002 A federal judge approved U.S. President George W. Bush's request to reopen West Coast ports, to end a caustic 10-day labor lockout. The lockout was costing the U.S. economy an estimated $1 billion to $2 billion a day.
08-10-2003 China announced that it would have a human crew orbit the Earth briefly on October 15.
08-10-2003 Vietnam and the United States reached a tentative agreement that would allow the first commercial flights between the two countries since the end of the Vietnam War.
08-10-2003 It was announced that Vivendi Universal and General Electric Co. had reached an agreement to merge. The name for the combined company was NBC Universal.
08-10-2003 Siegfried Fischbacher and his manager announced that the "Siegfried and Roy" show at the Mirage was canceled permanently. It was also said that if Roy Horn survived, after a tiger attack on October 3, the duo would continue to work together.
08-10-2004 The first-ever direct presidential elections were held in Afghanistan.
08-10-2004 At Alderson Federal Prison Camp, WV, Martha Stewart began her five-month prison sentence. The sentence was imposed for Stewart lying about a stock sale.
09-10-1635 Roger Williams, founder of Rhode Island, was banished from Massachusetts because he had spoken out against punishments for religious offenses and giving away land that belonged to the Indians. Williams had founded Providence, Rhode Island as a place for people to seek religious freedom.
09-10-1701 The Collegiate School of Connecticut was chartered in New Haven. The name was later changed to Yale.
09-10-1776 A group of Spanish missionaries settled in what is now San Francisco, CA.
09-10-1781 The last major battle of the American Revolutionary War took place in Yorktown, VA. The American forces, led by George Washington, defeated the British troops under Lord Cornwallis.
09-10-1812 During the War of 1812 American forces captured two British brigs, the Detroit and the Caledonia.
09-10-1855 Isaac Singer patented the sewing machine motor.
09-10-1855 Joshua C. Stoddard received a patent for his calliope.
09-10-1858 Mail service via stagecoach between San Francisco, CA, and St. Louis, MO, began.
09-10-1872 Aaron Montgomery started his mail order business with the delivery of the first mail order catalog. The firm later became Montgomery Wards.
09-10-1876 Alexander Graham Bell and Thomas Watson made their longest telephone call to date. It was a distance of two miles.
09-10-1888 The public was admitted to the Washington Monument for the first time.
09-10-1914 During World War I, German forces captured Antwerp, Belgium.
09-10-1919 The Cincinnati Reds won the World Series. The win would be later tainted when 8 Chicago White Sox were charged with throwing the game. The incident became known as the "Black Sox" scandal.
09-10-1930 Aviator Laura Ingalls landed in Glendale, CA, to complete the first solo transcontinental flight across the U.S. by a woman.
09-10-1935 "Cavalcade of America" was first broadcast on CBS radio.
