09-10-1936 The first generator at Boulder Dam began transmitting electricity to Los Angeles, CA. The name of the dam was later changed to Hoover Dam.
09-10-1940 St. Paul's Cathedral in London was bombed by the Nazis. The dome was unharmed in the bombing.
09-10-1943 "Land of the Lost" debuted on ABC radio.
09-10-1946 "The Iceman Cometh" opened in New York City, NY.
09-10-1946 The first electric blanket went on sale in Petersburg, VA.
09-10-1947 The Broadway show, "High Button Shoes", opened.
09-10-1963 Over 2,000 people were killed in northeast Italy when the Vaiont Dam was overrun by water. The incident was caused by landslide that occurred behind the dam.
09-10-1967 Che Guevara was executed by Bolivian soldiers for attempting to incite a revolution in Bolivia.
09-10-1974 Oskar Schindler died in Frankfurt, Germany. Schindler is credited with saving the lives of about 1,200 Jews during the Holocaust.
09-10-1975 Andrei Sakharov was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. The Soviet scientist is known as the "father of the hydrogen bomb."
09-10-1983 Helen Moss joined the Brownies at the age of 83. She became the oldest person to become a member.
09-10-1985 The hijackers of the Achille Lauro cruise liner surrendered after the ship arrived in Port Said, Egypt.
09-10-1986 U.S. District Judge Harry E. Claiborne became the fifth federal official to be removed from office through impeachment. The U.S. Senate convicted Claiborne of "high crimes and misdemeanors."
09-10-1986 Joan Rivers debuted her new "The Late Show" on the FOX network.
09-10-1986 The musical "Phantom of the Opera" by Andrew Lloyd Webber opened in London.
09-10-1989 The official Soviet news agency Tass reported an unidentified flying object. The report included a trio of tall aliens that had visited the city of Voronzh.
09-10-1991 The play revival "On Borrowed Time" opened.
09-10-1994 The U.S. sent troops and warships to the Persian Gulf in response to Saddam Hussein sending thousands of troops and hundreds of tanks toward the Kuwaiti border.
09-10-1995 Saboteurs tinkered with a stretch of railroad track in Arizona. An Amtrak train derailed killing one and injuring a hundred.
09-10-2000 Brett Hull (Dallas Stars) scored his 611th National Hockey League (NHL) goal. The goal allowed him to pass his father, Bobby Hull, on the all time scoring list bringing him to number 9.
09-10-2001 Prosecutors in Miami, FL, announced that they would seek a prison sentence if O.J. Simpson was convicted in his road rage trial. Jury selection began for the trial just after the announcement.
09-10-2003 Britain's Queen Elizabeth II knighted Roger Moore and made Sting a CBE (Commander of the British Empire).
10-10-1845 The United States Naval Academy opened in Annapolis, MD.
10-10-1865 The billiard ball was patented by John Wesley Hyatt.
10-10-1886 The tuxedo dinner jacket made its U.S. debut in New York City.
10-10-1887 Thomas Edison organized the Edison Phonograph Company.
10-10-1911 China's Manchu dynasty was overthrown by revolutionaries under Sun Yat-sen.
10-10-1913 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson triggered the explosion of the Gamboa Dike that ended the construction of the Panama Canal.
10-10-1928 "Hold Everything" opened on Broadway.
10-10-1932 "Betty and Bob" began on radio.
10-10-1932 "Judy and Jane" began on radio.
10-10-1933 Dreft, the first synthetic detergent, went on sale.
10-10-1937 The Mutual Broadcasting System debuted "Thirty Minutes in Hollywood".
10-10-1938 Nazi Germany completed its annexation of Czechoslovakia's Sudetenland.
10-10-1943 Chaing Kai-shek took the oath of office as the president of China.
10-10-1957 U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower apologized to Komla Agbeli Gbdemah, the finance minister of Ghana, after the official had been refused service in a Dover, DE, restaurant.
10-10-1959 Pan American World Airways announced the beginning of the first global airline service.
10-10-1963 A dam burst in Italy killing 3,000 people.
10-10-1965 The Red Baron made his first appearance in the "Peanuts" comic strip.
10-10-1970 Pierre Laporte, the labor minister of Quebec, was kidnapped by the Quebec Liberation Front (FLQ) during the October Crisis in Canada. He was found eight days later strangled to death.
10-10-1973 U.S. Vice President Spiro Agnew resigned after being charged with federal income tax evasion.
10-10-1973 Fiji became independent after of nearly a century of British rule.
10-10-1977 Joe Namath played the last game of his National Football League (NFL) career.
10-10-1978 The U.S. bill authorizing the Susan B. Anthony dollar was signed by U.S. President Jimmy Carter.
10-10-1985 U.S. fighter jets forced an Egyptian plane to land in Italy so that the hijackers of the Italian cruise ship Achilles Lauro could be arrested.
10-10-1986 An estimated 1,500 people were killed when an earthquake measuring 7.5 on the Richter Scale struck San Salvador, El Salvador.
10-10-1987 Tom McClean finished rowing across the Atlantic Ocean. It set the record at 54 days and 18 hours.
10-10-1991 The United States cut all foreign aid to Haiti in reaction to a military coup that forced President Jean-Claude Aristide into exile.
10-10-1994 Lt. Gen. Raoul Cedras resigned as Haiti's commander-in-chief of the army and pledged to leave the country.
10-10-1994 Iraq announced it was withdrawing its forces from the Kuwaiti border. No signs of a pullback were observed.
10-10-1995 Gary Kasparov won a chess championship against Viswanathan Anand that had lasted about a month.
10-10-1997 The Guggenheim Museum in Bilbao, Spain, opened to the public. Architect Frank Gehry designed the 450 ft. long and 98 ft. wide building.
10-10-2001 U.S. President George W. Bush presented a list of 22 most wanted terrorists.
10-10-2003 Rush Limbaugh annouced that he was addicted to painkillers and that he was going to check into a rehab center.
11-10-1759 Parson Mason Weems was born. He is remembered for his fictitious stories that he presented as fact. He was responsible for the story about George Washington cutting down his father's cherry tree.
11-10-1776 During the American Revolution the first naval battle of Lake Champlain was fought. The forces under Gen. Benedict Arnold suffered heavy losses.
11-10-1779 Casimir Pulaski, a Polish nobleman, was killed while fighting during the Revolutionary War Battle of Savannah, GA. He was fighting for American independence.
11-10-1809 Meriwether Lewis committed suicide along the Natchez Trace in Tennessee at an inn called Grinder's Stand.
11-10-1811 The Juliana, the first steam-powered ferryboat, was put into operation by the inventor John Stevens. The ferry went between New York City, NY, and Hoboken, NJ.
11-10-1869 Thomas Edison filed for a patent on his first invention. The electric machine was used for counting votes for the U.S. Congress, however the Congress did not buy it.
11-10-1881 David Henderson Houston patented the first roll film for cameras.
11-10-1890 The Daughters of the American Revolution was founded in Washington, DC.
11-10-1899 The Boer War began in South Africa between the British and the Boers of the Transvaal and Orange Free State.
11-10-1929 JCPenney opened a store in Milford, DE, making it a nationwide company with stores in all 48 states.
11-10-1932 In New York, the first telecast of a political campaign was aired.
11-10-1936 The radio show, "Professor Quiz", aired for the first time.
11-10-1939 U.S. President Roosevelt was presented with a letter from Albert Einstein that urged him to develop the U.S. atomic program rapidly.
11-10-1942 The Battle of Cape Esperance, during World War II, began in the Solomons.
11-10-1958 Pioneer 1, a lunar probe, was launched by the U.S. The probe did not reach its destination and fell back to Earth and burned up in the atmosphere.
11-10-1968 Apollo 7 was launched by the U.S. The first manned Apollo mission was the first in which live television broadcasts were received from orbit. Wally Schirra, Don Fulton Eisele and R. Walter Cunningham were the astronauts aboard.
11-10-1971 Hugh Downs left the "Today" show and "Concentration". He later became the host of ABC's "20/20".
11-10-1975 "Saturday Night Live" was broadcast for the first time. George Carlin was the guest host.
11-10-1975 Bill Clinton and Hillary Rodham were married in Fayetteville, AR.
11-10-1976 The "Gang of Four" of China was charged with plotting a coup and were arrested and imprisoned.
11-10-1983 The last hand-cranked telephones in the U.S. went out of service. The 440 telephone customers of Bryant Pond, ME, were switched to direct-dial service.
11-10-1984 Construction began on the Kamric/Cinergy Futursonics Studio in Houston, TX.
11-10-1984 American Kathryn D. Sullivan became the first female astronaut to space walk. She was aboard the space shuttle Challenger.
11-10-1984 Mario Lemieux (Pittsburgh Penguins) made his debut in the National Hockey League (NHL) against the Boston Bruins. He scored a goal on his first shot on his first NHL shift.
11-10-1994 U.S. troops in Haiti took control of the National Palace.
11-10-1994 Iraqi troops began moving away from the Kuwaiti border.
11-10-1994 The Colorado Supreme Court declared that the anti-gay rights measure in the state was unconstitutional.
11-10-2002 In Cedar Grove, WI, ten people were killed when more than two dozen vehicles crashed on a foggy highway.
12-10-1492 Christopher Columbus, an Italian explorer, sighted Watling Island in the Bahamas. He believed that he had found Asia while attempting to find a Western ocean route to India. The same day he claimed the land for Isabella and Ferdinand of Spain.
12-10-1792 The first monument honoring Christopher Columbus was dedicated in Baltimore, MD.
12-10-1810 Bavarian Crown Prince Ludwig married Princess Therese of Saxony-Hildburghausen. The royalty invited the public to attend the event which became an annual celebration that later became known as Oktoberfest.
12-10-1860 Inventor Elmer Sperry was born on this day. He held patents on more than 400 inventions. The most important being the Sperry Automatic Pilot.
12-10-1892 In celebration of the 400th anniversary of the Columbus landing the original version of the Pledge of Allegiance was first recited in public schools.
12-10-1895 In Newport, RI, the first amateur golf tournament was held.
12-10-1915 Former U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt criticized U.S. citizens who identified themselves by dual nationalities.
12-10-1915 British nurse Edith Cavell was executed by a German firing squad for helping Allied soldiers escape from Belgium during World War I.
12-10-1920 Construction of the Holland Tunnel began. It opened on November 13, 1927. The tunnel links Jersey City, NJ and New York City, NY.
12-10-1933 John Dillinger, bank robber, escaped from a jail in Allen County, OH. The sheriff was killed by his gang as they helped Dillinger escape.
12-10-1933 The U.S. Department of Justice acquired Alcatraz Island from the U.S. Army.
12-10-1937 "Mr. Keen, Tracer of Lost Persons" debuted on radio.
12-10-1938 Filming began on "The Wizard of Oz."
12-10-1942 During World War II, Attorney General Francis Biddle announced that Italian nationals in the United States would no longer be considered enemy aliens.
12-10-1945 Private First Class Desmond T. Doss was presented with the Congressional Medal of Honor for outstanding bravery as a medical corpsman. He was the first conscientious objector in American history to win the award.
12-10-1950 The Kefauver Crime Commission convened in New York to investigate interstate organized crime.
12-10-1960 Soviet premier Nikita Khrushchev pounded a shoe on his desk during a dispute at a U.N. General Assembly.
12-10-1961 The first video memoirs by a U.S. president were made. Walter Cronkite interviewed Dwight D. Eisenhower.
12-10-1964 The Soviet Union launched Voskhod 1 into orbit around the Earth. It was the first space flight to have a multi-person crew and the first flight to be performed without space suits.
12-10-1972 During the Vietnam War, a racial brawl broke out aboard the U.S. aircraft carrier Kitty Hawk. Nearly 50 sailors were injured.
12-10-1976 China announced that Hua Guo-feng was named to succeed the late Mao Tse-tung as chairman of the Communist Party.
12-10-1984 An attempt on British Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher's life was unsuccessful, but did take the lives of five people. The bomb had been planted by the I.R.A.
12-10-1988 Federal prosecutors announced that the Sundstrand Corp. would pay $115 million dollars to settle with the Pentagon for overbilling airplane parts over a five-year period.
12-10-1989 The U.S. House of Representatives approved a statutory federal ban on the destruction of the American flag.
12-10-1993 The play "Mixed Emotions" opened at the John Golden Theatre.
12-10-1994 Haitian military leader Raoul Cedras was granted political asylum by Panama.
12-10-1994 The Magellan space probe ended its four-year mission to Venus for the purpose of mapping.
12-10-1997 The St. Francis Basilica and 15th-century bell tower above Foligno city hall in Italy were damaged by 3 earthquakes.
12-10-1998 The U.S. House of Representatives passed the Online Copyright Bill.
12-10-1999 Rob Reiner received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
12-10-1999 In Pakistan, Pervez Musharraf seized power in a bloodless coup that toppled Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif. The Supreme Court ruled that the coup was legal but insisted that a civilian government be restored within three years.
12-10-2000 In Aden, Yemen, the USS Cole, a U.S. Navy destroyer, experienced a large explosion while refueling. The explosion was the result of a terrorist attack using a small boat. 17 crewmembers were killed and at least 39 were injured.
12-10-2000 In Denver, CO, the U.S. District Court denied Timothy McVeigh's request for a new trial.
12-10-2001 A special episode of America's Most Wanted was aired that focused on 22 wanted terrorists. The show was specifically requested by U.S. President George W. Bush.
12-10-2001 A car bomb exploded in Madrid, Spain, that injured 17 people. Basque separatists claimed responsibility.
12-10-2002 In Bali, Indonesia, over 180 people were killed and over 300 were injured when a bomb was detonated in a nightclub district.
12-10-2006 The Dow Jones industrial average advanced over 11,900 for the first time.
13-10-1775 The U.S. Continental Congress ordered the construction of a naval fleet.
13-10-1792 The cornerstone of the Executive Mansion was laid in Washington, DC. The building became known as the White House in 1818.
13-10-1812 American forces were defeated at the Battle of Queenstown Heights. The British victory effectively ended an further U.S. invasion of Canada.
13-10-1843 B'nai B'rith, the Jewish organization, was founded by Henry Jones and eleven others in New York City, NY.
13-10-1854 The state of Texas ratified a state constitution.
13-10-1924 The play "The Guardsman" opened in New York City, NY.
13-10-1943 During World War II, Italy signed an armistice with the Allies and declared war on Germany.
13-10-1944 American troops entered Aachen, Germany, during World War II.
13-10-1944 During World War II, British and Greek advance units landed at Piraeus.
13-10-1951 In Atlanta, GA, a football with a rubber covering was used for the first time. Georgia Tech beat Louisiana State 25-7.
13-10-1953 An ultrasonic burglar alarm was patented by Samuel Bagno.
13-10-1957 Bing Crosby and Frank Sinatra introduced the Ford Edsel on an hour long special.
13-10-1960 The World Series ended on a home run for the first time. Bill Mazeroski's homerun allowed the Pirates to beat the Yankees.
13-10-1962 "Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?" opened on Broadway.
13-10-1967 The first game of the new American Basketball Association was played.
13-10-1977 Four Palestinians hijacked an Lufthansa airliner to Somalia. They demanded the release of members of the Red Army Faction.
13-10-1981 Egyptian voters elected Vice President Hosni Mubarak as the new president one week after Anwar Sadat was assassinated.
13-10-1984 Jesse Jackson appeared on "Saturday Night Live."
13-10-1989 U.S. President George Bush called for an overthrow of the Panamanian ruler Manuel Antonio Noriega.
13-10-1990 Le Duc Tho died at the age of 79. He was a co-founder of the Vietnamese Communist Party.
13-10-1992 A commercial flight record was set by an Air France supersonic jetliner for circling the Earth in 33 hours and one minute.
13-10-1995 Walt Disney World Resort admitted its 500-millionth guest.
13-10-1998 The National Basketball Association (NBA) canceled regular season games, due to work stoppage, for first time in its 51-year history.
13-10-1999 The U.S. Senate rejected the ratification of the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty (CTBT).
14-10-1066 The Battle of Hastings occurred in England. The Norman forces of William the Conqueror defeated King Harold II of England.
14-10-1568 Mary, Queen of Scots, went on trial in England. She was accused of conspiring against Queen Elizabeth I. Mary was beheaded the following February.
14-10-1644 William Penn was born. Penn was the colonist that founded the Pennsylvania colony for Quakers.
14-10-1879 Thomas Edison signed an agreement with Jose D. Husbands for the sale of Edison telephones in Chile.
14-10-1887 Thomas Edison and George E. Gouraud reached an agreement for the international marketing rights for the phonograph.
14-10-1890 Dwight David 'Ike' Eisenhower was born. He became the Supreme Commander of Allied Forces in World War II and eventually the 34th U.S. President.
14-10-1912 Theodore Roosevelt was shot while campaigning in Milwaukee, WI. Roosevelt's wound in the chest was not serious and he continued with his planned speech. William Schrenk was captured at the scene of the shooting.
14-10-1922 Lieutenant Lester James Maitland set a new airplane speed record when he reached a speed of 216.1 miles-per-hour.
14-10-1926 The book "Winnie-the-Pooh," by A.A. Milne, made its debut.
14-10-1928 The first televised wedding took place in Des Plains, IL. James Fowlkes and Cora Dennison were married in a radio studio.
14-10-1930 Ethel Merman debuted on Broadway in "Girl Crazy."
14-10-1933 Nazi Germany announced that it was withdrawing from the League of Nations.
14-10-1934 "Lux Radio Theater" began airing on the NBC Blue radio network.
14-10-1936 The first SSB (Social Security Board) office opened in Austin, TX. From this point, the Board's local office took over the assigning of Social Security Numbers.
14-10-1943 The Radio Corporation of America finalized the sale of the NBC Blue radio network. Edward J. Noble paid $8 million for the network that was renamed American Broadcasting Company.
14-10-1944 German Field Marshal Erwin Rommel committed suicide rather than face execution after being accused of conspiring against Adolf Hitler and the execution that would follow.
14-10-1944 During World War II, the Second British Parachute Brigade liberated the city of Athens.
14-10-1947 Over Rogers Dry Lake in Southern California, pilot Chuck Yeager flew the Bell X-1 rocket plane and became the first person to break the sound barrier.
14-10-1954 C.B. DeMille's "The Ten Commandments", starring Charlton Heston, began filming in Egypt. The epic had a cast of 25,000 people.
14-10-1960 U.S. presidential candidate John F. Kennedy first suggested the idea of a Peace Corps.
14-10-1961 "How to Succeed in Business without Really Trying" opened on Broadway.
14-10-1962 The Cuban Missile Crisis began when U.S. reconnaissance aircrafts photographed Soviet construction of intermediate-range missile sites in Cuba.
14-10-1964 Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his non-violent resistance to racial prejudice in America. He was the youngest person to receive the award.
14-10-1968 The first live telecast to come from a manned U.S. spacecraft was transmitted from Apollo 7.
14-10-1970 Anwar el-Sadat became president of Egypt following the death of President Nasser.
14-10-1979 The first national homosexual rights march took place in Washington, DC, involving over 100,000 people.
14-10-1984 George ‘Sparky’ Anderson became the first baseball manager to win 100 games and a World Series in both leagues. (MLB)
14-10-1986 Soviet leader Mikhail S. Gorbachev charged that the U.S. wanted to "bleed the Soviet Union economically" with the arms race in space.
14-10-1987 Jessica McClure, 18 months old, fell down an abandoned well in Midland, TX. The rescue took 58 hours.
14-10-1992 In Russia, Andrei Chikatilo, was sentenced to death after being convicted of 52 serial killings.
14-10-1993 In Haiti, Justice Minister Guy Malary was assassinated by gunmen who were supporters of ousted President Jean-Bertrand Aristide.
14-10-1995 An armed gunman seized control of bus of tourists in Moscow's Red Square. The next day commandos stormed the bus freeing the four remaining hostages and killing the gunman.
14-10-1998 The FBI charged Eric Robert Rudolph with 6 bombings including the 1996 Olympic bombing in Atlanta. Rudolph was not in custody at the time the charges were filed.
14-10-1998 Kendall Francois pled innocent on charges of killing eight women in New York.
14-10-2000 A Saudi Arabian Airlines flight was hijacked just after takeoff from Jiddah, Saudi Arabia. The plane was taken to Baghdad, Iraq, where the two men surrendered peacefully after negotiations.
14-10-2001 Toys "R" Us introduced the new version of Geoffrey the giraffe in a 60-second commercial before WABC-TV aired Disney's "The Emperor's New Groove."
14-10-2002 Britain stripped power from the Catholic and Protestant politicians of Northern Ireland. Britain resumed sole responsibility for running Northern Ireland.
15-10-1815 Napoleon Bonaparte began his exile on the remote island of St. Helena in the Atlantic Ocean.
15-10-1844 German philosopher Friedich Wilhelm Nietzsche was born.
15-10-1860 Grace Bedell, 11 years old, wrote a letter to presidential candidate Abraham Lincoln. The letter stated that Lincoln would look better if he would grow a beard.
15-10-1883 The U.S. Supreme Court struck down part of the Civil Rights Act of 1875. It allowed for individuals and corporations to discriminate based on race.
15-10-1892 The U.S. government announced that the land in the western Montana was open to settlers. The 1.8 million acres were bought from the Crow Indians for 50 cents per acre.
15-10-1914 The Clayton Antitrust Act was passed by the U.S. Congress.
15-10-1917 Mata Hari was executed by a French firing squad. Hari was a Dutch dancer that had spied for Germany.
15-10-1931 "Cat and the Fiddle" opened in New York for the first of 395 performances.
15-10-1937 "To Have and Have Not" by Ernest Hemingway was published for the first time.
15-10-1939 New York Municipal Airport was dedicated. The name was later changed to La Guardia Airport.
15-10-1945 Pierre Laval, the former premier of Vichy France, was executed for treason.
15-10-1946 Hermann Goering, a Nazi war criminal and founder of the Gestapo, poisoned himself just hours before his scheduled execution.
15-10-1951 "I Love Lucy" premiered on CBS-TV.
15-10-1953 "Teahouse of the August Moon" opened on Broadway. It ran for 1,027 performances.
15-10-1962 The Cuban Missile Crisis began. It was on this day that U.S. intelligence personnel analyzing data discovered Soviet medium-range missle sites in Cuba. On October 22 U.S. President John F. Kennedy announced that he had ordred the naval "quarantine" of Cuba.
15-10-1964 It was announced that Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev had been removed from power. He was replaced with Alexei N. Kosygin.
15-10-1966 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson signed a bill creating the Department of Transportation.
15-10-1973 "Tomorrow" debuted on NBC-TV.
15-10-1983 U.S. Marines killed five snipers who had pinned them down in Beirut International Airport.
15-10-1989 South African officials released eight prominent political prisoners.
15-10-1989 Wayne Gretzky, while playing for the Los Angeles Kings, surpassed Gordie Howe's NHL scoring record of 1,850 career points.
15-10-1993 U.S. President Clinton sent warships to enforce trade sanctions that had been imposed on Haitian military rulers.
15-10-1993 South Africa's President F.W. de Klerk and African National Congress President Nelson Mandela were named winners of the Nobel Peace Prize for their efforts to end the apartheid system in South Africa.
15-10-1997 British Royal Air Force pilot Andy Green broke the land-speed record by driving a jet-powered car faster than the speed of sound.
15-10-1997 The Cassini-Huygens mission was launched from Cape Canaveral, FL. On January 14, 2005, a probe sent back pictures of Saturn's moon Titan during and after landing.
15-10-1998 Typhoon Zeb killed 24 people and drove 100,000 more from their homes when it hit the Philippines.
15-10-1998 The U.N. condemned the U.S. economic embargo on Cuba for the seventh year in a row.
15-10-1998 James Woods received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
15-10-2001 NASA's Galileo spacecraft passed within 112 miles of Jupiter's moon Io.
16-10-1701 The Collegiate School was founded in Killingworth, CT. The school moved to New Haven in 1745 and changed its name to Yale College.
16-10-1758 Author Noah Webster was born. He was a teacher and journalist whose name is associated with the word "dictionary."
16-10-1793 During the French Revolution, Queen Marie Antoinette was beheaded.
16-10-1829 The first modern hotel in America opened. The Tremont Hotel had 170 rooms that rented for $2 a day and included four meals.
16-10-1846 Ether, the painkiller, was used for the first time. The drug was invented by dentist William T. Morton.
16-10-1859 Abolitionist John Brown led a raid on Harper's Ferry, VA (now located in West Virginia).
16-10-1869 A hotel in Boston became the first in the U.S. to install indoor plumbing.
16-10-1898 Supreme Court Justice William Orville Douglas was born. He served for 36 years on the U.S. Supreme Court.
16-10-1916 Margaret Sanger opened the first birth control clinic in New York City, NY.
16-10-1923 Walt Disney contracted with M.J. Winkler to distribute the Alice Comedies. This event is recognized as the start of the Disney Company.
16-10-1928 Marvin Pipkin received a patent for the frosted electric light bulb.
16-10-1939 "Right To Happiness" debuted on the NBC-Blue network.
16-10-1939 "The Man Who Came to Diner" opened on Broadway.
16-10-1941 The Nazis advanced to within 60 miles of Moscow. Romanians entered Odessa, USSR, and began exterminating 150,000 Jews.
16-10-1942 The ballet "Rodeo" premiered in New York City.
16-10-1943 Chicago's new subway system was officially opened with a ribbon cutting ceremony.
16-10-1944 "The Robe," by Lloyd Douglas, was published for the first time.
16-10-1945 "His Honor the Barber" debuted on NBC Radio.
16-10-1946 10 Nazi war criminals were hanged after being condemned by the Nuremberg trials.
16-10-1955 Mrs. Jules Lederer replaced Ruth Crowley in newspaper using the name Ann Landers.
16-10-1962 U.S. President Kennedy was informed that there were missile bases in Cuba, beginning the Cuban missile crisis.
16-10-1964 China detonated its first atomic bomb becoming the world's fifth nuclear power.
16-10-1967 NATO headquarters opened in Brussels.
16-10-1970 Anwar Sadat was elected president of Egypt to succeed Gamal Abdel Nassar.
16-10-1973 Henry Kissinger and Le Duc Tho were named winners of the Nobel Peace Prize. The Vietnamese official declined the award.
16-10-1987 Rescuers freed Jessica McClure from the abandoned well that she had fallen into in Midland, TX. The was trapped for 58 hours.
16-10-1989 U.S. President Bush signed the Gramm-Rudman budget reduction law that ordered federal programs be cut by $16.1 billion.
16-10-1990 Comedian Steve Martin and his wife Victoria Tennant visited U.S. soldiers in Saudi Arabia.
16-10-1990 The play "Stand Up Tragedy" closed after only 13 performances.
16-10-1991 George Hennard crashed his truck into a Luby's Cafeteria in Killeen, TX and began a shooting rampage in which he killed 23 people before taking his own life.
16-10-1993 The U.N. Security Council approved the deployment of U.S. warships to enforce a blockade on Haiti to increase pressure on the controlling military leaders.
16-10-1994 German Chancellor Helmut Kohl was re-elected to a fourth term.
16-10-1995 The "Million Man March" took place in Washington, DC.
16-10-1997 Charles M. Schulz and his wife Jeannie announced that they would give $1 million toward the construction of a D-Day memorial to be placed in Virginia.
16-10-2000 It was announced that Chevron Corp. would be buying Texaco Inc. for $35 billion. The combined company was called Chevron Texaco Corp. and became the 4th largest oil company in the world.
16-10-2002 It was reported that North Korea had told the U.S. that it had a secret nuclear weapons program in violation of an 1994 agreement with the U.S.
16-10-2002 The Arthur Andersen accounting firm was sentenced to five years probation and fined $500,000 for obstructing a federeal investigation of the energy company Enron.
17-10-1777 American troops defeated British forces in Saratoga, NY. It was the turning point in the American Revolutionary War.
17-10-1880 Founder of the Kraft Food Company, Charles Kraft was born.
17-10-1888 The first issue of "National Geographic Magazine" was released at newsstands.
17-10-1917 The Radio Corporation of America (RCA) was formed.
17-10-1931 Al Capone was convicted on income tax evasion and was sentenced to 11 years in prison. He was released in 1939.
17-10-1933 "News-Week" appeared for the first time at newsstands. The name was later changed to "Newsweek."
17-10-1933 Dr. Albert Einstein moved to Princeton, NJ, after leaving Germany.
17-10-1939 "Mr. Smith Goes to Washington" premiered.
17-10-1945 Ava Gardner and Artie Shaw were married.
17-10-1945 Colonel Juan Peron became the dictator of Argentina after staging a coup in Buenos Aires.
17-10-1973 The Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) began an oil-embargo against several countries including the U.S. and Great Britain. The incident stemmed from Western support of Israel when Egypt and Syria attacked the nation on October 6, 1973. The embargo lasted until March of 1974.
17-10-1978 U.S. President Carter signed a bill that restored full U.S. citizenship rights to Confederate President Jefferson Davis.
17-10-1979 Mother Teresa of India was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.
17-10-1987 U.S. first lady Nancy Reagan underwent a modified radical mastectomy at Bethesda Naval Hospital in Maryland.
17-10-1989 An earthquake measuring 7.1 on the Richter Scale hit the San Francisco Bay area in California. The quake caused about 67 deaths, 3,000 injuries, and damages up to $7 billion.
17-10-1994 Israel and Jordan initialed a draft peace treaty.
17-10-1994 The Angolan government and rebels agreed to a peace treaty that ended their 19 years of civil war.
17-10-1997 The remains of revolutionary Ernesto "Che" Guevara were laid to rest in his adopted Cuba, 30 years after his execution in Bolivia.
17-10-2000 In New York City, Madame Tussaud's Wax Museum opened to the public. The 42nd Street location joined Tussaud's other exhibitions already in London, Hong Kong, Amsterdam and Las Vegas.
17-10-2000 Patrick Roy (Colorado Avalanche) achieved his 448th victory as a goalie in the NHL. Roy passed Terry Sawchuck to become the record holder for career victories.
17-10-2001 Israel's tourism minister was killed. A radical Palestinian faction claimed that it had carried out the assassination to avenge the killing of its leader by Israel 2 months earlier.
17-10-2001 Pakistan placed its armed forces on high alert because of troop movements by India in the disputed territory of Kashmir. India said that the movements were part of a normal troop rotation.
17-10-2001 The U.S. Capitol building was closed because of an outside threat. The Capitol building and all House office buildings were closed for inspection following the discovery of anthrax in a Senate office building.
17-10-2001 Italian priest Giuseppe "Beppe" Pierantoni was kidnapped by the terrorist group the "Pentagon." He was released on April 8, 2002.
17-10-2003 In the U.S., the Food and Drug Administration approved a drug, known as memantine, to help people with Alzheimer's symptoms.
17-10-2003 In Taipei, Taiwan, construction crews finished 1,676-foot-tall-building called Taipei 101. The building was planned to open for business in 2004.
17-10-2003 In northwest England, the Carnforth railway station reopened as a heritage center.
18-10-1469 Ferdinand of Aragon married Isabella of Castile. The marriage united all the dominions of Spain.
18-10-1685 King Louis XIV of France revoked the Edict of Nantes, which had established the legal toleration of the Protestant population.
18-10-1767 The Mason-Dixon line was agreed upon. It was the boundary between Maryland and Pennsylvania.
18-10-1842 Samuel Finley Breese Morse laid his first telegraph cable.
18-10-1860 British troops burned the Yuanmingyuan at the end of the Second Opium War.
18-10-1867 The U.S. took formal possession of Alaska from Russia. The land was purchased of a total of $7 million dollars (2 cents per acre).
18-10-1873 The first rules for intercollegiate football were drawn up by representatives from Rutgers, Yale, Columbia and Princeton Universities.
18-10-1892 The first long-distance telephone line between Chicago, IL, and New York City, NY, was opened.
18-10-1898 The American flag was raised in Puerto Rico only one year after the Caribbean nation won its independence from Spain.
18-10-1929 The Judicial Committee of England’s Privy Council ruled that women were to be considered as persons in Canada.
18-10-1931 Inventor Thomas Alva Edison died at the age of 84.
18-10-1943 The first broadcast of "Perry Mason" was presented on CBS Radio. The show went to TV in 1957.
18-10-1944 Czechoslovakia was invaded by the Soviets during World War II.
18-10-1944 "Forever Amber", written by Kathleen Windsor, was first published.
18-10-1950 Connie Mack announced that he was going to retire after 50 seasons as the manager of the Philadelphia Athletics.
18-10-1956 NFL commissioner Bert Bell disallowed the use of radio-equipped helmets by NFL quarterbacks.
18-10-1958 The first computer-arranged marriage took place on Art Linkletter's show.
18-10-1961 Henri Matiss' "Le Bateau" went on display at New York's Museum of Modern Art. It was discovered 46 days later that the painting had been hanging upside down.
18-10-1967 The American League granted permission for the A's to move to Oakland. Also, new franchises were awarded to Kansas City and Seattle.
18-10-1968 Two black athletes, Tommie Smith and John Carlos, were suspended by the U.S. Olympic Committee for giving a "black power" salute during a ceremony in Mexico City.
18-10-1969 The U.S. government banned artificial sweeteners due to evidence that they caused cancer.
18-10-1970 Quebec's minister of labor was found strangled to death after eight days of being held captive by the Quebec Liberation Front (FLQ).
18-10-1971 After 34 years, the final issue of "Look" magazine was published.
18-10-1977 A German special forces team stormed a hijacked Lufthansa airliner and killed all four hijackers and freed 86 hostages. The Palestinian hijackers had demanded the release of members of the Red Army Faction.
18-10-1977 Reggie Jackson tied Babe Ruth's record for hitting three homeruns in a single World Series game. Jackson was only the second player to achieve this.
18-10-1983 General Motors agreed to hire more women and minorities for five years as part of a settlement with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission.
18-10-1985 South African authorities hanged black activist Benjamin Moloise. Moloise had been convicted of murdering a police officer.
18-10-1989 Egon Krenz became the leader of East Germany after Erich Honecker was ousted. Honeker had been in power for 18 years.
18-10-1989 The space shuttle Atlantis was launched on a mission that included the deployment of the Galileo space probe.
18-10-1990 Iraq made an offer to the world that it would sell oil for $21 a barrel. The price level was the same as it had been before the invasion of Kuwait.
18-10-1997 A monument honoring U.S. servicewomen, past and present, was dedicated at Arlington National Cemetery.
18-10-2001 In New York, four defendants were convicted for the 1998 bombings of two U.S. embassies in Africa.
18-10-2001 It was announced that a New Jersey letter carrier and an employee in the office of CBS news anchorman Dan Rather's office had tested positive for skin anthrax.
19-10-1765 In the U.S., The Stamp Act Congress met and drew up a declaration of rights and liberties.
19-10-1781 British General Charles Lord Cornwallis surrendered to U.S. General George Washington at Yorktown, Virginia. It was to be the last major battle of the American Revolutionary War.
19-10-1812 Napoleon Bonaparte's French forces began their retreat out of Russia after a month of chasing the retreating Russian army.
19-10-1885 Charles Merrill, founder of Merrill-Lynch, was born.
19-10-1914 In the U.S., government owned vehicles were first used to pick up mail in Washington, DC.
19-10-1915 The U.S. recognized General Venustiano Carranza as the president of Mexico. The U.S. imposed embargo to all parts of Mexico except where Carranza was in control.
19-10-1933 Basketball was introduced to the 1936 Olympic Games by the Berlin Organization Committee.
19-10-1937 "Woman's Day" was published for the first time.
19-10-1937 "Big Town" made its debut on CBS.
19-10-1943 The Moscow Conference of Foreign Ministers began in Russia during World War II. Delegates from the U.S.S.R., Great Britain, the U.S., and China met to discuss war aims and cooperation between the nations.
19-10-1944 The play "I Remember Mama" opened on Broadway. Marlon Brando made his debut with his appearance.
19-10-1944 The U.S. Navy announced that black women would be allowed into Women Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service (WAVES).
19-10-1950 The United Nations forces entered the North Korean capital of Pyongyang.
19-10-1951 U.S. President Truman singed an act officially ending the state of war with Germany.
19-10-1959 Patty Duke, at the age of 12, made her Broadway debut in "The Miracle Worker." The play lasted for 700 performances.
19-10-1960 The United States imposed an embargo on exports to Cuba covering all commodities except medical supplies and certain food products.
19-10-1969 U.S. Vice President Spiro Agnew referred to anti-Vietnam War protesters "an effete corps of impudent snobs."
19-10-1974 The news program "Weekend" debuted on NBC.
19-10-1977 The Concorde made its first landing in New York City.
19-10-1983 The U.S. Senate approved a bill establishing a national holiday in honor of Martin Luther King Jr.
19-10-1984 Four U.S. employees of the CIA were killed in El Salvador when their plane crashed.
19-10-1987 The Dow Jones industrial average dropped 508 points. It was the worst one-day percentage decline, 22.6%, in history.
19-10-1989 The Guilford Four were cleared of all charges and released after 14 years in prison. The charges were from the 1975 IRA bombings of public houses in Guildford and Woolrich, England.
19-10-1989 The U.S. Senate rejected a proposed constitutional amendment that barred the desecration of the American flag.
19-10-1993 Benazir Bhutto was returned to the premiership of Pakistan.
19-10-1998 In Washington, DC, Microsoft went on trial to defend against an antitrust case.
19-10-1998 Fires in Nigeria swept through villages killing 500 people.
19-10-1998 Former heavyweight champion Mike Tyson got his boxing license back after he had lost it for biting Evander Holyfield's ear during a fight.
19-10-2001 Two U.S. Army Rangers were killed in a helicopter crash in Pakistan. The deaths were the first American deaths of the military campaign in Afghanistan.
19-10-2001 It was reported that a New Jersey postal worker and a New York Post employee had tested positive for skin anthrax.
19-10-2002 In York, PA, former mayor Charlie Robertson was acquitted and two other men were convicted in the shotgun murder of a young black woman during race riots in 1969.
19-10-2003 In London, magician David Blaine emerged from a clear plastic box and then suspended by a crane over the banks of the Thames River. He survived only on water for 44 days. Blaine had entered the box on September 5.
19-10-2006 The Dow Jones industrial average ended the day at 12,011.73. It was the first close above 12,000.
20-10-1740 Maria Theresa became the ruler of Austria, Hungary and Bohemia with the death of her father, Holy Roman Emperor Charles VI.
20-10-1774 The new Continental Congress, the governing body of America’s colonies, passed an order proclaiming that all citizens of the colonies "discountenance and discourage all horse racing and all kinds of gaming, cock fighting, exhibitions of shows, plays and other expensive diversions and entertainment."
20-10-1803 The U.S. Senate approved the Louisiana Purchase.
20-10-1818 The U.S. and Great Britain established the boundary between the U.S. and Canada to be the 49th parallel.
20-10-1827 The Battle of Navarino took place during the Greek War for Independence.
20-10-1873 A Hippodrome was opened in New York City by showman Phineus T. (P.T.) Barnum.
20-10-1892 The city of Chicago dedicated the World's Columbian Exposition.
20-10-1903 A joint commission ruled in favor of the U.S. concerning a dispute over the boundary between Canada and the District of Alaska.
20-10-1910 A baseball with a cork center was used in a World Series game for the first time.
20-10-1930 "The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes" debuted on NBC radio.
20-10-1935 Mao Zedong arrived in Hanoi after his Long March that took just over a year. He then set up the Chinese Communist Headquarters.
20-10-1942 Pierre Laval told the French labor that they must serve in Germany.
20-10-1944 Allied forces invaded the Philippines.
20-10-1944 During World War II, the Yugoslav cities of Belgrade and Dubrovnik were liberated.
20-10-1947 Hollywood came under scrutiny as the House Un-American Activities Committee opened hearings into alleged Communist influence within the motion picture industry.
20-10-1952 The Mau Mau uprising against white settlers began in Kenya.
20-10-1955 "No Time for Sergeants" opened on Broadway.
20-10-1957 Walter Cronkite began hosting "The 20th Century." The show aired until January 4, 1970.
20-10-1967 Seven men were convicted in Meridian, MS, on charges of violating the civil rights of three civil rights workers. Of the men convicted one was a Ku Klux Klan leader and another was a sheriff's deputy.
20-10-1968 Jackie Lee Bouvier Kennedy married Aristotle Onassis.
20-10-1976 More than 70 people were killed when the Norwegian tanker Frosta collided with the ferryboat George Prince on the Mississippi River.
20-10-1979 The John F. Kennedy Library in Boston was dedicated.
20-10-1984 The U.S. State Department reduced the number of Americans assigned to the U.S. embassy in Beirut, Lebanon.
20-10-1986 American mercenary Eugene Hasenfus was formally charged by the Nicaraguan government on several charges including terrorism.
20-10-1993 Attorney General Janet Reno warned the TV industry to limit the violence in their programs.
20-10-1995 Britain, France and the U.S. announced a treaty that banned atomic blasts in the South Pacific.
20-10-2003 A 40-year-old man went over Niagara Falls without safety devices and survived. He was charged with illegally performing a stunt.
21-10-1797 "Old Ironsides," the U.S. Navy frigate Constitution, was launched in Boston's harbor.
21-10-1805 The Battle of Trafalgar occurred off the coast of Spain. The British defeated the French and Spanish fleet.
21-10-1849 The first tattooed man, James F. O’Connell, was put on exhibition at the Franklin Theatre in New York City, NY.
21-10-1858 The Can-Can was performed for the first time in Paris.
21-10-1879 Thomas Edison invented the electric incandescent lamp. It would last 13 1/2 hours before it would burn out.
21-10-1917 The first U.S. soldiers entered combat during World War I near Nancy, France.
21-10-1918 Margaret Owen set a typing speed record of 170 words per minute on a manual typewriter.
21-10-1925 The photoelectric cell was first demonstrated at the Electric Show in New York City, NY.
21-10-1925 The U.S. Treasury Department announced that it had fined 29,620 people for prohibition (of alcohol) violations.
21-10-1944 During World War II, the German city of Aachen was captured by U.S. troops.
21-10-1945 Women in France were allowed to vote for the first time.
21-10-1950 Chinese forces invaded Tibet.
21-10-1959 The Guggenheim Museum was opened to the public in New York. The building was designed by Frank Lloyd Wright.
21-10-1966 In south Wales, 140 people were killed by a coal waste landslide engulfed a school and several houses.
21-10-1967 Thousands of demonstrators marched in Washington, DC, in opposition to the Vietnam War.
21-10-1980 The Philadelphia Phillies won their first World Series.
21-10-1983 The Pentagon reported that 2,000 Marines were headed to Grenada to protect and evacuate Americans living there.
21-10-1986 Pro-Iranian kidnappers in Lebanon claimed that they had abducted American writer Edward Tracy. He was not released until August of 1991.
21-10-1986 The U.S. ordered 55 Soviet diplomats to leave. The action was in reaction to the Soviet Union expelling five American diplomats.
21-10-1988 Former Philippine President Ferdinand E. Marcos and his wife, Imelda, were indicted in New York on fraud and racketeering charges. Marcos died before his trial and Imelda was acquitted in 1990.
21-10-1991 Jesse Turner, an American hostage in Lebanon, was released after nearly five years of being imprisoned.
21-10-1993 The play "The Twilight of the Golds" opened.
21-10-1994 North Korea and the U.S. signed an agreement requiring North Korea to halt its nuclear program and agree to inspections.
21-10-1994 Rosario Ames, the wife of CIA agent Aldrich Ames, was sentenced to five years in prison for her role in her husband's espionage.
21-10-1998 68 people were arrested in Indonesia for the killing spree that left nine suspected murderers dead.
21-10-1998 The New York Yankees set a major league baseball record of 125 victories for the regular and postseason combined.
21-10-1998 Cancer specialist Dr. Jane Henney became the FDA's first female commissioner.
21-10-2003 The U.S. Senate voted to ban what was known as partial birth abortions.
21-10-2003 North Korea rejected U.S. President Bush's offer of a written pledge not to attack in exchange for the communist nation agreeing to end its nuclear weapons program.
22-10-1746 The College of New Jersey was officially chartered. It later became known as Princeton University.
22-10-1797 Andre-Jacques Garnerin made the first recorded parachute jump. He made the jump from about 3,000 feet.
22-10-1836 Sam Houston was inaugurated as the first constitutionally elected president of the Republic of Texas.
22-10-1844 This day is recognized as "The Great Disappointment" among those who practiced Millerism. The world was expected to come to an end according to the followers of William Miller.
22-10-1879 Thomas Edison conducted his first successful experiment with a high-resistance carbon filament.
22-10-1883 The New York Horse show opened. The first national horse show was formed by the newly organized National Horse Show Association of America.
22-10-1907 The Panic of 1907 began when depositors began withdrawing money from many New York banks.
22-10-1934 Charles "Pretty Boy" Floyd, the notorious bank robber, was shot and killed by Federal agents in East Liverpool, OH.
22-10-1939 The first televised pro football game was telecast from New York. Brooklyn defeated Philadelphia 23-14.
22-10-1950 The Los Angeles Rams set an NFL record by defeating the Baltimore Colts 70-27. It was a record score for a regular season game.
22-10-1954 The Federal Republic of Germany was invited to join the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO).
22-10-1959 "Take Me Along" opened on Broadway.
22-10-1962 U.S. President Kennedy went on radio and television to inform the United States about his order to send U.S. forces to blockade Cuba. The blockade was in response to the discovery of Soviet missile bases on the island.
22-10-1968 Apollo 7 splashed down in the Atlantic Ocean. The spacecraft had orbited the Earth 163 times.
22-10-1975 Air Force Technical Sergeant Leonard Matlovich was discharged after publicly declaring his homosexuality. His tombstone reads " "A gay Vietnam Veteran. When I was in the military they gave me a medal for killing two men and a discharge for loving one."
22-10-1979 The ousted Shah of Iran, Mohammad Riza Pahlavi was allowed into the U.S. for medical treatment.
22-10-1981 The Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization was decertified by the federal government for its strike the previous August.
22-10-1983 At the Augusta National Golf Course in Georgia, an armed man crashed a truck through front gates and demanded to speak with U.S. President Ronald Reagan.
22-10-1986 U.S. President Reagan signed the Tax Reform Act of 1986 into law.
22-10-1991 The European Community and the European Free Trade Association agreed to create a free trade zone of 19 nations by the year 1993.
22-10-1995 The 50th anniversary of the United Nations was marked by a record number of world leaders gathering.
22-10-1995 British writer Sir Kingsley Amis died at the age of 73.
22-10-1998 The United Nations announced that over 2 million children had been killed in war as innocent victims since 1987.
22-10-1998 Pakistan's carpet weaving industry announced that they would begin to phase out child labor.
22-10-1999 China ended its first-ever human rights conference in which it defied Western definitions of civil liberties.
22-10-1999 The U.N. Security Council voted to send 6,000 troops to Sierra Leone to oversee a peace plan that had been signed in July.
23-10-1864 During the U.S. Civil War, Union forces led by Gen. Samuel R. Curtis defeated the Confederate forces in Missouri that were under Gen. Stirling Price.
23-10-1869 John (William) Heisman was born. He is recognized as one of the greatest innovators of the game of football.
23-10-1910 Blanche S. Scott became the first woman to make a public solo airplane flight.
23-10-1915 The first U.S. championship horseshoe tourney was held in Kellerton, IA.
23-10-1915 Approximately 25,000 women demanded the right to vote with a march in New York City, NY.
23-10-1929 In the U.S., the Dow Jones Industrial Average plunged starting the stock-market crash that began the Great Depression.
23-10-1930 J.K. Scott won the first miniature golf tournament. The event was held in Chattanooga, TN.
23-10-1942 During World War II, the British began a major offensive against Axis forces at El Alamein, Egypt.
23-10-1944 During World War II, the Battle of Leyte Gulf began.
23-10-1946 The United Nations General Assembly convened in New York for the first time.
23-10-1956 Hungarian citizens began an uprising against Soviet occupation. On November 4, 1956 Soviet forces enter Hungar and eventually suppress the uprising.
23-10-1956 NBC broadcasted the first videotape recording. The tape of Jonathan Winters was seen coast to coast in the U.S.
23-10-1958 Russian poet and novelist Boris Pasternak was awarded the Nobel Prize for literature. He was forced to refuse the honor due to negative Soviet reaction. Pasternak won the award for writing "Dr. Zhivago".
23-10-1962 During the Cuban Missile Crisis, the U.S. naval "quarantine" of Cuba was approved by the Council of the Organization of American States (OAS).
23-10-1962 The U.S. Navy reconnaissance squadron VFP-62 began overflights of Cuba under the code name "Blue Moon."
23-10-1971 The U.N. General Assembly voted to expel Taiwan and seat Communist China.
23-10-1973 U.S. President Richard M. Nixon agreed to turn over the subpoenaed tapes concerning the Watergate affair.
23-10-1978 China and Japan formally ended four decades of hostility when they exchanged treaty ratifications.
23-10-1980 The resignation of Soviet Premier Alexei N. Kosygin was announced.
23-10-1983 At Beirut International Airport, a suicide bomber destroyed a U.S. Marine compound and killed 241 U.S. Marines and sailors. 58 French paratroopers were killed in a near-simultaneous attack.
23-10-1984 "NBC Nightly News" aired footage of the severe drought in Ethiopia.
23-10-1985 U.S. President Reagan arrived in New York to address the U.N. General Assembly.
23-10-1989 In Boston, MA, Charles Stuart claimed he and his pregnant wife, Carol, had been shot in their car by a black robber. Carol Stuart and her prematurely delivered baby died. Charles Stuart later died, an apparent suicide, after he was implicated in the murder of his wife and child.
23-10-1989 Hungary became an independent republic, after 33 years of Soviet rule.
23-10-1992 Japanese Emperor Akihito became the first Japanese emperor to stand on Chinese soil.
23-10-1992 A former French health official was sentenced to four years in prison for allowing 1,200 hemophiliacs to receive AIDS-tainted blood.
23-10-1993 Joe Carter (Toronto Blue Jays) became only the second player to end the World Series with a homerun.
23-10-1995 Russian President Boris Yeltsin and U.S. President Bill Clinton agree to a joint peacekeeping effort in the war-torn Bosnia.
23-10-1996 The civil trial of O.J. Simpson opened in Santa Monica, CA. Simpson was later found liable in the deaths of his ex-wife, Nicole and her friend, Ronald Goldman.
23-10-1998 Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Palestinian Chairman Yasser Arafat reach a breakthrough in a land-for-peace West Bank accord.
23-10-1998 Japan nationalized its first bank since World War II.
23-10-1998 Dr. Barnett Slepian, a doctor who performed legal abortions, was killed at his home in suburban Buffalo, NY, by sniper fire through his kitchen window. James Kopp was charged with second-degree murder.
23-10-2000 Universal Studios Consumer Products Group (USCPG) and Amblin Entertainment announced an unprecedented and exclusive three-year worldwide merchandising program with Toys "R" Us, Inc. The deal was for the rights to exclusive "E.T. The Extra-Terrestrial" merchandise starting in fall 2001. The film was scheduled for re-release in the spring of 2002.
24-10-1537 Jane Seymour, the third wife of England's King Henry VIII, died after giving birth to Prince Edward. Prince Edward became King Edward VI.
24-10-1632 Scientist Anthony van Leeuwenhoek was born in Delft, Holland. He created the first microscope lenses that were powerful enough to observe single-celled animals.
24-10-1648 The Holy Roman Empire was effectively destroyed by the Peace of Westphalia that brought an end to the Thirty Years War.
24-10-1788 Poet Sarah Joseph Hale was born. She wrote the poem "Mary Had A Little Lamb."
24-10-1795 The country of Poland was divided up between Austria, Prussia, and Russia.
24-10-1830 Belva Lockwood was born. She was the first woman formally nominated for the U.S. Presidency.
24-10-1836 Alonzo D. Phillips received a patent for the phosphorous friction safety match.
24-10-1861 The first transcontinental telegraph message was sent when Justice Stephen J. Field of California transmitted a telegram to U.S. President Lincoln.
24-10-1901 Daredevil Anna Edson Taylor became the first person to go over Niagara Falls in a wooden barrel. She was 63 years old.
24-10-1929 In the U.S., investors dumped more than 13 million shares on the stock market. The day is known as "Black Thursday."
24-10-1931 The George Washington Bridge opened for traffic between New York and New Jersey.
24-10-1939 Nylon stockings were sold to the public for the first time in Wilmington, DE.
24-10-1940 In the U.S., the 40-hour workweek went into effect under the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938.
24-10-1945 The United Nations (UN) was formally established less than a month after the end of World War II. The Charter was ratified by China, France, the Soviet Union, the United Kingdom, the United States and by a majority of other signatories.
24-10-1945 Pierre Laval of France and Vidkum Abraham Quisling of Norway were executed. The two men were recognized as the two most prominent collaborators of the Nazis.
24-10-1948 The term "cold war" was used for the first time. It was in a speech by Bernard Baruch before the Senate War Investigating Committee.
24-10-1949 The cornerstone for the U.N. Headquarters was laid in New York City.
24-10-1960 All remaining American-owned property in Cuba was nationalized. The process of nationalizing all U.S. and foreign-owned property in Cuban had begun on August 6, 1960.
24-10-1962 During the Cuban Missile Crisis, U.S. military forces went on the highest alert in the postwar era in preparation for a possible full-scale war with the Soviet Union. The U.S. blockade of Cuba officially began on this day.
24-10-1969 Richard Burton bought his wife Elizabeth Taylor a 69-carat Cartier diamond ring for $1.5 million. Burton presented the ring to Taylor several days later.
24-10-1986 Britain broke off relations with Syria after a Jordanian was convicted in an attempted bombing. The evidence in the trial led to the belief that Syria was involved in the attack on the Israeli jetliner.
24-10-1989 Reverend Jim Bakker was sentenced to 45 years in prison and fined $500,000 for his conviction on 24 counts of fraud. In 1991, his sentence was reduced to eighteen years and he was released on parole after a total five years in prison.
24-10-1992 The Toronto Blue Jays became the first non-U.S. team to win the World Series.
24-10-1997 In Arlington, VA, former NBC sportscaster Marv Albert was spared a jail sentence after a courtroom apology to the woman he'd bitten during a sexual encounter.
24-10-1999 An Israeli court sentenced American teen-ager Samuel Sheinbein to 24 years in prison. The crime was killing an acquaintance in Maryland in 1997.
24-10-2001 The U.S. House of Representatives approved legislation that gave police the power to secretly search homes, tap all of a person's telephone conversation and track people's use of the Internet.
24-10-2001 The U.S. stamp "United We Stand" was dedicated.
24-10-2001 NASA's 2001 Mars Odyssey spacecraft successfully entered orbit around Mars.
24-10-2002 Microsoft Corp. and Walt Disney Co. announced the release of an upgraded MSN Internet service with Disney content.
24-10-2003 In London, the last commercial supersonic Concorde flight landed.
25-10-1400 Geoffrey Chaucer died at the age of 57. He was the first poet to be buried in Westminster Abbey.
25-10-1415 In Northern France, England won the Battle of Agincourt over France during the Hundred Years' War. Almost 6000 Frenchmen were killed while fewer than 400 were lost by the English.
25-10-1760 George III took the British throne after the death of King George II, his grandfather.
25-10-1812 During the War of 1812, the U.S. frigate United States captured the British vessel Macedonian.
25-10-1854 The Charge of the Light Brigade took place during the Crimean War. The British were winning the Battle of Balaclava when Lord James Cardigan received an order to attack the Russians. He took his troops into a valley and suffered 40 percent caualties. Later it was revealed that the order was the result of confusion and was not given intentionally.
25-10-1870 The first U.S. trademark was given. The recipient was the Averill Chemical Paint Company of New York City.
25-10-1881 The founder of "Cubism," Pablo Picasso, was born in Malaga, Spain.
25-10-1888 Richard Byrd, the first person to see the North Pole, was born.
25-10-1917 The Bolsheviks (Communists) under Vladimir Ilyich Lenin seized power in Russia.
25-10-1918 The Canadian steamship Princess Sophia hit the reef off the coast of Alaska. Nearly 400 people died.
25-10-1920 King Alexander of Greece died from blood poisoning that resulted from a bite from his pet monkey.
25-10-1929 Alber B. Fall, of U.S. President Harding's cabinet, was found guilty of taking a bribe. He was sentenced to a year in prison and fined $100,000.
25-10-1939 "The Time of Your Life," by William Saroyan, opened in New York.
25-10-1951 In Panmunjom, peace talks concerning the Korean War resumed after 63 days.
25-10-1954 A U.S. cabinet meeting was televised for the first time.
25-10-1955 The microwave oven, for home use, was introduced by The Tappan Company.
25-10-1958 U.S. Marines withdrew form Beirut, Lebanon. They had been sent in on July 25, 1958, to protect the nation's pro-Western government.
25-10-1960 The Accutron watch by the Bulova Watch Company was introduced.
25-10-1962 U.S. Ambassador Adlai Stevenson presented photographic evidence to the United Nations Security Council. The photos were of Soviet missile bases in Cuba.
25-10-1962 American author John Steinbeck was awarded the Nobel Prize in literature.
25-10-1971 The U.N. General Assembly voted to expel Taiwan and admit mainland China.
25-10-1983 U.S. troops and soldiers from six Caribbean nations invaded Grenada to restore order and provide protection to U.S. citizens after a recent coup within Grenada's Communist (pro-Cuban) government.
25-10-1990 It was announced by U.S. Defense Secretary Dick Cheney that the Pentagon was planning to send 100,000 more troops to Saudi Arabia.
25-10-1994 Susan Smith of Union, SC, claimed that a black carjacker had driven off with her two sons. Smith was later convicted of drowning her children in a nearby lake.
25-10-1999 Golfer Payne Stewart and five others were killed when their Learjet crashed in South Dakota. The plane flew uncontrolled for four hours before the crash.
25-10-2000 AT&T Corp. announced that it would restructure into a family of four separately traded companies (consumer, business, broadband and wireless).
25-10-2001 It was announced that scientists had unearthed the remains of an ancient crocodile which lived 110 million years ago. The animal, found in Gadoufaoua, Niger, grew as long as 40 feet and weighed as much as eight metric tons.
26-10-1774 The First Continental Congress of the U.S. adjourned in Philadelphia.
26-10-1825 The Erie Canal opened in upstate New York. The 363-mile canal connected Lake Erie and the Hudson River at a cost of $7,602,000.
26-10-1854 Charles William Post was born. He was the inventor of "Grape Nuts," "Postum" and "Post Toasties."
26-10-1858 H.E. Smith patented the rotary-motion washing machine.
26-10-1881 The "Gunfight at the OK Corral" took place in Tombstone, AZ. The fight was between Wyatt Earp, his two brothers and Doc Holiday and the Ike Clanton Gang.
26-10-1905 Norway gained independence from Sweden.
26-10-1914 Jackie Coogan was born. He became the first child to appear in a full-length movie, "The Kid."
26-10-1942 The U.S. ship Hornet was sunk in the Battle of Santa Cruz during World War II.
26-10-1944 During World War II, the Battle of Leyte Gulf ended. The battle was won by American forces and brought the end of the Pacific phase of World War II into sight.
26-10-1949 U.S. President Harry Truman raised the minimum wage from 40 to 75 cents an hour.
26-10-1951 Winston Churchill became the prime minister of Great Britain.
26-10-1955 New York City's "The Village Voice" was first published.
26-10-1957 The Soviet Union announced that defense minister Marchal Georgi Zhukov had been relieved of his duties.
26-10-1958 Pan American Airways flew its first Boeing 707 jetliner from New York City to Paris.
26-10-1962 The Soviet Union made an offer to end the Cuban Missile Crisis by taking their missile bases out of Cuba if the U.S. agreed to not invade Cuba and would remove Jupiter missiles in Turkey.
26-10-1967 The Shah of Iran crowned himself and his Queen after 26 years on the Peacock Throne.
26-10-1970 "Doonesbury," the comic strip by Gary Trudeau, premiered in 28 newspapers across the U.S.
26-10-1972 U.S. National security adviser Henry Kissinger declared, "Peace is at hand" in Vietnam.
26-10-1975 Anwar Sadat became the first Egyptian president to officially visit to the United States.
26-10-1977 The experimental space shuttle Enterprise successfully landed at Edwards Air Force Base in California.
26-10-1979 South Korean President Park Chung-hee was shot to death by Kim Jae-kyu, the head of the Korean Central Intelligence Agency.
26-10-1980 Israeli President Yitzhak Navon became the first Israeli head of state to visit Egypt.
26-10-1984 "Baby Fae" was given the heart of baboon after being born with a severe heart defect. She lived for 21 days with the animal heart.
26-10-1985 Approximately 110,000 people marched past the U.S. and Soviet embassies in London to pressure the two countries to end their arms race.
26-10-1988 Roussel Uclaf, a French pharmaceutical company, announced it was halting the worldwide distribution of RU-486. The pill is used to induce abortions. The French government made the company reverse itself two days later.
26-10-1988 Two whales were freed by Soviet and American icebreakers. The whales had been trapped for nearly 3 weeks in an Arctic ice pack.
26-10-1990 The U.S. State Department issued a warning that terrorists could be planning an attack on a passenger ship or aircraft.
26-10-1990 William S. Paley died at the age of 89. He was the founder of CBS Inc.
26-10-1990 Wayne Gretzky became the first NHL player to reach 2,000 points.
26-10-1991 Former Washington Mayor Marion Barry arrived at a federal correctional institution in Petersburg, VA, to begin serving a six-month sentence for cocaine possession.
26-10-1992 General Motors Corp. Chairman Robert Stempel resigned after the company recorded its highest losses in history.
26-10-1992 In Canada, voters rejected the Charlottetown accord, which was designed to unify the country.
26-10-1993 Deborah Gore Dean was convicted of 12 felony counts of defrauding the U.S. government and lying to the U.S. Congress. Dean was a central figure in the Reagan-era HUD scandal.
26-10-1994 Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin of Israel and Prime Minister Abdel Salam Majali of Jordan signed a peace treaty.
26-10-1995 Alec Baldwin got into a fight with a paparazzi in front of his home when he and his wife Kim Bassinger were bringing their first baby home from the hospital.
26-10-1995 Mario Lemieux (Pittsburgh Penguins) scored his 500th National Hockey League (NHL) career goal against the New York Islanders in his 605th game. He became the second-fastest player to attain the plateau. Wayne Gretzky had reached 600 goals by his 575th NHL game.
26-10-1996 Federal prosecutors cleared Richard Jewell as a suspect in the Olympic park bombing.
26-10-1998 A French lab found a nerve agent on an Iraqi missile warhead.
26-10-2001 It was announced that Fort Worth's Lockheed Martin won a defense contract for $200 billion over 40 years. The contract, for the "joint strike fighter," was the largest defense contract in history.
26-10-2002 Russian authorities pumped a gas into a theater where separatist rebels held over 800 hostages. The gas killed 116 hostages and all 50 hostage-takers were killed by the gas or gunshot wounds.
27-10-1659 William Robinson and Marmaduke Stevenson became the first Quakers to be executed in America.
27-10-1787 The first of the Federalist Papers were published in the New York Independent. The series of 85 essays, written by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison and John Jay, were published under the pen name "Publius."
27-10-1795 The United States and Spain signed the Treaty of San Lorenzo. The treaty is also known as "Pinckney's Treaty."
27-10-1858 Roland Macy opened Macy's Department Store in New York City. It was Macy's eighth business adventure, the other seven failed.
27-10-1878 The Manhattan Savings Bank in New York City was robbed of over $3,000,000. The robbery was credited to George "Western" Leslie even though there was not enough evidence to convict him, only two of his associates were convicted.
27-10-1880 Theodore Roosevelt married Alice Lee.
27-10-1904 The New York subway system officially opened. It was the first rapid-transit subway system in America.
27-10-1925 Fred Waller received a patent for water skis.
27-10-1927 The first newsreel featuring sound was released in New York.
27-10-1931 Chuhei Numbu of Japan set a long jump record at 26' 2 1/4".
27-10-1938 Du Pont announced "nylon" as the new name for its new synthetic yarn.
27-10-1947 "You Bet Your Life," the radio show starring Grouch Marx, premiered on ABC. It was later shown on NBC television.
27-10-1954 Marilyn Monrow and Joe DiMaggio were divorced. They had been married on January 14, 1954.
27-10-1954 The first Walt Disney television show "Disneyland" premiered on ABC.
27-10-1962 The Soviet Union adds to the Cuban Missile Crisis by calling for the dismantling of U.S. missile basis in Turkey. U.S. President Kennedy agreed to the new aspect of the agreement.
27-10-1978 Egyptian President Anwar Sadat and Israeli Prime Minister Menachem Begin were named winners of the Nobel Peace Prize for their progress toward achieving a Middle East accord.
27-10-1994 The U.S. Justice Department announced that the U.S. prison population had exceeded one million for the first time in American history.
27-10-1997 The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 554.26 points. The stock market was shut down for the first time since the 1981 assassination attempt on U.S. President Reagan.
27-10-1998 The reunion episode "CHiPs '99" aired for the first time on the cable network TNT.
27-10-1998 A car bomb exploded in the car of a Palestinian leader Mahmoud Majzoub. Majzoub, his wife, and his nine-month-old son, and a passerby were injured in the blast.
27-10-1998 "Lion King II: Simba's Pride" was released on video.
27-10-1998 Two boats hit head-on in India. One of the boats suffered no damage. The other sank and 60 people were missing.
27-10-1999 Armenia's Prime Minister and seven other government officials were killed during a parliamentary session. It was the believed that the gunmen were staging a coup.
27-10-2002 The Anaheim Angels won their first World Series. The beat the San Francisco Giants in Game 7 of the series.
27-10-2002 Emmitt Smith (Dallas Cowboys) became the all-time leading rusher in the NFL when he extended his career yardage to 16,743. He achieved the record in his 193rd game. He also scored his 150th career touchdown.
27-10-2002 Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva was elected president of Brazil in a runoff. He was the country's first elected leftist leader.
27-10-2003 Bank of America Corp. announced it had agreed to buy FleetBoston Financial Corp. The deal created the second largest banking company in the U.S.
28-10-1636 Harvard College was founded in Massachusetts. The original name was Court of Massachusetts Bay Colony. It was the first school of higher education in America.
28-10-1776 The Battle of White Plains took place during the American Revolutionary War.
28-10-1793 Eli Whitney applied for a patent for his cotton gin.
28-10-1886 The Statue of Liberty was dedicated in New York Harbor by U.S. President Cleveland. The statue weighs 225 tons and is 152 feet tall. It was originally known as "Liberty Enlightening the World."
28-10-1904 The St. Louis Police Department became the first to use fingerprinting.
28-10-1919 The U.S. Congress enacted the Volstead Act, also known as the National Prohibition Act. Prohibition was repealed in 1933 with the passing of the 21st Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.
28-10-1922 Benito Mussolini took control of the Italian government and introduced fascism to Italy.
28-10-1936 The Statue of Liberty was rededicated by U.S. President Roosevelt on its 50th anniversary.
28-10-1940 During World War II, Italy invaded Greece.
28-10-1949 U.S. President Harry Truman swore in Eugenie Moore Anderson as the U.S. ambassador to Denmark. Anderson was the first woman to hold the post of ambassador.
28-10-1958 Angelo Giuseppe Roncalli was elected Pope. He took the name John XXIII.
28-10-1962 Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev informed the U.S. that he had ordered the dismantling of Soviet missile bases in Cuba.
28-10-1965 Pope Paul VI issued a decree absolving Jews of collective guilt for the crucifixion of Jesus Christ.
28-10-1965 The Gateway Arch along the waterfront in St. Louis, MO, was completed.
28-10-1976 John D. Erlichman, a former aide to U.S. President Richard Nixon, entered a federal prison camp in Safford, AZ, to begin serving his sentence for Watergate-related convictions.
28-10-1983 The U.S. vetoed a U.N. Security Council resolution "deeply deploring" the ongoing U.S.-led invasion of Grenada.
28-10-1984 The New York City Marathon was marred by its first fatality when a French runner who collapsed and died.
28-10-1985 John A. Walker Jr. and his son, Michael Lance Walker, pled guilty to charges of spying for the Soviet Union.
28-10-1986 The centennial of the Statue of Liberty was celebrated in New York.
28-10-1988 Roussel Uclaf, a French manufacturer that produces the abortion pill RU486, announced it would resume distribution of the drug after the government of France demanded it do so.
28-10-1990 Iraq announced that it was halting gasoline rationing.
28-10-1993 Ousted Haitian President Jean-Bertrand Aristide, called for a complete blockade of Haiti to force out the military leaders.
28-10-1994 U.S. President Clinton visited Kuwait and implied that all the troops there would be home by Christmas.
28-10-1996 The Dow Jones Industial Average gained a record 337.17 points (or 5%). The day before the Dow had dropped 554.26 points (or 7%).
28-10-1998 An Air China jet was hijacked and flown to Taiwan by pilot Yuan Bin. He was upset with his pay and working conditions. The plane arrived safely and Yuan Bin was taken into custody.
29-10-1618 Sir Walter Raleigh was beheaded under a sentence that had been brought against him 15 years earlier for conspiracy against King James I.
29-10-1652 The Massachusetts Bay Colony proclaimed itself to be an independent commonwealth.
29-10-1682 William Penn landed at what is now Chester, PA. He was the founder of Pennsylvania.
29-10-1863 The International Committee of the Red Cross was founded.
29-10-1901 Leon Czolgosz, the assassin of U.S. President McKinley, was electrocuted.
29-10-1911 American newspaperman Joseph Pulitzer died.
29-10-1923 Turkey formally became a republic after the dissolution of the Ottoman Empire. The first president was Mustafa Kemal, later known as Kemal Ataturk.
29-10-1929 America's Great Depression began with the crash of the Wall Street stock market.
29-10-1940 The first peacetime military draft began in the U.S.
29-10-1945 The first ballpoint pens to be made commercially went on sale at Gimbels Department Store in New York at the price of $12.50 each.
29-10-1956 Israel invaded Egypt's Sinai Peninsula during the Suez Canal Crisis.
29-10-1956 "The Huntley-Brinkley Report" premiered on NBC. The show replaced "The Camel News Caravan."
29-10-1959 General Mills became the first corporation to use close-circuit television.
29-10-1960 Muhammad Ali (Cassius Clay) won his first professional fight.
29-10-1964 Three men stole the star of India and other gems from the American Museum of Natural History in New York. The men were later convicted of the crime.
29-10-1966 The National Organization for Women was founded.
29-10-1969 The U.S. Supreme Court ordered an immediate end to all school segregation.
29-10-1973 O.J. Simpson, of the Buffalo Bills, set two NFL records. He carried the ball 39 times and he ran 157 yards putting him over 1,000 yards at the seventh game of the season.
29-10-1974 U.S. President Gerald Ford signed a new law forbidding discrimination in credit applications on the basis of sex or marital status
29-10-1985 It was announced that Maj. Gen. Samuel K. Doe had won the first multiparty election in Liberia.
29-10-1989 A public mourning, involving over 20,000 East Berliners, was observed with a minute of silence for the people who had been killed while trying to flee over the Berlin Wall.
29-10-1990 The U.N. Security Council voted to hold Saddam Hussein's regime liable for human rights abuses and war damages during its occupation of Kuwait.
29-10-1991 The U.S. Galileo spacecraft became the first to visit an asteroid (Gaspra).
29-10-1991 Trade sanctions were imposed on Haiti by the U.S. to pressure the new leaders to restore the ousted President Jean-Bertrand Aristide to power.
29-10-1992 Depo Provera, a contraceptive, was approved by the Food and Drug Administration.
29-10-1993 A group of U.S. athletes were attacked by skinheads in Germany.
29-10-1994 Francisco Martin Duran fired more than two dozen shots at the White House while standing on Pennsylvania Ave. Duran was later convicted of trying to kill U.S. President Clinton.
29-10-1995 Palestinians swore revenge for the assassination of Dr. Fathi Shakaki.
29-10-1995 Jerry Rice of the San Francisco 49ers became the NFL's career leader in receiving yards with 14,040 yards.
29-10-1996 An auction was held to sell the artwork that had been stolen by the Nazis during the German occupation of Austria during World War II.
29-10-1998 South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission condemned both apartheid and violence committed by the African National Congress.
29-10-1998 The space shuttle Discovery blasted off with John Glenn on board. Glenn was 77 years old. In 1962 he became the first American to orbit the Earth.
29-10-1998 A Turkish Airlines flight was hijacked and ordered to fly to the Bulgarian capital of Sofia. The plane had 39 people on board.
29-10-1998 In Freehold, NJ, Melissa Drexler was sentenced to 15 years in prison for strangling her baby after giving birth in the bathroom at her senior prom.
29-10-1998 In London, Tom Cruise and Nicole Kidman accepted a substantial settlement from the Express Newspapers for an article that was run on October 5, 1997. The article claimed that both were homosexual and their marriage was a sham to cover the truth.
29-10-1998 James Orr was sentenced to 3 years probation and ordered to do 100 hours of community service for slamming Farrah Fawcett's head to the ground and choking her during a fight.
29-10-1998 A dance hall in Goteborg, Sweden, was gutted with fire killing 60 people. 173 were also injured in the fire.
29-10-1998 The oldest known copy of Archimedes' work sold for $2 million at a New York auction.
29-10-2001 KTLA broadcasted the first coast to coast HDTV network telecast.
30-10-1735 John Adams, the second President of the United States, was born in Braintree, MA. His son became the sixth President of the U.S.
30-10-1817 The independent government of Venezuela was established by Simon Bolivar.
30-10-1831 Escaped slave Nat Turner was apprehended in Southampton County, VA, several weeks after leading the bloodiest slave uprising in American history.
30-10-1875 The constitution of Missouri was ratified by popular vote.
30-10-1893 The U.S. Senate gave final approval to repeal the Sherman Silver Purchase Act of 1890.
30-10-1894 The time clock was patented by Daniel M. Cooper of Rochester, NY.
30-10-1938 Orson Welles' "The War of the Worlds" aired on CBS radio. The belief that the realistic radio dramatization was a live news event about a Martian invasion caused panic among listeners.
30-10-1943 In Moscow, a declaration was signed by the Governments of the Soviet Union, the United Kingdom, the United States and China called for an early establishment of an international organization to maintain peace and security. The goal was supported on December 1, 1943, at a meeting in Teheran.
30-10-1944 Martha Graham's ballet "Appalachian Spring" premiered at the Library of Congress.
30-10-1945 The U.S. government announced the end of shoe rationing.
30-10-1953 General George C. Marshall was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.
