30-10-1961 The Soviet Union tested a hydrogen bomb with a force of approximately 58 megatons.
30-10-1961 The Soviet Party Congress unanimously approved an order to remove Joseph Stalin's body from Lenin's tomb.
30-10-1972 U.S. President Richard Nixon approved legislation to increase Social Security spending by $5.3 billion.
30-10-1972 In Illinois, 45 people were killed when two trains collided on Chicago's south side.
30-10-1975 Prince Juan Carlos assumed power in Spain as dictator Francisco Franco was near death.
30-10-1975 The New York Daily News ran the headline "Ford to City: Drop Dead." The headline came a day after U.S. President Gerald R. Ford said he would veto any proposed federal bailout of New York City.
30-10-1984 In Poland, police found the body of kidnapped pro-Solidarity priest Father Jerry Popieluszko. His death was blamed on four security officers.
30-10-1989 Mitsubishi Estate Company announced it would buy 51 percent of Rockefeller Group Inc. of New York.
30-10-1993 Martin Fettman, America's first veterinarian in space, performed the world's first animal dissections in space, while aboard the space shuttle Columbia.
30-10-1993 The United Nations deadline concerning ousted Haitian President Jean-Bertrand Aristide passed with country's military still in control.
30-10-1995 Federalist prevailed over separatists in Quebec in a referendum concerning secession from the federation of Canada.
30-10-1997 The play revival "The Cherry Orchard" opened.
30-10-1998 The terrorist who hijacked a Turkish Airlines plane and the 39 people on board was killed when anti-terrorist squads raided the plane.
30-10-2001 In New York City, U.S. President George W. Bush threw out the first pitch at Game 3 of the World Series between the New York Yankees and the Arizona Diamondbacks.
30-10-2001 Michael Jordan returned to the NBA with the Washington Wizards after a 3 1/2 year retirement. The Wizards lost 93-91 to the New York Knicks.
31-10-1517 Martin Luther posted the 95 Theses on the door of the Wittenberg Palace Church. The event marked the start of the Protestant Reformation in Germany.
31-10-1860 Juliette Low, the founder off the Girl Scouts, was born.
31-10-1864 Nevada became the 36th state to join the U.S.
31-10-1868 Postmaster General Alexander Williams Randall approved a standard uniform for postal carriers.
31-10-1887 Nationalist Chinese leader Chiang Kai-shek was born. He was the first constitutional President of the Republic of China.
31-10-1914 The Ottoman Empire (Turkey) joined the Central Powers (Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Bulgaria).
31-10-1922 Benito Mussolini became prime minister of Italy.
31-10-1926 Magician Harry Houdini died of gangrene and peritonitis resulting from a ruptured appendix. His appendix had been damaged twelve days earlier when he had been punched in the stomach by a student unexpectedly. During a lecture Houdini had commented on the strength of his stomach muscles and their ability to withstand hard blows.
31-10-1940 The British air victory in the Battle of Britain prevented Germany from invading Britain.
31-10-1941 Mount Rushmore was declared complete after 14 years of work. At the time the 60-foot busts of U.S. Presidents George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt and Abraham Lincoln were finished.
31-10-1941 The U.S. Navy destroyer Reuben James was torpedoed by a German submarine near Iceland. The U.S. had not yet entered World War II. More than 100 men were killed.
31-10-1952 The U.S. detonated its first hydrogen bomb.
31-10-1954 The Algerian National Liberation Front (FLN) began a revolt against French rule.
31-10-1955 Britain's Princess Margaret announced she would not marry Royal Air Force Captain Peter Townsend.
31-10-1956 Rear Admiral G.J. Dufek become the first person to land an airplane at the South Pole. Dufek also became the first person to set foot on the South Pole.
31-10-1959 Lee Harvey Oswald, a former U.S. Marine from Fort Worth, TX, announced that he would never return to the U.S. At the time he was in Moscow, Russia.
31-10-1961 In the Soviet Union, the body of Joseph Stalin was removed from Lenin's Tomb where it was on public display.
31-10-1968 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson ordered a halt to all U.S. bombing of North Vietnam.
31-10-1983 The U.S. Defense Department acknowledged that during the U.S. led invasion of Grenada, that a U.S. Navy plane had mistakenly bombed a civilian hospital.
31-10-1984 Indian Prime Minister Indira Gandhi was assassinated near her residence by two Sikh security guards. Her son, Rajiv, was sworn in as prime minister.
31-10-1992 In Liberia, it was announced that five American nuns had been killed near Monrovia. Rebels loyal to Charles Taylor were blamed for the murders.
31-10-1993 River Phoenix died at the age of 23 after collapsing outside The Viper Room in Hollywood.
31-10-1993 The play "Wonderful Tennessee" closed after only 9 performances.
31-10-1994 68 people were killed when an American Eagle ATR-72, plunged into a northern Indiana farm.
31-10-1997 Louise Woodward, British au pair, was sentenced to life in prison after being convicted of second-degree murder in the death of 8-month-old Matthew Eappen. She was released after her sentence was reduced to manslaughter.
31-10-1998 Iraq announced that it was halting all dealings with U.N. arms inspectors. The inspectors were investigating the country's weapons of mass destruction stemming from Iraq's invasion of Kuwait in 1990.
31-10-1999 EgyptAir Flight 990 crashed off the coast of Nantucket, MA, killing all 217 people aboard.
31-10-1999 Leaders from the Roman Catholic Church and the Lutheran Church signed the Joint Declaration on the Doctrine of Justification. The event ended a centuries-old doctrinal dispute over the nature of faith and salvation.
31-10-2001 Microsoft and the U.S. Justice Department reached a tentative agreement to settle the antitrust case againt the software company.
01-09-1799 The Bank of Manhattan Company opened in New York City, NY. It was the forerunner of Chase Manhattan.
01-09-1807 Former U.S. Vice President Aaron Burr was found innocent of treason.
01-09-1810 The first plow with interchangeable parts was patented by John J. Wood.
01-09-1859 The Pullman sleeping car was placed into service.
01-09-1878 Emma M. Nutt became the first female telephone operator in the U.S. The company was the Telephone Dispatch Company of Boston.
01-09-1884 The Thomas A. Edison Construction Department and the Edison Company for Isolated Lighting merged.
01-09-1887 Emile Berliner filed for a patent for his invention of the lateral-cut, flat-disk gramophone. It is a device that is better known as a record player. Thomas Edison made the idea work.
01-09-1894 A forest fire in Hinckley, MN, killed more than 400 people.
01-09-1897 The first section of Boston's subway system was opened.
01-09-1905 Saskatchewan and Alberta became the ninth and tenth provinces of Canada.
01-09-1906 Jack Coombs of the American League’s Philadelphia Athletics pitched 24 innings against the Boston Red Sox. (MLB)
01-09-1922 The first daily news program on radio was "The Radio Digest," on WBAY radio in New York City, NY.
01-09-1923 About 100,000 people were killed when an earthquake hit Tokyo and Yokohama, Japan.
01-09-1939 World War II began when Germany invaded Poland.
01-09-1942 A federal judge in Sacramento, CA, upheld the wartime detention of Japanese-Americans as well as Japanese nationals.
01-09-1945 The U.S. received official word of Japan's formal surrender that ended World War II. In Japan, it was actually September 2nd.
01-09-1949 "Martin Kane, Private Eye" debuted on NBC-TV.
01-09-1951 The ANZUS Treaty, a mutual defense pact, was signed by the U.S., Australia and New Zealand.
01-09-1969 Col. Moammar Gadhafi came into power in Libya after the government was overthrown.
01-09-1970 The last episode of "I Dream of Jeannie" aired on NBC-TV. The show premiered was on September 18, 1965.
01-09-1971 Danny Murtaugh of the Pittsburgh Pirates gave his lineup card to the umpire with the names of nine black baseball players on it. This was a first for Major League Baseball.
01-09-1972 America’s Bobby Fischer beat Russia’s Boris Spassky to become world chess champion. The chess match took place in Reykjavik, Iceland.
01-09-1979 The U.S. Pioneer 11 became the first spacecraft to visit Saturn.
01-09-1982 J.R. Richard returned to major league baseball after a two-year absence following a near-fatal stroke.
01-09-1983 A Soviet jet fighter shot down a Korean Air Lines Boeing 747 when it entered Soviet airspace. 269 people were killed.
01-09-1985 The Titanic was found by Dr. Robert Ballard and Jean Louis Michel in a joint U.S. and French expedition. The wreck site is located 963 miles northeast of New York and 453 miles southeast of the Newfoundland coast.
01-09-1986 The Soviet Union announced the accident involving the Admiral Nakhimov the night before. 448 people died in the ship collision.
01-09-1986 Jerry Lewis raised a record $34 million for Muscular Dystrophy during his annual telethon for Jerry’s kids over the Labor Day weekend.
01-09-1993 Louis Freeh was sworn in as the director of the FBI.
01-09-1995 Illinois Congressman Mel Reynolds announced his resignation. He had been convicted of having sex with an underage campaign volunteer.
01-09-1997 In France, the prosecutor's office announced that the driver of the car, in which Britain's Princess Diana was killed, was over the legal alcohol limit.
01-09-1998 The movie "Titanic" went on sale across North America.
01-09-1998 Mark McGwire, of the St. Louis Cardinals, hit his 56th and 57th homeruns to set a new National League record. He would eventually reach a total of 70 for the season on September 27.
01-09-1998 Vietnam released 5,000 prisoners, including political dissidents, on National Day.
01-09-1999 Twenty-two of major league baseball's 68 permanent umpires were replaced. The problem arose from their union's failed attempt to force an early start to negotiations for a new labor contract.
02-09-0490 Phidippides of Athens was sent to seek help against the invading Persian Army. The runner was the inspiration for the 26-mile marathon of the Olympic Games in Athens in 1896.
02-09-1666 The Great Fire of London broke out. The fire burned for three days destroying 10,000 buildings including St. Paul's Cathedral. Only 6 people were killed.
02-09-1775 Hannah, the first American war vessel was commissioned by General George Washington.
02-09-1789 The U.S. Treasury Department was established.
02-09-1864 During the U.S. Civil War Union forces led by Gen. William T. Sherman occupied Atlanta following the retreat of the Confederates.
02-09-1897 The first issue of "McCall’s" magazine was published. The magazine had been known previously as "Queens Magazine" and "Queen of Fashion."
02-09-1901 Theodore Roosevelt, then Vice President, said "Speak softly and carry a big stick" in a speech at the Minnesota State Fair.
02-09-1930 The "Question Mark" made the first non-stop flight from Europe to the U.S. The plane was flown by Captain Dieudonne Coste and Maurice Bellonte.
02-09-1935 A hurricane hit the Florida Keys killing 423 people.
02-09-1938 The first railroad car to be equipped with fluorescent lighting was put into operation on the New York Central railroad.
02-09-1945 Japan surrendered to the U.S. aboard the USS Missouri, ending World War II. The war ended six years and one day after it began.
02-09-1945 Ho Chi Minh declared the independence the Democratic Republic of Vietnam.
02-09-1961 The U.S.S.R. resumed nuclear weapons testing. Test ban treaty negotiations had failed with the U.S. and Britain when the three nations could not agree upon the nature and frequency of on-site inspections.
02-09-1962 Ken Hubbs, of the Chicago Cubs, set a major-league baseball fielding record when he played errorless for his 74th consecutive game.
02-09-1963 The integration of Tuskegee High School was prevented by state troopers assigned by Alabama Gov. George Wallace. Wallace had the building surrounded by state troopers.
02-09-1963 "The CBS Evening News" was lengthened from 15 to 30 minutes.
02-09-1969 Ho Chi Minh died. He was the president of North Vietnam.
02-09-1969 NBC-TV canceled "Star Trek." The show had debuted on September 8, 1966.
02-09-1973 Billy Martin was fired as manager of the Detroit Tigers. Martin was relieved of his duties three days after ordering his pitchers to throw spitballs against Cleveland Indians batters.
02-09-1985 It was announced that the Titanic had been found on September 1 by a U.S. and French expedition 560 miles off Newfoundland. The luxury liner had been missing for 73 years.
02-09-1986 Cathy Evelyn Smith was sentenced to three years in prison for involuntary manslaughter in connection with the overdose death of John Belushi.
02-09-1991 The U.S. formally recognized the independence of Lithuania, Lativa and Estonia.
02-09-1992 The U.S. and Russia agreed to a joint venture to build a space station.
02-09-1996 Muslim rebels and the Philippine government signed a pact formally ending 26-years of insurgency that had killed more than 120,000 people.
02-09-1998 In Canada, pilots for Canada's largest airline launch their first strike in Air Canada's history.
02-09-1998 229 people were killed when a Swissair jetliner crashed into the Atlantic near Peggy's Cove, Nova Scotia. The pilot had reported smoke in the cockpit a few minutes before the crash.
03-09-1189 England's King Richard I was crowned in Westminster.
03-09-1658 Oliver Cromwell died.
03-09-1783 The Revolutionary War between the U.S. and Great Britain ended with the Treaty of Paris.
03-09-1833 The first successful penny newspaper in the U.S., "The New York Sun," was launched by Benjamin H. Day.
03-09-1838 Frederick Douglass boarded a train in Maryland on his way to freedom from being a slave.
03-09-1895 The first professional football game was played in Latrobe, PA. The Latrobe YMCA defeated the Jeannette Athletic Club 12-0.
03-09-1935 Sir Malcolm Campbell became the first person to drive an automobile over 300 miles an hour. He reached 304.331 MPH on the Bonneville Salt Flats in Utah.
03-09-1939 British Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain, in a radio broadcast, announced that Britain and France had declared war on Germany. Germany had invaded Poland on September 1.
03-09-1943 Italy was invaded by the Allied forces during World War II.
03-09-1945 Betty Hutton and Ted Briskin were married in Chicago's Drake Hotel.
03-09-1951 "Search for Tomorrow" debuted on CBS-TV.
03-09-1954 "The Lone Ranger" was heard on radio for the final time after 2,956 episodes over a period of 21 years.
03-09-1966 The television series "The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet" ended after 14 years.
03-09-1967 The TV game show "What's My Line?" broadcast its final episode. The show aired over 17 years on CBS.
03-09-1967 Nguyen Van Thieu was elected president of South Vietnam under a new constitution.
03-09-1967 In Sweden motorist stopped driving on the left side of the road and began driving on the right side.
03-09-1970 Vince Lombardi died of cancer at the age of 57.
03-09-1976 The U.S. spacecraft Viking 2 landed on Mars. The unmanned spacecraft took the first close-up, color photos of the planet's surface.
03-09-1981 David Brinkley left NBC News after 38 years to join with ABC.
03-09-1984 Bruce Sutter of the St. Louis Cardinals set a National League record by earning his 38th save of the season.
03-09-1986 Peat Marwick International and Klynveld Main Goerdeler of the Netherlands agreed to merge and form the world’s largest accounting firm.
03-09-1989 The U.S. began shipping military aircraft and weapons, worth $65 million, to Columbia in its fight against drug lords.
03-09-1989 A Cubana de Aviacion jetliner crashed in Havana killing 126 people on the plane and 26 people on the ground.
03-09-1991 A fire broke out in the Imperial Food Products Inc. chicken processing plant in Hamlet, NC. The fire killed 25 people.
03-09-1994 Russia and China announced that they would no longer be targeting nuclear missiles or using force against each other.
03-09-1994 In Alaska, two teenagers were exiled by an American Indian Tribal panel. The teenagers were sent to an uninhabited island for one year for beating and robbing a pizza deliveryman.
03-09-1999 Mario Lemieux's ownership group officially took over the National Hockey League's Pittsburgh Penguins. Lemieux became the first player in the modern era of sports to buy the team he had once played for.
04-09-0476 Romulus Augustulus, the last emperor of the western Roman Empire, was deposed when Odoacer proclaimed himself King of Italy.
04-09-1530 Russian Czar Ivan "The Terrible" was born.
04-09-1609 English navigator Henry Hudson began exploring the island of Manhattan.
04-09-1776 Francois Rene Chateaubriand was born. He was a French poet, novelist, statesman, historian and explorer.
04-09-1781 Los Angeles, CA, was founded by Spanish settlers. The original name was "El Pueblo de Nuestra Senora La Reina de Los Angeles de Porciuncula," which translates as "The Town of the Queen of Angels."
04-09-1825 New York Governor Clinton ceremoniously emptied a barrel of Lake Erie water in the Atlantic Ocean to consumate the "Marriage of the Waters" of the Great Lakes and the Atlantic.
04-09-1833 Barney Flaherty answered an ad in "The New York Sun" and became the first newsboy/paperboy at the age of 10.
04-09-1882 Thomas Edison's Pearl Street electric power station began operations in New York City. It was the first display of a practical electrical lighting system.
04-09-1885 The Exchange Buffet opened in New York City. It was the first self-service cafeteria in the U.S.
04-09-1886 Geronimo, and the Apache Indians he led, surrendered in Skeleton Canyon in Arizona to Gen. Nelson Miles.
04-09-1888 George Eastman registered the name "Kodak" and patented his roll-film camera. The camera took 100 exposures per roll.
04-09-1894 A strike in New York City by 12,000 tailors took place to protest sweatshops.
04-09-1899 An 8.3 earthquake hit Yakutat Bar, AK.
04-09-1917 Henry Ford II was born. He was the head of the Ford Motor Company for 40 years.
04-09-1917 The American expeditionary force in France suffered its first fatalities in World War I.
04-09-1921 The first police broadcast was made by radio station WIL in St. Louis, MO.
04-09-1944 During World War II, British troops entered the city of Antwerp, Belgium.
04-09-1948 The Dutch Queen Wilhelmina left her throne for health reasons.
04-09-1949 The longest pro tennis match in history was played when Pancho Gonzales and Ted Schroeder played 67 games in five sets.
04-09-1951 The first live, coast-to-coast TV broadcast took place in the U.S. The event took place in San Francisco, CA from the Japanese Peace Treaty Conference. It was seen all the way to New York City, NY.
04-09-1953 The New York Yankees became the first baseball team to win five consecutive American League championships.
04-09-1957 The Arkansas National Guard was ordered by Governor Orval Faubus to keep nine black students from going into Little Rock's Central High School.
04-09-1957 The Ford Motor Company began selling the Edsel. The car was so unpopular that it was taken off the market only two years.
04-09-1967 "Gilligan's Island" aired for the last time on CBS-TV. It ran for 98 shows.
04-09-1967 Michigan Gov. George Romney said during a TV interview that he had undergone "brainwashing" by U.S. officials while visiting Vietnam in 1965.
04-09-1971 An Alaska Airlines jet crashed killing 111 people near Juneau.
04-09-1971 "The Lawrence Welk Show" was seen for the last time on ABC-TV.
04-09-1972 Swimmer Mark Spitz captured his seventh Olympic gold medal in the 400-meter medley relay event at Munich, Germany. Spitz was the first Olympian to win seven gold medals.
04-09-1973 John Ehrlichman and G. Gordon Liddy were indicted with two others in connection with the burglary of a psychiatrist's office two years earlier.
04-09-1982 The Dorothy May Apartment-Hotel building in Los Angeles, CA was set on fire by an arsonist killing 25 people.
04-09-1983 U.S. officials announced that there had been an American plane, used for reconnaissance, in the vicinity of the Korean Air Lines flight that was shot down.
04-09-1986 South African security forces halted a mass funeral for the victims of the riot in Soweto.
04-09-1987 West German pilot Mathias Rust was convicted by a Soviet court and sentenced to four years in a labor camp. The charges were concerning his daring flight into Moscow's Red Square. He was released after one year.
04-09-1988 Bangladesh officials reported that at least 882 people had been killed by floods that had inundated their nation.
04-09-1989 A reconnaissance satellite was released by the Air Force's Titan Three rocket. The Titan Three set over 200 satellites into space between 1964 and 1989.
04-09-1993 Pope John Paul II started his first visit to the former Soviet Union.
04-09-1993 Jim Abbott, pitcher for the New York Yankees, pitched a no-hitter. Abbott had been born without a right hand.
04-09-1995 The Fourth World Conference on Women was opened in Beijing. There were over 4,750 delegates from 181 countries in attendance.
04-09-1997 A triple suicide bombing in the heart of Jerusalem killed seven people, including the three assailants.
04-09-1997 Three Buddhist nuns acknowledged in testimony to the U.S. Senate that their temple outside Los Angeles illegally reimbursed donors after a fund-raiser attended by Vice President Al Gore, and later destroyed or altered records.
04-09-1998 In Mexico, bankers stopped approving personal loans and mortgages.
04-09-1998 The International Monetary Fund approved a $257 million loan for the Ukraine.
04-09-1998 While in Ireland, U.S. President Clinton said the words "I'm sorry" for the first time about his affair with Monica Lewinsky and described his behavior as indefensible.
04-09-1999 The United Nations announced that the residents of East Timor had overwhelmingly voted for independence from Indonesia in a referendum held on August 30. In Dili, pro-Indonesian militias attacked independence supporters, burned buildings, blew up bridges and destroyed telecommunication facilities.
04-09-2002 The Oakland Athletics won their AL-record 20th straight game. The A's gave up an 11-run lead during the game and then won the game a Scott Hatteberg home run in the bottom of the ninth inning.
04-09-2003 Keegan Reilly, 22, became the first parapalegic climber to reach the peak of Japan's Mount Fuji.
05-09-1698 Russia's Peter the Great imposed a tax on beards.
05-09-1774 The first session of the U.S. Continental Congress convenes in Philadelphia. The delegates drafted a declaration of rights and grievances, organized the Continental Association, and elected Peyton Randolph as the first president of the Continental Congress.
05-09-1793 In France, the "Reign of Terror" began. The National Convention enacted measures to repress the French Revolutionary activities.
05-09-1836 Sam Houston was elected as the first president of the Republic of Texas.
05-09-1877 Sioux chief Crazy Horse was killed by the bayonet of a U.S. soldier. The chief allegedly resisted confinement to a jail cell.
05-09-1881 The American Red Cross provided relief for disaster for the first time. The disaster was the Great Fire of 1881 in Michigan.
05-09-1882 The first U.S. Labor Day parade was held in New York City.
05-09-1885 Jake Gumper bought the first gasoline pump to be manufactured in the U.S.
05-09-1900 France proclaimed a protectorate over Chad.
05-09-1901 The National Association of Professional Baseball Leagues was formed in Chicago, IL. It was the first organized baseball league.
05-09-1905 The Treaty of Portsmouth was signed by Russia and Japan to end the Russo-Japanese War. The settlement was mediated by U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt in New Hampshire.
05-09-1906 Bradbury Robinson executed the first legal forward pass in football. Robinson threw the ball to Jack Schneider of St. Louis University in a game against Carroll College.
05-09-1914 Babe Ruth hit his first home run as a professional player in the International League.
05-09-1914 The Battle of the Marne began. The Germans, British and French fought for six days killing half a million people.
05-09-1917 Federal raids were carried out in 24 cities on International Workers of the World (IWW) headquarters. The raids were prompted by suspected anti-war activities within the labor organization.
05-09-1930 Charles Creighton and James Hagris completed the drive from New York City to Los Angeles and back to New York City all in reverse gear. The trip took 42 days in their 1929 Ford Model A.
05-09-1938 The NBC Red network broadcast "Life Can Be Beautiful" for the first time.
05-09-1939 The U.S. proclaimed its neutrality in World War II.
05-09-1945 Iva Toguri D'Aquino was arrested. D'Aquino was suspected of being the wartime radio propagandist "Tokyo Rose". She served six years before being pardoned by U.S. President Ford.
05-09-1953 The first privately operated atomic reactor opened in Raleigh, NC.
05-09-1956 20 people were killed in a train crash in Springer, NM.
05-09-1957 Jack Kerouac's "On the Road" was first published.
05-09-1958 The first color videotaped program was aired. It was "The Betty Freezor Show" on WBTV-TV in Charlotte, NC.
05-09-1958 Boris Pasternak's "Doctor Zhivago" was published for the first time in the U.S.
05-09-1960 Cassius Clay of Louisville, KY won the gold medal in light heavyweight boxing at the Olympic Games in Rome, Italy. Clay later changed his name to Muhammad Ali.
05-09-1961 The U.S. government made airline hijacking a federal offense.
05-09-1971 J.R. Richard, of the Houston Astros, tied Karl Spooner’s record when he struck out 15 batters in his major-league baseball debut.
05-09-1972 Arab guerrillas attacked the Israeli delegation at the Munich Olympic games. 11 members of the Israeli Olympic team, five guerrillas and a police officer were killed in the siege.
05-09-1975 A Secret Service agent foiled an assassination attempt against U.S. U.S. President Gerald R. Ford. Lynette A. "Squeaky" Fromme was a follower of Charles Manson, who was incarcerated at the time. 17 days later, Sara Jane Moore attempted to assassinate Ford.
05-09-1977 The U.S. launched Voyager .
05-09-1980 The St. Gothard Tunnel opened in Switzerland. It is the world's longest highway tunnel at 10.14 miles long.
05-09-1982 Eddie Hill set a propeller-driven boat water speed record when he reached 229 mph.
05-09-1983 U.S. President Reagan denounced the Soviet Union for shooting down a Korean Air Lines. Reagan demanded that the Soviet Union pay reparations for the act that killed 269 people.
05-09-1983 "Sports Illustrated" became the first national weekly magazine to use four-color process illustrations on every page.
05-09-1983 The "MacNeil/Lehrer NewsHour" on PBS (Public Broadcasting System) became the first hour-long network news show.
05-09-1984 The space shuttle Discovery landed after its maiden voyage.
05-09-1984 Mortimer Zuckerman purchased the newsmagazine, "U.S. News & World Report" for $163 million.
05-09-1985 Rioting in South Africa spilled into white neighborhoods for the first time.
05-09-1986 A Pan Am jumbo jet carrying 358 people was hijacked at Karachi Airport. When security forces stormed the plane 21 people were killed and dozens were wounded.
05-09-1986 Merv Griffin aired his final program for Metromedia Television after 23 years on various talk shows.
05-09-1986 NASA launched DOD-1.
05-09-1989 Chris Evert retired from professional tennis after a 19 year career.
05-09-1989 Deborah Norville became the news anchor of the "Today" show.
05-09-1990 Iraqi President Saddam Hussein urged for a Holy War against the West and former allies.
05-09-1991 Soviet lawmakers created an interim government to usher in the confederation after dissolving the U.S.S.R. The new name the Union of Sovereign States was taken.
05-09-1991 In the trial of former Panamanian ruler Manuel Noriega jury selection began.
05-09-1992 A General Motors Corporation strike ended with a new agreement being approved. Nearly 43,000 workers were on strike.
05-09-1995 France set off an underground nuclear blast in the South Pacific.
05-09-1996 The play "Summer and Smoke" opened at the Criterion Theatre.
05-09-1997 Mother Teresa died in Calcutta, India, at the age of 87.
05-09-2000 Mark Bailey, 42, pled no contest to stalking and terrorizing Brooke Shields for the last 15 years. Baily was sentenced to probation and counseling after he agreed to stay away from Shields for the next 10 years.
05-09-2001 Peru's attorney general filed homicide charges against ex-President Alberto Fujimori. Fujimori was linked to two massacres by paramilitary death squads. At the time of the charges Fujimori was in exile in Japan.
05-09-2001 Fox News Channel terminated Paula Zahn for breach of contract.
05-09-2002 In Kabul, Afghanistan, a car bomb killed at least 15 people.
05-09-2002 In Kandahar, Afghanistan, President Hamid Karzai was unhurt in an assassination attempt. Kandahar governor Gul Agha Sherzai was wounded in the attack. Karzai's American body guards returned fire and killed three people.
05-09-2003 In London, magician David Blaine entered a clear plastic box and then suspended by a crane over the banks of the Thames River. He remained there until October 19 surviving only on water.
06-09-1620 The Pilgrims left on the Mayflower from Plymouth, England to settle in the New World.
06-09-1766 John Dalton was born. The teacher/physicist formulated the atomic theory.
06-09-1819 Thomas Blanchard patented a machine called the lathe.
06-09-1837 The Oberlin Collegiate Institute of Ohio went co-educational.
06-09-1860 Jane Addams was born. She was the founder of Hull House in Chicago and was the first woman to win the Nobel Peace Prize.
06-09-1876 The Southern Pacific rail line from Los Angeles to San Francisco was completed.
06-09-1888 Joseph P. Kennedy Sr. was born. He owned the Chicago Merchandise Mart and made his fortune in real estate, liquor and movies.
06-09-1899 Carnation processed its first can of evaporated milk.
06-09-1901 U.S. President William McKinley was shot and mortally wounded (he died eight days later) by Leon Czolgosz. Czolgosz, an American anarchist, was executed the following October.
06-09-1909 Robert Peary, American explorer, sent word that he had reached the North Pole. He had reached his goal five months earlier.
06-09-1939 South Africa declared war on Germany.
06-09-1941 Jews in German-occupied areas were ordered to wear the Star of David with the word "Jew" inscribed. The order only applied to Jews over the age of 6.
06-09-1943 The youngest player to appear in an American League baseball game was pitcher Carl Scheib of the Philadelphia Athletics. Scheib was 16 years, eight months and five days old.
06-09-1944 During World War II, the British government relaxed blackout restrictions and suspended compulsory training for the Home Guard.
06-09-1948 Queen Juliana of the Netherlands was crowned.
06-09-1952 In Montreal, Canadian television began broadcasting.
06-09-1959 The first Barbie Doll was sold by Mattel Toy Corporation.
06-09-1966 In Cape Town, South African Prime Minister Hendrik Verwoerd was stabbed to death by a deranged page during a parliamentary session.
06-09-1970 Palestinian guerrillas seized control of three jetliners. After the crews and passengers were evacuated the jets were blown up while on the ground in Jordan.
06-09-1972 Rick DeMont lost the gold medal he received in a 400-meter swimming event because a banned drug was found in his system during routine drug testing.
06-09-1972 The Summer Olympics resumed in Munich, West Germany, a day after the deadly hostage crisis that took the lives of 11 Israelis and five Arab abductors.
06-09-1975 Martina Navratilova requested political asylum while in New York for the U.S. Open Tennis Tournament.
06-09-1978 James Wickwire and Louis Reichardt reached the top of the world's second largest mountain, Pakistan's K-2. They were the first Americans to reach the summit.
06-09-1983 The Soviet Union admitted to the shooting down of Korean Air Lines flight 007. They said the pilots were not aware that their target was a civilian aircraft.
06-09-1985 31 people were killed when a Midwest Express Airlines DC-9 crashed just after takeoff in Milwaukee.
06-09-1986 22 worshipers were killed in a synagogue in Istanbul, Turkey. The people were attacked with machine guns and grenades. The gunmen eventually took their own lives.
06-09-1990 Iraq warned that anyone trying to flee the country without permission would be put in prison for life.
06-09-1991 The State Council of the Soviet Union recognized the independence of the Baltic states.
06-09-1991 The name St. Petersburg was restored to Russia's second largest city. The city was founded in 1703 by Peter the Great. The name has been changed to Petrograd (1914) and to Leningrad (1924).
06-09-1992 A 35-year old man died ten weeks after receiving a transplanted baboon liver.
06-09-1993 Renault of France and Volvo of Sweden announced they were merging. Volvo eventually canceled the deal the following December.
06-09-1995 Los Angeles police detective Mark Fuhrman invoked his Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination at the O.J. Simpson trial.
06-09-1995 U.S. Senator Bob Packwood was expelled by the Senate Ethics Committee.
06-09-1995 Cal Ripken played his 2,131st consecutive game setting a new record. Lou Gehrig previously held the record.
06-09-1996 Eddie Murray of the Baltimore Orioles hit his 500th career home run during a game against the Detroit Tigers. He was only the third person to have at least 3,000 hits and 500 home runs.
06-09-1997 More than 2 million people watched the funeral service of Princess Diana that was held at Westminster Abbey.
06-09-2000 The U.N. Millennium Summit began in New York. It was the largest gathering of world leaders in history with more than 150 present.
06-09-2001 The U.S. Justice Department announced that it was seeking a lesser antitrust penalty and would not attempt to break up Microsoft.
06-09-2001 Ebay Inc. was found not liable for copyright infringement because bootleg copies of a Charles Manson documentary had been sold on the site.
06-09-2002 In New York, the U.S. Congress convened at Federal Hall for a rare special session. The session was held in New York to express the nation's mourning for the loss on September 11, 2001 and unity in the war against terrorism.
06-09-2002 At the Smithsonian American Art Museum, the exhibition "George Catlin and His Indian Gallery" went on view. The exhibit contained over 400 objects.
07-09-1533 Queen Elizabeth I, of England, was born in Greenwich.
07-09-1812 Napoleon defeated the Russian army of Alexander I at the battle of Borodino.
07-09-1813 The nickname "Uncle Sam" was first used as a symbolic reference to the United States. The reference appeared in an editorial in the New York's Troy Post.
07-09-1822 Brazil declared its independence from Portugal.
07-09-1860 American painter Anna Mary (Robertson Moses) was born in New York. Today it is known as "Grandma Moses Day."
07-09-1880 George Ligowsky was granted a patent for his device that threw clay pigeons for trapshooters.
07-09-1888 Edith Eleanor McLean became the first baby to be placed in an incubator.
07-09-1896 A.H. Whiting won the first automobile race held on a racetrack. The race was held in Cranston, RI.
07-09-1901 The Boxer Rebellion began in China ending the Peace of Beijing.
07-09-1921 Margaret Gorman of Washington, DC, was crowned the first Miss America in Atlantic City, NJ.
07-09-1927 Philo T. Farnsworth succeeded in transmitting an image through purely electronic means by using an image dissector.
07-09-1930 The cartoon "Blondie" made its first appearance in the comic strips.
07-09-1940 London received its initial rain of bombs from Nazi Germany during World War II.
07-09-1942 During World War II, the Russian army counter attacked the German troops outside the city of Stalingrad.
07-09-1963 The National Professional Football Hall of Fame was dedicated in Canton, OH.
07-09-1966 The final episode of the original "The Dick Van Dyke Show" was aired on CBS-TV.
07-09-1971 "The Beverly Hillbillies" was seen for the final time on CBS-TV.
07-09-1977 The Panama Canal treaties were signed by U.S. President Carter and General Omar Torrijos Herrera. The treaties called for the U.S. to turn over control of the canal's waterway to Panama in the year 2000.
07-09-1977 G. Gordon Liddy was released from prison. He had been incarcerated for more than four years for his involvement in the Watergate conspiracy.
07-09-1979 ESPN, the Entertainment and Sports Programming Network, made its debut on cable TV.
07-09-1984 American Express Co. issued the first of its Platinum charge cards.
07-09-1986 Dan Marino of the Miami Dolphins threw his 100th career touchdown pass, in only his 44th NFL game, which set a NFL record.
07-09-1986 President Augusto Pinochet survived an assassination attempt made by guerrillas.
07-09-1986 Desmond Tutu was the first black to be installed to lead the Anglican Church in southern Africa.
07-09-1989 Legislation was approved by the U.S. Senate that prohibited discrimination against the handicapped in employment, public accommodations, transportation and communications.
07-09-1992 28 people African National Congress supporters were killed and 200 were wounded when fired upon by troops in South Africa.
07-09-1995 U.S. Senator Bob Packwood announced that he would resign after 27 years in the Senate.
07-09-1998 Mark McGwire set a new major league baseball record for most homeruns hit in a single season. The previous record was 61 set in 1961.
07-09-1999 The White House announced that 12 jailed members of the Puerto Rican independence group Armed Forces of National Liberation had accepted a clemency offer proposed by U.S. President Clinton.
07-09-1999 Viacom Inc. announced that it had plans to buy CBS Corp.
07-09-2001 Barry Bonds (San Francisco Giants) became the only the fifth player in major league baseball history to hit 60 home runs in a season.
08-09-1565 A Spanish expedition established the first permanent European settlement in North America at present-day St. Augustine, FL.
08-09-1664 The Dutch surrendered New Amsterdam to the British, who then renamed it New York.
08-09-1866 The first recorded birth of sextuplets took place in Chicago, IL. The parents were James and Jennie Bushnell.
08-09-1892 An early version of "The Pledge of Allegiance" appeared in "The Youth's Companion."
08-09-1893 In New Zealand, the Electoral Act 1893 was passed by the Legislative Council. It was consented by the governor on September 19 giving all women in New Zealand the right to vote.
08-09-1888 In London, The body of Annie Champman was found. She was the second victim of "Jack The Ripper."
08-09-1900 Galveston, TX, was hit by a hurricane that killed about 6,000 people.
08-09-1934 A fire aboard the liner Morro Castle off the New Jersey coast killed 134 people.
08-09-1935 U.S. Senator Huey P. Long, "The Kingfish" of Louisiana politics, was shot and mortally wounded. He died two days later.
08-09-1945 In Washington, DC, a bus equipped with a two-way radio was put into service for the first time.
08-09-1945 Bess Myerson of New York was crowned Miss America. She was the first Jewish contestant to win the title.
08-09-1951 A peace treaty with Japan was signed by 48 other nations in San Francisco, CA.
08-09-1952 The Ernest Hemingway novel "The Old Man and the Sea" was published.
08-09-1960 NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, AL, was dedicated by U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower. The facility had been activated in July earlier that year.
08-09-1966 NBC-TV aired the first episode of "Star Trek" entitled "The Man Trap". The show was canceled on September 2, 1969.
08-09-1971 In Washington, DC, the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts was inaugurated. The opening featured the premiere of Leonard Bernstein's "Mass."
08-09-1973 Hank Aaron hit his 709th home run.
08-09-1974 U.S. President Ford granted an unconditional pardon to former U.S. President Nixon.
08-09-1975 In Boston, MA, public schools began their court-ordered citywide busing program amid scattered incidents of violence.
08-09-1986 Herschel Walker made his start in the National Football League (NFL) after leaving the New Jersey Generals of the USFL.
08-09-1994 132 people were killed when A USAir Boeing 737 crashed as it was approaching Pittsburgh International Airport.
08-09-1997 America Online acquired CompuServe.
08-09-1997 The 10th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals affirmed the conviction of Timothy McVeigh for his role in the bombing of a federal building in Oklahoma City, OK.
08-09-1998 Mark McGwire, of the St. Louis Cardinals, hit his 62nd home run of the season. He had beaten a record that had stood for 37 years by Roger Maris. McGwire would eventually reach 70 home runs on September 27.
08-09-1999 Russia's Mission Control switched off the Mir space station's central computer and other systems to save energy during a planned six months of unmanned flights.
08-09-1999 U.S. Attorney General Janet Reno named former U.S. Senator John Danforth to head an independent investigation into the 1993 fire at the Branch Davidian compound in Waco, TX.
09-09-1776 The second Continental Congress officially made the term "United States", replacing the previous term "United Colonies."
09-09-1836 Abraham Lincoln received his license to practice law.
09-09-1850 California became the 31st state to join the union.
09-09-1898 In Omaha, NE, Tommy Fleming of Eau Claire, WI won the first logrolling championship.
09-09-1890 Harland Sanders was born. He was the founder of Kentucky Fried Chicken.
09-09-1893 U.S. President Grover Cleveland's wife, Frances Cleveland, gave birth to a daughter, Esther. It was the first time a president's child was born in the White House.
09-09-1904 Mounted police were used for the first time in the City of New York.
09-09-1911 Italy declared war on the Ottoman Turks and annexed Libya, Tripolitania, and Cyrenaica in North Africa.
09-09-1919 The majority of Boston's police force went on strike. The force was made up of 1,500 men.
09-09-1919 Alexander Graham Bell and Casey Baldwin's HD-4, a hydrofoil craft, set a world marine speed record.
09-09-1926 The National Broadcasting Company (NBC) was created by the Radio Corporation of America (RCA).
09-09-1942 Japan dropped incendiaries over Oregon in an attempt to set fire to the forests in Oregon and Washington. The forest did not ignite.
09-09-1943 During World War II Allied forces landed at Taranto and Salerno.
09-09-1946 Ben Alexander hosted "Heart’s Desire" for the first time on the Mutual Broadcasting System.
09-09-1948 North Korea became the People's Democratic Republic of Korea.
09-09-1950 Sal Maglie of the New York Giants pitched a fourth consecutive shutout. Only four other pitchers in the National League had ever accomplished this feat.
09-09-1957 The first civil rights bill to pass Congress since Reconstruction was signed into law by U.S. President Eisenhower.
09-09-1965 French President Charles de Gaulle announced that France was withdrawing from NATO to protest the domination of the U.S. in the organization.
09-09-1965 Sandy Koufax of the Los Angeles Dodgers pitched the eighth perfect game in major league baseball history.
09-09-1971 Inmates seized control of the Attica Correctional Facility near Buffalo, NY. Nine prisoners were held hostage and died along with their 32 captors when the prison was stormed four days later.
09-09-1971 Gordie Howe of the Detroit Red Wings retired from the National Hockey League (NHL).
09-09-1976 Communist Chinese leader Mao Tse-tung died at the age of 82.
09-09-1979 Tracy Austin, at 16, became the youngest player to win the U.S. Open women’s tennis title.
09-09-1983 The Soviet Union announced that the Korean jetliner the was shot down on September 1, 1983 was not an accident or an error.
09-09-1984 Walter Payton of the Chicago Bears broke Jim Brown’s combined yardage record when he reached 15,517 yards.
09-09-1986 Frank Reed was taken hostage in Lebanon by pro-Iranian kidnappers. The director of a private school in Lebanon was released 44 months later.
09-09-1986 Ted Turner presented the first of his colorized films on WTBS in Atlanta, GA.
09-09-1986 Gennadiy Zakharov was indicted by a New York jury on espionage charges. Zakharov was a Soviet United Nations employee.
09-09-1987 Mickey Spillane's Mike Hammer aired for the last time on CBS.
09-09-1990 Liberian President Samuel K. Doe was captured and killed by rebels.
09-09-1993 Israeli and PLO leaders agreed to recognize each other.
09-09-1993 Former Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos was buried in his homeland. The event occurred about four years after his death in exile.
09-09-1993 U.S. and Pakistani peacekeepers opened fire on Somalis that were attacking other peacekeepers. About a hundred Somali gunmen and civilians were killed.
09-09-1994 The U.S. agreed to accept about 20,000 Cuban immigrants a year. This was in return for Cuba's promise to halt the flight of refugees.
09-09-1994 Los Angeles prosecutors announced that they would not seek the death penalty against O.J. Simpson.
09-09-1994 The space shuttle Discovery blasted off on an 11-day mission.
09-09-1995 Amtrak's Broadway Limited service made its final run between New York City, NY and Chicago, IL.
09-09-1997 Sinn Fein, the IRA's political ally, formally renounced violence as it took its place in talks on Northern Ireland's future.
09-09-1998 Independent Counsel Kenneth Starr delivered to the U.S. Congress 36 boxes of material concerning his investigation of U.S. President Clinton.
09-09-1998 Four tourists who had paid $32,500 each were taken in submarine to view the wreckage of the Titanic. The ship is 2 miles below the Atlantic off Newfoundland.
09-09-1999 At least 93 people were killed when a bomb exploded in an apartment building in Moscow, Russia.
09-09-1999 The Sega Dreamcast game system went on sale. By 1:00pm all Toys R Us locations in the U.S. had sold out.
10-09-1608 John Smith was elected president of the Jamestown, VA colony council.
10-09-1794 America's first non-denominational college was charted. Blount College later became the University of Tennessee.
10-09-1813 The first defeat of British naval squadron occurred in the Battle of Lake Erie during the War of 1812. The leader of the U.S. fleet sent the famous message "We have met the enemy, and they are ours" to U.S. General William Henry Harrison.
10-09-1845 King Willem II opened Amsterdam Stock exchange.
10-09-1846 Elias Howe received a patent for his sewing machine.
10-09-1847 The first theater opened in Hawaii.
10-09-1862 Rabbi Jacob Frankel became the first Jewish Army chaplain.
10-09-1897 British police arrest George Smith for drunken driving. It was the first DWI.
10-09-1899 A second quake in seven days hit Yakutat Bay, AK. It measured 8.6.
10-09-1913 The Lincoln Highway opened. It was the first paved coast-to-coast highway in the U.S.
10-09-1919 New York City welcomed home 25,000 soldiers and General John J. Pershing who had served in the First Division during World War I.
10-09-1919 Austria and the Allies signed the Treaty of St.-Germain-en-Laye. Austria recognized the independence of Poland, Hungary, Czechoslovakia and Yugoslavia.
10-09-1921 The Ayus Autobahn in Germany opened near Berlin. The road is known for its nonexistent speed limit.
10-09-1923 The Irish Free state joined the League of Nations.
10-09-1924 Leopold and Loeb were found guilty of murdering a small boy. The case is known as the first "thrill kill."
10-09-1926 Germany joined the League of Nations.
10-09-1935 "Popeye" was heard on NBC radio for the first time.
10-09-1939 Canada declared war on Germany.
10-09-1940 In Britain, Buckingham Palace was hit by German bomb.
10-09-1942 U.S. President Franklin Roosevelt mandated gasoline rationing as part of the U.S. wartime effort.
10-09-1943 German forces began their occupation of Rome during World War II.
10-09-1945 Vidkun Quisling was sentenced to death in Norway for his collaboration with Nazi Germany after the 1940 invasion. He was the founder of Norway's National Party in 1934, which was an imitation of Hitler's National Socialist Party.
10-09-1948 Mildred "Axis Sally" Gillars was indicted for treason in Washington, DC. Gillars was a Nazi radio propagandist during World War II. She was convicted and spent 12 years in prison.
10-09-1950 Eddie Cantor began working on TV on the "Colgate Comedy Hour" on NBC.
10-09-1951 Britain began an economic boycott of Iran.
10-09-1953 Swanson began selling its first "TV dinner."
10-09-1955 "Gunsmoke" premiered on CBS.
10-09-1955 Bert Parks began a 25-year career as host of the "Miss America Pageant" on NBC.
10-09-1956 Great Britain performed a nuclear test at Maralinga, Australia.
10-09-1961 Mickey Mantle tied a major league baseball record for home runs when he hit the 400th of his career.
10-09-1963 Twenty black students entered public schools in Alabama at the end of a standoff between federal authorities and Alabama governor George C. Wallace.
10-09-1972 Gayle Sayers of the Chicago Bears retired from the National Football League (NFL).
10-09-1974 Lou Brock of the St. Louis Cardinals set a new major league baseball record when he stole his 105th base of the season.
10-09-1977 Hamida Djandoubi, convicted of murder, became the last person to be executed by guillotine in France.
10-09-1977 "Mickey Finn" appeared in the comic pages for the last time.
10-09-1979 U.S. President Carter granted clemency to four Puerto Rican nationalists who had been imprisoned for an attack on the U.S. House of Representatives in 1954 and an attempted assassination of U.S. President Truman in 1950.
10-09-1981 Pablo Picasso's mural Guernica was received in the town of Guernica.
10-09-1984 The Federal Communications Commission changed a rule to allow broadcasters to own 12 AM and 12 FM radio stations. The previous limit was 7 of each.
10-09-1985 In El Salvador President Jose Napoleon Duarte's oldest daughter was kidnapped by leftist rebels. Ines Guadelupe Duarte Duran was freed the next month in a prisoner exchange.
10-09-1989 Hungary gave permission to thousands of East German refugees and visitors to immigrate to West Germany.
10-09-1990 Iran agreed to resume full diplomatic ties with past enemy Iraq.
10-09-1990 Iraq's Saddam Hussein offered free oil to developing nations in an attempt to win their support during the Gulf War Crisis.
10-09-1992 In Minneapolis, MN, a federal jury struck down professional football's limited free agency system.
10-09-1995 A plane with a skydivers club aboard crashed in Shacklesford, VA, killing 10 parachutists, the pilot, and a man on the ground.
10-09-1998 Mac Davis received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
10-09-1998 U.S. President Clinton met with members of his Cabinet to apologize, ask forgiveness and promise to improve as a person in the wake of the scandal involving Monica Lewinsky.
10-09-1998 Northwest Airlines announced an agreement with pilots, ending a nearly two-week walkout.
10-09-1999 A bronze sculpture of a war horse just over 24 feet high was dedicated in Milan, Italy.
10-09-2002 Florida tested its new elections system. The test resulted in polling stations opening late and problems occurred with the touch screen voting machines.
10-09-2002 The "September 11: Bearing Witness to History" exhibit opened at the Smithsonian's National Museum of American History.
10-09-2002 Switzerland became the 190th member of the United Nations.
10-09-2003 Sweden's Foreign Minister Anna Lindh was stabbed while shopping in a department store. She died the next day from her wounds.
11-09-1297 Scotsman William Wallace defeated the English forces of Sir Hugh de Cressingham at the Battle of Stirling Bridge.
11-09-1499 French forces took over Milan, Italy.
11-09-1609 Explorer Henry Hudson sailed into New York harbor and discovered Manhattan Island and the Hudson River.
11-09-1695 Imperial troops under Eugene of Savoy defeated the Turks at the Battle of Zenta.
11-09-1709 An Anglo-Dutch-Austrian force defeated the French in the Battle of Malplaquet.
11-09-1714 Spanish and French troops broke into Barcelona and ended Catalonia's sovereignty after 13 months of seige.
11-09-1776 A Peace Conference was held between British General Howe and three representatives of the Continental Congress (Benjamin Franklin, John Adams and Edward Rutledge). The conference failed and the American war for independence continued for seven years.
11-09-1777 American forces, under General George Washington, were forced to retreat at the Battle of Brandywine Creek by British forces under William Howe. The Stars and Stripes (American flag) were carried for the first time in the battle.
11-09-1786 The Convention of Annapolis opened with the aim of revising the articles of the confederation.
11-09-1789 Alexander Hamilton was appointed by U.S. President George Washington to be the first secretary of the treasury.
11-09-1814 The U.S. fleet defeated a squadron of British ships in the Battle of Lake Champlain, VT.
11-09-1842 1,400 Mexican troops captured San Antonio, TX. The Mexicans retreated with prisoners.
11-09-1855 The siege of Sevastopol ended when French, British and Piedmontese troops captured the main naval base of the Russian Black fleet in the Crimean War.
11-09-1875 "Professor Tidwissel's Burglar Alarm" was featured in the New York Daily Graphic and became the first comic strip to appear in a newspaper.
11-09-1877 The first comic-character timepiece was patented by the Waterbury Clock Company.
11-09-1883 The mail chute was patented by James Cutler. The new device was first used in the Elwood Building in Rochester, NY.
11-09-1897 A ten-week strike of coal workers in Pennsylvania, WV, and Ohio came to an end. The workers won and eight-hour workday, semi-monthly pay, and company stores were abolished.
11-09-1904 The U.S. battleship Connecticut was launched in New York.
11-09-1910 In Hollywood, the first commercially successful electric bus line opened.
11-09-1926 In Honolulu Harbor, HI, the Aloha Tower was dedicated.
11-09-1936 Boulder Dam in Nevada was dedicated by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt by turning on the dam's first hydroelectric generator. The dam is now called Hoover Dam.
11-09-1941 U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt gave orders to attack any German or Italian vessels found in U.S. defensive waters. The U.S. had not officially entered World War II at this time.
11-09-1941 Charles A. Lindbergh brought on charges of anti-Semitism with a speech in which he blamed "the British, the Jewish and the Roosevelt administration" for trying to draw the United States into World War II.
11-09-1941 In Arlington, VA, the groundbreaking ceremony for the Pentagon took place.
11-09-1951 Florence Chadwick became the first woman to swim the English Channel from both directions.
11-09-1952 Dr. Charles Hufnagel successfully replaced a diseased aorta valve with an artificial valve made of plastic.
11-09-1954 The Miss America beauty pageant made its network TV debut on ABC. Miss California, Lee Ann Meriwether, was the winner.
11-09-1959 The U.S. Congress passed a bill authorizing the creation of food stamps.
11-09-1964 "Friday Night Fights" was seen for the last time.
11-09-1965 The 1st Cavalry Division (Airmobile) arrived in South Vietnam and was stationed at An Khe.
11-09-1967 The Carol Burnett Show premiered on CBS.
11-09-1970 The last "Get Smart" episode aired on CBS-TV.
11-09-1971 Former Soviet leader Nikita Krushchev died at the age of 77 from a heart attack.
11-09-1973 Chilean President Salvador Allende died in a violent coup. Police said he committed suicide. The coup was widely believed to have been linked to the CIA.
11-09-1974 "Little House On The Prairie" made its television debut.
11-09-1974 The St. Louis Cardinals and the New York Mets set a National League record when they played 25 innings. It was the second longest game in professional baseball history.
11-09-1985 Pete Rose of the Cincinnati Reds achieved hit number 4,192 to break the record held by Ty Cobb.
11-09-1985 A U.S. satellite passed through the tail of the Giacobini-Zinner comet. It was the first on-the-spot sampling of a comet.
11-09-1990 U.S. President Bush vowed "Saddam Hussein will fail" while addressing Congress on the Persian Gulf crisis. In the speech Bush spoke of an objective of a new world order - "freer from the threat of terror, stronger in the pursuit of justice, and more secure in the quest for peace".
11-09-1991 Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev announced that thousands of troops would be drawn out of Cuba.
11-09-1991 51 prisoners were released by Israel.
11-09-1992 Hurricane Iniki struck Hawaii. The storm damaged or destroyed over 10,000 homes and killed at least 5 people.
11-09-1994 Actress Jessica Tandy died at the age of 85 in Easton, CT.
11-09-1997 John Lee Hooker received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
11-09-1997 Scotland voted to create its own Parliament after 290 years of union with England.
11-09-1998 Independent counsel Kenneth Starr sent a report to the U.S. Congress accusing President Clinton of 11 possible impeachable offenses.
11-09-1999 The Wall Street Journal reported that Bayer Corp. had quit putting a wad of cotton in their bottles of aspirin. Bayer had actually stopped the practice earlier in the year.
11-09-2001 In the U.S., four airliners were hijacked and were intentionally crashed. Two airliners hit the World Trade Center, which collapsed shortly after, in New York City, NY. One airliner hit the Pentagon in Washington, DC. Another airliner crashed into a field in Pennsylvania. About 3,000 people were killed.
11-09-2002 Nick Nolte was arrested for allegedly driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs. He was released later in the day on $2,500 bail.
11-09-2003 Sweden's Foreign Minister Anna Lindh died from stab wounds inflicted by an unknown assailant the previous day.
12-09-1609 English explorer Henry Hudson sailed down what is now known as the Hudson River.
12-09-1814 During the War of 1812, the Battle of North Point was fought in Maryland.
12-09-1866 "The Black Crook" opened in New York City. It was the first American burlesque show.
12-09-1873 The first practical typewriter was sold to customers.
12-09-1878 Patent litigation involving the Bell Telephone Company against Western Union Telegraph Company and Elisha Gray began. The issues were over varios telephone patents.
12-09-1914 The first battle of Marne ended when the allied forces stopped the German offensive in France.
12-09-1916 Adelina and August Van Buren finished the first successful transcontinental motorcycle tour to be attempted by two women. They started in New York City on July 5, 1916.
12-09-1918 During World War I, At the Battle of St. Mihiel, U.S. Army personnel operate tanks for the first time. The tanks were French-built.
12-09-1922 The Episcopal Church removed the word "Obey" from the bride's section of wedding vows.
12-09-1928 Katharine Hepburn made her stage debut in the play "The Czarina." Four years later she made her film debut in "A Bill of Divorcement."
12-09-1938 In a speech, Adolf Hitler demanded self-determination for the Sudeten Germans in Czechoslovakia.
12-09-1940 The Lascaux paintings were discovered in France. The cave paintings were 17,000 years old and were some of the best examples of art from the Paleolithic period.
12-09-1943 During World War II, Benito Mussolini was taken by German paratroopers from the Italian government that was holding him.
12-09-1944 U.S. Army troops entered Germany, near Trier, for the first time during World War II.
12-09-1953 U.S. Senator John F. Kennedy married Jacqueline Lee Bouvier.
12-09-1953 Nikita Krushchev was elected as the first secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union.
12-09-1954 "Lassie" made its television debut on CBS. The last show aired on September 12, 1971.
12-09-1963 The last episode of "Leave it to Beaver" was aired. The show had debuted on October 4, 1957.
12-09-1966 "Family Affair" premiered on CBS television.
12-09-1974 Violence occurred on the opening day of classes in Boston, MA, due opposition to court-ordered school "busing."
12-09-1974 Emperor Haile Selassie was taken out of power by Ethiopia's military after ruling for 58 years.
12-09-1977 South African anti-apartheid activist Stephen Biko died at the age of 30. The student leader died while in police custody which triggered an international outcry.
12-09-1979 Carl Yastrzemski of the Boston Red Sox became the first American League player to get 3,000 career hits and 400 career home runs.
12-09-1980 Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini listed four conditions for the release of American hostages taken on November 4, 1979. The conditions were the unfreezing of Iranian assets, the return of the shah’s wealth to Iran, the cancellation of U.S. claims against Iran, and a U.S. pledge of noninterference in Iran’s internal affairs.
12-09-1983 Arnold Schwarzenegger became a U.S. citizen. He had emigrated from Austria 14 years earlier.
12-09-1984 Michael Jordan signed a seven-year contract to play basketball with the Chicago Bulls.
12-09-1984 Dwight Gooden of the New York Mets set a rookie strikeout record with his 251st strikeout of the season.
12-09-1986 Joseph Cicippio was kidnapped in Beirut. He was the acting comptroller at the American University in Beirut. Cicippio was released in December of 1991.
12-09-1986 The U.S. released Soviet physicist Gennadiy Zakharov and the Soviet Union released journalist Nicholas Daniloff. Both were put into the custody of their respective countries pending their espionage trials.
12-09-1988 Hurricane Gilbert hit Jamaica killing 45 people and causing about $1 billion in damage.
12-09-1991 The space shuttle Discovery took off on a mission to deploy an observatory that was to study the Earth's ozone layer.
12-09-1992 Police in Peru captured Shining Path founder Abimael Guzman.
12-09-1992 Dr. Mae Carol Jemison became the first African-American woman in space. She was the payload specialist aboard the space shuttle Endeavor. Also onboard were Mission Specialist N. Jan Davis and Air Force Lieutenant Colonel Mark C. Lee. They were the first married couple to fly together in space. And, Mamoru Mohri became the first Japanese person to fly into space.
12-09-1994 Frank Corder was killed when he crashed a stolen, single-engine Cessna on the South Lawn of the White House.
12-09-1995 Two Americans were killed when their hydrogen balloon was shot down by the Belarussian military during an international race.
13-09-1759 The French were defeated by the British on the Plains of Abraham in the final French and Indian War.
13-09-1788 The Constitutional Convention decided that the first federal election was to be held on Wednesday the following February. On that day George Washington was elected as the first president of the United States. In addition, New York City was named the temporary national capital.
13-09-1789 The United States Government took out its first loan.
13-09-1847 U.S. forces took the hill Chapultepec during the Mexican-American War.
13-09-1862 During the American Civil War General Lee's Order No. 191 was found by federal soldiers in Maryland.
13-09-1898 Hannibal Williston Goodwin patented celluloid photographic film, which is used to make movies.
13-09-1922 In El Azizia, Libya, the highest shade temperature was recorded at 136.4 degrees Fahrenheit.
13-09-1937 The first broadcast of "Kitty Keene, Incorporated" was heard on the NBC Red network.
13-09-1943 Chiang Kai-shek became the president of China.
13-09-1948 The School of Performing Arts opened in New York City. It was the first public school to specialize in performing arts.
13-09-1948 Margaret Chase Smith was elected to the U.S. Senate and became the first woman to serve in both houses of the U.S. Congress.
13-09-1949 The Ladies Professional Golf Association of America was formed.
13-09-1959 The Soviet Union's Luna 2 became the first space probe to reach the moon. It was launched the day before.
13-09-1960 The U.S. Federal Communications Commission banned payola.
13-09-1970 The first New York City Marathon took place. Fireman Gary Muhrucke won the race.
13-09-1971 In New York, National Guardsmen stormed the Attica Correctional Facility and put an end to the four-day revolt. A total of 43 people were killed in the final assault. A committee was organized to investigate the riot on September 30, 1971.
13-09-1971 The World Hockey Association was formed.
13-09-1977 The first diesel automobiles were introduced by General Motors.
13-09-1988 Forecasters reported that Hurricane Gilbert's barometric pressure measured 26.13. It was the strongest hurricane ever recorded in the Western Hemisphere.
13-09-1993 "Late Night with Conan O'Brien" premiered on NBC.
13-09-1993 Israel and Palestine signed their first major agreement. Palestine was granted limited self-government in the Gaza Strip and in Jericho.
13-09-1994 U.S. President Bill Clinton signed a $30 billion crime bill into law.
13-09-1995 A grenade was fired at the U.S. Embassy in Moscow. The wall was pierced but there were no injuries.
13-09-1998 The New York Times closed its Web site after hackers added offensive material.
13-09-1999 At least 118 people were killed when a bomb exploded in Moscow, Russia.
13-09-2000 In Albuquerque, NM, former Los Alamos scientist Wen Ho Lee pleaded guilty to one count of mishandling nuclear secrets.
13-09-2001 U.S. Secretary of State Colin Powell named Osama bin Laden as the prime suspect in the terror attacks on the United States on September 11, 2001. Limited commercial flights resumed in the U.S. for the first time in two days.
14-09-1807 Former U.S. Vice President Aaron Burr was acquitted of a misdemeanor charge. Two weeks earlier Burr had been found innocent of treason.
14-09-1812 Moscow was set on fire by Russians after Napoleon Bonaparte's troops invaded.
14-09-1814 Francis Scott Key wrote the "Star-Spangled Banner," a poem originally known as "Defense of Fort McHenry," after witnessing the British bombardment of Fort McHenry, MD, during the War of 1812. The song became the official U.S. national anthem on March 3, 1931.
14-09-1847 U.S. forces took control of Mexico City under the leadership of General Winfield Scott.
14-09-1866 George K. Anderson patented the typewriter ribbon.
14-09-1899 In New York City, Henry Bliss became the first automobile fatality.
14-09-1901 U.S. President William McKinley died of gunshot wounds inflicted by an assassin. Vice President Theodore Roosevelt, at age 42, succeeded him.
14-09-1915 Carl G. Muench received a patent for Insulit, the first sound-absorbing material to be used in buildings.
14-09-1927 Isadora Duncan died when her scarf became entangled in the wheel of her car.
14-09-1938 The VS-300 made its first flight. The craft was based on the helicopter technology patented by Igor Sikorsky.
14-09-1940 The Selective Service Act was passed by the U.S. Congress providing the first peacetime draft in the United States.
14-09-1948 In New York, a groundbreaking ceremony took place at the site of the United Nations' world headquarters.
14-09-1959 Luna II, a Soviet space probe, became the first man-made object on the moon when it crashed on the surface.
14-09-1960 The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) was founded. The core members were Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and Venezuela.
14-09-1963 Mary Ann Fischer gave birth to America's first surviving quintuplets.
14-09-1965 "My Mother The Car" premiered on NBC TV. The series was canceled after only a few weeks after the debut.
14-09-1972 "The Waltons" premiered on CBS-TV.
14-09-1975 Pope Paul VI declared Mother Elizabeth Ann Bayley Seton the first U.S.-born saint.
14-09-1978 "Mork & Mindy" premiered on ABC-TV.
14-09-1982 Princess Grace of Monaco died at the age of 52 because of injuries she suffered the day before in a car crash. She was formerly actress Grace Kelly.
14-09-1982 Bashir Gemayel, Lebanon's president-elect, was killed by a bomb at his party's headquarters in east Beirut.
14-09-1983 The U.S. House of Representatives voted 416-0 in a resolution condemning the Soviet Union for the shooting down of a Korean jet on September 1.
14-09-1984 Joe Kittinger became the first person to fly a balloon solo across the Atlantic Ocean.
14-09-1985 Reverend Benjamin Weir, an American missionary, was released after being held captive for 16 months by Shiite Muslim kidnappers in Lebanon.
14-09-1987 Tony Magnuson cleared 9.5 feet above the top of the U-ramp and set a new skateboard high jump record.
14-09-1989 Joseph T. Wesbecker shot and killed eight people and wounded twelve others at a printing plant in Louisville, KY. Wesbecker, 47 years old, was on disability for mental illness. He took his own life after the incident.
14-09-1994 It was announced that the season was over for the National Baseball League on the 34th day of the players strike. The final days of the regular season were canceled.
14-09-1998 Jaime Jarrin received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
14-09-1998 Israel announced that they had successfully tested its Arrow-2 missile defense system. The system successfully destroyed a simulated target.
14-09-1999 Disney World closed down for the first time in its 28-year history. The closure was due to Hurricane Floyd heading for Florida.
14-09-1999 It was announced that "US" magazine would change from monthly to weekly and change its name to "USWeekly."
14-09-2001 Nintendo released the GameCube home video game console in Japan.
14-09-2001 The FBI released the names of the 19 suspected hijackers that had taken part in the September 11 terror attacks on the U.S.
15-09-1775 An early and unofficial American flag was raised by Lieutenant Colonel Isaac Mott after the seizing of Fort Johnson from the British. The flag was dark blue with the white word "Liberty" spelled on it.
15-09-1776 British forces occupied New York City during the American Revolution.
15-09-1789 The U.S. Department of Foreign Affairs was renamed the Department of State.
15-09-1821 Costa Rica, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua and El Salvador proclaimed independence.
15-09-1853 Reverend Antoinette Brown Blackwell was ordained becoming first female minister was ordained in the United States.
15-09-1857 Timothy Alder earned a patent for the typesetting machine.
15-09-1858 The first mail service begins to the Pacific Coast of the U.S. under government contract. Coaches from the Butterfield Overland Mail Company took 12 days to make the journey between Tipton, MO and San Francisco, CA.
15-09-1883 The University of Texas at Austin opened.
15-09-1909 A New York judge rule that Ford Motor Company had infringed on George Seldon's patent for the "Road Engine." The ruling was later overturned.
15-09-1909 Charles F. Kettering applied for a patent on his ignition system. His company Delco (Dayton Engineering Laboratories Company) later became a subsidiary of General Motors.
15-09-1916 During the Battle of the Somme, in France, tanks were first used in warfare when the British rolled them onto the battlefields.
15-09-1917 Alexander Kerensky proclaimed Russia to be a republic.
15-09-1923 Oklahoma was placed under martial law by Gov. John Calloway Walton due to terrorist activity by the Ku Klux Klan. After this declaration national newspapers began to expose the Klan and its criminal activities.
15-09-1928 Alexander Fleming discovered the antibiotic penicillin in the mold Penicillium notatum.
15-09-1935 The Nuremberg Laws were enacted by Nazi Germany. The act stripped all German Jews of their civil rights and the swastika was made the official symbol of Nazi Germany.
15-09-1940 The German Luftwaffe suffered the loss of 185 planes in the Battle of Britain. The change in tide forced Hitler to abandon his plans for invading Britain.
15-09-1949 "The Lone Ranger" premiered on ABC. Clayton Moore was the Lone Ranger and Jay Silverheels was Tonto.
15-09-1950 U.N. forces land at Inchon, Korea in an attempt to relieve South Korean forces and recapture Seoul.
15-09-1953 The National Boxing Association adopted the 10-point scoring system for all of its matches.
15-09-1955 Betty Robbins became the first woman cantor.
15-09-1959 Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev arrived in the U.S. to begin a 13-day visit.
15-09-1961 The U.S. resumed underground testing of nuclear weapons.
15-09-1963 A church bombing kills four young girls in Birmingham, AL. Robert Chambliss was not brought to justice until 1977.
15-09-1965 "Lost in Space" premiered on CBS TV.
15-09-1965 "Green Acres" premiered on CBS TV.
15-09-1971 Greenpeace was founded.
15-09-1972 The Watergate indictments began against seven perpetrators.
15-09-1978 Muhammad Ali defeated Leon Spinks to win his 3rd World Heavyweight Boxing title.
15-09-1982 The first issue of "USA Today" was published.
15-09-1982 Sadegh Ghotbzadeh, Iran's former foreign minister, was executed. He had been convicted of plotting against the government.
15-09-1983 The U.S. Senate joined the U.S. House of Representatives in their condemning of the Soviet Union for shooting down a Korean jet with 269 people onboard.
15-09-1990 France announced that it would send an additional 4,000 soldiers to the Persian Gulf. They also expelled Iraqi military attaches in Paris.
15-09-1993 The FBI announced a new national campaign concerning the crime of carjacking.
15-09-1993 Katherine Ann Power surrendered to authorities to face charges in a 1970 bank robbery in which Walter Schroeder Sr. of the Boston Police was killed. She had been in hiding for 23 years.
15-09-1994 U.S. President Clinton told Haiti's military leaders "Your time is up. Leave now or we will force you from power."
15-09-1995 The U.N. Fourth World Conference on Women was held in Beijing.
15-09-1998 Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ordered the Iranian military to be on full alert and massed troops on its border with Afghanistan.
15-09-1998 It was announced that 5.9 million people read The Starr Report on the Internet. 606,000 people read the White House defense of U.S. President Clinton.
15-09-1998 David Andrew Douglas was convicted of strangling his 3-year-old granddaughter Shelby Lynne Barrackman because she licked icing off cupcakes.
15-09-1999 A gunman killed 7 people and himself after opening fire at the Wedgewood Baptist Church in Fort Worth, TX.
15-09-1999 The United Nations approved the deployment of a multinational peacekeeping force in East Timor.
15-09-2003 In Independence, MO, the birthplace of Ginger Rogers was designated a local landmark. The move by the Independence City Council qualified the home for historic preservation.
16-09-1400 Owain Glyndwr was proclaimed Prince of Wales after rebelling against English rule. He was the last Welsh-born Prince of Wales.
16-09-1620 The Mayflower departed from Plymouth, England. The ship arrived at Provincetown, MA, on November 21st and then at Plymouth, MA, on December 26th. There were 102 passengers onboard.
16-09-1630 The village of Shawmut changed its name to Boston.
16-09-1782 The Great Seal of the United States was impressed on document to negotiate a prisoner of war agreement with the British. It was the first official use of the impression.
16-09-1810 The Mexicans began a revolt against Spanish rule.
16-09-1893 The "Cherokee Strip" in Oklahoma was swarmed by hundreds of thousands of settlers.
16-09-1908 General Motors was founded by William Crapo "Billy" Durant. The company was formed by merging the Buick and Olds car companies.
16-09-1919 Marvin Middlemark was born. He was the inventor of the rabbit ears TV antenna.
16-09-1919 The American Legion was incorporated by an act of the U.S. Congress.
16-09-1924 Jim Bottomley knocked in 12 runs in a single game setting a major league baseball record.
16-09-1940 U.S. President Roosevelt signed into law the Selective Training and Service Act, which set up the first peacetime military draft in U.S. history.
16-09-1940 Samuel T. Rayburn of Texas was elected Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives. He served for 17 years.
16-09-1941 "The Arkansas Traveler" debuted on CBS Radio. The show was later renamed "The Bob Burns Show."
16-09-1953 "The Robe" premiered at the Roxy Theater in New York. It was the first movie filmed in the wide screen CinemaScope process.
16-09-1953 The St. Louis Browns of the American League were given permission to move to Baltimore, MD, where they became the Baltimore Orioles.
16-09-1963 "The Outer Limits" premiered on ABC-TV.
16-09-1965 "The Dean Martin Show" debuted on NBC-TV.
16-09-1968 "The Andy Griffith Show" was seen for the final time on CBS.
16-09-1972 "The Bob Newhart Show" premiered on CBS-TV.
16-09-1974 U.S. President Ford announced a conditional amnesty program for draft-evaders and deserters during the Vietnam War.
16-09-1976 The Episcopal Church formally approved women to be ordained as priests and bishops.
16-09-1982 In west Beirut, the massacre of hundreds of Palestinian men, women and children began in refugee camps of the Lebanese Christian militiamen.
16-09-1985 The Communist Party in China announced changes in leadership that were designed to bring younger officials into power.
16-09-1987 The Montreal Protocol was signed by 24 countries in an effort to save the Earth's ozone layer by reducing emissions of harmful chemicals by the year 2000.
16-09-1988 Tom Browning pitched the 12th perfect game in major league baseball.
16-09-1990 An eight-minute videotape of an address by U.S. President Bush was shown on Iraqi television. The message warned that action of Saddam Hussein could plunge them into a war "against the world."
16-09-1991 A federal judge in Washington dismissed the Iran-Contra charges against Oliver North.
16-09-1994 Exxon Corporation was ordered by federal jury to pay $5 billion in punitive damages to the people harmed by the 1989 Exxon Valdez spill.
16-09-1994 Two astronauts from the space shuttle Discovery went on the first untethered spacewalk in 10 years.
16-09-1998 Universal paid $9 million for the rights to the Dr. Seuss classics "How the Grinch Stole Christmas" and "Oh, the Places You'll Go."
16-09-1998 Meryl Streep received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
16-09-1999 In Volgodnosk, Russia, a bomb in an apartment killed at least 17 people. Chechen militants seeking independence from Russia were suspected as the planners.
17-09-1778 The United States signed its first treaty with a Native American tribe, the Delaware Nation.
17-09-1787 The Constitution of the United States of America was signed by delegates at the Constitutional Convention.
17-09-1796 U.S. President George Washington's Farewell Address was read before the U.S. Congress.
17-09-1862 The Battle of Antietam took place during the American Civil War. More than 23,000 men were killed, wounded, or missing. The Rebel advance was ended with heavy losses to both armies.
17-09-1872 Phillip W. Pratt patented a version of the sprinkler system.
17-09-1908 An airplane crashed during a demonstration in Arlington Heights, VA. Thomas Selfridge was killed, becoming the first person to be killed in an airplane accident. Orville Wright, the pilot, survived with multiple hip and leg fractures.
17-09-1911 The first transcontinental airplane flight started. It took C.P. Rogers 82 hours to fly from New York City to Pasadena, CA.
17-09-1920 The American Professional Football Association was formed in Canton, OH. It was the precursor to the National Football League (NFL).
17-09-1930 Construction on Boulder Dam, later renamed Hoover Dam, began in Black Canyon, near Las Vegas, NV.
