17-09-1932 Sir Malcolm Campbell set a speed record when he reached 276.27 mph over a half mile.
17-09-1937 At Mount Rushmore, Abraham Lincoln's face was dedicated.
17-09-1939 The Soviet Union invaded Poland. Germany had invaded Poland on September 1.
17-09-1944 Operation "Market Garden" was launched by Allied paratroopers during World War II. The landing point was behind German lines in the Netherlands.
17-09-1947 The first U.S. Secretary of Defense, James V. Forrestal, was sworn in to office.
17-09-1948 Count Folk Bernadette, the United Nations mediator for Palestine, was assissinated in Jerusalem by Jewish extremists.
17-09-1949 The Canadian passenger steamer Noronic was gutted by fire at a Toronto pier. 130 people were killed.
17-09-1953 Ernie Banks became the first black baseball player to wear a Chicago Cubs uniform. He retired in 1971 known as 'Mr. Cub'.
17-09-1953 The Ochsner Foundation Hospital in New Orleans, LA, successfully separated Siamese twins. Carolyn Anne and Catherine Anne Mouton were connected at the waist when born.
17-09-1955 "The Perry Como Show" moved to Saturday nights on NBC-TV.
17-09-1961 The Minnesota Vikings were debuted as the new National Football League (NFL) team.
17-09-1962 U.S. space officials announced the selection of Neil A. Armstrong and eight others as new astronauts.
17-09-1963 "The Fugitive" premiered on ABC-TV. The show starred David Janssen.
17-09-1964 "Bewitched" premiered on ABC-TV.
17-09-1965 "Hogan's Heroes" debuted on CBS-TV.
17-09-1966 "Mission Impossible" premiered on CBS-TV.
17-09-1972 "M*A*S*H" premiered on CBS-TV.
17-09-1976 NASA unveiled the space shuttle Enterprise in Palmdale, CA.
17-09-1980 Anastasio Somoza, former Nicaraguan president, was assassinated in Paraguay.
17-09-1983 Vanessa Williams, as Miss New York, became the first black woman to be crowned Miss America.
17-09-1983 Johnny Bench, of the Cincinnati Reds, retired after 16 years as a catcher.
17-09-1983 Carl Yastrzemski of the Boston Red Sox broke Hank Aaron's major league record for games played when he started his 3,299th game. (MLB)
17-09-1984 9,706 immigrants became naturalized citizens when they were sworn in by U.S. Vice-President George Bush in Miami, FL. It was the largest group to become U.S. citizens.
17-09-1984 Gordon P. Getty was named the richest person in the U.S. His fortune was $4.1 billion.
17-09-1984 Reggie Jackson hit his 500th career home run. It was exactly 17 years from the day he hit his first major league home run.
17-09-1986 A bomb exploded outside of a department store in Paris killing 7 and injuring 51.
17-09-1988 Lt. Gen. Prosper Avril declared himself president of Haiti after President Henri Hamphy was ousted.
17-09-1991 The United Nations General Assembly opened its 46th session. The new members were Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, North and South Korea, Micronesia and the Marshall Islands.
17-09-1992 Lawrence Walsh called a halt to his probe of the Iran-Contra scandal. The investigation had lasted 5 1/2 years.
17-09-1995 Hong Kong held its last legislative election before being taken over by China in 1997.
17-09-1997 Northern Ireland's main Protestant party joined in peace talks. It was the first time that all of the major players had come together.
17-09-1998 The United States government offered a reward for the capture of Haroun Fazil for his role in the U.S. bombing in Kenya on August 7, 1998.
17-09-1998 The U.S. announced a plan that would compensate victims in the Kenya and Tanzania U.S. Embassy bombings on August 7, 1998.
17-09-1998 A United Nations convoy was ambushed in Angola. There were no serious injuries from the fire fight that occurred.
17-09-2004 The USS Curts intercepted the fishing vessel Lina Maria about 300 miles southwest of the Galapagos Islands. The fishing boat had 30,000 pounds of cocaine on board. At the time it was the largest cocaine seizure in U.S. Coast Guard history.
18-09-1709 The creator of the first dictionary of the English language, Samuel Johnson, was born in England.
18-09-1759 The French formally surrendered Quebec to the British.
18-09-1763 It was reported, by the Boston Gazette, that the first piano had been built in the United States. The instrument was named the spinet and was made by John Harris.
18-09-1789 Alexander Hamilton negotiated and secured the first loan for the United States. The Temporary Loan of 1789 was repaid on June 8, 1790 at the sum of $191,608.81.
18-09-1793 U.S. President George Washington laid the actual cornerstone of the U.S. Capitol.
18-09-1810 Chile declared its independence from Spain.
18-09-1830 The "Tom Thumb", the first locomotive built in America, raced a horse on a nine-mile course. The horse won when the locomotive had some mechanical difficulties.
18-09-1850 The Fugitive Slave Act was declared by the U.S. Congress. The act allowed slave owners to claim slaves that had escaped into other states.
18-09-1851 The first issue of "The New York Times" was published.
18-09-1891 Harriet Maxwell Converse became the first white woman to ever be named chief of an Indian tribe. The tribe was the Six Nations Tribe at Towanda Reservation in New York.
18-09-1895 Daniel David Palmer gave the first chiropractic adjustment.
18-09-1927 Columbia Phonograph Broadcasting System made its debut with its network broadcast over 16 radio stations. The name was later changed to CBS.
18-09-1940 "You Can't Go Home Again" by Thomas Wolfe was published by Harper and Brothers.
18-09-1947 The U.S. Air Force was established as a separate military branch by the National Security Act.
18-09-1955 The "Ed Sullivan Show" began on CBS-TV. The show had been "The Toast of the Town" since 1948.
18-09-1961 United Nations Secretary-General Dag Hammarskjold was killed in a plane crash in northern Rhodesia (now Zambia).
18-09-1963 "The Patty Duke Show" premiered on ABC-TV.
18-09-1965 The first episode of "I Dream of Jeannie" was shown on NBC-TV. The last show was televised on September 1, 1970.
18-09-1975 The FBI captured newspaper heiress Patricia Hearst in San Francisco, CA. 19 months earlier she had been kidnapped by the Symbionese Liberation Army.
18-09-1981 A museum honoring former U.S. President Ford was dedicated in Grand Rapids, MI.
18-09-1984 The 39th session of the U.N. General Assembly was opened with an appeal to the U.S. and Soviet Union to resume arms negotiations.
18-09-1990 Charles H. Keating was jailed in Los Angeles after being indicted on criminal fraud charges concerning saving-and-loans.
18-09-1991 U.S. President Bush said that he would send warplanes to escort U.N. helicopters that were searching for hidden Iraqi weapons if it became necessary.
18-09-1994 Haiti's military leaders agreed to depart on October 15th. This action averted a U.S.-led invasion to force them out of power.
18-09-1997 Ted Turner, U.S. Media magnate, announced that over the next ten years he would give $1 billion to the United Nations.
18-09-1998 18 people, including adults and children, were massacred by gunmen in el Sauzal, Mexico. The victims were lined up in firing squad style after being dragged from their beds.
18-09-1998 The U.S. House Judiciary Committee voted to release to videotape of President Clinton's grand jury testimony from August 17.
18-09-1998 The FDA approved a once-a-day easier-to-swallow medication for AIDS patients.
18-09-2003 Robert Duvall received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
19-09-1777 The Battle of Saratoga was won by American soldiers during the Revolutionary War.
19-09-1796 U.S. President Washington's farewell address was published.
19-09-1819 John Keats wrote "Ode to Autumn."
19-09-1876 Melville R. Bissell patented the carpet sweeper.
19-09-1881 James A. Garfield died of wounds from an assassin. The 20th U.S. president lived for 11 weeks after the wounds were inflicted.
19-09-1891 "The Merchant of Venice" was performed for the first time at Manchester.
19-09-1893 In New Zealand, the Electoral Act 1893 was consented to giving all women in New Zealand the right to vote.
19-09-1934 Bruno Hauptman was arrested in New York and charged with the kidnapping and murder of the infant son of Charles and Anna Lindbergh.
19-09-1945 William Joyce, also known as "Lord Haw-Haw", was sentenced to death by a British court for his role as a Nazi propagandist.
19-09-1955 Eva Marie Saint, Frank Sinatra and Paul Newman starred in the "Producer's Showcase" presentation of "Our Town" on NBC-TV.
19-09-1955 Argentina President Juan Peron was ousted after a revolt by the army and navy.
19-09-1957 The U.S. conducted its first underground nuclear test. The test took place in the Nevada desert.
19-09-1959 Nikita Khruschev was not allowed to visit Disneyland due to security reasons. Khrushchev reacted angrily.
19-09-1960 Cuban leader Fidel Castro, in New York to visit the United Nations, checked out of the Shelburne Hotel angrily after a dispute with the management.
19-09-1970 "The Mary Tyler Moore Show" premiered on CBS-TV.
19-09-1982 Scott Fahlman became the first person to use :-) in an online message.
19-09-1983 Lebanese army units defending Souk el-Gharb were supported in their effort by two U.S. Navy ships off Beirut.
19-09-1983 The final episode of "M*A*S*H" was aired on CBS-TV.
19-09-1984 China and Britain completed a draft agreement transferring Hong Kong from British to Chinese rule by 1997.
19-09-1985 An earthquake registering 8.1 on the Richter Scale hit the Mexico City area. About 6,000 people were killed.
19-09-1986 U.S. health officials announced that AZT, though an experimental drug, would be made available to AIDS patients.
19-09-1988 Israel successfully launched the Horizon-I test satellite.
19-09-1989 A DC-10 belonging to the French airliner UTA disappeared while carrying 171 people to Paris. The wreckage of the plane was found the next day in Niger. It was believed a bomb was responsible.
19-09-1990 Iraq began confiscating foreign assets of countries that were imposing sanctions against the Iraqi government.
19-09-1992 The U.N. Security Council recommended suspending Yugoslavia due to its role in the Bosnian civil war.
19-09-1994 U.S. troops entered Haiti peacefully to enforce the return of exiled President Jean-Bertrand Aristide.
19-09-1995 The Unabomber's manifesto was published by The Washington Post and the New York Times.
19-09-1995 The U.S. Senate passed a welfare overhaul bill.
19-09-1995 The commander of American forces in Japan and the U.S. ambassador apologized for the rape of a schoolgirl committed by three U.S. servicemen.
19-09-1996 The government of Guatemala and leftist rebels signed a peace treaty to end their long war.
19-09-2002 In Ivory Coast, around 750 rebel soldiers attempted to overthrow the government. U.S. troops landed on September 25th to help move foreigners, including Americans, to safer areas.
19-09-2003 It was reported that AOL Time Warner was going to drop "AOL" from its name and be known as Time Warner Inc. The company had announced its merger and name change on January 10, 2000.
20-09-1519 Portuguese navigator Ferdinand Magellan left Spain to find a route to the Spice Islands of Indonesia. Magellan was killed during the trip, but one of his ships eventually made the journey.
20-09-1870 The Papal States came under the control of Italian troops, leading to the unification of Italy.
20-09-1881 Chester A. Arthur became the 21st president of the U.S. President James A. Garfield had died the day before.
20-09-1884 The Equal Rights Party was formed in San Francisco, CA.
20-09-1921 KDKA in Pittsburgh, PA, started a daily radio newscast. It was one of the first in the U.S.
20-09-1946 The first Cannes Film Festival premiered. The original premier was delayed in 1939 due to World War II.
20-09-1946 WNBT-TV in New York became the first station to promote a motion picture. Scenes from "The Jolson Story" were shown.
20-09-1953 The TV show "Letter to Loretta" premiered. The name was changed to "The Loretta Young Show" on February 14, 1954.
20-09-1953 Jimmy Stewart debuted on the radio western "The Six Shooter" on NBC.
20-09-1955 "You'll Never Be Rich" premiered on CBS-TV. The name was changed less than two months later to "The Phil Silvers Show."
20-09-1958 Martin Luther King Jr. was stabbed in the chest at a New York City department store by an apparently deranged black woman.
20-09-1962 James Meredith, a black student, was blocked from enrolling at the University of Mississippi by Governor Ross R. Barnett. Meredith was later admitted.
20-09-1963 U.S. President John F. Kennedy proposed a joint U.S.-Soviet expedition to the moon in a speech to the U.N. General Assembly.
20-09-1977 The first of the "boat people" arrived in San Francisco from Southeast Asia under a new U.S. resettlement program.
20-09-1982 U.S. President Ronald Reagan announced that the U.S., France, and Italy were going to send peacekeeping troops back to Beirut.
20-09-1984 A Hizbulla suicide bomber destroyed the rebuilt U.S. Embassy in Beirut. 25 people were killed.
20-09-1984 "The Cosby Show" premiered on NBC-TV.
20-09-1985 A second major earthquake hit Mexico City.
20-09-1988 The United Nations opened it 43rd General Assembly.
20-09-1989 The wreckage of a DC-10 belonging to the French airliner UTA was found in Niger. The plane disappeared on September 19 with 171 passengers onboard. The Paris-bound plane was believed to have been brought down by a bomb.
20-09-1989 F.W. de Klerk was sworn in as president of South Africa.
20-09-1991 U.N. weapons inspectors left for Iraq in a renewed search for Iraqi weapons of mass destruction.
20-09-1992 French voters approved the Maastricht Treaty.
20-09-1995 AT&T announced that it would be splitting into three companies. The three companies were AT&T, Lucent Technologies, and NCR Corp.
20-09-1995 The U.S. House of Representatives voted to drop the national speed limit. This allowed the states to decide their own speed limits.
20-09-1999 Raisa Gorbachev, wife of former Soviet President Mikhail Gorvachev, died of leukemia.
21-09-1792 The French National Convention voted to abolish the monarchy.
21-09-1784 "The Pennsylvania Packet and Daily Advertiser" was published for the first time in Philadelphia. It was the first daily paper in America.
21-09-1893 Frank Duryea took what is believed to be the first gasoline- powered automobile for a test drive. The "horseless carriage" was designed by Frank and Charles Duryea.
21-09-1897 The New York Sun ran the "Yes, Virginia, there is a Santa Clause" editorial. It was in response to a letter from 8-year-old Virginia O'Hanlon.
21-09-1931 Britain went off the gold standard.
21-09-1931 Japanese forces began occupying China's northeast territory of Manchuria.
21-09-1937 J.R.R. Tolkien's "The Hobbit" was first published.
21-09-1938 A hurricane struck parts of New York and New England killing more than 600 people.
21-09-1941 "The Second Mrs. Burton" premiered to the entire CBS Radio Network.
21-09-1948 Milton Berle debuted as the host of "The Texaco Star Theater" on NBC-TV. The show later became "The Milton Berle Show." Berle was the regular host until 1967.
21-09-1948 "Life With Luigi" debuted on CBS Radio.
21-09-1949 Communist leaders proclaimed The People's Republic of China.
21-09-1957 "Perry Mason", the television series, made its debut on CBS-TV. The show was on for 9 years.
21-09-1961 Antonio Abertondo swam the English Channel (in both directions) in 24 hours and 25 minutes.
21-09-1964 Malta gained independence from Britain.
21-09-1966 The Soviet probe Zond 5 returned to Earth. The spacecraft completed the first unmanned round-trip flight to the moon.
21-09-1970 "NFL Monday Night Football" made its debut on ABC-TV. The game was between the Cleveland Browns and the New York Jets. The Browns won 31-21.
21-09-1973 Henry Kissinger was confirmed by the U.S. Senate to become 56th Secretary of State. He was the first naturalized citizen to hold the office of Secretary of State.
21-09-1976 Orlando Letelier, former foreign minister for President Salvador Allende of Chili, was killed by a car bomb in Washington, DC.
21-09-1981 The U.S. Senate confirmed Sandra Day O'Connor to be the first female justice on the U.S. Supreme Court.
21-09-1982 National Football League (NFL) players began a 57-day strike. It was their first regular-season walkout.
21-09-1982 Amin Gemayel was elected president of Lebanon. He was the brother of Bashir Gemayel who was the president-elect when he was assassinated.
21-09-1984 General Motors and the United Auto Workers union reached an agreement that would end the previous six days of spot strikes.
21-09-1985 North and South Korea opened their borders for their family reunion program.
21-09-1989 Hurricane Hugo hit Charleston, SC, causing $8 billion in damage.
21-09-1989 21 students were killed in Alton, TX, when their bus was in an accident with a truck causing the bus to careen into a water-filled pit.
21-09-1991 Richard L. Worthington finally freed his nine hostages at the end of 18 hours in Sandy, UT. Worthington had killed a nurse before seizing control of a hospital maternity ward.
21-09-1993 Russian President Boris N. Yeltsin announced that he was ousting the Communist-dominated Congress. The action was effectively seizing all state power.
21-09-1996 The board of all-male Virginia Military Institute voted to admit women.
21-09-1996 John F. Kennedy Jr. married Carolyn Bessette in a secret ceremony on Cumberland Island, GA.
21-09-1998 The videotaped grand jury statement that U.S. President Bill Clinton made concerning the Monica Lewinsky case was made public.
22-09-1656 An all-female jury heard the case of a woman murdering her child. The jury in Patuxent, MD, voted for acquittal.
22-09-1776 During the Revolutionary War, Nathan Hale was hanged as a spy by the British.
22-09-1789 The U.S. Congress authorized the office of Postmaster General.
22-09-1792 The French Republic was proclaimed.
22-09-1828 Shaka, the African ruler and founder of the Zulu kingdom, was murdered by his half-brother Dingane. Shaka's mental illness had begun to compromise his leadership.
22-09-1862 U.S. President Lincoln issued the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation. It stated that all slaves held within rebel states would be free as of January 1, 1863.
22-09-1903 Italo Marchiony was granted a patent for the ice cream cone.
22-09-1914 Three British cruisers were sunk by one German submarine in the North Sea. 1,400 British sailors were killed. This event alerted the British to the effectiveness of the submarine.
22-09-1927 In Chicago, IL, Gene Tunney successfully defended his heavyweight boxing title against Jack Dempsey in the famous "long-count" fight.
22-09-1949 The Soviet Union exploded its first atomic bomb successfully.
22-09-1955 Commercial television began in Great Britain. The rules said that only six minutes of ads were allowed each hour and there was no Sunday morning TV permitted.
22-09-1961 U.S. President John F. Kennedy signed a congressional act that established the Peace Corps.
22-09-1964 "The Man From U.N.C.L.E." debuted on NBC-TV.
22-09-1966 The U.S. lunar probe Surveyor 2 crashed into the moon.
22-09-1969 Willie Mays hit his 600th career home run.
22-09-1975 Sara Jane Moore attempted to assassinate U.S. President Gerald Ford. 17 days earlier Lynnette "Squeaky" Fromme made an assassination attempt against Ford.
22-09-1980 A border conflict between Iran and Iraq developed into a full-scale war.
22-09-1986 U.S. President Ronald Reagan addressed the U.N. General Assembly and voiced a new hope for arms control. He also criticized the Soviet Union for arresting U.S. journalist Nicholas Daniloff.
22-09-1988 Canada's government apologized for the internment of Japanese-Canadian's during World War II. They also promised compensation.
22-09-1990 Saudi Arabia expelled most of the Yememin and Jordanian envoys in Riyadh. The Saudi accusations were unspecific.
22-09-1991 An article in the London newspaper "The Mail" revealed that John Cairncross admitted to being the "fifth man" in the Soviet Union's British spy ring.
22-09-1992 The U.N. General Assembly expelled Yugoslavia for its role in the war between Bosnia and Herzegovina.
22-09-1993 47 people were killed when an Amtrak passenger train derailed near Mobile, AL.
22-09-1994 The U.S. upgraded its military control in Haiti.
22-09-1995 AWACS plane crashed on takeoff at Elmendorf Air Force Base near Anchorage, AK. All 24 of the U.S. and Canadian military personnel were killed.
22-09-1996 Robert Dent, in Australia, became the first person to commit legally assisted suicide under a voluntary euthanasia law. Dent was suffering from terminal cancer.
22-09-1998 The U.S. and Russia signed two agreements. One was to privatize Russia's nuclear program and the other was to stop plutonium stockpiles and nuclear scientists from leaving the country.
22-09-1998 U.S. President Clinton, addressed the United Nations, and told world leaders to "end all nuclear tests for all time". He then sent the long-delayed global test-ban treaty to the U.S. Senate.
22-09-1998 Keely Smith received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
23-09-1642 The first commencement at Harvard College, in Cambridge, MA, was held.
23-09-1713 King Ferdinand VI of Spain was born in Madrid. He was king from 1746 to 1759.
23-09-1779 John Paul Jones, commander of the American warship Bon Homme, was quoted as saying "I have not yet begun to fight!"
23-09-1780 John Andre, a British spy, was captured with papers revealing that Benedict Arnold was going to surrender West Point, NY, to the British.
23-09-1806 The Corps of Discovery, the Lewis and Clark expedition, reached St. Louis, MO, and ended the trip to the Pacific Northwest.
23-09-1838 Victoria Chaflin Woodhull was born. She became the first female candidate for the U.S. Presidency.
23-09-1845 The Knickerbocker Base Ball Club of New York was formed by Alexander Joy Cartwright. It was the first baseball team in America.
23-09-1846 Astronomer Johann Gottfried Galle discovered the planet Neptune.
23-09-1897 The first recorded traffic fatality in Great Britain occurred. It happened 2 years before the first fatality in the U.S.
23-09-1912 "Keystone Comedy" by Mack Sennett was released.
23-09-1930 Flashbulbs were patented by Johannes Ostermeier.
23-09-1939 Sigmund Freud died in London. He was recognized as the founder of psychoanalysis.
23-09-1951 The first transcontinental telecast was received on the west coast. The show "Crusade for Freedom" was broadcast by CBS-TV from New York.
23-09-1952 The first Pay Television sporting event took place. The Marciano-Walcott fight was seen in 49 theaters in 31 cities.
23-09-1952 Richard Nixon gave his "Checkers Speech". At the time he was a candidate for U.S. vice-president.
23-09-1953 "The Robe" premiered in Hollywood a week after its premiere in New York. The 20th Century Fox movie had been filmed using the Cinemascope wide screen process.
23-09-1957 Nine black students withdrew from Little Rock Central High School in Arkansas due to the white mob outside.
23-09-1962 New York's Philharmonic Hall opened. It was the first unit of the Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts. The hall was later renamed the Avery Fisher Hall.
23-09-1962 "The Jetsons" premiered on ABC-TV. It was the first program on the network to be carried in color.
23-09-1964 The new ceiling painting of the Paris Opera house was unveiled. The work was done by Russian-born artist Marc Chagall.
23-09-1973 Overthrown Argentine president Juan Peron was returned to power. He had been overthrown in 1955. His wife, Eva Duarte, was the subject of the musical "Evita."
23-09-1981 The Reagan administration announced its plans for what became known as Radio Marti.
23-09-1986 Japanese newspapers quoted Prime Minister Yasuhiro Nakasone as saying that minorities lowered the "intelligence level" of America.
23-09-1990 Iraq publicly threatened to destroy Middle East oil fields and to attack Israel if any nation tried to force it from Kuwait.
23-09-1991 U.N. weapons inspectors find documents detailing Iraq's secret nuclear weapons program. The find in Baghdad triggered a standoff with authorities in Iraq.
23-09-1993 The Israeli parliament ratified the Israel-PLO accord.
23-09-1993 Blacks were allowed a role in the South African government after a parliamentary vote.
23-09-1998 Jamie Lee Curtis received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
23-09-1999 In Bryan, TX, Lawrence Russell Brewer was sentenced to death for the dragging death of James Byrd Jr. John William King was sentenced to die on February 25, 1999.
23-09-1999 A 17-month-old girl fell 230 feet from the Capilano Suspension Bridge in North Vancouver, British Columbia. The girl had bruises but no broken limbs from the fall onto a rocky ledge.
23-09-1999 Siegfried & Roy received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
23-09-1999 Jean-Claude Van Damme was arrested for drunk driving and driving without a license after he crashed his Mercedes-Benz into a restaurant. On July 10, 2000, Van Damme was given three years probation and fined $1,200.
24-09-1755 John Marshall was born. He was the fourth Chief Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court. His court was credited with defining the principles of government and the role of the Supreme Court.
24-09-1789 The U.S. Congress passed the First Judiciary Act. The act provided for an Attorney General and a Supreme Court.
24-09-1869 Thousands of businessmen were financially ruined after a panic on Wall Street. The panic was caused by an attempt to corner the gold market by Jay Gould and James Fisk.
24-09-1880 Sarah Knauss was born. She was the world's oldest person when she died at 119 years old on December 31, 1999.
24-09-1915 "The Lamb," Douglas Fairbanks first film, was shown at the Knickerbocker Theater in New York City, NY.
24-09-1929 The first all-instrument flight took place in New York when Lt. James H. Doolittle guided a Consolidated NY2 Biplane over Mitchell Field.
24-09-1933 "Roses and Drums" was heard on WABC in New York City. It was the first dramatic presentation for radio.
24-09-1934 Babe Ruth played his last game as a New York Yankee player.
24-09-1938 Don Budge became the first tennis player to win all four of the major titles when he won the U.S. Tennis Open. He had already won the Australian Open, the French Open and the British Open.
24-09-1948 Mildred Gillars, known as "Axis Sally", pleaded innocent to charges of treason. She ended up serving 12 years for being a Nazi wartime radio propagandist.
24-09-1953 The discovery of the antibiotic tetracycline was reported.
24-09-1955 U.S. President Dwight Eisenhower suffered a heart attack while on vacation in Denver, CO.
24-09-1957 The Brooklyn Dodgers played their last game at Ebbets Field.
24-09-1957 U.S. President Eisenhower sent federal troops to Little Rock, AR, to enforce school integration.
24-09-1960 The first nuclear powered aircraft carrier was launched. The USS Enterprise set out from Newport News, VA.
24-09-1961 "The Bullwinkle Show" premiered in prime time on NBC-TV. The show was originally on ABC in the afternoon as "Rocky and His Friends."
24-09-1963 The U.S. Senate ratified a treaty that limited nuclear testing. The treaty was between the U.S., Britain, and the Soviet Union.
24-09-1968 "60 Minutes" premiered on CBS-TV.
24-09-1968 "The Mod Squad" premiered on ABC-TV.
24-09-1969 The trial began for the "Chicago Eight," who were accused of inciting riots at the 1968 Democratic national convention.
24-09-1976 Patricia Hearst was sentenced to 7 years in prison for her role in a 1974 bank robbery. An executive clemency order from U.S. President Jimmy Carter set her free after only 22 months.
24-09-1977 "The Love Boat" debuted on ABC-TV. The theme song was sung by Jack Jones and was written by Paul Williams and Charles Fox.
24-09-1991 Jack Mann, a British hostage, was set free by Lebanese kidnappers. He had been held captive for more than two years.
24-09-1991 Theodor Seuss Geisel died at the age of 87. The children's author is better known as Dr. Seuss.
24-09-1994 Ten Haitians were killed when a firefight erupted between U.S. Marines and a group of armed Haitians in Cap-Haitian.
24-09-1995 Three decades of Israeli occupation of West Bank cities ended with the signing of a pact by Israel and the PLO.
24-09-1996 The United States, represented by President Clinton, and the world's other major nuclear powers signed a Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty to end all testing and development of nuclear weapons.
24-09-1998 Gianluigi Assennato, 34, will be tried for one count of stalking and three counts of making terrorist threats towards Andrea Thompson.
24-09-1998 The U.S. Federal Reserve released into circulation $2 billion in new harder-to-counterfeit $20 bills.
24-09-2001 U.S. President George W. Bush froze the assets of 27 suspected terrorists and terrorist groups.
24-09-2003 Anthony Hopkins received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
24-09-2004 The USS Crommelin stopped the fishing boat San Jose. The Coast Guard team found 26,000 pounds of cocaine.
25-09-1492 The crew of the Pinta, one of Christopher Columbus' ships, mistakenly thought that they had spotted land.
25-09-1493 Christopher Columbus left Spain with 17 ships on his second voyage to the Western Hemisphere.
25-09-1513 The Pacific Ocean was discovered by Spanish explorer Vasco Nunez de Balboa when he crossed the Isthmus of Panama. He named the body of water the South Sea. He was truly just the first European to see the Pacific Ocean.
25-09-1690 One of America's earliest newspapers published its first and last edition. The "Publik Occurences Both Foreign and Domestik" was published at the London Coffee House in Boston, MA, by Benjamin Harris.
25-09-1725 Nicolas Joseph Cugnot was born. He was the inventor and builder of two steam-propelled tractors. They are considered to be the world's first automobiles.
25-09-1775 Ethan Allen was captured by the British during the American Revolutionary War. He was leading the attack on Montreal.
25-09-1789 The first U.S. Congress adopted 12 amendments to the Constitution. Ten of the amendments became the Bill of Rights.
25-09-1847 During the Mexican-American War, U.S. forces led by General Zachary Taylor captured Monterrey Mexico.
25-09-1882 The first major league double header was played. It was between the Worcester and Providence teams.
25-09-1890 The Sequoia National Park was established as a U.S. National Park in Central California.
25-09-1890 Mormon President Wilford Woodruff issued a Manifesto in which the practice of polygamy was renounced.
25-09-1897 Author William Faulkner was born. He is remembered for his works "As I Lay Dying," "Light in August" and "The Sound and the Fury."
25-09-1919 U.S. President Woodrow Wilson collapsed after a speech in Pueblo, CO. The speaking tour was in support of the Treaty of Versailles.
25-09-1933 Tom Mix was heard on NBC Radio for the first time. His show ran until June of 1950.
25-09-1956 A transatlantic telephone-cable system began operation between Newfoundland and Scotland.
25-09-1957 300 U.S. Army troops stood guard as nine black students were escorted to class at Central High School in Little Rock, AR. The children had been forced to withdraw 2 days earlier because of unruly white mobs.
25-09-1965 Willie Mays, at the age of 34, became the oldest man to hit 50 home runs in a single season. He had also set the record for the youngest to hit 50 ten years earlier.
25-09-1973 The three crewmen of Skylab II landed in the Pacific Ocean after being on the U.S. space laboratory for 59 days.
25-09-1978 144 people were killed when a private plane and a Pacific Southwest Airlines Boeing 727 collided over San Diego, CA.
25-09-1978 Melissa Ludtke, a writer for "Sports Illustrated", filed a suit in U.S. District Court. The result was that Major League Baseball could not bar female writers from the locker room after the game.
25-09-1981 Sandra Day O'Connor became the first female justice of the U.S. Supreme Court when she was sworn in as the 102nd justice. The had been nominated the previous July by U.S. President Ronald Reagan.
25-09-1983 38 Irish nationalist guerillas shot their way out of prison near Belfast, Northern Ireland.
25-09-1983 A Soviet military officer, Stanislav Petrov, averted a potential worldwide nuclear war. He declared a false alarm after a U.S. attack was detected by a Soviet early warning system. It was later discovered the alarms had been set off when the satellite warning system mistakenly interpreted sunlight reflections off clouds as the presence of enemy missiles.
25-09-1986 An 1894-S Barber Head dime was bought for $83,000 at a coin auction in California. It is one of a dozen that exist.
25-09-1987 The booty collected from the Wydah, which sunk off Cape Cod in 1717, was auctioned off. The worth was around $400 million.
25-09-1990 The U.N. Security Council voted to impose an air embargo against Iraq. Cuba was the only dissenting vote.
25-09-1991 The U.N. Security Council unanimously ordered a worldwide arms embargo against Yugoslavia and all of its warring factions.
25-09-1992 In Orlando, FL, a judge ruled in favor of 12-year-old Gregory Kingsley. He had sought a divorce from his biological parents.
25-09-1992 The Mars Observer blasted off on a mission that cost $980 million. The probe has not been heard from since it reached Mars in August of 1993.
25-09-1995 Ross Perot announced that he would form the Independence Party.
25-09-1997 NBC sportscaster Marv Albert plead guilty to assault and battery of a lover. He was fired from NBC within hours.
25-09-1997 Mark & Brian received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.
25-09-1998 The death toll due to Hurricane Georges rose to 307 after the storm passed through Caribbean.
25-09-2001 Michael Jordan announced that he would return to the NBA as a player for the Washington Wizards. Jordan became the president of basketball operations for the team on January 19, 2000.
25-09-2002 In Karachi, Pakistan, seven people were killed and another were wounded by gunmen in the offices of a Christian welfare organization.
25-09-2002 U.S. forces landed in Ivory Coast to aid in the rescue foreigners trapped in a school by fighting between government troops and rebel troops. Rebels had attempted to take over the government on September 19.
26-09-1774 John Chapman was born. He was better known as Johnny Appleseed. He planted orchards, befriended wild animals, and was considered at great medicine man by Native Americans.
26-09-1777 Philadelphia was occupied by British troops during the American Revolutionary War.
26-09-1789 Thomas Jefferson was appointed America's first Secretary of State. John Jay was appointed the first chief justice of the U.S. Samuel Osgood was appointed the first Postmaster-General. Edmund Jennings Randolph was appointed the first Attorney General.
26-09-1892 "The King of Marches" was introduced to the general public.
26-09-1908 Ed Eulbach of the Chicago Cubs became the first baseball player to pitch both games of a doubleheader and win both with shutouts.
26-09-1908 In "The Saturday Evening Post" an ad for the Edison Phonograph appeared.
26-09-1914 The U.S. Federal Trade Commission was established.
26-09-1918 During World War I, the Meuse-Argonne offensive against the Germans began. It was the final Allied offensive on the western front.
26-09-1950 U.N. troops recaptured the South Korean capital of Seoul from the North Koreans during the Korean Conflict.
26-09-1955 The New York Stock Exchange suffered its worst decline since 1929 when the word was released concerning U.S. President Eisenhower's heart attack.
26-09-1960 The first televised debate between presidential candidates Richard M. Nixon and John F. Kennedy took place in Chicago, IL.
26-09-1962 "The Beverly Hillbillies" premiered on CBS-TV.
26-09-1964 "Gilligan's Island" premiered on CBS-TV. The show aired for the last time on September 4, 1967.
26-09-1969 "The Brady Bunch" series premiered on ABC-TV.
26-09-1980 The Cuban government abruptly closed Mariel Harbor to end the freedom flotilla of Cuban refugees that began the previous April.
26-09-1981 The Boeing 767 made its maiden flight in Everett, WA.
26-09-1984 Britain and China initialed a draft agreement on the future of Hong Kong when the Chinese take over ruling the British Colony.
26-09-1985 Shamu was born at Sea World in Orlando, FL. Shamu was the first killer whale to survive being born in captivity.
26-09-1986 The episode of "Dallas" that had Bobby Ewing returning from the dead was aired.
26-09-1986 William H. Rehnquist became chief justice of the U.S. Supreme Court following the retirement of Warren Burger.
26-09-1990 The Motion Picture Association of America announced that it had created a new rating. The new NC17 rating was to keep moviegoers under the age of 17 from seeing certain films.
26-09-1991 Four men and four women began their two-year stay inside the "Biosphere II." The project was intended to develop technology for future space colonies.
26-09-1991 The U.S. Congress heard a plea from Kimberly Bergalis concerning mandatory AIDS testing for health care workers.
26-09-1992 163 people were killed when a Nigerian military transport crashed shortly after takeoff.
26-09-1993 The eight people who had stayed in "Biosphere II" emerged from their sealed off environment.
26-09-1995 The warring factions of Bosnia agreed on guidelines for elections and a future government.
26-09-1996 Richard Allen Davis, the killer of 12-year-old Polly Klaas, was sentenced to death in San Jose, CA.
26-09-1996 Shannon Lucid returned to Earth after being in space for 188 days. The time set a record for a U.S. astronaut and a woman.
26-09-1997 In Indonesia, a Garuda Airlines Airbus crashed killing 234 people.
26-09-2000 The U.S. House of Representatives passed the Born-Alive Infants Protection Act. The act states that an infant would be considered to have been born alive if he or she is completely extracted or expelled from the mother and breathes and has a beating heart and definite movement of the voluntary muscles.
26-09-2000 Slobodan Milosevic conceded that Vojislav Kostunica had won Yugoslavia's presidential election and declared a runoff. The declared runoff prompted mass protests.
26-09-2001 In Kabul, Afghanistan, the abandoned U.S. Embassy was stormed by protesters. It was the largest anti-Amercian protest since the terror attacks on New York City and Washington, DC, on September 11.
26-09-2001 Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat and Israeli Foreign Minister Shimon Peres announced plans to formalize a cease-fire and end a year of fighting in the region.
26-09-2001 In New York City, hundreds of people began the process of filing for death certificates for family members still missing in the ruins of the World Trade Center. At the time more than 6,300 people were still missing.
27-09-1779 John Adams was elected to negotiate with the British over the American Revolutionary War peace terms.
27-09-1825 George Stephenson operated the first locomotive that hauled a passenger train.
27-09-1840 Thomas Nast was born. He was a political cartoonist that created the Republican elephant and the Democrat donkey.
27-09-1854 The steamship Arctic sank off Cape Race, Newfoundland, with 300 people onboard. It was the first major disaster in the Atlantic Ocean.
27-09-1894 The Aqueduct Race Track opened in New York City, NY.
27-09-1928 The U.S. announced that it would recognize the Nationalist Chinese Government.
27-09-1938 The League of Nations branded the Japanese as aggressors in China.
27-09-1939 After 19 days of resistance, Warsaw, Poland, surrendered to the Germans after being invaded by the Nazis and the Soviet Union during World War II.
27-09-1940 The Berlin-Rome-Tokyo Axis was set up. The military and economic pact was for 10 years between Germany, Italy and Japan.
27-09-1954 The "Tonight!" show made its debut on NBC-TV with Steve Allen as host.
27-09-1959 The Japanese island of Honshu was hit by Typhoon Vera. Nearly 5,000 people were killed.
27-09-1962 The U.S. sold Hawk anti-aircraft missiles to Israel.
27-09-1964 The Warren Commission issued a report on the assassination of U.S. President John F. Kennedy in November of 1963. The report concluded that Lee Harvey Oswald had acted alone.
27-09-1968 The U.K.'s entry into the European Common Market was barred by France.
27-09-1970 "The Original Amateur Hour" aired for the last time on CBS. It had been on television for 22 years.
27-09-1973 U.S. Vice President Spiro Agnew said he would not resign after he pleaded "no contest" to a charge of tax evasion. He did resign on October 10th.
27-09-1979 The Department of Education became the 13th Cabinet in U.S. history after the final approval from Congress.
27-09-1982 Italian and French soldiers entered the Sabra and Chatilla refugee camps in Beirut. The move was made by the members of a multinational force due to hundreds of Palestinians being massacred by Christian militiamen.
27-09-1983 Larry Bird signed a seven-year contract with the Boston Celtics worth $15 million. The contract made him the highest paid Celtic in history.
27-09-1986 The U.S. Senate approved federal tax code changes that were the most sweeping since World War II.
27-09-1989 Columbia Pictures Entertainment agreed to buyout Sony Corporation for $3.4 billion.
27-09-1989 Two men went over the 176-foot-high Niagara Falls in a barrel. Jeffrey Petkovich and Peter Debernardi were the first to ever survive the Horshoe Falls.
27-09-1990 The deposed emir of Kuwait addressed the U.N. General Assembly and denounced the "rape, destruction and terror" that Iraq had inflicted upon his country.
27-09-1991 U.S. President Bush eliminated all land-based tactical nuclear arms and removed all short-range nuclear arms from ships and submarines around the world. Bush then called on the Soviet Union to do the same.
27-09-1993 U.S. Senator Kay Baily Hutchinson, Rep.-TX, was indicted on charges that she misused state facilities and employees while she was the Texas state treasurer. The charges were later dropped.
27-09-1994 More than 350 Republican congressional candidates signed the Contract with America. It was a 10-point platform they pledged to enact if voters sent a GOP majority to the House.
27-09-1995 The U.S. government unveiled the redesigned $100 bill. The bill featured a larger, off-center portrait of Benjamin Franklin.
27-09-1996 The Taliban seized control of Kabul, the capital of Afghanistan, and hanged the former president Najibullah.
27-09-1998 In Germany, Social Democrat Gerhard Schroeder was elected chancellor. The election ended 16 years of conservative rule.
27-09-1998 Mark McGwire (St. Louis Cardinals) set a major league baseball record when he hit his 70th home run of the season.
27-09-2001 In Zug, Switzerland, an armed man killed 14 people and himself after entering the local parliament.
27-09-2002 In Senegal, over 1,000 people were killed when the ocean ferry MS Joola capsized.
27-09-2004 North Korean Vice Foreign Minister Choe Su Hon announced that North Korea had turned plutonium from 8,000 spent nuclear fuel rods into nuclear weapons. He also said that the weapons were to serve as a deterrent against increasing U.S. nuclear threats and to prevent nuclear war in northeast Asia. The U.S. State Department noted that the U.S. has repeatedly said that the U.S. has no plans to attack North Korea.
28-09-1066 England was invaded by William the Conqueror who claimed the English throne.
28-09-1542 San Diego, CA, was discovered by Portuguese navigator Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo.
28-09-1687 The Turks surrendered Athens to the Venetians.
28-09-1781 During the Revolutionary War, American forces began the siege on Yorktown, VA.
28-09-1787 The U.S. Congress voted to send the new Constitution of the United States to the state legislatures for their approval.
28-09-1850 The U.S. Navy abolished flogging as a form of punishment.
28-09-1850 U.S. President Millard Fillmore named Brigham Young the first governor of the Utah territory. In 1857, U.S. President James Buchanan removed Young from the position.
28-09-1892 The first nighttime football game in the U.S. took place under electric lights. The game was between the Mansfield State Normal School and the Wyoming Seminary.
28-09-1915 The British defeated the Turks in Mesopotamia at Kut-el-Amara.
28-09-1920 Eight members of the Chicago White Sox were indicted in what was called the "Black Sox" scandal. They were accused of throwing the 1919 World Series against the Cincinnati Reds.
28-09-1924 The first around-the-world flight was completed by two U.S. Army planes when they landed in Seattle, WA. The trip took 175 days.
28-09-1936 "Bachelor's Children" debuted on CBS Radio.
28-09-1939 During World War II, Germany and the Soviet Union agreed upon a plan on the division of Poland.
28-09-1939 "Fleischmann Hour" aired for the last time on radio.
28-09-1944 "The Boys From Boise" was shown on WABD in New York as the first full-length comedy written for television.
28-09-1950 The United Nations admitted Indonesia.
28-09-1955 The World Series was televised in color for the first time. The game was between the New York Yankees and the Brooklyn Dodgers.
28-09-1961 "Dr. Kildare" premiered on NBC-TV.
28-09-1961 "Hazel" premiered on NBC-TV.
28-09-1967 The first mayor of Washington, DC, Walter Washington, took office.
28-09-1968 The Atlanta Chiefs won the first North American Soccer League Championship.
28-09-1972 Communist China and Japan agreed to re-establish diplomatic relations.
28-09-1974 First Lady Betty Ford underwent a mastectomy to remove a lump in her breast.
28-09-1978 Heavy fighting occurred in Lebanon between Syrian peacekeeping troops and Lebanese Christian militiamen.
28-09-1978 Don Sherman, editor of Car & Driver, set a new Class E record in Utah. Driving the Mazda RX7 he reached a speed of 183.904 mph.
28-09-1984 Bob Hope showed outtakes of his 34 years in television on NBC.
28-09-1985 Rioting erupted in London's Brixton district that lasted for two days. The incident occurred after a black woman was shot by a police officer during a raid on her home.
28-09-1987 Mehdi Hashemi was executed for treason in an Iranian prison. Hashemi had at one time been a close aide to Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini.
28-09-1989 Ferdinand E. Marcos died in Hawaii, in exile, at the age of 72.
28-09-1991 In response to U.S. President Bush's reduction of U.S. nuclear arms Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev promised to reciprocate.
28-09-1991 Marion Barry, former mayor of the District of Columbia, was sentenced to six months in prison for possession of crack cocaine.
28-09-1992 In Nepal, 167 people were killed when a Pakistani jetliner crashed.
28-09-1994 900 people were killed when an Estonian ferry capsized in the Baltic Sea.
28-09-1994 Jose Francisco Ruiz Massieu was assassinated. He was the No. 2 man in the Institutional Revolutionary Party in Mexico.
28-09-1995 Yasser Arafat of the PLO and Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin signed an accord that transferred control of the West Bank.
28-09-1997 The 103rd convention of the Audio Engineering Society (AES) was held in New York City, NY. The official debut of the DVD format was featured.
28-09-2000 The U.S. Federal Drug Administration approved the use of RU-486 in the United States. The pill is used to induce an abortion.
28-09-2004 The U.S. Federal Reserve and the U.S. Secret Service introduced the first newly redesigned $50 bill.
28-09-2004 Nate Olive and Sarah Jones arrived at the U.S.-Mexico border to complete the first known continuous hike of the 1,800-mile trail down the U.S. Pacific Coast. They started the trek on June 8.
29-09-1758 England's Admiral Horatio Nelson was born.
29-09-1789 A regular army was established by the U.S. War Department with several hundred men.
29-09-1829 The first public appearance by London's re-organized police force was met with jeers from political opponents. The force became known as Scotland Yard.
29-09-1902 David Belasco opened his first Broadway theater.
29-09-1930 Lowell Thomas made his debut on CBS Radio. He was in the radio business for the next 46 years.
29-09-1930 Bing Crosby and Dixie Lee were married.
29-09-1940 The radio quiz show "Double or Nothing" debuted on the Mutual Radio Network.
29-09-1943 U.S. Gen. Dwight D. Eisenhower and Italian Marchal Pietro Badoglio signed an armistice aboard the British ship Nelson.
29-09-1946 "The Adventures of Sam Spade" debuted on CBS Radio.
29-09-1951 The first network football game was televised by CBS-TV in color. The game was between the University of California and the University of Pennsylvania.
29-09-1953 "Make Room for Daddy" premiered on ABC-TV.
29-09-1955 "A View From the Bridge," a play by Arthur Miller, opened in New York at the Coronet Theater.
29-09-1957 The New York Giants played their last game at the Polo Grounds. The next year the Giants were in San Francisco, CA.
29-09-1960 "My Three Sons" debuted on ABC-TV.
29-09-1962 U.S. President John F. Kennedy nationalized the Mississippi National guard in response to city officials defying federal court orders. The orders had been to enroll James Meredith at the University of Mississippi.
29-09-1963 "My Favorite Martian" premiered on CBS-TV.
29-09-1963 "The Judy Garland Show" premiered on CBS-TV.
29-09-1967 The International Monetary Fund reformed monetary systems around the world.
29-09-1977 Eva Shain became the first woman to officiate a heavyweight title boxing match. About 70 million people watched Muhammad Ali defeat Ernie Shavers on NBC-TV.
29-09-1978 Pope John Paul I was found dead after only one month of serving as pontiff of the Roman Catholic Church.
29-09-1982 In Chicago, IL, seven people died after taking capsules of Extra-Strength Tylenol that had been laced with cyanide. 264,000 bottles were recalled.
29-09-1983 The War Powers Act was used for the first time by the U.S. Congress when they authorized President Reagan to keep U.S. Marines in Lebanon for 18 more months.
29-09-1983 "A Chorus Line" with performance number 3,389 became the longest running show on Broadway.
29-09-1984 Irish officials announced that they had intercepted the Marita Anne carrying seven tons of U.S.-purchased weapons. The weapons were intended for the Irish Republican Army.
29-09-1984 Elizabeth Taylor was voted to be the world's most beautiful woman in a Louis Harris poll. Taylor was at the time in the Betty Ford Clinic overcoming a weight problem.
29-09-1986 Nicholas Daniloff was released by the Soviet Union. He had been held on spying charges.
29-09-1986 Mary Lou Retton announced that she was quitting gymnastics.
29-09-1988 The space shuttle Discovery took off from Cape Canaveral in Florida. It was the first manned space flight since the Challenger disaster.
29-09-1990 "Millie's Book" by First Lady Barbara Bush was the best-selling non-fiction book in the U.S.
29-09-1992 Magic Johnson announced that he was returning to professional basketball. The comeback ended the following November.
29-09-1992 Brazilian lawmakers overwhelmingly voted to impeach President Fernando Collor de Mello.
29-09-1993 Bosnia's parliament voted overwhelmingly to reject an international peace plan unless Bosnian Serbs returned land that had been taken by force.
29-09-1994 The U.S. House voted to end the practice of lobbyist buying meals and entertainment for members of Congress.
29-09-1995 Three U.S. servicemen were indicted on rape charges concerning a 12-year-old Okinawan girl. The men were handed over to Japanese authorities.
29-09-1998 Hasbro announced plans to introduce an action figure of retired U.S. General Colin Powell.
29-09-1998 In Austin, TX, nine carnival ride executives were indicted by a grand jury for the death of teenage girl who had been killed when thrown from a ride.
30-09-1399 Henry Bolingbroke became the King of England as Henry IV.
30-09-1630 John Billington was hanged for murder. He was the first criminal to be executed in the American colonies.
30-09-1777 The Congress of the United States moved to York, PA, due to advancing British forces.
30-09-1787 The Columbia left Boston and began the trip that would make it the first American vessel to sail around the world.
30-09-1846 Ether, an experimental anesthetic at the time, was used for the first time by Dr. William Morton at Massachusetts General Hospital.
30-09-1861 Chewing gum tycoon William Wrigley, Jr. was born.
30-09-1868 Spain's Queen Isabella was deposed and fled to France.
30-09-1882 In Appleton, WI, the world's first hydroelectric power plant began operating.
30-09-1924 Truman Streckfus Persons was born in New Orleans, LA. He later changed his name to Truman Capote.
30-09-1927 George Herman "Babe" Ruth hit his 60th homerun of the season. He broke his own record with the homerun. The record stood until 1961 when Roger Maris broke the record.
30-09-1930 "Death Valley Days" was heard for the first time on the NBC Blue radio network.
30-09-1935 "The Adventures of Dick Tracey" debuted on Mutual Radio Network.
30-09-1935 "Porgy and Bess" premiered in Boston.
30-09-1938 The Munich Conference ended with a decision to appease Adolf Hitler. Britain, and France allowed Czechoslovakia's Sudetenland to be annexed by the Nazis.
30-09-1939 "Captain Midnight" was heard for the first time on the Mutual Radio Network.
30-09-1946 An international military tribunal in Nuremberg, Germany, found 22 top Nazi leaders guilty of war crimes.
30-09-1947 The World Series was televised for the first time. The sponsors only paid $65,000 for the entire series between the Brooklyn Dodgers and the New York Yankees.
30-09-1949 The Berlin Airlift came to an end. The airlift had taken 2.3 million tons of food into the western sector despite the Soviet blockade.
30-09-1951 "The Red Skelton Show" debuted on NBC-TV.
30-09-1954 The U.S. Navy commissioned the Nautilus submarine at Groton, CT. It was the first atomic-powered vessel. The submarine had been launched on January 21, 1954.
30-09-1954 Julie Andrews made her first Broadway appearance in "The Boy Friend".
30-09-1955 Actor James Dean was killed in a car accident at the age of 24 near Cholame, CA. Dean's mechanic, who was also in the vehicle, eventually recovered from his injuries.
30-09-1962 James Meredith succeeded in registering at the University of Mississippi. It was his fourth attempt to register.
30-09-1963 The Soviet Union publicly declared itself on the side of India in their dispute with Pakistan over Kashmir.
30-09-1966 Albert Speer and Baldur von Schirach were released at midnight from Spandau prison after completing their 20-year sentences. Speer was the Nazi minister of armaments and von Schirach was the founder of Hitler Youth.
30-09-1971 The Soviet Union and the United States signed pacts that were aimed at avoiding an accidental nuclear war.
30-09-1971 A committee of nine people was organized to investigate the prison riot at Attica, NY. 10 hostages and 32 prisoners were killed when National Guardsmen stormed the prison on September 13, 1971.
30-09-1976 California enacted the Natural Death Act of California. The law was the first example of right-to-die legislation in the U.S.
30-09-1980 Israel issued its new currency, the shekel, to replace the pound.
30-09-1982 "Cheers" began an 11-year run on NBC-TV.
30-09-1984 107 Moslem extremists were sentenced to prison for their actions after the assassination of President Anwar Sadat in 1981.
30-09-1984 Mike Witt became only the 11th pitcher to throw a perfect game in major league baseball.
30-09-1984 "Doonesbury" by Garry Trudeau returned. The comic strip had not been printed in nearly 20 months.
30-09-1985 Four Soviet diplomats were kidnapped in Beirut by the Islamic Liberation Organization. One of the diplomats was killed and the other three were later released.
30-09-1986 The U.S. released accused Soviet spy Gennadiy Zakharov, one day after the Nicholas Daniloff had been released by the Soviets.
30-09-1987 Mikhail S. Gorbachev retired President Andrei A. Gromyko from the Politburo and fired other old-guard leaders in a shake-up at the Kremlin.
30-09-1989 Thousands of East Germans began emigrating under an accord between the NATO nations and the Soviet Union.
30-09-1989 Non-Communist Cambodian guerrillas claimed that they had captured 3 towns and 10 other positions from the residing government forces.
30-09-1990 The Soviet Union and South Korea opened diplomatic relations.
30-09-1991 Haiti's first freely elected president, Jean-Bertrand Aristide, was overthrown by Brigadier General Raoul Cedras. Aristide was later returned to power.
30-09-1992 George Brett of the Kansas City Royals reached his 3,000th career hit during a game against the California Angels.
30-09-1992 Moscow banks distributed privatization vouchers aimed at turning millions of Russians into capitalists.
30-09-1993 About 10,000 people were killed in India when an earthquake that measured 6.4 hit the southern part of the country.
30-09-1993 U.S. chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Colin Powell retired.
30-09-1994 The space shuttle Endeavor took off on an 11-day mission. Part of the mission was to use a radar instrument to map remote areas of the Earth.
30-09-1997 France's Roman Catholic Church apologized for its silence during the persecution and deportation of Jews the pro-Nazi Vichy regime.
30-09-1998 Gov. Pete Wilson of California signed a bill into law that defined "invasion of privacy as trespassing with the intent to capture audio or video images of a celebrity or crime victim engaging in a personal of family activity." The law went into effect January 1, 1999.
30-09-1999 The San Francisco Giants played the Los Angeles Dodgers in the last baseball game to be played at Candlestick Park (3Com Park). The Dodgers won 9-4.
30-09-1999 In Tokaimura, Japan, radiation escaped a nuclear facility after workers accidentally set off an uncontrolled nuclear chain reaction.
30-09-2003 The FBI began a criminal investigation concerning the possibility that White House officials had illegally leaked the identity of an undercover CIA officer.
